Injection of hydrocortisone into the~ prior to a passive avoidance training reduces the amnesic effect cf ECS given shortly after the training. It is probable that ECS produces ai-rmesia by preventing corti- costeroids from entering into the hippocampus to modify the neural activity :i.n the limbic sys ten.
Suckling Pattern and Related Behavior of Fsmale and Pup Grey Seals, Halichoerus grypus at three br.eeding sites.
abstract:
Major differences in caretaking b~haviour have been suggested hy
ambiguous qualitative studies at different breeding sites. In this
study, quantitative data concerning du:cation and frea_uency of suckling,
arrount of time females spend with their pups, and behavior toward unrelat.ed
pups were obtained at three separate breeding sites. The mean percentage

and 100%. Diffe!'.:"ences were also found in the rnear.. duration of and mean
frequency of suckling between mother and pup p:iirs. l\ high frequency
of alien suckling occurred at one beach (75% of all females), but was
relatively low at the other two breeding sites (20i and 6% of females).
Possible explanations for these differences a.re discussed in terms of
behavioral differences which might be mediated by the topography of the
breeding site.

title: Body Contact in the Play Behaviour of '·7olf PUP,:>.,•
abstract:
Body contact in wolf pups is analyzed by means of a fine grain method.
It is suggested that such an analysis is necessary for the w1derstanding
of some aspects of play behaviour. Sone behavioural regularities extracted
from analysed sequences are discussed.

title: Set-theoretic Ar-preaches to Quantitative Semantics
abstract:
Quantitative approaches to semantics based on the theory of mathe-
matical sets will be described and relevant data reported. Both traditional
set theory and the more recent fuzzy set theory might prove useful as
models of semantic processing. In the present paper, data is reported
which indicates the frJitfulness of both these approaches.

title: The Machinations of r1anova.
abstract:
Somewhat like Hata Hari, Multivariate Analysis of Variance has many
mysteries and magical properties. This talk \1ill discu:::s some of these;
in particular the three ways of judgir:g what is happening, whether the qi;.estions
about what is h2.ppening a.re relevant, a.nd when judgments are better on the
vertical (sta.,ding u9) or horizontal {lying down) dimensions.

title: Vocal Behaviour in Polves
abstract:
An individual's behavior is influenced by its social context. During
a group hm·1ling session, a wolf's howls e11idently reflect this influence
t.'1-irough the:i.r structure. Usinq film and sonagraphic analysis, howling-
interactions in red i•;olf-~oyote hyI1rids at Shubenacadie are being studied
to describe howl structure and its possible relation to social factors.

title: Matching on Mi:x:e<'l. Concurrent and Multiple Schedules.
abstract:
Hhen the discrminative stimuli are i:ernove0. from multiple and concurrent

their responses between the two conponent schedules in the sa"".leproportion

that responses are followed by reinforcement and that changes in the
component schedule followed by responses are followed hy reinforcenent
determine when the subject changes component schedules.

title: Relativistic Determination of the Values of Reinforcement Schedules.
abstract:
We are teaching pigeons a pair of multiple schedule discrimina-
tions in which the more favorable schedule in one discTiminaticn
(between VI 90" and ~'I 270") is the same as the less favorable schedule
in the other (between VI 30" and VI 90"). If the value of a schedule,
and of the stimulus associated with it, is determined in part by the
context of other reinforcement contingencies, then the pigeon ought to
prefer the stimulus that signals VI 90" in the former discrimination.
We have obtained evidence through several test procedures that supports
this expectation.

title: Synaptic 1'\c,difiability in Middle Aged and _Senescen_~Rats_.
abstract:
Synapses in middle aged ar,d senescent rats show pronounced
differences in their response to repeated presentations of high fre-
quency stimulation. The ability for these synapses to be modified is
crHically dependent on time of day; how8ver, the circadian cycle of
this effect is disrupted in the old animals.

title: Endorphinergic neurons and self-stimulation.
abstract:
Possibility that the en<lorphinergic neurons may be involved in
the generation of rewarding effect was tested using naloxone. Rats with
electrodes in the r.,edj al frontal cortex, lateral hypothalarnus, and
ventral tegmentum ,,,ere trained to press a. ba-:: for electrical stimulation.
When the response rate became stable, they were intraperitoneally injected
with naloxone hydrochloride. Suppression of bar-pressing was observed
for some of the electrode placements at relatively high doses of the drug.

title: Dissociation of Short and Long Lasting Modification of Synaptic Efficacy at the Terminals of the Perforant Path.
abstract:
At the perforant path-dentate granule cell synapse, the increased
synaptic efficacy following brief episodes of high frequency activation
can be resolved into a long lasting component and one or more short last-
ing components. The long lasting component requires about 50 seconds to
develop, following the stimulation, and may last, under some circumstances,
for weeks. The short lasting effects are present immediately following
activation, and decay completely within minutes. Different rules govern
the development of the two effects. They interact in a fashion con-
sistent with the hypothesis that a post-synaptic mechanism under]ies the
long lasting effect.

title: Hemispheric Processing Efficiency: A 90 Minute Rhythmic Alternation.
abstract:
Roger Broughton has proposed that the two hemispheres of the
brain "take turns" controlling processing. During sleep this alternation
is reflected in the cyclic occurrence of rapid eye movement sleep. We
are testing performance on two speeded classification tasks designed
specifically to tap the processing efficiency of the t\,O hemispheres.
Subjects are tested on both tasks every 15 minutes for 8 hours. So far
two subjects have been tested in 4 such sessions each. Our results
support Broughton's proposal that this rhythm continues during the day-
time.

title: Differences Between ~ormal and "Compress_ed" Tecta Seen by GoTg TtXeth od s . Observations of "compressed" tecta after impregnations hy the Golgi-Ccx technique suggest an expansion cf the :.iendritic arbor of some tcctal elements. Studies in progress using the Rapid Golgi technique to identify the elements in.vol ved, and quantitative aspects of the presumed dend-.ri tic spread are disc:uss ed. Also, the implications of such an expan- sion are considered.
Perception of a Phonetic Contrast in Multi- ~yll ahic Utterances by Two-Honth Olcl Infatrts.
abstract:
In the present study, three aspects of the two-month old's
perception of multisyllabic utterances were explored. First, do
infants perceive phonetic contrasts occurring either in the initial
(Bada-Gada) or media.I (Daba-Daga) positions of multisyllabic utterances?
Second-; are infants m·:::irelikely to perceive these contrasts in stressed
as opposed to unstressed syllables? Third, will infants detect a
difference between two stress patterns? Our results indicate the follow-
ing: (1) Two-month olds are sensitive to place-of-articulation differ-
ences occurring in either the initial or medial positions of multi-
syllabic stimuli; (2) Whether the contrast occurred between stressed or
unstressed syllables had little or no effect on the infant's ability to
detect it; (3) Infants as young as two-months old respond to differ-
ences between stress patterns.

title: _Effects of :-!onocular Deprivation on Cat Area 17 and ]8.
abstract:
Monocular deprivation has been shown to result !n a decrease in
cell size of layers of the lateral geniculate nucleus receiving pro-
jections from the deprived eye. This effect has been attributed to a
differential shinkage of the Y cell population in this nucleus, the X
cells being relatively unaffected. The present study attempts to ascer-
tain whether the Y system is indeed more sensative to deprivation. X
cells project only to area 17 of the cat visual cortex while Y cells pro-
ject to both area 17 and 18. By comparing the effect of M.D. on areas 17
and 18 this hypothesis may be tested. Preliminary results indicate that
monocular deprivation affect~ areas 17 a~d 18 equally.

title: Behavioural Evidence for Visual Plasticitv in Dark-Reared Cats.
abstract:
Recovery of visual function was measured behavicu:rally in 3 cats,
2 raised in darkness until they were 4 months and 1 until it was 6 months
old. Initially, the animals appeared to be totally blind, but within
about 1 week for the 4-month old cats and about 3 weeks for the 6-month
old cat signs of vision were evident. Thereafter, visual acuity, as
measured on a j ur.iriing st,md, progressively improved, a!Jprcachi:1g normal
values within 3 months. These results support recent physiological
evidence for recovery of function in the visual cortex following long-
term visual deprivation.

title: Care-Tak~ and Relatedness in Canids.
abstract:
Kin selection is a sociobiological model for the evolution of
non-parental altruism. Since close relatives share genes, genetic fit-
ness can be increased by promoting the interests of relatives, provided
that the cost is sufficiently low. The theory predicts that diffore.1t:ial
altruism towards relatives will be selected for. In wolves non-repro-
ducing pack members cissist in pup care-taking. I wili be investigating
care-givinz in a pack of captive timber wolves and a pack of captive
coyote-red wolf hybrids with particular attention to the relatedness of
pups and care-p.ivers.

title: Changing Size As a Cu2 to The Percep..!_~on of Movement in Dep:h.
abstract:
Adapting to a target whose size oscillates about a mean value
selectively depresses visual sensitivity to oscillating size. This
cannot he attributed to fatigued movement detectors. Furtherr.,ore, after
adapting to a target whose size increases wjth a ramp waveform, a sub-
sequently-vie\1ed test target appears to move continuously away in depth.
This aftereffect cannot he explained in terms cf the cl2.ssical m0vement
aftereffect. I propose that there are, in the human visual system,
detectors selectively sensitive to unidirectionally changing size 2nd
which can drive tl-:e neural mechanism that underlies the perception of
motion in depth.

title: Maintenance of Spatial Ori:ranization in Wolf P_£pulations_.
abstract:
Single species often display a remarkable array of social group-
ings and inter-group relationships as adaptations to variable local ecolo-
gical situations. In t!1e gray wolf (Canis l:_uous), some po_i::.ulations are
characteTized hy resident, territoricil packs whereas other populations
are composed of nomadic packs. Of interest here are the behaviors
involved ill naintenance of these different pack spatial, ian<l tenure
systems. Both sce!1t-marld ng an<l howI ing evidently play important roles
in maintenance of territorial spatia 1 sys terns. An analysis of their
respective roles suggests both may also play very s imi) ar roles for
nomadic land-tenure systems as welJ..

title: Serial Processing of Com.£2und ~lental Rotations.
abstract:
Compound mental rotations were made by combining Shepard &
Metzler's (1971) two separate rot at ions of complex abstract figures in
picture-plane and depth. Four subjects were each teste<l on 680 rotations,
both single cmd double, viewed in a tachistoscope. The task was to
decide whether a pair of figures were the same or different, and reactio:1
times and errors were recorded. Results showed that the time to per-
form the double rotations was equal to the sum of the times for the two
single rotations. It is concluded that the double operation is done
serially rather than in parallel.

title: The Distribution of Social Interactions A,11ongthe Member Pairs of a Group of c·apti\;e-\\'ol ~r_es. ·--------
abstract:
In addition to the analysis of the organisation of moven~ent in

at two higher levels of social hehaviour: relationship~ and social
structure. For the past two and one-half years inteTactions observed

The relevance of this patterning to a meaningful chaTactsrisation of soc-
ial relationsJ,ips and social structure 1.vill be discussed.

title: pependence of Enhancement on Stimulus .!_Etensity.
abstract:
Changes in intensity of electrical pulses applied to the per-
forant path input to the hippocarnpus narkeclly affected the number of
presynaptic fibres activated, and the resulting post-synaptic potential.
Changes in stimulus strength also ciffected the amount of enhancement of

more enhancement. This suggests that enhancement of synapses of one

title: Mechanisns of Pattern Vision in Fields of Uniform Luminances.
abstract:
When an edge is visible in a photic array whose luminance is
everywhere the sar1e, there must be a rlifferential stimulation of at
least more than one of the three cones in the photopic reti~a. The
question posed in this talk will be: which of the three cone types are
involved in the perception of edges (thus forms) ,-,;hen there is equivalent
stimulation of their SU~l everyi.-liere in the visual display? Two experi-
ments are reported that give strong evidence that only two of the three
cone types of the trichro~atic eye are necessary for such form percep-
tion., and one possible rneai's of carrying out this processins is discussed.
] 7. M. Cynader - Extension of the Critical Perio_?_~n Cat Visual Cortex.
It is now generally accepted that response characteristics of
sing] e cells in cat visual cortex can be modified by a typical visual
exposure only during the first three r.1onths of life. Monocular depri-

even after the "critical period". Strong effects of monocular depri-

title: Factors Which Influence the Demonstration of Infant Vocal Conditioning.
abstract:
Two groups of infants were identified on the basis of their
vocal behavior in the fiTst 2-min baseline period (u:nesponsive adult')
in a social conditioning study. Infants whose vocal rates were less
than 2 responses/min were the same infants who looked away from the
e).-perimenter' s face and who smiled less frequently. MoreoveT, these
low-vocal-rate infants weTe unaffected by social stimulation. For both
groups, baseline vocal rates Kere indicative of sociability, as measured
by smiling, eye contact and vocal responses to social stimulation.

title: 'Discrete' Behaviour Patterns?
abstract:
Discrete uni ts of behavioural action have long been the cornrr.on
currency of ethological description and explanation, but there is grow-

to attempt to resolve this problem for facial grool"ling in mice in order
to determine the applicability of various behavioural models used to
explain the sequential structure of behaviour. The approach has been to
quantify the limb and hody movements using co-ordinates from high-speed
film. The overt behavioural action, in which 'discrete' patterns have
been subjectively identified, is then reconstructed from these component
actions. When form discontinuities are identifiec'. at this level it may
then be assessed whether such discontinuities are a function of individ-
ual body components, or of their combined action.

title: Dunhar.1- Changes in Unpunished BeJ,avi9r Dur] ng_ Rc~opse Con_!ingent Punishment.

responding and a recc-nt critical test of these rules.

title: Effects of Strabisrnus and nonocular ncp7:'ivation on the Developi_!lg Kj-tt~Tl Visual Cortex.
abstract:
Kittens were reared normally until postnatal <ldy 15, when a

title: Active vs. Passive Exposure: Effects on Kitten Visual Cortex.
abstract:
Kittens were reared with one eye open when the animal v;as free

title: The Structure of the Normal Goldfish Tectum as Seen by Different Golgi Techniques.
abstract:
Different aspects of tectum morphology can be illustrated by
Golgi-Cox and Rapid Golgi techniques. The Golgj-Cox method shows good
overall impregnation of a variety of tectal demrnts. The various Rapid
Golgi techniques, which stain more selectively, show better the detailed

title: Den-Diggin_g and Related l3ehavior in a Captive Timber \fol f Pack.
abstract:
Timber wolves dig dens annually prior to whelping season in the
spring. Den-digging and related behavior of a captive pack of four

birth of six pups. Archj tectu:ral features, method of construction and
some aspects of den use not previously reported in the literature are

title: now Fuzzv_Ec the ~leanings of Verbal DeEcriptions?

Several experiments wi 11 be repoTted in which Zadeh' s fuzzy set

title: Mus l<usicus.
abstract:
As a musical composition integrated patterns of behaviour can
be thoueht of in te1111s of "notes", "rests", and higher--order "melodies".
Among questions of interest is how these levels of order emerge and

boration with Dr. Ilan Golani the developmental orchestration of some

\<Thotranslate scores into performancej and of the relations among neuronal

title: Self-Stimulat:ion in the Neonatal Rat.
abstract:
Electrical stimulation of the hypothalamus has Leen found to
operantly condition a contact response in rats under 17 days of age.
The development ;:ind refinement of simple methods of anaesthesia, implant-

are discussed. The difficulty of selecting an adequate operant response

with regard to the lability, vigour and ease of quantification of suc~1 a

technjque may clarify the role of t!1e various neural systems which

title: Sp2..ce-Time InteraEt:ions in Cat Visual Cortex.
abstract:
It was proposed that binocular cells wit!, interocular latency
di fforences could provide a suhstrate for a time l ased ster~~c>scopi c depth
mechanism. This idea 1,as based on the assumption that siuultaneity of

space ;:ind time, and suggest that both :,urameters are usrd for stereo-

title: New Classes of Neurons in Cat Parastriatc Cortex Sensitive to the Direction of ~,:otion in Thrce-DimensionaT"'space.
abstract:
Beverley & Regan suggested on psychophysical grom,ds that in
man there exists a binocular neural mechanism that e:r.phasizes movement
in depth, while the well-known stereoscopic mechanism emphasizes
position in depth. Cynader f; Regan now find in cat visual cortex three
types of neuron. Two are tuned to the direction of motion in depth, one
for missing, the other for hi tb ng the head: these neurons throw away
positional information ("I don't know where it is or what it is, hut
duck"). The third category may signal positional information in space.
The two new cell classes cut across established cell categories (simple,
complex, etc.): their behaviour is revealed only by exactly appropriate
stimulation. Hitting-the-head neurons occur in clusters-possibly columns.

title: Control of Choice Behaviour in Matching-to-Sample by Sample-:,pecific Behaviours.
abstract:
Pigeons were trained on matchinB-to-sample with differential
sample-response requirements for produciHg ccmparisons. Reversing the

matching-to-sar.ple but ii) rapid acquisition of oddi ty-fro:n-samp1 e.
Choices between comparisons were apparently controlled by sarr.ple-

title: Jusczyk of Rane. A woman who has heen raped is often doubly victimized: first, by the act; and second, by the legal process, the medical examinatio~, tl1e social embarrassment, and the emotional stress which follows. We will describe an empirical approac}l for deterJ11ining the level of social aware- ness, for t,nderstanding the personal stress of the victim, and for identifying the sources of disillusionment with the social and institutional response to rape. The focus of the research was designed to contribute to the public education, the personal counselling, and the social action functions of Halifax Rape Relief.
Time and !"'Jace: Sor.ie Properties of ~!ernory in a Neural Network.
abstract:
Separate short and long term changes occur at the same synapses
in a mammalian control nervous system. They are hased on different mechan-
isms and may have, respectively, pre-synaptic and post-srrnptic locations.
If all synapses in a theoretical neural net were constructed this way, the
error patterns generated during retrieval would differ as a function of
time since the learning experience. The differences in error type would
be similar in nature to those observed in experiIT1ent~ on human memory.
Since correct retrieval (the location of merrory) would not differ as a
function of time, a single place theory for experience,- st.oragc a:1d recall
j s pres erve<l.

title: Visu?.l Connections in the Dragonfly.
abstract:
Dragonflies are active visual predators with remarkably sophis-
ticated vision. Their photoreceptors are arranged in a characteristic
tiered fashion within each orrunatidium. Each ommatidium projects a set
of photoreceptor axons to its own cylindrical cartridge within the first
optic neuropile ,,·here synaptic associations are established with a small,
fixed group of interneu~o,1s of several classes. Electronmicroscopi c
studies reveal the spatial complexity of synaptic ir,terconnexion (dyadic
and triadic configurations of postsynaptic elements are found) and the
specificity with which particular configurations of elements are asso-
ciated. These will be illustrated from representati';e 111icrographs and
data presented to support numerically the range and consistency of synaptic
association encountered anato111ical ly.

title: Neuritic Outgrowths from the Adult Goldfish Retinal Tissue ·in Culture.
abstract:
The patterns of neuritic outgrowths from the retina are inves-
tigated in vitro under various experimental conditions by culturing the
retinal tissue, explanted from the adult goldfish. Under favorable
culture conditions, the retinal tissue shows vigorous outgrowth of
neurites as early as 12 hr and as late as 4-5 weeks after explantation.
The neuritic outgrowths from the retinal explant is enhanced by sec-
tion of the axons of the retinal ganglian cells prior to the explanta-
tion of the retinal tissue. The direction of neuritic outgrowths from
the retinal explant in vitro seems to follow the regularity in the
embryonic developmentof neuronal processes in vivo: the retinal
neurites tend to grow in the radial directiontowards the optic disc
at the center of the retina, especially at an early stage in culture.
In further experiments, a strip of retinal tissue and its topographi-
cally matching area of the optic tectum are co-cultured in various
geometric configuration within the same culture chamber. Some of the
retinal neurites eventually grow into the tectal tissue. It is not
known as yet whether these retinal neurites re-establish functional
synapses with appropriate visual neurones of the tectal tissue in vitro.

title: Measuring Copulatory Rate: A Mount-Bout Analysis.
abstract:
The male rat's rate of copulation (intromissions per minute)
has been used as a reflection of his level of sexual arousal. I have
shown that copulatory rate is not a primary measure, but is the product
of two more basic measures: mount-bout rate and the probability of
obtaining an intromission on each mount-bout. Mount-bout rate is shown
to be a reflection of arousal level while the probability of obtaining
an intromission reflects the male's performance mechanism and the level
of cooperation shown by the female. The use of mount-bouts is neces-
sary for the dissociation of arousal and performance mechanisms con-
trolling male rat sexual behaviour. k.,,,,,, ;.'f-:TJ.:;.:,v 1._; -;,Ltc-r ,_e,--,~t::::.-.,

title: Growth of the Goldfish Optic Jectum. Goldfish grow throughout their lives; their brains grow also. It is apparent that this growth occurs through addition of new cells and by increases in size of existing cells. I will describe the post- natal growth of the tectal stratum periventriculare and show that most of the growth in this region occurs through enlargement of existing cells. In light of growth by cell addition in the retina, this form of tectal growth is difficult to understand. The occurrence of continu- ously shifting synaptic connections, and the implications of this phenomenon in an active, functioning animal, will be discussed.
A Glimpse at Scaling Theory.
abstract:
This is not another talk about fish, but rather a brief intro-
duction to a mathematical approach which can lead to insights about
such problems as the general ftmction of a brain and the inner experi-
ence of animals.

title: Changing-site Channels for Different Object Sizes. We previously reported psychophysical evidence for the exis- tence of a neural mechanism specifically sensitive to changin_£ object size. We now report that f~r narrow adapting stimuli, the changing- size specific threshold elevation is significant, providing the widths of the test and adapting stimuli do not differ by more than 0.5°. However, no significant elevation occurs for adapting stimuli wider than 1.5°. Further evidence that the changing-size channel is sensitive only to objects smaller than 1.5° is that aftereffects to changing size are explicable in terms of Waterfall effects at the moving edges when adapting stimuli are wider than 1.5°.
Comparison of Synaptic Modifiability in the Medial and Lateral Perforant Pathways: Evidense for two Function- ally Discrete Subsyste~.
abstract:
The synapses of the perforant pathway from the entorhinal
cortex to the fascia dentata are organized in a clearly laminar fashion.
Those fibres orginating most laterally in the enterhinal area terminate
most distally on the granule cell dendrites, while the most medially
originating fibres terminate most proximally. This system has been
classically regarded as a functional unit. Physiological studies of
short-term synaptic plasticity, however, suggest two functionally dis-
crete subsystems. This data is in agreement with recent histochemical
studies.
L. White - Social Play Levels in Young Rhesus Monkeys.
Levels of social play of immature rhesus monkeys living in
small captive groups are extremely variable, and depend, to some extent,
on the composition of the social group.. It can be difficult to pick out
developmental changes in the social play tendencies of a single individ-
ual, as any change in interaction level or type is the result of changes
in the behaviour of both interacting animals. Here the importance of
studying individual behaviour in the context of the social group is
emphasized, and an attempt is made to assign responsibility for changes
in play level more to one play partner than to the other.

title: Remembering Processes in the Pigeon.
abstract:
Depending on the requirements of the situation, it is
believed that an organism, such as a pigeon, can either remember a
stimulus or a future behaviour required by the present stimulus. Honig
has shown that in situations in which a pigeon is only required to
remember whether to peck or not, remembering occurs over longer inter-
vals than are normally found with procedures which require the recall
of a stimulus. The present research uses a procedure which also requires
only that the pigeon remember whether to respond or not, It is simpler
than those that have been used previously. Since this seems to produce
as much recall in pigeons as Honig's procedure, this hypothesis is
VO"r2_<._ 9. B. Tansley & M. Regan - Estimation of the Sensitivity of FM Channels
~ ..1 in the Human Auditory S~tem.
Because of the high sensitivity of auditory mechanisms to
amplitude changes associated with frequency variations of an acoustic
stimulus, the estimation of frequency-modulation sensitivity via a
~},~ threshold method is often contaminated with input from channels selec-
~\'· / tively sensitive to changing amplitude. We propose a method whereby it
~ v .-\,1 appears possible to estimate the sensitivity of FM channels by
_•;.,---trr "titrating off'' the activity of AMchannels due to differential
ponses within the auditory system to equal amplitude frequency varia-
re-
tions. With this method, the sensitivity of the FM channel under test
~-I \1_/:r-~l appears to be lower than previously reported.
ll--~J
R. Douglas - Heterosynaptic Inhibition of Synaptic £1a~!icity.
There are two major inputs to the dentate area of the hippo-
campus. The perforant path fibers make excitatory synapses onto the
granule cells, and long-lasting increases in the effectiveness of these
synapses can be produced by high-frequency activation. The commissural ~
input has a predominately inhibitory effect on the granule cells, and
does not appear to be modifiable. However, high-frequency activity
along it can completely prevent the enhancement of the perforant path
synapses. The commissural effect on synaptic plasticity can be disso-
ciated from its effects on cell discharge.

title: Induced Anisocoria.
abstract:
Kittens raised with a dimming filter over one eye show long-
lasting changes in the pupilary light reflex. The deprived eye
becomes more sensitive to light, and when the filter is removed, that
pupil is consistently smaller. The anisocoria lasts for weeks. We
suspect that this plasticity is due to a cerebellar mechanism and are
now testing normal and lesioned adult cats.

title: Resolution of Ambiguity irt the PerceFtion of Motion in Depth.
abstract:
Increasing or decreasing retinal image size is an ambiguous
visual cue because it can mean either that an object's size is chang-
ing or that the object is moving in depth. How does the visual system
resolve this ambiguity so as to provide an unequivocal basis for
action? We will present evidence that there are separate information-
processing channels for changing size and for motion in depth, and
·that the visual system contains a biassing mechanism that is capable
of suppressing the activity of one or other channel. One trigger
that activates this biassing mechanism is the relationship between
the speeds of the horizontal and vertical edges: if the object's
shape is constant, then the changing-size channel is rendered less
effective.

title: The Effects of a Third Choice on Cooperation • in a Mixed-Motive Game.
abstract:
The generally low amount of cooperative (30%) as opposed to
competitive (70%) responding in the two-choice, prisoner's dilemma
game may be due to the meanings attached to the restricted choices
provided by the game. In the present study a third choice was added
whose mere presence changed the "meaning" of the cooperative and
competitive choices. Three "meanings" of the cooperative choice·were
compared - sharing, self-interest, and altruism. The sharing condi-
tion produced the most cooperative responding (sharing= 56%, self-
interest = 33%, altruism= 36%). The self-interest condition pro-
duced the most competitive responding (sharing= 25%, self-interest
= 43%, altruism= 35%). Finally, the altruism condition produced
the most third (contract) choice responding (sharing= 19%, self-
interest = 24%, altruism= 29%).

title: The Effects of Three Preprograrnm!d Strategies on Cooperative responding in a Three-Choice Mixed-Motive Game.
abstract:
Cooperative responding in the prisoner's dilennna game has
been increased from a free play 30% to 80% when a preprogrammed tit-
for-tat strategy is used by the "partner". This strategy involves
responding on each trial with the real subject's previous response.
Thus, the subject is rewarded for a cooperative response (i.e., with
cooperation) and punished for a competitive response (i.e., with
competition). The effectiveness of this and two other preprogrammed
strategies were compared in a three-choice mixed-motive game. The
results of the experiment suggest that the reward and punish strategy
of tit-for-tat is more effective in producing cooperation than other
more "altruistic" strategies. Furthermore, in the three-choice
game this strategy elicited 95% cooperation in the last 30 of the 120
trials.

title: The Investigation of Objects by Rats Placed in a Novel Environment.
abstract:
An exploring rat spends a great deal of time examining the
objects it encounters. Each object is visited repeatedly and con-
tacted with the snout until, after several such visits, the rat will
have had experience with its entire surface. Evidence will be pre-
sented to show that the location of these contacts, and hence this
progression across the object's surface, is highly systematic,
depending both on the object's physical characteristics and on the
location of previous contacts.

title: Spatial Memory Deficits in Senescent Rats.
abstract:
Middle-aged and senescent rats' ability to acquire and retain a
task which forced the use -of a spatial strategy for its solution was
examined. Although both groups improved the accuracy with which they
located the goal tunnel, the older rats never attained the same level
of proficiency as their young counterparts. Furthermore, when given
a one-week retention test, the old rats' performance had deterior-
ated, while the young rats' performance did not. These results are
discussed in terms of previous work which suggests plausible neuro-
physiological factors underlying the behavioral deficit, and in light
of ongoing work which will examine the possibility of producing a
"behaviorally senescent" young rat through artifically induced
synaptic modification.

title: Lateral S-R CompatiEility in a Simple Reaction Time Task as a Function of Handedness and Hand- Writing Posture_. -
abstract:
Three groups of subjects, normal right-handers (RN), "normal"
left-handers (LN), and "inverted" left-handers who assume a "hooked"
hand-writing posture (LI) 0 pressed a key with the index finger of one
hand whenever a stimulus appeared to the right or left of a fixation
point. Response hand was varied across blocks of trials. For RN
and LN subjects, RTs were faster when the response hand was the one
ipsilateral rather than contralateral to the stimulus position.
However, this ipsilateral hand-field compatibility effect was not
observed in LI subjects. Implications for Levy's ipsilateral motor
control ex lana • I writing are discussed.
r,. 18. J. Fentress - Thoughts on Functional Generalization and Behavioral
\ .• Development.
\ ..\.,~j•~~ t1 The first line of argument is that ethological studies of
p' .~_$ integrated behavior can benefit by an attempted combined liaison
..A,J, ,-· \ 2( with cognitive psychology and embryology. The second argument is
QA)\" ·. ~'f:-J that rules of functional generalization in species-characteristic
\ 1 ~~ behavior may provide an important link in this attempted synthesis.
\,f ,~\ Simple actual and potential experiments will be used to illustrate
, ~jJ this idea.
19. Z. Havkin -:-.Interactive Modes of Wolf (Canis lupus) Play.
Several sources of evidence indicate that play behaviour is a
well organized phenomenon. Although details of interactions are
unique, interactions as whole composites show a high degree of order.
In P. Weiss' words: " ... individual freedom in the small is compatible
with the existence of collective order in the gross ... ".
20. M. Cynader - Adaptive Modification vs Selective Atrophr in Post-
natal Development.
In recent years, many studies have shown that it is possible
to alter the recording frequency of cells with particular trigger
features by rearing animals in unusual visual environments. To date,
it has not been clear whether these changes can be ascribed to the
death (or atrophy, or loss of responsiveness) of unstimulated neurons,
or whether the trigger features of individual units are altered in a
constructive way by unusual exposure. The data to be presented pro-
vide strong evidence in favour of adaptive modification of the pro-
perties of individual neurons.
V

title: S. Nakajima - Epinephrine Secretion as an Additional Factor in Producing the Amnesic Effect of Cycloheximide.
abstract:
l6}\J My previous ~tudy that cycloheximide produces amnesia by
~~ ~ suppressing adrenocortical function has been criticized by two groups
.\ii of investigators. They found that aminoglutethimide; which inhibits
' \y ~, adrenocortical function but does not interfere with cerebral protein
o-Jv~i,~ synthesis, does not produce amnesia. I now report that aminoglutethi-
~, rnide suppresses adrenomedullary function (secretion of epinephrine),
0~ and that it produces amnesia if epinephrine is supplemented.
~~ \ 22. N. t\llen & J. Barresi - Competence Information and Extrinsic Reward
-'\\y-,\J~\\ Effects on Intrinsic Motivation.
Extrinsic reward has been shown to decrease rather than
increase performance (or liking) of intrinsically motivated activities.
~~~~f~ It has also been found that performance information (positive or
> ~\1'0 ~ negative) changes intrinsic motivation in the direction of the com~
,~,.:'() petence information. In the present study, the interaction of these
two variables was investigated. Both factors were found to affect
intrinsic motivation in a direction consistent with previous
. \.f research. However, the magnitude of the reward effect was larger
~/ when no competence information was provided. This suggests that
~ subjects attend more to competence information than to extrinsic

title: Hypothalamic Lesions Block the Arttigortadal Action of Melatonin.
abstract:
In hamsters an intact pineal gland is necessary for the anti-
gonadal effect of exposure to short photoperiods. The pineal product,
melatonin,has been shown to produce gonadal regression in short
photoperiods and to block gonadal regrowth in long photoperiods; it
is presumed that melatonin is a prime pineal antigonadotrophin.
Lesions of the suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN) of the hypothalamus
eliminate many circadian rhythms including that of pineal melatonin
formation; these same lesions prevent testicular regression in short
photoperiods. I have suggested four possibilities for the mechanism
of this effect; the simplest of these states that SCN destruction
prevents pineal melatonin formation, thereby lowering circulating
melatonin levels and permitting the testes to remain functional. On
this hypothesis, replacement of melatonin in SCN-lesioned hamsters
should exert an antigonadal action. This hypothesis was tested by
regressing the testes of hamsters in short photoperiods and using
melatonin to block regrowth when returned to a long photoperiod.
Melatonin blocked regrowth in intact hamsters but had no effect on
SCN-lesioned hamsters. This hypothesis is therefore ruled out,
leaving three choices: i) The critical factor is not the level of
melatonin but the rhythm of melatonin. ii) The lesions increase
gonadotropin levels independently of the pineal. iii) The lesions
destroy the site of melatonin uptake so that it cannot influence
pituitary function.

title: The Role of the Pituitary-Adrenal Axis in the lJCS Pre-exposure Phenomenon.
abstract:
Experiments tested the hypothesis that the pituitary-adrenal
\lff\V
_x.A
system mediates the decremental effect of prior exposure to an aver-
sive UCS on subsequent acquisition
response, i.e.,
of an excitatory conditioned
the UCS pre-exposure phenomenon. Groups of hypo-
physectomized, adrenal dernedullated, adrenalectornized and sham-operated
rats received either no pre-exposure treatment or pre-exposure to
electric shock, followed by CER conditioning. In no pre-exposure
treatment groups, only adrenalectomized rats showed retarded acquisi-
tion of the CER. Groups receiving pre-exposure to electric shock
generally acquired the CER at a slower rate than their no pre-
exposure counterparts. Interpretation of these findings indicated
~( that the pituitary-adrenal system does not mediate the UCS pre-
exposure phenomenon obtained with CER conditioning.

title: Changes in Cytoarchitecture of the Optic Tectl.iJil after_ Reimplantation and Transplantation in the Adult Gol~fish as seert by Golgi artd Autoradiographic Techniques.
abstract:
Following reimplantation of tectal tissues or reciprocal
transp-lantatian between a tectal tissue and a cerebellar (or forebrain)
tissue in the adult goldfish, stru~tural changes in the optic tectum
are examined by Golgi staining and autoradiographic methods. In cl.11
cases the changes in cytoarchitecture are consistent with the normal
pattern of connection of optic fibers from the retina to the optic
tectum.
M. Ozier - Orthographic and Semantic Access to the Memory Trace.
The episodic memory trace was studied by having subjects
attempt to access the memory trace according to different types of
retrieval codes. The results indicated that the first letter of the
name of a word may function as a retrieval code only if it is inten-
tionally marked as such, whereas the categoric meaning of a word is
marked for retrieval purposes automatically. In addition, it was
found that the names of words are not linked directly to one another
in the lexical dictionary, but the meaning of words have direct
connections. These results have implications for theories of encoding
specificity, the organization of the mental lexicon, and a speech-
production hypothesis of initial-letter cueing effects.

title: Bloom·_ Neonatal Assessment as a Model for Medical/Social Change.
abstract:
Neonatal assessment is a structured procedure in which
defined behaviors are observed and elicited. This process yields
not only an objective description of the newborn's medical and
behavioral status, but also a generalized affect upon the quality
of the observer's subjective judgments. In the former and traditional
way, the assessment procedure is used as a dependent variable. In
the latter way, assessment is viewed as an independent variable or
treatment of the adult (e.g., pediatrician, parent, child-care
specialist). The value of structured behavioral assessment for
teaching, primary medical care and research will be illustrated with
examples from pediatrics, developmental and clinical psychology.

title: The Power of Learning Theories.
abstract:
The nature of a theory of learning may be determined using
theoretical machines called finite automata. There is some finite
automaton which will behave in the same way as any organism. As an
organism learns, however, it changes its behaviour. Therefore, there
will be a way to change a finite automaton so that it also behaves in
this new way: A description of these changes will be equivalent to a
theory of learning. If it can be shown that a particular process is
required to alter a finite automaton so that it learns appropriate
behaviour, a theory of learning must also incorporate this process.
For example, if it can be shown that something more than stimulus-
response associations are required for a "naive" finite automaton
to learn some behaviour, then S-R theories will not be sufficient to
account for this learning.

title: The Abstraction of Spatio-T~mporal ~}~5!~re.
abstract:
A chain of 80 dot patterns was constructed by recursively
applying a distortion rule to the pattern constructed from the pre-
vious application of the rule. When these patterns are presented
visually for 300 msec each in their "natural" order, they appear as
a continuously changing shape. Surprisingly, subjects' ability to
identify patterns from the sequence is no better when the patterns
are presented in their "natural" order than when the sequence of 80
is presented randomly. However, both of these conditions yield much
better performance than does a sequence of 80 entirely random
patterns. These and other results suggest that resemblance rather
than continguity is the first associative principle.

title: Constraints on Learning: A Re-evaluation.
abstract:
Although rats preferentially condition to gustatory cues when
illness is the reinforcer, the argument that this reflects a specific
learning ability selected for by evolution is not especially compell-
ing. Data from experiments on blocking and second-order conditioning
which employed nongustatory CS's will be described which suggest
that the constraint is on the expression of learning, rather than on
the learning i tse If. The relevance of these findings to general
theories of learning will be discussed.

title: Visual Sensitization with a Large, Flashed Annulus.
abstract:
The addition of light to the visual system usually acts to
desensitize detection mechanisms. In this experiment, specific
stimulus conditions are defined that make an exception to this general
rule. The results show that the addition of a large, flashed annulus
leads to greater sensitivity in detecting a central test spot than
when the annulus is not presented. The characteristics of this sensi-
tization effect suggest that different neural mechanisms are involved
than those thought to be mediating Westheimer spatial sensitization
effects.

title: Cognitive and Rhythmic Control of Hemisphere Activation.
abstract:
The two cerebral hemispheres of the human brain are special-
ized for the performance of different cognitive processes. Meta-
control refers to mechanisms that influence which hemisphere is
activated and therefore likely to take control of processing in a
given situation. Two types of metacontrol are distinguished: one
is transient and cognitive, the other is rhythmic and automatic.
Cognitive metacontrol has been demonstrated in split-brain subjects,
but its elicitation in normal subjects has been unclear. Rhythmic
metacontrol has been hypothesized but not clearly demonstrated. We
will present clear-cut evidence for both types of metacontrol pro-
cesses in the normal intact brain.

title: Some Aspects of Communal Pup-Rearing i~the Timber Wolf.
abstract:
Communal pup-rearing is a major co-operative feature of pack
social behavior in the timber wolf (Canis lupus) which has not yet
been studied in detail. It is known that in the pack,adults assist
both mothers and pups in a variety of ways. The present study exam-
ined some aspects of pup-rearing in a pack of 12 captive timber
wolves and found that assistance to mothers was rendered in a highly
selective manner whereas all pups were cared for equally and without
distinction. In addition an analysis of the social relationship
between pairs of pack members suggests that little aggression occurs
during the breeding season between future mothers and their helpers.

title: Epithalamic Reinforcement System: Why is the Habenular Complex?
abstract:
The reinforcing consequences of electrical stimulation at
sites in the habenular complex and adjacent structures were inves-
tigated in 44 rats. Electrode locations in the stria medullaris,
habenular nuclei and fasciculus retroflexus, but not in adjacent
thalamic nuclei, support self-stimulation. Lesion studies suggest
that connections with the medial raphe and efferent fibers in the
stria medullaris generate the rewarding effect of stimulation in
the habenular complex.

title: Infants' Discrimination of the Relative Onset-Time of Two-Component Tones.
abstract:
A great deal of speech perception research has focused upon·
the nature of voice-onset-time differences in stop consonants. Yet,
the nature of the mechanisms responsible for the perception of these
differences is still the subject of much debate. The present study exa-
mines the infant's perception of a cue suggested as a possible psycho-
acoustic basis for the perception of voice-onset-time differences.

title: Spatial and Temporal Tuning Characteristics of Cat Superior Colliculus.
abstract:
We have measured the response of visual cells in the cat's
superior colliculus to moving gratings of sinusoidal luminance profile.
Spatial frequency (cycles/deg), velocity (<legs/sec) and contrast were
varied and presentations were randomly interleaved under computer con-
trol. Some cells preferred a particular temporal frequency while many
responded best at a certain spatial frequency and velocity.

title: Synaptogenesis in the Fly's Visual System.
abstract:
Rules which control the formation or selection of synapses
during development are the last step in a chain of cellular control
mechanisms by which neurons interconnect with great specificity. To
study the nature of these rules we have examined anatomically popula-
tions of synapses in the first optic neuropile, or lamina, of the fly
Musca in the adult and in various immature stages. For each has been
scored the number of synapses and the identity of elements communicating
synaptically. Synapses have been scored from limited series of up to 50
consecutive micrographs prepared from material fixed and processed con-
ventionally for electron microscopy. We have found that synapses form
with great specificity. In the adult, the first-order synapse is an
elongate tetrad with dyadic symmetry. The formation of these synapses
occurs relatively late in development (50% - 80% of pupal life) after
the neural elements are represented in their final positions, but before
the surrounding glial cell processes have ensheathed the elements. Some
characteristic features of synaptic ultrastructure are however acquired
between 80% - 100% of pupal life. These include the platform component
of a conspicuous T-shaped presynaptic density, a subsynaptic cisterna in
each of two (Ll and L2) postsynaptic elements, the full complement of
synaptic vesicles and the presence of nearby glial invaginations (capi-
tate projections).

title: Short-Term Memory in Pigeons.
abstract:
One method of assessing short-term memory in pigeons re-
quires that the characteristics of a stimulus be recalled. Another
methodallows the formation of an intention to respond which may be re-
called in lieu of the stimulus. The present research demonstrates that
pigeons must have prior training with delays if they are required to
recall the stimulus, but remembering is equally high if an intention to
respond could be produced, regardless of prior training.

title: Mixed Excitatory Inhibition Control of Dentate Granule Cells by Hippocampal Cornmissure Activation.
abstract:
Douglas showed that synaptic enhancement of perforant path
inputs to the hippocampus are aborted by high-frequency activation of
the hippocampal cornmissure - a pathway thought to be excitatory but
which appeared in our studies to be inhibitory. Application of Bicuculline
has revealed that the pathway is a mixture of both.

title: Binocular Interactions in Cat Visual Cortex.
abstract:
In cat visual cortex., many cells can be driven through one
eye only. Other cells can be driven equally through the two eyes. One
would think that neurons which receive excitatory input from only one
eye were important for monocular viewing while those receiving equal
input from the two eyes were important for binocular vision. The data
however, suggest otherwise.

title: Parsing Principles for Patterns.
abstract:
In several experiments subjects were asked to partition dot
patterns into their "natural" parts. Although the number of parts per-
ceived in a given pattern varied across subjects, most of their partitions
could be fit into a single nested hierarchy of parts. It is suggested
that a uniform set of parsing principles which generates such hierarchies
may form the basis of pattern recognition processes.

title: Abnormalities in the Vestibulo-ocular Reflex and Optokinetic Nystagmus of Dark-Reared Cats.
abstract:
The effectiveness of the vestibulo-ocular reflex of dark-
reared cats is very much less than that of normal cats. A large moving
visual stimulus is also less effective at producing a compensatory
nystagmus than in normal cats, although some directions are followed
better than others. There is little recovery within two months.

title: Role of Monoamines in Hippocampal Long-Term Potentiation.
abstract:
Noradrenergic pathways have been implicated in the "synaptic
switching" which can be induced in the visual cortex during early post-
natal life. We are investigating the possibility that the monoaminergic
projections to hippocampus play a role in the functional synaptic plasti-
city represented by long-term potentiation of synaptic transmission.

title: Good to the Last Drop?
abstract:
If thirsty rats drink novel, coffee-flavoured water and
then receive LH stimulation, they subsequently prefer coffee over tap
water. The relative magnitude of preferences induced by different dura-
tions of LH stimulation permits discrimination of the changes in the
reinforcing effect which occur during a stimulation train.

title: Temporal Relations as Discriminative Cues: Rate of Key Colour Alternation.
abstract:
Two rates of key colour alternation were discriminative
stimuli for pigeons in a two-component multiple schedule. Differences
between response rates were small, but responding to a control key that
allowed birds to shift freely between the two components showed good
discriminative performance. Generalization tests on the rate dimension
yielded sloping gradients and peak shift.

title: A Time for Decision: Evidence for Different Working Memory Processes in the Pigeon.
abstract:
Pigeons could decide whether to respond to the second stimu-
lus in a trial either on the basis of information contained in the
initial stimulus (Delayed Discrimination), or on the basis of a condi-
tional relationship between the two stimuli (Conditional Matching). The
former procedure resulted in better performance when delays between the
two stimuli were introduced.

title: Cortical Responses to Movement.
abstract:
Visual cells may respond to a wide variety of stimuli and a
satisfactory characterization of the responses of an individual unit
should go beyond a description of its optimum stimulus. It is however
uneconomical merely to list the unit's response to any of myriad possible
stimuli. We have devised a cuboidal structure whose axes.are space,
space, and time to enable us to predict the responses of a given unit to
arbitrary moving stimuli with different speeds, accelerations, and
directions.

title: Explorations of Mental Size Scaling.
abstract:
It has recently been demonstrated that subjects may gradually
transform the size of the representation of a visual object when deciding
if two differently sized stimuli have the same shape. Our experiments
reveal the generality of this finding, and by testing its boundary con-
ditions provide useful information about the nature of mental size scaling.

title: Changing-Disparity and Changing-Size Inputs Feed the Same Motion-in-Depth Stage.
abstract:
A sensation of motion in depth can be produced either by
changing-disparity stimulation or by changing-size stimulation. I intend
to report evidence that a changing-disparity stimulus and a changing-
size stimulus feed the same motion-in-depth mechanism.

title: The Mental Representation of Spelling in Adults.
abstract:
Pilot data are reported which show that recognition memory
for spelling errors is specific for the spelling pattern presented.
Judgments of meaning rather than spelling decreased spelling recognition
accuracy significantly but only slightly in absolute terms. The data
have relevance to our general knowledge about characteristics of the
episodic memory trace.

title: In Praise of Alchemy (or - Topsy Turvey).
abstract:
I previously got into trouble with this term. But I assert
that behavioral alchemy remains the zeitgeist for most experimental
psychology (not just clinical). And its not all bad. However, non-
equilibrium thermodynamics may be a more interesting model, with dynamic
pattern and self-organization as focal points. And even mice do it!

title: The Interaction of Retinal and Tectal Cells of Goldfish in Tissue Culture. The visual system in goldfish involves two populations of neural cells, those in the retina and those in the optic tectum. It is the interaction of these two populations in vitro (tissue culture), that I wish to talk about. -
abstract:
In culture retinal ganglion growth can be seen to enter
pieces of tectal tissue but it is unclear what happens when this occurs
without doing elaborate histological procedures. The difficulty in
tissue culture arises in dissociating tectal cells, and then determining
when a cell is living. Having made some progress in this, I wish to show
some results from early experiments combining retinal tissue and single
tectal cells in vitro.
s.

title: Visually-Guided Locomotion: A Psychophysical Channel Sensitive to Flow Patterns in the Visual Field.
abstract:
When the head moves forward, the stationary outside world
appears to flow radially outwards from the point towards which the head
is moving. Gibson proposed that this flow pattern is a stimulus for
motion perception, and can be used to estimate one's direction of motion
in three dimensions.
We previously reported evidence for psychophysical channels
that were specifically sensitive to changing size. Orthogonality was
the noteworthy property of these channels: they did not respond to
motion or to flicker, even though these stimuli necessarily accompany
changing size (Vision, Res., 18, 415, 1978). Properties of single units
in cat parastriate cortex could provide a physiological basis for these
channels (Regan &Cynader, ARVO,1978). Here we report that visual
sensitivity to changes in the size of a 0.5° test square was depressed
following 10 minutes inspection of a radially-flowing pattern of short
annular line segments. This depression only occurred, however, when the
square was located very close (about 0.3) to where the focus of the
adapting flow pattern had been located. Hence, we propose that visual
judgement of one's direction of forward motion may be mediated by
changing-size channels. Since changing-size channels respond only to
objects narrower than about 1.5° (Beverley &Regan, Vision Res., in
press), our proposal might go some way to explaining the precision of
directional judgements of motion when driving an automobile or landing
an aircraft.

title: Morphology of Tectal Reimplants in Goldfish.
abstract:
Physiological recordings from excised and reimplanted portions
of the optic tectum of goldfish have _been used to study factors involved
in the patterning of connections in the CNS of vertebrates. Unfortunately,
the recording techniques employed have not permitted distinction between
pre- and postsynaptic evoked potentials; the integrity of the reimplanted
tissue may be questioned. I will present preliminary results of anatomi-
cal studies of such reimplanted pieces of tissue.

title: Pre-ejaculatory Ultrasonic Vocalizations of Male Rats.
abstract:
Male rats emit 22 kHz pre-ejaculatory vocalizations when
approaching females after they had achieved four or more ejaculations.
The onset of these vocalizations was associated with increases in mounts
without intromissions and decreases in intromission rate.· On those
ejaculatory series with 22 kHz pre-ejaculatory vocalizations, the females
showed a lower lordosis intensity and a higher number of agonistic re-
sponses than on series with no vocalizations. It is suggested that the
pre-ejaculatory vocalizations may serve to inhibit female agonistic behavior
and stimulate sexual responsiveness.

title: Does Saccadic Suppression Have an Attent\onal Component?
abstract:
The allocation of attention to a particular task will reduce the
likelihood that nonattended inputs will be remembered or have access to any
but their most habitual associations. While many processes are automatic in the·
sense that they do not require attention, others will suffer if attention
is not available. It has been demonstrated that manual aimed movements
and manual pursuit tracking usually require attention. The similarity of
saccadic and pursuit eye movements to these manual movements suggests that
they too might require attention. Subjective experience, on the other hand,
suggests that eye movement control is usually reflexive or unconscious.
It is proposed that whenever saccades must conform to spatial and temporal
restrictions (as in most experiments where E tells S where and when to
look, and in many skills, e.g. reading) theTr initiation and execution
may not be automatic. This proposal predicts that the perception of stimuli
occurring during a saccade will be suppressed (in relation to a no movement
control) because attention will not be available to deal with them. It is
interesting to note that suppression of visual perception prior to and during
saccades has been well documented, but almost without exception this 11saccadic
suppression" phenomenon has been interpreted as a specifically visual effect.
No studies that I am aware of have tested for the suppression of nonvisual
stimuli, which if obtained would indicate an attentional contribution to
saccadic suppression. The design and results of just such an experiment
will be described.

title: Groaning ~lountains and Grunting Storms: TI1e Challenge of Outdoor Research.
abstract:
The laboratory demonstration of new senses in animals is most
exciting when it is supported by field demonstrations that these new senses
have an adaptive role in the life of the animal. My recent discovery that
homing pigeons can detect and discriminate extremely low tones (infrasounds)
raises some exciting questions about the function of this sensitivity.
The difficulties associated with field studies of infrasound are representative
of the challenges of working with any new sensory process. Many of these
problems are due to the very properties of infrasound which make it a
potentially useful environmental factor to many animals.

title: The Acquisition 'of Tacit and Explicit Knowledge of Contingent Probabilistic Structure.
abstract:
In a dual processing paradigm subjects are presented with cues
(2 digit numbers) shortly followed by one of four letters, where the
individual letter probabilities depend on the particular cue. The subject's
major task is to respond as rapidly as possible to each letter in a two
choice decision procedure. His minor task is to attend to the relationship
between the numbers and letters and at the end of each block to estimate
their probabilistic structure. Evidence from an initial experiment with
this paradigm suggests that the acquisition of tacit knowledge (reaction
time) and explicit knowledge (verbal awareness) of probabilistic structure
may involve partially independent processes.

title: Development of Odour Preferences in Rat Pups.
abstract:
Two experiments examined the proposition that rat pups' preference
for maternal odour reflects simple familiarity with the dam's scent. It
was found that odour preexposure could affect pups' odour preferences;
however, the data also indicate that a simple familiarity model does not
provide a complete account of maternal odour preferences.

authors_raw: G. V. Goddard, T.V.P. Bliss, H. A. Robertson and R. S. Sutherland
title: Evidence to Support Crow's Reinforcement Hypothesis that Biologically Significant Behavior is Associated with Activity of the Locus Coeruleus and that the Consequent Release of Noradrenaline throughout the Forebrain has the Effect of Strengthening the Efficacy of Recently Active Synapses.
abstract:
Rats were depleted of hippocampal nor adrenaline by systemic
1nJection of reserpine or by 6-0H-dopamine destruction of the dorsal nor-
adrenergic bundle. Verification was by radioenzymatic liquid scintillation
count. Brief high frequency electrical stimulation of perforant path fibers
was used to cause short-term and long-term potentiation of the synapses onto
granule cells as measured by the field potential response to test shocks in the
perforant path. Short term potentiation of the field excitatory post-synaptic
potential (EPSP) was unaffected by noradrenaline depletion whereas long-term
potentiation of the EPSP was reduced by more than 50%.

title: Mental Size Scaling.
abstract:
Previous work by Burdeson and Larsen (1975), has demonstrated a
phenomenon in which reaction time to determine if a pair of figures has the
same shape is a linear function of the size ratio beti<:een the two figures.
The functions for same and different data are parallel. Research by Klein
{1979), also showed linear functions for "same" data but parallel functions
were not reported for "different" data. Klein's data suggested that
"complexity" of the stimuli could be a factor affecting the data. The first of
two experiments run tested this complexity hypotheses. The second experiment
was designed with the intention of resolving the discrepancies in the Klein
and Bundeson "different" data. Results from the first experiment lend some
support to Klein's complexity hypothesis and data from the second experiment
indicate the differences in the Klein and Burdeson studies for the different
data was due to the type of "different" patt~rns used.

title: Hemispheric Asymmetry in Part-Whole Judgements.
abstract:
The work presented here is an attempt to obtain a test sensitive to
right hemisphere processes. Experiments with stick figures, extracted from the
16 segments obtained from a 3 x 3 matrix, appear promising. A three-segment
part, either contained within a foveally-presented six-segment figure or not,
was briefly exposed in the left or right visual field. Decisions (part
included in the figure or not) were made quicker for "easy" parts (e.g.
triangles) in the right visual field, and for "difficult" parts (unconnected
and scattered) in the left. These data suggest?- method for evaluating
perceptual and memory representations in the two hemispheres.
C. N. Bennett. Some Behavioral Correlates of Mate-Choice in the Coyote
(Canis latrans).
D. J. Raymond. Abnormal Grating Adaptation in Multiple Sclerosis.
Reduced contrast sensitivity is often found in individuals with
multiple sclerosis (MS). I investigated this abnormality of contrast vision
by measuring changes in contrast sensitivity after adaptation to a high
contrast sine wave grating pattern in MS patients and normal control subjects.
I found that after viewing a 70 90 contrast adapting pattern for 1 minute, 15 out
of 17 MS patients showed contrast sensitivity changes, or adaptation effects,
smaller than 2. 0 standard deviations below the mean adaptation effect for the
normal group with at least one eye. In normals, the size of the adaptation
effect increases both as the contrast of the adapting pattern is increased and
as the duration of exposure to the adapting pattern is increased. Although
these relationships also hold for the MS patients studied, the shapes of both
the contrast and duration functions are different for the MS group.
When the contrast of the adapting pattern is low but still visible
to the subject (less than 1.0 l.u. greater than the unadapted log contrast
threshold), an adaptation effect can always be observed in normal subjects.
However, in some MS patients adapting patterns of m1,lchhigher contrasts
(up to 1. 0 1. u greater than the log unadapted contrast threshold) are
insufficient to induce adaptation effects. Similarly, 20 to 60 seconds of
exposure to a 70% contn:.st adapting pattern are enough to produce near-maximum
adaptation effects in normal subjects. MS patients require exposures of 180
to 300 seconds before adaptation effects similar in size to those found in
normals can be observed.
These differences between MS patients and normal subjects cannot
be explained by reduced contrast sensitivity and indicate that the neural,
probably cortical, mechanisms mediating visual contrast adaptation may be
considerably altered by MS.
E. I. Meinertzhagen and A. Frtlhlich. Synaptogenesis in the Fly's Visual System.
The ultrastructure and composition of fly photoreceptor synapse
populations has been examined by serial electron microscopy of developing
animals. Adult synapses are tetrads with four postsynaptic elements. At
younger stages dyad and triad synapses (with nrn and three postsynaptic
elements, respectively) coexist alongside tetrads, those of older animals
having an average more postsynaptic processes per synapse. It is suggested
that individuc1.l synapses assemble piecemeal, element by element.
F. D. Quine. 8 Octaves Below Human Hearing: Pigeons Detect Infrasound.
Homing pigeons can detect sow1ds at frequencies as low as 0.05 Hz.
1nis poster will summarize the procedures used in measuring the infrasound
sensitivity of homing pigeons. Frequency discrimination thresholds at these
frequencies will be discussed in the contextofwhat adaptive value these
unusual sensory abilities may have to the pigeon. Possible uses include
navigation, weather prediction and flight control. A comparison will be made
with human low frequency hearing thresholds.

title: Dropped Ducks.
abstract:
\Vould your newborn off spring benefit from a fal 1 off a ten-storey
building? An equivalent drop seems to increase the chance that a newly
hatched captive wood duckling (Aix sponsa) will survive. In a pilot study
last May we found that the activity of wood ducklings dropped from a height
of at least a metre also differed from those gently placed into their brooder.
This year we plan to investigate this phenomenon further, under controlled
conditions.

authors_raw: C. Shaw, J. Mates and B. Rusak
title: The Psychology of Brain Slices.
abstract:
Several researchers in the Dalhousie Psyci1ology Department have
become interested in using brain slices in their work. B. Rusak plans to
record spontaneous neural activity in the hamster suprachiasmatic nuclei and
to study the effects of pharmacological manipulations in relation to the
circadian phase during which recordings are made. C. Shaw plans to examine
the neural circuitry and pharmacology of visual cortex in cats. J. Mates
wants to test the notion that the inhibitory feedback network containing
hippocampal pyramidal cells acts laterally between adjacent lamellae of the
hippocampus.

title: Play in Hospitalized and Non-Hospitalized Children.
abstract:
Are hospitalized children under stress? Does the existence of a
playschool in the hospital reduce this stress? In this initial study, the
play behaviour of hospitalized children was compared with that of children in
a daycare center. Differences in levels of play, social behaviour and
"emotional" displays wi 11 be discussed.
POSTERPRESENTATIONS
A. D. Moore. Scanning Electron Microscope Study of the Eye Disc in the Larval Fly.
Larval diptera show no external indication of their adult features.
However, early in development, cells within the body are sequestered away to
form imaginal discs, which transform into such adult structures as genitalia,
legs, wings, eyes and antennae. A scanning electron microscope study was
conducted on one of these discs, the eye disc, in the 3rd instar larva of
the housefly, Musca domestica.

title: Aspects of Dominance Behavior in Groups of Sibling Coyotes.
abstract:
Two unrelated groups of sexually mature sibling coyotes with three
males and two fema]es each were observed when each group was a) housed
together, b) divided by sex into four groups and c) reunited after three
months. T\vo dominance hierarchies (male and female) in each group were
determi1:ed by social display. TI1e effect of opposite-sex animals on
1) prosocial and 2) agonistic interactions, 3) scent-marking, 4) some aspects
of howling and S) spatial containment of lo1v-ranking animals was studied.
Same-sex aggression was higher in females than in males. The presence of
males correlated with a striking increase in levels of aggression between
females. TI1e rate of scent-marking and howling was greater for the dominant
females than for any other individual of either group. TI1e only incident
of spatial containment occurred among females.

title: From Percept to Concept: Parsing Prj..nciples for Perceptual Categories.
abstract:
When subjects are asked to partition random 9-dot patterns into
their "natural" parts, most of the subjects' partitions for a particular
pattern can be fit into a single nested hierarchy of parts. Such hierarchies
often differ even among quite "similar" patterns. How then do \ve form abstract
concepts to represent a category of such related patterns? Perhaps, with
experience, related patterns assimilate to a single nested hierarchy of parts
which comes to define the concept. Evidence will be presented which supports
this hypothesis.

authors_raw: W. J. Jacobs, J. Hill-Flewelling, and V. LoLordo
title: More Quantitative Properties of Anxiety.
abstract:
Three expei'iments investigated the effects of a CER procedure
(Estes and Skinner, 1941) upon appetitively-motivated wheel rwming. Rats
received the onset of a tone as a conditional stimulus and the presentation
of a brief electric shock as the unconditional stimulus. Experiment 1 found
<0 that, unlike the 1,·ell-documented suppression of an appetitively-motiv2ted
~ lever-press response, the current preparation produced acceleration of
appetitively-motivated wheel running in the presence of the conditioned
stimulus. Facilitation was not observed in the Random CS or No CS control
conditions. Experiment 2 demonstrated that conditioned facilitation is a
function of the intensity of the UCS: the more intense the UCS, the greater
the facilitation. In Experiment 3, each rat was trained both to run in a wheel
and to press a lever for food reinforcement. It was found that the rats
increased their rate of wheel running, but suppressed their rate of lever
pressing in the presence of the conditional stimulus. The results are discussed
in the context of Dickinson and Pearce's recent (1977) model of conditioned
suppression.

title: The Malheur Swallows: A Simple Field Experiment Relatedtoflight ~-lechanisms.
abstract:
Lifting the wings of captive Cliff Swallows, Petrochelidon
pyrrhonota, we triggered a fluttering of the body and tail. Onset and
termi11ation of the flutter occurred at fixed wing angles. Autonomous action
of mechanisms underlying the flutter may drive the fluttering of birds
voluntarily holding their wings up and back from their bodies.

title: The Response-Reinforcer Relation: A Pigeon's Eye View.
abstract:
Each presentation of a red response key ended with a food
presentation either following t1,o responses separated by at least 10 sec
(a DRL schedule) or folJowing a 10sec response-free period (a DROschedule).
A white side key was shown next and ended with response-dependent food
folloHing one schedule and a timeout following the other. Three pigeons
responded differentially following the DRL and DROschedules, but not simply
according to the schedule duration, numbers of responses or even the time
between a response and its consequence.

title: Func;tio~w.l Aspects an<l Neural Control of Daily Cycles of Male Sexual Performance.
abstract:
The circadian and neural mechanisms important for the functional
organization of reproductive behavior in the male golden hamster were examined.
It was found that males shoh' a diurnal rhythm of sexual behavior; latencies to
copulate and ejaculate were shorter in the dark phase, a time that coincides
with the female period of sexual receptivity. The integrity of the supra-
chiastmatic nuclei of the anterior hypothalamus appears necessary for this
rhythm. SCN lesions eliminated the rhyt~n in performance without interferring
with the ability of the w1imal to copulate. In contrast, the medial preoptic
area (MPOA)is essential for normal copulatory performance; in those animals
that continued to copulate, after MPOAlesions the circadian modulation of
sexual behavior remained intact. The regulation of sexual behavior by these
structures cons ti tut.es a useful model for the interaction of circadian and other
neural systems in the control of behavioral expression.
The significance of this rhythm is being tested by examrnrng the
ability of arrhythmic SD-lesioned males or males in different circadian
phases to copulate in a mating situation in which they must compete for
access to a single receptive female. If the rhythm of copulatory readiness has
adaptive value, then arrhythmic males or males tested in their light phase
may be at a disadvantage relative to males tested in their dark phase in terms
of reproductive success and fitness.

title: Selective Associations.
abstract:
TI1ere have been a number of demonstrations in recent years that
not all stimuli are associable with all reinforcers. One such selective
association is evidenced when a compound auditory/visual (A-V) stimulus is
paircci with an appetitive reinforcer. After the compound A-V stimulus has
acquired a high level of conditioned responding, the elements comprising
this compound are tested for stimulus control. The visual element appears to
overshadow or exert the dominant control over responding. Single stimulus
acquisition studies have demonstrated that the auditory element appears
completely 1mable to be associated with the same appetitive reinforcer employed
in the compound acquisition stL:dy. The present: experiment, however, suggests
that the failure to see a conditioned response is not a failure of association,
but is instead a failure of this association to be manifest in performance.

authors_raw: D. Regan and K. I. Beverley
title: Visual Responses to Changing Size and to Sideways ~lotion for Different Di rectio11s of Motion in f!~rth: Linec:.rization of Visual Respo1'.:;es.
abstract:
This paper is concerned with how the visual system computes motion
in depth from changes 111the size of the retinal image.
The 2-Hz oscillations of our stimulus squr..re 's edges were
equivalent to the square's oscillating along one of 11 trajectories in depth.
All 11 traj cctories had the same velocity component (Vz) along a line through
the eye (i.e. in the z-direction), but the 11 trajectories had different
velocities co1!1ponents (Vx) in the x-direction perpendicular to the z-direc:tion.
In separate experiments subjects adapted to each trajectory and we meas'-.ired
threshold elevations for two 2-Hz test oscillations, one equivalent to pure
z-dircction motion and the other equivalent to pure x-dircction motion. To a
first approximation threshold elevations for the z-clirection test were the same
for all trajectories, with the greatest departure from constancy (:::N)
\vhen um edges of the adapting stimulus were stationary (i.e. equi valcnt to
trajectories that just grazed the eye). Adding an 8-Hz 11jitter 11 osc.illat.ion
to the 2-Hz adapting stimuli "linearised" the visual response so that
threshold elevations were rendered accurately constant (:5%) for all trajectories
tested.
We propose that the changing-size channels precisely compute the
algebraic difference between the velocities of opposite edges of the target,
thus extracting velocity in the z-direction (Vz) independently of the target's
trajectory, so that (with added "jitter") this computation is accurately
independent of the x-direction cornpcnent of motion at least up to Vx=8V2 •
We have no evidence for a complementary channel that computes Vx independently
of Vz over any comparable range of V :V ratios.
X Z

title: Motor Traps in Ontogeny.
abstract:
A characteristic of early behavior in mammals is the frequency
of abrupt transitions between fw1ctional ly di verse activities. Recer.t
observations in rodents, canids, and children indicate that many of these
transitions can be accounted for by similarities of movement used in
functionally different contexts. These observations reduce .internreted
uncertainty of temporally sequenced actions, and provide independent evidence
for a developmental hierarchy in integrated performance. They also bear
upon ho\\' we think about "pieces" of behavior as distinct from their relations
in time.

authors_raw: G. J. Hollway and r,1. Ozier
title: The Encoding of Frequency of Occurrence and the E}?J.~dic i'-lemory Tr.:i_cc.
Neural and Hormonal Regulation of Seasonal Reproductive Cycles_.
abstract:
Golden hamsters (and most other animals) undergo seasonal cycles of
reproductive activity and quiescence. For hamsters, the external trigger for
this cycle is exposure to the sho:ctening daylengths of autumn; the internal
neuroe;iclocrinc transducer of this signal appears to be the pineal gland.
Pineal removal locks the hamster into the reproductive phase regardless of
day length. Destruct:i on of the suprachiasrnatic nuclei (SC:N) of the hypothalamus
