occluded. However, we have reported here earlier that the recovery
promoted by this procedure (reverse occlusion) is not maintained
afterwards. We now report that it is possible to achieve total
behavioural recovery by instituting part-time occlusion where the
nondeprived eye is occluded for only part of each day followed by a daily
period of at least two hours during which both eyes are open. Thus while
only limited permanent recovery is achieved in situations when either of
these conditions are imposed full-time, a combination of both can lead to
complete behavioural recovery for certain conditons of deprivation.

title: Psychophysio~1ogical Research in Schizophrenia
abstract:
A brief overview of the rationale and objectives of psychophysiologlcal
research in the schizophrenias will be presented. An example of basic
psychophysiological research applied to psychopathology will be
discussed. Recently completed work on event-related brain potentials
relevant to the dichotic listening procedure together with in-progress
tachistoscopic data will be presented. These and other data will be used
to suggest that so-called "higher order cognitive deficits" seen in
schizophenia are attributable to "lower order" abnormalities in sensory
and perceptual processes.

title: Differing Emotional Responses~ the Two Cerebral Hemispheres: The Effects of Scale and Gender
abstract:
Previous authors (see, e.g., Natale, Gur & Gur, 1983) report that the two
cerebral hemispheres differ in their emotional responses. The general
finding is that stimuli projected to the right field-left hemisphere are
rated more positively than those projected to the left-field-right
hemisphere. Most studies have employed left-to-right positively
ascending scales. The present series of experiments was carried out to
evaluate the effects of scale on judgements of emotion. Sad, happy and
neutral faces were presented unilaterally and centrally and right-handed
subjects rated the degree of happy or sad emotion on one of four types of
scales. Our results qualify previous findings in this area. They
suggest that careful attention must be paid to the effects of spatial
compatibility in laterality research.

title: Spatial properties of direction-selective neurons in striate cortex of the cat
abstract:
Bar-shaped stimuli flashed sequentially at adjacent points in the
receptive fields of direction-selective striate cortex neurons produced
direction-selective behavior comparable to that observed with smoothly
moving stimuli. Independent variation of the positions of the 2 flashes
allowed determination of the optimal displacement, Dop t' for direction-
selectivity from sequential flashes. Measured values of D0 P-t typically
ranged from 0.3 to Q.8 deg for receptive field sizes of ca 4-7 deg.
The spatial separation of 2 simultaneously presented bars which produces
the largest lateral inhibition was also determined for each direction-
selective neuron. Twice this value provided the spatial subunit ,
wavelength, A , for a given neuron. Measurements of A were obtained
from line weighting functions, in the case of Simple cells, or from
double-line interaction functions, in the case of Complex cells 1 .
Measured values of A were generally ca 1-5 deg.
For the majority of neurons studied, the ratio of optimal displacement
for direction-selectivity (D0 t) to subunit wavelength (A ) fell in the
range of o.10-0.3 5. These 1i-esul ts imply a systematic quan ti tati ve
relationship between the spatial subunit wavelength and mechanisms for
direction-selectivity.
Supported by Centennial Fellowship (Canadian MRC) to CLB and a Canadian
~ MRC (PG-29) to MC,

title: Functional anatomy of gastropod neurons
abstract:
While gastropods have been used extensively for the last two decades as
preparations for studying the physiological bases of behaviour,
relatively few techniques are available for examining the details of
neurooal morphology in this class of animals. Over the last year I have
been applying two techniques to the gastropod nervous system which reveal
much new information about the functional anatomy of single identifiable
neurons.

title: The "Milk Run": ! New Apparatus for Studying Spatial Memory in Pigeons
abstract:
Pigeons, unlike rodents, do not display good spatial memory in
traditional radial-arm mazes without special and extremely lengthy
training. Thus, they seem able, but "unprepared" to use spatial memory
in radial mazes, perhaps because labyrinthine structures are constraining
for pigeons who, in the wild, generally feed in open fields. In support
of this, pigeons displayed spatial memory without extensive pretraining
in an open-room "flight maze" (reported at the 1984 In House). However,
since some birds proved reluctant to make the flight response, we have
recently developed an open-field, ground feeding version of the "maze"
that involves a number of "feeding stations" (milk cartons baited with
peas) placed on the floor. Our preliminary results suggest that this
apparatus overcomes the response problems of the flight maze, and that
pigeons readily display good spatial memory to efficiently locate the
food.

title: Further Explorations of Changes with Age in the Integration of Shape
abstract:
Experiments with observers aged 6-24 years examined changes with age in
the sequential integration of shape in a free-viewing task. The stimuli
were line-drawings of "r;l.bbons" which consisted of a series of panels
connected randomly at 900 angles. The task was to indicate whether the
end-panels of each ribbon displayed the same surface or different
surfaces to the viewer. Age-related improvements were found in the speed
and accuracy of this task (Experiment 1). Moreover, these improvements
with age were found even when the distance over which observer's eyes
travelled was held constant (Experiment 2) and when the need for any
overt eye movements was eliminated (Experiment 3), supporting the view
that the ability to construct cognitive "schemas" of novel objects
improves with age. A final experiment explored the effects of
instructing observers to use special strategies to solve the task.

title: Forward Masking Studies of Cat Auditory Cortex Cells
abstract:
Single neurons in the cat's auditory cortex encode stimulus intensities
over limited ranges. The "dynamic range" undergoes a shift towards
higher sound pressure levels if the stimulus of interest is presented in
a background of continuous white noise. The magnitude of the dynamic
range shift is linearly related to the intensity of the noise, and is
comparable in cells that are excited or inhibited by noise bursts. To
examine the sequence of neural events underlying this dynamic range
shift, cortical cells were studied parametrically with preferred-
frequency tones delivered at various delays after the onset and offset of
a noise mask. These experiments shed light on the mechanisms used by the
central nervous system to dafict signals in noise.

title: Trace Acquisition with and without Awareness
abstract:
One hundred and sixty-three subjects were tested individually on the Hebb
digits task in which they listened to 24 lists of 9 digits read at a rate
of about 1 per sec and following each list, attempted to recall the
digits in their correct order; however, they were not informed that the
series on Trial 3 was repeated on every subsequent third trial- On the
basis of a post-experimental questionnaire, subjects were categorized as
Unaware (n = 21), Late Aware (n = 29), and Early Aware (n = 29) with
respect to the reoccurrence of the repeating string. Similar rates of
learning of the critical series were found for the three groups,
supporting the contention that repetition of a set of digits casn
directly set up a structural or long-term memory trace-

title: Modulation of Palatability by Trigeminal Tactile Sensation
abstract:
The trigeminal (5th cranial nerve) system carries somatosensation from
the face and mouth. Trigeminal sensation is crucial to normal feeding.
This experiment examined in rats how trigeminal tactile input modulates
the perceived palatability of tastes, as measured by species-typical
actions that are sensitive to palatability evaluations. The results
suggested that low-level trigeminal input amplifies selectively positive
palatability while having little or no effect on the aversive limb of
palatability evaluations. Trigeminal deafferentation thus unbalances the
palatability of tastes.

authors_raw: K.M. Wassenberg, S.E. Bryson & J.F. Werker
title: Consonant Substitution Errors in Reading-Disabled and Normal Children: Phonetic, Visual and FrequencyEffects
abstract:
Consonant misreadings were examined in two groups of severely reading-
disabled children, and both age- and reading-watched, normal controls.
Stimuli were single-syllable nonsense words with consonants in word-
initial and/or -final position.
Substitution errors were classified according to: i) word-position; ii)
phonetic simularity; ii) visual similarity. Comparisons between groups
showed effects of position and phonetic similarity for the normal
children. The reading-disabled groups showed no effect of position, a
strong visual effect and differing patterns of phonetic error types.
In addition, in both reading-disabled groups, unlike the normal children,
/d/ was the most frequent error response. These results are discussed in
terms of the articulatory hypothesis (Werker & Bryson, 1984).

title: The Effect of a Sandy Substrate on Intra-Specific Agonistic Behavior in the Syrian Golden Hamster (Mesocrioetus auratus).
abstract:
The agonistic behavior of male' Syrian golden hamsters was recorded in
environments with sandy and barren substrates. The effect of the sandy
substrate was to decrease the amount of agonistic behavior shown by the
hamsters in comparison to levels observed when the substrate was barren.
This effect was observed across varying motivational states, prior
housing conditions of the animals, lighting conditions of the test arena,
light dark cycles and social groupings during the test. The results are
discussed in terms of traditional conceptualizations of the hamster's
social tendencies in the wild.

title: Dissecting the Components of the Maternal Odour of Mice
abstract:
In a follow-up to my last in-house presentation, we have conducted
another five experiments on the attraction of 16-20 day old mice to
"maternal odours" and I shall discuss the results of these with reference
to last year's results. Using feces from mice as stimuli in a series of
forced choice tests, we have examined the effects of diet (Purina vs•
Halifax Aquarium House), species (rat vs. mouse), sex (male vs. female),
lactation (lactating vs. nonlactating females) and relatedness (own vs.
another mother) on odor preferences. Each of these factors appears to
contribute to the attractiveness of the maternal odour, with diet playing
an especially important role. These results indicate that the "maternal
odour" is a complex stimulus which includes many components and that pups
may attend to some of these components more than others. Cheryl Reed-
Elder will give an interpretation of these results.

title: Is the Concept of a Maternal Pheromone useful to explain the Attractiveness of Maternal Odours? - ,,.,:
abstract:
Studies of odour preferences were examined in an effort to·answer some
questions regarding the interpretation of such preferences as evidence
,._ ;·-, for a "maternal pheromone". Some odorous substances, in particular,
feces and ventral scent gland sebum, both of which are copiously produced
by lactation females, are preferentially approached by preweanling pups.
The attractiveness of an odour to pups may, in part, be a function of the
odour's saliency, its familiarity, or its similarity to other, already
familiar odours. Unfamiliar odours can be made attractive by exposure,
and familiarity with an odour seems to be both necessary and sufficient
to increase the attractiveness of that odour, whether it is produced by a
conspecific or not. Simple exposure has been shown to be effective in
establishing an odour as attractive but this attraction may be increased
if conditioning contingencies are also present. Since the attractiveness
of maternal odours can be explained simply by assessing the role of
familiarity, the concept of a "maternal pheromone" is not necessary and
is not parsimonious with the data. Furthermore, it is not successful in
explaining the modifiability of odour preferences through experience.
6° R. Mistlberger & B. Rusak
Effects of Paraventricular Nuclei Lesions on Entrainment of Circadian
Activity Rhythms to Restricted Feeding in the Rat
Periodic food availability cycles (2h food - 22h fast) can entrain a
variety of circadian rhythms in rats. The phase angle of entrainment is
such that these rhythms anticipate the daily mealtime; for example, the
rat becomes active 2-3h before mealtime. The neural mechanisms mediating
anticipatory, food entrainable rhythms are functionally and antomically
distinct from those controlling free running, light entrainable rhythms
since suprachiasmatic nuclei lesions abolish or severely disrupt free
running but not food anticipatory rhythms, Previous attempts to localize
critical components of the food entrainment mechanism indicate that the
pineal and adrenal glands, the hypophysis and the ventromedial
hypothalamus are non-essential. The present study investigates whether
the paraventricular nuclei (PVN) might be involved, in view of their
purported role in the regulation of food intake and the preminence of
food intake over light in the phase control of certain PVN receptor
rhythms. Circadian locomotor rhythms were monitored in 9 rats on
restricted food schedules (2h diurnal mealtime) for 2 weeks prior to and
3 weeks after bilateral PVN ablation. Six rats displayed signs of
obesity typically associated with PVN lesions. Anticipatory activity was
markedly attenuated in 3 of these rats and largely unaffected in the
remaining 6. The animals will be tested again after several months to
assess the stability of this effect.

title: Synaptic Promiscuity and Neuronal Fidelity as the Forces of Destiny.
abstract:
Comparative ethologists have long suggested that animal behaviour has
evolved progressively as speciation has proceeded. Changes in behaviour
may have been just as important in evolution as the more obvious
alterations in body plan, for instance as mechanisms ensuring
reproductive isolation. By contrast, neuroscientists have had little
that is useful to offer about the evolution of the brain, which after all
supposedly controls behaviour. They offer unlikely tales that changes in
neuron size and shape, in brain size or in the direction taken by
neuronal tracts, are the main correlates of brain evolution. A more
appealing idea is that new neuronal classes arise at each twist and turn
of evolutionary advance, accounting for each new behaviour, but this
appears confounded by the facts. In cases where we can identify neurons
as individuals, they appear to have persisted as recognizable entitles
for perhaps )100 million years (admittedly few cases so far, mostly in
invertebrates). How might this apparent bottleneck to the evolution of
brain function be overcome? If "new" neurons cannot readily emerge in
the CNS, we speculate instead that rearrangement of synaptic connections
amongst existing nerve cells must take precedence, as a fundamental mode
of evolutionary progression. Evidence supporting this new if obvious
hypothesis will be presented, drawn from a comparative EN study of a
particular class of sensory synapse that is made between identifiable
neurons, and that must have existed since the Carboniferous (~250MYr BP).
We have discovered that the composition of the post-synaptic set of
elements at the synaptic has altered, during the monophyletic
evolutionary divergence into the many modern families. Alterations in
some other types of synapse have also been noted.

title: Dopamine Receptors Involved in Food Reinforcement \.'
abstract:
Rats trained to press a bar for food pellets under CRF, VI-1 min, or VI-3
min schedule were injected with SCH 23390 (dopamine Dl receptor blocking
agent), sulpiride (D2 blocker), or physiological saline. D1 blocking
suppressed responding, but D2 blocking did not. Responding under the VI
schedules was more susceptible to Dl blocking than the responding under
CRF.

title: Attention and Feature Integration
abstract:
Treisman's Feature Integration theory of attention predicts that focal
attention is needed to correctly combine the different features which
make up a visual pattern or object. The present study was intended to
determine whether orienting of attention to (or away from) a given
spatial location would affect the tendency for subjects to report
illusory conjunctions at that location. Subjects searched for the letter
R with two sets of distractors, PB and PQ. Results showed that
performance was better when the location cue was valid than when it was
invalid, and importantly, that this effect was no greater for the PQ
distractor set than for the PB distractor set. The data are inconsistent
with the notion that allocating attention spatially serves a role in
integrating the features of an object correctly.

title: The Cell Lineage of the Ascidian Neural Plate
abstract:
Ascidians, or sea -squists, are unprepossessing sessile, marine animals
which produce embryos demonstrating almost perfect mosaic development.
The embryo produces a motile larva with chordate-like characteristics
including a dorsal tubular nerve cord which forms from a neural plate and
on the basis of which the group is thought to resemble the primitive
chordate ancestor. We have examined the lineage of the cells of this
neural plate and have now accounted for all the cell divisions (about 12)
which generate all the cells (about 350) of the nervous system. We are
thus how ready to examine the effects of cell deletions and thus test the
ability of the neural plate to regulate both the cell number and cell
type of its progeny.

title: Category Learning in Pigeons
abstract:
Four groups of pigeons were trained on a go/no go task that required
birds to discriminate slides of naturalistic stimuli. Pairs of slides
had identical backgrounds, but within each pair the slides were
distinguised by the presence or absence of a human figure. The group of
birds trained to respond to human present slides acquired the
discrimination. However, a second group, one that was trained to respond
to human absent, failed to acquire the discrimination. Two additional
groups that were given "pseudo-concept" training (i~., both the positive
and the negative slide sets contained human-present and human-absent
slides) also failed to acquire the discrimination.

title: Stimulating the Subjective Lexicon
abstract:
Galton's studies of associative processes suggested a complex mental
machinery that seemed unavailable to conscious introspection. That
machinery which Ga.lton likened to the "pipes and flues and bell wires" in
one's basement might today be called the subjective lexicon (~ Miller)
or semantic memory (Quillian, Tulving). The most popular paradigm for
exploring properties of the subjective lexicon has been to examine the
effects of prior or simulataneous stimulation of some region of the
lexicon upon task performance involving the same or different region.
The evolution and current status of this paradigm will be described and
exemplified with some recent data from our lab.
i 4 .---J.F entres s -
Modulations Muddle Monolithic Modules in Mamma~lian Movement
As in figurative tongue twisters, movements of the tongue and other
appendages in rats show incomplete encapsulation (modularity). Kent
Berridge and I have recently been able to show this by examining the
detailed form of facial and forelimb actions in trigeminally deafferented
animals under various motivational and sequential contexts. The point of
the exercise is not merely that strict dichotomies between "reflex" and
"centrally patterned" actions are both a logical and biological misnomer,
but that even relatively simple behavioural patterns exhibit dynamic and
relational control preperties that deserve further investigation.
Contextual factors are necessary complements to more traditional
(fragmented) conceptualizations of control-

title: The Effect of Viewing Pornographic, Violent and Pornographic Violent Film on Viewer'sSubsequent Administration of Reward and Punishment
abstract:
Twenty-seven undergraduate males participated in this study designed to
examine the effect of pornographic and pornographic violent film on
viewer's tendency to reward and punish a female. Following exposure to
either a pornographic, pornographic violent, neutral or violent 10 minute
videotape segment, subjects had the opportunity to reward and punish a
mythical female learner in a bogus teaching task. The effect of the film
was measured as the ratio of the total duration of rewards to the total
duration of punishment. Sequential analysis ( o( =-05) indicated
significant differences in the reward to punishment ratios for all film
s
conditions. The highest ratio was found for the pornographic film and
the lowest for the pornographic violent film. Results are discussed in
terms of the aggressive cue value of the films. Several explanations,
including reference to the social learning theory, were offered to
account for this unexpected pattern of results.

title: Effects of Prenatal Stress on Parental Behavior of Male Rats ---- ----- -- --- --- Prenatal environmental stress has been shown to both feminize and
abstract: demosculinize the sexual behavior of male rats (Ward, 1972). The extent of these effects is influenced by prepuheral rearing conditions (Ward and Reed, in press). We are conducting an experiment on another sexually dimorphic behavior. This tests the interaction between prenatal stress and post-weaning social environment or parental behavior of male rats. Data available from this experiment (which is currently in progress), will be presented.

title: Developmental Perturbations in Postsynaptic Cells Produced by Cold-Shock during Development
abstract:
After studying normal development leading to the formation of tetrad
synapses by the visual receptors in the complex eyes of the house fly, we
have now started to disturb the developmental processes to further
unravel developmental rules governing synapse formation. In a pilot
study using (for the time being) the sledge hammer method of applying
heat- or cold-shocks during development we hope to disturb the formation
of the tetrad synapse, for instance by knocking out propective post-
synaptic cells. Preliminary results are presented.

title: The Effect of Context Shifts upon the Resistance-to-Reinforcement Assay of Conditioned Inhibition
abstract:
Pigeon's exposed to a negative CS-UCS contingency show attenuated
acquisition of excitatory responding when these stimuli occur later in a
positively contingent relation. This effect constitutes the traditional
resistance-to-reinforcement assay of conditioned inhibition. Our most
recent work suggests that his acquisition deficit may be composed of
three separable processes, only one of which is attributable to pre-
exposure to the negative CS-UCS relation per se.

title: The ~ Grail: ~ Quest for Circadian Rhythmicity in the Life of ! Fly
abstract:
Nassel et al recently discovered an efferent serotonergic neuron TAN-3,
that runs out from the brain to end in diffuse branches in the fly's
peripheral visual system. The structure and location of TAN-3's endings
suggest that it releases its neurotransmitter non-synaptically throughout
the eye, perhaps such as to influence some "global" attribute of visual
processing. Since very large circadian changes in visual sensitivity are
found in the eyes of many arthropods at dawn and dusk, and are mediated
by bioamine-containing efferent neurones in one known case, we
hypothesized that TAN-3 could be another such circadian controller
neuron. We shall describe long-term retinal recordings made to test this
idea, that ought to reveal any circadian changes in visual sensitivity
taking place either in the photoreceptors themselves, or in the neurons
immediately post-synaptic to them. This being a psychology department,
we also looked to see if this strain of flies showed any behavioural
evidence for circadian rhythmicity. Actually, our most intriguing
finding has nothing much to do with all this: it is that long term
light-adaptational depression follows exposure to simple room lights.
Adaptational recovery is quite fast at the level of the photoreceptors,
and the long term changes therefore must have been added at the synaptic
zone itself. This suggests, tentatively, a different "global" role for a
neuron like TAN-3 in visual processing, that should be testable in
future.

title: Lost in Space and Lost in Time
abstract:
The relationship between spatial and temporal factors underlying velocity
selectivity in cat striate cortex neurons was studied by obtaining
responses to 2-flash stimuli for all combinations of a series of spatial
and temporal separations between the two flashes. Analysis of the
nonlinear components underlying the direction selectivity revealed
interactions (space-time non-separability) which were generally small in
magnitude, and uncorrelated in either magnitude or sign with the velocity
preference measured in the same neurons. These results indicate that
direction selectivity in striate cortical neurons depends separately on
spatial and temporal parameters, rather than on the velocity implied by
their ratio.

title: Meaning in Motion: A reanalysis of the unit £!_ perception of ongoing behavior
abstract:
Three experiments were conducted in order to investigate the unitization
of ongoing behavior, In the first experiment subjects were instructed to
press a button while watching a film (1) "when a meaningful act ends and a
different one begins", or (2) "when a meaningful act occurs". No
difference was found between these instructional sets. On the whole, the
results of this and two subsequent experiments, as well as, previous
research suggest that people identify the meaning of events by observing
rapid changes in motion that can be meaningfully interpreted rather than
by identifying the beginnings and ends of hierarchically organized
behavioural units,

title: Early Developmental Changes in Timber Wolf Social Behaviour
abstract:
The social organization of wolf packs is maintained by a complex variety
of ritualized postures and actions. Here I document the early development
of these postural displays plus the social interactions within which they
occur, Daily video recordings of social interactions of two hand-reared
wolf pups (Canis lupus) from eight days of age were used to document the
ontogenetic emergence of the postural components of adult social
interactions, I transcribed interaction sequences from video records to
examine temporal patterns of pup-pup interactions. I found that postural
components emerge in a consistent order, with a progressive narrowing of
their contextual associations, Interactions between pups initially
involved maintaining contact, but by three weeks of age, biting and
pushing were common, By the fifth week, biting elicited yelps, At this
time pups were also likely to raise their tails vertically before biting,
At seven weeks of age, contacts became truncated, These data indicate the
operation and development of processes that may be relevant to a number of
mammalian social interactions,
is theoretically "strong"). Your laboratory microcomputer can help you
think more clearly about these issues.

title: CSc Explicitly Unpaired with the US Become Conditioned Inhibitors when CS and US Trials Occur in Single Alternation and also when they Occur in Unpredictable Order,
abstract:
Two groups of rats were trained with Rescorla's (1969) "Explicitly
Unpaired Procedure" (EUP) whereby US (0,5s foot-shock) and CS (60s white
noise) occured alone, separated by long intertrial intervals (III=7 min).
For one group the US and CS trials occured in single alternation (EUPs),
while for the second the sequence of US and CS trials was randomized
(EUPr). Strong conditioned inhibition accrued to both the EUPs- and EUPr-
trained CSs as indexed by both summation and retardation tests and
compared to a control group. This result, although in accord with
Rescorla's (1969) theoretical formulations on conditioned inhibition
resulting from negative CS-US contingency, is contrary to recent
demonstrations by Fowler (1985) that have followed from Wagner's SOP
theory.
10 ■ o. Treit
Pharmacological evidence for a continum between anxiety and convulsive
states
A variety of clinical observations have suggested that some convulsive,
epileptic states are correlated with "fear" or "anxiety". One specific
hypothesis that can be derived from these observations is that "anxiety"
might represent a mild, or "sub-clinical" epileptic state; i.e., anxiety
and convulsions might lie along the same mechanistic continum, Consistent
with this speculation, many potent anti-anxiety drugs also are potent
anti-convulsants, and some potent convulsant drugs appear to produce
"anxiety". These pharmacological relationships may not be coincidental,
and there is evidence that all of these drugs may act upon a similar
neurological substrate. My research on the interactive effects of
convulsants and anxiolytics within an animal model further supports the
notion of a link between anxiety and convulsant states.
capture the point, which will be discussed briefly in light of some
current perspectives.

title: Cell assembly: Hebb's neuronal model of learning
abstract:
One of the basic assumptions in the cell assembly hypothesis is that
synaptic connections are changeable, Studies in our Department by
Cynader, Yoon, and Goddard have all supported this assumption.
Experiments on invertebrates provides further insight into the nature of
synaptic change: cyclic AMP plays an important role in producing lasting
modifications of synaptic functions. Recent findings that the type of
dopamine receptors which is critical in producing reinforcement effect is
the Dl type -- the type that increases the production of cyclic AMP
suggest a future direction of the development of the model,

title: Hebb's contribution~ contemporary cognitive psychology
abstract:
Through his effective attack on radical behaviorism, his unflagging
emphasis on internal mental processes (thought), his cogent demonstration
of weaknesses in some key assumptions of the Gestalt and S-R frameworks,
and his own cell-assembly proposal, Hebb may be said to have plowed the
soil from which the rennaissance in cognitive psychology would grow &
flourish,

title: Object Superiority Effect~~ Function of Diss!_ Dimensionality
abstract:
The identification of a briefly presented line segment is sometimes
facilitated by embedding the target line in a context of other
noninformative lines (the "object superiority" and "object line" effects),
This study compared two alternative hypotheses for these effects, One
hypothesis claims that line figures with interpretations as three-
dimensional objects are processed more rapidly and accurately by the
visual system that two-dimensional figures (Weisstein and Harris, 1974),
The second hypothesis claims only that some lines-in-context are more
discriminable in a brief flash than others because of emergent features
created by the interactlon of target and context lines (Enns and
Prinzmetal, 1984),

authors_raw: G, Eskes, T. McCormick ands. Bryson
title: Autistic children's comprehension of single words,
abstract:
There is considerable evidence that children with early childhood autism
suffer a general language impairment, This impairment is manifested as a
difficulty in understanding the meanings of words and in organizing and
also illustrates a general principle of human and animal neural behavior
which is "Show me something I don't already know".

title: Changes in cell proliferation in the goldfish retina following optic~ crush.
abstract:
In goldfish, new cells are continuously added to the retina throughout
life. Optic nerve axotomy modulates this ongoing histogenesis. Labelling
densities in the ganglion cell/optic fiber layer and the photoreceptor
layer are affected following unilateral optic nerve crush, when fish are
allowed to survive for 1 day following injection of tritiated thymidine.
25. 1. McCook
The Electroretinogram of Insect Compound Eye: The origins of the ERG, and
its ~ In examining tne determination orcontrast codingin thevTsuaI'
system.
The cellular origins of the electroretinogram (ERG) of the blowfly
compound eye were investigated for both waveform and stimulus-response
characteristics. The results suggest that the ERG may be due to a
summation of the responses of the photoreceptors and the lamina large
monopolar cells. The ERG was also used to study the determination of .the
monopolar cell response to visual contrast, using flies reared under
abnormal contrast conditions. The results suggest that there may be some
visual plasticity, or environmental regulation of contrast coding in the
visual system, which is contrary to expectations for a short lived animal.

authors_raw: F. A. Baker, M. G. Yoon, and 1. R. Benowitz
title: The Optic Tectum Influences Production and Transport of Proteins in Retinal Ganglion Cells of Adult Goldfish.
abstract:
Ganglion cells in the goldfish retina produce proteins which are
transported through optic fibers to their target tissue, the optic tectum.
During regeneration of optic fibers the presence of the optic tectum
enhances the prod11ction of rapidly-transported proteins with molecular
lesion results, in short, indicate that the hypothalamus can take a
licking, but the FED keeps on ticking. Our current efforts to localize
the FED by assessing temporal variations of local cerebral 2-deoxyglucose
uptake will also be discussed-

authors_raw: R. T. Ross and V. M. LoLordo*
title: Behavior is in the eye of the Beholder: Differences in Response DefinitioncanEngencfer~verse-i,:ieoretical Interpretations
abstract:
Two procedures for observing rearing behavior during a localized visual CS
paired with food were compared within the same subjects. Using the method
and response definition described by Holland, rearing was acquired during
conditioning trials and declined with introduction of extinction. Using
the method and response definition described by Kaye and Pearce, rearing
declined from baseline levels during conditioning but was temporarily
restored with extinction. These within-subject outcomes indicate that the
response definitions used by Holland and by Kaye and Pearce, which differ
along a continuum of "directedness", assess different components of
behavior occurring in response to a visual CS reflecting associative
strength or associability, respectively. Thus, differences in response
definition, if not recognized, can give rise to theoretical conceptions of
the processes that mediate overt behavior which may appear contradictory.
* With thanks to Cheryl Reed Elder and Anastasia Droungas for serving as
additional observers.

title: Muscarinic and nicotinic acetylcholine binding sites in the developing cat visual cortei:""
abstract:
We have demonstrated that at distinctly different periods during
development, both muscarinic and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors are
specifically localised to layer IV of primary visual cortex. The
muscarinic receptors exemplify this pattern only during early postnatal
life but the nicotinic receptors develop this characteristic during the
physiologically defined critical period and express it into adulthood.
The different anatomical distributions of muscarinic and nicotinic
receptors in the visual cortex together with other physiological evidence
suggests distinctive roles for each of these receptor systems in the
control of plasticity.

authors_raw: D, E. Mitchell, K. M. Murphy, H. Dzioba and J. A, Horne
title: The requirements for optimum behavioural recovery from the effects of early monocular deprivation in kittens. -- --
abstract:
Last year we described a procedure that permitted an amazing degree of
recovery from the very severe behavioural effects of early monocular
deprivation, The method combined daily periods of reverse occlusion
(patching) where the kitten is forced to employ its formerly deprived eye,
with periods of binocular visual exposure. In kittens monocularly
deprived to 6 weeks of age, the vision of the formerly deprived eye
recovered to normal levels if the formerly nondeprived eye was patched for

title: Faps, Maps and Saps: Contextual Rules of Integrated Action
abstract:
Ethologists have documented, in behavioural terms, the existence of
numerous "fixed action patterns" (FAPS) for diverse animal species, have
more recently liberalized notions of extreme motor stereotypy in terms of
"modal action patterns" (MAPS), and have argued for the necessary
existence of independently ordered "specific action potentials" (SAPS).
However, a limit to current models is an a·ppropriate documentation of
variations in the form and central control of movement sequences as a
function of the broacie'r contexts within which these movements occur. I
thus report upon contextual modulations of integrated movement in rats,
with and without specific sensory & central lesions, based upon
collaborative work with Kent Berridge. • These investigations suggest new
ways to conceptualize the neural control of integrated behavioural
patterns in mammals.
ADDENDUM

title: REax;NITION OF (X)riGENICSTRAINSOF RATS. Richard~- Brown!, Prim_!!. ~2, and Bruce.:!_.Roser2.
abstract: 1 ( subdeparonent of Animal Behaviour, Madingley and 2 Institute of Animal Physiology, Babraham, Carnbridge,England). Recognition of different major histocanpatibility (MHC) types of mice is based on urinary charosignals fran congenic strains 'Nhich differ only in the MHC(Yamazaki, et al, 1979, J. Exp. Med., 150, 755-760). CA.trexperiment provides the first eviaence"tnat congenic strains of rats wich differ only in the MHCalso produce different urinary cheroosignals. Urine fran adult male PVGand PVG-Rlrats was used in a habituation - dishabituation test {Sundberg, et al, 1982, Behav. Neur. Biol., 34, 113-119) wit.'1 male PVG-RTlUrats as subjects. Urine fran PVGmales was easily distinguished fran that of PVG-Rl males (p. C:.001). Individual PVGmales ~re not distine;uished by their urinary odours, but iniividual PVG-Rl males did produce discriminabl y different odours (p. <. .05) . These results deroonstrate that PVGand PVG-Rlmales, which idiffer only in the A region of the MHC,produce different •urinary cheoosignals and that i.rxiividual males of the PVG-Rl strain also produce distinguishable "individual" odours. These may be related to individual differences in the quantity of A .region classical class 1 antigens in the urine of PVG-Rlrats (Singh and Roser, tmpUblished observations). We are now using I.imillnological assays to determine how the quality and quantity ~ of class 1 antigens or their fragments in the urine of these rats enables recognition of their substrain and of individuals within a highly inbred substrain.

title: A Review of Recent Epidemiological Studies of Mental Retardation: Canadian and Non-Canadian Data~ Prevalence, Associated Disorders and Aetiology
abstract:
This poster will review recent epidemiological studies of mental
retardation. Discussion will focus on Canadian versus non-Canadian data
pertaining to 1) prevalence, 2) associated disorders, and 3) aetiology. The
prevalence of mental retardation has been shown to vary as a function of
degree of retardation, chronological age, and gender. Moreover, a large
proportion of mentally retarded individuals present with additional
disorders (e~-, language problems, behavioural difficulties, epilepsy, and
cerebral palsy). The number of disorders varies with the degree of mental
retardation. Investigations into the aetiology of mental retardation have
focussed on the contributions of pre-, peri- and post-natal factors.
Although it has traditionally been assumed that mild retardation (IQ=S0-70)
has a cultural familial basis, recent data have challenged this claim. The
aetiologies of mild and severe (IQ(SO) retardation will therefore be
compared. The limitations of current epidemiological- data and implications
for future research will be discussed.

title: Does "inhibition of return" operate in visual search?
abstract:
After a peripheral event the processing of nearby stimuli is facilitated for
about 300 msec. From about 300 msec onwards there is a longer lasting
inhibitory effect that has been called "inhibition of return". We conducted
two experiments to determine if "inhibition of return" might operate to make
visual search more efficient. Our main hypothesis was that when visual
search appears to be serial and self-terminating (i.e. attention is
inspecting each location), then inhibition would be left behind at each
inspected location. We used a dual task in which the search response was
followed immediately by a simple luminance detection probe that was
presented "on" a potentially inhibited location or on some other location
("off")· Using Treisman 's "popout" condition as a control for most visual
variables we found evidence consistent with a functional role for inhibition
of return in visual search.

title: Peripheral filtering of unattended input revisited: A brainstem auditory evoked response study.
abstract:
Psychological theories of selective attention have been particularly
concerned with the degree to which unattended inputs are processed.
Proposals have ranged from peripheral filtering of unattended input to
complete central processing of all input before the initiation of any
selective processes. Attempts to determine which of these theories is
correct and the nature of the physiological mechanisms involved have
produced conflicting results. Experimentation in non human species has
demonstrated attention driven efferent modulation of brainstem
(olivocochlear bundle) responses to auditory input. Attempts to duplicate
these findings in humans have resulted in a good deal of controversy with
claims of both success and failure. In a recent study (Connolly, Aubry and
McGillivary, 1987) the conditions associated with peripheral fl ltering 1n
animals were adapted to human experimentation. No evidence was found in the
brainstem auditory evoked response (BAER) for attention related response
modulation. This failure to support the idea of peripheral filtering of
unattended input in humans could not be considered definitive until greater
task demands were introduced into the design. We report here BAERamplitude
and latency data collected when subjects were required to attend and respond
to: 1. the auditory stimuli evoking the response; 2. visual stimuli in
conditions where the concurrent auditory stimuli assisted performance; 3.
visual stimuli where the auditory stimuli hindered performance; and 4.
visual stimuli where the auditory stimuli were of little consequence for
performance. Results are discussed in terms of their implications for
cognitive theories of selective attention and the putative physiological
mechanisms mediating the process.

title: Individual Differences in Attention: Type~ and Type Band Cognitive Failure
abstract:
A significant negative correlation was found between two self-report
measures: the JAS (Jenkins Activity Survey) and the CFQ (Cognitive Failure
Questionnaire). The JAS is used to distinguish Type A from Type B
individuals. The CFQ assesses a person's absent-mindedness and everyday
errors, Thus, the correlation showed that Type A's reported low CFQ and
Type B's reported high CFQ, Previous literature has shown that ( 1) Type A
and Type B individuals differ in their attentional mechanisms, and (2) high
CFQ and low CFQ differ in attention, My research will involve looking at
whether or not these attentional differences really exist,

title: From Moscow with Love -------
abstract: My plan in this talk is to discuss some recent data on contextual controls in integrated movement (with K, Berridge: rats) in the still broader context of the March, 1987, Moscow meeting on movement, The key problems from which details will be selected are: a) how do we deal with separable action properties that are also connected to one another; b) how do we deal with movement dynamics and also stabilities in action; c) how do Halifax rats relate to Russian polit burocrats? Scenic slides as well as data (I hope!),

title: The effectiveness of part-time reverse occlusion as a means for promoting recovery from the pnysiological effects of monocular deprivation in the kitten visual cortex - --
abstract:
Previously it has been shown that certain regimens of part-time reverse
occlusion can promote recovery of normal visual function in the deprived eye
of kit tens that had been monocularly deprived to 6 or 8 weeks of age. We
now report on the extent of the physiological recovery in the visual cortex
of four such kittens that had recovered normal vision in their deprived eye.
Considerable shifts of occular dominance towards the deprived eye were
observed in one of the two conditions of occlusion. Moreover, a number of
cells exhibited tuning for retinal disparity that was of sufficient
magnitude to provide a possible substrate for stereopsis.

title: Is Psychology an 'etic' or 'emic' science? And what of Biology?
abstract:
An 'etic' science (e.g. 'phonetics') takes as its starting point a set of
categories that are to apply to the phenomena of interest based on Cartesian
principles of a hypothetics - deductive science. An 'emic' science (e.g.
'phonemics') applies categories that are grounded in the mental life of the
subject of study, whether it be a man, a culture, or another organism. Its
task is to rationally reconstruct that mental life in terms of rules that
apply to mental activity in general. I will argue that the interests of
these two types of science differ, and that psychology tries,
unsuccessfully, to wed the two approaches.

authors_raw: R. Marois, B.J. Chiasson, R.P. Croll
title: Embryonic Development of Serotonin-Like Immunoreactivity in the Snail, Lymnaea
abstract:
Modern immunohistochemical techniques allow for the identification of
subpopulations of cells with dis ti nc t biochemical phenotypes within the
central nervous system (CNS). The use of these techniques also allows for
descriptions of the number, sizes and some aspects of the morphology of
these cells. In this study we have exploited these advantages to identify
the approximately 250 serotonergic cells found within the CNS of the pond
snail, Lymnaea stagnalis at the time of hatching and then describe aspects
of their embryonic development.

title: Post-hatching Development of Serotonin-Like Immunoreacti vi ty in the Snail, Lymnaea
abstract:
Post-hatching development of Lymnaea involves a 10-20 fold increase in the
linear dimensions of ganglia comprising the central nervous system (CNS)-
As a first step toward an explanation of this growth we have examined the
post-hatching development of a subpopulation of neurons which exhibit
serotonin-like immunoreactivity. Growth of the CNS can be partially
explained in terms of increases in both cell size and cell number within
this subpopulation. Several large, identified neurons increase greatly in
soma cross-sectional diameter. A large number of cells which are
identifiable only as members of clusters tend to be smaller throughout
development and tend to increase more slowly in cross-sectional diameter.
In addition to these increases in cell size the population numbers of most
cell clusters increase throughout development.

title: Distribution of Monoamines in the Hatchling Land Snail, Achatina Fulica
abstract:
As was shown in the previous two presentations histochemical procedures can
be used to describe single cells or clusters of cells within the nervous
system of certain invertebrates. These techniques can also serve as a basis
for the tentative identification of cellular homologies between species.
The glyoxylic acid histofluorescent method for the demonstration of
monoamines and immunohistochemical procedures for the localization of
serotonin were applied to the central ganglia of the land snail, Achatina
fulica. Based on cell body size and location, biochemical content and
neuritic projection patterns, several cells in Achatina appear to be
homologous to cells previously identified in Lymnaea and several other
gastropods.

title: Preliminary Report on~ Epidemiological Study of Autistic Syndromes
abstract:
Autism is a stable disorder beginning in early childhood, and characterized
by severe impairment in cognitive, linguistic and social development. The
diagnosis of this syndrome has been the source of considerable contraversy
and confusion. The study to be described is an in-depth epidemiological
investigation of the full spectrum of autistic conditions. In addition to
providing the first North American prevalence data, we are examining the
relationships among a number of psychological and physical variables. The
aim is to provide data relevant to the altiology of autism. This talk will
focus on findings regarding genetic and traumatic contributory factors.

title: Reinforcer location effects in simple conditioning: How does the rat know when and where to go ~ pick up food?
abstract:
Two experiments inquire as to how the rat is sensitive to manipulations that
vary the location of a reinforcer during simple conditioning procedures. In
the first, we asked whether changing the location of a preferred edible
after the rat had already learned that a stimulus signalled its availability
in another place, would affect responses conditioned to that stimulus.
Following the location-shift manipulation, conditioned responses continued
to be elicited by the stimulus but they were directed to the shifted
location. In the second experiment, we asked whether presenting the
preferred edible in one location before the rat had learned that a stimulus
signalled its availability in a different location, would affect the course
of learning accruing to that stimulus. Preliminary data indicate that the
rate of learning was enhanced in the location-shifted group relative to a
non-shifted group. It is suggested that these data pose problems for most
"reflex-oriented" conceptions of behavior.

title: The mystery of the misplaced clock
abstract:
Several laboratories have now reported that circadian rhythmicity, disrupted
after ablation of the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), can be restored by
transplantation of fetal hypothalamic tissue containing SCN neurons.
Transplant tissue has usually been placed into the part of the brain where
the SCN is normally located - the base of the third ventricle. In
collaboration with Dr. p. Decoursey, I tested whether SCN tissue
transplanted to a site distal to the hypothalamus would be capable of
restoring circadian rhythmicity in hamsters made arrhythmic by SCN lesions.

title: The pigeon and the polka dot
abstract:
Pigeons were trained to discriminate among matrices of 36 randomly arranged
dots, some of them red, and the rest blue. If a matrix with red was
positive, then the birds provided an orderly declining gradient of
generalization as the proportion of red dots decreased. If blue was
positive, the opposite gradient was obtained. During a transfer test with
matrices of 64 dots the pigeons provided similar gradients according to the
same ratios of red and blue as those that controlled the behavior with 36
dots.

title: The Painless Tale of a Tunicate Headache Identification of specific neurons in the larval tunicate
abstract:
The larval acsidian (tunicate) has some vertebrate affinities and its
nervous system contains relatively few neurons (about 50). This preparation
provides a unique opportunity to study the development of nervous systems.
In the present investigation, an acetylcholinesterase (AChE)-selective
staining method and a methionine (MET)-enkephalin immunocytochemical
technique was utilised to examine the nervous system in the larvae of Ciona
intestinalis. Eight-10 primary sensory neurons and a pair of neurons in the
vesical ganglion were identified by AChE staining and a single caudal neuron
in the tail nerve cord showed MET-enkephalin immunoreactivity. This is the
first demonstration of AChE-positive and MET-enkephalin reactive neurons in
the larval tunicate, and the latter is also the first evidence for neuronal
perikarya in the nerve cord.

title: The shape of things to come: Spotlight failure in exogenous covert orienting
abstract:
A briefly presented visual stimulus shown in the periphery, is assumed to
orient attention automatically. In fact, dissociations have been
demonstrated between the effects of visual-spatial attention thus oriented;
and attentional orienting which is more voluntary in nature. One rule of
thumb which has been adopted is that a peripheral cue will enhance the
processing of any possible item occuring at the cued location. The studies
I report examine the alternative possibility that a peripheral cue, in fact,
enhances processing of subsequent stimuli only to the extent that they are
somehow physically similar to that cue. The rationale will be discussed,
and preliminary data presented.

title: Does Life Imitate Art? -------- The development of perspective in children's drawings will be examined with an eye on the development of perspective in the history of Western art. Several experiments that examine one aspect of perspective in children's drawings -- how to draw occluded objects -- will be reported.
Coding of Small Amplitude.:: Modulations by Cat Auditory Cortex Neurons
abstract:
As part of an on-going research program on the neural coding of complex
sounds, we have examined the responses of cat cortical cells to small (6--dB)
linear modulations of the amplitude of continuous carrier tones. We found
that these neurons were sensitive to the direction and rate of intensity
change, and to the carrier level on which those modulations were imposed.
Moreover, the fashion in which neural response rates varied with alterations
in any of those stimulus parameters was of the kind predicted by our recent
model of the afferent input to cortical cells. In conjunction with our
previous evidence on the sensitivity of cortical neurons to other complex
sounds, notably frequency modulations and tone-noise combinations, these
data reinforce our view that the auditory cortex contains much or all of the
neural machinery required for the analysis of sounds with complex spectral
and temporal microstructure, including speech.

authors_raw: P,J, McLeod, J, Mencher, J. Ryon, J.C. Fentress
title: Why do dogs roll in stuff that stinks?
abstract:
Despite the widespread occurrence of scent-rubbing behaviour in mammals,
neither the factors responsible for eliciting this behaviour nor the
function(s) it serves are understood, The question we address here is which
characteristics of an odour are important in eliciting rubbing. To
determine this, 14 odors in 7 classes were presented to groups of wolves.
Their responses to these stimuli were analyzed using multidimensional
scaling (MOS) techniques to illustrate how the wolves themselves were
categorizing the odors. Differences in rubbing behaviour among individuals
were also analyzed using MDS to examine the possible role of social factors
in scent-rubing behaviour.

title: ~ methodology for explanation in the social sciences
abstract:
Explanation of action involves finding and modelling the generative
mechanisms which produce manifest behaviours. The method employed in the
natural sciences is appropriate for the social sciences: Positivism's
attempt to limit knowledge only to accessible mechanisms has stunted
explanation in the social sciences. Underlying intentional and meaningful
action are intrinsic and extrinsic generative mechanisms, both of which are
necessary for a complete explanation of action. To produce models of these
forces, the developmental methodology, as employed by Piaget and Vygotsky,
is valuable. In overcoming constraints, the organism is seen in change,
thus showing the generative mechanisms~ action, effecting behaviour.

title: Of Holes ~ Space and Shifts in Time
abstract:
In the cosmic space-time continuum there are billions and billions of stars.
There are more cells in the brain than there are stars in the sky. Each
neuron in our visual cortex views one small place in visual space. Light
from distant stars must travel through space until after some fixed amount
of time, it reaches our eyes. But the long journey is not over for that
signal must then travel through many synaptic connections until it is
registered in our visual cortex, The time necessary for this synaptic trek
appears to be a constant (40-60 ms), We have investigated the effect of
repeated stimulation of small portions of the receptive fields of neurons in
area 17 of the cat visual cortex. We examined the changes in the spatial
properties of these receptive fields and the temporal characteristics of the
cells. We have found that if a small part of the receptive field undergoes
adaptation, the following four events may occur 1) no adaptation; 2) general
adaptation of the entire receptive field; 3) a decrease in response to
stimulation only at the point of adaptation; or 4) a decrease at the point
of adaptation as well as increased responses at adjacent points in the
receptive field. We also examined the cell's latency to respond to see if
these changes in the spatial properties of the receptive fields could result
from making the afferent neurons more sluggish.

authors_raw: S.J. Hasan, S.G. Marlin, M.s. Cynader
title: Keeping ~ Eye £E: Your Other
abstract:
Studies of individual neurons in visual cortex have demonstrated selective
adaptation of neuronal responses to such stimulus parameters as direction of
motion, orientation and spatial requency. We have examined interocular
transfer of some of these adaptive effects in neurons displaying different
strengths of excitatory input from the two eyes. An optimally oriented high
contrast square wave grating, selected to evoke vigorous responses from a
given unit, was swept across the receptive field. Unit responses for both
preferred and null directions of grating movement in each eye were
determined prior to and following one minute of unidirectional stimulation.
Following adaptation, most units showed decreased responsivity to
stimulation in both the dominant and non-dominant eyes. A clear minority of
cells, however, showed adaptation effects specific to the eye which had been
stimulated. The degree to which direction selective adaptation effects
transferred interocularly was unrelated to the cell's ocular dominance
categories as measured by Hubel and Wiesel's seven point scale. Both simple
and complex cells exhibited comparable amounts of interocular transfer of
adaptation. A large fraction of the neurons studied exhibited interocular
transfer of direction,,,.. selective adaptation. However, direction selective
adaptation could occur ~ithout obvious interocular transfer. In addition,
interocular transfer could occur even when prolonged stimulation in one
direction resulted in response decrement in both directions of stimulation.
Since the motion aftereffect displays marked interocular transfer in humans,
it is possible that cells like those exhibiting interocular transfer of
direction selective adaptation play a significant role in this illusion.
The further characteristics of these cells are currently under
investigation.

title: Midlocation placement strategy in the orienting of visual attention: A spotlight £E: the hemi.field activation hypothesis
abstract:
Previous research has shown that we are able to orient visual attention to
spatial locations without moving our eyes. How is this visual attention
distributed? Posner, Snyder & Davidson (1978) have proposed a spotlight
metaphor describing a spatially restrictive phenomenon. In contrast, Hughes
& Zimba (1985) have presented evidence suggesting that attention is
allocated to the entire left or right visual half-fields. However, the
Hughes and Zimba data could have been generated by a midlocation placement
strategy (MPS) of an attentional 'beam'· Evidence against the hemifield
activation hypothesis and current research on MPS is discussed.

title: Nick: ~ teen with terminal foresight
abstract:
We have characterised and localised a population of nicotinic acetylcholine
(ACh) receptors in the cat visual cortex, superior colliculus (SC) and
laterageniculate nucleus (LGN). In visual cortex, the distribution of these
receptors is associated with the pattern of LGN termination. The receptor
distribution in the SC and LGN closely resembles the pattern of retinal
termination. Surgically undercutting the visual cortex eliminates receptors
in this area, however they remain intact when cortical cells are selectively
removed. Localised, unilateral lesions of the LGN abolishes nicotinic
receptors in the areas of the visual cortex corresponding to the LGN lesion
area. Bilateral eye removal eliminates nicotinic receptors in the SC of
both tecta, and unlilateral enucleation selectively destroys these receptors
in the SC contralateral to the ablation. In the LGN, bilateral enucleations
diminish nicotinic receptors in all retinal projection areas, and unilateral
ablations reduce the number of receptors only in the areas recetvlng an
afferent projection from the previously intact eye. Taken together, these
data describe the affinity of nicotinic ACh receptors for primary sensory
afferent terminals in the SC and LGN, and LGN terminals in the visual
cortex. These receptors, via this strategic location, provide a mechanism
by which ACh can modulate transmitter release in the visual system.

title: Love and hate among the gerbils
abstract:
We examined sexual behaviour and odour preferences in mated pairs of
gerbils. They were given sexual behaviour tests with their own mate and
with a novel partner. We predicted that gerbils would show more sexual
behaviour and less aggression to familiar partners. While many of the
behaviours showed this trend, the differences were not significant. Steve
Holman has been repeating this experiment and I hope he has significant
results by now. In a second experiment we examined responses of gerbils
housed in mixed- or same-sex pairs to the urine odours of their cage-mates
and novel gerbils. Gerbils housed in mixed-sex pairs approached and
investigated the odour of their cage-mate and produced more modulated
ultrasounds in the presence of this odour than in the presence of the odour
of a novel gerbil. Gerbils housed in same sex pairs showed no such
differential responsiveness to the odour of their cage-mates. Odour may be
used for individual recognition and function to reduce aggression in social
interactions between mated pairs of gerbils.
24: c. Reed-Elder, v.M. LoLordo
Comparison of pavlovian conditioned inhibition procedures having different
temporal arrangements of excitor and inhibitor on nonreinforced trials
Pigeons were trained with simultaneous (Sim) or serial (Ser) Pavlovian
conditioned inhibition procedures. Transfer of inhibition was tested. In
the first test, the inhibitor was presented in various temporal arrangements
with the training excitor (transfer across procedures). In the second test,
the inhibitor was presented in compound with a second excitor (transfer
across excitors). Inhibitors conditioned with Ser procedures suppressed
responding both Sim and Ser trials but did not suppress responding to
another excitor. Inhibitors established with a Sim procedure transferred
across excitors but showed poor transfer to Ser procedures.
Orientation tuning and binocularity of binocular-rivalry adaptation in human
vision
Subjects viewed adaptation stimuli for l minute. Then two rival test
gratings, differing in orientation from each other by 90 , were presented
one to each eye, for l minute and subjects recorded binocular rivalry. When
one eye was adapted, rivalry alternations were briefer for adaptation and
test gratings of similar orientation, especially in the adapted eye.
However, when both eyes were adapted by rival gratings, the duration of
rivalry alternations increased in both eyes equally, to produce symmetrical
U-shaped orientation functions, This implies that rivalry adaptation
affects only binocular units in the human visual system,·

authors_raw: I.A. Meinertzhagen and A. Ferrus*. *C.s.r.c., Velazquez
title: Madrid, Spain.
abstract:
The Influence of Cell Size Upon Synaptic Frequency in Drosophila.
The size of a neuron provides a major prediction of both the number of its
synapses and of its aggregate area of synaptic contact. Our previous
studies establish a close correlation between the number of afferent
synaptic sites and the presynaptic membrane surface area over which these
are distributed, in the terminals of photoreceptors innervating the first
optic neuropile, or lamina, underlying the fly's eye. A similar
relationship also exists for their target interneurons, Ll and 12, each
unique occupants of one of the cartridges of lamina neuropile, the
dendrites of which also receive synaptic input in proportion to their
surface area. All such studies are correlative, however, and fail to
distinguish directly whether large neurons bear more synapses or,
alternatively, whether neurons with more synapses grow larger, perhaps for
some trophic reason,
I will describe experiments in Drosophila in which the causal relationship
between cell size and synaptic number may be established by analysing
synaptic frequencies in neurons which are made independently large, using
genetic mutants.

authors_raw: S. Shaw, D, Moore, A. Frohlich, J. Dawson and P. Dickson, The Fly Collective
title: La nouvelle sensation: plus~ change, plus ce n'est pas la meme mouche.* ~Supported by NSERCand a rather small dictionaryj':- -- -- ---
abstract:
The nature of evolutionary change has been little studied at the cellular
level in any nervous system, and so constitutes an important lacuna in
knowledge. We have told you before that in the system we study for
convenience, in insects, evidence suggests that the evolution of function
has been accomplished by saving the same fixed set of ancestral neuronal
homologues, but allowing these to vary some of their mutual synaptic
connections, Attempts to justify a statement of such penetrating
simplicity will be made with the help of actual results, drawn from our
current work on a particularly stupid and probably primitive fly. The new
results throw more light on the things that may change more readily, and
things that may not, in the evolution of a nervous system,

authors_raw: B. Rusak, R. Mistlberger, B, Losier and C,H. Jones
title: The Food, the Pad and the Cuddlies,
abstract:
Rats and a few other species have been shown to anticipate a daily time at
which they are fed by increasing their activity before this time, This
increased anticipatory activity has been shown to be under the control of
a food-entrainable oscillator (FEO), a component of the circadian system.
This oscillator is separate from the light-entrainable pacemaker in the
obtained, Numerosity is established as a "robust" stimulus dimension,
The mechanism is a mystery, but the adaptive value of the discrimination
would be considerable for a foraging animal.
I 7. A, Droungas and V. LoLordo
Short Exposure to Long Noises in the Midst of Shocks Will Make Rats Relax:
Reasons Remain to be Determined- -- --- --- ---
It is well documented that a CS, like a noise or a light, that is
presented on trials during which shock is postponed will become a fear
inhibitor, In the presence of such a CS, which has been "explicitly
unpaired" with shock, rats will continue to lever-press for food even if
another CS that has been "explicitly paired" with shock is concurrently
present. The present experiment demonstrated that such fear inhibiting
property was acquired by the CS after what is typically considered
"limited" amount of training (8 conditioning sessions) but only if the
explicitly unpaired CS was of relatively long duration (60 s). An
identically trained CS of short duration (10 s) failed to become a fear
inhibitor,

title: Blockade of Dopamine D2 Receptors in the Brain Suppresses Operant Responding-. -
abstract:
Raclopride, a dopamine antagonist, has a high affinity for D2 receptors
but extremely low affinity for Dl receptors, Intraperitoneal injection of
raclopride suppressed bar-pressing for food (CRF and VI-30 sec), ventral
tegmental stimulation, and intravenous heroin infusion in the rat. The
degree of suppression was related to the dose, and the maximal effects
were observed from 30 to 180 min after injection, Raclopride injection
into the nucleus accumbens suppressed ipsilateral tegmental self-
roles of experience, b) rules of generalization, and c) the organism's
handling of incomplete or distorted information. This talk is designed to
highlight some of these issues, with reference to existing literature and
potential experimental approaches for future work. Naturally, mice and
wolves will play a key (but not exclusive) role here!
J. Barresi
The Myriad Mind: Steps Toward~ Science of Persons.
Psychologists have given insufficient attention to the possible use of
biographical materials involving whole lives as a data base for the
development of the psychology of persons. In a book that I am currently
working on, I will argue for an approach for using such data which treats
individual lives as a unit. According to this approach, competing
theories would be developed to account for the causal history of
particular lives, and the power of these theories would be assessed
relative to alternative theories in terms of their simplicity, coherence,
and plausability in accounting for the particular life, and in terms of
the generality and testability of their principles in applying to other
lives.

authors_raw: P. Dunham, F, Dunham and C. Davis
title: Mother Care and Other Care: Some Differences in Mother-Infant Social Structure:----
abstract:
While studying the structure of mother-infant social interactions, we
encountered some unexpected and interesting differences when comparing
mothers employed outside the home and mothers who care for their infants
full-time in the home.

authors_raw: E. Walsh, H. Legerstee and K. Kienapple
title: The Development of Imitation During the First Year.
abstract:
Many studies have looked at imitation in infants under constrained,
unnatural conditions and at a particular age. In contrast, this study has
taken a longitudinal approach to study the development of imitation by
infants when engaged in natural interactions with their mothers. Play
sessions of 2 mothers and their infants were videotaped. Dyads were
observed biweekly from 3 to 25 weeks of age and monthly thereafter until
12 months. Sessions were of 45 sec durations at 3, 5 and 7 weeks and of 60
sec thereafter. Patterns of imitation (especially in mouth opening, lip
movements, smile and vocalization) indicate that an imitative ability may
be present in the first 3 to 9 weeks of an infant's life. Imitation
appears to decrease at about 17 weeks and increase again at about 34 to 40
weeks. The results suggest that imitation may be an integral part of
early mother/infant social interactions.

title: Does Covert Visual Orienting Affect Stimulus Encoding or Decision Criteria?
abstract:
The debate goes on. Investigators have applied paradigms from within
mental chronometry, signal detection theory and electrophysiology to
answer this question. This literature will be critically reviewed with
particular emphasis on the distinction between endogenous and exogenous
covert orienting. Mathematical frameworks like the accumulator and random
walk models will be applied to the corpus of chronometric data from the
Hansen thesis and similar follow-up experiments conducted in our
laboratory. By focussing attention on a detailed analysis of these
studies we hope to enhance the "signal" in this noisy area. Strategies
for future research will be presented.

title: Implicit Expectancies in Automatic Visual Orienting.
abstract:
Peripheral visual stimuli can attract attention very quickly, and such
orienting effects appear to be automatic. It is usually assumed that the
cue operates via a system which informs higher-level processes 'where' in
space an event occurs, and that 'what' is at the cued location is
irrelevant. From this it follows that (all other things being equal) a
given cue will have identical effects on all possible classes of targets
which occur. This is not the case. Cueing effects are modulated by the
physical similarity between the cue and target, indicating that when a
peripheral event occurs, information concerning attributes such as form,
orientation, and motion is used to facilitate processing of subsequent
targets. Color information does not appear to be used in this manner. It
seems that when one orients attention to a location via some peripheral
'event', one expects to find the object responsible for that 'event'.

authors_raw: G. Eskes, S. Bryson and T. McCormick
title: Further Studies of Comprehension in Autistic Children.
abstract:
Converging evidence from our studies and those from other laboratories
suggest that high-functioning autistic children's comprehension of single
words is similar to that of carefully matched control children. However,
while single word comprehension may be developmentally normal, both
clinical reports and language comprehension studies agree that autistic
children do have trouble comprehending more complex, sequentially ordered
information, such as sentences or paragraphs. This talk will describe a
study we are about to begin that will examine their understanding of the
same words presented individually or in the context of a sentence.

title: To Tune or Not to Tune: Cognitive and Sensory Influences on Memory for a Microtonal ScaleasShown by ~ Signar-Detection Analysis.
abstract:
Musically trained and less trained subjects completed nine conditions of
an absolute judgment task resulting from three sequential (melodic) and
between people and objects, they do not seem to differentiate between the
active and inactive person as ~arly and as clearly as do normal infants.
Rather than showing distress, OS babies continue to smile and vocalize
with neutral faces when in front of the nonresponsive adult ■ This lack of
communication skills may be one of the contributing factors to their
delayed linguistic development. Additional results focused on the
evolution of the responses to people and objects of these OS infants
during their first year of life.

title: Effects of Genetic Differences at the Major Histocompatibility Complex and Diet ~ Attraction ~ the Maternaliliour by H-2 Congenic Mice.
abstract:
Mouse pups are attracted to the "maternal odors" produced by lactating
females. This experiment investigates the role of diet and the Major
Histocompatability Complex (MHC) in maternal odor recognition. Two
congenic strains of mice, which differ in the MHCwere used (C57BL/6J and
B6C-H-2bml/BYJ). Pups were tested for odor preferences using maternal
urine and feces odors in 2 min. tests, If the MHCplays an important role
in maternal odor identification, odors from the same strain should be
preferred to those of the other strain. If diet influences odor
identification, odors from mothers on the same diet should be preferred.
Finally, it is possible that the MHC will influence preferences for urine
~while diet influences preferences for feces odors.

title: Catecholamines in the Central Nervous System of Lymnaea,
abstract:
The dist ri bu tion of catecholamines (CA's) within the central 0 anglia of
the pond snail, Lymnaea stagnalis was examined through the use of
glyoxylic acid and aqueous formaldehyde histofluorescence techniques, We
found prominent clusters of CA cells located in the right and left
parietal ganglia and along the anteriolateral margin of the pedal ganglia.

title: Hale Parental Behaviour in the House Mouse (Mus musculus).
abstract:
All studies of paternal behaviour have been done with ad lib food and
water in a warm environment. No studies have examined the importance of
the male when environmental conditions mimic the real world, i.e. food
must be foraged. Two groups of house mice were compared in a controlled
environment: litters with their dam alone and litters with both parents.
The environment consisted of a home cage with a running wheel and a feeder
controlled by a computer. The amount of running needed to obtain food was
manipulated. The growth and development of the pups was monitored by
measuring the number of pups/litter, pup weight, growth rate, eye and ear
opening, the number of pups at weaning and the weight of pups at weaning.
Differences on these measures between the two groups was determined•
Behavioural observations were also conducted to examine the male's
contribution to litter development.

title: Cell sizes in the Lateral Geniculate Nucleus of theCat Followin~ Reverse Occlusion Re~mens which Optimize Visual Recovezy from Early Monocular Deprivation.
abstract: Monocular deprivation in early life causes the anatomical and physiological development of the central visual pathway of kittens to be severely impaired and results in visual deficits resembling those observed in severe forms of amblyopia. Following monocular deprivation from birth to six weeks of age, the cells in the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) which receive input from the deprived eye are 25-30% smaller in area than those cells which receive input from the non-deprived eye, The present research was designed to investigate further the duration of the critical periods in the kitten •LGN during which these anatomical changes occur. The kittens were monocularly deprived from birth to six weeks of age and then received a period of either full-time or part-time reverse occlusion. In addition, we examined the effects of a period of binocular vision imposed after the critical period on the morphology of the LGN. Finally several kittens were either reverse sutured or monocularly deprived at three months of age. The results indicate that a period of binocular visual exposure can alter LGN cell areas even when imposed after the critical period. In addition, the anatomy of the LGN is not directly correlated with the final visual capacities of the cat, but instead may reflect the visual acuities of the two eyes at the age of three months.

title: Cortical areas mediating behavioural recovezy following early periods of monocular deprivation in kittens,
abstract:
Previous work in the laboratory has shown that following termination of six weeks of reverse
occlusion of kittens that had been monocularly deprived until six weeks of age there is a surprising
change in the visual acuities of both eyes. Although the visual acuity of the initially deprived eye
improves substantially during the period of reverse occlusion, during the subsequent binocular period
the vision of the eye deteriorates rapidly while that of the initially non-deprived eye regains almost
normal acuity. As of yet, the mechanism mediating these changes is still unknown. I:esions of
areas 17 and 18 are being carried out to determine the location of the mechanism. Possible outcomes
and their meaning will be discussed.

authors_raw: M.G. Yoon and Myonghae Joy Yoon
title: Development of Narrative Discourse in Normal Children
abstract:
We study the development of narration in the way how normal children represent an input-story into
