their own narrative outputs. Child-subjects (2.5 - 8 years old) and their mothers are invited to
converse over an internal telephone system across two separate test rooms, while they can view each
other via a reciprocal video-monitoring system. The mother reads a short story to the child over the
phone as many times as the child wants. When the child is ready, he/she retells the story to a third
party, the experimenter. All utterances of the child and the experimenter are recorded on
videotapes, and later transcribed verbatim for detailed analyses. The utterances are segmented into
unitary propositions with differential symbolic notations. Individual children's recounts of each
story are depicted into characteristic textural graphs: unitary propositions constitute nuclear nodes
which are interconnected with various cohesive ties into a semantically coherent pattern. These
textural graphs are analyzed with respect to the categorical spectra of recalled propositions,
modifications of syntactic structures, the pattern of cohesive ties, and the types of semantic inferences
as compared with the template graph of the input-text The results show that preschool children
recalled action-propositions at higher frequencies whereas elementary school children recalled external
state-propositions at higher rates. Few children recalled narrator's perspectives. The modes of
syntactic combination of propositions became more elaborated as they grew older. The degree of
causal interpretations inferred from the input-propositions also increased with age.

title: Children's understanding of the expression of uncertainty
abstract:
Modal terms (e.g. must, might, probably, possibly) are commonly used in natural language to
express different degrees of the mental state of uncertainty. This paper reports an experiment which
examined the development of children's understanding of this function of modals. Sixty children
between the ages of three and six were presented with a task in which they had to find the location of
an object hidden in one of two boxes. Their only clues were two statements said to them by two
puppets. The two statements differed only in terms of the particular modal verb (of must, might,
and could) that was employed in the sentence frame "It [verb] be in the red/blue box" or modal
adjunct (of probably and possibly) employed in the sentence frame "It's [adjunct] in the red/blue
box." Results revealed that children's understanding of the distinctions between the modal terms
with respect to certainty and uncertainty develops at around four to five years of age. This finding is
in agreement with results from studies on mental terms such as know and think, and implies that
children's understanding of the mental state concept of uncertainty develops at about this age.

authors_raw: Nameera Akhtar, Frances S. Dunham, and Philip J. Dunham
title: Directive interactions and early vocabulazy development: The role of joint attentional focus.
abstract: Several dimensions of maternal speech correlate with aspects of language acquisition in the second year of life. Directiveness, for example, when assessed by syntactic or pragmatic aspects of the mother's speech (i.e. frequency of prescriptives), predicts slow vocabulary development As prescriptives are most often used to direct a child's attention to a different activity or object, it may be that attentional regulation is the critical factor in this negative relationship. In the present study, twelve mothers were videotaped interacting with their thirteen month old children, and 100 maternal utterances were coded for pragmatic intent Prescriptives were coded as either leading or following the child's previously established focus of attention. The frequency of mothers' follow-prescriptives was positively correlated with a productive vocabulary measure taken at 22 months. These results indicate that directing a thirteen month old's behavior can have beneficial effects on subsequent vocabulary development, if it occurs during periods of joint focus.

authors_raw: Mary E. Farmer and Susan E. Bryson
title: Spatial and Sequential Processin~ in Dyslexic and Normal Readers
abstract: Some researchers claim that the problem dyslexics· have with remembering the order of items is specific to phonetically cod.able mateii.al. On the other hand, Tallal (1984) has proposed that the difficulty is with processing any rapidly occurring sequential stimuli. These alternative hypotheses were investigated by presenting three tasks to three groups: dyslexics, age-matched, and reading- matched controls. The three tasks involved reproducing the identity and/or location of four letters which appeared at various rates either simultaneously or sequentially in a matrix, or sequentially in the same spot. Results indicated that dyslexics had more difficulty than age-matched controls with sequential presentation, and their performance deteriorated even more when both identity and location were required. Our findings provide support for the idea that dyslexics suffer a deficit in processing both sequential and phonetically codable material.

title: The frequency and behavioral manifestation of post-traumatic stress disorder in sexually abused children: A proposal.
abstract:
The purpose of the proposed study is to assess the frequency and behavioral manifestation of PTSD
in school-age children who have been sexually abused, using a representative sample of verified
cases. There will be a matched comparison group of physically abused children. The types and
prevalence of behavior problems exhibited by sexually abused children will be assessed using the
Child Behavior Checklist (Achenback & Edelbrock, 1983). Any problems found will be examined
for relationships with characteristics of the abusive event, the child's pre-abuse development, the
number and type of negative life events experienced by the child, family characteristics and family
support. It is expected that children with high levels of behavioral problems will have had a more
severe abuse experience and/or a more difficult pre-abuse history and will be more likely to have
symptoms of PTSD.

title: Descartes' mind, Descartes' body: The onto~enesis of a distinction between mind and matter.
abstract:
It is generally acknowledged that a major turning point in western man's self-understanding occurred
when Descartes originated the distinction between mind and matter. Descartes' distinction and his
mind/body dualism struck such a responsive chord that we have yet to extricate ourselves from it, as
indicated by the existence of cognitive science and neuroscience as separate but interactive
disciplines. In the present talk I will try to provide a theoretical account of the causal origin of this
distinction through a psychosocial investigation of Descartes' life history and its socio-cultural
context. I will also try to account for its long-term consequences.

title: Pi~eons Discriminate Numbers of People in Pictures
abstract:
Pigeons readily learn to distinguish slides of natural scenes that contain a person from slides that do
not. We asked whether they would also be sensitive to the number of people in a picture. Four
birds were trained to discriminate slides containing one person from slides containing "many", where
"many" was defined as eight or more. For two birds, one person was positive; for two others,
"many" was positive. The birds were then tested with other numerical categories: the presence of 2,
3, 4-5, and 6-7 persons in the picture. All birds provided orderly discrimination gradients among
these test values. We asked whether the numerosity discrimination had to be made explicit in training
in order to obtain such a gradient. For this purpose, we trained a second group of birds that had to
discriminate "many" from the absence of any person in the picture. This was an easy discrimination
for them, and a test with the same numerical categories again provided an orderly discrimination
gradient. These data provide evidence for number discriminations within "natural" categories, but
that is not the same as counting.

title: Flashes of red, flashes of blue, If you're a pi~eon, what do you do?
abstract:
Pigeons can discriminate the relative numerousness of one type of stimulus element versus another
type when these two types of stimuli are presented simultaneously in a two dimensional array
(Honig, last year). Recently we have been investigating pigeons ability to discriminate the relative
numerousness of two types of stimuli presented sequentially. Pigeons are presented with 5 "flashes"
of light. Some flashes are a blue light, others are a red light. If the sequence contained more blue
than red (an extreme example would be 5 blue and Ored) then pecking the left key may provide a
reinforcer, but pecking the right key darkens the chamber for 3 seconds. If there were more red, then
these contingencies are reversed. Accuracy in this task appears to be a function not only of the
relative number of elements, but also their arrangement within the sequence.

title: Black is beautiful, but white is alri~ht,
abstract:
When given a choice, rats prefer spending time in a black compartment of a two-compartment
apparatus when the other compartment is painted white and brightly lit. This unconditioned
preference for black was used as a baseline against which the effects of various Pavlovian treatments
on contextual learning was assessed. Rats were locked in the black compartment and given mild
electric shocks. To our surprise they now preferred the white compartment in a follow-up preference
test.

authors_raw: Anastasia Droungas, Andrew Delamater, and Vincent LoLordo
title: Is dis~st necessary in taste-mediated potentiation of cue aversion
abstract:
Animals learn aversions to exteroceptive cues, e.g. visual, auditory, olfactory, and contextual cues
only if during the poisoning episode these cues are compounded with taste. This phenomenon has
been termed taste-mediated potentiation of cue aversion and seems to defy the general principle of
stimulus equipotentiality. Garcia has proposed that potentiation is a phenomenon unique to the
feeding system since it is observed only in preparations where LiCl is used as the US and not in
preparations where shock is used. Li Cl is a potent toxicosis inducing agent that also produces shifts
in the hedonic evaluation of tastes that have been paired with it Shock on the other hand, is a fear
inducing agent and it does not produce palatability shifts. This talk will explore whether it is
necessary to use toxicosis inducing agents in order to produce potentiation.

title: BEER IN LOUNGE
abstract: Tuesday, 25 April

authors_raw: D.P. Phillips, S.E. Hall and J.L. Hollett
title: Summertime Diversions of a Man and Two Women, Or,Why It's Important That Spikes bePrecisely Timed
abstract: A much-favoured neural code for the frequency content of a sound is the temporal one. This theory proposes that each acoustic event in the stimulus generates a spike discharge in an auditory neuron, so that the time between spikes specifies the spectral and temporal content of the sound. There is abundant evidence in the auditory nerve not only that this temporal representation exists, but also that it is competent for frequency elements up to about 3500 Hz. The further question, however, is the extent to which this temporal code significantly penetrates the central auditory nervous system. This question is not unreasonable, because the greater the number of serial synapses in the pathway transmitting the time code, the greater must be the imprecision in spike timing at the end of the neural pathway. In studies conducted last summer, we obtained evidence on this issue from single cat cortical neurons, by studying the effects of placing increasing temporal demands on a neuron's spike response rate. Our data show that cortical neurons which entrain spikes to brief tones presented at low repetition rates (eg., 1 Hz) fail to do so for tones at higher repetition rates, even though the precision of their spike timing is more than capable of supporting perfect entrainment This suggests that factors other than spike time precision limit the temporal representation of acoustic events in the cortex. Second, we provided evidence that even under the most optimal of stimulus conditions, cortical cells probably do not have sufficient precision of spike timing to support a temporal code for components in excess of about 100 Hz. This suggests to us that the cortex does not possess a representation of the fine time structure of a sound's waveform.

title: A Comparison of two Methods of Assessment of the Visual Acuity of Cats: A Search for Evidence of Aliasing,
abstract:
The ability to resolve grating patterns at a given retinal location has traditionally been thought to be
limited by the spacing of photo-receptors or sampling elements at that location. However, it is
possible for acuity to exceed this limit in certain situations because of spurious resolution of patterns
generated by undersampling of gratings by a regular array of sampling elements. The term
"aliasing" is applied to such spurious resolution since the pattern observed has a spatial frequency
different (lower) than that of the pattern that is actually imaged on the retina. Because of the
potential for aliasing in the cat we have compared estimates of acuity obtained on a detection task
(subject to aliasing) with estimates derived on a discrimination task that should be immune to
aliasing effects. Since the two estimates were very similar it was concluded that conventional
_E.1easuresof acuity in the cat are not contaminated by aliasing.

title: Is Phillip Rushton wrong? Can one both have a large brain and be sexually well endowed?
abstract:
Recent work indicates that the nervous systems of gastropods grow post-embryonically by increasing
both the size and the number of resident neurons. For example, the Pelb cluster within the pedal
ganglia of the pond snail, Lymnaca. is undetectable in juvenile snails. It first appears near the time of
sexual maturity and thereafter the cells within the cluster increase rapidly in both size and number.
Parallel growth occurs in the cluster's target tissue, the penis. Our future studies will employ both
ablation and transplantation experiments to test the role of the penis in determining the growth of the
Pelb cluster. We hypothesize that contrary to certain well-established (but perhaps not scientifically
supported) beliefs, a large penis and perhaps a well used penis might enhance neural development. If
generalized to other animals this hypothesis could have profound social and ethical ramifications.

title: Foscinating rhythm, I'm under your spell.
abstract:
We have been studying the neural mechanisms by which lighting cycles entrain (synchronized)
circadian (daily) rhythms in mammals. The suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN) in the hypothalamus serve
as the dominant pacemaker of the circadian system, and cells here are responsive to retinal
illumination. We have recently found that these cells alter their gene expression in response to photic
cues. Brief light exposures cause SCN cells (and not other retinorecipient cells) to increase
expression of the cellular-ms. (c-fu.s) gene, a member of the family of proto-oncogenes. This
discovery presents new opportunities for analysing the transmitters in the entrainment pathway, the
intracellular events involved in entrainment, and the biochemical mechanism of the circadian clock.

authors_raw: J.H. Brandstatter, LA. Meinertzhagen, and S.R. Shaw
title: Degenerate Synapses
abstract:
We are interested in the plastic changes which synapses undergo during normal adult life. In the fly's
visual system, for example, synapses formed by the terminals of photoreceptors in the first optic
neuropile, or lamina, gradually decrease in number with age. The dynamic events of their loss are
being investigated by procuring the death of photoreceptors by photoablation, following treatment
with a dye sulphorhodamine. The terminals commence degeneration within one hour, progressing
through characteristic changes of mitochondrial swelling, terminal shrinking and cytoplasmic
darkening. Their synapses disappear, but asynchronously, progressing through the following
sequence: first the presynaptic ribbon disappears, then the postsynaptic elements separate from the
presynaptic site (usually together), and finally glia insinuate themselves into the enlarged synaptic
cleft.

title: Inter-s_peciesocular trans_plantationin flies: intended synaptic bestiality.
abstract:
Transplantations of eyes and brains have been undertaken between flies, of the same or different
species, with the aim of procuring inter-species synaptogenesis. Our objective is to examine how
conserved the intercellular signals that are widely hypothesised to underly synapse formation have
been between flies of progressively remote ancestry. Current progress and its implications will be
reported.

title: Evolution of the visual optics and associated neural wirin~ in Diptera,
abstract:
In pursuit of the nature of evolutionary modification in nervous systems, so far we have looked
mostly at neuronal connectivity, finding that there has been wholesale change in the synaptic
connections between an invariant set of neuronal homologues, in Diptera (flies). The visual optics
have received less attention, but evolved in parallel from a more primitive symmetrical state to form
the asymmetric clusters of photoreceptors found exclusively in today's advanced families. This
switch apparently happened in a single transition in an ancestor back in the Triassic or earlier. A
highly stereotyped projection pattern of axons is needed behind the eye to take advantage of the new
optics, raising the question of whether this "neural wiring" changed in step with the optical changes,
or occurred earlier or later, out of synchrony. Reconstructions of the axonal projection pattern in
several primitive groups indicates a very tight linkage between the optics and the wiring, pointing
probably to some kind of coupling between the two seemingly independent developmental
programmes. In attempting to shed more light on the transition state, I have started to examine
the optics in a primitive group that lies near the stem line of the advanced Diptera, as a prelude to
tracing its axonal projection., and will show how the optics can be analysed in vivo with a suitably
arranged microscope, and what the results predict so far.

authors_raw: P. Kind, D.E. Mitchell and C. Beaver
title: Hybridoma Technolo~: A Technique for Identifying the Molecular Basis Developmental Plasticity.
abstract:
Many hypotheses for the specificity of connections in the nervous system involve the presence of cell
surface molecules which act as recognition sites between appropriate neurons. Indeed, many
theories concerning the molecular mechanisms which underly plasticity in developing mammalian
pathways include the presence of surface molecules capable of mediating synaptic reorganization.
Hybridoma technology provides a potential vehicle for identifying the cellular antigens which underly
synaptogenesis and developmental plasticity. In the present study, the visual cortex of kittens reverse
sutured at four weeks for four days were used to immunize adult mice in an attempt to produce
antibodies specific for developmental antigens of the visual system. We will describe our attempt
with the hybridoma procedure and in addition present some results obtained with CAT 301, a
monoclonal antibody produced by Dr. Susan Hockfield at Yale University which labels a subset of
neurons in the cat visual system.

title: Rearing rats in a germ-free environment eliminates their odours of individuality,
abstract:
To test the hypothesis that commensal bacteria influence the urinary odours of individuality, we
collected urine from PVG and PVG.Rl male rats born by Caesarian section and reared in a germ-free
environment. Urine from the germ-free rats was not discriminated in a habituation-dishabituation
test, while urine from males kept in a conventional animal house could be discriminated. When the
germ-free rats were moved to a conventional animal house their urine was discriminable. These
results provide the first evidence that the :MHC and commensal bacteria interact in the production of
the unique individuality signal in the urine of congenic rat strains. Similar interactions between
animals and their commensural bacteria are responsible for producing other social odours.

authors_raw: Heather A. Schellinck, Valerie C. Rouse & Richard E. Brown
title: The use of operant conditioning methods to study discrimination of complex social odours in rats,
abstract:
Using an habituation-dishabituation task, Brown, Singh & Roser (1987) showed that the urine of rats
which differed only in the A region (Class 1) of the Major Histocompatibility Complex (:MHC) was
discriminable by other rats. To further this research, we constructed a small animal olfactometer so
that operant conditioning techniques could be used to test the ability of rats to discriminate between
odours. Preliminary results suggest that the procedure is appropriate for demonstrating the ability of
rats to discriminate between the odours of two individual male rats of an outbred strain; the odours of
congenic strains of rats and human odours. However, inconsistencies in the results would suggest a
slack of stimilus control and modifications are now being made before further hypotheses are tested.
The nature of these hypotheses will be discussed.

title: Vocal communication systems of Dendroica warblers.
abstract:
The songs of birds play an important role in intraspecific communication, though mechanisms
involved remain poorly understood. Song use in Dendroica warblers suggests different songs
serve different functions: Males use A songs when advertising for mates and B songs when
interacting with other males. A and B songs differ structurally and are associated with different
singing behaviours (slow, repetitive, stereotyped vs. rapid, alternating, variable.) This unusual vocal
system presents an opportunity to study underlying mechanisms, including the neural organization of
singing behaviours, the control of switching between singing behaviours, and the process of learning
songs and their appropriate uses.

authors_raw: Elizabeth M. Coscia, Dennis P. Phillips, and John C. Fentress
title: Moan, groans, screams, and whines, was what she heard from behind the blinds,
abstract:
The development of the wolf (Canis lupus) vocal repertoire was examined in a litter of wolf pups
during the first 35 days of life. Audio and video recordings were collected from inside a den located
within a 3.8 hectare wooded enclosure. The vocalizations recorded were analyzed spectrographically
to trace changes in acoustic content with postnatal age. The neonatal sounds varied in hannonic
structure, fundamental frequency, duration, frequency modulation, temporal patterns, and time of
first appearance. Hannonically-rich vocalizations present on day one were seen to lower in
fundamental frequency with increasing age. Other, often non-harmonic vocalizations, were less
common during the first two weeks but by the end of the first month had acoustic structures
consistent with their being precursors to adult vocalizations.

authors_raw: Catherine L. Ryan and Richard E. Brown
title: The modification of neonatal ultrasound vocalizations in therat by nore_pinephrinea~onists and
abstract: anta~onists. Infant murid rcxlents emit ultrasonic vocalizations (UV) to guide maternal behavior. Although it has been reported that lesions to the noradrenergic (NE) system do not alter the frequency of UV emitted by rcxlent pups, we investigated the relationship between NE activity and UV using drugs which act as NE agonists or antagonists. Rat pups were tested at 4, 8, and 12 days of age. Five groups of pups were given a single s.c. injection of one of two doses of clonidine (alpha 2 agonist) or propranolol (beta blocker) or saline. Twenty minutes following injection, activity levels and isolation- induced ultrasounds were assessed using an ultrasonic microphone and a computer compatible ultrasound digitizer. Significant alterations in UV were detected. These results suggest a role for NE in the manifestation of UV and indicate that measurement of neonatal UV can be a sensitive tool for detecting perinatal perturbations of the NE system.

authors_raw: Sandra L. Wright and Richard E. Brown
title: Importance of paternal care in mice when required to fora~e for food.
abstract:
Studies of parental behaviour in rcxlents generally provide the animals with ad lib food and water in a
warm environment No studies have examined parental behaviour when environmental conditions
mimic the real world, i.e. food must be foraged and temperature changes accommcxlated. In this
experiment we examined the male's contribution to the pup's development in mice (Mus musculus)
when wheel running was necessary to obtain food. Two groups of mice were compared: solitary
dams with litters (sire absent) and paired dams with litters (sire present). Paired dams weaned more
pups than solitary dams but there were no differences in pup weight from birth to weaning. Both
groups showed parental care in the light phase and wheel running in the dark phase of the light:dark
cycle. However, paired dams alternated parental care and running with the sire. When the sire ran,
the dam was on the nest and when the dam ran the sire was on the nest These results suggest that the
benefits of paternal care will be more evident in extreme environmental conditions than in the standard
laboratory environment.

authors_raw: Richard E. Brown and Lynda A. Wisker
title: Diet and Genetic differences influence maternal odour preferences of infant mice. Olfactory signals communicate information about the species, sex, reprcxluctive condition, individuality and maternal state of an animal (Brown, 1979, Adv. Study Behav .. 10, 103-162). The
abstract:
subjects were 16-19 day old mice of two congenic strains (C57BL/6J and B6-ARK-H-2k), which
differ only in the major histocompatibility complex on chromosome 17. We tested the preferences of
these mice for maternal odours of lactating females of the two strains on two different diets. Pups
were attracted more to odours from lactating females of the same strain and on the same diet as their
mother to lactating females of the other strain or those on the other diet. These results indicate that
pups may "imprint" on different components of the maternal odour. Dietary information so learned
may be used to direct food choices and genetic infonnation to influence the choice of social partners
and mates.
2 9. Maria Legerstee
Communicative Imitation in mother-infant and stranger-infant dyads during the first year.
This study focused on the development of imitation between infants, their mothers and female
strangers, and how these behaviors of the participants changed over time. Eight infants were
observed longitudinally during the first year of life in natural interactions with the adults. Five
behaviors were measured of the interactants: Mouth Open, Eye Open, Lip Protrusion, Smiling,
Negative Face and Vocalizing. Lag sequential analyses were performed for each dependent variable.
The z-score calculations were subsequently adjusted for the effects of autocorrelations. Significant
imitative patterns for all behaviors occurred for the infants and adults at various points throughout
development, although the vocalizations of the infants suppressed those of the adults. However,
imitative development was not uniform. Peaks in imitative development occurred between 9 and 23
weeks for both infants and adults and between 34 and 45 weeks for infant and mother only. Log
linear analyses revealed that significantly more imitative responding took place between mother
and infant, than between stranger and infant. Differential imitative responsiveness occurred as early
as three weeks for mouth open and vocalizations, and significantly more smiles of the mother were
imitated throughout development. The study reveals that imitation is an intergral part of infant
communication and illustrates the usefullness of this paradigm for investigating the development of
imitation during the first year.

authors_raw: Jenny Ryon, Richard Brown, and John Fentress
title: Bringing home the bacon: food provisioning within a captive pack of coyotes (Canis latrans)
abstract:
Social behaviour in a pack of coyotes was observed for six years. This report concerns pup and
related feeding by adults. All adult members of coyote packs feed young. Some members also feed
pregnant and lactating females and yearlings. In our study, feeding began just before the birth of
pups and continued for about 24 weeks. One adult male (Obie) was the only individual to feed the
mother as well as the pups in the two years pups were born. The mother, another adult male and
adult female also.fed the pups in these two years. Obie and the mother but not the other adults also
fed the yearlings. All males were vasectomized before the mating season of the third year, thus no
further pups were born. Obie continued to feed his mate (the mother in past years) at the appropriate
time of year (i.e., just before the expected time of birth, as measured from the time of mating). He
also fed his "pups" from previous years. He continued this pattern of feeding for four years
(until his death) despite the fact that one of the male "pups" he fed had become dominant to him and
one of the female "pups" had become dominate to his mate. The mother also continued to feed
older off spring in the absence of new pups. We present data for six years and discuss these with
respect to the stimuli which activate food provisioning within coyote packs.

title: Agin~ andAttention
abstract:
This study examined age differences in attention and the relationship among different tasks previously
used to measure attention. Sixteen community-dwelling older adults (X age= 61.1) and 16 young
undergraduates (X age= 19.4) were tested on three attention tasks: Treisman's serial search task,
Eriksen's distractor task and LaBerge's attentional range tasks. For all tasks, stimuli were arranged
on an imaginary circle in order to eliminate any effects of retinal eccentricity. In Treisman's task the
old showed slower search rates than young which suggests that the old were slower to shift their
attention than the young. Both the amount of interference and the shape of the interference function in
the Eriksen task was similar for both young and old indicating that there was no age difference in the
size of the attentional 'beam'; the probe data from the LaBerge narrow task also support this
conclusion. In sum, the results show that older subjects are able to control the size of the attentional
'beam' as well as young subjects, but they may not be as efficient at shifting their 'beam' as the
young. •

title: Context effects on discrimination of complex tones, As part of a research program examining the effects of small integer frequency ratio relations on memory for unfamiliar (microtonal) sets of tones, discrimination of nine complex tones (tones separated and discriminanda differing by one-third semitone) was tested under simultaneous and successive context conditions. The pattern of benefits of context differed from that obtained in absolute judgement studies of the same stimuli. This sensitivity of context effects to memorial demands of the task assists in solving the puzzle of whether benefits of musical relations are sensory or cognitive in origin.
abstract:
3 3. Ray Klein, Glen Berry, Kevin Briand and Barbara D'Entremont
What the eye tells the brain declines with increasing retinal eccentricity: but does it do so differently
for good and poor readers?
Geiger & Lettvin answer this question affirmatively in a recent article in the prestigious New England
Journal of Medicine. In their study normal readers were more accurate at reporting letters near
fixation than dyslexic readers, but at greater eccentricities the dyslexics were better. This pattern
survived several methodological improvements imposed by psychophysically minded psychologists
(W. Dember et al), suggesting a basic difference in the eye-brain interface. Close scrutiny of both
studies reveals several methodological flaws, which leave wide open the possibility that the dyslexic
and normal readers use different attentional strategies. Following closely the task used by Geiger &
Lettvin and Dember, but improving upon their displays to minimize the possibility of strategy
differences, we seek to provide a more definitive answer to our title. Other research bearing on the
nature of dyslexia may be described.

title: "Great Expectations" and other tales of attention and set.
abstract: Delayed crossing the Atlantic Ocean

title: Sensitivity or criterion: do ERPs enhance our understandin~ of the issue?
abstract: This interdisciplinary study was concerned with a central issue in visual attention research: does advance knowledge of the spatial location of an impending stimulus facilitate its detection by enhancing early perceptual processing (Posner et al., 1978; 1980) or does expectancy affect decision criteria for responding (Shaw, 1984)? Both event-related potentials (ERPs) and RT performance data were collected in three conditions: simple RT, choice RT, and a second discrimination task that involved the manipulation of stimulus-response likelihood. The RT data show costs and benefits of cuing for the simple, choice and the likely condition of the third task, while the least likely stimulus condition of the third task does not show a cuing effect when validly cued, a replication of Klein and Hansen's (1987) finding of spotlight failure. The onset of ERP enhancements were compared both across the three conditions and between the likely and unlikely conditions of the third task and will be discussed in relation to the issue.

authors_raw: N.A. Phillips, J.F. Connolly, G.A. Eskes, and C. Mate-Kole
title: Recognition and recall: Late ERP components in patients with Alzheimer's Disease, MID, alcohol- related disorders and normal controls. This is a presentation of proposed research that will employ a late positive event-related potential (ERP) component ("P300") as an index of memory processing in aged patients with memory dysfunction and controls. Past research has indicated a positive relationship between amplitude of the "P300" elicited by a word upon initial presentation and the probability of that word being later remembered. In phase 1, ERPs will be recorded in response to words presented during an incidental learning task. Subjects will then be asked to recall as many of these words as possible. In phase i, using a slightly different list, subjects will be asked to indicate (recognize) whether the word had been presented during Phase 1. The relationship between "P300" amplitude and memory performance will be examined to determine neurophysiological differences between the clinical groups.

authors_raw: D.P. Phillips, W. Danilchuk, J. Ryon and J.C. Fentress
title: "Cache me if you can", the chuckling chicken cackled, or How Captive Coy Canids Cache Cadaverous Chicks.
abstract:
Behaviors as (arguably) simple as leech swimming, or as (arguably) complex as mammalian
walking reflect control by central motor programs: they are overt expressions of the activity of
neural mechanisms which stereotype a temporal sequence of motor responses. When the sequence
links distinct movements of normally unrelated body parts, there may be evidence of "synactic
rules of action". To date, the only published evidence for such rules in mammals has come from
studies of grooming in laboratory rodents. As part of a much larger study, we have gathered
evidence of a highly temporally structures, protracted sequence of distinct actions used by two
canoid species to cache food for later retrieval. The action sequence was independent of the sex of
the animal, the season in which the observations were made, and the substrate in which the food
was buried. We argue that some features of the action sequence are not constrained by factors
external to the animal. The action sequence may thus be the first demonstration in a large mammal,
in a natural setting, of endogenous rules of action syntax.

title: The Role of Expressed Emotion in Relapse of Schizophrenia
abstract:
Research is examining the role of the emotional climate within families of schizophrenics plays in
the exacerbation of schizophrenic symptomatology has been ongoing for the past 30 years.
Studies in this area have generated a body of literature focusing on the degree of "expressed
emotion" (EE) in families, repeatedly finding a strong relationship between high familial EE and
the subsequent relapse of schizophrenic members. Despite numerous replications of this effect, the
concept of EE continues to provoke controversy. Some recent studies have produced negative
results; variability in approach and methodology have served to obscure the nature of the EE effect.
Fundamental methodological issues involved in the EE controversy and the current status of the
contruct in clinical management of schizophrenia will be presented.

title: A Comparison of the Sequential Processing Abilities of Dyslexic and Normal Readers
abstract:
It has been suggested by Tallal that the phonological difficulties apparent in many dyslexics are in
fact symptoms of an underlying deficit in processing rapidly occurring sequential stimuli.
Perception of spoken language is particularly vulnerable to such a deficit, because many of the
component sounds in speech involve rapid spectral changes over time periods in the tens of
milliseconds range. It is proposed to assess the rapid sequential processing ability of adolescent
dyslexics, using auditory, visual, and motor sequencing tasks. The dyslexics' performance on all
tasks will be compared to that of both age-matched and reading-level matched normal readers of
equivalent intelligence.

title: Twenty years a rat-man:Searching for a Common Thread Amon~theTan~led Web of My Research
abstract: My research on animal social behaviour has been organized over the last 20 years by a series of planned experiments, accidental encounters, serendipidous results and late nights in the pub. In this talk I will attempt to illustrate the common thread in a tangled web of experiments on sexual behaviour, aggression, parental behaviour, olfactory communication, ultrasonic vocalizations, sexual differentiation, hormonal stimulation, developmental differences, mate recognition, behaviour genetics and operant conditioning. The results will examine the characters, setting and, possibly the solution to the elusive question "What am I doing here?". -=-=--

title: A "We" Mindis Bi~~r and Better <:
abstract: Individualism has produced a psychology which gives{i~torical and@.#ial explanatioiof the mind. A non-individualist ontology, grounded in the social relations between individuals and mediated to individuals through social practices (e.g., language), is developed. Wittgenstein's rejection of individualism through his remarks on rules and private language are reviewed. In response to the popularity of mind-brain supervenience models, Burge's counterfactual example of "arthritis" on Earth and twin-Earth is used to show that different mental states are possible when identical brains exist. It is argued that the language--or more broadly, the social relations--of a given community must be used to determine the mental state of an individual.

title: Theorv of Mind in Autismand Psychwathy
abstract:
In this paper, we suggest that two psychopathological syndromes may represent a double
dissociation of two components of a theory of mind. Recent work of autism has shown that
autistics do not show protodeclarative communicative acts, which have been assumed to index the
initial stages of the capacity for representation. Austistics do, however, produce and comprehend
protoimperatives communicative acts, which indicate the expression of emotional states of desires.
Psychopaths do appear to recognize the existence of belief states in themselves and others.
However, current theortical work on psychopathy suggests that the primary problem in
psychopaths is a lack of intense emotional states. This deficit prevents the psychopath from
experiencing through empathy the emotional states of others and of self across time. They are thus
unable to construct a concept of desire. This account predicts that there will be a subgroup of
conduct disordered children who, while performing essentially normally on belief tasks, will show
particulary difficulty with tasks assessing the understanding of desire.

title: Revolutionazy DietPlan: SlimDownandGetEntrained! Applesauce
abstract: The circadian system of hamsters does not typically become synchronized (entrained) to daily presentations even of a highly preferred, palatable diet We asked whether hunger would potentiate the effects of daily.Je~ing schedules on the circadian system. When hamsters' body weights were reduced to 80-,S~,,,% of free-feeding weights, many hamsters showed clear anticipatory increases in activity before the daily feeding times. Some hamsters also showed entrainment on their freerunning circadian rhythms to these schedules. The results suggest that in an appropriate motivational state, hamsters can be entrained by non-photic cues acting directly or indirectly on the circadian pacemaker.

title: Preccwulatozy Mate-~ardin~ in Gammarus lawrencjanus
abstract:
We will tell you a few things you probably don't know about the sex life of the amphipods in St.
Margaret's Bay. At the minimum, this knowledge should make your next visit to tide pools at
Peggy's Cove a little more interesting.

authors_raw: M. G. Yoon and Myonghae Yoon
title: Develo_pmentof Narrative Representation
abstract:
We study how normal children acquire the cognitive competence for mental representation of a
story by longitudinal and cross-sectional observations of their narrative utterances, and detailed
analyses of their linguistic structures and cognitive organizations. Preliminary results on the
following questions will be presented: (1) At what age do children begin to produce narrative
utterances which are relevant to the input story? (2) Which functional categories of the constituent
propositions of the input-text do the children regard important and/or interesting to represent them
in their narrative outputs? (3) At what age do the children become able to narrate the gist of the
story? (4) How do children interact pragmatically with their addresses during their naration (e.g.
scaffolding, presupposition, etc.)? (5) Do the children make their own interpretations (e.g. casual
inference, projection of certain propositional attitudes, imputation of different mental states or
perspectives to the characters in the story, etc.) of the input-text, and thus transform it in a certain

authors_raw: Heather Schellinck and Richard Brown I /
title: ..I''.½ ~ " c€ J-1:5. i'O -,,,_,1'--cr1. U" . (1._l -u..u ~ -
abstract:
GeneticandMicrobiological
Basesof IndividualOdoursin Rats
Both genetic and microbiological factors may influence the ability of animals to discriminate
between individuals by their odours. To determine the role of each, we examined the ability of
Long Evans male rats to discriminate between urine odours from the following pairs of male rats in
an olfactometer: (1) outbred Long Evans, (2) MHC congenics, (3) germfgree MHC congenics,
(4) genti.cally indentical individuals. Discriminable differences were present in the urine odours
used in all four tasks. Analysis of the errors to a critereon of 85%correct discrimination responses
revealed different learning patterns in each task indicating that genetic and microbiological
differences both contribute to their individual odours of rats. When there are no genetic differences
(Task 4) or no microbiological differences (Task 3) discrimination was most difficult; when both
genetics and bacteria differed (Tasks 1 & 2) discrimination was easier. The easiest discrimination
(Task 2) involved the presence of bacteria and a genetic difference at the major histocompatibility
complex (MHC) of genes on chromosome 14.

They have not however, provided any guidance to clinicians or researchers in how to assess pain. Because of the lack of an appropriate theoretical model pain assessment has not progressed as much as one of pain assessment that may provide a strategy for advancing pain assessment 1 ,1\tQ.'· 1 ;:, 'I, '\'ls. /_f (\_ ,J ~ '.'> ' 1, ,~. Importance of Paternal Care for Pup Survival andDevelopment in Monogamous vs. Poly~nous Mice
abstract:
In monogamous species of mammals, males usually show paternal behaviour, while in
polygynous species males are not normally parental care on pup development in a monogamous
species (P. californicus) and a polygynous species (M. musculus) of mouse. Since paternal care
may be shown only when females need to leave the nest, we designed an experiment in which
wheel running is necessary to "forage" for food. This 2 x3 mixed factorial experiment has two
levels of parental care mixed factorial (male absent vs. male present) and three levels of foraging
(wheel running for food; free food plus wheel running; free food and no wheel). We measured
litter weight, parental behaviour and number of revolutions run per hour in each group in both the
light and dark phase of the L:D cycle. This talk will focus on species differences in pupweight and
pup swvival under the six conditions and discuss species differences in paternal care.

authors_raw: James L. Potvin and S. Nakajima /Qu,~...,,~ Lt l-c-t
title: /---i:A..,~) h'.,-t( Neural and Behavioural Effects of Domoic Acid, an Amnesic Shellfish Toxin, in the Rat l
abstract:
Among those who were poisoned by shellfish containing domoic acid, 13 persons suffered from
varying degrees of memory loss. To study the mechanism of the amnesic effect, domoic acid was
injected into the cerebral ventricle of 53 rats. They showed spike discharges in EEG and loss of
pyramidal neurons in the hippocampus. They were also impaired in learning a radial-arm maze. In
the animals previously trained on the maze task, domoic acid interfered with relearning. The
memory deficits following the ingestion of tainted shellfish seem to have resulted from lesions in

title: Split Personality and Identity Crisis in a Subject with Sensational Legs
abstract:
If a part of a nervous system can be turned on in two quite distinct ways by the hapless
experimenter, how does the onlooker decide which of these two is the proper route to activation?
Suggestive comments will be made about one way to arbitrate on this dilemma, while taking a
fresh look at a previously misinterpreted neural system. The results indicate provisionally that in
order to arouse this subject, a properly pitched word in the ear beats the more traditional hand-on-
the-knee approach.

authors_raw: Bradley W. Frankland, Annabel J. Cohen
title: Effects of Tonality on Accuracy and Reaction Time in an Intewlated Pitch Memozy Task
abstract:
Three groups of listeners (N = 12) differing in level of musical training (no, low, and high)
indicated, on each trial, whether the first (standard) and last (comparison) tone of a five-tone
melody were the same or in different pitch. On one-half of the trials, the standard and comparison
tone were identical; on the other half the comparison was one semitone higher than the standard.
The three middle (interpolated) tones of each five-tone melody formed either a major or minor triad
in a key either near to or remote from that of the standard, thus providing four levels of decreasing _
tonality relation with the standard. Accuracy increased and reaction time decreased with increasing
tonality relation between the interpolated tones and the standard, producing significant linear trends
for both dependent measures. Both measures demonstrated flatter functions for the group which
had no formal musical training. These results are consistent with the application of Krumhansl's
tonal hierarchy for musically trained listeners. The study also confirms the use of reaction time for
measuring the mental representation of melodic structure. (Work supported by NSERC).

authors_raw: C. I.Beaver and D. E. Mitchell
title: Cortical Areas Mediatin~ Behavioural Recovro: Followin~ EarlyPeriods of Monocular Deprivation in Kittens
abstract:
Previous work in the laboratory has shown that following termination of 6 weeks of reverse
occlusion of kittens that had been monocularly deprived until 6 weeks of age there is a surprising
change in the visual acuities of both eyes. Although the visual acuity of the initially deprived eye
improves substantially during the period the vision of the eye deteriorates rapidly while that of the
initially non-deprived eye regains almost normal acuity. As of yet, the mechanism mediating these
changes is still unknown. Lesions of areas 17 and 18 have been carried out to determine the
location of the mechanism. Outcomes and their meaning will be discussed.

title: Visual Attention Bias in Trait Anxiety
abstract: In the last decade there has been a blossoming interest by some cognitive psychologists/researchers investigating information processing models in various affective states. The studies carried out so far have been rather convincing at showing how individuals with various affective states differ from each other and from normals in the way they process information. The differences have been observed at various stages of the information processing continuum, be it at the perceptual, interpretive or recall/response level. This line of research can assist in our understanding of these disorders and may have implications in their treatment. I will talk about an empirical research to investigate biases at the visual attentional/perceptual stage of information processing in high and low trait anxious individuals.

authors_raw: A. Cohen, B. Frankland, M. Lamoureux, D. Dunphy
title: Items and Orders in Melodic Trackin~: The NeXT Step
abstract: Listeners indicated the serial order of 8 tones in 36 sequences representing all combinations of 4 8- note scales and 9 serial orders. Accuracy was higher for scales with wider frequency ranges, for serial order with simpler contour changes, for sequences in which scale did not vary from trial to trial and for listeners with musical training. These effects are discussed from the perspectives of concatentative versus rule-governed accounts of sequential learning. The experiment also represents successful application of the NeXT computer in a serial tracking task . ------·=:rG=::::.. ..-,,,;asz;:::·

title: LookMom! No Malke in Co~itiveland
abstract: One of the interesting but poorly explored questions in ethology is how we code behaviour. What features do we attend to, what are our biases, and how do observation profiles emerge during development? In this talk I offer some preliminary explorations. First, a brief comment on work being done with Rita Anderson at Memorial (wolf watching). Secondly, confessions from sabbatical voyeurism at Oregon ("spot" watching). With Mike Posner and Mary Rothbart I scored video tapes of adult and infant attentional movements. Preliminary analyses of "attention runs" suggest parallels to ethological evaluations of patterned animal performance. If McDonnell-Pew cooperates, Posner, Rothbart and I plan to extend this research line.

authors_raw: Jill A. Goldman, Elizabeth M. Coscia, and John C. Fentress
title: Wolf ear movements: A Measure of Maternal Responsiveness
abstract:
We examined communication between a mother wolf and her pups in the den. Previous work has
shown that although neonatal pups emit a diverse repetoire of sounds during this period, the
mother rarely responds by vocalizing (Coscia, 1989, unpublished). These findings prompted our
investigation of pup vocalizations which might elicit other forms of behavioural response. A 180-
hour videotape database gave us the opportunity to observe mother and pups' behavioural and
vocal activity during the first 5 postnatal weeks. Mother's responses were coded for both ear and
body movements. Toe data suggest that most pup vocalizations do not elicit an overt behavioural
response. However, the majority of mother's ear movements do occur while the pups are active
and vocal. Toe latter has given us reason to believe that the mother's ear movements represent a
form of monitoring which is used to decipher which pup vocalizations are acted upon.

title: Concept Development in Preschoolers
abstract: It has been shown that preschoolers organize their concepts thematically (e.g. dog-bone), but the presence of a novel label biases them to look for a taxonomic relationship (e.g. dog-dog). Fifty- six three-year-old children were tested to determine if a general labeling pretreatment would also bias them to respond taxonomically. A picture was placed in front of the child and they were asked to "find another one that is the same as this one" from a taxonomic, thematic or irrelevant choice. In the novel label conditions, the child was asked to "find another X that is the same as this X", where "X" was a nonsense word. Toe labeling pretreatment consisted of asking the children to label various pictures. Four groups were formed (pretreatment-labeV-no label, no pretreatment- label/-no label). Results showed the novel label group made more taxonomic choices than the no label group only when there was no pretreatment. When there was a pretreatment, the no label group did as well as the novel label group. This supports the argument that language provides a taxonomic constraint on children's interpretation of word meanings.

title: NoPain NoGain: Lookin~ back at Pain. How ~ood were the ~ains? Treatment strategies for pain abound. Toe efficacy of these strategies is well documented: major review papers tell us this! Unfortunately, most of the reviews are flawed. In ten minutes, I will (1) review problems with the way researchers synthesize knowledge, (2) revive the adage, "garbage in, garbage out"-as it applies to methods of data collapsing, and (3) present a proposal to implement what I have facetiously called, " A Best Evidence Critical Appraisal Meta-Analytic Synthesis"**

authors_raw: B. Rusak, H.A. Robertson, W. Wisden and S.P. Hunt
title: Gettin~ into your Genes: It's not always worth the Fos
abstract:
Last year, we found that light stimuli cause increased levels of the Fos protein to appear in cells in
the hamster circadian pacemaker, the suprachiasmatic nucleus. We have since found that this
increase results from altered gene expression in response to light input This altered gene
expression occurs at the time of day when light can shift the circadian clock, but not at other times
of day when light is ineffective in shifting the clock. Toe results imply that altered gene expression
is intimately linked to the mechanism that resets the circadian clock. These findings may provide a
novel approach to the identification of the genes responsible for running the circadian pacemaker.

title: Timin~ is Transient Stimulus Events by Cortical Auditory Neurons in the Cat
abstract:
My lab is engaged in a long-term study of the temporal grain with which the spike activity of
auditory cortical neurons can represent the time structure of sounds. Last year, we talked about
why these cells are unable to encode, in spike times, stimulis periodicities in excess of about 100
Hz. In this year's presentation, we show that in response to transient stimuli, many cortical cells
discharge spikes with a precision in the range from a few hundred migQseconds to a few
milliseconds. We show that this response precision can suppon a temporal resolution in the range
from about a millisecond to a few tens of milliseconds. Interestingly, this is the grain of temporal
processing lost after lesions to the homologous region in man.

title: The Ability of the Rat to Discriminate Human Urine Odours
abstract: Genetic differences at the major histocompatability complex (MHC) contribute to individual differences in urine odours of both rats and mice which can be detected in discrimination tasks. Discrimination experiments with dogs have suggested a genetic connibution to human body odours. Operant conditioning techniques were used to determine the ability of rats to discriminate human urine odours from pairs of boys which differed in genetic relatedness: unrelated, brothers, fraternal twins, indentical twins. All animals were able to discriminate between the two odours on all tasks. Analyses revealed no diiference in task difficulty. It was concluded that rats were not attending to genetic cues in the odours but were relearning the discrimination tasks each day based on urine concentration, metabolites in the urine or other variables.

title: Persona Grata: Fragments for a History of the Concept of Person
abstract: The criteria by which personhood is ascribed to an object or being have changed dramatically from primitive through civilized cultures. In the present paper I can only highlight some of the more interesting transformations and try to place them in their historical context

title: The Si~ificance of Maternal Feedback on Children's Line:µistic Development
abstract:
To date, the role of the linguistic environment in facilitating language acquisition is unclear. In an
attempt to resolve this issue, the present study examined one particular aspect of input, maternal
sensitivity to the grammatically of the child's utterance. The purpose of the present study was two-
fold The first issue was to investigate whether mothers provided differential feedback towards
their child's grammatical and ungrammatical utterances. The second issue was whether such
maternal feedback had any influence on the child's subsequent utterance. Nineteen children were
observed with their mothers in naturalistic conditions at two age levels, 24 months of age and 36
months of age. Transcribed conversations between the mother and child were analyzed, and the
results indicated that mothers were indeed sensitive to the grammaticality of the child's utterance.
In addition, the results suggest that the children were influenced by their mother's immediate
feedback and often reformulated their subsequent utterance to reflect this understanding.

title: Musical andVisual ProcessinJi in Film
abstract:
The present study examines whether processing of musical soundtracks exploits general of
modality-specific resources. Subjects received either a film + music, film-only or music-only
presentation for 20 min and subsequently completed a respective 25-item recognition test. In the
film + music test, recognition excerpts were either unchanged, had a new musical track, had a new
visual track, were mismatched visual and musical tracks, or were entirely novel. Music-only and
film-only tests used one modality from the film+ music test tape. Subjects distinguished old from
new material in both modalitites in both single- and double- modality response patterns confirmed
that processing film music did not compete for visual resources. Intermodal effects were also
apparent.

title: Can Auditmy Freguency Control the Direction fo Visual Attention?
abstract: Tones of fixed frequency (250, 650 and 2500Hz) of frequency glides (900-400Hz and 700- 1200Hz) emanating from straight ahead had no effect on reaction time to luminance targets above or below fixation. This suggests the the phenomenal correspondance between pitch and location does not involved reflexive control of visual orienting mechanisms. Recent studies manipulated the relationship between the direction of the frequency glides and the likelihood of the visual target appearing above or below fixation. The results indicate that subjects can shift their visual attention in the congruent direction within about 500ms while incongruent shifts are not observed with 990ms.

authors_raw: C.L. Ryan and R. E. Brown
title: The Effects of Y ohimbine on the Copulat01y Behaviour of Older Male Rats
abstract:
The present study was designed to determine whether age related deficits in copulatory behaviour
in male rats could be reversed by the alpha 2 antagonist, yohimbine, a reported aphrodisiac.
An acute dose of yohimbine was administered to eight 15 month old male rats, who had
previously demonstrated deficits in copulatory behaviour compared to that of eight 6-8 month old
male rats. The copulatory behaviour was assessed in a 20 minute trial with a receptive female.
Y ohimbine altered copulatory performance and tended to shift the behaviour of the older
males towards that of the younger age performance. The drug also caused a decrease in 22kHz
post ejaculatory vocalizations. This may reflect changing arousal levels or a specific effect of the
drug on ultrasonic vocalizations.

authors_raw: Anastasia Droungas and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: In Search of Parametres that Yield a Pure Explicitly Unpaired Safety Si!iDal
abstract:
As a result of Pavlovian Explicity Unpaired (EUP) conditioning, whereby a CS and shock US
occur on separate trials, the CS becomes a safety signal. We have shown that a CS trained with a
variant of not the EUP procedure ( a brief CS singly alternated with shock with a short intertrial
interval) functioned both as a safety signal AND as a fearful stimulus. We argued that the CS
functioned as a safety signal because, during conditioning, it was negatively correlated with
shock; and as fearful stimulus because, during conditioning, it was associated with the fearful
conditioning context. The current experiment explored parametric variations of EUP aiming at
establishing conditions which will yield a "pure" EUP safety signal.

title: In Search of Inhibition of Return
abstract:
Events in the visual field often attract attention as indicated by a nearly immediate facilitation for the
processing of nearby stimuli. In contrast, Posner and Cohen discoverd a delayed inhibitory effect
which begins about 300 ms after a visual event if attention is not maintained at the location of that
event. This phenomenon, known as "inhibition of return", serves to decrease the probability that
attention retwns to a previously attended location. This talk will focus on the experimental
conditions necessary for producing this inhibitory effect

title: Numerosity as a Perceptual Continuum: Howto ~et by WithoutActually Countin~
abstract: Cognitive and developmental psychologists are preoccupied with counting, especially of smaller numbers, perhaps up to the "Magical Number Seven". This may be due to the fact that people can "subcept" such numbers and count them even after a brief presentation. As a result, many animal psychologists concentrate on counting with small numbers as well. We have data that show that pigeons can discriminate among small numbers of elements in the way that they typically do among different values on other sensory continua: They provide orderly gradients of discrimination, for example, with 2+, 3+, 4+, 5-, 6- and 7-. They cannot however, respond differentially to adjacent small numeroisty values, such as 2+, 3-, 4+ and 5-.

authors_raw: J.H. Brandstatter, I. A. Meinertzhagen, S. R. Shaw
title: Floatin~ Synapses
abstract: In a continuation of last year's project-the disassembly of afferent photoreceptor synapses following photo-degeneration-we have examined the effects of the loss of receptor terminals on a second major class of synapses in the lamina of the fly's eye, the feedback synapses of one of the monopolar cells, L2, back upon the receptor terminal. A first and for us, very surprising finding was the presence of "floating synapses", presynaptic ribbons separated from their membrane sites by invagination to form an organelle that is free floating in the cytoplasm of the L2 axon. These floating synapses seem to be a degradation product of feedback synapses. Despite this loss, we found no decrease in synaptic fequency, but an increase attaining a twofold value of 8d, suggesting the occurrence of reactive synaptogenesis. L -=

title: Orientin~ of Visual Attention Percpetion of a visual scene can be spatially selective in two senses. First,~ adjustments of gaze direction can be made to control which regions of the visual field are processed by the sensitive fovea and its associated neural machinery. Second,~ adjustments can be made in which specific objects or regions are selected ( in the absence of gaze changes) for preferential treatment. Overt and covert visual orienting can be controlled exon~nously by features of the visual scene as well as endo&enously by the observer's expectations and intentions. Imagine the 2x2 matrix implied by this classification. In this talk I will concentrate on the cells representing endogenous and exogenous control are quite different. This might be interpreted to imply that two different attentional systems are involved. Alternatively, a single attentional system might bring different resources or strategies to bear depending on the nature of the control signal.

authors_raw: Noel B. O'Regan and S. Nakajima
title: Reinforcin~ Characteristics of Brain Stimulation Altered by Rece.ptor:=Specific Dopamine A~nists and Anta~onists
abstract:
The involvement of dopaminergic system to the generation of the reinforcing effect of brain
stimulation was examined. Eight rats were trained to press a bar for hypothalamic stimulation and
injected intraperitoneally with agonists and anatagonists selective to either D 1 or D2 subtypes of
dopamine receptors. Quinpirole (D2 agonist) reduced the threshold for self-stimulation while
raclopride (D2 antagonist) and SCH 23390 (Dl antagonist) increased it. SKF 38393 (Dl agonist)
and physiological saline solution had no effect These results suggest a critical involvement of
dopaminergic system with D2 as well as Dl receptors in the reinforcing effect of brain stimulation.

authors_raw: D. Mitchell, M. Ozier, J. McCabe
title: Reco~nition of Videotape Sports; Coaches andPlayers Compared
abstract:
Expert and novice coaches and players were presented 5-second samples of a ten-minute video
exerpt of a basketball game they had seen, along with samples taken from elsewhere in the same
game. The samples were of three types: moving samples, still photos of the video material, or still
photos taken from a view point different to the video. The subjects' task was to indicate whether
the sample had occurred in the ten-minute video or not The results indicated that coaches were
more able to recognize the samples than were the players, and that three types of display differed in
difficulty, the moving displays being recognized by all groups better than the stills.

authors_raw: K. G. Bina, M. Wilkinson and B. Rusak
title: Daily Variation in Muscarinic Bindin~ Sites in the Cortex andHypothalamus of Golden Hamsters
abstract:
Circadian variation in the binding of (3H)N-methyl scopolamine ((3H)NMS), a hydrophylic
muscarinic receptor antagonist was studied in the hypothalamus and cortex of the Golden Hamster
(Mesocricetus auratus) using a novel micropunch technique which selectively detects only cell
surface receptors. Intact and suprachiasmatic nucleus abated (SCNX) animals maintained in light
dark cycle (LD) 14:10 were killed every four hours starting at lights on and binding assays
performed on hypothalamus and the cortex. Binding assays were also performed on intact animals
maintained in constant darkness (DD) every four hours starting at circadian time (CT) 0. The intact
LD animals showed a significant daily rhythm in (3H)NMS binding in the cortex with highs
occurring 12 hours after lights on and lows at lights on. No significant rhythm was detected in the
hypothalamus. The rhythm in the cortex was abolished in the SCN-ablated animals and in animals
maintained in DD. The data suggest that photic information mediated through the SCN or the
pacemaker property of the SCN is responsible for this rhythm. Autoradiography ((3H)NMS;
2nM, 3 weeks exposure) clearly revealed specific binding in the SCN and surrounding
} periventricular region.
'#::::'.'. Cathy Ryan and Richard Brown
~ Mcxiification of Neonatal lTitrasonic Vocalizations in Rats by Adrenergic Drugs
There is some confusion regarding the role of the noradrenergic (NA) system in regulating neonatal
rodent ultrasonic vocalizations (UV). We decided to systematically investigate the role of NA in
the production of these infant distress vocalizations.
Hooded rat pups received a single injection of one of four adrenergic drugs: propanolol,
yohimbine, prazosin, or clonidine at 4, 8, 12, or 16 days of age. With the exception of clonidine,
all drugs depressed isolation induced UV. Clonidine enhanced vocalizations with its effects on UV
being age-dependent The role of the NA system in the manifestation of these findings.

authors_raw: Chris Moore, David Furrow and Lorraine Chiasson
title: MentalstateLan~a~ in Mother-ChildCommunication
abstract: The acquisition of a mental state language has been assigned some significance in the development of a theory of mind. For example, Olson ( 1988) has argued that without means of representing such attitudes, the child would never be able to acquire and understanding of propositional attitudes. However, little is known about the conditions under which mental state language is acquired. In this paper, we report data from a longitudinal study of 19 mother-child pairs in communication when the child was 2 and 3 years of age. All occurrences of maternal and child utterances containing a mental term were coded for function. The results show that while the overall use of mental terms by the mother when the child was 2 years correlated with the child's use at 3 years, particular maternal functions appeared to be especially important. Specifically, mothers who used mental terms in open-ended directives to their children at 2 years has children who used mental terms more at 3 years. We argue that mother's use of open-ended directives serves to direct their children's thought rather than action, and that such direction is important for fostering a sensitivity on the pan of the child to the existence of mental life.

title: Express Saccades
abstract: Fischer and colleagues (e.g Fischer and Breitmeyer, 1987; Fischer and Ramsperger, 1984) have reported that human eye movement latencies to a peripheral target are unusually brief (lOOms - 120ms) when a fixation point is removed shortly before target onset This new phenomenon is called 'express saccades'. It has also been reported that it is not the offset of the fixation stimulus per se that permits express saccades, but rather the offset of any stimulus ( even a parafoveal one) that is attended. This observation appears to provide a strong link between attention and the eye movement system: attention must be disengaged before a saccade can be inititated. But the conclusion is questionable since the studies which 'manipulated' attention told subjects to attend to a peripheral stimulus without providing any objective evidence that this instruction was followed. We report a series of experiments which confirm the express saccade phenomenon, explore variables such as temporal spatial (un)certainty which may mediate the effect, and examine the role that attention may play in its occurrence.

title: Bilateral Ablation of the Forebrain Enhances the Optomotor Res,ponses in Goldfish
abstract: The topographic pattern of visual projection from the eye to the brain in goldfish can be experimentally induced to undergo two types of dramatic reorganizations: (1) the filed compression following partial excision of the optic tectal lobe, and (2) the retention of original topographic polarity by tectal tissue following reimplantation after 180 rotation. We study the functional significance of these two types of neural reorganization in the goldfish by various behavioural tests. One of the innate visual behaviours we use is the optomotor response. It is not easy to elicite a reliable optomotor response to a well localized moving stimulus in normal goldfish. About two weeks after bilateral ablation of the forebrain in goldfish, however, we find that the operated fish tend to follow the moving visual target with remarkably enhanced consistency. Thus, the forbrain seems to exert a distracting influence on the optomotor response generating mechanism in normal goldfish.

title: C-fos expression in the visual pathways of dark-reared kittens following initial exposure to light
abstract: The anatomical and physiological development of the central visual pathways of frontal eyed mammals is influenced strongly by early visual experience. In fact, the organization of central visual structures may remain close to the level that existed at birth in animals deprived of any visual input However, upon subsequent exposure to light the anatomical and physiological organization of these structures is altered in a dramatic fashion. We have demonstrated that one of the family of immediate-early genes (c-fos) is expressed in visual cortical (but not subcortical) neural structures shortly after such animals are first exposed to light. This suggests that this gene and possibly other proto-oncogenes may play an important early role in mediating the anatomical changes that are induced by early visual experience.

title: Pigeons discriminate uniform from mixed arrays of visual elements One property of an array of visual elements is the degree of uniformity -- the extent to which the elements are all the same, or mixed. Pigeons were trained to respond to uniform arrays of two kinds of elements, and not to respond to mixed arrays of the same elements. We obtained orderly gradients that reflected the degree of uniformity (or mixture). This discrimination is sensitive to the discriminability of the elements. It can be transferred between different sets of elements following appropriate training procedures.

authors_raw: Dennis P. Phillips and Jack B. Kelly
title: Physics and brain design, or what rats tell us about where god went
abstract:
wrong
By comparison with cats and monkeys, rats and other small mammals are very poor at
localizing sounds in space. The most recent accounts of this disparity are couched in
terms of the ethological demands on the species, or in terms of the correlation between
localization acuity and the horizontal width of the species' visual streaks or areae centrali
(since this width determines the auditory localization acuity required to bring the target
into the part of the visual field with greatest acuity). These accounts in no way explain
why small rodents are so poor at localizing sounds: they simply point to correlates of that
performance. Jack and I measured the ability of rat cortical auditory neurons to encode
interaural time differences, which are a major cue for sound location. We found that the
dynamic range of this code was the same as that seen in cats and primates; this despite the
fact that rats have much smaller heads, and therefore will only ever encounter very small
interaural time disparities. We suggest, then, that for rats, there exists a mismatch
between the behaviorally-relevantrange of interaural time differences and the range of time
differences that the animal's brain encodes. This may be one reason why small mammals

authors_raw: are so poor at sound localii.ation
title: Hiroshi Abe, Benjamin Rusak and Harry Robertson Photic induction of Fos protein in the suprachiasmatic nucleus is
abstract: inhibited by NMDA receptor antagonists Exposure of hamsters to light can induce c-fos immunoreactivity in cells of the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), which is a dominant pacemaker in the mammalian circadian system. Excitatory amino acids (EAAs) have been implicated in both the entrainment pathway by which light synchronizes circadian rhythms and in the induction of c-fos in other parts of the nervous system. We examined the effects of pre-treatment with MK-801 and ketamine which are non-competitive N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonists on the induction of Fos-like immunoreactivity (Fos-lir) in cells in the hamster SCN. Pretreatment with these drugs blocked photic induction of Fos-lir in a dose-dependent manner in the rostral SCN and ventral portions of the caudal SCN, but not in a discrete region of the dorsolateral SCN. These results suggest that the NMDA-type of EAA receptor may be involved in the photic induction of Fos-lir in most of the SCN but not in one portion of the SCN.

title: Betcha Bob's E~ is better than the bruited ~ by a bundle The traditional anova procedures is badly flawed so far as type-I error-rate is concerned, leading to a dramatic loss of power ~ as the numerator degrees of freedom of the variance- ratio increases, given a fixed NL( µj - µ_)2 162 . There are also serious ambiguities involved in setting ~ for a pre-chosen .r(µj - µ_)2. These problems are resolved if Rodger's (1967-) method is used. That sets rejection rates for null contrasts at Ea when they are true and at E~ when they are false by the pre-chosen amount g 6..['f;;2j·The amount of treatment effect claimed after analysis can be justified by maximum likelihood if E~ has been chosen wisely.
Development of early interlimb coordination
abstract: Recently, increasing attention has been devoted to the application of dynamical theories of motor coordination to infant motor developmenL A number of studies indicate little synchrony in early movement patterns but a significant increase over the first 6 months. A recent study by Thelen and colleagues found the legs to be coupled in the form of compensatory limb movements at 6 weeks of age. However, as yet, the developmental trend of compensatory limb movements has neither been documented for the legs nor has its existence even been explored in the anns. The present study looks developmentally at these two types of motor coordination (i.e., synchronous and compensatory movements) in both arms and legs in order to understand better the nature and ontogeny of early interlimb coordination.

title: Sodium Amytal memory testing: Use in prediction of post-surgical
abstract: amnesia The intracarotid sodium amytal procedure is used worldwide to determine lateralization of speech (Wada, 1949) and memory (Milner et al., 1962) prior to temporal lobe resection for epileptic seizure control. Jack et al. (1988) have advocatedinjection of sodium amytal directly into the posterior cerebral artery (PCA), rather than the internal carotid artery (!CA), for the selective examination of memory functions. They argue that more direct inactivation of the posterior hippocampus would improve prediction of post-surgical amnesia; the increased risk associated with catheterizing the PCA makes this a controversial suggestion. In addition, Milner (1972) and Rausch et al. (1989) claim that using the standard ICA injection, filling of the PCA is not necessary to produce amnesia. We examine the relationship between filling of the PCA (blind angiographic ratings) and recognition memory in 83 epileptic patients undergoing the traditional ICA procedure. We also discuss other factors related to the reliability and validity of the intracarotid sodium amytal memory tesL

authors_raw: The ability to imitate movements, previously demonstrated clearly only among
title: "higher" mammals (certain primates and cetaceans), is shown in a psittacine bird. The animal, a Grey Parrot (Psittacus erithacus) uses its feet, wings, head, beak and tongue in
abstract: unambiguous imitation of human movements.
