authors_raw: S.-A. Clain, S.L. Hargrave, S.A. Sark and D.P. Phillips
title: Studies on the human pure-tone audiogram The normal human audiogram has a U-shape, and it depicts the relative sensitivity of the
abstract: listener to pure tones of frequencies spread across the audible range. It is thought to represent the envelope of the cochlear/neural channels with the greatest sensitivities. We conducted three perceptual experiments to study these channels. First, we obtained audiograms from three normal listeners, and confirmed the U-shaped distribution of audiometric sensitivity. Second, we swdied the effects of continuous wide-band noise masking on the audiogram. These studies revealed that, for any given noise level, thresholds for tone detection were most elevated for frequencies to which the subject was most sensitive in quiet This differential sensitivity means that the sensory channels comprising the audiogram can function independently. Third, using a modem pure-tone masking paradigm, we explored the frequency selectivity of individual sensory channels. The resulting psychophysical "tuning curves" were strikingly reminiscent of the frequency selectivity of cochlear nerve fibers seen in neurophysiological studies. Taken together, these psychophysical studies are compatible with the view that cochlear output consists of narrowly-wned channels that function independently.

authors_raw: Carrie L. Johnson, Catherine L. Ryan and Richard E. Brown
title: Measures of reactivity and CNS development in the hooded rat pup Lewis, Worobey, and Thomas (New Directions for Child Development,~. 33-37) report
abstract: a relationship between early measures of reactivity and later health status in human neonates. Reactiveness of the neonate at 2 days of age is predictive of the robustness of the 18 month old infant. We will present data illustrating the relationship between measures of reactivity (ultrasonic vocalizations, von Frey hair reaction, and open field testing) and measures of robustness (weight and neurobehavioural maturation) in the Long-Evans rat pups. The aim of this study is to determine the predictive value of these early measures of reactivity in the rodent for later health status. This could then be used as a model for screening environmental factors for human teratogenic potential.

title: Memory complaints before and after temporal lobectomy This clinical study identified clinical outcome variables that influenced memory complaints in surgical candidates with temporal lobe epilepsy
abstract: Right-handed adult epileptics (N = 4 7) with medically intractable seizures were assessed both pre-operatively and one year after a left or right temporal lobectomy. The Memory Observation Questionnaire measured self-report of memory complaints. The delayed Paired Associates Test and the Rey-Osterrieth Figure measured verbal and nonverbal memory. The results showed that memory complains diminished post-operatively, but this decline was less striking in patients of average or higher W AIS-R Full Scale IQ. Regression analyses revealed that dosage of anti~pileptic medications, seizure frequency and depression were predictive and post-operative memory complaints. Objective memory scores deteriorated after the surgery depending on side of the resection and sex of the patient Together these findings suggested that most epileptic patients hold a positive impression that their memory function improved after temporal lobectomy, an impression that appears to be influenced by positive surgical outcomes rather than actual performance.

authors_raw: C.L. Ryan, R.E. Brown and A.M. West
title: Is the measurement of isolation cries a sufficient indicator of distress?
abstract: It has been proposed that neonatal rodent ultrasonic vocalizations (UVs) can be used to screen drugs for particular psychopharmacological profiles and human teratological potential. Previous literature proposes that an increase in UV rate should occur following administration of a drug that is stressful, while a decrease in UV rate characterizes a drug with anxiolytic potential. We will present data which challenges this interpretation. We have found that the prototypic anxiogenic compounds lithium chloride (LiCl) an~ corticotropin releasing factor (CRF) fail to increase the rate of neonatal vocalizations as would be expected from the standard experimental interpretation. Rather, our data suggests that in evaluating the psychopharmacological profiles of drugs in the neonate, a change in UV rate alone, may be the significant factor and that the direction of this change cannot, by itself, predict the affective properties of the drug.

title: Effects of exposure to a map on accuracy of distance estimation
abstract: In a study of cognitive mapping, 30 Dalhousie University freshmen first drew a map of their campus. Two-thirds of the subjects then briefly studied a printed map of the campus, and one-third studied a map of SL Mary's campus. Then, all subjects estimated distances between locations on the Dalhousie Campus. Of the subjects who had previewed the Dalhousie map, one-half were asked to remember that map, while the other half was asked to remember their drawn map. It was hypothesized that exposure to the Dalhousie map combined with instructions to consult this map would lead to the greatest sensitivity in the estimation task. Although mean slopes and correlations followed this pattern, the differences were not significant There were however significant differences in the kind of imagery used in the distance estimation task: the group who had nQ1 previewed the Dalhousie map reported significantly greater use of imagery of locomotion and less use of a map-like image. These data are consistent with the notion that multiple representations of large-scale space can be independently consulted in spatial problem solving.

title: Things that go jump in the dark Compared vernier and step displacement acuities in human foveal vision. When test and reference stimuli were of the same contrast, vernier thresholds were lower than step thresholds. For opposite-contrast stimuli, vernier thresholds increased four-five fold but step thresholds by very little. Further evidence was found demonstrating the difference between spatial and temporal visual mechanisms. Preliminary data obtained in a cat subject are also shown.
Wonder if smoking releases dopamine in crabs too? Perfusion of nicotine into isolated thoracic ganglion of crab, caused an increase in the
abstract: frequency of bursts from the ventilatory neural pattern generator (CPG). Similar action was not elicited by acetylcholine nor was it blocked by curare. Dopamine elevated the burst frequency of the CPG. Domperidone, a D2 specific dopamine antagonist had a reversible depre~t effect, whereas, SKF-83566. HCl a D1 specific antagonist had no effect The excitatory action of nicotine could be blocked by Domperidone but not SKF- 83566. HCI. Dopamine appears necessary for the 'generation and maintenance of the rhythm of the CPG. Nicotine is known to release dopamine from dopaminergic neurons in higher vertebrates and may be acting via a similar mechanism in crabs too.

title: The shocking truth: Fear-evoking stimuli acquire their properties independently of one another
abstract: It has been popular in the fear conditioning literature to assume that predictive stimuli compete with each other to signal a fear-eliciting event. We provide "shocking" new evidence that this claim is untrue under many conditions: Contextual and temporal predictors of footshock evoke the same level of anticipatory fear in rats (in the form of conditioned freezing) regardless of whether they have supposedly been made redundant by the presence of a more predictive discrete tone signal.

title: About the 'aboutness' of intentionality's intentions': Can we find a meaning for 'meaning' in the causes of 'becauses'
abstract: Over the past few years I have been approaching the concept of intentionality from a variety of directions, both in my own independent work and in collaborative work with others including Valerie Bolivar, Tim Juckes and Chris Moore. Yet, despite an increase in my understanding of the nature of intentionality, I still find it a somewhat mystical concept In the present paper I will try to de-mystify intentionality by focusing on its major defining attribute - 'aboutness' - and by trying to provide a causal-materialistic reduction of this attribute. Even if unsuccessful, it is my hope that this attempt will - at least - put some greater light on the meaning of intentionality, which I believe is a concept of central importance for the ultimate integration of the bio-psycho-social sciences.

title: Interaction between 01 and 02 dopaminergic receptors in self- stimulation of the lateral hypothalamus in the rat Previous studies in our laboratory have shown that the reinforcing effect of brain stimulation can be attenuated by either selective D 1 dopamine antagonist or a selective D2 antagonist, and enhanced by D2 agonists but not by a D 1 agonisL Rats with electrodes implanted into the lateral hypothalamic area were ttained to press a bar for a train of pulses with variable frequencies (12 - 200 pulses per s) and a frequency-response curve was plotted. A D2 agonist quinpirole (2.0 mg/kg ip) increased responding when injected alone, but failed to show this effect when injected after a DI antagonist SCH 23390 (0.04 mg/kg, ip). SCH 23390 shifted the frequency-response curve to a higher frequency range regardless of whether it was injected alone or after quinpirole. These results suggest that the enhancement of reinforcing effect by a D2 agonist manifests itself as long as D1 receptors are activated by dopamine normally present in the rat brain but not when they are blocked by a D 1 antagonisL These findings are consistent with the reports that these subtypes of dopamine receptors work synergistically.
abstract:
Sandra L. Wright
Neuromotor maturation and the ontogeny of ultrasonic vocalizations
in the California mouse (peromyscus californicus)
Ten female and eleven male P. californicuspups from 8 litters were individually marked,
weighed and tested for ultrasonic vocalizations (UVs). UVs, locomotion, surface
righting, negative geotaxis, acoustic startle reflex and eye opening were recorded from 2 to
30 days of age. UVs at 37, 42, 47 and 52 kHz were digitized and recorded by computer
and an event recorder program which was simultaneously used to record locomotor
behaviour. Results were analyzed by sex of pup and litter size. Put weight was
• negatively correlated with UVs (r=-.58, p<.05). UVs decreased as pups aged (p<.01).
Females vocalized more than males (p<.01) and vocalizationsincreased for both males and
females after they spent more than 180 sin coordinated movement (p<0.01). There was
no effect of litter siu on UVs. After the auditory startle reflex was shown, UVs decreased
significantly (p<.01). Although there was no effect of eye openingon UVs, pups from
litters of two opened their eyes earlier than pups from larger litters {p<.01). Males
opened their eyes earlier than females (p<.05). These results indicate that P. californicus
has a pattern of UV development that is related to weight, age, sex, development of
coordinated movement and auditory startle reflex. Surface righting, negative geotaxis and
eye opening were not related to the developmentof UVs.

authors_raw: Steve Shaw, Kathy Kokic and Don MacIntosh
title: Ears on insect knees Crickets carry a pair of directionally-selectivehearing organs on their forelegs, which they
abstract: use to home in on the calling songs of potential mates. They are distantly related to cockroaches, which have a simpler organ that until recently was believed to respond only to vibration, but which we showed last year also responds sensitively to sound. The roach subgenual ("below the knee") organ is particularly interesting in that it forms a conceptual bridge to explain the evolution of the more elaborate, pure auditory cricket's ear from an ancestral pure vibration receptor. We have found, unexpectedly, that the roach ear is also strongly directional. According to the standard physical theory applied to insect hearing organs, possession of directionality implies the presence of two sound input pathways to the organ. In crickets, the two entry points are an external tympanum on the leg, and internal air tubes (tracheae) connected to openings (spiracles) on the sides of the body. Only the tracheal pathway is obvious in roaches, with no evident tympanum. Attempts to pinpoint how and where sound gets into the knee will be described, along with tests that demonstrate that roaches can respond to sound, albeit feebly, given an appropriate behavioral assay.

title: Identification, starting at the top Viewing the same object from different angles results in different retinal images. We are
abstract: able to recognize these different visual inputs as the same object This is known as object constancy. When line drawings of familiar objects are presented to subjects, the time to name the object increases linearly for objects rotated up to 120 degrees from the vertical. This function suggests that during object identification, the input is being normalized to the upright, until a match is found in memory. Biederman (1989) has suggested that the effects of orientation are due to finding the top of the object In order to test this hypothesis, valid and invalid precues of object orientation will be presented prior to the display of the object to be named. If the effect of orientation is in finding the top, as Biederman has suggested then the valid cue condition should show less of an effect of orientation than the invalid cue.

title: Temporal auditory processing deficits in developmental dysphasia The most comprehensive, if somewhat controversial, account of developmental dysphasia is Tallal's. Briefly, she argues that the speech reception deficit is due to an auditory temporal processing disorder: the disordered mechanism is thought to be cortical in locus, and to affect the perception of both linguistic and nonlinguistic sounds. The most compelling demonstration of the impairment lies in the inability of these children to discriminate the order of two brief (75 ms) tone pulses if the duration of the silent period between them is less than 100 to 300 ms, a task which normals perform with near-perfect accuracy for inter-stimulus intervals as small as O ms. The intended connotation of the term "temporal processing" is, however, far from clear in Tallal's published work. By the same token, nothing is known about the generality of any deficit across auditory perceptual dimensions that have their origins in different forms of time-related or time- dependent sound processing. In this talk, I will present sketches of two of a long series of experiments, based on modem auditory neuroscience, that we have planned to address these lacunae.

authors_raw: A.I. Cohen, D. Dunphy, M. Lamoureux and B. Frankland
title: Battery includes music Musical stimuli engage a variety of sensory, perceptual and cognitive processes. Yet,
abstract: none of the numerous neuropsychological tests in existence examines these processes. Drawing upon tasks used in research in the psychology of music, we devised such a test It examines discrimination, memory, frequency direction, tonal structure, meaning, and preference. Datafromtwo sets of college students are presented showing high agreement on test items. The potential value to neuropsychology is discussed.

authors_raw: Richard Brown, Thad Murdoch, Will Moger and Paul Murphy
title: Hormonal and experiential factors regulating paternal behaviour in
abstract: male rodents Our current knowledge of the hormonal regulation of parental behaviour in rodents is based primarily on the maternal behaviour of the rat Because the rat is a polygynous species, in which the male show~s little parental care, the rat model may not be appropriate for the study of the hormones and paternal behaviour in biparental species such as gerbils and California mice. We are dveloping a model of the hormonal and experiential factors involved in the stimulation of male parental care in biparental rodents. I will outline this model and describe the results of three experiments on hormones and male parental behaviour based on this model: (1) changes in testosterone and prolactin levels across the reproductive cycle in male rats and gerbils; (2) hormonal responses of male and female rats and gerbils to one hour of pup replacement following four hours of pup removal; (3) the effects of prolactin on the parental behaviour of virgin male gerbils.

authors_raw: Myong Yoon and Myonghae Joy Yoon
title: Children's reasoning for prediction of human actions: Developmental studies We study young (2-5 years old) children's cognitive development with respect to their abilities to make appropriate predictions of a protagonist's actions and to explain their own judgements. Four brief movies are designed to test (1) "Simple Desire Reasoning", (2) "Belief/Desire Reasoning", (3) "Descepant Belief Reasoning", and (4) "False Belief Reasoning" (Wellman & Woolley, 1990; Pemer, 1990) as follows: [a] Let a child (the subject) assume an active role of 'explainer' who tells his/her addressee (the experimenter) what is going on in a test story enacted in the movie that the child has been watching (instead of being passive observer of the test situation presented by the experimenter). Then the experimenter asks the child to predict what the protagonists in the movie will do. [b] Video-recording of all utterances and communicative gestures produced by the chidren and the experimenter during the entire discursive organizations as well as for their lexico-syntactic structures and semantic cohesion. These analyses will reveal not only the cognitive abilities of the children for different types of reasoning but also their pragmatic competence in communicating their thoughts and emotions at various stages of their develoment [c] Longitudinal studies with individual children (2 to 5 years old) to denote the individual patterns of developmental changes through consecutive tests.

authors_raw: P.C. Kind, C.J. Beaver and D.E. Mitchell
title: The effects of monocular deprivation and reverse occlusion on the expression of CAT-301 in the LGN of kittens
abstract: Recent attempts to reverse the severe behavioural effects of an early period of monocular deprivation (MD) in kittens by subsequent imposition of a period of reverse occlusion (RO) have revealed a very diverse range of eventual outcomes from good vision to both eyes to either monocular or binocular amblyopia. We have examined the expression of CAT-301 (a monoclonal antibody that recognizes a proteoglycan associated with the surface of Y-cells in the cat visual pathway) in the visual pathways of animals subjected to certain of these rearing regimens and have found that many regimens of RO, and especially those that do not favour one eye, lead to a substantial diminution of CAT-301 immunoreactivity in all laminae of the LON.

title: Attention and express saccades Our initial studies confirm the observation that removing a fixation stimulus shortly before the appearance of a visual target elicits unusually rapid ("express") saccadic responses even when target direction is uncertain. The popular explanation for express saccades is that attention must be disengaged before a saccade can be initiated, and that offset of any attended stimulus (pre-) disengages attention, thus permitting express saccades. Studies directly assessing the relationship between attention and express saccades will be presented.
abstract:
29. • J.A. Goldman and J.C. Fentress
Does the squeaking wolf always get the response?
Research at the Dalhousie Animal Behaviour Field Station (DABFS) is currently
examining communication between a mother timber wolf and her pups, during the first 5
postnatal weeks. Previous work on activity inside the den, has shown that although the
mother rarely responds to her pups' vocalizations with overt behaviours, she does monitor
their vocal output by exhibiting subtle changes in ear direction. Now, the pups'
responsiveness to the mother's vocalizations is under investigation. A 460-hour
videotape database permits examination of vocal and behavioral activity inside the den, as
well as, vocalizations which occur outside the den. Pup behaviour is coded for response
to the adult 'Squeak', a common social vocalization. Preliminary data suggests that not
all 'Squeaks' elicit the same response.

title: Neuropsychological assessment of subcortical dementia in Huntington's Disease Individuals with Huntington's Disease and Dementia of Alzheimer's Type (DAT) were examined on a neuropsychological battery designed to assess frontal, memory, and language abilities. The purpose of testing both DAT and HD subjects was to examine the distinctiveness of cortical and subcortical dementias and, by doing so, clarify the possible role that the basal ganglia pathology has in the mediation diverse set of cognitive proc~. This comparison is especially important in light of the lackof direct comparisons made between HD and DAT. It was hypothesized that deficits in HD would be characterized by focal frontal deficits and subtle declines in memory while DAT would be madced by language difficulties coupled with generafu.edmemory deficits. Preliminary analysis indicates the existence of qualitative differences between HD and DAT which appear to reflect divergent pathological processes. It is proposed that cognitive changes in • HD and DAT support the position that dementia may be differentiated in terms of a cortical/subcorticaldimension.
abstract:
3 1. Kazue Semba
What does the shape of neurons tell you about what neurons do?:
Morphology of cholinergic and non-cholinergic cortically projecting
neurons in the rat basal fore brain
Neurons appear in different shapes. Whatever factors might be involved for these various
shapes to form during development and then change during normal aging (as well as in
pathological conditions), neuronal morphology often provides interesting insights as to
functions of individual neurons. Previously we have shown that there are two
physiological subtypes of cortically projecting neurons in the basal forebrain, and that
these are also morphologically distinct, and have hypothesized that one subgroup is
cholinergic and the other non-cholinergic. In the present study, we test the morphological
aspect of this hypothesis, using retrograde tracing, single cell injections, and
irnmunohistochemistry.

title: Pigeon umwelt: Is a picture worth a thousand words? Operant chamber techniques have yielded a diversity of information regarding various facets of pigeon vision. More recently complex photographic material in the form of slides, depicting sundry categories such as trees, cats, cars and people, has been employed in category discrimination tasks. In such studies the assumption is presumably made that the photographs are perceived by the Pigeons as representative of the three-dimensional objects or scenes that they depict Are there enough salient dimensions present in a slide photograph to allow it to function as a suitable representation of the Pigeons environment and, if so, could it be used to explore the complexity of the Pigeon Umvelt? Some recent work in this area will be presented.
abstract:
3 3. Elzbieta Pyza
The effect of quipazine • a serotonin agonist, on the circadian rhythm
of locomotor activity and the serotonin level in the brain of the house
cricket (Acheta domesticus L.)
The rhythm of locomotor activity in insects results from the function of an endogenous
pacemaker system and entrainment of this system by environmental factors (light)
changing during the 24 hour period. The aim of this study was to examine the role of
serotonin (5-HT) in the regulation of circadian rhythms. Results obtained indicated that
the circadian system controlling locomotor acitvity rhythms in the house cricket is
composed of two oscillators which act jointly (in DD - constant dark),or separately (in
LL constant light) and that 5-HT takes part in the regulation of overt rhythmicity. In LL,
injections of quipazine affected the phase of two components of the rhythm or changed
one of the components present in activity into the second one (changes of the rhythm
period). In DD, quipazine administrations caused only phase shifts of the rhythm which
were phase-dependent Quizapine injections decreased also the 5-HT concentration in the
cricket brain which may be a result of a negative feedback process triggered by direct or
indirect activation of 5-HT receptors.

title: Symmetry and orientation normalization in the recognition of naturalistic stimuli Tarr and Pinker (Psychological Science, 1990) showed that mental rotation is used in recognizing misoriented assymetrical patterns, but not symmetrical patterns, using novel stimuli. They inferred that object-centered representations code spatial relations among features on one dimension only. The generality of this finding was assessed by retrospectively analysing three experiments' worth of latencies to name misoriented drawings of common objects. For the first block of trials, symmetrical and asymmetrical objects showed equivalent effects of orientation between upright and 120°. In contrast, effects of orientation were significantly greater for asymmetrical than symmetrical objects over three subsequent blocks of trials. These results are consistent with the hypothesis that object-centered representations code object features along a single dimension.
abstract:
Nameera Akhtar and Philip J. Dunham
Comprehension strategies of young children
Previous research (Shatz, 1978) has indicated that young children are biased to respond to
language with action. This study attempted to replicate this finding in a group of 64 24
month olds. These children heard 30 prerecorded sentences differing in syntax presented
through a speaker in a robot (thereby eliminating extralinguistic cues such as gesture and
gaze). Their responses were videotaped and coded as Action, Informing, or Other.
Results indicated that the children were not biased to respond with action, and were able to
discriminate among the five sentence types. The data suggest that this discrimination
may be based on surface markers such as the presence of an action verb, and the mention
of an object present in the immediate context

title: The secret to creating life Do humans equate spontaneous movement with being alive and having internal states?
abstract: Do children think the same way as adults on these issues? Recently, researchers have been grappling with the effects of movement patterns on animacy and intentionality attributions. Unfortunately, most of these researchers have used objects familiar to the subject and hence they are subject to biases associated with previous experience. In the present study, we examine the role of endogenous movement in the attribution of animacy, motivation and internal states to unfamiliar objects. Subjects in grades 1, 4 and 7 and university were asked to respond to the following statement "Tell me a story about what you have just seen", after viewing a computer generated display consisting of two circular stimuli exhibiting three types of movement Results indicate that age, gender, as well as movement patterns all contribute to differences in these attributions.

authors_raw: Mark Farrell and Raymond M. Klein
title: The effect of regional compactness upon region discrimination time is modulated by oculomotor programming
abstract: Podgomy & Shepard (1983) demonstrated that reaction time to determine if a target is on or off an attended region decreased with increasing compacbless of that region. Six experiments explored the nature of this compacbless effect and its relationship to visual attention. Subjects viewed a 3 x 3 grid in which there were shaded and unshaded regions. In four experiments their task was to determine a) if a target letter appearing in the shaded or unshaded region was a Tor L, orb) if a target stimulus was presented in the shaded or unshaded region. Eye position monitoring was used to ensure that subjects were not shifting gaze to inspect the display. The compacbless effect was not obtained in any of these experiments. In two further experiments eye position monitoring was NOT employed and the pattern reported by Podgomy & Shepard • a negative correlation between RT and compacbless • was observed. The RT/compactness relationship discovered by Podgomy & Shepard appears to be mediated by the programming of the oculomotor system and does not reflect the distribution of endogenously conttolled attention.

title: Integration of kinematic information in emotional judgments Adults rated the happiness/sadness of 36 short film excerpts of a person whose facial and body movements varied from excerpt to excerpt. There were three different facial expression (sad, neutral and happy as reflected by mouth position and eye focus) and three different body movement patterns (sad, neutral and happy as reflected by position and rate of limb movement) combined factorially. The influence of both sources of information on ratings was highly significant and in the manner predicted. Thus, emotional meaning can be communicated by multidimensional kinematic information. Results are compared with those from preschool children.

authors_raw: Heather Schellinck and Richard E. Brown
title: Discrimination of human male odours by rats is not influenced by the relatedness of the urine donors
abstract: Rats can be trained in an olfactometer to discriminate between the urine odours of MHC congenic rats and mice. They learn these discriminations quickly and remember the information from one daily session to another. Such results indicate that constant odour cues are used to discriminate between these urines (Schellinck, Brown and Slotnick, Anim,Learn,Behay,. in press). To determine if rats can discriminate between human urines, we tested five Long-Evans rats in a go/no discrimination in an olfactometer. Discriminable differences were present in urines from monozyotic twins, dizogotic twins, siblings and unrelated males. Analysis of the erros to criterion over eight 200 trial sessions indicated that the four human urine odour discriminations were equally difficult to learn. Although all test rats were able to discriminate the urine pairs to a criterion of 90% correct within one or more of the eight sessions, only one of the rats was able to maintain this level of performance from session to session. The other four subjects either relearned the discrimination within a session or performed at chance levels on subsequent sessions. When the ability of rats to learn to discriminate between the urine odours of rats, mice and humans is compared, it appears that constant cues are being used to discriminate between the urine odours of rats and mice whereas this is not the case for hwnan urine odours.

title: The effects of play structures on infant initiated imitative play Nonverbal social exchanges or interactions are crucial steps in infants becoming full
abstract: partners in a social communicative context. Studies in the area have shown that imitation is a key element in the development of coordinated joint attention. It is argued that the period in which infants are sensitive to the imitative structures is at 24 month of age. The present study observed 30 nine month olds to investigate whether infants are sensitive to the imitative structures of play at this age. Subjects were randomly assigned to three treatment groups, Synchronous-Contingent, Asynchronous-Contingent and Noncontingent. Infants in the Synchronous-Contingent group engaged in more imitative play while the infants in the other two treatment groups engaged in more parallel play. Being imitated actually encouraged the infants to engage in more reciprical imitative play. Although not statistically different, the Noncontingent infants displayed a tendency to engage in less of both imitative and parallel play. Noncontingent infants were said to be somewhat sensitive to their play structure given that they looked more at the experimenter prior to responding than the Synchronous-Contingent group did.

authors_raw: P.G. Ritvo, JD. Fisk, C.J. Archibald and T.J. Murray
title: Neurological and psychosocial predictors of mental health in patients with Multiple Sclerosis In the initial study of an ongoing project investigating quality of life and psychosocial functioning in MS patients, 89 subjects from the Dalhousie MS Research Clinic were assessed. Scores on the Mental Health Inventory were used as the dependent measure in a multiple regression analysis that included measures of social support, fatigue, responsiveness to stress, physical handicap, neurological disability, duration of disease and disease activity. Results indicate that social support and fatigue are important issues for MS patients. These two factors alone accounted for 44.8% of variance in Mental Health scores of the total sample. Furthermore, standard status meaures of neurological disability and physical handicap were not significant predictors of Mental Health status in MS patients.

authors_raw: K.G. Bina, K. Semba and B. Rusak
title: Localization of cholinergic and nerve growth factor-receptor neurons projecting to the suprachiasmatic nucleus of the rat Circadian rhythms in mammals are regulated by neural mechanisms centered in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the anterior hypothalamus. Recent immunohistochemical findings indicate that both choline acetyltransferase (ChAT), the synthesizing enzyme for acetylcholine, and nerve growth factor-receptor (NGF-R) are present in the SCN. In order to identify the source of this immunoreactivity, a combination of retrograde tracing and immunohistochemistty for ChAT and NGF-R was performed. Following iontophoretic injections of fluorogold into the SCN, in addition to previously described areas, retrogradely labelled neurons were seen in the substantia innominata. the part of the magnocellular basal nuclear complex near the SCN. Some of these retrogradely labelled cells were also single- or double-labelled with ChAT and NGF- R. Unilateral knife cuts directed lateral to the SCN decreased NGF-R immunoreactivity in the ipsilateral SCN, whereas, unilateral enucleation decreased immunoreactivity in the contralateral SCN.
abstract:
4 3. Scott Bishop
The relationship between catastrophizing and negative affect
Two studies were conducted to examine the cognitive-affective correlates of
catastrophizing. Catastrophizing has been defined as a tendency to engage in excessively
negative cognitions related to aversive stimuli such as pain. Study 1 examined the
reliability and construct validity of a self-report measure of catastrophizing cognitions. A
13 item scale, developed for this study was shown to have high internal consistency
(alpha = .90). In addition, the scale showed the same relationship with subcomponents of
depressive symptomatology shown with clinical measures of catastrophizing. Study 2
examined subjects evaluations of the valiance of positive, negative and pain-related words
following a success or failure experience. Results indicated that catastrophizers who
experienced success on a problem solving task rated positive and pain-related words more
negatively than noncatastrophizers. Study 2 also addressed the test-retest reliability of the
catastrophizing measure. The results are discussed in terms of the relationship between
catastrophizing and negative affect

title: Imagination and social understanding It has been claimed that social understanding is achieved not so much through the development and application of a "theory of mind" but through the ability to pretend or imagine self in situations appropriate to another time or person. In this paper, I discuss recent work of ours on social reasoning tasks, in which children's judgements have to be made either from a third-person perspective or from an imagined first-person perspective. Initial results seem to support the idea that imagined or pretend contexts facilitate social reasoning.

authors_raw: J.H. Brandstatter,H.S. Seyan and I.A. Meinertzhagen
title: The dynamic properties of synapses in the fly's lamina In the first optic neuropile, or lamina. beneath the fly's (Musca domestica L.) compound
abstract: eye photoreceptor terminals (Rl-R6) and intemeurons are reciprocally connected. At each afferent synaptic sites a presynaptic ribbon in R-R6 abuts a postsynaptic tetrad containing processes of two monopolar cells, Ll & L2. L2 in tum provides feedback input to Rl- R6 at a synaptic dyad. We have examined both afferent and feedback synapses, in each case following the loss of their main target cells. When R 1-R6 degenerated by photoablation, presynaptic ribbons of feedback synapses separated from teh presynaptic membrane and soon appeared free floating in L2's cytoplasm. Despite the presence of floating ribbons the number of L2 synapses increased twofold during an overlapping period of reactive synaptogenesis. When L1 and U degenerated by lesioning, presynaptic ribbons of afferent synapses were only mod~tely affected with slight decreases in their frequency. Thus many presynaptic ribbons in Rl-R6 outlive their postsynaptic partners. This difference in the reaction of photoreceptor and monopolar cells following the loss of postsynaptic partners, reflects a greater dependence of monopolar cells upon their targets, Rl-R6, than of Rl-R6 upon their targets, L2 and Ll.

title: Psychological sequelae of carbon monoxide poisoning and their
abstract: treatment with hyperbaric oxygen One of the most frequently observed symptoms of carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning is "mental" or "cognitive" impairment. These symptoms often persist even when other medical and neurological symptoms have disappeared. The standard treatment for coexposure is hyperbaric oxygen. If symptoms are minor, or exposure time is short, patients are sometimes treated only with oxygen at normal atmospheric pressure or are not treated at all. There are, however, "hints" that failure to treat even mild cases may result in relatively permanent neuropsychological deficits. Some preliminary data and suggestions for research on the problem will be presented.

title: Cholinergic and aminergic inputs from the mesopontine area to the basal forebrain and thalamus: A hypothesis for brainstem involvement in cortical arousal Researchers of behavioral states have investigated the role of specific direct and indirect cholinergic inputs of the basal forebrain and mesopontine tegmental cholinergic areas in the regulation of waking and sleep. This study examines the contribution of tegmental cholinergic, serotonergic and dopaminergic cell groups in modulating the activity of both thalamic and basal forebrain neurons in cortical arousal. Following multiple injections of both FITC beads into the basal forebrain, and Fluorogold into the thalamus, alternate sections from the forebrain and brainstem were processed for immunohistochemistry with antibodies to visualire cholinergic and aminergic neurons. Injection sites were plotted and alternate brainstem sections were surveyed for the presence of triple labeled neurons.
Non-temporal and temporal effects on the perception of pattern in sequential stimuli
abstract: The analysis of the perception and/or cognition of pattern in sequentially presented stimuli is constrained by non-temporal and temporal factors that limit the number and type of possible temporal patterns. This work will discuss the effects of such constraints on the perception of pattern in the contour (the pattern of up and down) in sequentially presented tones. In particular, a brief review of the role of tonality (essentially, a non- temporal constraint) and the role of short term memory (essentially a temporal constraint) on the perception of pattern in contour will be presented. --------

title: Mood and memory: Open your Ears and close your eyes Usually we remember better if given a category cue to the meaning of the word we are
abstract: seeking in memory than if we are given an alphabetic one. That finding reverses if we are induced into depression before trying to remember. Induced depression results in a dramatic decrease in the potency of category cues to meaningful words. Previous reports have been based upon an experimental design in which words were presented visually and test cues presented visually. The current experiment asked whether the decrease in potency of category cues under induced depression would occur if presentation and test were auditory rather than visual. The loss of meaning as a function of induced depression seems to be specific to the visual modality. The moral is: If you feel down, open your ears and close your eyes. This remedy is not recommended if you feel down while you are driving a car.

title: Injury-induced neuroplasticity in the snail Following a crush to a central pathway in the snail, the nervous system responds by
abstract: regenerating lesioned axons, by rerouting some fibres to contralateral pathwyas and by sprouting new projections from uninjured neurons. Together these responses lead to supernumerary neurites throughout the nervous system. These abberrent projections are gradually lost, and normal projection patterns return by 5 weeks after the lesion. When regeneration is prevented by cutting rather than crushing the nerve, supernumerary elements continue to increase with no return to normal numbers evident after 15 weeks. Together these results suggest that the cessation of sprouting and the retraction of supernumerary fibres are dependent upon successfulregeneration of the injured neurons.

title: Studies of ambiguous pitch: Direction and reaction time measures In line with our interest in the representation of sequential patterns, listeners judged the pitch direction of pairs of randomly selected Shepard tones (synthesized from 10 consecutive octave components with amplitude envelope peaking over central components) from the set of 12 semitones in the octave. Direction judgments were consistent for short absolute distances between tones and were bivalent for distances of 5- 6 semitones. Reaction time data and the pattern of directions preceding ambiguous intervals reveals whether ambiguous pitch is disambiguated by preceding redundant directional patterns.

authors_raw: John Fentress, Valerie Bolivar, Elizabeth Coscia and Wanda Danilchuk
title: The marvels of mutant mice in motion The marvels of mouse movements provide the inspiration for this projecL The small but sophisticated brains of these little beasts enable them to articulate beautifully coordinated actions. These, as other animal (and human) actions, challenge us to think carefully about how mammalian brains achieve their essential balance between isolable operations and the rules of combination in space and time among these operations. We shall treat the troops with our first attempts to employ benign developmental analyses of neurological mutant strains to examine fundamental problems of pattern formation in behavioral organization. The data are preliminary, but you'll love our mice!

authors_raw: Patrick McGrath, G.Allen Finley, Gillian McNeill, Paula Forward and Peter Fitzgerald
title: PARENTAL MANAGEMENT OF POST-OPERATIVE PAIN
abstract:
Although most children's post-operative pain is managed by parents at home, no
studies have examined parental management of the adequacy of their practices. We
will present data on 200 subjects from a survey of parents who have children aged
2-12 years who have undergone surgery and been discharged within a day. Data
will be presented on levels of pain and treatment used for different groups.

title: •THE DEVELOPMENT OF TRIADIC INTERACTIONS IN WOLVES (CANIS LUPUS) AND RED FOXES (VULPES VULPES)
abstract:
Triadic interactions are social interactions that involve three individuals. They likely
require greater social sophistication than dyadic interactions, and may be important
for animals that live in complex social groups and engage in activities requiring
coordination of behaviour. If triadic interactions are adaptive, then they should be
exhibited more often and display a more coordinated form in social canids (wolves)
than relatively asocial canids (foxes). Preliminary analyses indicate that during
ontogeny, wolf pups do become more triadic in their social interactions than fox
kits do. Further analysis will show whether the triadic interactions take on a more
cooperative form in wolves.

title: ORIENTATION EFFECTS DURING OBJECT NAMING
abstract:
Are the effects of orientation on object naming due to finding the top of rotated
objects or in identifying the object? Experiment 1 cued the location of the top prior
to an object naming task. Experiment 2 cued object identity prior to a top-bottom
discrimination task. Experiment 1 showed no influence of cuing, and Experiment 2
showed a benefit of cuing identity. Neither experiment showed a reduction in the
effects of orientation due to cuing. The effects of orientation found in top-bottom
discriminations may be explained as response competition.

authors_raw: Jenny Ryon, Paul Paquet and Richard Brown
title: WOLVES' RESPONSES TO CONSPECIFIC URINE-MARKS
abstract:
We studied urine-marking and responses to marks in a pack of gray wolves (Cm
~ housed at the Dalhousie Animal Behaviour Field Station for five years.
Dominant wolves of both sexes urinated at higher frequencies than subdominant
wolves. Responses included sniffing, over-marking, and licking. The type of
response varied with the sex, age, reproductive status, and/or age of both the
marking and the investigating individual. In adults, marks of female wolves were
investigated more than those of males. The frequency of all types of response for
the marks of adult females followed the same circannual rhythm of urine-marking
frequency, with a peak during the winter mating season. Urine-marks may aid in
reproductive synchrony in some wolves, while suppressing reproductive readiness
in others.

authors_raw: Heather Schellinckl, Cathy Smyth, Richard Brownl,2 & Michael Wilkinson
title: Departments of Psychology 1, Physiology and Biophysics2 and Obstetrics and Gynecology3, Dalhousie University
abstract:
EXPRESSION OF C-FOS IN THE ACCESSORY OLFACTORY SYSTEM
OF JUVENILE FEMALE MICE AFTER EXPOSURE TO MALE-SOILED
BEDDING
Exposure to the urine or soiled bedding odours of novel adult males is known to
accelerate puberty in juvenile female mice. To determine what part of the olfactory
system is activated by these odours, the expression of cf os in the main olfactory
bulb (MOB) and the accessory olfactory bulb (AOB) of juvenile female mice was
examined after their exposure to male-soiled bedding, peppermint extract or their
own bedding. Well defined immunoreactivity was found throughout the AOB of the
juvenile female mice exposed to the bedding odours of adult males for 3 and 12
hours. In contrast, dense fos staining was found in the granular cell layer of the
MOB of mice exposed to peppermint odours for 3 or 12 hours. Of the mice
exposed to their own bedding for 3 or 12 hours, none showed immunostaining for
fos in the AOB; slight or no staining was observed in the MOB. These data indicate
that social odours activate fos in the AOB while non-social odours activate the
MOB.

authors_raw: April D'Aloisio, Mick Sullivan and Fionnula Murphy
title: DYSPHORIA AND CERTAINTY FOR SOCIAL JUDGEMENTS
abstract:
Nineteen dysphoric and 20 nondysphoric subjects read brief person descriptions
that were either high or low in information value, and were asked to make trait
ratings for each person described. Subjects were then asked to indicate their level
of certainty in the ratings they made. Results showed that dysphoric subjects were
less certain than nondysphoric subjects in the trait ratings they made for low
information descriptions. It is argued that, in the absence of detailed stimulus
information, dysphoric individuals may be impaired in their ability to generate
heuristic mental representations of social stimuli. The findings are discussed in
relation to current positions on biased and realistic information processing in
dysphoria.

title: DYSPHORIA AND APPLICATION OF GENDER STEREOTYPES
abstract:
This present study investigated the relationship between mood and application of
gender stereotypes. Dysphoric and non-dysphoric subjects read statements
describing a male or a female target behaving in a manner consistent or inconsistent
with stereotypes for the target's gender. A three-way interaction was found
between mood (dysphoric, non-dysphoric), target sex and stereotype consistency
of behaviour (consistent, inconsistent). Dysphoric subjects rated female targets as
more likely to engage in stereotype consistent behaviour than non-dysphoric
subjects. As a function of limited cognitive resources, dysphoric subjects may have
used stereotypes as a heuristic to reduce the cognitive processing demands of the
prediction task.

authors_raw: Mariana Tihova, B. Tattrie, and Peter Nicholls
title: FREEZE-FRACTURE ELECTRON MICROSCOPY OF RECONSTITUTED CYTOCHROME OXIDASE
abstract:
Eukaryotic cytochrome oxidase (CO) is a multisubunit enzyme spanning the inner
mitochondrial membrane. The size, shape and other gross features of the 3-
dimensional structure of this protein have been obtained only for its isolated crystal
form. The enzyme can be incorporated into liposomes of a defined lipid
composition. Freeze-fracture electron microscopy of cytochrome oxidase vesicles
(COV) was used to study the structure of the reconstituted enzyme. The enzyem
incorporation was characterized by size distribution of the protein "bumps" of Pt/C
replicas of convex and concave surfaces determined using a MCID program
(Imaging Res. Inc.). Ta/W shadowing was used to obtain images with higher
resolution, which allow us to find out some details on the surfaces of the protein.

title: THE ONTOGENY OF ULTRASONIC VOCALIZATIONS IN CNS MYELIN DEFICIENT (JIMPY) MICE
abstract:
We recorded both the ultrasonic vocalizations (UVs), at 65, 70, 75 and 80 kHz,
and motor behaviours of jimpy mouse pups at 2 day intervals from days 2 to 20
postpartum. Jp/Y males vocalized less frequently than their normal male littennates
from 2 to 8 days of age. For both jp/Y and normal mice, the rate of UVs was
highest at day 2 and decreased with age up to day 8, after which UVs ceased almost
completely. Our results indicate that UVs may be useful as an early predictor of
future neuromuscular anomalies.

authors_raw: Chris Beaver and Donald E. Mitchell
title: PATTERN OF C-FOS EXPRESSION IN AREA 17 OF VISUALLY DEPRIVED KITTENS FOLLOWING INTRODUCTION OF NORMAL VISUAL INPUT
abstract:
Last year we reported on the rapid expression of cf os in various visual cortical
areas of dark-reared kittens following their initial exposure to light. Here we focus
on the pattern and time course of this expression visualized in both coronal
secretions and in unfolded and flattened sections of the visual cortex. Data relevant
to two issues will be discussed, namely, the possible involvement of cfos in the
signal pathway that mediates experience-dependent change in the visual cortex
during development, and possible modular organizational principles of the cat
visual cortex.

authors_raw: Dennis P. Phillips, Jamie M. Rappaport and J. Mark Gulliver
title: ODE TO A HAIRDRESSER. WHY SHE SMILES AND NODS AT PARTIES, OR, WHEN "YES" MEANS "I DIDN'T UNDERSTAND A DAMNED WORD YOU JUST SAID".
abstract:
One of the most common complaints of listeners with noise-induced cochlear
hearing loss is that they have difficulty discriminating speech in noisy
environments. Historically, this problem has been attributed to a loss of frequency
resolving power in the cochlea. This is because the impaired frequency resolution
is independently demonstrable, and because it is, in the principle, the kind of deficit
that might prevent the ear from encoding the distributions of spectral energy that
identify the phonemes of speech-a problem that is presumably only exacerbated
by the presence of masking noise. In this talk, we present evidence which (a),
knocked our socks off, and (b) suggests that a defect in temporal, rather than
frequency, resolution accounts for the perceptual difficulty reported by these
patients.

title: DISCRIMINATION BY PIGEONS OF UNIFORMITY AND MIXTURE IN ARRAYS OF STIMULUS EFFECTS
abstract:
Pigeons pecked at a video "touch screen" on which randomized arrays of 36 small
squares of two different colors were presented in various proportions. With
uniform arrays of red or blue elements, responding to the right side of the screen
was rewarded. With all mixed arrays, containing both red and blue elements,
responding to the left side was rewarded. The pigeons provided orderly
proportions of choices in the form of gradients which reflect the degree of stimulus
mixture in the arrays. This discrimination transferred readily to arrays of green and
blue elements, and to arrays consisting of fewer (25) and more (64) elements than
the training arrays.

title: LOCATION OF DOPAMINERGIC SITES CRITICAL FOR BRAIN-STIMULATION REW ARD
abstract:
The locus of dopamine receptors critically involved in the generation of reinforcing
effect of brain stimulation was examined.
Rats were trained to press a bar for the lateral hypothalamic stimulation, and
D 1 or D2 antagonists was injected through the cannula during the operant
responding.
D1 antagonists suppressed responding when injected into a part of the
nucleus accumbens, ventral tegmentum, or their vicinities. D2 antagonist
suppressed responding only when injected into the nucleus accumbens, and not into
any other loci.

authors_raw: Gail Eskes, Morris Moscovitch and Mary McCauley
title: A PICTURE IS NOT ALWAYS WORTH A THOUSAND WORDS: DISSOCIATIONS BETWEEN EXPLICIT AND IMPLICIT MEMORY TESTS.
abstract:
Performance on explicit (e.g., free recall) and implicit (e.g., word stem completion)
memory tests can be dissociated experimentally; implicit tests are said to involve
automatic or unconscious memory processes, while explicit tests require strategic or
consciously-controlled processes. Thus, it has been traditionally asswned that
explicit tasks would benefit from elaborative processing during study
(e.g.,imagery), while this kind of processing would have no effect on implicit
memory tasks. The current study suggests that while imagery does benefit explicit
test performance as expected, using imagery during study can actually reduce
implicit task performance in young adults. In contrast, older adults show reduced
benefits of imagery on explicit tasks, and fewer costs of imagery on implicit tasks.
Performance on some implicit memory tasks may not be so automatic, after all.

authors_raw: Patricia Cole and Werner K. Honig
title: PIGEON UMWEL T II: IS A PICTURE AS GOOD AS A PLACE TO A PIGEON?
abstract:
Pigeons were trained on a simultaneous slide discrimination between colored slides
of various views of the ends of a rectangular room. A control group was trained on
an irrelevant slide discrimination between outdoor scenes. After reaching criterion
on this "2D" task, the birds were released into the actual room previously
represented in slide photographs and allowed to forage. Their behaviour in this
"3D" task suggested the photographic medium may capture real world cures that are
salient to pigeons as well as humans.

title: MEDITATIONS ON MALCOLM X: A SOCIO-PSYCHOLOGICAL ANALYSIS
abstract:
The relationship between individual and social forces in the historical
transformation of society is not well understood. On the one hand, it appears that it
is the action of individuals in pursuit of personal (subjective) goals that produces
social change. On the other hand, it appears that (objective) social forces produce
change. We (Juckes and Barresi, in preparation) have developed a model that
articulates how these two types of forces can be understood jointly to produce
social change. In the present paper the life-history of Malcolm X and his role in the
development of black consciousness in America will be used to illustrate the
usefulness of this model.

authors_raw: Chinglu Li and Ian A. Meinenzhagen
title: IN VITRO STUDY OF DROSOPHILA VISUAL SYSTEM
abstract:
With its much simpler structural and functional organization compared to that of the
vertebrates, the visual system of fruit fly, Drosophila,provides an ideal preparation
forthe studies of synaptogeneseis. Success of in vitro culture of this system can
funher simplify this system, permitting experimental control over spatial and
temporal aspects of its development. By screening different culture conditions, we
• have established primary cultures of eye imaginal discs and its target neuron, the
optic neurons.

title: CUES FOR JOINT VISUAL ATTENTION IN INFANTS
abstract: Joint visual attention refers to the ability to follow another's direction of gaz.e.This project examined the relative importance of eye and head orientation in the establishment of joint attention in infants ranging from 6 to 19 months. In a face to face interaction with the experimenter, infants were exposed to four types of trials in which head and/or eye position varied: H (head only), E (eyes only), H+E (head & eyes match), and H-E (head & eyes mismatch). Results indicate that 6 to 10 month olds do not reliably engage in joint attention based on information from any of the cues while 12-13 month-olds establish joint attention based on head position alone. In contrast, 15 to 19 month olds rely on congruent head and eye position for determining direction of gaze and joining attention. At no age did infants establish joint attention based on eye position alone whether presented in isolation or in the context of the H-E cue. Based on these findings it appears that while head orientation is initially the most salient cue, by 15 months, head and eye orientation play equal roles in the establishment of joint attention.

title: ELECTROPHYSIOLOGICAL INVESTIGATIONS ON THE SUPRACHIASMATIC NUCLEUS IN VITRO
abstract: The suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN) of the hypothalamus house a dominant circadian pacemaker. Our research involves monitoring the effects of exogenously administered neurochemicals on the extracellularly recorded spontaneous firing activity of SCN cells maintained in a brain slice preparation in vitro. In particular, we examine how substances which are thought to be released from the visual projections to the SCN and substances which are intrinsic to the SCN affect SCN cells. Through this research, we aim to characterize the neuropharmacology of the circadian clock.

authors_raw: Karina Davidson, William Chaplin and Kenneth Prkachin
title: USER FRIENDLY STATISTICS FOR EVALUATING RISK FACTORS FOR CHD MORTALITY
abstract:
Large scale studies have relied on tests of statistical significance to index and
evaluate risk factors to coronary heart disease (CHD). Because such statistics are
influenced by sample size and measurement error, they do not provide a clear
indication of the practical implications of risk factors for mortality from coronary
heart disease.
In this paper, we describe an alternative to traditional tests of statistical
significance, Rosenthal's binomial effect size display (BESD), that effectively and
unambiguously communicates the pragmatic importance of risk factors for coronary
heart disease (CHD) mortality. Moreover, the BESD facilitates both the evaluation
of the relative importance of risk factors within one study, and the stability of the
same risk factor in different studies.

title: NAMING FAMILIAR FACES ROTATED IN TWO AND THREE DIMENSIONS
abstract:
Naming latencies have supported mental representations of objects in 3/4 view.
Yet, cells in the monkey temporal gyrus respond preferentially to frontal and profile
views of faces. Are objects and faces represented in fundamentally different ways?
Frontal, 3/4 and profile views of familiar faces were named. Like objects, upright
faces in 3/4 view were named the fastest However, rotations in the picture-plane·
had equivalent effects on latencies for 3/4 and frontal views and much greater
effects for profile views.

authors_raw: Linda M. McAlpine and Patrick J. McGrath
title: A YOUNG CHILD'S DEVELOPING UNDERSTANDING OF PAIN
abstract:
Further advancement in assessment and treatment of pain in children has been
hampered by our limited knowledge of emerging concepts of pain in preschool
children and by their immature expressive language. Methodologies used to
research a child's developing "theory of mind" proved useful in revealing how
young children conceptualize pain. It was hypothesized that preschool children
would progress from recognizing pain in others to imputing to others the emotions
which pain evokes and subsequently to predicting behaviour resulting from pain
experience. This hypothesis was investigated through collection of a two week
language sampling from subjects (30 children, aged 18-76 months) augmented by
data collected during structures play sessions in which the children assume the
characters in pictures and of toy figures who experience pain. Comparisons were
made to the children's developing concepts of internal states in themselves and
others.

authors_raw: John F. Connolly and Natalie A. Phillips
title: EVENT-RELATED POTENTIALS TO PHONOLOGICAL AND SEMANTIC PROCESSING IN SPOKEN SENTENCES
abstract:
In previous studies (Connolly et al., 1990; 1992) a negative-going component
peaking at about 200-250 msec after the onset of the terminal word of a low
contextually constrained (spoken) sentence was observed in conditions which also
elicited a N400 response. The N200 and N400 were found to be differentially
sensitive to phonologically correct masking-N400 peak latency was delayed while
N200 was unaffected. The N200 was hypothesized to be sensitive to the initial
phoneme of the terminal work and a reflection of the acoustic-phonetic process in
the phonological stage of word processing. The present experiment was designed
to explore further the N200 and the N400 elicited by the terminal words of spoken
sentences. Four conditions were employed which elicited the N200 and N400
together, separately and not at all. Results are interpreted as supporting the idea of
the N200 as reflecting an acoustic-phonetic process and the N400 as a process
dependent upon the "outcome" of the process(es) manifested by the N200. Results
for the N200 are also interpreted in relation to the cohort model of spoken word
recognition with particular reference to the concept of recognition-point

title: MEMORY AND AGEING: HOW DOES SEMANTIC PROCESSING HELP?
abstract:
While there is general agreement that semantic processing during item study
enhances later recall, little attention has been directed at investigating whether
different types of semantic processing (e.g., cueing superordinate vs subordinate
properties) are differentially effective in increasing memory performance. The
results of this study suggest that some types of semantic processing can have
differential effects on memory performance. In general, however, processing of
superordinate or subordinate features had similar effects on memory, while words
associated with more elaborative processing were better recalled by both young and
older adults.

title: VISUAL WORD RECOGNITION AND VISUAL ATTENTIONAL PROCESSES IN DEVELOPMENTAL DYSLEXIA: A FAMILIAL CASE STUDY*
abstract:
Reading and visual attentional processes were studied in a family with two known
developmentally dyslexic children. The family underwent neuropsychological
assessment and a series of cognitive experiments. Control group S's provided
normative data. The sets included: Coltheart's reading battery; a test of memory
for visual spatial and temporal information; Olson's component reading skills task;
visual attentional distractor tasks, and 4 separate tests of exogenous and
endogenous control over overt and coven orienting of visual attention. Both
parents and 4 of the 5 children displayed reading disabilities and several similar
problems with visual attentional processes.
* Previously presented at the Rotman Research Institute's conference on
attention (Toronto, March, 1992).

authors_raw: Valerie Bolivar, Kevin Manley, Wanda Danilchuk and John Fentress
title: DEVELOPMENT OF MOVEMENT SEQUENCES IN JIMPY (CNS MYELIN DEFICIENT) MICE
abstract: We examined the ontogeny of grooming and swimming behaviors in mutantjimpy male pups and their littennate controls. The animals were videotaped at 2 day intervals from the 3rd to the 21st day postpartum. Developmental measures included the percent time spent grooming, the number of grooming bouts and interlimb coordination during swimming. Results indicate that the mutants displayed a developmental lag in swimming ability, however, they did spend more time grooming and groomed earlier than littermate controls. Our data confirms the importance of detailed behavioural documentation if we are to understand how gene expression affects motor development

authors_raw: Natalie A. Phillips, C. Charles Mate-Kole, and R. Lee Kirby
title: COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT IN PATIENTS WITH LOWER EXTREMITY AMPUTATIONS SECONDARY TO PERIPHERAL VASCULAR DISEASE
abstract:
Peripheral vascular disease (PVD) refers to disease of any of the blood vessels
outside the heart and accounts for approximately 75-80% of lower-extremity
amputations performed today. Although patients with PVD are considered
neurologically normal, PVD might lead to cerebral ischemia or cerebrovascular
disease. We hypothesized that patients with PVD would show cognitive deficits on
neuropsychological assessment Memory, language function, visuopatial ability,
and problem solving were evaluated in 14 patients with lower-extremity
amputations secondary to PVD and 16 healthy elderly age-matched controls.
Patients with PVD performed more poorly than controls on tests of visuopatial
ability, problem solving and abstract reasoning. It is suggested that the cognitive
status of amputee patients with PVD should be considered in prosthetic
rehabilitation programs.

authors_raw: Donald E. Mitchell and Chris Beaver
title: DEVELOPMENT IN KITTENS BASED ON OBSERVATIONS OF CAT-301 IMMUNOREACTIVITY IN THE dLGN OF VISUALLY DEPRIVED ANIMALS
abstract:
The monoclonal antibody, Cat-301, recognizes a surface associated antigen (a
chondroitin pulphate proteoglycan) on a specific class of neurons (Y-cells) in the
central visual pathways of cats. Cat-301 is first expressed in the dLFN of kittens at
4 weeks of age and adult levels are not reached until 6 months of age, a time course
that is mirror symmetric to the declining ability of the central visual pathways for
experience-dependent change. During the course of experiments that explored
further the experience-dependent nature of Cat-301 expression, we observed that
short periods of monocular deprivation resulted in low overall immunoreactivity in
both the deprived and nondeprived laminae of the dLDN's. This and other findings
from this investigation hold implications for the timing sensitive periods in visual
development.

authors_raw: Dennis P. Phillips and Susan E. Hall
title: SELECTIVE ADAPTATION STUDIES OF CAT CORTICAL AUDITORY NEURONS
abstract: Studied with tone pulses of any given amplitude, neurons of the cat's primary auditory cortex are excited by a narrow range of tone frequencies. Curves plotting response rate as a function of frequency have an inverted- V shape, and are very narrow in relation to the animal's audible range. On the basis of persuasive, if somewhat thin evidence, we once argued that in some neurons, such a curve actually represents the envelope of the response ranges of a number of independent inputs tuned to similar, but not necessarily identical, frequencies. We have now directly tested this notion by obtaining response-vs-frequency functions for tone impulses in the absence and presence of a continuous tonal masker whose frequency was set at the edge of a neuron's effective frequency range. Our data support the hypothesis that the response-vs-frequency function is indeed, for some neurons, the envelope of those for a number of independent, closely-tuned points.

authors_raw: Myong G. Yoon and Myonghae Yoon
title: ACTION DEVELOPS EARLIER THAN ATTRIBUTION OF THINKING OR RETROACTIVE JUSTIFICATION
abstract:
We studied the development of children's understanding of false beliefs in cross-
sectional (3ylm - 5y0m; N=88) and longitudinal (2y8m - 4y6m; N=l 1)
experiments. During 3ylm- 3y6m, 52% of 22 children were able to make correct
predictions such that the protagonist's action would be consistent with the ,
protagonist's false belief. Most (95%) of them, however, were unable to explain
their own correct predictions, and rarely made an explicit attribution of the false
belief to the protagonist. They also showed difficulties in justifying the
protagonist's futile action retroactively, in spite of their preceding successful
predictions of the protagonist's action. During 3y7m - 4y0m, 38% of 22 children
acquired new abilities to explicitly attribute the false belief to the protagonist,
whereas they made only a minor improvement in making correct predictions (61%).
In general, children's ability for a correct prediction of action developed 4-7 months
earlier than those for explanation of prediction, attribution of thinking, or retroactive
justification of the protagonist's futile action.

authors_raw: Elzbieta Pyza and Ian A. Meinertzhagen
title: DAY/NIGHT AND CIRCADIAN RHYTHMS IN THE FIRST VISUAL NEUROPILE IN THE OPTIC LOBE OF THE FLY MUSCA DOMESTICA
abstract:
In the fly's visual neuropile, or lamina of the optic lobe, the frequency of synaptic
profiles changes during the 24 hours period. Under light/dark conditions (LD
12: 12), the number of afferent tetrad synapses formed upon two interneurons Ll
and L2 is higher during the day but numer their reciprocal counterparts from L2
formed back upon receptor terminals is higher during the night. Examination of the
presynaptic ribbon profiles under constant darkness conditions (DD) revealed a
strong circadian rhythm in frequency of feedback synapses and less pronounced
rhythm in frequency of tetrad synapses.
In the present study, the seize of presynaptic ribbon profiles in single EM
sections has been measured at LD 12:12 conditions. Moreover, we have measured
area, perimeter, length, formfactor and diameter of two intemeurons Ll and L2
under LD and DD conditions. The results obtained from this study have shown no
statistically significant differences in the seize of presynaptic ribbon of tetrad and
feedback as well as synapses. In parameters of Ll and L2 intemeurons, however,
there were remarkable differences at all levels oflamina depth between day and
night at LD and between subjective day and subjective night at DD conditions. Ll
and specially L2 intemeurons were bigger during the day and subjective day than
during the night and subjective night.

title: CHOLINERGIC AND SEROTONERGIC MECHANISMS OF RAPID EYE MOVEMENT (REM) SLEEP
abstract:
Cholinergic neurons in the mesopontine tegmentum have implicated in the
regulation of REM sleep. Serotonergic suppression of these cholinergic neurons
has long been postulated on the basis of behavioural studies, but no direct proof
was available. We have investigated serotonergic input to cholinergic neurons
using both physiological (intracellular recordings using brain slices in vitro) and
anatomical (tract tracing, irnmunohistochemistry, and electron microscopy)
techniques. The results were consistent with the suggested role of serotonin in
modulating rapid eye movement sleep phenomenology through its inhibitory action
on cholinergic neurons in the mesopontine tegmentum.

authors_raw: Raymond Klein, Tracy Taylor and Alan Kingstone
title: WHY DO FIXATION OFFSETS FACILITATE SACCADIC LATENCIES?
abstract: When the fixation point is removed 200 ms prior to the appearance of a visual target (gap condition), subjects can initiate saccades within 100 ms. This is so much faster than the typical (when the fixation remains present, overlap condition) saccadic reaction time (RT) that the label "express saccade" has become popular. We will describe some problems with this label and suggest that investigators focus their attention on those factors which act to reduce RT in the gap condition. Our research suggests a 2- component model of the gap effect: 1) motor preparation which can operate in any response modality and is dependent on the foreperiod between any warning signal and a subsequent target, and 2) saccadic disengagement which (normally) depends on removal of the fixation stimulus, operates specifically to reduce saccadic latencies, and may be mediated by the rostral pole of the superior colliculus.

title: CHRONOMETRIC ANALYSIS OF THE EFFECTS OF FAMILIARITY UPON VISUAL ORIENTING: DOES WHAT WE KNOW AFFECT WHAT WE NOTICE, AND IF SO HOW AND WHEN?
abstract: It is often assumed (c<f> Farah) that the system responsible for perception of the visual array is richly interconnected with both a spatial attention system and a system of stored representations. Contemplating this arrangement one of us (Christie) proposed that familiar items should exert a stronger pull on attention than novel ones, because the region in the visual array occupied by a familiar item will be more highly activated due to greater feedback from its stored representation. The relevant empirical literature, which appears to be full of contradictions, will be critically reviewed. The method we have developed to explore our proposal will be described, and the timecourse of visual orienting to familiar vs unfamiliar objects, as seen using our method, will be revealed.

title: DEVELOPMENT AL EMERGENCE OF JOINT VISUAL ATTENTION
abstract:
The present study examined joint visual attention in a conditioned head tum
paradigm in 63 infants from 6 to 11 months of age. Results suggested three distinct
patterns of response, which were developmentally ordered. Older infants tended to
show spontaneous NA, without the need for conditioning. Infants at an
intermediate age showed no spontaneous NA but did learn to match an adult's head
tum given contingent feedback. Finally, a perseverative pattern was demonstrated
by the youngest inf ants who tended to show either a majority of head turns in one
direction or several series of turns in the same direction. These results suggest that
the onset of joint attention, in line with general sensorimotor development, may be
tied to infant's ability to respond flexibly to two separate spatial locations on the
basis of different cues.

authors_raw: Myong G. Yoon and Myonghae Yoon
title: THE DEVELOPMENT OF CHILDREN'S UNDERSTANDING OF FALSE BELIEFS
abstract:
We studied the developmental sequence in the following aspects of children's
understanding of false beliefs: Predictions of a protagonist's action, explanations of
their own predictions, explicit attributions of the protagonist's thinking,
explanations of their own attributions, and retroactive justifications for the
protagonist's futile past action. The results show that a child's ability to make a
correct prediction of a protagonist's action develops earlier than his/her abilities to
explain the correct prediction, to impute the protagonist's mental states, and/or to
justify the protagonist's futile past action: During 3y lm - 3y 6m, 52 % of
younger 3-year-olds made correct predictions of the protagonist's action. Most (95
%) of them, however, were unable to make an explicit attribution of false belief to
the protagonist. They also showed difficulties in justifying the protagonist's futile
action retroactively, in spite of their preceding successful predictions of the
protagonist's action. During 3y 7m - 4y Om, 38 % of older 3-year-olds acquired
new abilities to explicitly attribute false belief to the protagonist, whereas their
correct predictions (61 %) changed insignificantly. Longitudinal studies show that
children's correct predictions of action developed 4 - 7 months earlier than their
explicit attributions of false beliefs to protagonists. This developmental trend
suggests that a normal child's understanding of false beliefs develops gradually
from (a) the 'ego-centric (Level 0) conceptual perspective taking' stage
(characterized by 'incorrect prediction of action and inability to attribute false
belief) to (b) the transitory intermediate stage of 'Level 1 conceptual perspective
taking' ('correct prediction of action without being able to attribute false belief),
and then to (c) the stage of 'Level 2 conceptual perspective taking' ('attribution of
false belief as well as correct prediction of action') during the formative period
between 3 and 4 years of age.

title: DEVELOPMENT AL RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN PRODUCTION AND COMPREHENSION OF MENTAL TERMS
abstract:
Production of mental terms, including belief and desire terms, was studied in a
sample of 14 mother-child pairs longitudinally when the children were 2;0, 3;0, and
4;0. In addition, children at 4;0 were tested for comprehension of the distinctions
between the belief terms 'know', 'think', and 'guess' using a task designed to
assess the understanding of the expression of relative certainty. Children's
comprehension of belief terms at 4;0 was significantly correlated with mothers' use
of belief terms at 2;0, children's use of desire terms at 2;0 and children's use of
belief terms at 3;0. These results reflect individual developmental differences in the
development of belief-desire reasoning and are consistent with a picture of maternal
linguistic influence on their children's developing theory of mind.

title: THE VIEW FROM ELSEWHERE: OR, EVERYTHING THAT YOU WANTED TO KNOW ABOUT YOUR SELF BUT WERE AFRAID TO ASK
abstract:
Instead of engaging in experimental research, philosophers like to explore
theoretical questions by imagining various sorts of possible worlds interestingly
different from our own. Such imaginative worlds can sometimes be used to answer
questions inaccessible to empirical study. One such question that we hope to
explore using this method in a future paper is: How would our self-knowledge
change if we had access to perceiving ourselves from both a first and third person
perspective? The way to approach this question is to imagine that a person could
fission into two identical individuals, as do some other biological species on our
planet, and that this is a typical happening in the adult lives of individuals on this
imagined world What would self-knowledge amount to under circumstances when
you had recently divided in two? In this talk our intuitions into this matter will be
explored.

title: THE PSYCHOSOCIAL AND PHYSICAL PREDICTORS OF ADOLESCENTS' RECOVERY FROM ORAL SURGERY This study expanded medical models of prediction and outcome related to surgery in adolescents. Fifty two adolescents undergoing 3rd molar surgery participated. Adolescents' negative affectivity, expectancies, coping styles and illness behavior encouragement were assessed before surgery. Extent of surgery was assessed by the oral surgeon. Outcome measures included resumption of initial mouth opening, disability and pain. After controlling for extent of surgery, the psychosocial parameters accounted for 16% of the variance in mouth opening and 24% of the variance in disability. Extent of surgery did not predict recovery. Expectancies had an independent and significant association with recovery. Gender effects masked associations between some predictors and recovery.
