Adding pain as a predictor more than doubled the variance explained in disability. Expectancies about recovery should be included in models of adolescents' recovery from surgery. Interventions which modify pessimistic expectancies and improve pain treatment may enhance recovery.

authors_raw: Natalie A. Phillips and Jeannette McGlone
title: FEW COGNITIVE ABILITIES CHANGE FOLLOWING TEMPORAL LOBECTOMY
abstract:
The frequency of clinically significant change greater than one standard
deviation on 14 cognitive neuropsychological tests was examined in 47
patients undergoing temporal lobectomy for medically intractable epilepsy.
The majority of scores (68%) did not change more than one standard
deviation, and 17% actually improved following surgery. Clinically
significant decline in neuropsychological functioning was relatively
infrequent (15%). Data were also grouped into material-specific domains
(verbal versus non-verbal memory, and language versus visuospatial
abilities) to determine how often decline was related to side of surgery in 35
right-handed patients (13 left-temporal; 22 right-temporal). Material-
specific change occurred only after left temporal lobectomy where relatively
more verbal memory scores declined (17%) than improved (8%) and more
non-verbal memory scores improved (22%) than declined (3%). Taken
together, these data indicate that most patients with longstanding temporal
lobe seizures experience relatively little cognitive change following a
temporal lobectomy.

title: RE-EVALUATION OF THE MOST SENSITIVE KNOWN "VIBRATION" DETECTOR
abstract:
In vertebrate auditory systems, basilar membrane vibration has been measured
directly: absolute threshold is reached at displacement amplitudes of -4 x 10-9
meter, i.e. at dimensions that are small on an atomic scale. It has been claimed that
the supersensitive knees of some insects do even better by the large factor of-200
(0.002 nm threshold), raising the question of whether these magnificent animals
have discovered some superior biophysical principle that has been missed in
vertebrate evolution. The lab cockroach is the best of all, but previous students of
its knees have failed to agree on just how sensitive these really are. Further, none
of them calibrated their vibrators directly at the tiny amplitudes with which they
worked, but trusted the perilous procedure of unsupported linear extrapolation
downwards from much larger calibrated movements. Finally, some investigators
delivered stimuli that may have unwittingly included an acoustic component. We
have subsequently shown that the cockroach leg's "vibration" detector is a sensitive
auditory organ, and that this may even be its primary response mode. In light of
this, a new device has been developed that allows the leg's vibration threshold to be
measured directly without acoustic complications. Overlapping calibrations
eliminate the potential problems issuing from non-linear extrapolation. Some
preliminary results will be described.

title: TEMPORAL RESOLUTION IN DIFFERENT FREQUENCY CHANNELS OF HUMAN COCHLEAR OUTPUT
abstract:
Last year, Phillips and colleagues reported that patients with selective high-
frequency cochlear hearing losses evidenced a temporal resolution defect in their
auditory perception. This defect expressed itself as a compromised ability to
discriminate spoken words against a background of noise that fluctuated in
amplitude, a task that requires the auditory system to resolve the speech fragments
that occur in the troughs of the noise envelope. We argued that these listeners were
performing the task using their (normal) low-frequency hearing, and that the low
frequency channels of cochlear output have inherently poor temporal resolution.
To test this hypothesis we have studied normal listeners with the same general
paradigm, and under two listening conditions: (a), normal, and (b), one which
prevented them from using their high-frequency hearing, namely low-pass filtering
of the stimuli. The pattern of responses obtained under the "filtered-stimulus"
condition precisely mimicked those obtained in patients with high-frequency,
sensorineural hearing loss. This made us very happy troopers.
It.· i'f! rii • r

title: CIRCUITOUS CIRCADIAN CIRCUITS
abstract: The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus is the major pacemaker of the mammalian circadian timing system. It is synchronizedby environmental light cues conveyed from the eyes to the SCN directly via the retinohypothalamic tract and indirectly through a portion of the lateral geniculate nuclei called the intergeniculate leaflet (IGL). We have used light-induced expression of the immediate-early gene cfos as a marker for retinal input to the SCN, in order to study which neurotransmitter receptors must be activated in order to convey light information to this nucleus. Evidence from many sources indicates that the NMDA receptor is critical to the photic entrainment process. Nevertheless, neurons in one restricted portion of the SCN continue stubbornly to express cfos under photic control, despite blockade of either NMDA or non-NMDA amino acid receptors. We found evidence that suggested that these cells were continuing to get input indirectly from the IGL, but further tests have refuted this model. Our newer results now suggest that there are two pharmacologically and anatomically distinct direct retinal projections to the SCN. This revised model may also require reinterpretation of the results of several earlier studies.

title: THE ROLE OF THE PREFRONTAL CORTEX IN CONDITIONAL
abstract:
DISCRIMINATION LEARNING
Rats with prefrontal lesions are impaired on conditional discrimination learning
(CDL), a task that requires them to associate one response to a particular stimulus
and a differentresponse to another stimulus. The role of the prefrontal lobe in this
task is unclear. Deficits on CDL could be due to either an inability to learn the
necessary associations between the different stimuli and responses, and/or to a
difficulty in selecting the correct response, given a particular stimulus. The present
study was designed to attempt to distinguish between these two alternatives. Rats
with prefrontal lesions (PFC, n=I0) and sham-operated controls (SC, n=ll) were
first compared using the standard CDL paradigm, in which they were required to
learn to respond to one of two bars, depending on the location of the light stimulus.
We confirmed the impairment of PFC lesioned animals on this paradigm as
previously reported (Winocur, 1991). In the next phase, we attempted to eliminate
the necessity of selecting between the two equally probable response alternatives by
providing only one bar during any one light stimulus. Correct responding (either
pressing the correct bar, or not pressing the incorrect bar) was rewarded. Thus, in
this new procedure, rats were tested for their knowledge of the association between
the light and bar, in the absence of the need to make a choice between the two
available bar press responses. Under these conditions, PFC animals were not
impaired on CDL when compared to controls. Performance averaged over the last
2 days of testing were: 67.4%± 4.39 correct vs 66.49% ± 4.53, PFC vs controls,
respectively). These data suggest that deficits seen in conditional discrimination
learning due to prefrontal lesions are due to a difficulty in selecting between
response alternatives, rather than in forming associations between stimuli and
responses.
(In collaboration with Gordon Winocur)

authors_raw: Tara Fidler and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: EFFECTS OF PREEXPOSURE ON FEAR CONDITIONING TO A CONTEXT
abstract:
Nonreinforced exposure to a stimulus typically makes it harder to condition that
stimulus when it is reinforced. This phenomenon is called latent inhibition. In the
present experiment, which used a 3 X 2 factorial design, groups of rats were
repeatedly, but briefly, preexposed to: (1) the context in which they would
subsequently be conditioned, or (2) a tone in that context, or (3) a different context
Then rats from each of these groups were randomly assigned to two subgroups.
One set of subgroups received several sessions in which the tone occurred and was
followed by a brief footshock. The others received the shock but not the tone.
Conditioned freezing was assessed. Rats that were preexposed to the tone in the
conditioning context and then given tone-shock pairings in that context learned to
freeze in that context sooner than any of the other groups, and maintained a higher
level of freezing there over a number of extinction sessions. This result is
noteworthy because it is the opposite of the latent inhibition usually obtained. We
will speculate about its basis.

title: NUMEROSITY DISCRIMINATIONS BY PIGEONS OBTAINED WITH A TOUCH-SCREEN PROCEDURE The "touch screen" provides a sensitive method for assessing the discrimination of complex arrays of elements. Pigeons were trained to peck at one side of a randomized array when there were more blue than red elements, and at the other side with the opposite proportions. This yielded orderly maintained discriminations of response location based on relative numerosity. Corresponding results were obtained when pigeons had to respond to the left side when uniform arrays were presented, and to the right side when other, mixed proportions were shown. This general method has the advantage that the different elements are equally associated with reinforcement during training.

authors_raw: Kelly A.K. Forbes and John F. Connolly
title: MEMORY PERFORMANCE, ERPS AND PHONOLOGICAL
abstract:
ENCODING DURING SILENT READING
The dual-route theory (e.g. Coltheart, 1978) proposes that the initial interpretation
of written text is phonologically mediated. Phonological mediation is later
superseded by orthography as reading becomes more proficient Research indicates
that the interpretation of written text may continue to be influenced by the
phonological representation of the words (e.g. Doctor & Coltheart, 1980). The
present study investigates the effects of prior exposure on the later interpretation of
written text containing homophones using explicit and implicit memory measures.
Subjects were right-handed, English speaking undergraduates (N=40). Event
related potentials (ERPs) were recorded to highly contextually constrained
sentences completed with homophones. The manner in which the sentences were
completed was manipulated in the study phase. Sentence endings were completed
with homophones that were semantically and phonologically appropriate (S+P+),
semantically incongruous but phonologically appropriate (S-p+) or semantically
and phonologically incongruous (S-P-) to the sentence context During the test
phase, subjects completed a recognition task or an oral spelling task requiring them
to identify homophones previously seen or to spell homophones the first way they
came to mind, respectively. ERP findings indicate that s+p+ and s-p+ are
processed similarly and both are processed differently than s-p-. In contrast,
behavioural measures indicate that comprehension is influenced by spelling
frequency and the degree to which the infonnation agrees with the sentence context.
Findings support phonological mediation in reading and are discussed in light of
current theories.

authors_raw: Kelly Frame, John Christie and Raymond Klein
title: NEGATIVE PRIMING IN A LOCALIZATION TASK: A METHODOLOGICAL CRITIQUE AND AN EMPIRICAL PUZZLE
abstract:
When a subject is presented with pairs of trials (prime followed by probe) in which
a target (0) must be localized, sometimes in the presence of a distracting non-target
(+, distractor), response time is slower if a probe trial's target appears in the
location occupied by the previous (prime) trial's distractor than when it appears in a
new location. This finding (negative priming) suppons the proposal that selective
attention is accomplished by active inhibition of irrelevant information. The
literature, however, is characterized by an insensitivity to design features necessary
to ensure the purity of the observed effects. When all necessary conditions are
included, our results reveal interference, negative priming, and both inhibition and
facilitation at the previous target's location. Yet these mechanisms, alone, are
insufficient to explain the data. Will there still be a role for negative priming when
the full puzzle is solved?

authors_raw: Jeff P. Hamm and Patricia A. McMullen
title: RESPONSE COMPETITION CONTRIBUTES TO THE EFFECTS OF ORIENTATION WHEN LOCATING THE TOP OR BOTTOM OF ROTATED OBJECTS The time to determine if a dot is located near the top or bottom pole of rotated objects increases linearly for rotations up to 120 deg. This effect has been linked to a similar effect of orientation found for rotated object naming. However, different causes for these effects may be inferred if it was shown that they were panly due to response compatibility in the top-bottom discrimination task. Results from this task showed that responses to dots at the top were faster than those to dots at the bottom of the screen. This effect was additive with the effects of object orientation. However, compatibility between location of the dot on the screen and response was confounded with object orientation. Objects near upright orientations were confounded with compatible responses and objects at more extreme orientations were confounded with incompatible responses. To determine if response compatibility accounted for some of the effects of orientation during top-bottom discriminations, "top" and "bottom" keys were positioned horizontally or vertically. Vertical key placement, which was thought to improve response mapping, resulted in a greater effect of orientation than horizontal placement The effects of orientation during top-bottom discriminations may partially reflect response competition, suggesting these orientation effects are not reflective of those found during rotated object naming.

authors_raw: Christopher Dywan, Joseph Byrnes and John Connolly
title: ELECTROPHYSIOLOGICAL CORRELATES OF SINGLE-WORD RECEPTIVE VOCABULARY IN CHILDREN
abstract:
A computerized version of the Peabody Picture Vocabulary Test, Revised, was
developed to examine potential electrophysiological correlates of single-word
receptive vocabulary in normal IO-year-old children. Of primary interest will be
ERP components that may differentiate between picture-word pairs that match or
mismatch at 3 levels of difficulty (below age, at age and above age level). This
paradigm may potentially facilitate objective assessment of single word receptive
vocabulary in patients whose conditions, such as Cerebral Palsy, may preclude
traditional assessments based on verbal or motor responses. If available,
preliminary data will be presented and discussed.

authors_raw: Heather A. Schellinck and Patricia A. McMullen
title: WHY DOES ORIENTATION-SENSITIVITY DIMINISH DURING REPEATED NAMING OF ROTATED OBJECTS: FEATURES OR OBJECT-CENTRED REPRESENT ATIO NS?
abstract:
Effects of orientation on rotated object naming diminish over presentation blocks. Is
this effect due to emergent naming based on orientation-invariant features (Jolicoeur
& Milliken, 1989) or on object-centred representations (see Marr, 1982)? If the
former, then repeated naming of objects whose features are spatially scrambled
should diminish effects of orientation on a final block of intact, rotated object
naming in the same way that repeated blocks of intact, rotated object naming does.
In contrast, if naming over blocks is based on emergent reliance on object-centred
representations, then repeated naming of feature-scrambled objects should not result
in diminished effects of orientation on a final block of intact, rotated object naming.
To test which hypothesis is better supported, one group of subjects repeatedly
named feature-scrambled objects followed by a final block of intact, rotated object
naming. A second group named intact rotated objects in all blocks. A final group
repeatedly named intact, upright objects followed by a block of intact, rotated
objects. Effects of orientation on the final block, were diminished after repeated
naming of intact, rotated and feature-scrambled objects but not after repeated
naming of upright objects. These results support the hypothesis that feature-based
naming underlies the diminished effects of orientation over repeated blocks of
rotated object
naming.

authors_raw: Peter C. Kind, Chris J. Beaver and Donald E. Mitchell
title: THE EFFECTS OF MONOCULAR DEPRIVATION AND REVERSE OCCLUSION ON THE EXPRESSION OF CAT-301 IN AREA 17 OF KITTENS
abstract:
Although considerable recovery can occur from the effects of an early period of
monocular deprivation (MD) following prompt imposition of a period of reverse
occlusion (RO), recent behavioural work has revealed that this procedure can result
in a wide variety of other outcomes, including severe bilateral amblyopia. In an
attempt to understand the mechanisms that underlie the behavioral findings, we
have examined the expression of the Cat-301 antigen in area 17 of kittens
monocularly deprived to 4, 5, 6 or 8 weeks of age that subsequently received either
a period of RO (that lasted from 9 days to 12 weeks) followed by binocular visual
exposure, or else the latter alone. We have reported previously that certain of these
regimens that employ short periods of RO result in very low levels of Cat-301
expression in the dLGN, equivalent to levels reported in dark-reared animals. We
now report that these same rearing regimens also result in a substantial reduction of
Cat-301 expression in area 17. The largest decrease (as much as 75%) occurred in
the supragranular layers with a smaller decrement (about 30%) evident in the upper
portion of layer 4.

title: IN THE MOOD: INFLUENCE OF MOODS ON SELF-DISCREPANCIES
abstract:
The purpose of the present study was to examine the causal relation between self-
discrepancies and mood. According to Self-discrepancy theory (e.g. Higgins,
1987), various self-concept discrepancies represent enduring cognitive structures,
which when activated produce specific changes in mood. The purpose of the
present study was to test the hypothesis that transient mood states can influence the
type of self-discrepancies reported.
Subjects were assigned to one of two mood induction conditions: sad, or
anxious. After mood was induced, subjects completed the Selves Questionnaire
which measures self-concept discrepancies. Results of analyses support Higgins'
proposed causal relation between mood and self-discrepancies. When subjects
were classified as dysphoric or non-dysphoric based on BDI scores, dysphoric
subjects were found to have higher ideal discrepancy scores as well as greater ought
discrepancy scores, regardless of mood induction condition. Results support a uni-
directional relationship between self-discrepancies and mood and argue against a
reverse causality hypothesis.

title: EFFECTS OF SEX HORMONES ON COGNITIVE ABILITIES: A CRITICAL REVIEW
abstract: Effects of sex hormones on cognitive abilities in humans has been investigated 1) prenatally, by acting to organize neural pathways and 2) by activating previously organized functions in adulthood. Because it is difficult to manipulate hormones in humans, research on activational effects has relied upon naturally occurring bio- rhythms, and medical conditions. The menstrual cycle is a convenient way to study how hormonal fluctuations might influence cognitive abilities in women. For example, high levels of estrogen (follicular phase) have been associated with poorer performance on spatial tasks compared to performance during menses when hormones are low (Hampson, 1990). Other studies have focused on a cycling of cognitive performance in relation to brain laterality effects. Heister et al. (1989) reported right hemisphere superiority on a visual perception task during menses which decreased linearly through follicular and luteal phases to a small left hemisphere superiority effect during the premenstrual phase. To date, theoretical explanations are unconfirmed and methodology is problematic. A summary of relevant results as well as theoretical implications and methodological suggestions will be proposed.

title: SEX DIFFERENCES IN TARGET HOSTILITY RATINGS
abstract:
This study examined: 1) Sex differences in the ability to detect hostility during an
audio recording, and; 2) the extent to which such differences affect subject- and
friend- agreement about the subject's hostility level. Data was collected from 112
undergraduates who each provided the name of a close friend To determine their
ability to detect hostility the friends completed the MacGregor Hostility Recognition
Scale (MACH-0 after listening to either a high or low hostile audio recording.
Results indicated that in both the high and low hostile recordings males (M=3.4 and
M=2.7 respectively) reported significantly less hostility to be present than females
(M=4.0, 1(52)=-2.9,12.-.005and M=3.1, 1(51)=-1.8, 12-.056 respectively). When
male and female ratings of hostility were subtracted from the actual levels of
hostility in the recordings (as determined by expert raters) females (Mdiff=.27)
were significantly more accurate than males (Mdiff=.86, 1(52)=2.9, l2,_.005)on the
high hostile recordings. No difference in accuracy was found between males
(Mdiff=-.72) and females (Mdiff=-1.13, t=l.80, 12>.05)on the low hostile
recordings.
Second, the subject's hostility level, measured by the Cook-Medley
Hostility Scale, was rated by both the subject and the subject's friend and subject-
friend agreement was determined Male friends had less agreement with male
subjects about hostility (M=19.43. SD=l0.14) compared to female friends with
male subjects (M.=25.47, SD=8.30, 1(34)=-2.00, 12-.053). Thus it appears that
males are less accurate in detecting hostility.

authors_raw: Rita Ingenmey and Philip J. Dunham
title: GENDER DIFFERENCES IN SOCIAL REFERENCING DURING A STRANGER INTERACTION
abstract:
We asked 1) whether 9-month-old males and females differ in their tendency to
visually reference their mother during the infants' interaction with a stranger and 2)
if gender differences are observed in infant's emotional reactions. Maternal
employment conditions were also included as a moderator variable. Thirty-four
infants played "peek-a-boo" with a female experimenter while the mother's profile
was in the infant's view. Looks at mother were used as an index of social
referencing and Izaard's (1983) AFFEX was used to assess the infant's emotional
expression. Females referenced their mothers more .f(l, 29) = 6.05, 12<.02, than
males but no gender differences were observed in the infants' emotional reactions.
Infants with mothers employed outside the home smiled more frequently at the
stranger, E,(1,29) = 7.58, 12<.01, than infants with mothers working inside the
home. The results suggest that gender differences in social referencing behaviour
are observed as early as 9 months and that these gender differences in referencing
are not emotionally mediated.

title: GENDER DIFFERENCES IN THE CONSTRUCT VALIDITY OF TWO HOSTILITY MEASURES Objective: To detennine the construct validity of the Ho subscale of the MMPI and the structured interview method (SI) of hostility measurement while examining gender differences. Design: Correlational Setting: University Participants: From an introductory psychology class. Mean age of nineteen. 54 male and 87 female volunteers were used. Interventions: Subjects were given a standard structured interview and asked to fill out the Ho scale and the PANAS negative affect (NA) scale. Measures: Two tailed t-tests were used to compare means of males with females. Simple correlations and intercorrelations between Ho and SI measures were used. Results: For females 9% of the variance (r=.30) in scores on the Ho scale were accounted for by variability of scores on the NA scale. No significant variability in PH scores was accounted for by NA scores in females. In males, 11% of the variance (r=.34) in scores on the Ho scale were accounted for by variability in NA scores. Scores of PH accounted for an additional 33% of variability in Ho scores in males. Conclusions: It appears that Ho and SI methods of hostility assessment are not measuring the same construct for females, but have high construct validity for males. Rater-reliability and other possible confounding factors for the female hostility data will be presented. There are significant gender differences in expression of hostility.
abstract:
AWARDS
In the spirit of years past, the In-House Convention Committee would like to continue the fine
tradition of presenting an award for the most entertaining title. However, this year we would like
to put a twist into the tradition by asking you, the attendees, to chose via the democratic method,
those titles that you feel are most deserving.
Please denote your choice in the spaces provided below, and place the ballot in the box located in
the presentation room.

title: LAB, LIBRARY, LAN, AND (L')INTERNET
abstract: This past year has seen the arrival of some exciting new technologies for information access. At Dalhousie, the University Library has been purchasing multi-user network versions of several computer-readable databases, including those most widely used by students in Psychology. It is now possible for our students to access CD-ROM databases located in the Killam Library from any terminal in the Psychology Computer Laboratory. At the same time (and from the same computer), students can check Novanet for availability of journals at the libraries, and even order a fax copy of an article over the Internet. For the benefit of those who have not yet utilized these new tools, we will quickly demonstrate use of PsycLIT, Med.Line and Science Citation Index, and then introduce some new tools for finding information on the Internet. . \ ', ~- 'i ,

title: LINE BISECTION AND VISUAL ORIENTATION ERRORS ALONG
abstract:
HORIZONTAL AND VERTICAL PLANES IN PERIPERSONAL AND
EXTRAPERSONAL SPACE IN PATIENTS WITH VISUOSPATIAL
NEGLECT: A PROPOSAL
In recent years, line bisection and visual orientation studies of visuospatial
neglect have been prolific, but the evidence provided has been fixed by
physical limitations and methodological moderations. Line bisections are
performed manually and are confined to peripersonal space. Thus,
knowledge of visuospatial deficits outside of arm's reach (e.g., extra-
personal space) is missing. Similarly, the visual orientation task is carried
out by comparing responses along a left-right continuum. Thus,
investigation of visuospatial neglect along multiple planes is absent. The
purpose of the present study will be twofold. First, it proposes to
investigate line bisection and visual orientation errors along horizontal and
vertical planes in peripersonal and extrapersonal space. Second, a model
explicating line bisection errors is missing. It is suggested that the
theoretical framework of the visual orientation task may provide an
opportunity to funher elucidate on mechanisms of line bisection errors.
Thus, the present study intends to illuminate mechanisms underlying
bisection errors by comparing displacement errors on line bisection to errors
on a visual orientation task. A proposal outlining the background and
research plan will be presented.

title: GENDER DIFFERENCES IN THE RELATION BETWEEN MEASURES OF HOSTILITY AND RESTING SYSTOLIC BLOOD PRESSURE
abstract:
Gender differences in the ability of the Cook-Medley Hostility Scale (HO) and its
subscales, and the Anger Expression Scale (AES) to predict resting systolic blood
pressure were examined. Four blood pressure readings were taken at three-minute
intervals and the fourth reading was used as the baseline. Following baseline
readings, 99 male and 109 female undergraduate subjects completed the HO and the
AES.
Results indicated that for males the following HO scales were significantly
positively correlated with restirtg systolic blood pressure: The HO total hostility
score (I= .245, 12< .01), Costa's subscales of cynicism and paranoid alienation (r
= .231, 12< .05 and r = .263, ll < .01 respectively), and Barefoots' subscales of
cynicism, hostile emotion, and aggressive responding (r = .208, ll < .05; r = .328,
ll < .001; and r = .315, ll < .001 respectively). For females none of the HO
subscales were significantly correlated with resting systolic blood pressure. There
was no relation between the AES and resting systolic blood pressure for either
gender.
These results are discussed in terms of the implications of using traditional
measures of hostility for predicting risk for CHD in male and female populations.

authors_raw: Raymond Klein, Alan MacDonald & John Christie
title: HOW CAN WE DETERMINE IF ATTENTION IS THE GENERATOR RESPONSIBLE FOR ILLUSORY LINE DRAWING? Two circles, separated by 4° are displayed on a screen. One briefly brightens and then a line connecting the two circles is added to the display. Although the line appears instantaneously, the subject's subjective experience is that the line is "drawn" from the flashed circle toward the unchanged one. The flashed circle does attract the observer's attention, and Shimojo, who discovered this line drawing illusion, attributes it to the locus of attention. Alternatively, the illusion might depend on purely visual mechanisms activated by the stimulus, such as those associated with apparent motion. We plan to compare the strength of the illusion under conditions of exogenous (externally generated, e.g. following abrupt peripheral luminance increment) and endogenous (internally generated, e.g. following verbal instruction) control over attention. Our first step was to determine if the line drawing illusion could be cancelled by presenting a line that WAS drawn over time in the direction opposite to the illusory motion.
NEGATIVE PRIMING: OBJECT FILE REVIEW OR SELECTIVE INHIBITION? THE METHOD MATTERS!
abstract: Negative priming is the impaired performance that occurs when a subject has to respond to a target that occupies the same space as a previously ignored distractor. Object file review and selective inhibition are the two principal explanations for this phenomena. Object file review explains negative priming as caused by mismatch between the attributes of the target and those of the item that previously occupied the location. According to selective inhibition negative priming occurs because a distractor is not merely ignored, but actively inhibited as part of the selective process and that this carries forward to items presented later in the same space. Two experiments, using the most common methodologies were designed to test these two theories. It was also found that the relative contribution of object file review and selective inhibition depends on, as yet indeterminate methodological factors. ---

title: THE NART AS A TOOL FOR ESTIMATING PREMORBID INTELLIGENCE IN A NORTH AMERICAN POPULATION: A WILD GOOSE CHASE?
abstract: Current level of cognitive performance is not indicative of premorbid level of functioning. Estimates of premorbid intelligence are essential for determining the extent of cognitive impairment in brain damaged populations. A relatively new approach to estimating premorbid intelligence is based upon the relationship that exists between word-reading ability and general intelligence (Nelson & McKenna, 1975; Nelson & O'Connell, 1978). The National Adult Reading Test (NART), which contains orthographically irregular words, was normed on a British population and provides estimates based upon the WAIS. The present study based estimates of intelligence in a North American population on the W AIS-R. NART based predictions of intelligence were compared to other estimates based upon the Vocabulary Age Scaled score and the VIQ-PIQ discrepancy in performance on the W AIS-R. Results indicate that the NART is a useful tool for predicting premorbid intelligence in normal and brain damaged populations within a North American population.

title: CHILDREN'S UNDERSTANDING OF MENTAL MISREPRESENTATIONS
abstract:
We studied the development of children's understanding of two common types of
mental misrepresentations: Holding false beliefs and forgetting previous beliefs. A
person may hold a false belief about a current state of affairs due to ignorance of
externally caused changes or perceptual illusions. Or a person may misrepresent a
current state of affairs by simply forgetting its previous mental representation
caused by internal changes within the person. The results of our experiments
suggest that children's abilities for retroactive inferencing of the protagonist's
forgetting from her incongruous action begin to emerge around 4y8m, about a half
year later than their abilities for attribution of a false belief.

authors_raw: D.E. Mitchell, G. Bateman, C.J. Beaver, and P. Ritchie
title: SINGLE-CELL RESOLUTION OF THE CHANGES IN THE PATTERN OF INNERVATION OF AREA 17 BY THE TWO EYES FOLLOWING MONOCULAR DEPRIVATION AND ITS REVERSAL IN KITTENS
abstract:
C-Fos,the protein product of the c-fos gene, has been shown (l) to be
expressed rapidly in the visual cortex of kittens following brief exposure to
light Our purpose was to explore the use of Fos immunohistochemistry as
a means for examining the three dimensional pattern of innervation of area
17 by the two eyes in monocularly deprived kittens as well as the speed and
nature of the changes that occur subsequently following reverse occlusion.
The experiments were performed on kittens that had been monocularly
deprived at 1 week of age until they were 5 weeks old. To visualize the
pattern of innervation of area 17 by a given eye, the animal was first placed
in a darkroom for a period (24 hrs) that was sufficient for constitutive Fos
expression to be driven below baseline levels of detectability. The kitten
was then allowed normal visual experience with just the eye of interest open
for 2 hours at which point the animal was euthanized and perfused. The
immunocytochemical procedures (1) were conducted on 50 um sections cut
from unfolded and flattened visual cortex.
In animals exposed through the deprived eye, immunoreactive
neurons were observed in patches in superfical and deeper layers of area 17.
The number of immunoreactive neurons within each patch as well as the
size and shape of the patches changed with increasing length of deprivation.
This method permits, for the first time, visualization with cellular resolution
of the columnar arrangement of territory innervated by a given eye
throughout area 17 and as such may prove to be a superior method for
assaying the effects of deprivation than existing methods.

title: THE FAST LETTER GAME
abstract:
When subjects identify a target in a rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP)
stream, their ability to detect a subsequent probe stimulus is reduced.
Known as an attentional blink (AB), we determined whether this
phenomenon depends on shared categorical membership between target and
probe stimuli. We presented subjects with RSVP streams of black letters in
which one white (target) letter was embedded. In half the streams, a probe
(0) was one of eight post-target stimuli. For half the subjects this probe
was referred to as the letter "O"; for the other half it was referredto as the
digit "zero". Relative to an ignored-target control, an AB occurred in both
experimental groups. However, the AB was attenuated when subjects
detected zero rather than "O". This dependence of AB on categorical
membership of target and probe stimuli was confirmed in a study that had
subjects detect a "6" following letter identification.

title: EMBRYONIC NEURAL GRAFTS INDUCE REPAIR BY THE JUVENILE NEOCORTEX IN MICE - AN IMMUNOHISTOCHEMICALAND AUTORADIOGRAPHIC STUDY
abstract: The postnatal central nervous system (CNS) of mammals has a diminished restorative capacity. Recent research proposes that embryonic neural grafts transferred into a damaged region of the CNS may lead to a certain degree of anatomical and functional recovery. Until now, it was generally believed that it is the graft which is the source of the reconstituted tissue. Our observations (J.Ourednik et al. 1993, W. Ourednik et al. 1994) indicate that in the CNS the regenerative capacity is not lost irreversibly and that embryonic grafts from the presumptive murine cerebral cortex may induce restorative processes and cell proliferation within the severely injured cerebral cortex of young mice. Our investigation is based on immunohistochemical distinction of donor and host tissues of two congenic mouse strains which express two different allelic forms of the Thy-1 antigen, and on detection of newly formed cells by their incorporation of 3H-thymidine administered to the recipients shonly after transplantation. Neurons are revealed by antibodies against neuron-specific marker proteins (MAP2, PY or NF-h). Radioactively labelled cells are detected in the immunohistochemically stained sections by autoradiography. We demonstrate that in animals that had received a tiny graft, a serious mechanical insult of the neocortex was repaired mainly by proliferation of host cells of both neuronal and non-neuronal origin. During the 2-month survival period, the graft was either completely resorbed, or participated in a minor way in the reconstruction. Ourednik, J., Ourednik, W., Van der Loos, H., Do fetal neural grafts induce repair by the injured juvenile neocortex? NeuroReport 5: 133-136 (1993) Ourednik, W., Ourednik, J., the late Van der Loos, H., Newly formed host cells in grafted juvenile neocortex expressing neuron-specific marker proteins. NeuReport 5: (1994, in press)

title: -- \ OLFACTORY AND TASTE ABILITIES IN PRE- AND POSTPARTUM WOMEN This study will assess the olfactory and taste abilities of women before and after birth. Sense of smell and taste may be altered during and after pregnancy for three reasons: (1) smell and taste are sensitive to endocrine changes and pregnancy involves significant endocrine changes (e.g. progesterone, estrogen, HPL, HCG); (2) pregnancy-associated alterations in food palatability may be an adaptation to protect the embryo from food toxins, and these changes may be mediated by smell and taste changes; and (3) olfaction may play an important role in the mother's ability to recognize and bond with her infant, and thus may be enhanced postpartum. Subjects' performance will be measured in a longitudinal design once during the 2nd trimester, once shortly after parturition (~2 weeks), and finally at 3 months postpartum. Smell will be assessed using an odour identification test and an odour threshold detection test. Taste will be assessed using a whole-mouth taste recognition test. Theoretical and methodological criticisms are welcome.
abstract:
Tl 2. Scott Bishop
DOES ATTENTION MEDIA TE THE RELATION BETWEEN
CATASTROPHIZING AND PAIN?
Catastrophizing has been identified as an individual difference variable that
contributes to increased distress reactions during painful experiences.
Catastrophizing has been described as a tendency to engage in pain-related negative
ideation characterized by rumination, magnification of threat, and lack of confidence
and control. Research has been consistent in showing that catastrophizing is
associated with increased pain and emotional distress during painful stimulaton.
However, there has been little research examining the mechanisms that
mediate the relation between catastrophizing and distress reactions. Based on recent
findings in our lab, I will present a model in which will suggest that attentional
processes mediate the relation between catastrophizing and pain. Future research
and implications will also be discussed.

authors_raw: Michael Antle, Hugh Piggins, & Benjamin Rusak
title: Getting a GRiP on GRouP GRoPe in the Light
abstract:
In rodents, the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus is the
circadian pacemaker. The SCN contains various substances which may be
involved in photic entrainment of the pacemaker. The bombesin like peptide,
gastrin-releasing peptide (GRP) is one such substance. The present study was
designed to determine if GRP would phase shift hamsters housed in continuous
illumination. GRP was found to produce "light pulse-like" shifts when injected to
the SCN. These results suggest that GRP may be involved in photic entrainment.

authors_raw: D.P. Phillips, M.N. Semple and L.M. Kitzes
title: FUNCTIONAL SPECIALIZATION IN THE CAT'S AUDITORY CORTEX (Or: What Needles in the Posterior Area Reveal About God's Inventiveness)
abstract:
The cat's auditory sensory cortex contains a number of territories distinguished by
their anatomy and the internal organization of their frequency maps. Much
quantitative evidence is available on the physiology of cells in the primary auditory
field (AD, but virtually nothing is known of the physiology of neurons in the other
territories. In anesthetized, adult cats, we explored the posterior auditory field (P),
studying individual neurons with stimulus paradigms that have previously been
used to study AI cells. We found that the majority (70-90%) of field P neurons
had properties seen in a minority (20-30%) of AI cells: powerful lateral inhibition,
apparent tuning to the amplitude of preferred-frequency tones, and sensitivity to the
rise-time of tonal signals. The minimal latencies of field P cells were slightly
longer than those of AI cells. These findings raise the question of whether field P
cells derive their input from the subset of AI cells that already possess these
properties.

title: WeOn Geons
abstract:
Biederman's (1987) Recognition-by-Components proposal argued that a limited set
of categorically-perceived, geometric, three-dimensional visual primitives (geons)
form the (neurological) basis of all object perception. To test the prediction of
categorical perception, a continuum of three-dimensional volumes between a block
and cylinder (two geons advocated by Biederman) was constructed, rendered in a
two-dimensional computer display, and presented to subjects. In a multiphase, two-
altemative, timed, forced-choice decision, subjects repeatedly labelled each volume
as block or cylinder. In Phase 1, subjects established a baseline boundary between
the block and cylinder. The sharp discontinuity between the two provided evidence
for a categorical boundary. In Phase 2, subjects were presented with either the
block or cylinder in an anempt to exhaust the (neurological) detectors for that object
(adaptation). In Phase 3, the categorical perception of block versus cylinder was
reassessed. After adaptation, relative to its Phase 1 position, the categorical
boundary shifted towards the adapting stimulus. The shift was in the predicted
direction supporting the existence of geons.

title: NOW YOU SEE IT, NOW YOU DON'T: ROLE OF ATTENTION IN PRIMING
abstract:
Many recent studies show that attention manipulation has little effect on perceptually
driven implicit tests of memory. In these studies, priming (nonconscious facilitation
of processing due to previous study) is obtained even when attention is divided
between the target information and another ongoing task. There are, however, no
studies that have directly compared the amount of priming obtained as a function of
the amount of attention given to target information. The present study was
conducted to further investigate whether focal attention was necessary for priming.
It was hypothesized that if focal attention was crucial for priming, then greater
priming could be expected where attention was focused on the target information.
The Stroop Color-Word paradigm was used to manipulate attention to the words. In
this paradigm, different groups of subjects either read words, named the ink in
which the the words were printed or named the ink color of a series of XXX:s
which were presented simultaneously at different locations on a computer screen.
Retention of the words was later tested using both cued recall (explicit) and word
fragment completion (implicit) tests. Priming was obtained in all conditions of the
experiment. However, only priming for the word-reading condition was
significantly different from baseline performance. There were no differences in
priming for naming the ink of the words and the XXX:s. There was also greater
explicit cued recall when subjects read the words. Implication of these results will
be discussed in the framework of attention and information processing.

title: SOCIAL AND SPATIAL ORGANIZATION OF FREE-RANGING SHREWS
abstract:
The social organization of free-ranging Sorex coronatus and Neomys fodiens was
studied by measuring the spatial distribution and temporal avoidance or aggregation
of individuals, using simultaneous radio-isotope tracking of several shrews. Sorex
coronatus was strictly territorial during the non-breeding season, with little overlap
between home ranges and low coincident activity between individuals. In the
breeding season, females remained strictly territorial, whereas large home range
overlap between males and females and also between males was observed. During
this season, males kept intensive watch on one female and occasionally visited a
second, in tum was regularly visited by another male. This behaviour suggests a
monogamous mating system with a tendency towards polygamy. Neomys fodiens
• was territorial during the non-breeding season. Each individual defended a portion
of a river but a slightly greater home range overlap was observed between than
within the sexes. During the breeding season, females remained strict!~ territorial,
whereas males tended to wander, indicating an ]Joverlap promiscuous,I mating
system. After weaning, juveniles of both species dispersed rapidly and became
territorial until the following breeding season. These results were compared with
published data on Crocidura russula. During the non-breeding season, there are two
types of social organization: a colonial system in C. russula, where coincident rest
between individuals allows them to reduce their daily expenditure of energy by
social thermoregulation; and a territorial system in S. coronatus and N. fodiens,
which may ensure an adequate food supply. During the breeding season for all
three species females are strictly territorial perhaps to ensure they have sufficient
food for their litters. For the males, three different strategies have been observed
and these are also discussed .

title: MET ABOTROPIC GLUTAMATE RECEPTORS IN THE HAMSTER SUPRACHIASMATIC NUCLEUS.
abstract:
The suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN) of the mammalian hypothalamus function as a
pacemaker which controls mammalian circadian rhythms. The SCN receive photic
input via the geniculohypothalamic tract (GHT), an indirect visual pathway arising
from cells in the intergeniculate leaflet (IGL) of the lateral geniculate body, with
neuropeptide Y and GABA being likely neurotransmitter candidates. The SCN also
receive direct visual input via the retinohypothalamic tract (RHT), and evidence
suggests that the excitatory amino acid glutamate mediates neurotransmission in the
RHT. Recent reports have demonstrated the presence of ionotropic excitatory amino
acid receptors of the NMDA and non-NMDA receptor subclasses in the SCN. In
addition, metabotropic excitatory amino acid receptors have been located in the
SCN. To date, very little information is available on the effects of metabotropic
receptor activation in the SCN, however, initial studies in our laboratory have
demonstrated that 1S,3R ACPD, a specific metabotropic receptor agonist, causes a
dose-dependent increase in neuronal activity in the SCN in vitro. Further studies
aim to pharmacologically characterise metabotropic glutamate receptors in the SCN
and elucidate the role of this receptor type in the SCN in vivo.
T2 l. Anne-Marie Brown
MEMORY FUNCTIONS IN MULTIPLE CHEMICAL SENSITIVITY
Case series of patients with Multiple Chemical Sensitivity (MCS) suggest difficulty
on several memory tests in addition to cacosmia. Bell (1992) suggested that this
pattern of symptoms might implicate focal dysfunction of the temporal lobes. To
test this hypothesis a case control study was initiated to compare the pattern of
memory loss in MCS, healthy controls matched for age, education and sex and
: \ patients with temporal lobectomy on measures of estimated pre-morbid ability,
••intelligence, attention, immediate and delayed memory for verbal and nonverbal
material. Twenty-six employees from a "sick building" were referred for extensive
neuro-psychological evaluations based on impairments documented in cognitive
screening. The majority had MCS. Group results for MCS patients, controls and
patients with temporal lobe lesions will be presented.

authors_raw: Hugh D. Piggins, David J. Cutler, Kazue Semba & Benjamin Rusak
title: TIME FOR SUBSTANTIAL P?
abstract:
The tachykinin substance P (SP) is found throughout the mammalian central
nervous system. We have demonstrated SP-like immunoreactivity in cells, fibres,
and terminals of the Syrian hamster suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN), which contain
the primary circadian pacemaker. The possible role of SP in circadian processes
has yet to be established. We examined the effects of ionophoretically applied SP
on the extracellularly recorded firing rates of hamster SCN cells maintained in an in
vitro brain slice preparation. SP evoked responses from ~54% of 85 cells tested.
Most responsive cells were activated (~85%) while ~ 15% were suppressed by SP.
A greater proportion of cells responded when tested during the projected day (
~72%) than during the projected night (~38%). These preliminary data indicate that
SP can alter hamster SCN neuronal activity, particularly during the subjective day.

authors_raw: Sherry H. Stewart & Sharon B. Zeitlin
title: UNIVERSITY STUDENTS' DRINKING MOTIVES: RELATIONSHIPS TO ANXIETY PRONENESS, ANXIETY-SENSITIVITY, AND GENDER
abstract:
The present study examined the relationships between anxiety-proneness
(trait anxiety), anxiety-sensitivity (fear of anxiety), and self-reported
reasons for drinking using a newly-validated measure of drinking motives.
The State-Trait Anxiety Inventory-Trait subscale (STAI-T), the Anxiety
Sensitivity Index (ASI), and the Drinking Motives Questionnaire (DMQ)
were administered to 314 university students. Higher ASI scores were
associated with greater scores on the Coping Motives (CM; drinking to
reduce or avoid negative emotional states) subscale of the DMQ, particularly
in women. In contrast, ASI scores were not related to scores on either the
Enhancement Motives (EM; drinking to facilitate positive emotional states),
or Social Motives (SM; drinking to meet social norms) subscales of the
DMQ. A regression equation involving scores on the ASI and STAI-T
significantly predicted DMQ-CM scores; the regression equation was
superior in predicting the frequency of CM drinking for women vs. men.
These results imply greater risk for alcohol abuse in individuals (particularly
women) who are high in both anxiety-sensitivity and anxiety-proneness.
i.

title: FIXATION OFFSETS AND COVERT ORIENTING: THE FAILURE TO FIND EFFECTS
abstract:
When a fixation stimulus is offset prior to saccadic target onset, eye movement
latencies are facilitated. The present experiment determined whether facilitation is
also observed for coven attentional orienting. Subjects were presented with
predictive verbal (endogenous), unpredictive visual (exogenous), or predictive
visual (endogenous+exogenous) cues to one of four possible target locations. The
target was the appearance of an horizontally- or a vertically-oriented ellipse, in
response to which subjects made a speeded manual discrimination. The timecourse
of orienting --revealed by the function relating reaction times to cue-target onset
asynchrony -- was examined under three "gap" conditions, wherein on one-third of
trials there was no fixation offset, on one-third of trials fixation offset occurred
simultaneously with cue onset, and on one-third of trials fixation offset occurred
210 msec prior to cue onset. In none of the cue conditions was the speed of coven
orienting facilitated by fixation offset.

authors_raw: Richard Brown, Thane Calder & Charles Gilben
title: BEHAVIOURAL DIFFERENCES OF MHC-CONGENIC MICE DURING DEVELOPMENT
abstract:
Since 1979, it has been known that mice that differ only in the genes of the major
histo-compatibility complex (MHC) on chromosome 17 produce different urine
odours. These odours thus provide phenotypic markers of the genotype of the
mouse which can be used for mate selection and kin recognition. We investigated
differences in the development of the behaviour of C57BL/6J (H-2b) and B6.C-H-
2bml mice, two strains which differ only in the class 1 (H-2K) region of the MHC.
Thane Calder found differences in ultrasonic vocalization rates, growth rates, slight
movement, grooming and eye opening in these mice and Charles Gilbert found
differences in their behaviour in an open field. As developmental biologists have
found that MHC-congenic mice differ in their embryonic growth rate, our
hypothesis is that postnatal growth and development are also influenced by MHC
type. The behavioural differences between genotypes may provide additional
information for mate selection and kin recognition.

title: SYNAPTIC CONTROL OF WAKE- AND SLEEP-ACTIVE FOREBRAIN NEURONS
abstract:
In our continuing effon to elucidate the neural networks involved in the
regulation of sleep and wakefulness, we propose a series of working
hypotheses that can be used to investigate synaptic control of wake- and
sleep-active neurons in the basal forebrain as an integral component of
regulatory mechanisms for conical arousal.

title: CARGO CULTS, BRANCH DAVIDIANS, BORN AGAIN VEGETARIANS, AND "THE PIOUS GENE."
abstract:
The present paper is a commentary on a target article to be published in Behavioral
and Brain Sciences by-D~S.~ils-c,_]!· &t.SoBerl;itled, "Re-introducing Group
Selection to the HumaifBeliavioral Sciences."-Wilson and Sober argue that the
process of group selection has~rly devalued as an evolutionary force during
the past thirty years. Usin(!?_awkini,.ooncept of "vehicle", they propose an
evolutionary model with a nestecffiierarchy of vehicles of selection ranging from
genes to groups. In my commentary I consider the role that group selection may
have ~yed in the e~olution of human religious _behavi~)Ur.In p~icular, I consider
select:J.onmodel of the psychological trait of "docility" and how
L ·reltgious beliefs could serve as absolute authoritative sources for eliciting docile
behaviours that are often of genetic, but not necessarily of "rational", self-interest to
the individual.

I

title: SELECTIVE DEPLETION OF GUT BACTERIA ALTERS THE ODOURS OF INDIVIDUALITY IN RATS
abstract:
Urinary odours of individuality in rats may be produced by the action of specific
gut bacteria. To test this hypothesis, we used three types of discrimination tasks to
assess the abilty of male rats to discriminate between the urine odours of outbred
Long-Evans male rats before and after their gram negative or gram positive bacteria
were depleted by drug treatment. Results were similar whether gram positive or
gram negative bacteria were removed. When tested in an operant-olfactometer, rats
previously trained to discriminate between the urine odours of two rats did not
generalize to the urine odours of the same rats collected after their bacteria were
removed, indicating that removal of the bacteria altered their individual odours.
However, these same subjects made fewer errors in learning to discriminate
between urine samples from rats after their bacteria were selectively removed than
in learning to discriminate between samples from previously unknown rats
indicating that they recognized some components of these odours (savings effect).
In a habituation-dishabituation task, rats detected discriminable differences between
the urine odours of the same rat before and after its bacteria were selectively
depleted further indicating that bacterial depletion altered the rat's odour. The fact
that the odours of two individual rats could still be discriminated after removal of
bacteria indicates that, while their individual odours were altered by removal of
bacteria, they were not eliminated.

authors_raw: Janet L. Ingles, C. Charles Mate-Kole, & John F. Connolly
title: DISSOCIATION OF VISUAL AND AUDITORY COMPREHENSION IN A CASE OF FLUENT APHASIA
abstract:
A 24-year old woman developed fluent aphasia and anomia following bi temporal
brain damage due to herpes simplex encephalitis. Her language disturbance
showed exceptional features: reading was neologistic and yet reading
comprehension was superior to auditory comprehension. Event-related brain
potential responses (N400) recorded in response to semantically-incongruous
sentence endings in the visual but not auditory modality further support this
dissociation. These findings suggest that despite a breakdown of phonological
output some preservation of visual and semantic routes for reading may occur.
These features are discussed with reference to other acquired language disorders.

title: ATTENTION: HOW DOES IT MOVE US? A KINEMATIC ANALYSIS OF THE EFFECTS OF ATTENTION ON MANUAL AIMING MOVEMENTS
abstract:
How does the way we attend to objects influence aspects of our movement to these
objects? The effect of attention on the kinematic parameters of motor function has
largely been ignored. Since attention and motor function are intertwined in
everyday life, it makes sense from an ecological perspective to empirically
investigate their interaction. The proposed experiments have been designed to
investigate the effects of visually directed attention on the kinematic parameters of
manual aiming movements. The paradigm incorporates basic aspects of Posner's
(1980) cost/benefit cuing paradigm with those of Rosenbaum's (1980) precuing
paradigm for motor function. Possible outcomes of these experiments will be
discussed as will potential contributions of this research to the areas of attention and
motor function.

title: i
abstract:
REPRESENTING FACES IN A NEURAL NETWORK
A simple perceptron neural network was implemented to represent three distinct
faces based on Tanaka's (1991) caricature recognition model. Modifications to his
original model were compared including passing the output through a linear transfer
function, adding a hidden layer with back propagation, back propagation with
momentum and back propagation, with momentum and an entropic error surface
and a winner-takes-all network. As was found with the original model, all modified
models correctly "recognized" each face input vector and produced higher output
activation levels to caricatures than veridical faces. However, only the original
model produced equal activation levels in all three output nodes to an average
prototypical face. Output nodes of all modified models failed to show any activation
to prototypical input. These findings suggest that the system which represents a
generic or superordinate face may be independent of the system which represents
specific face exemplars.

title: THE APPLICATION OF EVENT-RELATED BRAIN POTENTIAL MEASURES TO NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT
abstract:
In general, this research is aimed at the development of electro-physiologically-
based assessment tools for use with patients who are unable to be tested in the
conventional manner on standard neuropsychological tests (e.g., expressive
aphasics, coma patients). The present study represents an intial validation of an
event-related brain potential (ERP) adaptation of the vocabulary sub-tests of the
Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, and the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale.
Preliminary findings with normal subjects are presented, and suggest that a late
positive component (i.e., P300) in the ERP waveform may be an accurate index of
individual test performance, independent of overt behavioural response.

title: REDUCING PSYCHOLOGICALRISK FACTORS: A HEALTH PROFESSIONAL INTERVENTION
abstract: The Nova Scotia Heart Health Project was undertaken in the summer of 1989 to enable Nova Scotians to take action to improve cardiovascular health by controlling or reducing their risks for disease. As part of this multifaceted community wide effort, materials aimed at increasing the effectiveness of a psychological intervention will be provided to health professionals. Included in such materials will be a treatment manual providing practical information on how to identify and manage psychological risk factors for CHD such as hostility and social support. For example, information will be provided on how to quickly assess social network and then give practical advice on social network enhancement. Once psychological risk factors have been managed, adherence to interventions for other established risk factors such as obesity and smoking is expected to increase. This practical guide for health professionals will be presented. It will then be used, in addition to other materials, in Nova Scotia to assess the efficacy of reducing community level risk factors.

authors_raw: Alex Trillo, Cupido Daniels & Raymond M. Klein
title: THE PHANTOM CHEST: A STUDY IN RADIOLOGY
abstract:
Previous research has shown there to be a roughly thirty percent error
rate across all specialties in radiology. Research related to reducing this
error rate has looked at everything from screening radiology applicants
based on their cognitive or perceptual skills to employing the help of
computer classifier programs. In our experiment we endeavored to see
if there might be a correlation between a radiologist's visual search
skills and their performance on a series of chest x-rays. We developed
a set of visual search tasks and used fifty chest films, half of which
contained abnormalities and half of which were phantoms, in order to
assess whether there was a relationship between these two tasks. We
found that there was an approximate 30 percent error rate on the films
across level of experience and radiological specialty. Additionally,
there were some correlations between the film scores the visual scores,
but these are not easily interpretable.

authors_raw: Farhad N. Dastur & Richard E. Brown
title: THE EFFECTS OF HYPOTHYROIDISM ON THE ULTRASONIC VOCALIZATIONS OF INF ANT LONG-EV ANS RATS (RA TTUS NORVEGICUS)
abstract:
Eighty Long-Evans rat pups were made hypothyroid by administration of
methimazole (MMI) in the drinking water of their dams (el 7 - pndlO). Dams were
randomly assigned to one of four conditions: (1) control (no drug in water); (2)
0.05 mg/ml (MMI dissolved in water); (3) 0.1 mg/ml, or (4) 0.2 mg/ml. The
dependent measure was the number of ultrasonic vocalizations (UV s) emitted
during an 8-minute test on postnatal days 8, 10, 12, 14, and 21. Hypothyroidism
had a significant effect on UVs, F(3,76) = 33.40, p < .001, and was significant at
all ages tested (p < .001). The hypothyroid pups vocalized more than the control
pups at all ages except day 8, suggesting a developmental shift towards later ages in
the pattern of UV production. There was no effect of sex; however, the age effect
was significant, F(4,304) = 1159.59, p < .001. A significant age x drug
interaction was also found, F(12,304) = 15.61, p < .001. Since the internal body
temperature of hypothyroid pups was lower than that of controls, F(3, 72) = 81.90,
p < .001, it was concluded that the hypothyroid pups experienced greater thermal
stress than controls and this accounted for their increased number of UV s.

authors_raw: Tara L. Fidler & Vincent M. LoLordo
title: STRESS AND ALCOHOL PREFERENCE Volpicelli, Ulm, and Hopson (1990) found that rats given a choice between 5% ethanol and water in the home cage showed enhanced ethanol preference following four daily 1-hr sessions of inescapable shock in another environment, relative to a no-treatment control. We attempted to replicate this effect including a shocked group, a no-treatment control, and a group taken to the distinctive environment each day but not shocked. Our baseline ethanol preferences were much higher than theirs, 0.55 versus 0.35. Additionally, whereas their ethanol preferences increased from baseline to shock to post-shock phases, our ethanol preferences decreased over phases, especially in the no-treatment group. In the second replication, the pattern of results was the same, although baseline preferences were like those of Volpicelli et al. Thus we did not replicate the stress effect on alcohol consumption observed by Volpicelli et al. Additional research aimed at resolving this discrepancy is in progress.
DYSPHORIA AND UTILIZATION OF STEREOTYPES
abstract:
The present study investigated the relationship between dysphoria and utilization of
stereotypes. Dysphoric and non-dysphoric subjects were asked to view
photographs of male and female target people and asked to guess the height of each
target to the nearest half inch. In the no-instruction condition, subjects were simply
asked to guess the height of each target. In the instruction condition, subjects were
told that male and female targets were actually matched for height so that the
stereotype that men are taller than women would not be a useful heuristic to aid
them in making their judgements. Stereotype utilization was measured by
subtracting the average height estimate of female targets from the average height
estimate of male targets. It was hypothesized that dysphoric subjects would rely
more heavily on the stereotype that men are taller than women to make their height
estimates. However, it was found that dysphoric subjects were actually less likely
to rely on the stereotype to make their judgements than non-dysphoric subjects.
Results are consistent with research suggesting that dypshoric people are more
effortful information processors than non-dysphoric people.

title: GENDER DIFFERENCES IN THE RATING OF HOSTILITY
abstract:
The effect of coder gender on hostility ratings of structured interviews (Sis) was
examined. Two male and two female actors were trained to exhibit high levels of
both emotional and behavioural hostility during an SI. The actors were interviewed
by either a male or female interviewer, resulting in four SI videotapes representing
all possible interviewer/interviewee gender combinations. Trained male and female
coders independently rated the resulting SI videotapes to ensure that there was an
equal intensity of behavioural and emotional hostility displayed in each videotape.
All four videotapes were then rated for hostility level in a counterbalanced order by
105 male and 116 female undergraduates.
There was no main effect for gender of the undergraduate coder or gender
of the interviewer in either emotional or behavioural hostility ratings. However,
there was a main effect for gender of the interviewee. Female interviewees were
rated as being more behaviourally hostile (E (1,242) = 6.31, 12< .01) and less
emotionally hostile (E (1,242) =8.42, 12.< .01) than male interviewees.
Results are discussed in terms of the possible impact gender may have for
future research using SI scored hostility.

authors_raw: Susan D.Sweet, George R. Rioux & Patrick J. McGrath
title: PAIN INDUCED CENTRAL PLASTICITY: A REVIEW
abstract:
This study reviews the literature examining changes pain causes in the central
nervous system, which we may call pain induced plasticity. Such studies show that
pain may sensitize the body to further pain, or, conversely, that it may inhibit the
sensation of further pain. Studies examining pain induced plasticity through self-
reports of pain sensitivity or pain threshold by means of the cutaneous flexor reflex
were considered for inclusion. Studies were rated by two independent observers
on criteria examining their methodology and the type of pain produced. Nineteen
studies met the initial criteria and were further examined to determine consistencies
which would be useful to create a model of the differential effects pain may have on
the central nervous system. Future directions of this work are discussed in terms of
its implications for pain assessment and management, especially for children too
young to report their level of pain.

authors_raw: C. A. Dywan, J. McGlone, & A. Fox
title: UNAWARENESS OF HEMIPLEGIA MAY BE LINKED TO POOR RECOGNITION MEMORY FOLLOWING INTRACAROTID SODIUM AMOBARBIT AL INJECTIONS CONTRALATERAL TO THE TEMPORAL LOBE SEIZURE FOCUS
abstract:
Unawareness of hemispheric deficits experienced following intracarotid barbiturate
injections (i.e., Wada testing) has been assessed upon the recovery of function as a
model of anosognosia. Unawareness of drug-induced hemiplegia was examined
upon clearing of the deficit in 83 patients who were counterbalanced for side of
temporal-lobe seizure focus and who underwent left and right sodium amobarbital
injections. Unawareness was common (66%) and occurred with equal frequency
following left- and right- injections. These findings were not moderated by sex,
handedness or speech dominance. Unawareness was demonstrated more
frequently after injections contra- than ipsi-lateral to the major seizure focus.
Objective memory performance and unawareness were related after contra- but not
after ipsi-lateral injections. Thus when assessed following the recovery of
function, unawareness of hemiplegia was found to be related to the functional status
of the temporal lobe independently of hemispheric laterality.
