these presentation appear overleaf.

title: POSSIBLE CONSTRAINTS ON THE CUES INVOLVED IN JOINT VISUAL ATTENTION
abstract:
Joint visual attention (NA) which can be defined as simply looking
where someone else is looking, has been found to develop in infants in
response to the head tum of an experimenter around 10 to 12 months of
age. Recent work, however, has shown that infants of only 8 months of
age are able to be conditioned to make a NA-like head tum response if
rewarded by an interesting sight located in the experiment's direction of
gaze. These findings suggest learning as a possible mechanism through
which JV A may develop. However, subsequent research indicates that
infants are unable to be conditioned to make a NA-like head tum
response in a direction opposite to the experimenter's head tum cue.
Although this finding cannot rule out learning as a mechanism for the
development of NA it certainly does suggest that the cues which elicit
JV A in infants may be constrained. The proposed research intends to
determine if the cues involved in NA may be constrained to those
containing movement and if NA is able to be conditioned to as non-
social stimuli. Possible implications for mechanisms of NA are
discussed.

authors_raw: S. Gadbois, W. Moger, P.J. McLeod, C.J. Ryon and J.C. Fentress
title: Social Stress In Captive Timber Wolves (Canis lupus): Individual Differences In Cortisol Levels
abstract: Urine samples from fourteen known individuals within a captive timber wolf pack were collected during the breeding season from snow and analyzed for levels of stress hormone. Social behavior during this period was also recorded. Individual differences were found in urinary cortisol concentrations (expressed as cortisol:creatinine ratio, correcting for differences in urine concentration and dilution in snow). The lowest ranking female had a significantly elevated cortisol:creatinine (c:c) ratio than other females and the second (beta) ranking male, whose social position was less stable, had significantly higher c:c ratios than most other males. The data presented also suggest c:c levels increase during the course of the breeding season and may vary in response to even short periods of high social stress. Similar ratios have been reported from domestic dogs and wild wolves, suggesting that data from the present sample of captive wolves are not abnormal.

authors_raw: D.P. Phillips, M.N. Semple. L.M. Kitzes and S.E. Hall
title: Once Is Not Enough: Turning Your Partner On With Multiple Spikes
abstract: In independent studies, Phillips (Dalhousie) and Eggermont (Calgary) are compiling circumstantial evidence on local circuits in the cerebral cortex. Phillips & Sark (1991) reported that many cells in the cat's primary auditory cortex (AI) had responses consisting of a burst of spikes; the inter-spike intervals in these responses were very short(= 2 ms) and were typically stimulus-invariant. Bowman, Eggermont & Smith (1995, submitted) confirmed and extended these findings. They suggested that most extracellular recordings in AI come from larger neurons (likely pyramidal and basket cells), while the smaller (nonpyramidal) cells are relatively inaccessible to metal electrodes. There is reason to believe that the small cells are probably inhibitory interneurons that receive input from the pyramidal cells. and which. either through post-synaptic membrane specializations or local GABA-mediated inhibition (Winer, personal communication), are best excited by a train of closely-spaced pyramidal cell spikes. If these pyramidal celVnonpyramidalcell circuits are a property of the cerebral cortex generally, rather than a property specifically of AI, then the same phenomenology should be seen in other cortical areas. We have obtained evidence on the responses of neurons in the posterior auditory field, studied under the same conditions as our earlier studies of AI. Can you guess what we've found?

title: Histamine Activates / Inhibits / Modulates / Has No Effect / Does Whatever I Want It To* In the SCN (*Delete As Appropriate)
abstract: These studies investigated the effects of histamine on spontaneous and NMDA-evoked activity in the hamster SCN in vitro. Application of histamine either activated (n=l3/27), attenuated (n=6/27) or had no effect (n=8/27) on spontaneous firing in SCN cells. Attenuation of spontaneous firing rate by histamine was evident at higher ejection currents than those which elicited activations. Application of NMDA increased the firing rates of single SCN cells in a current-dependent manner. Co-application of histamine, at ejection currents which elicited no direct response to histamine itself, potentiated NMDA-evoked activity by up to 40% (61 % of cells tested). These results suggest that, in addition to having direct effects on spontaneous neuronal activity in the SCN, histamine may modulate the activity of NMDA receptors at glutamatergic synapses in the hamster SCN. And a bunch of other stuff. (-j ~-fu:~v- ~c--:ttl-.... -;'

authors_raw: Processing of Motion Stimuli In Normal and Stereoanomalous Observers Shelley McColl
title: Interocular Transfer Of Motion Adaptation Effects: Higher-Order
abstract: Interocular transfer (IOT) of conventional motion aftereffects (MAE.s) has been employed as a psychophysical technique to assess the binocularity of the human visual cortex. Like IOT of other aftereffects, conventional MAE's show partial IOT (approx 60%). Recently, Ra~ond (1~3) observed that with a higher-level motion coherence after-effect, humans wiffi"nonn stereopsis show complete IOT. Her argument is that conventional MAE's probe lower-level visual areas, where both monocular and binocular cells exist, whereas higher-order MAE's tap into higher-order visual areas, where cells are almost exclusively binocular. In view of the fact that the cortical area MT (in monkeys), is thought to mediate coherent motion, and is more-binocular than area 17, the present experiment will revisit the issue of how stereoabilities are related to IOT using Raymond's (1993) higher-order MAE task.

title: The Magnitude of the Fixation Offset Effect with Endogenously and Exogenously Controlled Saccades
abstract: Two experiments examined saccadic reaction time (RT) as a function of fixation offset condition (no offset; simultaneous and 200 ms offset-target SOA) in prosaccade and antisaccade tasks. So that observers would be equally prepared in each condition auditory warning tones preceeded target onset on every trial. The second experiment also included saccades in response to verbal commands. The fixation offset (or gap) effect (FOE) was identical in the prosaccade and verbal tasks. The FOE in the antisaccade task was significant but it was significantly smaller than in the other tasks. We speculate that the reduced FOE in the antisaccade task may be due to the requirement to inhibit the superior colliculus when the target directed saccadic programs are, by instruction, erroneous.

title: Two Year Olds' Conversation With a Robot Linda Lambrecht
Do Mutant Mice Make Sense?
abstract:
We think so.
But only if evaluated in detail.
So, we designed some tests.
These tests involve careful developmental analyses of motor development
They are really cool.
So, you should listen to us tell you about them.
For example: How do you sort out the compromising effects of different brain regions
in development and motor performance? Or, how do you think about single gene
effects on single brain regions or single aspects of motor performance? Or, how do
you think about network properties in behaviour?
Maybe mutant mice can help us here.
But we have to think carefully.
So, why study grooming in weaver mice? Come by, and we will tell you!

authors_raw: Kazue Semba, Laszlo Detari & Douglas Rasmusson
title: Synaptic Control of Wake- and Sleep-Active Basal Forebrain Neurons: An Update
abstract: (Physiology and Biophysics) Last year we presented a series of working hypotheses to examine the role of basal forebrain neurons in cortical arousal and, in particular their synaptic inputs ascending from the brainstem. We (Laszlo) have since done experiments and some of the data with brainstem stimulation were rather unexpected, as will be discussed. These data nevertheless support the hypothesis that basal forebrain neurons receive strong activation from the brainstem and provide in turn a major excitatory drive to the cerebral cortex.

authors_raw: Northern Blots, Polymerase Chain Reactions, Antisense Oligonucleotides
title: NMDA receptors, MK -801, C-fos, DNA, mRNA, Protein Synthesis,
abstract: and Behaviour. Is this the future of Psychology? Richard Brown A few years ago, I read a paper entitled "Basic concepts of molecular biology for the reproductive endocrinologist" (Plouffe, L. 1991. Seminars in Reprod,Endocrinol, 9, 1-13) and thought that a paper entitled "Basic concepts of molecular biology for the experimental and clinical psychologist" might be useful. Lately, we have been working on a grant proposal entitled "Molecular mechanisms underlying odour-induced puberty in mice" and I have read a paper entitled "Antisense oligonucleotides: A new tool in neuroscience" (Pilowsky, et al., 1994, Clinicaland Exp, Phannacol.Physiol.,21, 935-944). Should there be a similar paper entitled "Antisense oligonucleotides: A new tool in experimental and clinical psychology"? My talk will focus on my struggle to understand the new molecular biology approaches to the study of behavioural neuroscience, with reference to our proposed research on odour-induced puberty. It will, no doubt, bear on some of the topics to be discussed in the Symposium on Future Directions in Psychology.

authors_raw: Reported Alcohol Consumption Levels and Selective Processing of Alcohol
title: "I Don't Drink Very Often, But When I Do, I Drink A LOT!": Self-
abstract: Cues Among Dietary Restrictors Sherry Stewart and Sarah Barton Samoluk Previous research suggests important relationships between restrained eating and alcohol use/abuse in non-clinical samples. However, previous studies have failed to separately assess quantity and frequency measures of typical alcohol consumption, so that it remains unclear whether the observed positive relationship between dietary restraint and typical alcohol consumption levels is due to an increased quantity and/or frequency of alcohol consumption among highly restrained eaters. Moreover, previous research has demonstrated that non-clinical samples of restrained eaters demonstrate a cognitive bias favouring the selective processing of food cues. Whether this bias extends to the selective processing of alcohol cues remains unexplored. A sample of university students (78% F) was divided into three dietary restraint groups based on scores on the Restraint Scale. Subjects completed a reliable and valid measure of alcohol consumption, which assesses typical frequency of drinking episodes per week, and typical number of alcoholic drink equivalents consumed per drinking occasion. Subjects then completed a modified version of the Stroop task in which they colour- named balanced sets of food, alcohol, and neutral control words. Consistent with previous results, high restraint subjects reported drinking significantly more alcoholic beverages on a weekly basis than low restraint subjects. However, although high restraint subjects reported drinking significantly more alcoholic beverages per drinking occasion than low restraint subjects, there were no significant group differences in reponed drinking frequency. Thus, high restraint subjects appear to drink no more frequently than others, but when they do drink, they drink significantly greater quantities of alcohol. Also consistent with previous research high, but not low or moderate, restraint subjects required significantly longer to colour-name food vs. neutral control words. High, but not low or moderate, restraint subjects also required significantly longer to colour-name alcohol vs. neutral control words. Thus, high restraint subjects displayed a cognitive bias favouring the processing of alcohol cues as well as food cues. Implications of the results for risk for abusive drinking among high restraint university students will be discussed.

authors_raw: W. K. Honig and W. R. Matheson
title: Evidence For Transfer Between Spatial Locations And Their Pictured Representations In The Pigeon
abstract: In a rectangular room, three groups of pigeons first discriminated the end in which a baited feeder was located. The birds then discriminated between pictures of the ends of the room. For group AAB, this picture discrimination was congruent with initial training; for Groups ABA and ABB, it was incongruent. In the third phase, Group ABA found food in the initially baited feeder, while Groups AAB and ABB found food in the initially unbaited feeder. The latter groups learned the picture discrimination in the second phase more slowly than AAB, indicating transfer effects from room training to picture discrimination, which had not previously been obtained by Cole and Honig (1994). These data suggest that experience with nonreinforcement during the first phase influenced the degree of positive or negative transfer between the spatial and picture discriminations. In the third phase, all groups acquired the second feeder discrimination with few errors.

authors_raw: World, Rotated Objects? Patricia A. McMullen
title: How Relevant are Orientation-Specific Templates to Identification of Real-
abstract: The time to name a set of objects rotated in the picture-plane increases monotonically with object orientation. This effect supports normalization of object images to the upright. However, it diminishes as the objects are repeatedly named in different orientations. Using a set of visually-similar, nonsense objects, Tarr and Pinker (1989) demonstrated that this effect diminishes because orientation-specific templates are formed each time the objects are identified. Consequently, object images have decreasing angular distance to nonnalize to the nearest orientation-specifictemplate over successive identifications. However, Murray, Jolicoeur, McMullen & Ingleton (1993) failed to support this view for real-object naming. Are their results discrepant with those of Tarr & Pinker because orientation-specific template formation relies on identification of nonsense objects, or visually-similar objects? In new experiments reported here, Tarr & Pinker's findings were replicated when their objects were identified. However, no support for the formation of orientation-specific templates was found in two other experiments, one in which a set of visually- dissimilar nonsense objects was identified, and another in which a set of visually-similar real objects were identified. These findings undermine the relevance of orientation-specific template formation to object identification. However, human faces are probably the most visually-similar stimuli we normally identify. In a final experiment designed to optimize the formation of orientation-specific templates using real-world stimuli, a set of human faces is being identified.

title: Now You See It, Now You... See It Again? The Two Motion Percepts Illusion
abstract: If attention is exogenously cued, with a small lead time, to the location corresponding to one end of a quickly drawn line, then illusory line motion results with the percept that the line was drawn away from the cued location (Hikosaka, Miyauchi, & Shimojo, 1991). The current paper reports a similar illusion which replaces the line rendering with a dot moving at high velocity toward (or away from) the cued location. This method yields two percepts of motion, hence it is called the "Two Motion Percepts (TMP) illusion". The dot is (potentially) incorrectly perceived as travelling away from the locus of attention and then to double back and travel toward the locus of attention. The illusion was investigated through three experiments. Experiment 1 reports on the perceptions of 35 undergraduates when presented with the TMP displays. Over ninety percent of individuals reported the illusion. An extended version of Hikosaka et al. 's gradient model was proposed to account for both illusions. An additional assumption was required that attention not only speeds the initial transmission of signals, but that it also temporally extends signal transmission. Five predictions of the model were tested with eight observers in Experiment 2. All five predictions of the model were verified, and the data suggested that the effects of attentional cuing covered a large spatial area. Experiment 3, a control experiment with three observers, verified that cuing attention to a spatial location can affect stimuli as far as 1· away. Some implications of the extended model are discussed.

title: Space, The Final Frontier (Pavlovian). A Study Designed To Boldly Go Where No One Has Gone Before
abstract: There has been no research that I am aware of that has tested whether spatial contiguity of CS and US--unconfounded by delay of rft--influences Pavlovian conditioning. A unique box was designed that allowed CS-US distance to vary without varying CR-US interval (delay of rft). Four ring necked doves were autoshaped in this apparatus. Spatial contiguity of CS and US had no detectable effect

authors_raw: Tara L. Fidler and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Shock Induced Finickiness: A Preliminary Result
abstract:
There are many reports in the literature of finickiness in rats which have been shocked
but not in rats which have not been shocked. Finickiness is a suppression of
consumption of for example a bitter tasting solution relative to baseline consumption
levels. In studies reported previously (for example see Job and Barnes, 1995,
BehavioralNeuroscience,109, 106-116) the finickiness effect has been reported only
following the sorts of shock parameters that result in learned helplessness, 100+
shocks of 2-60s in duration. Previous work done in our lab had incorporated a one
hour shock session of 60 2-s shocks and had failed to show a relationship between
shock and alcohol consumption. In order to address the possibility that this failure
resulted from a failure to produce differences in level of stress or fear between our
shock and no-shock rats we sought independent evidence that animals shocked in our
experiments experienced more "stress" than animals which were not shocked. Using
our shock parameters in annickiness experiment we showed that shocked rats did
suppress their consumption of quinine relative to baseline levels of water consumption
while no-shock rats did not.
Tl?. Stop Waving The Frogs' Legs, Just Pass Me The Toast
Steve Shaw and Jane Anne Home
Proximity to high voltage transmission lines and to some electrical equipment can
expose the body to low-frequency electromagnetic fields (EMFs). While EMFs are
reputedly able to interact with biological tissue, the effects are expected to range from
mild to non-existent at the field strengths normally encountered by most of us, although
there is some controversy about this. An exception to the supposed subtlety of action is
a sledgehammer effect reported five years ago by two British anaesthesiologists using a
well-known preparation, the frog's sciatic nerve. They claimed that a short exposure to
EMFs generated by a small coil surrounding the nerve is sufficient to disable it for
several minutes, after which transmission recovers fully. They attribute this result to
direct action by the applied EMF on some component of the nerve, which leads to what
they term "electromagnetic inhibition" (EMI). The mechanism of action has not been
investigated, but might be due to field-induced changes in the distribution of ions in or
around the axons, they say.
Sensing a sizeable rodent here while on the look-out for more modern material to
incorporate into a teaching lab, we have attempted to retrace the footsteps of these
pioneers. We are able to reproduce what looks like their basic EMI phenomenon,
although it is not possible to duplicate the exact conditions they used originally because
too little detail is given in the published methods. Our preliminary measurements have
led us to reject their explanation of EMI and to propose a rival theory, unveiled in
public for the first time here: the Electromagnetic Toaster (EMT) hypothesis of nerve
inhibition.

title: Conflicting Desire And The Child's Theory Of Mind
abstract:
We contrast the standard representational theory of mind approach to the understanding
of mental states with an alternative view which has it that theory of mind tasks require
executive functioning or the inhibition of more 'cognitively salient' information. In the
first experiment 3-5-year-olds were tested on their understanding of desire in others
either when they themselves held a strong and conflicting desire or when they had no
strong desire. Results showed that under the condition of having a strong and
conflicting desire, only 5-year-olds were able to recognize that another person might
desire something different In contrast when the children themselves held no strong
desire, even 3-year-olds were able to judge another's desire correctly. The second
experiment compared 3-year-olds' performance on a standard false belief task with that
on an equivalently structured desire task in which subjects had again to inhibit their
own strong and conflicting desire. Results showed similar performance on the
traditional false belief task and the new conflicting desire task.

authors_raw: John Barresi, Carol Selig and Chris Moore
title: Beyond Immediate Self-Interest: Or, What Good Comes From Having A Theory of Mind?
abstract: The purpose of this research was to investigate the early development of prudence and altruism involving future situations. The study tested the hypothesis that these are interrelated phenomena and that both should develop at approximately four years of age. Using a modified delay of gratification paradigm, 3- to 5-year-olds were tested on their ability to forgo a current desire for some stickers in order to further their own future interests or the current or future interests of a research assistant. Results showed that in current situations, altruism was unrelated to age. However, prudence and altruism involving the future situations were correlated with one another and with age. Children under four years of age performed significantly less well than the older children on the prudence task (F(l, 49) = 15.75; p<.001) and on the altruism task involving future situations, (F(l, 49) = 33.24; p<.001). Between three and five years, prudence and altruism showed a similar developmental pattern with performance increasing at age four. In Defense of Defenses: A User's Guide to the Psyche ' 'FW. title: 92 (humour) in study two
abstract: These results indicate that defense mechanisms can be reliably assessed using a standardized clinician-guided q-sort technique. The applications of these results to the assessment of normal and abnormal personality will f-¼~h:----. be discussed.

authors_raw: Alan MacDonald, Sherry Stewart, Sarah Samoluk, Susan Sweet
title: Gender Differences In Consistency of Alcohol Consumption Measures
abstract:
Kidorf et al. (1990) found that the self-reports of males were highly predictive of their
analogue alcohol consumption, whereas the self-reports of females bore no relation to
their analogue consumption. The present study examined the effects of social context
on the consistency between self-report and analogue alcohol consumption in males vs
females.
Male and female university students, assigned to either a social or alone drinking
context, provided self-reports of alcohol consumption, and then participated in an
analogue drinking test disguised as a beverage taste-rating task. Alcohol consumption
dependent measures were analyzed as a function of context and gender. Preliminary
correlations show that self-reports were more consistent with analogue drinking in both
males and females in the social vs the alone context. Consistency between analogue
drinking and self report was approximately equal for males and females in both
contexts. Possible reasons for differences between these results and those reported by
Kidorf et al. (1990) are discussed.

authors_raw: B. W. Frankland & A. J. Cohen
title: Boundary Formation and Melodic Memory
abstract: Most people can recognize a large repertoire of melodics. The present two-part study examines how their melodic information is stored. In particular, it focuses on the role of boundary formation. It first addresses the issue of determining where boundaries arise. Secondly, it examines how boundaries at different positions in a melodic excerpt affect memory. Following comparable work in psycholinguistics (Jarvella, 1970; Wingfield & Butterworth, 1984), it was hypothesized that excerpts not straddling a boundary would be more easily recognized than excerpts which straddle a boundary. In Part 1 of the Experiment, 20 subjects listened to a familiar melody and depressed a key each time they detected a boundary. Note-by-note boundary profiles generated for each subject were significantly intercorrelated (with the exception of one subject). Group profiles were highly consistent across three presentations of the song. In Part 2, subjects heard 16 four-note excerpts from the song (50% contained an error) and judged whether the exerpt belonged to the melody. Generally, excerpts which straddled the boundary led to poorer performance. Excerpts that did not contain boundaries and excerpts that ended with a boundary led to higher performance. Thus, the memory structure for melodies entails a systematic boundary formation process not unlike that found for language. While this proposal is not new for music, the current methodology permits its quantitative investigation by the use of boundary assessment and subsequent memory testing that is tailored to the individual subjects within the study.

authors_raw: Karina Davidson, Yori Gidron and Bill Chaplin
title: The Statistical Versus Clinical Significance of Medication, Psychotherapeutic and Cardiac-Rehabilitation Trials with Post-MI Patients
abstract: This study compared the methodology and efficacy of Medication, Psychological and Mixed (cardiac-rehabilitation) trials with post-myocardial-infarction (Ml) patients, using meaningful effect sizes. Twenty-four randomly selected and representative trials (eight per each trial type) were compared regarding prevention of reinfarcts and mortality. Tests of statistical significance (X2) and clinical significance (number of prevented reinfarcts or deaths in treatment group per 100 people), and a comprehensive methodological assessment scale were employed. Medication trials obtained statistically significant effects at the highest level. However, Psychological trials prevented 10 times more reinfarcts (2.9) and three times more mortalities (1.7) per 100 people than did Medication tirals (.3, .5, repsectively). Medication trials obtained higher mean quality ratings (38) than Psychological (31) and Mixed trials (33). Thus, tests of statistical significance may be misleading when comparing clinical trials with different sample sizes. Psychological trials should increase their sample sizes and improve their methodology to reveal their stronger clinical significance with greater reliability and validity.

title: Caught in the Web: Psychology on the Internet in 1995
abstract:
The past year has seen a tremendous amount of activity on the World Wide Web,
currently one of the most popular segments of the Internet. Individuals, businesses,
governments and institutions of all sorts are making information about themselves
available electronically, taking advantage of the hypertext and graphic capabilities of
web browser software. Today it is possible to tour the Louvre Museum, book a hotel
room in San Francisco (and view the room, of course), or order fresh Nova Scotia
lobsters delivered anywhere in North America, all from your computer workstation.
Tomorrow even more will be possible. We should be taking advantage of this medium,
and in this presentation, I will demonstrate a Web site (Home Page) for the Dalhousie
Psychology Departtnent.
