authors_raw: Bruno J. Losier, C.D. Bilsbury & A. Richman
title: The Justification For Depression Scales: A Bibliometric Analysis
abstract:
Depression is a topic of increasing importance in both research and therapy. Its
measurement has for three decades dependent largely on two rating scales, the Hamilton
and the Beck. These warhorses vie for acceptance as the gold standard. Is there
justification for their preeminence? This bibliometric analysis begins to answer this
question. What are the time trends in citations? Are the Hamilton and Beck co-cited? Is
the selection of one justified over the other? When both scales are used, are the results
compared? We have used PSYCLIT and Science Citation Index to count citations to each
of these scales during each year over the past two decades. We examined representative
samples of 20 publications from 1974 to 1979 and 20 more from 1989 to 1994. Neck and
neck over the years, the number of citations for each scale steadily increased. However,
there was little growth in the concurrent use of both scales, and of these publications less
than a quarter provided analyses of their differential outcomes. Reading the source
publications showed little if any discussion of the rationale underlying the selection of
these scales and little if any qualification of the overall results of the investigation as being-
specific. Thompson's (1989) content analysis of depression scales by domains shows
major differences between the Beck and the Hamilton. These differences are obscured in
the articles reviewed. Each paper regards the scale they used as the gold standard without
justifying its selection. We recommend that the particular symptoms that brought the
patient to therapy should be considered in assessing clinical change. Clinimetric advances
(Feinstein, 1987) have recognized that the measurement of change must be 6ased on the
clinical phenomena presented by the individual patient, rather than utter dependence on
alterations in scores from psychometrically derived constructs.

title: Attentional Biases In Peripersonal And Extrapersonal Space: An Analysis Of Line Bisection And Visual Orientation Performances In A Group Of Young Adults
abstract: In recent decades, attentional biases along three-dimensional spatial coordinates have been a topic of increased interest. However, physical boundaries, as well as methodological restraints have precluded a thorough and systematic investigations of human performances along these planes. The currentstudy proposed to address some of these limitations by investigating line bisection and visual orientation performances along horizontal and vertical planes in peripersonal (within hand's reach) and extrapersonal (outside of hand's reach) space. Subjects bisected a total of 72 computer-generated horizontal and vertical lines projected in each spatial location. Furthermore, on any given trial, horizontal lines appeared at the top, centre or bottom of the screen, while vertical lines appeared at the left, centre or right of the screen. As well, a bisecting cursor appeared at either end of each line. In the visual orientation task, subjects reponded to 229 validly or invalidly cued targets in each spatial location. On any given trial, targets appeared at the top left, top right, bottom left or bottom right of the screen. Overall, horizontal and vertical lines were bisected to the left and above the midpoint, respectively. Moreover, analyses of variance revealed that bisecting biases varied significantly in peripersonal and extrapersonal space contingent upon bisector and line position on the screen. Overall, subjects responded more quickly to validly than invalidly cued targets. Analyses of variance revealed that response time also varied significantly and differently along horizontal and vertical planes in peripersonal and extrapersonal space. These findings are discussed in light of current models of visual attention. ~ O.C.E.A.N. - ology 1-u Michael Wm. MacGregor and Karina Davidson The five-factor model is a taxonomy of ~rsonali~ traits in terms of five broad dimensions: Openness to Experience, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism. Although evidence suggests that these five factors are useful in studying and modelling normal personality, there is less consensus regarding their ability to model abnormal personality, particularly Eersonali!}'.disorders. This study investigated the relationship between the five factor model ofpersonality and personality disorders. Two hundred and fifty undergraduate students completed the Personality Diagnostic Questionnaire-Revised (PDQ-R)as a measure of personality disorders and the Big Five Inventory (BFI-54) as a measure of the five factors of personalty. Results are discussed with respect to the theoretical relationship between personality disorders and the five factor model. 1'28. Channel Surfing Without A Remote Control Tracy L. Taylor(l), Dennis P. Phillips(l,2), & Susan E. Hall(l). Departments of Psychology(l) and Otolaryngology(2) In an auditory gap detection task, three highly practiced listeners were presented with pairs of noise stimuli. Each stimulus consisted of leading and trailing noise bursts which bordered a temporal gap interval: In the test stimulus, this gap interval was varied systematically; in the reference stimulus this gap interval was maintained at a duration of 1 msec and was inaudible. The subject made a forced choice manual discrimination to indicate which of the two randomly presented stimuli contained the test gap. The dependent variable of interest was the shortest temporal gap that could be detected reliably. Subjects performed this task under conditions in which the perceptual channel used to define the leading and trailing edges of the gap was manipulated with respect to central frequency and laterality. In other words, perceptual channels were defined according to frequency and ear, such that the leading and trailing noise hurts shared no or complete spectral overlap and were presented to the same or to different ears. Results demonstrated poorer gap detection for across-channel (>20 msec) than for within-channel(< 5 msec) conditions, indicating that having to extract temporal information across frequency or ear limits the ability to resolve a temporal gap. It is noted that the thresholds observed in the across-channels conditions could be responsible for the +20 to +40 msec voice onset time (VOT) that typically marks the categorical boundary between voiced and voiceless stop consonants. ------------------------~~,· '11tl,, 1111

title: Sex Differences: It's Not the Size, It's the Shape
abstract:
As long ago as 1880, English surgeon James Crichton-Brown reported slight
differences in the brain anatomy of men and women - a few more neurons here in one
sex, a few more there in the other. But by far one of the most frequent findings
through the years has been that th~us the main fiber tract connecting the
callOSE!Jl...:
two cerebral hemispheres - tends 1o larger in women than in men. It has also been
found to vary in shape between the sexes. Researchers, such as deLacoste-Utamsing
and Holloway (1982), have speculated that if a larger corpus callosum implies a larger
number of fibers interconnecting cortical areas and the number of interhemispheric
fibers correlates inversely with lateralization of function, then this finding would be
consistent with neuropsychological theories that men's brains are more lateralized for
function than women's. We review the last ten years of anatomical and radiological
studies of sex differences in inter-hemispheric fibers pointing out inconsistencies in the
methodologies employed. We conclude that certain segments of the corpus callosum
linked to posterior association areas are greater in women. Whether these relate to
functional asymmetries remains to be seen. Stay tuned!

title: The Key Is In The Pee
abstract: Individual animals can be identified by their urine odours. To determine the influence of genetic, microbiological and dietary factors in producing urinary odours of individuality in rodents, a series of experiments were undertaken using two different paradigms. Results from a habituation-dishabituation task and a go/no go operant task revealed that rats and mice with genetic differences at the major histocompatibility complex have urine odours which can be discriminated by conspecifics. Bacteria are necessary for the production of these urine odours and the removal of different types of bacteria by drug treatments changes the nature of the urine odour, however, the specific nature of the odour-producing bacteria could not be determined. Mice and rats on different diets produce urine odours which are easier to discriminate than urine odours from mice of different MHC genotypes. These results indicate that odours of individuality are multidimensional and that when dietary cues are available, they may be more influential than genetic factors in providing discriminable cues.

title: Imagine That
abstract:
Previous research suggests that semantic processing using imagery reduces priming on
implicit retrieval tasks of memory while increasing performance on explicit tasks.
Eighty undergraduates were randomly assigned to study conditions involving the
reading of words, imagery of the words, imagery of different words, and reading of
words followed by reading different words. The implicit and explicit retrieval tests
were word stem completion and cued recall, respectively. Preliminary results will be
discussed.

title: Infant "Surprise" Expressions as Coordinative Structures
abstract:
Although infant "surprise" facial expressions involve opening the mouth and raising the
eye-brows, these movements may represent movement synergies. Thirteen infants (5-7
months-old) were videotaped during toy presentations. Facial movements were coded
for each infant. For target segments, the facial expressions were identified and coded
using Ekman and Friesen's Facial Action Coding System. Two 4-sec target and two
4-sec control segments were coded while the infant's mouth was open (AU 26 or AU
27). All segments were coded for brow, eyelid, and nose movements. All infants
produced no brow movements during control episodes. Mouth opening does recruit
brow raises in nonemotional situations.

authors_raw: Mario E. Guido, Benjamin Rusak and Harold Robertson
title: Studies On The Expression Of Immediate-Early Genes (IEGs) In The Mammalian Circadian System
abstract: The mammalian suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the anterior hypothalamus is the site of a endogenous pacemaker that is responsible for the timing and overt expression of daily rhythms. Light-dark cycles synchronize circadian rhythms. The SCN receives 2 projections with visual information, one originating in the retina and the another one originating in a retinorecipient area of the lateral geniculate nucleus. A molecular correlate of photic entrainment is the rapid and transient induction of various IEGs in SCN cells. IEGs encode for a family of transcription factors such as Fos and Jun proteins; they may act as intracellular third messengers in the transduction of external signals to DNA. IEG proteins form complexes that bind to specific DNA sequences, promoting transcriptional activation of target genes. The pattern of IEG proteins induced by light is similar to the pattern of retinohypothalamic fiber terminals in the SCN. Fos and Jun proteins could be regulating transcriptionally the expression of genes responsible for the timing of intracellular circadian pacemaker. In order to obtain more information about the role that IEGs could be playing in the SCN, we have tried to characterize the photic and circadian expression of 4 different IEGs: c-fos, fosB, c-jun and junB by in situ hybridization. At first, we studied the time course of expression of these genes in response to a light pulse, during the dark phase, when light has phase shifting effects. Second, we assessed the circadian expression of the mentioned genes at different circadian times, during the subjective day and night. JunB appears to play a special role at the beginning of subjective day.

title: Orientational Singletons Evoke Facilitation As Measured Using Illusory Line Motion
abstract: A growing body of research suggests that a highly salient featural singleton will only capture attention if the singleton is task-relevant (Yantis et al., ARVO .94). Such research, which uses visual search methodologies, assumes that the attentional capture is the result of a deliberate shift in attention towards the informative singleton. If the singletons are not informative, no facilitative advantage is said to be observed to such items. The present experiments probed for the existence of spatial facilitation around task- irrelevant orientational singletons using illusory line motion (Hikosaka et al., ARVO .91). Under task-irrelevant conditions, orientational singletons were found to produce facilitation as detected by illusory line motion. The observed difference in results between visual search and illusory line motion may result from the measurement of different types off acilitation by these two tasks.

authors_raw: Farhad Dastur, Richard Brown, Patrick McGrathl, Donald Brown
title: The Scent Of A Pregnant Woman: Rescent And Scentsitive Procedures For Making Scentse Out Of Changes In The Smell And Taste Scentses
abstract: Women frequently report changes in odor and taste perception during pregnancy. Despite the potential such changes have in modifying food intake--thereby affecting nutritional health--there are a scarcity of comprehensive, well-designed studies examining the specific nature and direction of smell and taste alterations associated with pregnancy. In this prospective, controlled, longitudinal study, 64 women (32 pregnant subjects, 32 control subjects) will be assessed four times over a nine month period: (1) 6-10 weeks postconception (middle of first trimester); (2) 14-18 weeks postconception (middle of second trimester); (3) 28-32 weeks postconception (early to middle of third trimester); and (4) 4-8 weeks postpartum. Non-pregnant women will be tested at approximately equivalent intervals from the time of their first assessment and at consistent times during the early part of their menstrual cycle. Alterations in smell and taste will be assessed using a battery of objective psychophysical tests, questionnaires, rating scales, and diet records designed to measure the following: (a) odor and taste thresholds; (b) odor and taste identification; (c) qualitative ratings of odor and taste preference, familiarity, intensity, arousal, and palatability; (d) odor-evoked memories; (e) the development of food cravings and aversions; and (f) the preferences of the smells and tastes of non-food items. For the pregnant women, test battery results will be correlated with their serum honnone levels of estradiol, progesterone, human chorionic gonadotropin, oxytocin, and prolactin in an effort to reveal hormonal influences on the smell and taste senses. Additionally, the degree of pregnancy sickness during the first trimester will be assessed and will be correlated with performance on the perceptual tests. We hypothesize that changes in smell and taste perception during pregnancy may be the sensory mechanism underlying food cravings and aversions. We also hypothesize that during early pregnancy superior smell and taste performance should be positively correlated with the degree of severity of pregnancy sickness. These hypotheses are based on the theory that pregnancy sickness is a maternal evolutionary adaptation designed to avoid the ingestion of naturally occurring toxins, thus protecting the embryo from exposure to potentially harmful substances. A potential application of this research will be to help provide a rational basis for updating the nutritional guidelines available to pregnant women. ----------~----- ----------- ----------~, ..·---,---rr-n,--~--

authors_raw: C.D. MacKinnon and W. G. Tatton
title: Voluntary Finger Movements Can Be Controlled By The Supplementary Motor Area Without Participation Of The Primary Motor Cortex
abstract: Prominent direct projections from the supplementary motor area (SMA) to the spinal cord in monkeys have been hypothesized to drive movements independently of the primary motor cortex (Ml) (Dum and Strick, 1991). We tested this hypothesis by determining the location and time course of intracerebral activity time-locked to the generation of voluntary finger movements by collecting EEG for dipole source analysis (DSA) and positron emission tomography (PET) simultaneously. Experiments were conducted to directly compare unilateral serial, repetitive finger opposition movements (SMs) performed at 2 Hz to the same movements performed intermittently (™s) at 0.2 to 0.08 Hz. EEG epochs were obtained during the IMs by back-averaging 2.5 sec prior to the onset of of EMG activity and 0.5 after EMG onset. H2150 PET scans were performed during the collection of the EEG epochs and foci co-registered on MRI and the DSA solutions. PET data demonstrated that the Sms involved significant activation of the contralateral Ml together with other structures in the cerebello-thalamocortical pathways and minimal activation of the SMA with minimal activation of Ml. The SMA dipole orientation suggested that the ™s were generated by neuronal populations in the dorsal tier of the cingulate sulcus. We hypothesize that ™s are generated via basal ganglia-thalamocritical pathways which do not involve Ml and are separate from the pathways generating SMs. Both the SMs and IMs are presently being investigated in patients with Parkinson's disease and neuroleptic induced Parkinsonism. (C.D.M. supported by Ontario Ministry of Health Fellowship #04515).

title: Object Recognition And Orientation: Basically Independent While Specifically Dependent
abstract: The time to name line drawings of objects rotated in the picture-plane show effects of orientation which support normalization of the image through the shortest angular distance from the upright (Jolicoeur, 1990) prior to identification. However, this notion is logically unsound (Corballis, 1988) in that the determination of the shortest direction requires information pertaining to the top of the object, which relies on knowing the identity of the object (Rock, 1973). How then is an image rotated for identification if the rotation process requires knowing the identity? The current research suggests a resolution to this conundrum lies in the level to which the object image must be identified. Using a word-picture verification task, Rosch, Mervis, Gray, Johnson, and Boyes-Braem, 1976 and Jolicoeur, Gluck, and Kossyln (1984) have shown that objects are initially identified at a basic level (i.e., "dog") and that subordinate identification (i.e., "collie") requires further visual processing of the image. We report a word-picture verification task which extends these findings by showing that access to a basic level representation is orientation invariant, while access to the subordinate representation is sensitive to image orientation. These findings place the locus of the orientation effects in the visual processing required for subordinate access (Jolicoeur, et al, 1984). We speculate that these additional visual processes may include the processing the spatial relations of object parts which is known to be sensitive to stimulus orientation (McMullen and Jolicoeur, 199?; Koriet and Norman, 1989).

authors_raw: Farhad Dastur, Richard E. Brown, Denise Wilesl and Iain McGregor
title: Effects of Dopaminergic Dl, D2 and D3 Receptor Agonists and Antagonists on Body Temperature, Activity Level and the Emission of Ultrasonic Vocalizations by Rat Pups
abstract: Infant rodents emit ultrasonic vocaliz.ations (USVs) when they are cold, isolated from their littermates or roughly handled. As dopamine Dl receptor agonists induce hyperthemria, while D2 and D3 agonists induce hypothermia, we predicted that the D 1 agonists would reduce USVs while D2 and D3 agonists would increase USVs in 11-13 day old rat pups. Pups were injected with drugs or saline and maintained at 33-36° C for 30 min before a 5 min USV test was conducted in a test chamber at 22-24° C. Body temperature (auxillary) was measured with a surface probe before and after USV recording. USV s were monitored by a QMC bat detector coupled to a computer and activity was recorded by an infrared detector. Dur ing the 5 min test, the body temperature of the pups given the high dose (2.5 mg/kg) of the D 1 agonist SKF 81297 was unexpectedly lower than the controls and the pups emitted fewer USVs and had decreased activity levels. As SKF increases body temperature in adults, the reduced body temperature in pups was unexpected. The low dose of SKF 81297 (0.25 mg/kg) had no significant effect on body temperature, USVs or activity. SCH 23390 (0.3 mg/kg) but not Raclopride (0.3 mg/kg) reversed the effect on temperature. The D2 agonist Quinpirole caused a significant decrease in body temperature at both low (0.3 mg/kg) and high (3.0 mg/kg) doses and both doses increased activity levels, but only the high dose reduced USVs. These effects were reversed by Raclopride, but not SCH 23390. The D3 agonist 7-OH-DPAT reduced temperature at the high dose (1.0 mg/kg) but not at the low dose (0.1 mg/kg). Both doses increased activity levels and this effect was reversed by both antagonists. Only the high dose of 7-OH-DPAT reduced USVs and this effect was reversed by Raclopride but not by SCH 23390. Neither the D 1 antagonist SCH 23390 (0.3 mg/kg) nor the D2 antagonist Raclopride (0.3 mg/kg) given alone altered body temperature or USVs, but SCH 23390 reduced activity levels. The DI, D2 and D3 agonists all reduced USVs at high doses and, across all groups, as body temperature dropped, there were fewer USVs, but higher activity levels. These results indicate that the drop in body temperature during the 5 min test was not the most important stimulus for emission of USVs in 11-13 day old rats. The overall reduction in USVs by dopamine agonists suggests that the antianxiety or antidepressant actions of these drugs, their reinforcing properties, or their effects on respiration alter USV emission in infant rats, rather than their thermoregulatory actions.

title: Bill Chaplin
abstract:
An Alternative Approach to Statistical Inference: Rejecting a Null
Hypothesis is as Boring as this !J:alk T 1~
Some thoughts on an alternative approach to statistical inference will be presented.

authors_raw: W.R. Matheson, Simon Gadbois, and W. K. Honig
title: Initial Steps Toward A Model Of Numerosity Discrimination In The Pigeon
abstract:
A series of studies has shown that pigeons can estimate the relative and absolute
numerosities of arrays of small elements, and can discriminate related properties such
as uniformity. These abilities have been demonstrated with both successive and
simultaneous discrimination procedures, and with elements which differed on
dimensions including color, shape, and size. We have begun to develop an information
processing model to provide a framework to explain these results, and, more
specifically, to analyze relative numerosity discriminations as secondary to concurrent
processes of absolute discrimination. Our model is based on the work of J. Gibbon,
R. Church, and C.R. Gallistel, particularly on Gibbon's scalar timing theory. We are
also using Monte Carlo simulations to derive predicted relative numerosity gradients
from data obtained during absolute numerosity tasks. In future research, the collection
of trial by trial data will assist in narrowing the range of possible models.

authors_raw: R.E. Brown, T. Moran & B. Slotnick
title: What a Rat's Nose Knows
abstract:
In experiment 1 an aqueous solution of amyl acetate was used as an odour cue for illness
using a one-bottle choice test with CS-US intervals ranging between 15 minutes and 8
hours. The results showed that amyl acetate was an effective cue for illness with CS-US
delays up to two hours. In experiment 2 the effects of the muscarinic antagonist
scopolamine HBr on odour aversion learning was examined using both one and two bottle
choice tests. Injection of scopolamine HBr (0.01 and 0.1 mg/kg) 30 minutes prior to the
conditioning trial suppressed fluid intake on the conditioning trial, but did not disrupt
acquisition of an illness-induced aversion to the amyl acetate solution. Injection of
scopolamine HBr (0.1 mg/kg) 15 minutes after the conditioning trial did not suppress fluid
intake and did not interfere with acquisition of an illness- induced odour aversion.
Experiment 3 assessed the effects of the NMDA receptor antagonist MK-801 on acquisition
of an odour aversion. MK-801 (0.05 mg/kg) did not interfere with acquisition of an odour
aversion. Experiment 4 examined the effects of odour exposure only on acquisition of an
illness odour aversion. The results demonstrated that fluid intake appears to be necessary
for an illness-induced odour aversion to form. These results challenge the Sensory and '.'.r(_.\l)}'t-
Gate Channeling theory that only tastes are capable of acting as cues for illness in food
aversion learning (Rusiniak, Hankins, Garcia & Brett, 1979). These experiments also
demonstrated that glutamatergic and cholinergic neurotransmitter systems appear to play ~-
little or no role in acquisition of an illness-induced odour aversion.
The Cannabinoid Agonist CPSS,940 Reduces Ultrasonic Vocalizations of
Infant Rats
Richard Brown, Farhard Dastur, Ashley McLennan and Iain McGregor
We injected 12 day old rats with the cannabinoid agonist CP55,940 (10, 100 or 1000
mg/kg) or with combinations of CP55,940 and the cannabinoid antagonist SR141716A (20
mg/kg), the opioid antagonist naloxone (1.0 mg/kg), the dopaminergic D1 antagonist
SCH23390 (0.5mg/kg) or the benzodiazepine antagonist flumazenil (20 mg/kg). CP55,940
dose-dependently reduced body temp., activity levels and the number of UV s emitted.
These effects were antagonised by SR141716A, but not by SCH23390, naloxone or
flumazenil These results suggest that CP55,940 has anxiolytic effects in rat pups through
an action that is specific to the cannabinoid receptor.

authors_raw: Pup Survival and Female Reproductive Success Richard E. Brown and Debora Cantoni
title: The Influence of the Male California Mouse (Peromyscus californicus) on
abstract: The California mouse is a monogamous rodent in which the male shows a high level of parental care. In our experiments, these mice were required to "forage" for food by running in a wheel. Male-female pairs could rear larger litters of pups to weaning than females alone and the pups with both parents grew faster. Single females who reared their pups to weaning had a longer inter-birth-interval than paired females. Our presentation will discuss the behaviour of the male which increases pup survival and facilitates implantation in the female.

authors_raw: Kara Firth, Paul Neumann and Richard Brown
title: Sexual and Maternal Behavior Correlates of Neuroanatomical Differences Between Inbred Mouse Strains
abstract: In rodents, the preoptic area (POA) of the hypothalamus has been demonstrated to have critical involvement in sexual and maternal physiology and behaviors. Structural differences in the POA have been found between two inbred mouse strains, DBN2J (02) and C57BU6J (B6) (Robinson et al, 1985). The B6 strain lacks a densely packed neuronal cluster (termed 'medioventral pars compacta') that is found in the D2 strain in the POA, on either side of the third ventricle; this compact nucleus may be homologous to The sexually dimorphic nucleus (SON) in rats. Preliminary results (Mathieson and Neumann, unpublished) suggest that this hypothalamic structural variant is an X-linked trait. These strains also show differences on sexual behavior measures such as male sexual performance (McGill and Manning, 1976) and maternal behavior measures such as infanticide (Svare and Broida, 1982; Svare et al, 1984). It is therefore possible that the structural variation between the two strains is responsible for the observed behavioral differences. Specific aims of this project are 1) to find experimental protocols that will reproduce published strain differences in sexual and maternal behaviors between D2 and B6 mice, and/or establish new ones, 2) to determine the mode of inheritance of these differences in classical crosses, and 3) to correlate variation in these behaviors with inheritance of the hypothalamic variant Therefore, this project provides a unique opportunity to consider a model system involving genetic variation and the resulting structural differences between mouse strains, and the postulated behavioral manifestations of these differences.

title: Effects of Various Planning Prompts on Searching an Unfamiliar Text
abstract:
The ability to search for and locate information in a textbook is a valuable literacy skill in
university. It has been shown that search accuracy improves when students are prompted
to plan their search, particularly if the question does not contain an explicitly stated search
term. Also, the selection of appropriate categories of text to search is facilitated by prior
knowledge of the topic area. This study was designed to assess the utility of three prompt
sets (general, specific, or general+ specific) on more and less difficult search tasks. It
was hypothesized that specific prompts alone would be as facilitative of search as the
general + specific prompts. Sixty introductory psychology students completed six search
questions in an unfamiliar introductory psychology textbook, with 45 receiving one of the
three prompt sets before each task. All had some level of prior knowledge, as they has
completed as least one term of Introductory Psychology. No differences in accuracy,
speed or category selection were found among the prompt conditions. Indexed quesitons
were completed faster than non-indexed questions, with fewer pages viewed, but there was
no interaction with the prompt conditions. Results from this study are compared with a
previous study from our lab, and issues related to textbook familiarity, time limits, and plan
generation skills are discussed.

authors_raw: Darcy Santor and Zindel V. Segal
title: Predicting Relapse in Depression from Improvement Rates Achieved in Therapy
abstract: Studies examining treatment efficacy for psychotherapeutic interventions and pharmacological agents have focused primarily on the degree to which symptoms have been reduced or ameliorated over the course of the treatment protocol. Few studies have examined how individual differences in (a) the rate of change or (b) week-to-week variability are related to termination or follow-up symptom scores. In the present study, levels of depression were assessed weekly and again at a 3-month follow-up using the Beck Depression Inventory in a sample (N=106) of depressed outpatients receiving cognitive-behavioural therapy. Results showed that change is extremely variable across individuals (see Figure 1) and that depressive severity at the 3-month follow-up was strongly related to (a) the rate at which individuals improved during therapy and (b) the amount of week-to-weekvariability. Although all individuals receiving therapy had termination BDI scores within a non-clinical range, the rate at which individuals improved was crucial in determining 3-month follow-up BDI scores. Results suggest the need for identifying factors that influence not just the amount of change achieved in therapy but the manner in which that change is achieved. Failure to obtain smooth and steady improvement within initial sessions may represent a risk factor for relapse. 6 1996 In-House Convention

authors_raw: Susan Jerrott, Sherry Stewart and Sandra Dunphy
title: A Closer Examination of the Time-Dependent Effects of Benzodiazepines on Memory
abstract: Research has indicated that all benzodiazepines impair perfonnance on tests of explicit memory while only lorazepam impairs perfonnance on implicit memory tasks. However, Stewart, Rioux, Connolly, Dunphy & Teehan (1996) tested memory performance at two times after drug administration (2 hours and 2.8 hours) and found that at "time 2" both lorazepam and oxazepam impaired implicit memory performance. However, it remains unclear whether these novel findings represent true time-dependent effects of oxazepam or whether the implicit memory task was being contaminated at time 2 through subjects' use of explicit memory strategies. The present study was designed to clarify Stewart et al.'s findings. Thirty subjects were administered an acute dose of oxazepam, lorazepam or a placebo and were tested on a series of explicit and implicit memory tasks identical to those in the previous study. However, in this study subjects were only tested at Stewart et al.'s "time 2" in order to rule out the "implicit memory contamination" explanation of the previous findings. If oxazepam persists in producing impairments on the implicit memory test without any prior exposure to the explicit memory task, then the results will provide support for the "time-dependence" interpretation. The results of this study are currently being analyzed and will be presented and discussed.

authors_raw: Survey P. McGrath, P. Forward, D. MacK.innon and J. Swann
title: Management of Frequent Recurrent Headache: A Community
abstract:
This community telephone survey was designed to detennine pain management patterns
used by frequent headache sufferers. 1856 eligible households were contacted and 336
eligible contacts were identified. 274 frequent headache sufferers (81.6%) participated and
describe their types of headaches, how they managed each and their attitudes to over the
counter (OTC) medication. Headaches were classified using the International Headache
Society's (IHS) system.
Females (n=33) reported an average of 1.9 types of headaches and males (n=141)
reported 1.5 headache types. There were 465 headaches; 129 tension headaches, 158
migraine headaches, 8 chronic tension headaches and 148 headaches which were
unclassifiable using IRS criteria. 56% of respondents used acetaminophen for tension type
and 60% used acetaminophen for migraine. ASA was used by 15% for tension and 5% for
migraine. Ibuprofen was used by 9% for tension and 8% for migraine. 9% used
acetaminophen or ASA with 8mg of codeine for tension headache and 15% used these
preparations for migraine. 1% used prescription medication for tension headache and 12%
for migraine. The perceived effectiveness of OTC medication was approxiamtely 70% for
tension headaches and 60% for migraine. Both tension headache and migraine headache
sufferers waited about 1 hour before taking any medication. Tension headache sufferers
waited until the headache was above 5 on a Oto 10 scale (4.6 for migraine). Level of pain
before taking medication was related to fear of tolerance and endorsement of stoicism. Fear
of drug abuse predicted amount of medication taken.
Most community headache sufferers use OTC medication to manage headaches. They
delay until pain is significant before taking any medication. Delay and amount of
medication taken is related to attitudes to medication.

authors_raw: Sonya Symons, Heather MacLatchy-Gaudet, and Jacqueline Milner
title: Slow Down or You'll Miss It!
abstract:
Locating information in text is the most prevalent workplace literacy task, yet one that most
people experience a lot of difficulty with. We know little about how to instruct students to
locate information in text, primarily because we know little about the cognitive strategies
that facilitate locating information. There are data suggesting that efficiency in locating
information is associated with taking a planful approach to search, thereby allocating more
time to locating the "best" places to find the information and less time to extracting
information by reading text Furthermore, reading rate data suggest that speed reading
does not exist (i.e., that information, and even gist, is lost when readers increase their
reading rate). We examined reading rate patterns of university students searching for
information in a textbook chapter presented on a computer. We found that fast searchers
differentiated between the pages of text that contained the targetted information (key
pages) and those that did not (non-key pages) by reading the key pages at a slower rate
than they read the non-key pages. Slower searchers skimmed both types of pages at
similarly high rates. This increased the likelihood that they would miss the information and
need to re-inpsect pages that they had already skimmed in order to locate the sought-after
information. These data will be compared to normative reading rate data on skimming
and scanning text in order to speculate on the interface of technolopgy and literacy.

title: On Becoming a Person
abstract:
What is a person? How does an animate or inanimate object become a person? In this talk
I will consider the kinds of criteria we use to decide whether and when something becomes
a person. I then consider and briefly discuss six ways for an object to become a person:
1) construction; 2) evolution; 3) development; 4) life-historical; 5) socio-cultural; and 6)
cognitive and moral ideals. From a scientific point of view, each of these approaches can
provide us with a deeper understanding of what it means to become a person.

title: The Effect of Dopamine Antagonist Injected into the Ventral Tegmental Area on Self-Stimulation in Rats
abstract:
It has been suggested that dopamine (DA) antagonists reduce the reinforcing effect of self-
stimulation (SS) by acting on the terminal region of the mesolimbic DA pathway. There is
a DA autoregulatory system in the ventral tegmental area(VTA) (AlO). DA antagonists act
on the autoreceptors on the soma and dendrites of DA neurons to block the inhibitory
feedback to the cell body, causing a significant increase in cell firing. Funher stimulation
depolarizes AlO neurons to the point of acute depolarization block. Thus, there is a
possibility that the depolarization block in the somatodendritic region of AlO neurons may
also be responsible for the attenuation of SS after systemic injection of DA antagonist
Gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) is an inhibitory neurotransmitter which causes hyper-
polarization. But under the situation of depolarization block, GABA should bring the
depolarized neurons back to their resting state, restoring the ability to produce action
potentials. To test this hypothesis, we injected DA antagonist raclopride alone or raclopride
mixed with GABA into VTA. The results of preliminary tests sfiow that injection of
raclopride alone greatly attenuated SS, whereas injection of Raclopride-GABA mixture did
not result in any suppression of SS. The results strongly support our hypothesis that the
suppression of SS after systemic injection of DA antagonist may be caused by
depolarization block in VTA.

authors_raw: Tara L. Fidler and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Stress and Ethanol Consumption in Rats
abstract:
Two ideas have motivated research on the effects of stress on ethanol consumption in
animals. The tension reduction hypothesis asserts that 1) ethanol reduces tension or anxiety
and 2) animals will learn to consume ethanol for its tension reducing properties. The relief
hypothesis asserts that the reinforcing effect of ethanol is enhanced in the presence of safety
signals. There is some support in the literature for the relief hypothesis, but a series of
experiments from our laboratory which included additional controls failed to support it
Experiments bearing on the tension reduction hypothesis are currently underway in our
laboratory.

title: Various Types of Linguistic Processings Studied with Event Related Brain Potential Measures: A Research Proposal
abstract: We plan to study charateristic changes in event-related potentials (ERP), recorded from normal subjects in response to the following types of linguistic processing tasks: 1) Semantic anomaly (between the main verb and its objective noun phrase, and/or between the subject and its main verb). 2) Various violations of local phrase structure rules (inversions between the main verb and its object NP, and/or between a preposition and its objective noun, faulty negative questions, etc.). 3) Faulty modifications of the matrix clause with subjacent clausal (infinitive or participle) phrases. 4) Negation and questions of semantically anomalous declaratives. 5) Bridging assumptions of antecedents. 6) Verification of an assertion about a state of affairs in the world. 7) Mental transformations for equivalent propositional functions. 8) Violation of quantity

authors_raw: Medicated Samples of Children With and Without ADHD: A Meta-Analytic Review Bruno Losier, Pat McGrath and Ray Klein
title: Error Patterns on the Continuous Performance Test in Unmedicated and
abstract: Losier et al provide the first quantitative analysis of the performance deficits of children with ADHD on the Continuous Performance Test and the effects of methylphenidate on these deficits. Unmedicated samples of children with ADHD produce significantly more errors of omission and commission than their non-AD HD cohort. Moreover, methylphenidate reduce both types of errors in children with ADHD and, in the single study using them, in normal children as well. Additionally, submitting the data to the framework of the Signal Detection Theory suggest that children with ADHD are less sensitive to the difference between the targets and nontargets than non-ADHD children, while the two groups do not differ in response bias. Correspondingly, methylphenidate improves sensitivity while leaving bias unaffected.

authors_raw: Alan MacDonald, Jan Baker and Sherry Stewart
title: "I'd Rather be Wasted than Worried": Coping-Related Drinking in High AnxietySensitive Individuals
abstract: Stewart and Pihl (1994) have shown that moderately intoxicating doses of alcohol reduce reactivity of highly anxiety sensitive subjects to signalled stressors compared to low anxiety sensitive controls. Based on these results, Stewart has suggested a modification of Sher's (1987) stress-response dampening theory to explain a heightened risk of alcohol abuse in high AS individuals (McNally, in press). The present study evaluated this theory by extending previous work on the sober reactivity of high AS vs. low AS subjects to a theoretically relevant stressor (i.e. hyperventilation, known to induce symptoms feared by high AS subjects) (Donnell & McNally, 1989), and the effects of alcohol administration on high AS vs. low AS subjects' responses to stress (Stewart & Pihl, 1994). Preliminary results suggest that high AS subjects administered a placebo showed greater reactivity to a hyperventilation challenge on affective and cognitive measures than low AS subjects. Furthermore, both high and low AS subjects who received a moderately intoxicating dose of alcohol showed less reactivity on somatic measures compared to placebo, but on affective and cognitive measures, the strongest reductions in reactivity were seen in high AS subjects. Thus, alcohol may be reinforcing for high AS subjects by eliminating their tendency to become fearful and catastrophize about aversive experiences.

authors_raw: Factors and Treatment Yori Gidron, Ph.D
title: PTSD Following Terrorist Attacks: Situational Analysis, Prevalence, Risk
abstract: Terrorist attacks (TA) consitute a growing source of trauma world-wide and particularly in Israel, and may result with post-traumatic stress-disorder (PTDS) in certain individuals. Several situational parameters are unique to TA (e.g., they are ideologically driven, affect a random sample of the population, are acute and "chronic" stressors). Approximately 26% of survivors (and rescuers) develop PTSD following TA. Certain situational parameters (degree of injury, degree of exposure) and personal parameters (e.g., prior depression) are significant risk factors for developing PTSD after TA. Group-therapy and debriefing are among the common treatment approaches, and provide opportunities for screening and treating survivors. These issues together with methodological limitations of previous studies will be discussed in this review, that is dedicated to the victims of terror in Israel and elsewhere. 12 1996 In-House Convention

authors_raw: Doug Symons, Elizabeth McLaughlin and L. Noiles
title: Age, Birth Order, and Emotional Coherence as Predictors of Performance on Theory of Mind Tasks
abstract: Object identity and object location are two tasks designed to assess a child's theory of mind, or knowledge of the mental states of others. These tasks were administered to 70 children, ranging in age from 32 to 76 months (2 to 6 years). Consistent with previous research, performance on each task improved with age. The relative effectiveness of using age as a continuous, versus a categorical, variable will be discussed. In contrast to recently reported findings, birth order and number of siblings could only predict performance on one component of the object identity task (representational change). Children were also given an opportunity to identify emotional experience throughout each task by pointing to one of six photographs of faces exhibiting different emotional expressions. Preliminary analyses with 30 subjects revealed that children with more sophisticated emotional knowledge performed better on the theory of mind tasks, and that items answered appropriately had a more coherent emotional response than those addressed incorrectly. The data address the potential interdependence of the cognitive and emotional features of social understanding.

title: What's the Point?
abstract:
Inf ants start to point between 10 and 14 months of age. The pointing gesture, which seems
·7 to arise de novo in the child's behavioral repertoire at this time, has been described as an
intentional communicative act which serves either to request an adult to get something
(protoimperative) or to share with an adult attention to an interesting sight (proto-
/"\"\JI,~ declarative). In both cases it is typically assumed that the point is used to redirect the
attention of the adult towards something of interest. If so, then the infant must be aware of
whether or not the adult has seen the target object or event. We have examined
experimentally whether infants are sensitive to the adult's perspective by presenting the
infant with a novel, interesting sight to the side when the mother is either looking at that
sight, looking to the opposite side, or reading and not interacting. Spontaneous pointing
was measured in all three conditions. In contrast to what common sense might suggest,
infant of 12 and 18 months point more when mother has already seen the novel target than
when her attention has been elsewhere. These results suggest that initially pointing does
not serve to redirect another's attention but rather acts as a 'comment' on an already shared
focus of attention.

title: Self-awareness in Preschoolers: The Development of Temporal Self- continuity
abstract: Self-continuity through time refers to a connection between the past, the present, and the future self. In this study, a group of 3-year-olds completed two tasks: a video self- recognition task to measure the past self and a delay of gratification task to measure the future self. Previous findings have indicated that older children are able to integrate their past and present selves as well as being able to choose greater rewards for their future self. Therefore, children who demonstrated self-recognition in the video task were expected to reliably choose the delayed reward in the future task. Results will be presented and issues for a second experiment will be discussed.

title: The Ultrasonic Vocalization Paradigm as an Animal Model of Anxiety
abstract:
Ultrasonic vocalizations (UVs) are emitted by rat pups and serve as a form of
communication. For example, infant rats emit UV s in response to many situations
including, separation from the litter, exposure to a cool environment, or tactile stimulation,
and such calls elicit retrieval responses from the dam. Based on these findings, it is evident
that UVs accompany a diverse range of ethologically relevant behaviours. Research has
demonstrated the utility of the UV paradigm as a novel animal model of anxiety. In this
respect, the UV paradigm has been revealed to be an effective method of screening novel
non-benzodiazepine based anxiolytics. This finding is critically important as established
animal models of anxiety have failed to externally validate the clinical effectiveness of non-
benzodiazepine based anxiolytics. The vast majority of studies which have used the UV
paradigm as an animal model of anxiety have focused on pharmacologically-induced
alterations of UV rate only, and have not evaluated changes across other UV parameters
(e.g., call frequency, call duration, time between calls). As a result, it is likely that many
drug-induced changes in UV call patterns go unnoticed. In our lab, we now have the
equipment necessary to concurrently measure different UV call parameters. Research
attempting to determine the effects of various anxiolytic and anxiogenic agents across a
wide range of UV call parameters will be discussed. The findings from these studies will
help determine the utility of the UV paradigm as an animal model of anxiety.

title: Teaching Via the WWW at Dalhousie: Are We Ready for Prime Time?
abstract:
A rapidly developing trend involves making teaching material available to students via the
World Wide Web. This has a particular attraction for those interested in distance education,
but is also useful to supply materials for regular university classes. Access to such material
can be open and available to anyone in the world, or restricted to certain users, such as
students registered in a particular class. This talk will demonstrate one such WWW site set
up for Psychology 2500 this year, and point out some of the hazards encountered in trying
to use this new technology.
IvVu\j .\.

title: Webicising Your Survey, or How To Adminster Surveys over the World Wide Web 1 ~ al l • · . . •--y:r 11Ill..1/'. {l
abstract: World Wide Web technology can act to decrease the amount of time, effort and costs ,AJi" V-..Q,"1 incurred through survey research. Additionally, this medium opens one's subject pool to a r~s;~f6-tft mich wider, or specialized, audience than can be accessed through conventional channels. This talk will introduce the audience to the requirements, methods, and potential problems that are encountered when carrying out survey research on the World Wide Web.

authors_raw: T.L. Taylor, D.P. Phillips, S.E. Hall, M.M. Carr and J.E. Mossop
title: Auditory Gap Detection: On the Trail of the Processes Involved
abstract:
Speech sounds occur as a nearly continuous stream, broken only by short periods of
silence (e.g., voice onset times). The durations of these "gaps" in the stream of sound are
critical to the identification of some consonants. The perceptual processes involved in the
discrimination of these silent intervals have typically been studied using an analytic "gap
detection" paradigm. in which the task of the subject is to discriminate between two bursts
of spectrally identicalsound that differ only in that one of them contains a very brief silent
interval ("gap"). We argue that the traditional gap detection paradigm is inappropriate to
study the processes involved in speech recognition, because the gaps that occur in speech
are bounded by spectrally dissimilarnoises. We have redesigned the gap-detection
paradigm to make it more speech-relevant, and have revealed two separable processes
involved in the detection of silent intervals in ongoing streams of sound.

authors_raw: Dennis Phillips, Tracey Taylor and Susan Hall
title: More About Auditory Gap Detection: On the Trail of Voice Onset Times
abstract:
Voice onset times (VOTs) are the durations between the onset of the (broadband)
consonantal burst and the onset of (low-frequency) voicing, and are what distinguish the
acoustic signals for stop consonants of the same place of articulation (e.g., da and ta).
VOT recognition is known to be categorical; studied with synthetic consonant-vowel
syllables, subjects label all consonants as the short-VOT element unless the VOT exceeds a
critical value (30-40ms), after which they label the consonant as the long-VOT element.
The reasons for the categorical nature of the percept (and for the 30-40ms VOT cutoff) are
not known, but its existence has been used as evidence that "speech is special". In the
present experiment, we modified the traditional gap-detection paradigm so that we could
study the processes involved in detecting VOT-like gaps in non-speech signals. Our data
provide a new insight into why VOT discrimination is categorical, and into why the
boundary between short and long perceived VOTs has the value that it does.
1996In-HouseConvention 15

title: Mental Rotation: It's All in the Name
abstract:
The time to name objects rotated in the picture plane increases linearly as a function of the
angular deviation from the upright for orientations between Oand 120 degrees. Effects of
orientation on naming latencies have been interpreted as support for orientation correction
of misoriented images prior to object identification. After repeated presentations of
stimuli, the magnitude of this effect of orientation tends to diminish. This diminishment
is specific to a stimulus set. Alternative explanations suggest that orientation correction
occurs after identification. Previous work has suggested that objects are initially identified
at a basic categorical level (i.e. dog), and further visual processing is required for
identification at a specific subordinate level (i.e. Collie). Orientation correction prior to
identification predicts large effects of orientation for naming at both the basic and
subordinate levels. Orientation correction after identification predicts minimal effects of
orientation for naming at the basic level. Forty-eight subjects named two blocks of 72 line
drawings depicting items from 6 basic categories. On the first presentation, subjects were
instructed to give the basic level name while on the second block subjects gave the
subordinate level name. Half the subjects were presented objects for a short exposure
followed by a visual mask. The other subjects were presented objects until named.
Results showed minimal effects of orientation for basic level naming with large effects with
subordinate level naming. No effect of exposure duration was found with naming
latencies. However, naming accuracy showed a significant linear decrease in the short
exposure condition which did not interact with the level of identification. Results support
the view that the majority of the effects of orientation are post-basic level identification.

title: Magnocellular and Parvocellular Feature Manipulations in Visual Search
abstract:
According to Treisman's feature integration theory, searching for a conjunction of simple
perceptual object features such as colour and form (e.g. a target red X among distractor
blue Xs and red Os), requires attention and so results in a serial as opposed to a parallel
search. Contrary to this prediction, McLeod, Driver and Crisp (1988) demonstrated that
search for a moving X among moving Os and stationary Xs, pops out in a parallel manner.
We speculated that this result may depend on a conjunction of features across separate
processing channels such as the magnocellular (responsible for movement detection) and
parvocellular (responsible for form detection) systems. To test this notion, a visual search
task in which targets conjoined two magnocellular features, depth and direction of
movement was designed. Stereo displays of dots moved in near and far depth planes. One
plane contained a target that moved in one direction. In this plane, the target was embedded
among distractors that moved in a different direction. Distractors in the other plane moved
in both the same and different directions to the target If parallel search results from a
conjunction of features across channels, then this search should be serial. Alternatively, if
search is parallel, whenever one magnocellular feature such as movement, needs to be
conjoined with any other feature, this search should be parallel.

title: It's a Small World After All: Invertebrates as Model Systems for Studying the Physiology of Behaviour
abstract: My laboratory is currently involved in three different research projects. 1) Parasitic effects on host behaviour. Parasites that alter host behaviour can be used as probes to help us understand how behaviour is regulated in normal animals. The tobacco homworm, Manducasexta, is parasitized by the wasp Cotesiacongregata.During parasitism host feeding is suppressed, apparently by manipulating the host's octopaminergic system. 2) Psychoneuroimmunology of insects. Activating an insect's immune system can induce a change in its behaviour. In the cricket, bacteria induce behavioural fever, but gregarines and tachinids do not Although raised temperatures increase survivial in crickets infected with bacteria, it has no effect on hosts infected with gregarines and tachinids. The latter 2 pathogens invoke a different type of immune response from that induced by bacteria. These different immunological mechanisms appear to have different effects on the central nervous system. 3) Communicating intent Cephalopods such as the cuttlefish Sepia officinalis, visually signal their tendency to attack. a temperatures The lack of sexual dimorphism in this species may help to explain this unusual behaviour.

authors_raw: Paul Campagnaro, George Scott, and Benjamin Rusak
title: Modulation by a Metabotrophic Glutamate Receptor Agonist of Phase Shifts Induced by VIP Injection Into the SCN
abstract:
The suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN) receive direct photic input via the retinohypothalamic
tract (RHI), and glutamate is strongly implicated as the primary neurotransmitter of this
pathway. Scott and Rusak (1996) found that 1S,3R-aminocyclypentane-1,3-dicarboxylic
acid (ACPD), an agonist at type 1/5 metabotropic glutamate receptors (mGluRl/5),
activates a large proportion of SCN neurons in vitro. These receptors are unusual among
mGluR's in that they are positively coupled to phosphatidylinositol (Pl) hydrolysis.
Winder et al. (1993) reported that application of ACPD to rat hippocarnpal slices,
potentiated cAMP formation stimulated by activating receptors coupled to adenylate
cyclase, including vasoactive intestinal polypeptide (VIP) receptors. Further, Piggins et al.
(1995) found that injection of VIP into the SCN region can phase shift hamster locomotor
activity rhythms. We therefore investigated whether PI hydrolysis stimulated by rnGluRl/5
activation can potentiate cAMP formation coupled to VIP activation in the SCN, and
thereby modulate the phase shifting effects of VIP. Male Syrian hamsters were implanted
with cannulas aimed at the SCN region and housed in constant dim red light, in cages
equipped with running wheels monitored by a microcomputer. Animals were injected with
VIP (150 pmol), ACPD (1.5 mM), VIP+ ACPD (150 pmol & 1.5 mM), or saline vehicle
(0.9%) at one of three circadian times (CT), CT6, CT13, or CT20 (where CT12 = activity
onset). Injection of ACPD alone resulted in phase shifts which did not differ from vehicle
injections. VIP injections produced small phase delays at CTl 3, advances at CT20 and no
shifts at CT6. Coinjection of ACPD potentiated VIP-induced phase shifts selectively at
CT13. These data support the hypothesis that IP hydrolysis coupled to mGluRl/5
activation can modulate the phase shifting effects of VIP receptor activation, early in the
subjective night, possibly via potentiation of cAMP formation. (Supported by grants from
the US AFOSR and NSERC of Canada)

title: Gene Transfer Into The Rodent Nervous System
abstract:
The study of nerve cell death has become a major concern in neuroscience research over
recent years. In particular, there has been a concerted effort to elucidate the cellular
mechanisms of apoptosis, a distinct form of neuronal death. Extensive studies using
isolated cells in vitro have identified 'families' of genes that appear to be important in
defining the pathways leading to apoptotic cell death. Up- or down-regulation of these
genes has been shown to both increase and decrease cell survival. However, a
fundamental question is how relevent are these genes in modulating cell death in vivo. In
this context, I will describe an approach we are taking in our laboratory to clirectly transfer
genes to rat motor neurons in order to examine their effects on subsequent experimentally-
induced neuronal damage.
18 1996 /n-House Convention

authors_raw: Mario Guido, Donna Goguen, Benjamin Rusak and Harold Robertson
title: When the Lights are Turned Off, Something Stays Turned On
abstract:
The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of mammals is the site for the endogenous pacemaker
that drives many biological rhythms. Light plays a key role in the mechanism of
entrainment Cells in the retinorecipient area of the SCN respond to retinal illumination with
changes in both firing rates and immediate-early gene (IEG) expression. Fos and JunB are
among the most studied IEGs; they exhibit a pronounced circadian variation in sensitivity to
the inductive effects of light in the SCN, and they could be involved in mediating the
response of the SCN to photic cues. We found thatjunB mRNA is expressed in the SCN
of hamster in response to light throughout the subjective night and during the early
subjective day (after 48 h in constant dark). Light-evoked expression was found
throughout the SCN but was usually stronger in the ventral half. We were surprised to
find, however, that junB mRNA was also expressed spontaneously in darkness during the
transition from the projected night to the projected day. Its spontaneous expression began
abruptly and persisted for at least 4 h into the projected day, and was always more robust
in the dorsal SCN. Both the light-induced and spontaneous expressions of junB mRNA are
translated into protein in SCN cells, with similar anatomical distributions. The expression
of junB mRNA may play a role both in mediating circadian responses to photic stimuli and
in spontaneous oscillation of elements of the SCN pacemaker.
(Supported by MRC of Canada and CONICET of Argentina).

authors_raw: Shui-Wang Ying, Benjamin Rusak, Philippe Delagrange, Elisabeth Mocaer, Pierre Renard and Beatrice Guardiola-Lemaitre
title: Is There an Effective Melatonin Receptor Antagonist?
abstract: We studied the effects of drugs related to the pineal hormone melatonin on neuronal firing activity in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), intergeniculate leaflet (IGL) and other brain areas in urethane-anesthetized Syrian hamsters. We tested melatonin and two naphthalenic derivatives of melatonin, a putative agonist (S20098) and a putative antagonist (S20928). Both melatonin and S20098, when given by systemically, were able to suppress firing rates of cells in a similar dose-dependent manner, but the effects of S20098 were longer lasting. Iontophoresis of melatonin dose-dependently depressed spontaneous and light- evoked activity of cells in the SCN and IGL, while iontophoresis of S20098 was relatively ineffective, probably because it is a poorly charged compound. S20928 (2.0-10 mg/kg) alone decreased firing rates of light-sensitive cells the SCN and IGL; however, low doses (<2.0 mg/kg) of S20928 partially blocked the effects of melatonin agonists on most cells tested. The nonselective serotonin antagonist metergoline did not block the effects of either melatonin agonist, indicating that melatonin agonists did not act on serotonin receptors. Both melatonin agonists and antagonists were less effective when applied to cells in the hippocampus and dorsal lateral geniculate nucleus. These results indicate that S20098 is an agonist acting probably on melatonin receptors in the Syrian hamster brain. S20928 may have mixed agonist/antagonist properties, but at low doses appears to function as an antagonist at melatonin receptors in the SCN and IGL, which are critical to the regulation of circadian rhythms. 1996 ln-House Convention 19

title: Illusory Line Motion, What Turns You On?
abstract:
When a line is presented all at once with one end near the location of a peripheral stimulus
or cue, the line appears to be drawn away from cue. We sought to determine if illusory line
motion (lLM) is turned on by visual attention. Lines were presented as probes in the
context of a detection task in which attention was endogenously or exogenously attracted to
one of 2 circles by a central (arrow) or peripheral (brightening of one of the circles) cue. On
most trials the cue was followed by a target (dot inside one circle) requiring a simple
detection response. On the remaining trials, a line was presented, all at once or drawn over
time, between the two circles and the subject indicated the direction and strength of
perceived motion. Performance on the detection task revealed that attention was attracted to
the cued location by both types of cues. ILM was strongly aroused by exogenous attention
but was left unmoved by endogenous attention.
The Gradient Model of Illusory Line-Motion: Converging Support from
Multiple Effects
William C. Schmidt
If a line appears all at once terminating nearby a previously cued spatial location, then
motion within the line is experienced away from the cued location. This effect is known as
illusory line motion. The explanation put forward by its discoverers is that the cue attracts
attention, resulting in speeding signal transmission in the visual system along a spatial
gradient extending from the cued location. This talk will present phenomenological
problems for the gradient model and overcome them by extending the gradient model
explanation to include the notion that the cue not only accelerates signal transmission, but
extends the duration of such transmission along the gradient.

title: What I Learned the Day-After the Infancy Conference
abstract:
In order to investigate interrelations of emotional adjustment, attachment security, and
behavior problems, data from 57 mothers of 2-year-old infants was examined. Cluster
analyses were conducted of maternal reports of emotional adjustment, parental stress, and
coping. A three cluster solution resulted in three groups: 22 mothers with positive
emotional adjustment, low parental stress, and high social support, 14 mothers with poor
emotional adjustment, high parental stress, and low social support, and 21 mothers with
high parental stress but good emotional adjustment. Attachment security was higher in
group 1 than 2, and externalizing behavior problems were higher in groups 2 and 3 than 1.
These data add to the literature suggesting maternal depression is related to infant
attachment security in a normal sample, and further suggest behavior problems are related
to attachment security. The data are the focus of a current paper being written.

title: Non-invasive Predictors of Laterality in Speech Production and Comprehension by Voyer, D.+, McGlone, J.*, and Savoie, J*.
abstract: The present study investigated the value of non-invasive measures of laterality in predicting speech lateralization as determined with the intracarotid amobarbital procedure (IAP). Seventy right- and non-right-handed pre-operative persons with epilepsy completed four baseline laterality tasks (i.e., dichotic listening with words, tachistoscopic letter naming, object naming and dot localimtion). Verbal expression and comprehension were tested after the left and right injection of sodium amobarbitol. A multiple regression analysis indicated that handedness and dichotic listening laterality were significant predictors of both expression and comprehension, whereas object naming laterality was an additional predictor of expression. Differences between handedness groups were also obtained.

authors_raw: Anne-Marie Brown-DeGagne, Jeannette McGlone, and Darcy Santor
title: More Somatic Than Cognitive-Affective Symptoms of Depression Among Individuals with Multiple Chemical Sensitivity
abstract: The Beck Depression Inventory (BDn was used to document severity of depressive symptomatology in 42 individuals with Multiple Chemical Sensitivity (MCS). The pwpose was to determine the extent to which somatic complaints contributed to the total BDI score. Analysis of cognitive-affective and somatic-performance complaints subscales indicated a significantly higher mean item score on somatic items relative to cognitive-affective items (t = 6.43, 11< .05). The total BDI score classified a greater percentage (43%) of the sample as moderately depressed than did the cognitive-affective subscale score (29%). An item analysis of the BDI revealed that, relative to a sample of depressed outpatients, individuals with MCS tended to endorse more somatic items. Findings suggested that: a. the BDI total score overestimated severity of depressive symptomatology in this sample, and/or b. individuals with MCS tended to express depressive symptomatology in terms of somatic complaints. Conclusions recommended using caution to assess psychiatric disturbance in individuals with MCS with measures that include items of somatic complaints.

title: An Examination of the Long-Lasting Effects of Oxazepam on Implicit and
abstract: In the present study the effect of oxazepam on implicit and explicit memory was tested at two time points. Twenty university students were tested at 170 minutes after drug administration (close to the peak plasma concentration of oxazepam) and twenty students were tested at 240 minutes after drug administration (post-peak). At each time point, the subjects were randomly assigned to the drug condition (30 mg of oxazepam) or the placebo condition. Consistent with previous findings, oxazepam impaired performance relative to placebo on both the explicit memory test (cued recall) and the implicit memory test (word stem completion task) at 170 minutes post drug. Likewise, oxazepam also impaired explicit and implicit memory performance at 240 minutes post-drug. Oxazepam-induced memory impairments did not change relative to placebo from 170 minutes to 240 minutes post drug administration. Clinical implications of these long-lasting memory impairments will be considered. Although drug group effects were evident on attentional measures, (e.g. forward vs. backwards digits on the WAIS-R) these did not appear to be responsible for the observed memory effects. title: Assessment of language comprehension with event-related potentials
abstract:
The objective of the proposed research involves the assessment of receptive language
comprehension with event-related potentials (ERPs). While there exists a panoply of
electrophysiological investigations oflanguage, recent studies (Byrne et al., 1995;
Connolly et al., 1995; Ingles et al., 1996) have adapted standardized neuropsychological
measures for computer presentation with ERPs. These investigations have demonstrated
that ERP components (e.g., N400 and P300) can be elicited bystandardized test stimuli and
thus utilized to evaluate language functions in the absence of a behavioral response.
Receptive language dysfunction is frequently noted in aphasic individuals suffering from a
stroke, but is not always readily assessable with traditional psychometric measures due to
limitations in motoric and communicative responses. Accordingly, the proposed
investigation will adapt three prominent neuropsychological tests, the Token Test (De Renzi
and Vignolo, 1962), sections of the Western Aphasia Battery (Kertesz, 1979), and a
section from the Psycholinguistic Assessment of Language Processing in Aphasia (Kay,
Lesser, and Coltheart, 1992). Performance on the computer tasks will be contrasted with
the respective standardized tests for validation. It is hypothesized that ERP components
will successfully differentiate behavioral responses for test items within an individual's
ability, but not beyond The results of this investigation, independent of the outcome, will
provide insight into the neurological basis of language and the utility of ERPs in
assessment.

authors_raw: Anna W. Lee & Richard E: Brown
title: Effect of Male Removal on Gestation Length and Time of Parturition in Female California Mice (Peromyscus californicus)
abstract: California mice are a monogamous species with biparental care (Gubemick & Alberts, 1987, J. Comp. Psychol., 101, 169-177). The presence of the male facilitates reproductive success by increasing pup survival and reducing the female's inter-birth-interval (Cantoni & Brown, Anim. Behav., in press). The present study investigated the effect of male removal on day 32 of gestation on the timing of parturition. When compared to control pairs, females which had their mates removed had a longer gestation length and gave birth later in the day. Possible nemoendocrine mechanisms underlying the effects of the male on the reproductive physiology of the female California mouse will be discussed.

title: Spatial localization in normal and amblyopic cats
abstract:
The main deficit in human amblyopia (blunt vision) concerns the coding of space and in
particular, the relative localization of image features. This behavioral research project will
study spatial localization tasks in normal and amblyopic cats using Gabor stimuli and a
jumping stand in order to ascertain if the animal performance is similar to human data
Two parameters will be evaluated: the size and the contrast of the stimuli. The ultimate
goal of the study is a better understanding of the neuronal mechansims potentially
responsible for the visual deficits described in human amblyopia

authors_raw: Sonia H.- Greaven, Darcy A. Santor, Richard Thompson and David C. Zuroff
title: Is Self-Handicapping in Young Adolescent Girls Related to Mothers' Parenting?
abstract: The use of self-handicapping strategies, which has been studied primarily in adults, may be related to the acceptance and reinforcement children experience while growing up. This study explored whether young adolescent girls' tendency to self-handicap was associated with their mothers' parenting characteristics. Mothers' ratings of care and stress were related to self-handicapping in their young adolescent daughters, as well as to their daughters' level of dysphoria. Regression analyses showed that higher maternal care was associated with lower levels of self-handicapping over and above the effect of their daughters' dysphoria, while mothers' stress from parent-child interactions was predictive of dysphoria in their daughters but not of self-handicapping.

title: An Evolutionary Approach to the Signaling and Detection of Altruism
abstract: In order for altruismbetweennon-kin to be evolutionarilystable cheatingmust be detectable. However,the divisionof labour in many human cooperativeventures may render overt cheatingundetectable.It is hypothesizedthat non-cheatingindividuals communicatetheir dispositionswith hard-to-fake,nonverbal signals. Previous experimentshave been consistentwith this hypothesis (Frank,Gilovich, & Regan, 1993; Palameta & Brown, 1996). Video-clipsof 4 altruists and 4 non-altruistswere matched for physical attractiveness,expressivenessand content. One-hundredand twenty university students made open-endedpersonalityassessmentsof the 8 targets. In addition, targets' facial micro-movementswere coded by two blind raters. Results will be discussedin light of current conceptualmodels explainingthe evolutionof altruismbetweennon-relatives.

authors_raw: D.P. Phillips, T.L. Taylor and S.E. Hall
title: Son of Gap Detection: In One Ear and Then the Other
abstract: Last year, in fit of unbridledenthusiasm,we presented two lines of evidence that suggested that all was not as it appearedto be in the world of gap detection. Recall that gap detection is an auditoryperceptionparadigmused to probe the temporalprocesses involved in speech recognition. It measuresthe shortest-durationsilent period that is detectablebetween two noises. Historically,researchershave kept those noise markers identical - which is not the case for the interestinggaps in speech. Last year, we showed that when the gap- detectionparadigmis modified to mimic the speech signal (e.g., by making the noise markers spectrallydissimilar,so that they activatedifferentperceptualchannels),two differentperceptualprocesseswere engaged. One of these processes seemed to explain why there is a categoricalboundaryin the perceptionof voice onset times, and why it has the value that it does (about 35 ms). This year, we report an experimentin which we performed a new interrogationof this perceptualtiming process.

authors_raw: John J. Christie Our data from spatialparadigmsthat typicallygenerateNegativePriming (Tipper,Brehaut
title: On the (ethereal, ephemeral) nature of neg3tive priming
abstract: and Driver, 1988)reveal that there may be importantartifactsin unbalancedexperimentsof this kind: The costs in the ignoredrepetitionconditionare not due to specialprocessingof the distractor. We subsequentlyconductedbalancedand imbalanceddesigns in the identity task while removingpotentialartifactsfrom localization. We generatedtypicalnegative priming effects with an imbalanceddesign; but these effects disappearedwhen subjectsno longer expected a target repetitionevent on the probe trial. Given that these basic paradigmsused to study negativepriming are broughtinto questionit is likely that all of the paradigmsthat are believedto produce negativepriming need to be re-examined. Additionally,the dramaticdifferencesin the results of the spatiallocation and identity paradigmswithout biases may provide a platformfor explainingdifferencesbetween these two paradigms pointed out by others (Fox, 1995). 6 1997 In-House Convention

title: Voices from within: Parasitic manipulation of host behaviour
abstract:
The parasitic wasp Cotesiacongregatainjects its eggs into the larvae of the tobacco
horn worm, Manducasexta. Homworms were found to feed normally during the growth
of the parasitic wasps. However, M. sexta stopped feeding several hours prior to the
emergence of the wasp larvae. Hosts were not debilitated by the parasites and survived
another 2 weeks without feeding after the wasps emerged. In the field, unparasitized M.
sexta ate C. congregatapupae (n=l0/10) suggesting that the suppression of host feeding
prevents predation of the parasitic wasps by the host. The decline in host feeding correlated
with a long-lasting increase in the concentration of octopamine in the host's hemolymph
(22.2 ± 2.1 pg/µL (n=7) to 143.7 ± 7.8 pg/µL hemolymph (n=16)). Injections of
octopamine depressed feeding in unparasitized M. sexta. The wasp larvae were not the
source of the host's increased octopamine. Feeding was also temporarily depressed in M.
sextaby injecting sephadex beads, bacteria (Serratiamarcescens)or lipopolysaccharides
(derived from S. marcescens).In all 3 cases, octopamine concentration in the hemolymph
increased after injection (18.7 ± 4.2 to 54.7 ± 7.8 pg/µL hemolymph for bacteria-injected,
n=lO)). Feeding resumed with the decline in octopamine levels. Sephadex beads, bacteria,
lipopolysaccharides and the emerging wasp larvae all activate the host's immune system.
The data are consistent with the hypothesis that part of the parasite's ability to suppress
host feeding involves permanently activating mechanisms that suppress feeding during an
immune challenge.
Tl 0. Perceptions of movement
