John Fentress
Animal behavior is something of a mobile projective test, no matter how objective we try
to be. What cues do we use, for example, to interpret the actions of other creatures? Part
of the answer depends upon our measurement systems: "What you measure is what you
get!" Of equal importance, measurements are inevitably incomplete and thereby can be
filled-in through often undefined expectations, biases, and the like. We have thus
launched a pilot project in which standardized behavior tapes and computer representations
of movement are presented to people under different settings. The results can be scary (as
in scare, not scar). It is clear that: a) cues can be used in an idosyncratic manner, b)
background events can change perceptions of behavior, and c) internal expectations of the
observer can dictate what is recorded, and how these recordings are interpreted. Certainly
the definition of objectivity must move beyond the position of shared bias. There may be a
rich field for cognitive psychologists as well as ethologists to explore here. Suggestions
are solicited.
1997In-HouseConvention 7
Tl 1. Behavioral sequences in canids: a new perspective on the plasticity of
action and movement patterns
Simon Gadbois
Important dimensions of behavioral patterns may have been overlooked in the past because
of (1) the species chosen, (2) the action patterns investigated and (3) the methods of
analysis selected. The issues of intentionality (control) and automaticity, plasticity and
stereotypy, stochasticity and derterminism are reviewed in the light of new observations on
caching sequences in canids. I will also discuss the pertinence of the concept of intensity
patterns in behavioral sequences, which are often ignored to the profit of seguential
patterns. An analogy with prosody in linguistics is suggested for the development of future
models of temporal organization of behavior.

authors_raw: Heather M. Schellinck and E. Barry Keveme
title: Major urinary proteins of the strange male house mouse (Mus musculus) confer the specificity required to induce pregnancy block
abstract: 8 1997In-House Convention

title: The Hebb-Williams Maze: Fifty years of research (1946-1996)
abstract:
The Hehir Williams (H-W) maze was designed as a test of animal intelligence and since
1946 has been used to study the learning abilities of a wide variety of species including
fish, raccoons and cows, as well as mice and rats. The H-W maze is an open field, with a
start box, a goal box and moveable barriers. Each animal runs through a battery of twelve
mazes of increasing difficulty, created with the movable barriers. As well as comparative
studies of learning using different species, the H-W maze has been used to assess the
effects of environmental enrichment or deprivation on learning and the impact of hormones,
X-irrad.iation, specific brain lesions, changes in diet, and a variety of drugs on learning.
Currently, the H-W maze is used to study genetic influences on learning and the effects of
prenatal manipulations of diet, stress, hormones and drugs on learning, as well as the
neural basis of learning. The present talk discusses the value of the H-W maze for
studying advanced cognitive tasks and its potential as a tool for measuring "intelligence" in
congenic strains of mice, transgenic mice and knockout mice.

authors_raw: Lianne Stanford, Jennifer Stapleton, Patti Dill, Alison Oarke, Harleen Randhawa, Deana Dixon, Asma Syed, Yasmin Mamoun & Richard Brown
title: Measuring learning in CDl mice in the Hebb-Williams Maze: Practical problems in the use of a complex maze test of intelligence
abstract: Although the Hehir Williams (H-W) maze was developed as "a method of evaluating animal intelligence", the procedures used for testing subjects in this maze have varied greatly. The most commonly used procedure is that developed by Rabinovitch and Rosvold (1951, Can. J. Psych., 5, 122-128). As we plan to use this maze to study learning in different mouse strains, it is important to develop a standardized procedure. Using data from pilot studies with CDl mice, this talk will focus on maze design, floor patterns, scoring procedures, motivation and all of the little things that can go wrong. We will also include some video footage of mice learning the H-W maze.

authors_raw: P.A. McMullen, A. Braithwaite, and J.M.McGlone
title: Separate Orthographic Input and Output Lexicons: Evidence from a Surface Dysgraphic
abstract: A surface dysgraphic was investigated to determine: 1). his writing deficit within a cognitive model and 2). support for a single or separate input and output orthographic lexicons. Regular words and nonwords were spelt perfectly, unlike irregularly spelt words (66% errors). All of these words were read perfectly. These results, along with normal cross-case transcription (e.g. CaSe- cAsE) supported intact orthographic input and output lexicons. Written rhyme fluency and deciding if words that sounded the same were spelt the same (e.g. tough-buff) was poor. Results were most consistent with a disconnection between phonological and orthographic output lexicons.

authors_raw: Myong G.Yoon and John F. Connolly
title: Influences of sentential contexts on event related brain potential responses to semantic anomaly and/or verb inflectional disagreement in compound sentences.
abstract: We studied the characteristic patterns of neural responses of the human brain to various combinations of semantic anomalies and/or verb inflectional disagreements during rapid reading of compound sentences by analyzing the event related brain potentials (ERP) recorded from normal adult subjects. [l] ERP of semantically anomalous verbs showed negative shifts peaking at about 400 msec (N400) with respect to the semantically appropriate verbs. The onset time of the N400 responses depended on the syntactic contexts of the verbs. [2] ERP of inflectionally incorrect verbs made positive shifts at about 600 msec (P600) with respect to control verbs. A repetition of the inflectional disagreement in the second clause diminished the P600 response. The result suggests that the repeated inflectional disagreement tends to be ignored by the neural mechanism which has responded to the previous inflectional disagreement as P600. [3] The predominant component of neural responses to semantically anomalous and inflectionally incorrect verbs was P600 rather than N400. Thus inflectional disagreement (rather than semantic anomaly) seems to be a predominant linguistic feature, detected by those neurons whose activities happened to be monitored by our recording methods. [4] Inter-sentential influence of the semantically anomalous transitive verb (with respect to its subject) upon its (acceptable) object; ERP to the object words of semantically anomalous verbs showed large negative shifts during a period between 300 and 750 msec. The magnitude and duration of the negative shift in the object word was far grater than that in the anomalous verb itself. The result reveals the prominent influence of the two diffemet sentential contexts on the same terminal words . [5] Inter-sentential influence of inflectional disagreement of semantically normal transitive verb upon its appropriate object in the second clause; ERP to the object words of the inflectionally incorrect normal verbs showed large negative shifts during a period beginning 300 msec. This large negative shift in the object words reflects the influence of the preceding inflectional relations between the subjects and the verbs in the compound sentences. Tl 7. Gender differences in anxiety sensitivity dimensions Stewart, S. H., Taylor, S., & Baker, J. M. The present study examined whether there are gender differences in the hierarchical factor structure of the Anxiety Sensitivity Index (ASI), and/or patterns of ASI factor scores. Principal components analyses on the ASI items of male (N =290) and female (N =528) students revealed nearly identical lower-order factor structures across genders (i.e., physical, psychological, and social concern factors). Nearly identical unidimensional higher-order solutions were also found across genders. Analyses on ASI lower-order factor scores showed that: females scored higher than males only on the physical concerns factor; females scored higher on the physical concerns factor than on the psychological and social concerns factors; and males scored higher on the psychological and social concerns factors than on the physical concerns factor. Females also scored higher than males on the higher-order ASI factor. The study provides support for the conceptual distinctions between the three lower-order ASI factors, as well as the first empirical demonstration of a hierarchical structure for the ASI. Men and women also appear to differ on AS dimensions in ways consistent with sex role socialization practices. 10 1997 In-House Convention Tl 8. Memory bias for food information in restrained eaters Anne Israeli and Sherry Stewart Research on cognitive patterns in individuals high in dietary restraint indicate an attention bias toward food related information (see Francis, Stewart, and Hounsell, in press). The cwrent study was conducted to see if restrained eaters show a memory bias for food words in comparison to animal words (control list) in relation to individuals low in restraint Thiny females (Mean age= 21.50, SD= 2.97) encoded a list of words (15 food, 15 animal) presented in an incidental learning task. Participants were then asked to freely recall as many words as they could remember and fill out questionnaires including the Restraint Scale (Herman & Polivy, 1980). Participants were classified as high in restraint (n=16) and low in restraint (n=14) on the basis of the scale. Preliminary analyses indicate that individuals high in restraint remember more food than animal words and individuals low in restraint do not demonstrate such a bias. The results support (Bemis-Vitousek & Hollon's (1990) theory that restrained eaters have highly organized cognitive structures (schema) which permit then to avoid dangers (food) in their environment

authors_raw: Michael MacGregor, Karina Davidson and Stephanie Gustys
title: A translation of borderline and histronic personality disorders
abstract:
This paper addresses the translation of Borderline and Histrionic personality disorders into
the language of the Five-Factor model of personality. Based on theoretical expectations,
we investigated the empirical relationship between the Five-Factors of personality and these
two personality disorders in a non-clinical sample. As expected, the personality disorders
were characterized by high levels of Neuroticism. For Borderline personality disorder, our
expectations regarding Agreeableness were confirmed, however, those for Extraversion
were not As well, additional relationships were found between both Openness and
Conscientiousness and Borderline personality disorder. For Histrionic personality
disorder, our expectations for Extraversion were confirmed, however, those for Openness
were not Additionally, both Agreeableness and Conscientiousness demonstrated and
unexpected relationship with Histrionic personality disorder. While some of the expected
relationships between the Five-Factors and Borderline and Histrionic personality were
confirmed. others were not It appears that theoretical or clinical expectations are not
necessarily confirmed by empirical findings in a non-clinical sample. Results are discussed
in terms of the utility of the Five-Factor model in describing personality disorders.

title: Becoming conscious of 'consciousness': Notes for a history of an important psychological concept
abstract: During the 17th century the term of 'consciousness' suddenly appeared as a psychological concept. Since then this concept has become increasingly important both in the foundations and later developments of 'mental philosophy' and scientific psychology. Although during the heyday of behaviourism it diminished in importance, interest in consciousness has been revived again toward the end of this century. In the present talk I will look at the origins of the use of this term, its ancient sources in Greek and Latin, its major 'discovery' in Descartes, and its later development in the works of Cudworth, Locke, Leibniz, Hume, and others in the late 17th and early 18th century.

authors_raw: Raymond M. Klein & Jeff P. Hamm
title: Attention (to the moving line): "Where you lead, I will follow".
abstract:
When a line is presented in the vicinity of a recent luminance change (peripheral cue) it is
perceived to be drawn over time away from its "cued" end even though the line is actually
presented all at once. This study was designed to determine if attention, exogenously
attracted to the cue, would come under the exogenous control of this illusory motion and
follow the drawn line from its cued end to its terminus. Each trial began with the display of
4 small squares at the corners of an imaginary square centered about fixation. After 750 ms
one of the 4 squares brightened briefly (50ms) in the cued conditions. Simultaneous with
the offset of the cue a horizontal line was presented joining the upper or lower boxes.
After 100 ms one of the 4 squares was transformed into a horizontal or vertical rectangle
for 150 ms. The subject's task was to make a horizontal/ vertical decision as rapidly as
possible. The different events (cues, lines and targets) were combined factorially so that
neither cues nor lines were informative about target location. Responses to targets at an
uncued location that was at the end of an illusorilly drawn line were as fast as to targets in
the neutral and "valid" conditions, and much faster than the remaining invalid conditions,
strongly suggesting that attention follows the motion in the "shooting line" illusion.

authors_raw: Sol Simpson, Thomas Trappenberg, & Raymond Klein
title: Integration of Environmental and Intentional Information within the Saccadic Programming System: A Computational Perspective.
abstract: Saccadic reaction time (SRT) is influenced by both the observer's environment (exogenous factors) and intentions (endogenous factors). To investigate these two SRT contributions, the time interval between the removal of a fixation point and the presentation of a peripheral target (the Gap Paradigm) as well as the probability of a target appearing at a given location were varied. It was hypothesized that if endogenous information affects SRT within the same functional layer of the saccadic programming system that is responsible for exogenous control (the superior colliculus), the effect of target location information would be significantly larger in conditions where the fixation stimulus was removed prior to target presentation than in conditions where the fixation stimulus remained present following target onset. This interaction was obtained with saccadic but not manual RTs, suggesting that the lateral inhibition of fixation cells in the rostral pole (RP) of the superior colliculus (SC) onto the saccade related cells of the SC may memate the effects of both exogenous and endogenous inputs on SRT. This presentation will attempt to explain this interaction using a computational perspective within which a neural field model of saccadic programming is constrained by recent neurophysiological data 12 1997 In-House Convention

title: Word learning in the context of referential and salience cues
abstract: Previous research has shown that word learningcan occur in the context of joint attention, however it remains unclear whether such learningoccurs because the child understandsthe referentialintent of the speakeror because the speaker'sreferentialbehavior tends to make the intendedobject more salient In this work, both referentialand saliencecues were independentlymanipulatedin a study of novel word learning. Eighteen-and 24-month-old children were presented with a novel word in the context of two toys, which could be activatedby remote conttoL In the match condition,the toy which was the target of the adult's referentialbehaviorwas activatedwhile the other was not In the mismatch condition, the toy which was the target of the referentialbehavior was not activatedwhile the other was. Two control conditionshad either both toys simultaneouslyactivatedor neither toy activatedwhile the adult made referenceto one. Comprehensionand productionposttests assessed the extent of acquisitionof the novel word. The results showed that at 24 months children tended significantlyto pick out the target toy in response to the novel word under all conditionsand that there was no significantdifferenceacross conditions. There was also no difference across conditionsin children's ability to produce the novel word appropriately. At 18 months, however, there was a differencein performanceacross conditions. In the mismatchcondition, the children were more likely to pick out the activatedtoy in response to the novel word, while in the other three conditions,they were more likely to pick out the target toy. These results imply that word learningin joint attentionsituationsmay initiallyoccur becauseof the salienceof the target object but soon does occur becausethe child understandsthe referentialintent of the speaker.

title: What does that Robot want from me now? An investigation of young children's abilities to repair contingent queries.
abstract: The ability of 2-year-oldchildrento respond differentiallyto specificand neutral contingent queries was examinedwith a toy robot as the communicativepartner. 22 children (mean age =2:9 years) were asked contingentqueries immediatelyfollowing 10 of their utterances in a free play session. 5 specific queries (ie."That's a what?") and 5 neutral queries (ie. ''What?") were asked to each subject Children'sreplies were coded to determine the type of response strategy used: repetition;specification; or reformulation.Results revealed that children were significantlymore likely to specifypart of their originalutterancefollowinga specificquery, whereasfollowinga neutral query they were significantlymore likely to repeat their entire utterance. No differenceswere observed in the number of reformulations made. Results will be discussed in terms of the young child's developing theory of mind. Additionalresearch being designed as a follow up to these results will also be discussed.

title: Inhibition as the Primary Deficit in ADHD
abstract:
Researchers are continually trying to isolate a core deficit in children with attention deficit
hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Recently, Barkley (1997) proposed that behavioral
inhibition was the primary deficit, and that it set the stage for the observed deficits in
executive function and response execution in children with ADHD. Pure behavioral
measures of inhibition, however, may be confounded by socialization, and offer little in the
way of an explanation of the mechanism(s) underlying the behavioral disturbances.
Cognitive or attentional inhibition may be a more reliable and valid construct for study.
Deficits in this area correspond with evidence suggesting that ADHD may result, in part,
from frontal lobe dysfunction. Research on cognitive inhibition in children with ADHD
will be reviewed, and further studies will be proposed that could validate and enhance
Barkley's model.

title: Teaching Children How to Locate Information: Strategy Instruction for Students in the Elementary School Grades
abstract: The present study examined the effects of instructing elementary school students how to most efficiently locate information in a children's encyclopedia. The instructional protocol emphasized the importance of using the index, reading the text carefully and self- monitoring. 155 students from grades 3, 4, and 5 were randomly assigned to one of three groups: Strategy for Locating Information (SLI), Strategy for Locating Information without Monitoring (SLI-M), or Non-Instructed Control (CON). Although students in both the SLI and SLI-M groups found more correct answers than those in the CON group, the monitoring component was important in improving search efficiency (as measured by number of correct answers found per minute). Students who received instruction were more likely to use the Index than other students, and they also searched fewer total pages than the CON group.

title: The National Conference on Psychology as a Science
abstract:
From 8-11 May, 1997, CPA and BBCS have invited 45 Canadian psychologists to a
conference to discuss the future directions in MRC, NSERC and SSHRC funded research.
The goal of the National Conference is to "develop a vision of research and training in
psychology that is in tune with the increased realization that many of our Society's most
pressing needs and exciting opportunities will benefit directly from accurate and
sophisticated knowledge of human thought, emotion and behavioural processes". "We
must identify research priorities that will allow psychology to make a meaningful
contribution to the national research agenda, as described in a recent report on Science and
Technology entitled 'Healthy, wealthy and wise: A framework for an integrated federal
science and technology strategy'." In brief, the question that we have been posed is:
"Whither psychology in the 21st century?".

authors_raw: Tara L. Fidler and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Does manipulation of thirst change ethanol consumption by rats anticipating footshock?
abstract: Rats were given the opportunity to drink ethanol, sucrose or water in a distinctive environment prior to shock sessions in a third environment. Since thirsty rats will learn to consume otherwise aversive fluids when there is no other fluid available we wanted to show that our rats were motivated to consume ethanol for reasons in addition to thirst Therefore on some days the rats had an opportunity to consume water in their home cages prior to being placed in the drinking environment Preliminary results appear to show that water consumption in the drinking environment is more affected by this manipulation than is consumption of ethanol or sucrose. However, fear in the shock box (as measured by bolus production) appears to be differentially affected in the ethanol and sucrose groups.

title: Pitfalls in odour and taste preference conditioning
abstract: The standard procedure used to condition flavour preferences involves a within subject differential conditioning paradigm in which the cue flavours and the appropriate reinforcers are presented simultaneously. In the ensuing test phase, animals typically show a robust preference for the CS+ which does not readily extinguish. The present research, which investigated the ability of a taste to potentiate the formation of a conditioned odour preference has necessitated several modifications to this standard procedure. These included the use of a reverse order conditioning procedure adapted from Boakes & Lubart instead of a simultaneous procedure, between subjects rather than within subject comparisons, and animals which received restricted rather than ad lib water access. Each of these procedural modifications will be assessed in terms of its influence on our failure to obtain robust odour and taste preferences.

title: Anatomical and Neurochemical Characterization of the Development of the Embryonic Brain of Drosophila melanogaster
abstract: Current and proposed research consists of three inter-related projects. Current work involves the production of a detailed, three-dimensional anatomical map of the developing embryonic brain and ventral nerve cord (vnc) of Drosophila. This map will be a detailed template onto which information about cell lineage, gene expression, neurotransmitter expression, and biochemical activity can be mapped. The second phase focusses on localization of serotonergic expression in the embryonic brain and vnc. The expression of nitric oxide (NO) during embryonic neural development will also be characterized. NO is a putative neurotransmitter which has been implicated in neuronal development, death, and plasticity.

authors_raw: Marleen de Groot and Ben Rusak
title: A Little Light Learning?
abstract:
The circadian system responds directly to biologically relevant environmental stimuli such
as the light-dark cycle. A recent study (Stewart & Amir, 1996) showed that an external
stimulus that does not induce phase shifts or expression of the immediate-early gene
product c-fos in the SCN of rats can be made to do so after being paired repeatedly with a
phase-shifting light pulse in a Pavlovian conditioning paradigm. Using appropriate
conditioning and control procedures, we are attempting to assess whether this phenomenon
is reliable and occurs in both rats and hamsters. Animals were housed in isolation boxes
containing an activity wheel, a light source and a fan. Wheel-running activity was
monitored throughout the study, and was used as the behavioural measure of circadian
phase. The animals were entrained to a standard light dark cycle, and conditioning took
place during the dark phase of the cycle. The unconditioned stimulus (US) used was a
fifteen minute light pulse, and a twenty minute air disturbance served as the conditioned
stimulus (CS). Results of our initial studies will be described.

authors_raw: Carol Thompson, Henny Westra, Sherry Stewart, and Alan MacDonald
title: Relations between Benzodiazepine Use, Agoraphobic Avoidance, and Autobiographical Memory in Clinical Anxiety
abstract: The purpose of this study was to investigate overgeneral reporting of past personal events in clinically anxious patients, as a function of benzodiazepine (BZ) use and avoidance of fearful situations. Using the Autobiographical Memory Test, BZ-using anxiety patients, nonmedicated anxiety patients, and controls were asked to recall specific personal memories in response to cue words which varied in emotional valence. Results showed that, among BZ-using patients, more overgeneral memories were recalled in response to negative cues as cumulative BZ dose increased. A positive relationship between overgeneral recall and a tendency to avoid anxiety-arousing situations (as measured by the Mobility Inventory) was also found in the clinically anxious groups but not in the control group. These results suggest that, among clinically anxious patients, overgeneral reporting of past personal events is related to BZ dose and to a tendency to avoid anxiety-arousing situations. Clinical implications of these findings will be discussed. 16 1997 In-House Convention

title: Are you feeling childish about your present concerns? Some reasons why.
abstract:
A narrative research methodology developed by H.J.M. Hermans (1992) is being used to
investigate relationships between peoples' earliest memories and their present concerns
which have been postulated by Adlerian and Cognitive-Perceptual (CP) theories. Adler
viewed earliest memories as relatively constant reflections of one's lifestyle, while CP
theory predicts shifts in earliest memories with changing present concerns (Bruhn, 1992).
Opposing predictions based on these two hypotheses were tested in an experiment
involving forty-five students who participated in two sessions separated by a month. In
each session they provided narrative descriptions of six earliest memories and six present
concerns as well as self-rated affective profiles for each of these life experiences.
Comparisons of life experiences and affect profiles over time will address the issue of
permanance among earliest memories, where Adler's theory would predict one's very
earliest memory to be the same across two sessions and relate more strongly to present
concerns than would other memories, and CP theory holds that all early memories are
subject to change along with changing present concerns.

title: Footprints on the Internet: What do they tell us?
abstract:
The Psychology Department home page officially went on the World-Wide Web on
November 29th, 1996. The site lives on a server provided by the University Computing
and Information Services, and a log is kept of all visits to the pages in the site. Every time a
page is visited, information is logged about the visitor. In this talk, we'll have a look at
what these "footprints" in the log tells us about who has visited the site, where they come
from, and what gets looked at Secret features of the Psychology site will be revealed. The
feedback features of the site will get special attention.

title: Surveys and Tests on the Web: A Tutorial
abstract:
As many members of the department are aware, I have created a tool that allows non-
programmers to implement surveys and tests electronically on the world-wide web. The
tool is freely available to members of the department, and I would like to see it used to
benefit researchers at Dalhousie, regardless of whether they use it to collect data from the
web populous or internally from subject pool participants. I will demonstrate the creation of
a web survey from start to finish, and suggest possible uses within the department

title: Predicting Children's Pain
abstract:
This srudy examined the pain experienced by 112 children aged 4 to 6 receiving routine
DPTP immunization. Pain during the immunization was measured using the Child Facial
Coding System (Chambers, et al., 1996). The Child Facial Coding System is an
observational scale used to record the intensity of 13 facial actions believed to be indicative
of pain in children. Ratings of one second intervals were made from videotape over a 30
second period which included: 10 seconds prior to injection, 10 seconds during injection,
and 10 seconds after injection. After immunization, each child, their parent, and an
observer rated the child's pain on a Faces Pain Scale. The adults also rated the child's pain
on a Visual Analogue Scale. The results indicate that post- immunization pain ratings were
related to: the informant, the sex of the parent, the age of the child, whether the child was
on medication during the previous month, whether they were currently on medication, and
whether they had previous experience with needles. Principal components analysis of the
13 facial actions resulted in four factors common to all three periods: horizontal mouth
movements, open mouth, blinking, and eye squeezing. The relative importance of these
factors, however, varied by time period. Finally, different combinations of facial actions
and medical history were found to predict post-immunization pain ratings, depending on
theinformantandthemeasureused.

authors_raw: Dean Tripp, Michael Sullivan and Nancy Neish
title: What Hurts and What Helps During Dental Hygiene Treatment?
abstract: Given the lack of research quantifying pain during various dental hygiene procedures, pain and pain coping attempts were explored in 48 patients (27 male, 21 female) undergoing routine dental hygiene treatment Dental anxiety and self-efficacy of pain coping attempts were also explored in relation to pain. The majority of patients (79%) report none or very little pain during clinical procedures. In direct contrast, 21 % of sample reported pain scores that were extreme in comparison to the majority. Empathic discussions with the hygienist, self-distraction from the pain, and information seeking from the hygienist, were the most often employed pain coping strategies. In dividing the sample by pain reports (i.e., low and high pain), no group differences in regards to dental anxiety, age, education, brushing habits, or the degree of tre~tment difficulty were found. However, the low pain group flossed more (t=3.01, l2.,.<.005)and had greater levels of self-efficacy for pain coping attempts (t=2.28, l2.,.<.027)than did the high pain group. There were no differences in how many pain coping strategies were reported between low and high pain groups as well as low and high self-efficacy for pain coping. Results suggest that feelings of personal control during dental hygiene treatment may be a factor in successful pain management. Future research is discussed in terms of other trait-like cognitive factors such as pain catastrophizing. 18 1997 In-House Convention Behaviors caregivers use to determine pain in non-verbal, cognitively impaired children Patrick J. McGrath, Christina Romos, Carol Camfield, Mary Ann Campbell, Allan Hennigar Primary caregivers of 20 children (age=14.5 years and language level=lO months) were recruited and interviewed to recall 2 instances of short sharp pain and 2 of longer-lasting pain and describe their child's behaviour. Transcribed interviews were reviewed and sets of non-overlapping items were developed. Thirty one behaviours were extracted from the interviews. The specific behaviors were often different from one child to another but the classes of behaviors (Vocal, Eating/Sleeping, Socia1/Personality, Facial Expression, Activity, Body & Limbs and Physiological) were common. Relibility of using the checklist on interviews was good (kappa=.77). The checklist has excellent content validity and will be useful for caregivers of cognitively-impaired, non-communicating children to report on pain behaviors.

title: Transposition of an Intermediate Numerosity Discrimination
abstract:
Pigeons were trained to respond differentially to an array with a relative numerosity
(proportion of red to blue elements) intermediate to that of two other arrays in a successive
conditional discrimination, and were then transferred to different sets of numerosities. The
mechanism of transfer appeared to be absolute, rather than relational. The procedure was
then changed to a simultaneous forced choice of the intermediate numerosity among three
arrays. Performance within sessions suggested the operation of a relational process. The
apparent discrepancy between the two results, and the relative influence of numerosity and
entropy, will be discussed.

authors_raw: Margo C. Watt, Sherry H. Stewart, and Brian J. Cox
title: Learning Experiences in the Development of Anxiety Sensitivity
abstract:
The present study invo~ved a conceptual replication of Anlce Ehlers' (1993) work on panic
and childhood learning experiences, but also extended her work by investigating the
relationship between these learning experiences and the development of anxiety sensitivity
(AS). Five hundred and fifty-one students participated in a retrospective assessment of their
childhood and adolescent learning experiences with respect to somatic symptoms. Panic-
reporters in the study revealed a pattern of learning history results similar to Ehlers',
however, a different pattern emerged in the learning histories of highly anxiety sensitive
individuals (HAS). Unlike panickers, and contrary to predictions, the learning experiences
of HAS individuals were found not to be specific to anxiety symptoms but involving
parental reinforcement and modeling of sick-role behaviour related to somatic symptoms in
general. HAS subjects reported both more anxiety and cold symptoms prior to age 18 than
individuals with lower levels of AS. In addition, both cold and anxiety symptoms elicited
special attention and/or instructions for HAS individuals to take special care of themselves.
These findings are contrasted with the results for panickers in the sample who reported
learning experiences (modeling and parental reinforcement) specific to anxiety-related
symptoms. Purported links between AS, panic, and hypochondriasis are discussed.

authors_raw: Alan MacDonald, Michelle Skinner, Sherry Stewart, Brent Conrad
title: A Dose-Response Study of the Effects of Alcohol on the Response to Hyperventilation in High and Low Anxiety Sensitive Participants
abstract: Previous research suggests that high levels of anxiety sensitivity (AS; fear of anxiety symptoms) may constitute a risk factor for alcohol abuse. The present study evaluated the hypothesis that high AS levels may heighten risk for alcohol abuse by increasing a person's sensitivity to the stress response dampening (SRD) effects of alcohol, which could negatively reinforce drinking in this population. Additionally, the study investigated whether relatively high doses of alcohol are needed in order to see significant SRD effects among high AS individuals and thus explain their more frequent drinking to the point of legal intoxication. Seventy-seven student participants (39 high AS; 38 low AS) were randomly assigned to one of three beverage conditions: placebo, low dose alcohol, and high dose alcohol. Following beverage consumption and absorption, participants underwent a three minute voluntary hyperventilation challenge to induce bodily arousal symptoms. High AS placebo participants reported greater affective and cognitive reactivity in response to the challenge than low AS placebo participants, indicating increased fear and catastrophising during hyperventilation among sober high AS individuals. Both the high and low doses of alcohol eliminated the differences in responding between AS groups by normalizing the affective and cognitive reactivity of the high AS students to the challenge. However, only high AS subjects administered the high alcohol dose (blood alcohol concentration (BAC) =0.08) showed reduced cognitive and affective reactivity to the hyperventilation challenge when compared to high AS subjects administered placebo. Finally, all subjects (high and low AS alike) in both alcohol conditions showed reduced somatic reactivity to the challenge compared to subjects in the placebo condition. Clinical and theoretical implications of the findings are discussed.

authors_raw: X.J. Sun and I.A. Meinenzhagen. Combined with powetful digital imaging techniques, confocal laser scanning microscopy
title: Looking through the tly's brain - application of confocal laser scanning microscopy to the study of Drosophila development.
abstract: (CLSM) provides unprecedented views of biological specimens. We present here some recent CLSM data to study the development of the brain in the fruitfly Drosophila melanogaster. Using antibodies against the synaptic vesicle protein synaptotagmin, and various other markers, synaptic regions and specific cell types in Drosophila's brain are readily revealed in three dimensions throughout development. This technique reveals new images of, and insights to, the functional architecture and make-up of the brain in this genetically manipulable animal. The use of this technique in genetic screens will be shown by CLSM analyses of structural brain mutants. 20 1997 In-House Convention

title: Electrical- and dye coupling between presynaptic photoreceptor axons in fly visual system
abstract: Photoreceptors from different facets of the fly's compound eye converge in stereotyped groups of 6, the cylindrical synaptic modules ("cartridges") that make up the first synaptic zone behind the eye (lamina). There they make about 1200 histaminergic synapses to postsynaptic neurons, in big flies. In addition, electronmicroscopy shows that each pair of terminals in the cartridge is interconnected presynaptically by about 30 small maculate gap junctions each, raising the expectation that the 6 contributing photoreceptor axons to a cartridge should be strongly electrically coupled (Shaw & Stowe, 1982). We will briefly review older evidence (Shaw 1984, van Hateren 1986) that the terminals are indeed electrically coupled, when probed optically to stimulate cells from the six different facets of the 6-way convergence. The results allow the degree of coupling to be estimated, confirming that it is indeed strong. When neurons have been found to be electrically coupled with gap junctions in other neural systems, injections with small fluorescent tracers have often showed them to be "dye- coupled" as well. Lucifer Yellow (MW 457) is small enough to pass through the central connexon channel of some gap junctions. Unfortunately, many tests so far (Hardie, Shaw) have completely failed to detect dye-coupling between photoreceptor terminals. This has now been repeated with the slightly smaller, more recently introduced tracer, biocytin (MW 372). After many test injections, biocytin has been found to spread to either one or two other terminals in the same cartridge group, always on a next-neighbour basis. This finally confirms the other evidence, and suggests that coupling strength is greatest between next- neighbour cartridge terminals, as expected.

authors_raw: M. Shimohigashi I and I.A. Meinertzhagen 1Current Address:
title: The gene shaking-B (Passover) regulates the number of gap junctions in the visual system of Drosophila: the jump between behaviour and molecules
abstract: Gap junctions are the structural contacts that mediate electrical coupling between neurons in nervous systems. In vertebrates, gap junctions form from connexin proteins, but in invertebrates the corresponding proteins remain elusive. Recently, however, the shaking-B gene in Drosophila is suggested to encode a connexin-like protein, and mutants of shaking- B, including Passover, a null mutant with a defective escape jump, exhibit coupling defects. An antibody raised against the shak-B peptide expresses in the the lamina, where terminals of the eye's photoreceptors are electrically coupled by gap junctions. Compared with the wild type, there are significantly fewer (ca 20%) gap junctions in the shak-B mutant, but only in the upper levels of the lamina. At deeper levels, the numbers are smaller in both mutant and wild type, and both the same. Thus, the lamina's population of gap junctions is partitioned into at least two strata, and the numbers in the mutant imply the existence of at least two genetic factors, possibly proteins, encoding the number of gap junctions. ./\:9~ Neural Statistics, Human Failing, and the Backward Propagation of Error '1'45:- D.A. Santor This brief talk introduces the basics of neural networks and machine learning and illustrates how for some (if not many tasks) we're better off not relying on human judgement.

title: Recurrent network dynamics
abstract:
The brain does not process information in a strictly linear fashion. It has rather a large
amount of recurrent pathways which we believe are the root of its rich dynamical behavior.
In this talk we will discuss some properties of very simple recurrent abstract neural
networks. We will demonstrate their behavior with some simulations.

title: The evolution of the blood-brain barrier in the insect eye: is there a barrier in Archaeognatha?
abstract: In vertebrates, the blood-brain barrier (BBB) is located in the brain capillary walls and stabilizes the brain's micro-environment, protecting it from potentially harmful incursions of blood chemicals, etc. The evolution of the barrier mechanism is not easy to study by comparative methods, since all known vertebrates have it. The other larger group of terrestrial colonizers, winged insects (Pterygota), also have a BBB with the same role at the surface of the CNS, and which extends out into the eye. In trying to trace its evolution, we have looked to see if the eye barrier is present in the more primitive wingless insects (Apterygota), from which pterygotes originally evolved back in the Paleozoic era. We collected Petrobius maritimus (jumping bristletails) of the most primitive extant apterygote family Machilidae (Archaeognatha) on the shore near Parrsboro. Compared to other apterygotes, they have very well developed compound eyes. A colony of about 40 has been kept successfully for about 6 months in the lab. Using microelectrodes to pressure-inject small fluorescent tracers into the haemolymph (blood) of the head. we observed that these dyes lit up the eyes within a few minutes of injection. Specimens fixed and sectioned showed that dye is indeed present in the eye, concentrating particularly in the outermost optical layer, the cornea, which could only have reached by diffusing up from the base of the eye. We are currently setting up to check this, using electron-microscope tracers. Next, bugs were injected on a tilt stand (goniometer) mounted directly on the microscope, allowing fluorescence emission from part of the eye surface to be measured optically in real time with a sensitive detector. The measured time profiles confirm that dye uptake is extremely rapid (tl/2 = 0.4 - 1.4 min), although it then disappears again from the eye. Finally, the path-length for diffusion into the eye has been measured and the expected diffusion profile modelled, from a solution to the diffusion equation for the case with no barrier. This predicts similar values of 11/2 (0.8 - 1.1 min) without further assumptions, confirming that the eye space must be completely open to the circulation. The situation in flies (advanced pterygotes) is quite different, with very slow penetration and loss of dyes. The key reason for the lack of a barrier in machilids may be the primitive stage of evolution of the tracheal system in the group, which needs upgrading before barriers cutting off the retina become feasible. 22 1997 In-House Convention

title: Digitize that Image
abstract: This talk will introducethe process of digitizingimages and the subsequenttechniquesthat can be used to analyze or process a digitizedimage. I will give a brief review of some of the techniques hat have been successfullyused in recent years, with some insights on what a digitized image is and some hints on getting the best image possible. I will then summarizethe resources that are availableon campus and show how you can also use digital photographywithout owning specializedequipment

title: The central nervous system of the ascidian Ciona intestinalis
abstract: Ascidians, also called tunicates, are a group of marine creatures that exhibit several of the defining chordate features of the vertebratebrain. They are thought to lie closest to the presumed ancestral group of all chordates,includingvertebrates, from the body plan of their tiny tadpole larva, only several millimetersin length. Despite its size, the larva has a perfectly diminutive chordate central nervous system (CNS) with only several hundred cells in total. This makes the ascidianlarva an attractiveanimal in which to perform detailed studies of a primitive and relatively simple CNS of the vertebratetype. 3-D computerreconstructionswere made of all the CNS cells of individual larvae from the Southern Hemisphere, from serial sections. The cells within the CNS were classified by their nuclei and the appearanceof their cytoplasm,as one of twelve possible types. Cell counts were compared with those from a previous study on larvae collected from the Northern Hemisphere. Several novel conclusionson larval morphology were drawn from this serial-sectionstudy and the resulting reconstructions. These data indicate a remarkable similarityin larval organizationbetween individualsof this species.

authors_raw: Boyer Winters and Richard E. Brown
title: Continuous delayed match/non-match-to-sample: An odor-guided test of working memory in the rat
abstract: Animal models of working memory have traditionally used a delayed matching-to-sample problem with visual or spatial stimuli. We have developed a more ethologically valid procedure to assess working memory in the rat using olfactory stimuli. 1bis task utilizes a go-left/go-right procedure and thus eliminates both the ambiguity of null responses inherent in the go/no go olfactory learning paradigm and the problem of mediating behaviours which plague other putative tests of working memory such as the delayed matching-to-position task (Stanhope et al., 1995, Psychopharmacology, 122, 268-280). Five rats were trained on a continuous delayed match/non match-to-sample (CDM/NMS) task in an operant olfactometer with four different delays (3, 30, 60, and 120 sec) between the sample and choice odors. The percent correct in this task decreased with increasing delay, indicating a working memory function and suggesting that this procedure is a good model for testing drugs which influence short-term memory. When a stable baseline level of performance was reached, the effect of the central muscarinic antagonist scopolamine hydrobromide (HBr) was assessed. Many experiments suggest that scopolamine has a detrimental effect on learning and memory processes (Smith,1988, Brain Research Reviews, 13, 103-118). However, other studies suggest that the effect of scopolamine may be due to something other than memory impairment (Herremans et al., 1996, Brain Research, 711, 102-111). We injected rats i.p. 30 minutes before testing on different days with one of five treatments: saline, 0.3 mg/kg scopolamine methylbromide (a peripherally acting muscarinic antagonist), 0.03 mg/kg scopolamine HBr, 0.1 mg/kg scopolamine HBr, and 0.3 mg/kg scopolamine HBr. There was a significant decrease in performance with increasing delays and a significant decrease in percent correct as the dose of scopolamine HBr was increased. There was no interaction between drug and delay. The general decrease in percent correct was interpreted as indicating an effect of scopolamine on a non-mnemonic process which influences performance in the CDM/NMS olfactory learning task. It is suggested that scopolamine induces a failure to attend to relevant information. 24 1997 In-House Convention

authors_raw: M.C. Dorris, T. Taylor, D.P. Munoz, R. Klein
title: Saccadic Reaction Times are Influenced Similarly by Previous Saccadic Metrics and Exogenous Cueing in Monkey
abstract: Human subjects are slower to initiate saccadesto targetspresented at previously exogenously-cuedlocations (inhibitionof return- IOR). We extended this same finding to rhesus monkeys using an IOR paradigm. Then using IOR as a tool we studied the link between the mechanismsunderlyingsaccade generationand exogenousattentionalshifts. We repeated the same IOR experimentbut insteadof the first peripheralstimulusbeing an exogenouscue, it was the target for a saccade. The monkey was required to fixated a centrally presentedfixation point which steppedout to a peripherallocation,back to center, and back to a peripherallocation.The locationof the two peripheraltargets were randomly varied so as to range from coincidentto highly disparate. Instead of increased SRTs when the two peripheral stimuluiwere coincident,as was observed for the exogenously-cued IOR paradigm, SRTs were increasedwhen the two peripheral stimuli were of equal amplitudebut oppositedirectionswith respect to the fixation point. Therefore,when two successive saccadeshad the same metrics (e.g., from a 10 leftward target to center, and center to a 10 rightward target: two successive 10 rightward saccades)SRTs were increased. These findings are interpretedin terms of shared processingof oculomotor programmingand visuospatialattentionon the same neural structure. To provide a direct confirmationthis interpretationactivityof single neuronsin the superiorcolliculusof this monkey were recorded, and we hope to have some of these data analyzed in time for presentationof this poster.

title: The Effects of Nicotine on Spatial and Non-spatial Expectancies in a Covert Orienting Task (or "Reduce Costs by Smoking")
abstract: Observersrespond faster to targetspresented at previouslycued locations. Alzheimer's patients and monkeys with lesions of the basal forebraincholinergic system show an increasein this cuing effect We hypothesizedthat becausenicotine is a cholinergicagonist it might have the oppositeeffect (reducingthe cuing effect). 3 dose levels were achieved by testing casual smokersat each of three delays after smokingone cigarette. Peripheral flashes drew attentionto the locationwhere the size of a peripheralobject was most likely to be changed, and participantsperformed a speededdiscrimination (expansion/contraction).The performancedecrement(cost) obtainedwhen the target appearedat an uncued locationwas smallestin sessionsrun immediatelyafter smoking (when nicotine levels would have been highest),suggestingthat nicotinemay increase the ease with which attentioncan be disengagedfrom a cued location.Our findingsconverge with other research supportinga role for the basal forebraincholinergicsystem in visual orienting. 1997 ln-House Convention 25

title: Auditory and Visual Control of Fixation
abstract:
In the rostral pole of the superior colliculus (CS) are fixation cells that inhibit the remainder
of the intermediate SC when a visual stimulus is being fixated. This inhibition is removed
when the visual fixation stimulus is extinguished coincident with or prior to the onset of a
peripheral target. resulting in facilitated saccadic reaction times (RTs). Because the SC also
receives auditory input. the purpose of the present study was to determine whether this gap
effect could also be obtained for saccade targets that follow the offset of a fixed auditory
stimulus. Experiments 1 and 2 presented undergraduate subjects with three light emitting
diodes (LEDs) mounted in front of speakers and spaced 10 degrees apart along the
horizontal meridian. At the centre location, subjects were required to fixate an illuminated
LED or else an auditiory stimulus (noise in El; a tone in E2). This fixated stimulus was
offset simultaneous with or 200msec prior to the onset of a noise and/or LED presented
equiprobably to the left or right. In E2, a condition was included for which the fixated
stimulus remained present for the trial duration. The results demonstrated a facilitatory
effect of removing a fixated auditory stimulus but only when the saccade target had a visual
component (either visual or visual+auditory); the same was true for a tIXated visual
stimulus. When the saccade target was auditory, there was interference from the
simultaneous offset of an auditory fixation, but no such interference from the offset of a
visual fixation. This interference effect notwithstanding, the results demonstrate the
similarity of visual and auditory stimuli for controlling fixation and suggest that
examination of auditory input into the fixation cells of the rostral SC would be fruitful.

authors_raw: Bruno J. Losier and Raymond M. Klein
title: Visual Orienting in Peri- and Extra-Personal Space by Patients with Stroke- Induced Parietal Damage
abstract: When patients with posterior right-hemisphere lesions perform Posner's visual orienting task a "disengage deficit" for uncued targets in contralesional space is consistently obtained. Converging evidence from single unit recording and human line bisection studies suggests that the parietal lobes preferentially process visual stimuli within grasping (peripersonal) space. This led us to hypothesize that the disengage deficit would be more severe in peripersonal than extrapersonal space, and in the lower visual field. Fifteen patients with posterior right-hemisphere lesion documented by CT scan (and normal controls) responded to validly, invalidly and neutrally cued target stimuli presented at the four comers of an imaginary square (8 deg across) at two distinct viewing distances (.280 m.=peripersonal and 2.24 m.=extrapersonal space), while maintaining central fixation. Cuing effects did not differ in peripersonal and extrapersonal space. A sub-sample of patients with parietal lesions showed greater deficits to both cued and uncued targets in the contralesional than ipsilesional visual field For these patients, the disengage deficit was greater in the lower (contralesional) quadrant. Surprisingly, following contralesional cues performance was identical for targets at the cued and uncued locations within the contralesional field A dissociation in the previous pattern of response in peripersonal and extrapersonal space was noted in some parietal patients. Our findings are discussed in light of Previc's proposal of a parietal specialization for the lower visual field, as well as Graves' suggestion that neglect is akin to the converse of blindsight (awareness without localization). • 26 1997 In-House Convention

authors_raw: Farhad Dastur, Donald Brown, Kathy Harrigan, Heather Wile
title: Smell perception and pregnancy sickness
abstract: OBJECTIVE: To examine changes in smell perception (sensitivity, identification, qualitative ratings) during pregnancy and how those changes influence symptoms of "pregnancy sickness" (PS): food/odor aversions, nausea, retching, and vomiting. DESIGN: A prospective, controlled, and longitudinal design. SETTING: A quiet, ventilated testing room in the !WK-Grace Health Centre. PARTICIPANTS: 20 healthy adult pregnant women and 20 healthy adult nonpregnant women not taking hormone-based contraceptives. MAIN OUTCOME MEASURES: Olfactory detection threshold; olfactory identification score (UPSm Liken-scale ratings of odour preference, intensity, fammarity, nauseousness, palatability, and evocativeness; number of food aversions and cravings; energy intake (kJ); nausea (frequency, duration, and distress); retching (frequency and distress); and vomiting (frequency, quantity, and distress) over a 12-hour period. RESULTS: A'pilot study revealed that first trimester pregnant women had a lower olfactory threshold (i.e., greater olfactory sensitivity) than nonpregnant women (t(8) = 2.46, p < .05, two-tailed). More recent and comprehensive results will be presented. CONCLUSIONS: These preliminary results are consistent with the clinical observation and the theoretical prediction that first trimester is associated by marked changes in olfactory perception, specifically increased olfactory sensitivity.

title: The Temporally Extended Self in Young Children
abstract:
Preschool children's awareness of self through time, or their Temporally Extended Self,
was investigated in this experiment Forty children aged 3;~;0 (years;months) completed
a delayed self-recognition task, an episodic memory task, and a task to measure future-
oriented prudence. Results indicated that most children were able to correctly label their
video image as "me". However, children who showed delayed self- recognition scored
higher on both memory and future-oriented prudence than children who did not
demonstrate delayed self-recognition. We propose that awareness of the temporally
extended self may develop first as an identity relation and then as a causal relation.

authors_raw: Robert C. Barnet, Laura H. Corbit and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: To squeak or not to squeak: The relation between ultrasonic vocalization and behavioural immobility in the rat
abstract: Experiments with adult rats were conducted to explore predictions of a leading model of defense fear (Fanselow, 1994). In the present work, rats were exposed to footshock that occurred at a particular and consistent temporal location withing training sessions during which behavioural immobility ("freezing") and ultrasonic vocalizations (UVs) were recorded. Of interest, was the course of acquisition for the ability of contextual cues of the training chamber to elicit behavioural immobility and UVs. Fanselow's (1994) model assumes freezing to be controlled by the ventral periaqueductal gray matter of the midbrain (vPAG), and ultrasonic vocalization to be controlled by the dorsolateral periaqueductal gray (d.IPAG). Activation of the dlPAG is thought to inhibit activation of vPAG. Accordingly, UVs and freezing should not co-occur and the model predicts increases in contextually- elicited freezing. Data from the present work challenge this prediction of the model. Benefits and limitations of using ultrasonic vocalization as a measure of learned fear are discussed. 28 1997 In-House Convention

authors_raw: Jeannette McGlone, S.E. Black and A. Sita
title: Construct validity of a yes/no recognition memory test used to predict and prevent global amnesia
abstract: This investigationexaminedthe constructvalidityof a yes/no recognitionmemory protocol routinely used to predict amnesiaduring an injectionof intracarotidsodium amobarbital prior to a temporallobectomy. One patient with global amnesia,two with severe but not global amnesia, and one with Wemicke's aphasia were administeredthe protocol without any drug injection. The globallyamnesicpatient scored below chance on the memory protocol, and 100%correct on the languageprotocol. Two cases of severe selective amnesiapassed both the memory and languageprotocols. The aphasic passed the memory protocol, but made errors on the language protocol. Combined,these preliminaryresults indicate that this yes/no memoryprotocolhas both convergentand divergentvalidity for differentiatingglobal amnesia and aphasia. Pl 1. The Description of Personality from a Defense Mechanism Perspective: A Nomothetic and an Idiographic Approach MichaelMacGregorand Darcy Santor Interest in defense mechanismshas increased,however, there is little consensus how defense mechanismscan be used to understandpersonality(nomotheticallyor idiographically). This paper discusseshow an idiographicinstrumentcan be used nomotheticallywith group norms to understandpersonalityfrom a defense mechanism perspective. The Defense-Q was used to assess 250 students (125 men) for the use of 25 defense mechanisms. The BFI-54 was used to assess the five factors of personality. When describingmen and women, men had high use of Rationalizationand low use of Acting Out Women had high use of Rationalizationand Passive-Aggression.When describingthe five factors, individualshighest on Conscientiousnesshad high use of Pseudoaltruismand ReactionFormation. Analyses were conductedfor each of the five factors. Results are discussed in terms of group norms for idiographicmeasures of defense mechansimsand the relationshipbetweendefense mechanismsand personality.

title: The Role of Objects in the Fixation Offset Effect
abstract:
Saccadic reaction time (SRT) is reduced when the fixation stimulus is removed immediately
before the target appears, an effect that is thought to be mediated by intra-collicular
inhibition of the motor programming machinery in the superior colliculus emanating from
the fixation zone in the rostral pole. To explore the role of object properties in mediating
this effect, observers fixated the intersection of a horizontal and a vertical line which
bisected the display monitor while an outline 2.8· square, drawn in the same color and
thickness as the lines, appeared 3.7" above the fix'ersection. On 1/2 of the trials this
square appeared solid and occluded the vertical line; on the remaining trials the outline
square was merely added to the drawing. One of three types of events occurred
simultaneous with (step), or 200 ms before (gap), a visual target (filled circles) that
appeared randomly 5.7" to the left or right of fixation: 1) center disappear (the central 2.s•
portion of the fix'ersection disappeared) 2) square disappear (the square was removed from
the display); 3) square to center (square moved from its position above the fixation to the
center of the fix'ersection). In the main experiment observers made saccadic eye
movements to the targets. SRT on step and gap trials was facilitated equally (compared to
square disappear) whether the intersection was removed (center disappear) or occluded (in
the square to center condition). On step, but not gap, trials SRTs were delayed in the
square to center condition. In a control experiment, where manual responses were made to
indicate on which side the target appeared, the pattern of performance was quite different,
indicating an oculomotor locus for the aforementioned effects which are interpreted in terms
of neural systems involved in oculomotor programming.

authors_raw: W. C. Schmidt and Raymond M. Klein
title: Object Influences on Illusory Line Motion
abstract:
We sought to determine whether illusory line-motion would show object-level effects
beyond those reported previously. Three experiments were conducted using ratings of Il.,M
frequency and strength as dependent measures, and convergences was demonstrated with
data collected by others using traditional measures of visual and cognitive processing.

title: The Effects of Pain on Event-Related Potentials
abstract:
The importance of accurate pain assessment and measurement cannot be overstated. This
presentation will briefly outline the progress in pain assessment leading up to the research
to be presented. As a result of collaboration between the Pediatric Pain Lab and the
Cognitive/Clinical Neuroscience Unit, research has investigated the utility of event-related
potentials (ERPs) as a pain measurement tool. This work capitalizes on the cognitive
relation between pain and distraction and is founded on an attentional resource allocation
model. Distraction appears to channel attention away from pain stimuli, and the P300 ERP
wave amplitude has been found to be related to attentional resource utilization. The
proposed model therefore predicts that pain will act as a consumer of attentional resources
and will consequently influence the amplitude of the P300. Preliminary study data will be
presented along with a discussion of possible brain mechanisms that underlie these
findings.

authors_raw: Myong G. Yoon and John F. Connolly
title: Neural interactions during syntactic and semantic processing of compound sentences
abstract: Using event-related brain potential (ERP) methods, we tested whether the putative semantic processing subsystem (the neural provenance of the N400) and the putative syntactic processing subsystem (that of the P600) function independently or they interact during the neural processing of semantically anomalous and/or syntactically incorrect compound sentences. We found that the two neural subsystems interfered each other during on-line processing of a simultaneous semantic anomaly and inflectional rule violation. On the other hand, the semantically incongruous state of a verb enhanced the neural responses (P600) to the inflectional disagreement in the same verb. The latter result shows an example of the positive mutual interaction (i.e., enhancement) between the putative semantic and syntactic subsystems. Strain and Sex Differences in MHC-Congenic Mice in Learning the Hebb- Williams Maze L. Stanford & R. E. Brown Using a mouse version of the Hebb-Williams Maze (HWM), we examined learning in male and female mice of two congenic strains (C57Bl/6J & B6-H-2K) for cereal reward. During acquisition males required fewer sessions than females to learn the task. There were significant differences in the number of errors over the 12 test mazes, a maze pattern by trial interaction, sex by trial, and sex by trial by maze pattern interactions, but no strain differences. The maze was then filled with water and the mice were tested under escape motivation to reach a raised platform in the goal box. In the swim-escape test the B6-H-2K mice reached criterion earlier than the C57Bl/6J mice. In the test trials, there were significant main effects for maze pattern and trial as well as the interaction of maze pattern by trial, but no strain or sex differences. These data illustrate the strength of the HWM to detect differences in learning and memory.

authors_raw: Renee Duquesnay Harry A. Robertson and Benjamin Rusak 2Department of
title: Antisense oligonucleotides decreases the phase shifting ability of light
abstract:
Pharmacology 1 and Psychology2
Circadian rhythms are synchronized primarily by cues from daily light-dark cycles. Light
that occurs at a time of day when it can shift (and therefore synchronize) the internal
circadian clock also causes increases in gene expression in cells of the suprachiasmatic
nucleus (SCN). We have investigated whether blockade of this increased gene expression
locally in the SCN can alter the phase-shifting responses of the circadian system of
hamsters to light. Hamsters were implanted with indwelling cannulas aimed at the SCN and
were housed in constant darkness. Hamsters received either control injections or injections
of antisense oligonucleotides for the IEGs c-Fos and JunB into the SCN at circadian time
(CT) 12 and were exposed to a 30 min light pulse at CT 18.
Preliminary results indicate that the antisense oligonucleotides can reduce the phase-shifting
effects of light, and suggest that gene expression induced by light plays a role in mediating
light effects on the circadian pacemaker.

title: Whose turn is it? Using impoverished social environments to explore human interaction.
abstract: Contmeporary analyses of human language use portray concersations as a hierarchically organized process requiring coordination of linguistic, paralinguistic and semantic information at several differen levels of activity (e.g. Clark, 1996). One implication of this heirarchical model of human communication is that activities identified at lower levels of coordination (e.g. establishing predictable temporal and sequential patterns of signals, manipulating joint attentional states) must be implemented in order for higher level activities to emerge (e.g. agreed upon knowledge states and social attibutions). Fortunately, in most face-to-face interactions mature speakers display exquisite control over the lower level activities in this hierarchical collaborative process. However, more impoverished social contexts (e.g. immature speakers, computer-mediated communication) may undermine the effective coordination of the lower level activities with the implication that emergent mutually agreed upon knowledge states and social attributional processes will also be disrupted. In this presentation I will describe my initial attempts to manipulate lower level communicative activities within the context of computer-mediated, text-based interactions. 6 1998 In-House Convention

title: The Voice from Within: Parasitic Manipulation of Host Hormones and Behaviour
abstract: When the larvae of the parasitic wasp, Cotesia congregata, emerge from their parasitized host, Manduca sexta, the host exhibits an irreversible decline in feeding and locomotion. The decline is not due to debilitation. In a search for the mechanism(s) underlying the lack of sensory responsiveness, we are measuring the levels of biogenic amines, such as octopamine, in the central nervous system of both control and parasitized M. sexta using HPLC-ED (high performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection). Octopamine is thought to regulate behavioural responsiveness in insects. In our experiments, we allow C. congregata to parasitize M. sexta on the first day of the fourth instar. Then, at various times during development, we dissect out the ventral nerve cord of control and parasitized and M. sexta and determine levels of biogenic amines. Preliminary data suggest that octopamine decreases and dopamine increases in concentration during parasitism.

authors_raw: Nadine H. F. Rossy and M. J. L. Sullivan
title: How Pain Castrophizers Cope with Stressful Life Events
abstract:
Individuals differ markedly in the degree of distress they experience when faced with an
aversive situation. Research ahs shown that individuals who 'catastrophize' are more likely
to experience heightened pain and emotional distress than indiviudals who do not
catastrophize.While the relation between catastrophizng and pain has been studied
extensively, little is know about how pain catastrophizers cope with non-painful stressful
situations.
The present study examined the relation between catastrophizing and methods of coping
with stressful major life events. Approximately 150 undergraduates were asked to fill out
the Ways of Coping questionnaire, the Pain Catastrophizing Scale and the Pain Self-
Efficacy Scale. Following completion of these measures, participants were asked to
immerse one arm in ice water for one minute. Consistent with previous research, we
predicted that Catastrophizing would be associated with heightened pain intensity during
the ice water immersion. Based on research showing a relation between catastrophing and
depression, we predicted that catastrophizers would report greater use of avoidant and
escape startegies to deal with non-painful life stressors. No predictions were made about
the relation between the subscales of the Ways of Coping Scale and responses to the ice
water immersion.

authors_raw: Fernando A. Larrea & John D. Fisk
title: The Prevalence of Cognitive Impairment and Dementia as defined by Neuropsychological Test Performance
abstract: The Canadian Study of Health and Aging (CSHA) estimated the prevalence of dementia for those aged 65 and older to be 8%. Other studies have produced both higher and lower prevalence estimates. Methodological factors that may contribute to these differences include: the use of, or the reliance on, neuropsychological testing, the consideration of functional impairment as a criterion of dementia, and the inclusion of the category of cognitively impaired, not demented (CIND) in the diagnostic classification. In order to examine these issues we conducted a reanalysis of the CSHA database for those individuals which completed neuropsychological testing. If the diagnosis of dementia required only the presence of impairments on neuropsychological testing, an increased prevalence estimate of dementia relative to the final (i.e. consensus) diagnostic opinion resulted. Adding functional impairment for the diagnosis of dementia reclassified many of the individuals as CIND and reduced this discrepancy in the prevalence estimates of dementia. However, the prevalence of CIND as defined by neuropsychological testing was higher than that defined by consensus diagnosis. The implications of these findings for neuroepidemiological studies of dementia and CIND will be discussed.

authors_raw: Good KP, Martzke JS, Lopeter W, Honer WG, Kopala LC
title: Vancouver Unimanual Tactile Word Identification Test (VUTWIT) ability in patients with schizophrenia: relationship to olfactory function.
abstract: Previous research has shown that olfactory identification deficits are common in patients with schizophrenia. Whether the olfactory deficits are specific to the olfactory system or represent more generalized processing abnormalities has not yet been resolved. Task complexity, generalized memory dysfunction and attentional compromize appear to be unrelated to olfactory deficits. However, comparison tasks used in these studies differed from the olfactory tasks in their reliability. The purpose of this study was to investigate the specificity of olfactory identification deficits in patients with schizophrenia by utilizing two analogous tasks of identification ability which were psychometrically similar, but differed in the sensory modality tested. The University of Pennsylvania Smell Identification Test (UPSIT) was adminsitered unirhinally and the Vancouver Unimanual Tactile Word Identification Test (VUTWIT) unimanually to 24 patients with schizophrenia and 44 normal controls. Controls outperformed patients on right and left UPSIT and VUTWIT. Significant Spearman correlations were observed between left VUTWIT and right and left UPSIT. These data suggest that in patients with schizophrenia, olfactory identification deficits may not be olfactory in origin, but rather may reflect other processing difficulties.

title: VRML Analyses of Movement
abstract:
The complexity of movement demands representations that allow the investigator to dissect
individual movement properties, and also establish rules for how these properties combine
in space and time. We shall show preliminary representations of movement made possible
by computer VRML methodologies. Eshkol-Wachman choreography systems of
individual movement parameters can be modeled, and thus analyzed.

authors_raw: Ryan C.N. D'Arcy, John F. Connolly, Gail A. Eskes, Stephen J. Phillips, & Edmund R. Harrison
title: The application of event-related potentials (ERPs) in the assessment of receptive language functions
abstract: While there is a long and distinguished history of basic research with event-related potentials (ERPs), more concentration on the clinical applications of this technique is paramount. Language is well suited for investigations of an applied nature because various ERP components associated with linguistic processing have been identified. Accordingly, we have explored the feasibility of adapting standardized neuropsychological measures of language for computer presentation with ERPs. These investigations have demonstrated that ERP components related to speech perception and recognition memory can be elicited by standardized test stimuli and utilized to evaluate language independent of behavior. Specifically, studies of vocabulary and comprehension have demonstrated that: 1) test items within an individual's ability can be differentiated on the basis of their neural responses; and 2) these experimental findings have been demonstrated at the individual level, thus maintaining clinical applicability. The potential implications of this novel technique will be discussed along with the presentation of recent experimental findings from the Psycholinguistic Assessments of Language Processing in Aphasia (Kay et al., 1992).

title: Gaze following and the control of attention in infancy
abstract:
In this talk I will consider the development of gaze following during infancy. I will
suggest that the phenomena of gaze following are best interpreted in terms of developing
attentional control. The infant's own attention is initially controlled exogenously by a
particular set of social stimuli but gradually comes under more endogenous control. Only
towards the end of infancy, does such endogenous control provide us with a warrant to
claim that infants understand the attentional states of others.

authors_raw: Tara L. Fidler and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Stress and Finickiness
abstract:
Finickiness, suppressed consumption of a novel bitter solution relative to baseline
consumption levels, has been observed following stress in humans and animals. In studies
reported previously (for example see Job and Barnes, 1995, Behavioral Neuroscience,
109, 106-116) the finickiness effect has been reported only following the sorts of shock
parameters that result in learned helplessness, 100+ shocks of 2-60 s in duration. Our rats
demonstrated finickiness for novel quinine 40 minutes after a shock session, consisting of
60 shocks of 2-s duration on a FT-60 s schedule. The effect was reinstated 27-28 days later
with only 10 shocks. Uncontrollable shock stress may increase the aversiveness of bitter
tastes.

authors_raw: William M. Brown, Boris Palameta & Chris Moore
title: Signaling Benevolence: An Evolutionary Perspective on the Encoding and Decoding of Altruism
abstract:
Emotions associated with benevolence can be viewed as evolved commitment devices that
help solve social dilemmas which require trust (Frank, 1988). Non-kin altruism may be
evolutionarily stable if these emotions are signaled and decoded accurately. It was
hypothesized that altruists produce involuntary facial signals which are detectable by
others. One-minute video-clips were constructed of 5 self-reported altruists and 5 self-
reported non-altruists. Altruist/non-altruist pairs were videotaped while playing the game
"Mastermind" jointly against the experimenter. In one condition the altruist received credit
for game performance; while in the other condition the non-altruist received credit. Under
blind conditions, one-hundred and sixty eight undergraduates rated the 10 video-targets'
helpfulness, concern, attentiveness and expressiveness. Results suggest that individuals
can detect altruists. Altruists were viewed as significantly more attentive and concerned
than non-altruists when playing the game for their partner's benefit. Non-altruists were
rated significantly more attentive and concerned than altruists when playing the game for
their own benefit. Regardless of condition altruists were viewed as more helpful than non-
