altruists. Results will be discussed in light of current conceptual models explaining the
evolution of altruism among non-relatives.

title: An Attention Based Negative Priming Effect Really!!
abstract:
Negative Priming (NP) was once thought to be the way to prove a hypothesis first put forth
by Treisman (1964). If one ignored an item, while attending another, the ignored item
would be inhibited. For the first time hundreds of researchers started to study inhibitory
effects of attention through the use of NP. However, since NP was discovered nearly all
of the founders of the phenomenon have abandoned this attentional explanation in favour of
a memorial one. Those who have not abandoned the inhibitory attentional explanation have
decided to stick with models that can only account for a fraction of the available data.
While current memorial explanations are difficult, if not impossible, to defeat, one NP
effect has been found that is best explained by attentional orienting. The reasons for the
generation of this particular effect may also allow one to design other experiments to
examine inhibition.

authors_raw: J .R. Blackbum, R.J. Mcinerney, & RM. Klein
title: Development of a brief battery of attentional tasks.
abstract:
In order to determine whether attentional capacity changes as individuals spend time
playing a video lottery terminal (VLT), we have developed a battery of computerized
attentional tasks that may be administered both before and after a session of VL T play. Six
previously characterized tasks have been modified to make them appear similar to each
other and to make them more like games for our gambling subjects. The six tasks were a
continuous performance task (CPT), a CPT-AX task, a Simon effect task, a flanker task, a
Rapid Serial Visual Perception (RSVP) task, and a stop signal task. A factor analysis of
data from twenty undergraduate subjects will be presented, indicating whether there are
identifiable factors of attention and whether the battery can be shortened by removing
redundant tests.
\ l

title: Melatonin and Light Effects on Circadian Rhythms and Thermoregulation
abstract:
Internally generated biological rhythms are synchronized to the external day principally by
light-dark cycles. Melatonin is secreted by the pineal gland at night and it too can influence
rhythm synchronization, apparently by having effects opposite to those of light. Melatonin
has, therefore, been used to treat disorders of rhythm entrainment in humans. The normal
nocturneµ_cle~linein body temperature in human appears to be related to melatonin
~ecJe_ti9_n.
Light exposure at night reverses the nocturnal temperature decline by inhibiting
melatonin secretion, an effect that can be opposed by giving exogenous melatonin.
Melatonin administration also appears to attenuate the phase-shifting effects of nocturnal
light. Since it is not clear how melatonin and light interact to affect circadian rhythm
synchronization and thermoregulation, we are studying light and melatonin interactions
using rodent model systems, in which levels of the two factors can be manipulated for both
nocturnal and diurnal species. Changes of core body temperature, hypothalamic
temperature, and locomotor activity in response to light exposure and melatonin
administration are being monitored continuously. Preliminary findings related ot the effects
of light and melatonin on body temperature, activity, and circadian rhythms will be
discussed.

authors_raw: Jean Saint-Aubin and Raymond M. Klein
title: The influence of parafoveal identification on letter detection accuracy revisited
abstract: When asked to detect target letters while reading a text, subjects miss more letters in highly frequent function words than in less frequent content words. According to the parafoveal- processing hypothesis, omissions occur when a word is identified parafoveally, instead of being fixated. Function words would be more likely to be identified parafoveally, because their high frequency allows identification on the basis of minimal visual information. This hypothesis has been tested with rapid serial visual presentation in which fixation was encouraged by presenting words one at a time on a computer screen. Detection accuracy was compared for target letter t embodied in the or in content words. Eighteen subjects read three prose passages, each presented at a different speed : 200, 350 or 500 msec per word. Contrary to the parafoveal-processing hypothesis, results showed more omissions for the at the fastest presentation rate. However, due to ceiling effects, there were no effects at the slower presentation rates. i • - •.'

authors_raw: Sherry H. Stewart, Heather Devine, and Margo C. Watt
title: Relationships of Anxiety Sensitivity and Dimensions of Alexithymia in a Young Adult Sample
abstract: The relationships between anxiety sensitivity (AS) and alexithymia dimensions were examined in a non-clinical sample of young adults, after controlling for item redundancy between the Anxiety Sensitivity Index (ASI) and the 20-item Toronto Alexithymia Scale (TAS-20). A sample of 238 undergraduate students completed the ASI and the TAS-20, as well as measures of trait anxiety and panic atta~k history. As hypothesized, high AS students (n =36) scored significantly higher than low AS students (n =41) on the two TAS-20 subscales suspected of sharing a functional relationship with AS (i.e., difficulty identifying emotions; difficulty describing emotions), whereas no AS group differences were found for the TAS-20 subscale believed to be functionally unrelated to AS (i.e., external-oriented thinking). Moreover, the pattern of relationships between ASI scores and alexithymia dimensions persisted following the removal of TAS-20 items identified as potentially conceptually redundant with the ASI, confirming the prediction that the relationship between AS and alexithymia is not merely an artifact of item redundancy. The results also revealed that individuals who both experience frequent anxiety (trait anxiety) and who greatly fear their anxiety symptoms (AS) appear most likely to use alexithymic coping strategies. Finally, the results showed that high AS in the absence of a history of panic attacks appears sufficient to motivate the use of alexithymic coping strategies. Potential clinical implications of the findings will be discussed. 12 1998 In-House Convention

authors_raw: Boris Palameta, Christina Staples, & Debbie Bird
title: Waist-to-Hip Ratio and Perceptions of Female Attractiveness
abstract: In a series of influential papers, Dev Singh has argued that human males are selected to use .. J I ·~':·_ h-·:. female waist-to-hip ratio (WHR) as a criterion for mate choice, because low WHRis an ~ ! honest signal of health and fertility. This paper reports the results of experiments designed to test some of Singh's hypotheses. A computer program (Adobe Photoshop) was used to manipulate the WHRs of photographed figures in 4 ways: 1) remove weight from waist (reduce WHR); 2) remove weight from hips (increase WHR); 3) add weight to waist (increase WHR); 4) add weight to hips (reduce WHR). Consistent with Singh's theory, underweight and average-weight figures were rated as more attractive when weight was removed from the waist rather than the hips. However, the reverse was true for overweight figures. Furthermore, despite their low WHRs, figures with experimentally enlarged hips were not perceived to be more attractive than figures with enlarged waists. Gender differences in attractiveness ratings were also investigated.

authors_raw: B. Rusak, J, LeBlanc, R. Morehouse, M. Rajda, S. Thompson, S. Kutcher
title: "Yet a little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to sleep" (Proverbs 6:10)
abstract: In the clinical psychiatric literature (including the DSM IV), a sleep disorder known as Delayed Sleep Phase Syndrome (DSPS) has been described. It is characterized in both adolescents and adults as a disorder of the timing of sleep and waking, in which sleep onset is strongly delayed when the individual is allowed to sleep at his or her preferred time, but is structurally normal. Under usual work or school conditions, however, sleep is severely disrupted because the individual is essentially incapable of shifting from the very delayed timing to more socially acceptable patterns. It is generally regarded as a disorder of circadian system entrainment. DSPS can have very serious consequences for school attendance, school performance and job opportunities. The incidence of this disorder has been estimated in several studies, but these estimates have varied over a couple of log units. We undertook an epidemiological survey of -1600 adolescents in Halifax Regional Municipality to assess sleep disorders, mood disorders and their relation to reported school performance, with an emphasis on identifying the incidence of DSPS in this population. While most of the data remain to be analysed, we will report on our preliminary findings and discuss the difficulties of definition and diagnosis in this area.

authors_raw: David I. Shore and Raymond M. Klein
title: Exploring The Potential Link Between Saltation, Reading, And Perceptual Grouping
abstract:
We explored the auditory variant of the saltation illusion (the mislocalization of a brief
stimulus followed by a stimulus at a different location) and developed a unique
measurement tool. Prior research has shown that auditory saltation is more robust for adult
dyslexics and, as such, has been proposed as a measure of temporal processing. Subjects
participated in three tasks: a subjective rating of the saltation illusion, a click enumeration
task, and the Olson reading tests. Click enumeration is proposed to provide an index of
perceptual grouping while the Olson test provides separate measures of orthographic and
phonologic coding. Individual differences in these three measures were compared. The
complex interactions between these three measures are discussed in relation to proposed
links between temporal processing, perceptual grouping and reading.

title: The joy of "hide-and-seek": Why is it harder to find people than objects within a false belief task
abstract: In order to better understand the interface between social and cognitive development in the preschool period, current research in my lab has focused on false-beliefs held for social referents. The paradigm we have developed is based on the standard Maxi task in which a participant is asked where a story-character will look for a caregiver or object relocated within the task without the character's knowledge. We are interested in why children do more poorly on tasks involving people compared to objects, and have varied the reason for relocation of the sought item to determine why. A series of studies will be presented to show that animacy and intentionality confound performance and delay standard age-effects when the sought item is a person. One explanation is that developing a theory of mind actually reduces performance when the sought-item has a mind, and ultimately, such awareness fits in with relationship constructs.

title: A Paradigm for Investigating Olfactory Conditioning and Differential Immediate Early Gene Expression in Mice
abstract: The purpose of the present experiment was to determine whether mice can acquire a conditioned response to an odour paired with a caloric reinforcer. Food restricted mice were exposed to a differential conditioning procedure in which experimental animals received odour A paired with sucrose in a dish covered with bedding and odour B presented alone in a dish with bedding. Control animals were either exposed to odour A and odour B alone, or to no odour with bedding. On day 5, animals were placed in a chamber divided into three equal sections with odour A and odour B on each end of the chamber. Experimental animals spent significantly more time digging in the dish which contained Odour A than Odour B relative to control animals. On day 6, all animals received exposure to odour A, odour B or no odour, 2 hours prior to perfusion. Evidence for immediate early genes expression for c-fos was assessed in brain structures such as the olfactory bulbs, amygdala and the basal ganglia. Immunohistochemical results will be discussed. I \ ' -y ' 14 1998 In-House Convention

title: Contralateral Dopaminergic Neurons are Involved in Generating the Reinforcing Effect of Brain Stimulation
abstract:
It is well known that the mesolimbic dopaminergic path plays an important role in
generating a reinforcing effect of brain stimulation. The majority of studies in this field,
however, dealt with only one side of the brain-the side where the electrode is implanted,
and the contralateral effect has been ignored. The functional relationship of contralateral
dopaminergic pathways involved in brain stimulation reward was examined. Rats were
implanted with two cannulae into the VTA and the NAc contralateral to a lateral
hypothalamic stimulating electrode and trained to bar-press. The frequency of stimulation
pulses was systematically changed, and a frequency-response curve was plotted for each
rat before and after 6-OHDA injections into the VTA. Haloperidol was also injected into
the NAc before and after placing the lesion to further examine the role that contralateral
dopaminergic pathways play in self-stimulation. The reward threshold increased after
either a 6-OHDA lesion if the VTA or a blockade of dopaminergic terminals in the NAc.
The results suggest that a contalateral pathway is involved in the reinforcing effect of
hypothalamic stimulation.

title: Depression and Substance Use Disorder Comorbidity Patterns Among Adolescents
abstract:
Objectiv~ Since the introduction of DSM-ill-R, clinicians and investigators have shown
increasing interest in the study of conditions comorbid with affective disorders.
Understanding depression and substance use disorder comorbity patterns among
adolescents is needed to guide treatment, research, and prevention/early intervention
approaches. Metho_d: 1) The literature on the comorbidity of depression and substance
use disorder among-adolescents from the past 10 years was reviewed to explore the most
prevalent patterns of depression-substance use disorder comorbidity; 2) examine the
correlates and longitudinal predictors of comorbidity; and 3) determine the extent to which
comorbid patterns convey unique information concerning etiology and treatment. ~~:
a body of literature concerning the comorbidity of depression and substnace use disorder,
with a substantially smaller amount of data conecerning other comorbid conditions. In
manu areas the literature was sparce, and pertinent questions concerning comorbidity
patterns remain unexplored. Nonetheless, available data warrant that interventions among
adolescents dealing with these comorbid conditions are likely to be quite important to
society. ~onclusjo!!~: Additional studies of the frequency of cormorbidity and associated
factors are greatly needed, to include studies of differential effects of treatment of
adolescents with various comorbid depression and substance use disorders, as well as of
depression and substance use disorder adolescents who differ in etiological factors .
Department of Psychology and Neuroscience
24 th Annual In-House Psychology Convention

title: Development and evolution of Ascidian nervous systems
abstract:
I am interested in mechanisms of neuronal differentiation in development. Ascidians are
closely related to vertebrates and are thought to be the closest extant form resembling the
early chordate common ancestor. The combination of invertebrate characters with their
vertebrate heritage make ascidians ideal for studies in neuronal development. I hope to
extend a previous study tracing the cell lineage development in larval Ciona intestinalis
through confocal reconstructions with various immuno-probes, as well as further
identifying cells with regard to their transmitter phenotype. This work will establish
whether or not lineage and cell type differentiation are related. I also hope to explore the
evolutionary relations between the ascidian groups by comparing the development of the
dorsal nervous system across different ascidian species.

title: On the causes, effects, functions and neural implementation of "inhibition of return"
abstract: Immediately following an uninformative peripheral stimulus (cue) there is a short-lasting facilitation of response times in the region of the cue which is followed by a longer lasting inhibition. This inhibitory effect of the cue upon subsequent responses to targets was named inhibition of return (IOR) to capture the dominant interpretation that, after having oriented to a location in space, attention is inhibited from returning there. This phenomenon has been the subject of intense interest because of its putative role in improving our ability to forage the visual environment for consequential information (Posner & Cohen, 1984; Klein, 1988; see Taylor & Klein, in press, for a review). I will present evidence from various methods in cognitive neuroscience which converge on: oculomotor programming as the cause of the inhibition, localization dependent processes as affected by the inhibition, enhanced search efficiency as one function of the inhibition, and the superior colliculus as the main neural structure involved in generating the inhibition. @ Effect of Male Removal on Gestation Length in Female California Mice (Peromyscus Calif ornicus) A.W. Lee, D. Cantoni & R.E. Brown California mice are a monogamous species that show biparental care. In mated pairs, the presence of the male facilitates reproductive success by increasing pup survival and reducing the female's gestation length. Under wheel running conditions for food, females living with their mate have a mean gestation length of 33 days. Females which have their mates removed 2 days postpartum, have a mean gestation length of 52.5 days, suggesting that these females had delayed implantation. The present study investigated the effect of male removal near the middle (Day 17) or end (Day 32) of the gestation period. Females living with their mate with ad lib food, have a mean gestation length of 34 days. Females which have their mates removed on Day 32 of gestation have a mean gestation length of 36 days. Females which have their mates removed in on Day 17 of gestation either have a mean gestation length of 36 days or fail to give birth. These results suggest that the presence of the male is important throughout the gestation period in maintaining a normal, gestation length in female California mice. Aspects of the male's behavior during parturition which may facilitate parturition will be discussed. 16 1998 In-House Convention

authors_raw: Guy Gingras and Donald E. Mitchell
title: Copy Cat: Spatial Localization Deficits in Cats with Experimentally Induced Amblyopia are Identical to Those Experienced by Human Amblyopes
abstract: Although human amblyopia is defined as uncorrectable poor visual acuity, not all forms of acuity are affected equally. The worst deficits are observed on tasks that measure the ability to locate differences in the position of objects in space. This research had as its goal a comparison of the performance of normal cats and humans on the same localization task (a test of the ability to detect a misalignment between 3 Gaussian blobs) and to document the deficits on this task of cats with various forms of early visual deprivation (monocular deprivation or strabismus). Deprived cats demonstrated localization deficits that changed with spatial scale (blob size) in a manner identical to that observed in human amblyopes. Moreover, like human amblyopes, the spatial deficits on the alignment task were considerably larger than the deficits of grating acuity. The results justify the use of these animals models of human amblyopia.

title: Instant identification of popular songs: Insights into auditory memory representations
abstract:
We examined memory representations for popular songs by testing listeners' ability to
identify brief excerpts from familiar recordings. Although songs are defined solely by
pitch and temporal relations, such representations might also contain information about
timbre (sound quality). Listeners were required to match 200- or 100-ms excerpts with the
song titles and artists. Performance was well above chance levels for 200-ms excerpts, and
poorer but still better than chance for 100-ms excerpts. Performance fell to chance levels
when dynamic information was disrupted by playing the 100-ms excerpts backwards, and
when high-frequency information was omitted from the 100-ms excerpts; performance was
unaffected by the removal of low-frequency information. In sum, successful identification
required the presence of dynamic, high-frequency spectral information. This finding
indicates that the timbre of specific recordings is encoded into memory representations of
popular songs.

authors_raw: D.P. Phillips, S.E. Hall, I.A. Harrington and T.L. Taylor
title: Space, the Final Frontier (for Gap Detection)
abstract:
The detection of brief periods of quiet ("gaps") in the ongoing stream of speech is
extraordinarily important for the identification of the stop consonants. In a previous
lifetime, we argued that the perceptual task of detecting such a gap can exploit either a
discontinuity detection mechanism, or a relative timing mechanism, depending on whether
the noises that bound the gap are the same or different. That is, the gap detection operation
can either be executed within a particular perceptual channel, or as a cross-correlation of the
activity in different channels. In our previous work, we forced the use of different
perceptual channels by using gap markers that stimulated different populations of peripheral
neurons. This year, we describe the gap detection behavior of listeners in an experiment in
which the gaps were bounded by markers that stimulated the same sets of peripheral
neurons, but different central perceptual channels.

authors_raw: S.E. Boehnke, D.P. Phillips, and S.E. Hall
title: Gaps: The Next Generation
abstract:
In the previous abstract, Phillips et al. showed that separating the leading and trailing
noises of a gap stimulus in space results in the gap detection task becoming a "between-
channel" one. We thus suspected that the gap detection paradigm might be used to
determine the tuning of spatial channels in human hearing. Consequently, I have looked at
the effect of systematic changes in the spatial location of the leading and trailing markers of
a gap stimulus. This task requires a relative timing operation to occur between two
populations of central neurons coding for spatial locations of the leading and trailing
markers. Intriguing results point to the existence of three spatial channels - left hemifield,
right hemifield, and midline. Equally fascinating however, is that these results map almost
perfectly onto the underlying neurophysiology of the central auditory coding of the binaural
cues for sound location.

title: Presynaptic adrenergic receptors inhibit REM sleep by attenuating pontine cholinergic transmission.
abstract:
Mesopontine tegmental cholinergic neurons are thought to be responsible for inducing
REM sleep by releasing acetylcholine (ACh) in the pontine reticular formation (PRF).
However, many of these neurons appear to be active (thus, potentially releasing ACh in the
PRF) not only during REM sleep but also during wakefulness. One explanation for this
apparent paradox is that, during wakefulness, ACh release in the PRF is strongly inhibited
by noradrenaline acting on presynaptic adrenergic receptors on cholinergic terminals.
Noradrenaline is likely to be released in the PRF during wakefulness by locus coeruleus
neurons. Our data using behavioural, in vitro electrophysiological, and in vivo
microdialysis techniques support this hypothesis, and suggest that presynaptic mechanisms
might play an important role in the control of behavioural state .

authors_raw: Fruitfly as a Young Egg Jason Emsley
title: The Embryonic Brain of Drosophila melanogaster, or A Portrait of the
abstract: The fruitfly, Drosophila melanogaster, has long been used a vital tool for neuroscientific research of a genetic and anatomical nature. Because of its stereotypical pattern of development and its relatively simple nervous system, Drosophila is a powerful tool for understanding the relationship between gene and neurotransmitter expression and development. I will present several views of the development of this simple nervous system, considering how information about gene and neurotransmitter expression can be related to the actual anatomy of the developing CNS. Semi-thin sections of embryonic tissue have been serially aligned and, from these sections, I have developed a computerised, three-dimensional anatomical atlas of the Drosophila nervous system. Images of this atlas will be considered, alongside images produced via fluorescence immunohistochemistry and confocal microscopy. 18 1998 In-House Convention (~ Behavioural Differences in MHC-Congenic Mice: The Step-Down Passive ~ Avoidance Test Tim Bredy, Will Moger and Richard Brown Although there are a multitude of physiological and pathological traits in which the major histocompatibility complex (MHC) might influence behaviour (lvanyi, 1978, Proc. R. Soc. Lond. B, 202, 117-158), MHC-congenic mice have primarily been tested for mate selection and individual recognition by odour (Yamazaki et al., 1976, J. Exp. Med.,144, 1324-34; Manning et al., 1992, Nature, 360,581-583). The present experiment investigated the possiblity that two strains of MHC congenic mice (C57Bl/6J vs. B6 H-2K) would differ in their ability to learn and remember a passive avoidance task. Glucocorticoids influence learning and memory (Smith, 1996, Beh. Brain Res., 78, 25-36) and there are strain differences in hippocarnpal glucocorticoid binding capacity in mice (Patacchioli et al., 1990, Beh. Brain Res., 37, 185-188). Plasma corticosterone concentrations were therefore measured using radioirnrnunoassay following the passive avoidance test. There was a significant strain difference in the ability of the no shock control group to habituate to the step-down passive avoidance apparatus that can be attributed to a strain difference in learning. The results indicated no strain differences in retention. Corticosterone levels are presently being analyzed. Strain differences in passive avoidance learning and in corticosterone levels may explain some of the MHC-related strain differences in mate selection, intermale aggression and other behaviours.

title: The tenacious blink
abstract:
When stimuli (e.g., letters) are presented in rapid succession, individuals are typically poor
at identifying a target (probe) that follows an earlier target (prime) by 100-500 ms. Four
studies investigated the nature of this perceptual failure, which is known as the "attentional
blink". These studies show that the strength and quality of the attentional blink is very
robust. Studies 1 and 2 compared two distinct methods of measuring the blink: letters
presented within a stream of numbers versus single letters followed by pattern masks, with
the ISi between letters held constant across studies. As predicted, blink strength was
highly correlated within subjects across studies. Studies 3 and 4 investigated whether
manipulating the quality of the physical evidence for the target stimuli (either prime or
probe) would affect the attentional blink. These maniputions had the expected effect upon
the processing of the degraded target (accuracy was a monotonic function of the quality of
the evidence), but contrary to our expectations, manipulating the difficulty of extracting the
identity of either the prime or probe target had no impact on the strength of the blink. The
implications of these findings for understanding the mental processes underlying the blink
will be discussed.

title: An Examination of Differences in the Time-Course of Oxazepam's Effects on Implicit Versus Explicit Memory
abstract:
The present study was designed to examine the effects of oxazepam on implicit versus
explicit memory processes, as a function of this drug's time course. The effects of
oxazepam (30 mg) or placebo on directly comparable tests of implicit memory (word-stem
completion) and explicit memory (cued-recall) were examined at three time points: 100
minutes post-drug (prior to the peak plasma concentration of oxazepam; i.e., "pre-peak"
condition), 170 minutes post-drug (close to peak; i.e., "peak" condition) or 240 minutes
post-drug (following peak: i.e., "post-peak" condition). Sixty volunteers were randomly
assigned to either the drug condition or the placebo condition in a double-blind design and
were tested on both memory tests at one of the three time points. In the "pre-peak"
condition, oxazepam impaired cued recall performance but did not impair priming. In the
"peak" condition, oxazepam impaired performance on both memory tasks. In the "post-
peak" condition, cued recall performance in the oxazepam group remained significantly
impaired. However, oxazepam-induced impairments in priming were only marginal,
suggesting that oxazepam-induced impairments in implicit memory processes begin to
wane following peak drug concentrations. These findings support the hypothesis that
benzodiazepines exert time-dependent effects on implicit memory processes. The results
also support the theoretical distinction between implicit and explicit memory processes,
since the directly-comparable implicit and explicit tasks showed different impairment curves
_I over time.

authors_raw: Sonia H. Greaven, Darcy A. Santor & Vivek Kusumakar
title: Life stress and depressive severity in adolescents with mood disorders
abstract:
This study investigated the occurrence of life stressors with mood disorders in an
adolescent population. Forty-two clinically-referred adolescents with primary mood
disorders (30 major depressed, 12 bipolar disordered), between the ages of 12 and 21 (M =
16.06 years) participated. Each participant underwent a systematic lifetime diagnostic
interview (K-SADS-PL),two measures of current depressive severity, and a computerized
inventory of life stressors within the past two years. Results indicated that the total
number of life stressors, as well as the severity of the stress associated with these, were
related to current depressive severity. Examination of correlations with specific stressor
categories revealed that overall, minor hassles, as opposed to major life events, were
related to depressive severity.

title: Neuraltransplantation for Parkinson's disease and Gene therapy for malignant brain tumors
abstract:
No abstract \f42) Cognitive Sequelae of Cancer and its Treatment in Children
Veronique Mackenzie-Bourne and Richard E. Brown
We reviewed 60 papers on the cognitive and neurologic sequelae resulting from the three
main cancer treatments, alone and in combination, for the treatment of brain tumours and
acute lymphoblastic leukemia. The initial goal of this paper was to examine studies
concerning long-term effects in cancer survivors under fifteen years of age and over the age
of sixty-five. The main body of literature, however, focuses on children. Long-term
cognitive effects in older survivors of childhood and adult cancers have not been studied.
The research reviewed suggests that some forms of cancer treatment are worse than others
for resulting cognitive sequelae, e.g. whole-brain irradiation and some chemotherapeutic
agents (e.g. methotrexate), especially in children under 3 years of age. However, a direct
link has yet to be found between cancer treatment and long-term cognitive dysfunction.
This is due, in part, to a lack of consistency in research design, method, and populations
studied. Research concerning the cognitive problems of treating cancer in the elderly and in
determining the long-term effects of cancer treatments in elderly people who are survivors
of earlier cancer must also be developed.

authors_raw: Marleen de Groot and Benjamin Rusak
title: Pavlovian Conditioning in the Circadian System: Fact or FANtasy?
abstract:
Environmental stimuli such as light can shift or entrain circadian rhythms and induce
physiological changes in cells comprising the central circadian pacemaker (the
suprachiasmatic nucleus: SCN), including altering the expression of immediate-early genes
(IEGs). A recent study (Stewart & Amir, 1996) showed that a stimulus that does not
normally have any of these effects can be made to do so after being paired repeatedly with a
phase-shifting light pulse in a Pavlovian conditioning paradigm. We assessed whether
such Pavlovian conditioning can occur in the circadian system. During training phases, a
20 min air disturbance (CS) was either paired or explicitly unpaired with a 60 min light
pulse (US) twice daily for ten-and-a-half days. Behavioural and gene expression results
show that the CS does not come to mimic the effects of light on the circadian system
following pairing. The failure to find evidence of conditioning could not be explained by a
lack of sensitivity to the CS used, since all subjects showed clear behavioural responses to
the presentation of the CS during both the training and testing phases of the studies.

authors_raw: Robin L. Patterson and Shinshu Nakajima
title: Suppression of protein kinase C in the basal forebrain reduces the rewarding effect of brain stimulation.
abstract: Activation of D1 and D2 dopamine receptors in the nucleus accumbens (NAc) is important for the rewarding effect of various reinforcers including brain stimulation. Blockade of D2, but not D3 or D4, receptors in this region with dopamine antagonists increases the threshold of reinforcement for brain stimulation. Activation of D 1 receptors is necessary to activate D2 receptors._ In the present study, the reward mechanism within NAc was studied by using falphostin C, a selective inhibitor of protein kinase C. The subjects were 42 male albino rats--:-nachof them was implanted with a bipolar electrode into the medial fore brain bundle and two cannulae bilaterally into NAc. They were trained to press a bar to receive electrical stimulation, and the threshold of reinforcing effect was measured before and after the injection of calphostin through the cannulae. Bilateral injection of calphostin into NAc increased the threshold of reinforcing effect in 2 hours and the effect reached the maximus in 4-6 hours. Recovery was observed in 24 to 48 hours after injection. Unilateral injection, either ipsilateral or contralateral to the electrode, was not effective. The results suggest that the rewarding effect of medial fore-brain stimulation is mediated by the activity of protein kinase C in NAc neurons. Symposium To Hebb and Beyond Donald Hebb was one of Canada's most famous and distinguished scientists, whose contributions were manifold and writings prolific. Hebb is widely recognized for his neo- connectionist assumption that correlated firing results in improved synaptic transmission (increase in connection weight) and his proposal of the cell assemblies. Hebb was a major advocate of psychology as a science and was a major force in directing its evolution in the 20th century. In order to examine Hebb and his work in a broader context, we will attempt to situate Hebb and his ideas within history, both in terms of the people who influenced him as well as in how his ideas shaped (or failed to shape) subsequent researchers. Some of the areas influenced by Hebb include LTP,kindling, neural networks, animal learning and memory, mental imagery, stopped retinal image, pain, aging and the role of early experience in cognitive and perceptual development. A life history of Hebb connecting significant life events to the various areas of interest integrates the diverse fields in which Hebb made a notable impact. For example, the publication of the Organization of Behavior ( 1949), which was influenced by the writings of William James and Santiago Ramon y Cajal, has exerted an ever-growing influence on those interested in mind, brain and how brains implement mind. 22 1998 In-House Convention Posters What does that robot want from me now? Young children's abilities to repair contingent queries. Suzanne Ferrier The ability of two-year-old's to respond differentially to specific and general contingent queries was examined with the use of an immobile robot as the conversation partner. Twenty-one 2;9-year-old and 21 2;3-year-old children were queried (5 specific and 5 general) immediately following 10 of their spontaneous utternaces in a free play session. The type of response strategy (repetition or specification) was coded for each queried utterance. Regardless of age, children tended to respond to a general query with an exact repetition of their original utterance. Age differences were found, however, in their replies to a specific query. The 2;9-year-olds most often replied with the specific information requested, whereas the 2;3-year-olds often repeated their original utterance, providing their partner with too much information. These findings suggest that it is not until 2;9 years of age that children efficiently satisfy their communicative partner's informational needs when conversational exchanges involve only linguistic cues. Temperature Selection and Use of Torpor by the Marsupial Sminthopsis Macroura Xiaowei Song* and Fritz Geiser Department of Zoology, School of Biological Sciences, University of New England, Australia *present address: Rusak Lab, Department of Psychology, Dalhousie University Many small mammals display daily torpor to minimize energy expenditure during the rest phase when faced with unfavorable environmental conditions. However, given a choice of thermal environments, it is not clear whether these daily heterotherms select ambient temperatures based on whether these minimize metabolic rates during torpor or during normothermia. It is also not clear whether they prefer being normothermic or torpid. In this study, we investigated temperature selection, activity patterns body temperature patterns, and the use of torpor in Sminthopsis macroura in a thermal gradient when food was freely available or restricted. Animals employed torpor regularly immediately after cessation of activity even when excess food was provided. Nevertheless, they selected high ambient temperature near the zone of thermoneutrality during both normothermia and torpor. Reduced food supply did not affect either temperature preference during torpor. Our study shows that S. macroura selected a high ambient temperature to reduce energy costs during normothermia. Nevertheless, daily torpor was frequently employed at the high temperature, especially when food was restricted. Measurement of Pain in Non-verbal, Cognitively Impaired Individuals Lynn Breau, Patrick J. McGrath, Carol Camfield, Christina Rosmus, G. Allen Finley A preliminary investigation of the properties of the Non-communicating Children's Pain Checklist was conducted. Caregivers of 38 individuals with Cerebral Palsy, Austism/PDD or a neurological disorder completed the checklist at home: twice when that person was in pain, once when they were in distress, and once when they were calm. Caregivers also completed the Checklist concerning their participant's past pain behaviour in general. Analyses were conducted to assess the Checklist's internal consistency, concurrent validity, sensitivity, specificity, and reliability. The results indicate that, when used by caregivers on actual events in a natural environment, the checklist can discriminate between calm and pain, is reliable over time, correlates with a visual analogue measure of pain, and is internally consistent. No differences were found between diagnostic groups, although reported behaviour did vary as a function of the relationship of the caregiver to the individual, and that individual's level of verbal comprehension. Evidence Based Decision Making: Construct Validity of a Sodium Amobarbital Recognition Memory Protocol McGlone, J., Black, S.E., Evans, J., Grantmyre, A., Moscovitch, M., Parkin, A., Sadler, M., Sita, A., Squires,E., Stuss, D., and Wilson, B.A. Purpose: We tested whether the Intracarotid Arnobarbital Protocol had criterion and concurrent validity. It was hypothesized that a recognition memory test designed for intracarotid injections and used to predict the risk of global amnesia prior to an elective temporal lobectomy should identify persons who are severely amnesic due to other neurological causes. •Speech tasks would be unaffected by amnesia, but would identify aphasia. The impact of aphasia on memory outcome would be negligible. Methods: Fifteen persons with severe amnesia and one aphasic were administered a four- item yes/no recognition memory protocol and a speech protocol. No drug injection occurred. Standardized neuropsychological tests were used to divide the amnesic group into those with Global Amnesia (i.e., retain no ongoing memories), Dissociated Amnesia, and Amnesia plus other neuropsychological deficits (i.e., Non-Dissociated). Results: Two Global Amnesic persons obtained scores at or below chance (i.e., failed) on the memory protocol. Unexpectedly, the remaining 13 severely amnesic persons (Dissociated and Non-dissociated), obtained passing memory scores. Amnesia had no impact on the speech protocol. An aphasic individual passed the memory protocol and was impaired on the speech protocol. Conclusions: This IAP memory protocol showed good criterion validity in identifying Global Amnesia, but it was insensitive to other disabling, severe amnesic disorders. Discriminant validity was shown by amnesic persons perf orrning well on IAP speech tasks. These results suggest that this IAP protocol might have validity in predicting a post surgical Global Amnesic disorder, but it did not identify and therefore could not predict other severe amnesic disorders 24 1998 In-House Convention The Effects of Alcohol and Alcohol Expectancy on Responses to Hyperventilation in High Anxiety Sensitive Individuals Alan MacDonald, Rochelle Hutson and Sherry Stewart High anxiety sensitivity (AS) levels are significantly associated with increased beliefs about the tension-reduction properties of alcohol (Karp, 1993). The present study examined the effect that these alcohol expectancies might have on alcohol's reactivity dampening effects in high AS young adults (Baker, MacDonald, Stewart & Skinner, under review), in addition to alcohol's pharmacological anxiolytic effects. A three cell beverage condition design was used to assess the contributions of expectancy vs. expectancy plus pharmacology: told alcohol-given alcohol (A-A, pharmacology + expectancy); told alcohol- given placebo (A-P, expectancy only); and told placebo-given placebo (P-P, control). High AS student participants were randomly assigned to each condition. Following alcohol consumption and absorption, participants underwent a 3 minute voluntary hyperventilation challenge. Preliminary analyses indicate that A-A participants show greater reductions in affective, cognitive and somatic symptoms in response to challenge than A-P and P-P participants. However, A-P participants do not show reduced affective and cognitive symptoms to challenge compared to P-P participants. In fact, correlational analyses suggest a positive relationship between subjective intoxication measures and levels of affective reactivity within the the A-P group (a "reverse expectancy" effect), and a negative relationship between these measures in the A-A group. Implications of these results for prevention of alcohol abuse in high AS individuals are discussed. Role of Sulfated Cholecystokinin Octapeptide (CCK-SS) in the Modulation of Separation-Induced Ultrasonic Vocalizations in 12 Day Old Rat Pups Paul D. Mendella, Anna Maria Szczesniak, Richard E. Brown Rat pups emit ultrasonic vocalizations (UV's) when separated from the dam and littermates. Anxiogenic drugs increase UV s, while anxiolytic drugs decerase UV s. The neuropeptide cholecystokinin (CCK) induces anxiety in health human volunteers and in individuals diagnosed with panic disorder. The purpose of the present investigation was to assess the effects of CCK-8S on UVs, locomotor activity, and body temperature in 12 day old rats pups, 15 or 30 minutes following drug treatment. Pups were injecteJ intraperitoneally with either vehicle or CCK-8S (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0 µg/kg). After 15 minutes, male pups emitted more UVs and less turing behaviour than female pups. This is one of the few studies to find sex differences in rat pup UVs. After 30 minutes CCK-8S (4.0 mg/kg) significantly reduced line crossing frequency. In addition, male pups completed fewer head raises compared to female pups 30 minutes following drug treatment. Neither UV rate or body temperature was significantly altered by CCK-8S after 15 or 30 minutes. These findings suggest that CCK-8S is not directly involved in the modulation of anxiety-related behaviour in the separation-induced UV model of anxiety. Saccharin must be added to the taste cue to condition robust taste preferences Cathy Forestell and Vin LoLordo A reverse-order differential conditioning procedure was employed to condition taste preference in rats. During training, one taste solution (CS+) was presented 3 min after a calorific reinforcer (glucose), and another taste solution (CS-) was presented 3 min after water. For half of the animals, both taste solutions always contained saccharin, whereas for the remaining animals, the tastes were mixed in water. Subsequently, two-bottle tests revealed a significant preference for the CS+ relative condition. These results suggest that without the presence of sacchrin in the CS solutions, conditioned preferences do not occur. Simulating oculomotor behavior within a neural field model S. D. Simpson, R. Klein, T. Trappenberg Oculomotor programming can be viewed as the process of integrating exogenous (environmental) and endogenous (intentional) signals into a unified motor goal. Just as many experimental researchers recognize the importance of understanding both the bottom- up and top-down influences on oculomotor programming, we believe it is important to recognize that computational modeling of such systems should also be constrained by multiple levels of analysis; specifically by behavioral and neurophysiological data obtained from the area of interest. By extending and modifying a current neural field model of oculomotor programming (Kopecz and Schoner, 1996) we have developed a computational account of both behavioral and neurophysiological data from a variety of saccadic paradigms including target location preparation (Munoz & Dorris, this meeting), antisaccades (Everling et al, this meeting), and distractor manipulations. In the model, environmental and endogenous signals are integrated within a buildup layer allowing for competition among possible oculomotor programs. The activity of this buildup layer serves as input to a burst layer whose activity represents a specific oculomotor command. This poster will describe the model's architecture and demonstrate its ability to simulate saccadic reaction time patterns and neuronal activity of buildup and burst neurons within the Superior Colliculus. Possible enhancements to the model will also be discussed .

title: Pain, Pain Coping Strategies, Affective Distress, and Return to Function in Athletes Following ACL Surgery
abstract:
This study has examined pain, coping, and functional recovery in athletes who have had Anterior
Cruciate Ligament (ACL) reconstructive surgery. All patients were examined pre and post-op on a
variety of measures. Post-op assessment periods were: 24, 48, and 72 hrs, 2, 8, 16 weeks. As may be
expected pain and functional disability peaked shortly after surgery and gradually declined over the
course of 16 weeks. The relationship between pain coping and effective distress however tells a much
more interesting story ... stay tuned.

authors_raw: Ivan Kiss, Wolf Fazikas, Hannah Pazderka-Robinson and Darlene Floden
title: Electrophysiological Studies of Task Complexity: Does P3b Latency Reflect Stimulus Evaluation Time?
abstract:
P3B latency has been considered a measure of stimulus evaluation time (Kuras, McCarthy & Donchin,
1977; McCarthy & Donchin, 1980; Magliero et al., 1984). However, it is not clear which of the
processes involved in stimulus evaluation are indexed by P3B; whether it reflects detection of salient
stimulus features, completion of cognitive processes required for full target identification beyond the
physical stimulus level or some intermediate stage.
We have completed two experiments in which task difficulty was manipulated with minimal changes
to stimulus properties and response requirements. P3b latency measures and the emergence of
additional ERP features confined to more difficult processing tasks suggest that:
1) P3b does not index completion of stimulus evaluation
2) Longer latency components emerge with more complex tasks.
We also report results of preliminary studies involving a "depth of processing" manipulation which
suggest that P3b latency is sensitive to semantic processing requirements.

authors_raw: Jana Podhorna and Richard E. Brown
title: Nitric Oxide and Anxiety in Rat Pups
abstract:
The role of nitric oxide (NO) in anxiety was studied in the model of ultrasonic vocalization (USV) in rat
pups using drugs that manipulate formation of NO in the brain.
Both the selective neuronal nitric oxide synthase (NOS) inhibitor (7-nitroindazole) and the non-
selective NOS inhibitor (L-NAME) decreased USV in a dose-dependant manner. However, the non-
selective inhibitor also suppressed locomotion. The inactive isomer of the NOS inhibitors, D-NAME,
produced no effect on USV or locomotion. The NO precursor, L-arginine, had no effect on USV but
increased locomotion at low doses.
Thus, drugs that decreased NO synthesis showed anxiolytic effect. However, only the selective NOS
inhibitor had a selective anxiolytic effect, i.e., reduced USVs without causing sedation. Increased
synthesis of nitric oxide in the brain had no apparent effect pro-anxiety effect but increased locomotion
in this model of anxiety.

title: An Investigation of Dissimulation in a Chronic Pain Sample
abstract:
Research aimed at detecting pain patients' dissimulated responses on psychological self-report measures
has been conducted under the assumption that a small number of patients may intentionally exaggerate
their psychological symptoms in order to receive increased financial compensation (e.g., continued
disability benefits). In the present study, a sample (N=85) of chronic pain patients assessed at a
multidisciplinary rehabilitation program was utilized to investigate the incidence of various self-
presentation styles. Previous assumptions were not supported as scores on the validity scales of the
Personality Assessment Inventory (Morey, 1991) were indicative of a high frequency of overly positive
self-portrayals and the absence of unrealistically negative self-portrayals. Correlations between the
validity scales and subscales of the Multidimensional Pain Inventory indicated significant negative
associations between positive self-presentations and pain-related variables such as pain severity and
pain-related distress. The implications of these findings for research and clinical practice will be
discussed.

title: Does Sex Make You Sick?: Trade-offs Between Immunity and Reproduction in the Cricket, Gryllus integer
abstract:
Animals have finite energy budgets. The immunocompetence hypothesis postulates that males will
down-regulate their immune systems at the time of sexual maturity and divert this energy into
reproduction. This theory is controversial, largely due to the difficulty in testing it in vertebrates. The
theory can be better tested in crickets, which have simpler immune systems. I estimated the amount of
energy invested in immunity during a male's lifespan by 1) counting the number of circulating immune
cells and 2) by measuring the titres of an anti-microbial protein (prophenoloxidase). The ability of the
immune system to destroy parasites and kill bacteria was also tested at different ages.
There was no evidence for a decline in energy invested in immunity in sexually mature males over
their lifespan or relative to females. It is possible that immune system costs have already been
minimized, and further reductions in immune capacity would lead to a shortened lifespan and reduced
total lifetime fecundity.

authors_raw: Lianne Stanford, Richard E. Brown, Sean Taylor, and Paul E. Neumann
title: Using Mouse IQ to Assess Learning and Anxiety Behaviour in Barrelless Mice
abstract:
The barrelless (brl) mouse strain was derived from a naturally occurring mutation observed in ICR mice
at the Universite de Lausanne (Switzerland). brl/brl mice show a lack of specific cortical organization
known as barrelfields. In control mice, barrelfields are formed by the whisker neuron thalamocortical
organization. A high-resolution genetic map of the region around the brl locus revealed that the gene for
adenylyl cyclase 1 (Adcyl) was missing in the mutant. Adenylyl cyclase 1 is associated with
neuroplasticity in the hippocampus and cerebral cortex (Abdel-Majid et al. 1998. Nat Genet, 19, 289-
91). Using targeted mutagenesis, and Adcyl knockout mouse model was created that performed poorly
in some measures of the Morris water maze task (Wu et al., 1995, PNAS, 92, 220-4). To further
investigate the role of Adcyl in memory processes, brl/brl and control (brl/- C57BV6J) mice of both
sexes were studied using tests that measure locomotion and exploration (open field), anxiety (elevated
plus maze), novel object recognition, and spatial learning (Morris Water maze). Differences between
genotype and sex in a variety of measures on each test will be reported. This will provide insight into
potential behavioural deficits due to the cortical misorganization present in the brVbrl mice. The
importance of using multiple tasks to assess cognitive function in mice will also be discussed.

authors_raw: William M. Brown and Chris Moore
title: Is Prospective Altruistic Detection an Evolved Solution to the Adaptive Problem of Subtle in Cooperative Ventures?
abstract:
Reciprocal altruism in humans may be made possible in part by the existence of information-processing
mechanisms for the detection of overt cheating. However, cheating may not always be readily
detectable due to the division of labour. Subtle cheating poses a serious problem for the evolution of
altruism. This paper argues that subtle cheating may have exerted selective pressures on early hominids
to be sensitive to information regarding the genuineness of an altruistic act. In a two experiments,
subjects were required to complete Wason selection tasks designed to allow for the detection of altruism.
Performance on the altruist-detection tasks was compared to performance on control Wason selection
tasks (Experiment 1) and to performance on control and cheater detection tasks (Experiment 2).
Participants were significantly better at solving cheater detection and altruist detection versions
compared to control versions of the problems and there was no significant difference between altruist
and cheater detection. Results are discussed in relation to recent conceptual models for the evolution of
altruism. Specifically, it is argued that non-kin altruism may be an evolutionarily stable strategy if
altruists can detect one another and form mutually beneficial social support networks.

title: Role of Suppressed Feeding in Manduca sexta in Response to a Bacterial Infection.
abstract:
Typically animals suppress their intake of food when sick. Traditionally, this was viewed as a
maladaptive response since increased energy is required for an effective immune response. However,
there is some evidence to suggest that infection-induced anorexia may aid the host in suppressing
bacterial growth. It has been suggested that infected animals suppress their feeding to deprive invading
bacteria of iron, an essential element for their survival. In this study, iron levels were manipulated in
tobacco homworms infected with the insect pathogen, Serratia marcescens. On day one of the fifth
instar, animals were fed either an iron-rich diet, a diet with normal amounts of iron, or an iron-free diet
and the effect on the amounts of free and bound iron in the hemolymph was assessed. Six hours later,
these animals were injected with an LD50 dose of Serratia marcescens. It is hypothesized that the
animals fed the iron-rich diet will show a greater mortality than those fed the normal iron diet, whereas
those fed the iron-free diet will show lower mortality than those fed the normal iron diet.

title: Adaptive Changes in Egg-laying in the Cricket, Gryllus integer, in Response to Infection by the Potentially Lethal Bacterium, Serratia marcescens
abstract:
Animals must distribute resources to both somatic and reproductive activities, and the energy allocated
to reproduction at each age is selected so that lifetime reproductive success is maximized. However, if
the probability of future reproduction declines due to age or illness, then it would be adaptive for
animals to re-allocate current resources, and increase reproductive effort at the expense of survival.
Previous tests of this hypothesis have been performed primarily on birds or mammals, where
parental care complicates the issue. We are currently testing the hypothesis in the field cricket, Gryllus
integer. G. integer females lay eggs, but do not exhibit parental care. Their life history suggests that
they should increase egg-laying when infected with a potentially lethal pathogen, such as the bacterium,
Serratia marcescens. Experiments are in progress.

authors_raw: Sonia H. Greaven, Darcy A. Santor, Vivek Kusumakar
title: Timing of Transitions in Adolescence
abstract:
The study of adolescent transitions, which mark the development of the individual from childhood to
adulthood, must address the issue of temporality. Although the changes associated with adolescence
have been shown to occur in an interrelated manner (e.g., Lerner et al, 1996), the effects of the timing of
adolescent transitions has not been adequately explored. The prevalence of various transitions in a
normal adolescent population (N = 596) was examined. The analyses of these data determine a) whether
each of 12 transitions has been experienced by a majority of adolescents, and b) which transitions are
viewed as most important by the adolescents. The effect of timing of transitions will be addressed in 3
ways. First, whether the timing of transitions in comparison with the adolescents' social referent will
affect how the transitions are experienced. Secondly, whether particular subsets of these changes occur
in an interrlated and/or sequential manner. Finally, the question of whether the lack of a given transition
within a normative sequence represents a potential risk factor will be addressed.

title: Issues of Confidentiality in Testimony Given by Psychotherapists
abstract:
The role of a psychotherapist is changing dramatically. Psychotherapists are professionals who examine
issues that an individual may have through the use of a variety of techniques. In addition, a
psychotherapist may act as a resource seeker, detective, confidante, mediator, and team coordinator.
More recently, psychotherapists have acquired two roles in the legal arena. In the legal arena, the
psychotherapist may act as an expert witness or a fact witness. As an expert witness the psychotherapist
is asked to give his/her opinion about legal issues and/or may act as a forensic consultant. In contrast, as
a fact witness, the psychotherapist testifies to the direct observation of the client during therapy. The
issues of confidentiality, informed consent, and legal privilege that arise for a psychotherapist in the role
of fact witness will be presented as well as alternative courses of action that may be taken. It is
concluded that a need for education about this dilemma exists.

authors_raw: Karen E. Jaskie and Patricia A. McMullen
title: The Role of Semantic Memory in Category-Specific Visual Agnosia
abstract:
Category-specific visual agnosia (CSV A) is a disorder of object recognition in which the ability to
recognize objects belonging to one or more categories is selectively impaired following brain damage.
In one form of CSV A, the ability to recognize living things is impaired, while recognition of non-living
things remains relatively intact. Case studies of CSVA have indicated damage to both semantic memory
mechanisms and visual perceptual mechanisms. A variety of theories have been developed to explain
the existence of a living/non-living double dissociation. McRae et al. (1997) emphasize the importance
of intercorrelations amongst individual semantic features. Upon analyzing 190 different concepts, they
found that there was a much higher intercorrelation between the features of living things than artefacts.
Using a lexical decision priming task, we plan to investigate differences in the connectivity of the
features of living things and the features of artefacts within the network of semantic memory.

title: Orientation Effects in Face Recognition: How Similar Are They to Object Orientation Effects? i' Mary-Ellen Large
abstract:
A common debate in face and object recognition literature is whether faces and objects share similar
processing systems or not. As an extension to this debate my proposed research investigates whether
faces exhibit the same characteristic responses to orientation as objects. Joliceur (1985) found that the
time to name rotated objects (between 0 deg and 120 deg) varies systematically as a function of
departure from an upright orientation. With practice the magnitude of this orientation effect decreases
significantly. Tarr and Pinker (1989) demonstrated, using novel abstract stimuli, that this effect
diminishes because orientation-specific templates are formed each time the objects are identified.
However, Murray et al (1993) failed to support this view when they used line drawings of real objects.
Further work by Schellinck, McMullen and Hamm (manuscript) using feature scrambled objects
suggests that practice effects are based on orientation-invariant features. The purpose of further study is
to discover whether a practice effect occurs with photographic face stimuli. If a practice effect is found
the next stage will be to investigate whether this phenomenon is based on orientation-specific templates
or orientation invariant representations.

authors_raw: Deanna Braaksma, Allen Finley, Patrick McGrath, and John Connolly
title: Awareness and Implicit Memory Using Event Related Potentials During Recovery from Anesthesia in Adolescence
abstract:
Explicit and implicit memory has been shown after General Anaesthesia (GA) suggesting that
information was processed deeply enough to access its semantic content. A late latency Event Related
Potential (ERP) component, the N400, is sensitive to the semantic features of language and requires
attention to both the eliciting stimulus and its context. In the present study, auditory ERPs to
semantically congruent and incongruent word pairs will be used to assess unconscious cognitive
processing and the presence of implicit memory during recovery from anesthesia. Adolescents who are
booked for minor procedures under GA will be recruited from a Childrens Day Surgery ward. Eight
channels will be recorded and word pair stimuli will be presented preoperatively and during and after
recovery from GA. Implicit memory will be assessed using a word association task to primed words. It
is hypothesized that the presence of an N400 will be related to the presence of implicit memory.

title: Animate and Inanimate Entities ' '\ Nicole Blanchet Traditional Piagetian theory has argued for a developmental change in children's early conceptual strategies called the thematic-taxonomic shift. In conventional match-to-sample and sorting tasks, young preschoolers have been described as employing thematic strategies (e.g. matching dogs with bones), while older preschoolers have been said to employ taxonomic strategies (e.g. matching dogs with dogs). More recent research has questioned this view, suggesting instead that children across this age range reveal a persisting tendency to organize their world into like kinds based on perceptual similarity. These more recent data have also provoked a new set of questions about the factors that might undermine the pervasive early influence of perceptual similarity, and facilitate the adoption of thematic conceptual strategies instead. In this presentation, I will describe preliminary plans for my thesis research in which I hope to examine some potentially differential effects of animate and inanimate stimuli on children's tendencies to adopt taxonomic and thematic conceptual strategies. Tl 7. Using Stimulus-response Compatibility to Stimulate Relative Contribution of Intention of Attention to Inhibition of Return
abstract: Two experiments examined the interaction between the Simon effect (SE) and inhibition of return (IOR). The SE refers to better performance when the task-irrelevant location of the imperative stimulus and response spatially correspond relative to when they do not correspond. IOR refers to better performance when the location of a precue and target do not correspond elative to when they do correspond, when cue location is task irrelevant and stimulus onset asynchrony (SOA) is generally greater than 400 ms. In the present investigation, the form of the precue provided subjects with response selection information, and the target provided response execution information. To the extent that IOR has a motoric component, inhibition at the cued location should reduce the SE. Similarly, this prediction is in line with an attention shifting account of the SE: there should be a smaller SE at the cued location relative to the cued location. At the long SOA, IOR was larger when the spatial location of the target and response corresponded relative to when they did not correspond. Similarly, SE was greater at the uncued than at the cued location. The magnitude of IOR was influenced by the intentions to produce a response. The results support an attentional-motor view of IOR and they support an attention-shift account of the SE.

title: Cued Judgments of True and False Memory Reports: The Effect of Training and Individual
abstract: ., i Mary Ann Campbell & Stephen Porter The false memory debate has highlighted the complexity of evaluating memory reports in both legal and therapeutic settings (e.g., "recovered" memories of abuse, erroneous eyewitness statements and false confessions). As such, the present study examined the influence of cue training and individual differences on the ability to make accurate credibility judgments of true and false memories. Information about the personality characteristics and baseline measures of perceived cues to false memory reports were obtained from the sample of 100 university undergraduate students. Half of the sample received information about cues research has shown to be characteristic of true or false memories (training group). A control group received no training. Both groups viewed a video of a common target recounting a truthful and implanted childhood memory in separate interviews and were asked to judge the credibility of both reports and indicate the cues used to assist their judgments. Judgment accuracy was assessed separately for true and false reports. Although results are not yet available, a significantly greater use of appropriate cues and better detection accuracy of the trained group compared to controls would support the hypothesis that training improved detection of false memory reports. The results of this study will offer insight into the benefits of training and the role of individual differences in judgments of memory credibility. Further, the findings will contribute to a framework on which the training of law enforcement professionals and therapists could be developed to assist in making more accurate credibility assessments. Wur'f.v-y ?J/'-f/i,ttY,_j

title: Executive Function Abilities and Social Behaviour in Young Children < Shana L. Nichols & Chris Moore
abstract:
Interest in executive function abilities in young children has flourished within the area of developmental
psychopathology. However, little is known about the normal development of executive functions and the
relationship between executive function abilities in young chidren and social behaviour has been largely
unstudied. Recently, Moore, Barresi, and Thompson (1998) found a relationship between children's
performance on an inhibitory control task and their ability to engage in future-oriented sharing
behaviour. Further research is needed in order to address the nature of executive function development
in young children and its implications for emerging social behaviour. The goal of the present research
was to explore possible links between executive functions and social behaviour in three-and-a-half year
olds. Executive function tasks included measures of inhibitory control, attentional flexibility, and
working memory. Social behaviour was examined in two ways: behavioural observations during a free
play session and a future-oriented sharing task.

title: Self-processes in Early-elementary Age Children ? Sharon Clark •""", '. ()'.: , < ,._
abstract: A sense of self is thought to emerge from early interactive relationships with significant others. Internal working models form two complementary models of self and other and include the child's beliefs concerning worthiness of care in the conceptualisation of self, and statements regarding the dependability and acceptance of the attachment figure in other. Concurrent and predictive relations of child-mother attachment security to self-concept and self-esteem in 29, 5-year-old children (girls n = 10, boys n = 19) were examined. Self-esteem was defined as the evaluative component of the self and self- concept as the descriptive component of self. Attachment security was measured at age 2 and at age 5. Children who were able to admit imperfections in themselves and maintained a positive valence on the self-esteem measure had higher attachment security scores at age five than at age two and had higher self-concept scores than the other children. Data currently being collected will be discussed along with these earlier findings.

title: Event-Resolution Imaging: Overcoming the Limitations of Signal Averaging in ERP Research '~ Randy Lynn Newman
abstract:
The interpretation of event related potential data has typically relied on the process of signal averaging
to extract meaningful information from background noise. The major limitation of this process is that
numerous trials are required to generate a single meaningful waveform. A new method of interpretation
developed by Thoughtform Corporation, called event-resolution imaging (ERi), appears to overcome
this limitation. ERi employs a variety of methods to discover a set of features which best discriminates
and identifies epoch types. This information is encoded as an interpretation map, which is subsequently
used to analyze and interpret raw EEG data on an epoch by epoch (i.e. single trial) basis. The result of
this analysis, is the production of a high signal-to-noise, non-averaged waveform. A demonstration of
ERi will be performed, to be followed by a discussion of its potential role in current ERP research.

authors_raw: Fernando A. Larrea and John D. Fisk
title: Are There Differential Effects of a Selective Reminding Procedure on the Learning Characteristics of Individuals with Alzheimer's Disease and Isolated Memory Impairment?
abstract:
There is currently debate regarding the effect of cues on the learning of subjects with Alzheimer's
Disease (AD). Some have suggested that cuing is particularly important in AD, whereas others don't
support this facilitation effect. This study examined responses on the Buschke Cued Recall Test
(BCRT) to compare free and cued recall in groups of subjects in the Canadian Study of Health and
Aging with either AD or isolated memory impairment. Subjects with isolated memory impairment
performed better than the AD subjects on all measures. However, both groups benefitted to a similar
extent from cues on the initial learning trials and both had a similar rate of learning on the free recall
trials. The quantitative but not qualitative differences in performance of the groups suggests that many
subjects with isolated memory impairment are in the pre-clinical stages of AD. The positive learning
slope for the AD group suggests that a cued recall procedure can facilitate learning in AD subjects.
Keynote Speech: The Discovery of Spoken Language
\' Dr. Peter Jusczyk
To acquire a native language, a learner has to correctly identify the units (words, phrases, and clauses)
that are combined and manipulated in forming utterances in that language. A failure to identify the
appropriate units in the speech input would greatly complicate learning how sentences are structured in
the native language. There is considerable evidence that, during the second half of the first year, infants
rapidly converge on the specific properties that distinguish the sound organization of their native
language from other languages. Infants' developing sensitivity to native language sound structure not
only plays a role in helping to segment words from fluent speech, but has consequences for learning
about the language's grammatical organization. Thus from an early age, infants appear to use
information in the speech signal to group the input in a way that facilitates the discovery of the structure
of utterances. Findings from our laboratory show that infants detect cues to clausal and phrasal units in
utterances well before they actually begin producing and combining words. Furthermore, the groupings
that infants perceive in the input influence what information they encode and retain about speech. In
addition, there is evidence that infants are sensitive to the sequential order of elements in the information
that they retain. I will discuss some of these findings and their implications for understanding how
learners discover the organization of their native language.
Jusczyk, P. W. (1997). The discovery of spoken language. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press.

authors_raw: \/ Ryan C.N. D'Arcy, John F. Connolly, Larry Gates, and Elisabet Service
title: An Investigation of Sentence Reading Using Event-related Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) ,,
abstract: Neurophysiological studies of reading have successfully elucidated electrical and magnetic correlates of cognitive processing, such as semantic analysis manifested by the N400. We used an established paradigm from ERP/MEG research to study the neuranatomical regions of activation during sentence reading. The participants read a series of sentences with terminal words that were either semantically congruent or incongruent (e.g., The pizza was too hot to: eat/sing). In both conditions, activation was observed in those neuroanatomical regions (e.g., visual cortex) thought to be involved in reading. However, additional activation was observed in several regions, including the left angular gyrus, for the semantically incongruent condition. The findings concurred with previously localized N400 dipoles using MEG with the same experimental paradigm.

title: Looking at Looking by Mind and Brain Ray Klein and the SOPOS collaboration Cognitive neuroscience is concerned with how the mental representations and processes that underly complex behavior are implemented by neural structures and processes in the brain. Our interdisciplinary team focussed on the control of gaze direction (overt visual orienting), in particular the control of fixation and saccadic eye movements, as a "model system" for exploring the interplay between volitional and reflexive control systems, the mechanisms of preparation, the influence of objects on actions. With a few examples, I will illustrate our interdisciplinary approach, which is characterized by a joint consideration of normal performance, the behavior of neurons during normal performance and the changes in performance that occur as a result of manipulations of (e.g. damage to) brain structures. Our understanding of how the brain controls looking is both reflected in and driven by our neural field model which is able to simulate oculomotor performance and the behavior of neurons in a wide range of tasks. An asymmetry in children's comprehension of verbal ironic criticisms and compliments.
An Asymmetry in Children's Comprehension of Verbal Ironic Criticisms and Compliments
abstract:
Extant research on children's comprehension of verbal irony has focused on critical forms of irony to the
exclusion of less typical complimentary forms. Two experiments examined children's ability to detect
and comprehend ironic criticisms and compliments presented in videotaped stories. In experiment 1,
children more frequently detected the non-literal nature of ironic criticisms than ironic compliments
(first-order attribution). However, children that successfully detected the non-literal nature of an ironic
statement were able to comprehend the intended meaning of both types of irony equally well (second-
order attribution). Experiment 2 employed the same procedure but also manipulated the degree to which
ironic statements echoed the previous statement in the story. A similar pattern of results was obtained,
although this manipulation reduced the asymmetry in detecting the non-literal nature between ironic
criticisms and compliments. The asymmetry across these two exemplars of irony suggests that a single
underlying cognitive development (e.g., second-order attribution) may be a necessary condition for the
detection of irony, but not sufficient.

title: Stripping A way a Layer of Perception: Binaural & Monaural Spatial Channels
abstract:
Two of the major perceptual dimensions of a sound are its frequency content and its spatial location.
While the structure of the perceptual channels mediating frequency processing are well understood, the
perceptual channels used for sound localization are not. We have used gap-detection methodologies to
investigate this. The size of gap-detection thresholds can indicate whether sounds on either side of the
silent period (gap) are activating the same or different perceptual channels. We varied speaker locations
of the leading and trailing gap markers around the head, and obtained gap thresholds. The results
supported a model of two broadly-tuned hemifield channels overlapping through the midline. The next
step is to examine the channel tuning under monaural conditions in the same listeners (repeating the
experiment with one ear plugged). This eliminates the binaural cues that contribute to sound
localization, viz., the interaural differences in the arrival time and intensity of a sound. That is, ear
plugging removes a layer of processing contributing to our perception. Results from this monaural study
will be presented.

authors_raw: Darcy A. Santor and Vivek Kusumakar
title: A Controlled Trial of Interpersonal Therapy and Sertraline in Adolescents with Depression
abstract:
Interpersonal Therapy was initially developed as a brief, focused treatment to alleviate symptoms of
depression and address current interpersonal problems experienced by individuals with depression.
