Although several studies have shown that IPT is effective in treating depression in adults, only one open
trial of IPT for the treatment of unipolar depression in adolescents has been conducted. However, there
are some concerns with this study, namely that treatment was provided by just one highly-trained
clinician and that the sample in which effects were observed was extremely homogeneous. To address
some of the concerns with the original open trial conducted, we conducted an open trial of IPT with a
sample of 24 depressed adolescents who were matched on age and gender to a sample of 24 depressed
adolescents receiving Sertraline. IPT was provided by 10 therapists, trained specifically in Interpersonal
Therapy, each of whom saw between 1 and 3 clients. Results showed young persons improved
substantially whether they received IPT or Sertraline; however, young persons receiving IPT improved
more. h'

title: Nicotine, Muscarine and Circadian Rhythms ,, , . },~._J '"'~· -->f'k;. )
abstract: There has been a long history of controvery about how the neurotransmitter acetylcholine affects circadian rhythms, and especially what role it plays in the synchronization (entrainment) of these rhythms by environmental lighting cycles. Some studies reported that cholinergic drugs act via nicotinic receptors to mimic the effects of light while others reported that nicotinic receptors were not relevant, but that muscarinic receptors mediated cholinergic effects on rhythms. In a combination of in vivo and in vitro neuro-physiological studies of cells in the suprachiasmatic nucleus, we have examined how each of these classes of cholinergic receptors interact with excitatory and inhibitory amino acid transmitters involved in circadian rhythm entrainment. Actions on nicotinic receptors appeared to be presynaptic and involved in the regulation of release of glutamate and GABA reaching SCN cells from other neurons, while actions via muscarinic receptors appeared to be postsynaptic; i.e., directly on SCN neurons.

title: Molecules of Synaptic Transmission
abstract:
Synaptic transmission is the process used by neurones to communicate with other neurones and skeletal
muscle. During synaptic transmission, a neurotransmitter released at a nerve ending diffuse to the
synaptic space to reach receptors at the target cell. There it binds to ligand-gated channels, the
neurotransmitter receptors that mediate fast-synaptic transmission .. During the last decade, research has
revealed the process of ion movement through these channels. Initially, computer models, which showed
the interactions between ions and the channel protein, guided the design of mutation. The study of
mutated channels elucidated the process of ion selectivity, the process by which a channel can
distinguish among ions. Pore size well as polarity of the filter determined ion conduction. Research on
acetylcholine, glutamate (NMDA as well as AMPA/ Kainate) receptors, however, has not yet uncovered
the mysteries of Ca conduction. Presently, I am interested in the study of natural mutation that occur in
some neurological diseases.

authors_raw: { Leticia Materi, Doug Rasmusson and Kazue Semba
title: Why You Get Sleepy When You Stay A wake Too Long: The Role of Adenosine
abstract:
The release of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) is associated with an increase in the excitability
of cortical neurons, as during wakefulness in general, and in attentive states in particular. However, as
we stay awake and vigilant for a long period of time, we get drowsy. What causes this? To obtain clues
to answer this question, we have investigated how the neuromodulator adenosine, which is released by
brain cells during increased metabolism, affects ACh release, using in vivo microdialysis in anesthetized
rats. We found that adenosine inhibits ACh release evoked by stimulation of brainstem reticular
formation. This inhibition was concentration-dependent, mimicked by adenosine Al, but not A2a,
receptor agonist, and blocked by caffeine, a non-selective adenosine receptor antagonist. These results
suggest that, during prolonged wakefulness, adenosine released as a result of increased neuronal activity
could diminish ACh release, inducing global inhibition of the cerebral cortex and a decrease in
vigilance.

authors_raw: & Steve Shaw Insects such as crickets bear specialized ears complete with ear drums on their front legs, and use them to locate distant singing mates. It is therefore behaviorally advantageous for these ears to be strongly directional, and the directional sensitivity of the cricket ear at around kHz is better than dB. Insects are rather small to achieve directionality by using sound- shadowing or time delays, and crickets do it by an interference mechanism, reinvented more recently by humans for directional microphones. Sound traveling outside the body to the front door of the tympanum interferes with sound traveling down enlarged tracheal air tubes inside the body to the back door of the tympanum. Internal transmission is effective even via a large trachea crossing from the opposite side of the body. Directionality is conferred because cancellation varies in effectiveness with position, as the bug rotates itself relative to the sound source. Specialized mechanisms usually don't just emerge de novo, but evolve from some related but more general precursor state present in an ancestor -- a preadaptation. Vibration detectors are present in the tibia of each leg below the knee, forming the subgenual organ, from which, in crickets in early development, the sensory neurons bud off that will later form the ear. Cockroaches, an ancient ancestor- model group, have been examined physiologically to see if some parts of the directional mechanism are recognizable in a more general insect leg, since the roach SGO also responds to sound, best around kHz. The results presented from semi-intact roaches will show that sound can indeed travel down the leg's tracheae to the SGO, that this can interfere with and completely cancel sound entering through the leg's surface, and that low frequencies can even spread across the midline from the opposite leg, within the tracheal system. Much of the groundwork for response directionality must have been laid down quite early on in insect legs, which crickets later took over and refined
title: In One Ear and Out the Other? Sound Transmission in a Generalized Bug Leg
Left Limb Movement Improves Visual Scanning in Hemispatial Neglect f::,,,"-{.{\,r· 9 Beverly Dt:i"rffittt(l,2), Gail Eskes(l,2), Ed Harrison(l,2), & Alison MacDonald(2), Dalhousie
abstract: Hemispatial neglect is a deficit in directing attention/movement toward the contralesional space. Robertson and North (1992;1993) reported in case studies that active left-limb movement in left hemispace can improve visual scanning in neglect, but that passive left-limb movement was not effective. We attempted to confirm and extend the findings regarding passive limb movement using functional electrical stimulation (FES) to produce passive movement. Subjects with neglect (n=6) detected visual targets under conditions of no movement and passive left-limb movement in left hemispace. Three non-paretic subjects also participated in an active movement condition. The passive movement group showed an improvement in target detection on the left (p<.05), while 3/3 subjects improved with active movement. Intentional motor programming does not appear required for activating perception-action circuits involved in attention. In addition, FES may have a role in the rehabilitation of hemispatial neglect.

authors_raw: \' Daniel A. Waschbusch, Eric Daleiden, Ronald S. Drabman
title: The Criterion Validity of Parent Ratings of their Child's Cognitive Abilities {\
abstract:
Examined the criterion validity of parent ratings of their child's cognitive abilities. Participants were
192 children referred to an outpatient mental health clinic for cognitive testing. Criterion measures
were children's scores on the Woodcock-Johnson Psycho-Educational Battery - Revised and the
Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children - Third Edition. Results supported the validity of parent
ratings of their child's fluid reasoning, visual-motor processing and verbal-auditory processing. Parent
ratings of comprehension-knowledge and memory did not perform well. The findings provide evidence
that parents may be accurate reporters of some of their child's specific cognitive abilities.

title: Nicotine: How Does it Affect Performance in a Memory Scanning Task? \ Michael E. Houlihan
abstract:
Nicotine absorbed from cigarette smoke improves performance on many cognitive tasks. Although
overall response times generally are faster after smoking/nicotine, few studies have tried to isolate the
effect of smoking/nicotine on the speech of the short-term memory (STM) scanning substage of
information processing. The present study investigates the effect of smoking/nicotine on STM scanning
using both reaction time (RT) and event-related potentials (ERPs). An STM scanning task was
performed before and after smoking each of two cigarettes. One cigarette had a 0.05-mg nicotine yield
and the other had a 1.I-mg yield (FTC method). Two, three or four consonants were displayed as a
memory set. After a brief interval, a single probe consonant was displayed. If the probe was in the
memory set (positive probe), a right button press was required and if the probe was not in the memory
set (negative probe), the left button was pressed. RT was faster after smoking the 1.1-mg cigarette but
not after smoking the 0.05-mg cigarette. However, memory-scanning speed, as estimated from the
increase in RT as a function of increasing set size, was not affected by nicotine. Smoking the higher-
yield cigarette reduced NI latency to the negative probes (at Cz) and also reduced N2 latency to both the
memory set and negative probes. These ERP latency effects were small compared to the effects of
nicotine on RT suggesting that nicotine shortens RT by affecting response-related processes. Nicotine
produced a sustained positive shift in ERP amplitude beginning around 100 ms post stimulus.

authors_raw: P David I. Shore, Lianne Stanford, Joseph W. Macinnes
title: Of Mice and Men: Species & Individual (Human) Differences in Wayfinding Using a Virtual Hebb-Williams Maze
Relations Between Personality Domains and Drinking Motives in Young Adults
abstract:
The purpose of the present study was to place drinking motives within the context of the Five-Factor
Model of personality. Specifically, we sought to determine whether certain personality domains of the
Revised NEO Personality Inventory (NEO-PI-R; Costa & McCrae, 1992) predict Enhancement, Coping,
Social, and/or Conformity drinking motives from the Revised Drinking Motives Questionnaire (DMQ-
R; Cooper, 1994). A sample of 256 university student drinkers (Mage= 21.3 years) completed the
NEO-PI-R and DMQ-R. In bivariate correlations, the two negative reinforcement motives (Coping and
Conformity) were positively correlated with Neuroticism and negatively correlated with Extraversion,
whereas the two positive reinforcement motives (Enhancement and Social) were positively correlated
with Extraversion and negatively correlated with Conscientiousness. Multiple regression analyses
revealed that personality domain scores predicted two of the four drinking motives (i.e., the internal
drinking motives of Coping and Enhancement), after controlling for the influences of alternative
drinking motives. Enhancement Motives were predicted by high Extraversion and low
Conscientiousness, and Coping Motives by high Neuroticism. These results provide further validation
of Cox and Klinger's (1988; 1990) 2 x 2 (valence [positive vs. negative reinforcement] x source [internal
vs. external]) model of drinking motivations, and confirm previous speculations that drinking motives
are distinguishable on the basis of higher-order personality domains. Understanding the relations
between personality and drinking motives may prove useful in identifying young drinkers whose
drinking motivations may portend the development of heavy and/or problem drinking.

authors_raw: \ Steve Porter, Angie Birt, & Mary-Anne Campbell
title: Profiles of Psychopathy in Incarcerated Sexual Offenders
abstract:
We investigated whether psychopathy would contribute to an understanding of the nature and
heterogeneity of sexual violence. Using the Psychopathy Checklist-Revised (PCL-R; Hare, 1991), level
of psychopathy, callousness (Factor 1), and antisocial patterns (Factor 2) were examined in a diverse
sample (N = 329) of incarcerated adult male sex and non-sex offenders. The offenders were categorized
according to their crimes (extra-familial molesters, intra-familial (incest) molesters, mixed offense
molesters, rapists, mixed rapist/ molesters and non-sex offenders). Mixed rapist/ molesters, rapists, and
non-sex offenders scored significantly higher on the PCL-R than molesters, although all sex offender
groups showed elevated Factor 1 scores. Most (64%) mixed rapist/ molesters were psychopathic,
indicative of a "sexual psychopath" whose thrill-seeking is focussed on diverse sexual victims. There
were different interpersonal and social deviance profiles among the groups and an interaction between
factor scores and offense type. These profiles have implications for recidivism, treatment, and, more
generally, a comprehensive theory of sexual violence.

authors_raw: Ryan C.N. D'Arcy and Jeannette McGlone
title: Profound Amnesia Does Not Impair Performance on 36-item Digit Memory Test - A Test of Malingered Memory
abstract:
Global amnesia spares immediate span of attention while compromising retrieval after a delay filled
with distractions. Accordingly, we hypothesized that the Digit Memory Test (DMT) would classify
persons with severe, disabling amnesia as "non-malingerers" better than malingering tests utilizing
interference or recall paradigms. Fourteen cases with profound amnesia, including 2 persons with global
amnesia, obtained scores of 100% correct on the 36-item DMT. However, the Rey 15-item Test of
malingering and a multiple choice delayed recognition memory test yielded false positive outcomes.
We conclude that the DMT has excellent specificity with respect to correctly identifying persons with
severe memory disorders due to neurological lesions by correctly classifying all as "non-malingerers".

authors_raw: G. Gingras, D.E. Mitchell, R.F. Hess
title: The Spatial Localization Deficit in Visually Deprived Kittens: Comparison with Human Amblyopes
abstract:
Purpose In order to provide data that could assist resolution of a debate over the nature of the
underlying neural deficit in amblyopia, we measured the deficits in spatial localization of visually
deprived kittens by use of the same tests as those employed for the assessment of human amblyopes.
Methods. The tests were conducted on one normal and 6 selectively deprived kittens. Four of the kittens
were monocularly deprived by eyelid suture while on two others esotropia was induced by section of
the lateral rectus muscle of one eye when either 17 or 29 days old. Of the 4 monocularly deprived
kittens, 2 were deprived from near birth to 60 (C773) and 82 (C772) days of age while the others were
deprived for either 6 (C791) or 30 (C775) days beginning at respectively, 35 and 38 days. When the
animals were 5 months of age, measurements were made of the accuracy with which they could detect a
misalignment between three Guassian blobs of high contrast. The outer two blobs were in vertical
alignment and measurements were made, by use of a jumping stand and a method of descending limits,
of the minimum detectable horizontal misalignment of the middle blob for both the deprived (or
deviating) and nondeprived eyes. Measurements of alignment accuracy were made at different spatial
scales (where the stimuli were spatial scaled versions of one another) and as a function of contrast.
Results. For both the normal animal and the nondeprived eyes of each of the visual deprived kittens,
there was a comparable proportional relationship between alignment accuracy and Guassian blob size to
that observed in humans. Moreover, there was a similar weak dependency on stimulus contrast. As
with amblyopic humans, the deficits in alignment accuracy were scaled in proportion to blob size but
were in general larger than those reported in human amblyopes. The deficits were greatest in the
strabismic cats where the alignment accuracy of the deviating eye was 30 times worse than that of the
other eye. The deficits in the deprived eye of the monocularly deprived animals were not related in any
simple way with either the length of the deprivation or the deficits in grating acuity. Conclusions. The
similarity of the pattern of results to that observed in human amblyopes suggests that by suitable
titration of the timing of deprivation it will be possible to produce deficits similar to those exhibited in
human deprivation and strabismic amblyopia thereby allowing for converging information on the nature
of the underlying neural deficits.

title: Inhibitory Control in Children With Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder
abstract:
In an effort to fully explore the validity and nature of the recently proposed inhibitory control deficit
theory of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD; cf Barkley, 1998), we tested children with and
without ADHD on five different tests of inhibitory control. The Colour-Word Stroop test assessed
negative priming and inhibition of distracting information. A Simon/Erikson task tested filtering ability
and inhibition at the response selection stage. A delayed eye movement task examined the ability to
suppress reflexive and memory-guided saccades. Inhibition of return was measured in a covert visual
orienting task. A stop-signal task measured the ability to inhibit a visually-triggered response. We will
report on the degree to which children with ADHD differ from controls on the measures of inhibition
derived from these tasks, as well as on the degree to which these inhibitory mechanisms tend to covary.

authors_raw: Ryan C.N. D'Arcy and John F. Connolly
title: Increased Processing Demands During Target Error Detection on the Computerized Token Test
abstract:
Recently, we have adapted the Token Test (De Renzi and Vignolo, 1962, Brain, 665-678) for computer
presentation with simultaneous event-related brain potential (ERP) recordings. The Token Test is a
standardized neuropsychological measure of auditory comprehension. The objective of this study was to
investigate whether variations in stimulus complexity influenced the cognitive processes involved in
spoken word identification. The computer Token Test (CTI) was designed as a target detection task
that required individuals to understand spoken sentences in order to detect an incorrect target word. For
each trial on the CTI, participants were instructed to study a short animation sequence presented on a
computer monitor. The animation sequence consisted of a pointer that touched either one or two tokens.
Immediately after the animation, participants heard a single sentence description that was either correct
or incorrect. ERPs were recorded to the onset of incorrect target words within the spoken sentences. In
the Similar condition, the initial phonemes of the incorrect words were similar to the initial phonemes of
the correct words (e.g., circle/square). In the Different condition, the incorrect target words had
different initial phonemes and thus could be identified as incorrect at word onset (e.g., green/red). The
ERP responses to words in the Similar condition elicited a negativity in the 250ms to 350ms region,
which was not observable in the ERP responses to incorrect words in the Different condition. The
negativity was hypothesized to reflect increased processing demands during error detection when the
initial phonemes of the target words were similar.

authors_raw: Susan E. Buffett-Jerrott, Sherry H. Stewart, Margo Watt and Kerry L. Jang
title: The Relationship Between Anxiety Sensitivity Scores of Parents and Their Adult Children
abstract:
Calamari et al. found a strong positive relationship between the anxiety sensitivity scores of parents on
the ASI, and their young children on the CASI. In a twin study, Stein et al. found evidence for
heritability on the social and physical subscales of the ASI but not on the psychological subscale. The
present study examined ASI scores of 160 university students and their parents. Results indicate that
total ASI scores of parents and their adult children are marginally related. Consistent with the findings
of Stein, there are significant positive relationships for the physical and social subscales, but not the
psychological subscale. Overall, the results are consistent with Calamari's findings that ASI scores of
parents and their children are correlated. However, the magnitude of the relationship is much smaller in
the present study. This is likely due to the many intervening events besides parental influences that may
affect the development of anxiety sensitivity in individuals as they get older.

authors_raw: Anne Israeli and Gail Eskes The current study was conducted to assess whether cognitive function and activities of daily living, as measured using the Neurobehavioral Cognitive Status Exam and Barthel Index, would predict short-term outcome following stroke. Forty-two patients with first stroke completed the NCSE within two weeks of admission to an acute stroke service. Discharge BI scores were calculated 2-4 days prior to discharge. Three months post-stroke outcome was measured with the BI, the Reintegration to Normal Living Scale and the Medical Outcomes SF-36, obtained via telephone interview. A series of stepwise regressions revealed that discharge BI scores and the Orientation subscale of the NCSE predicted follow-up BI and RNL scores, but did not predict MOS-SF-36 scores. During the acute phase following stroke, basic cognitive processes such as orientation and activities of daily living may provide relevant information regarding short-term outcome
title: Neuropsychological Predictors of Quality of Life in Stroke Survivors
Left Temporal Cortex Activation Reflects Distinct Time Courses of Word and Context Comprehension
abstract:
The time course and cortical basis of reading comprehension were tracked using
magnetoencephalography CMEG). The cortical structures implicated most consistently with
comprehension were located in the immediate vicinity of the left auditory cortex, where final words
totally inappropriate to the overall sentence context evoked enduring activation between 250 ms and 600
ms after word onset. Contextually appropriate but unexpected words produced weaker activation, which
terminated earlier. We propose that the point in time (350 ms after word onset) where the response to
appropriate but unexpected endings started to diverge from those to contextually inappropriate endings
reflects the boundary between understanding a single word and the meaning of a whole sentence.

authors_raw: John F. Connolly, Ryan C.N. D'Arcy, Alma M. Major, Sheri L. Allen, Gail A. Eskes, & Stephen J. Phillips
title: Neuropsychological Assessment of Receptive Vocabulary and Comprehension With Event-related Brain Potentials
abstract:
The clinical application of evoked potentials has proven invaluable for assessing sensory functions.
Recently, we have adapted standardized neuropsychological tests for computer presentation with
simultaneous event-related brain potential (ERP) recordings for assessing cognitive functions. The
Vocabulary and Similarities sub-sections of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale-Revised as a
Neurological Instrument (W AIS-R-NI) were used to assess receptive single word vocabulary and verbal
concept formation, respectively. The Written Comprehension section of the Psycholinguistic
Assessments of Language Processing in Aphasia (PALPA) was used to assess receptive sentence
comprehension. Test items from all three paradigms consisted of a study phase and a test phase. During
the study phase, the test question and response alternatives were presented on a computer screen.
Participants were requested to study the question, select an answer, and remember the answer. During
the test phase, the response alternatives were presented individually and ERPs were recorded.
Participants were requested to identify the correct and incorrect response alternatives. Items which the
individual indicated as 'correct' were differentiated from alternative items by the presence of a P300
response. The P300 response was hypothesized to represent recognition memory and contextual
updating processes following the presentation of a meaningful stimulus. Receptive language
dysfunction is frequently noted in aphasic individuals suffering from stroke or traumatic brain injury.
However, due to communicative limitations, these patients cannot be readily assessed with traditional
methods. In these and similar instances, it is possible to assess language and other cognitive functions
with neural responses that are relatively independent of behaviour.

authors_raw: Robert L.H. Clements and Richard E. Brown
title: r Differences)n,Children's C~~ceptual Strategies When•f;J:iinkipg4\bo11t~•Century of Rats in Mazes
abstract: Between 1898 and 1900, Willard S. Small studied development and general behaviour in rats at Clark University. In order to quantify their learning ability, he tested them in a rat-sized model of the Hampton Court Maze. With this study, Small (1901, The American Journal of Psychology, 12:207-239) was the first to combine laboratory investigation using rats and the concept of an intellectually puzzling maze. Over the last one hundred years, a wide variety of mazes have been used to study basic questions regarding the sensory and neural basis of learning, motivation and perception. A multitude of species have been tested in mazes, from worms to humans. Innumerable mazes have been developed and used widely in psychological research, including the T-maze, Lashley's type 3 maze, Tolman's sunburst maze, the Hebb-Williams maze, Barnes Maze and the 8 arm radial maze. Mazes such as the Porteus maze, developed in 1917, and newer computerized virtual mazes, are still used as standardized tests for human mental abilities. The current literature lacks a comprehensive overview of the use of mazes in scientific research. Our paper provides a history of this fascinating apparatus and its use. An in-depth review of the literature found over 11,000 papers on maze learning in the last 100 years and we are going to tell you about every last one of them, with particular emphasis on some of the currently popular mazes used in psychology (Morris water maze, 8-arm radial maze, elevated plus maze). The discussion summarizes the advantages and disadvantages of using mazes in psychological and medical research.

authors_raw: Catherine A. Forestell,1 Heather M. Schellinck,1 Richard E. Brown,1 Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Olfactory Conditioning and Differential Immediate Early Gene Expression in Mice
abstract:
The present experiment investigated whether adult mice acquired conditioned responses to an odour
paired with a caloric reinforcer and which parts of the brain are involved in the formation of this
association by looking for differences in c-fos-like-immtinoreactivity. Food restricted mice were
exposed to a differential conditioning procedure in which experimental animals received an odour (CS+)
paired with sucrose pellets in a dish covered with bedding and another odour (CS-) presented alone in a
dish with bedding. Control animals were either exposed to the odours separately in a dish containing
bedding and no reinforcement, or to no odour. After 32 conditioning trials, animals were placed in a
chamber divided into three equal sections with CS+ and CS- on each end of the chamber. Experimental
animals spent significantly more time digging in the dish containing CS+ than CS- relative to control
animals. On the next day, all animals received exposure to CS+, CS-, or no odour for 10 min, 2 hours
prior to perfusion. Fos-like-immunoreactivity was assessed in brain structures such as the glomerular
layer of the olfactory bulbs, piriform cortex, the hippocampus, and the periventricular nucleus of the
thalamus.

authors_raw: Melanie McFadyen, Norman Carrey, and Richard Brown
title: Effects of Methylphenidate Hydrochloride (Ritalin) Injections During Development on Locomotion, Exploration, Fear, and Cognitive Processes in Mice
abstract:
Although the use of the stimulant methylphenidate (Ritalin) is increasing among children, little is known
about the potentially deleterious effects it may have on their cognitive and behavioural development.
Our study uses a mouse model to examine the developmental effects of methylphenidate (MPH) (daily
40 mg/kg sc vs. saline injections) in male CD-1 mice, from postnatal days 26-32. The mice were tested
from postnatal days 33-37 for locomotion and exploration in the open field (day 1), anxiety in the
elevated plus maze (day 1), and learning the Morris water maze (days 2-5), with a visible platform for
three days and a hidden platform on day 4. Our results indicate that MPH-treated mice were more active
in the open field, entering more centre squares than the saline controls; MPH-treated mice spent more
time in the open arms and exhibited more head dips in the elevated plus maze than controls, and that,
while there was no significant difference between MPH and saline-treated mice in the time taken to find
the visible platform in the Morris water maze, the MPH-treated mice showed a tendency to take longer
to find the hidden platform. The results indicate that treatment with methylphenidate has significant
effects on later anxiety, reducing fear and increasing locomotion, but less of an effect on learning in a
swimming task. However, since the methylphenidate-treated mice were hyperactive, their learning may
be affected more in tests of inhibition.

authors_raw: Alan B. MacDonald and Sherry S. Stewart
title: A Factor Analytic Investigation of the Alcohol Expectancy Questionnaire -3rd Version
abstract:
The Alcohol Expectancy Questionnaire - 3rd version (AEQ-III; Goldman, Greenbaum & Darkes, 1997)
is a self-report instrument consisting of 68 items designed to assess positive expectancies or beliefs
about the outcomes of drinking alcohol. The AEQ-III is a revised version of the original, 90-item AEQ,
and has not yet been evaluated extensively from a psychometric perspective. The purpose of the present
study was to explore the factor structure of the AEQ-III in an undergraduate university sample and
attempt to replicate the factor structure found by Goldman et al. (1997). Eight-hundred and nine
undergraduates students were administered the AEQ-III. Exploratory principal components analysis
with oblique (Oblimin) rotation identified that six factors best explain the underlying dimensions of this
measure in this population. These factors include: 1) Social Assertiveness (SA); 2) Global Positive
Changes (GP); 3) Social & Physical Pleasure (SP); 4) Sexual Enhancement (SE); 5) Arousal/Aggression
(AA); and 6) Relaxation (R). The factor structure proposed by Goldman et al. (1997) was replicated.
Implications for the use of this measure in determining potential alcohol expectancies and patterns of
drinking in young adults are discussed.

authors_raw: Catherine A. Forestell, Tara M. Humphrey and Sherry H. Stewart
title: Is beauty in the eye of the beholder? Restraint, body shape and weight in attractiveness ratings of self and others.
abstract:
The present study tested the degree to which body-shape characteristics of line drawings influence
restrained and nonrestrained eaters' ratings of attractiveness for female figures. Female line drawings
(Tassinary & Hensen, 1998, Psychological Science: 9, 150-155), which varied systematically in weight
and waist and hip circumference, were used as stimuli. Thirty-two female undergraduate Psychology
students (15 nonrestrained and 17 restrained, according to the Restrained Eating Scale; Herman &
Polivy, 1980, Obesity. pp 208-225), were asked to rate these stimuli in attractiveness, identify the ideal
body figure, and the figure which best represented their own body type. Restrained eaters did not differ
from nonrestrained eaters in their ratings of attractiveness nor in their perception of the ideal female
figure. However, after controlling for body weight and shape, it was found that restrained eaters did
perceive themselves as larger than nonrestrained eaters. Thus it appears that the motivation to diet in
restrained eaters may stem from an overestimation of their perceived body size.

authors_raw: Sharon Clark, Joanne Gusella and Erica van Roosmalen
title: Form or Function? A body-image assessment tool
abstract:
A negative body image is one of the primary features of an eating disorder and often develops from an
exclusive focus on body appearance, or the form of one's body, rather than considering the functional
aspects of how the body works. We developed and conducted testing of a new assessment measure of
body image. This measure compares two dimensions of body image perceptions: form and function. It
was hypothesized that if the functional activities of the body are emphasized, and contrasted to what is
liked about the body, the discrepancy between form and function would become apparent. This may be
an important component in changing cognitive distortions involved in body image problems while
beginning to create a sense of appreciation for what one's body does rather than what it looks like.
Results of pilot testing with the measure will be presented.

authors_raw: Anna W. Lee & Richard E. Brown
title: Effects of lesions of the medial preoptic area on parental behaviour in male and female California mice, Peromyscus californicus
abstract:
Peromyscus californicus are monogamous and bi parental mice. The male shows all aspects of parental
care, except lactation, as soon as the pups are born. Thus, they are an ideal species in which to compare
the neuroanatomical basis of maternal and paternal behaviour. The present study investigated the effects
of lesions in the medial preoptic area on the expression of parental behaviour in male and female
California mice. Electrolytic or sham lesions were performed two days postpartum on a male or female
from each male-female pair. Mice were tested for parental responsiveness for 10 days, beginning one
day post surgery. Preliminary results suggest that medial preoptic lesions disrupt parental behaviour in
both male and female mice.

title: Odour-induced egr-1 expression in the rat olfactory bulb
abstract:
The expression of immediate early genes (IEGs) have allowed for the mapping of neuronal activation.
Both c-fos and egr-1 have been widely used to this end. While expression of c-fos seems to be induced
as a function of neural activation, there is some evidence that egr-1 expression is induced only by novel
stimuli. Songbirds in captivity show an increase in egr-1 expression when presented with a novel
conspecific song. Induction of egr-1 decreases with repeated presentations of the same song but can be
re-elicited with the presentation of a novel conspecific song.
In this pilot study the effects of a novel odour on the expression of egr-1 in the rat olfactory bulb
are analyzed. Animals were exposed to a novel odour (1 % propionic acid) for an hour and a half and
then sacrificed. Controls were sacrificed without any novel odour stimulation. Immunoreactiviy against
egr-1 in the main olfactory bulbs of each group is compared.

title: Adult egocentrism: Conceptualization and a new measure
abstract:
Egocentrism is the tendency to construe events and persons according to private knowledge, without
appreciating the privileged character of such construal. Although pervasive in young children, it is
proposed that habitual egocentrism in adults reflects deficient development and awareness of the self-
system. Accordingly, it is hypothesized that dispositional egocentrism in adults should be associated
with inadequate self-knowledge, low self-regard, and heightened self-preoccupation, along with a
reduced capacity for understanding and empathizing with others. Two studies of college students are
presented in support of this hypothesis. In Study 1, a self-report measure of the disposition to respond
egocentrically, the Egocentric Response Scale (ERS) is introduced. Greater egocentrism on the ERS
was associated with alexithymia, lower self-esteem, rumination about the self, public self-consciousness,
and immature empathy. Study 2 showed that the ERS correlated positively with a measure of attitudinal
egocentrism, and that on observer versions of both scales observers' ratings exhibited consensus about
targets' egocentrism, and correlated positively with self-ratings of egocentrism. Both scales correlated
negatively with measures of self-knowledge, mature empathy, and decoding others' emotions from
nonverbal signals.

title: Eliminating the costs of task set reconfiguration
abstract:
Given insufficient time to fully prepare for a switch in task, a deterioration in performance on the first
trial of a new task would be expected. The interest of researchers has been captured by the residual
switch costs that surprisingly remain despite sufficient time to prepare. We used a very simple task to
investigate the costs to reaction time and accuracy associated with changing between two different
instructional sets every eight trials. Subjects responded to left and right visual targets by making either
spatially compatible or incompatible button press responses. Subjects were cued which of these
responses to make by the color of a border appearing on the perimeter of the display. The large costs to
reaction time found at a 200 ms border-target SOA were completely eliminated at longer intervals
between the border and the stimulus, but costs to accuracy remained. An identical task using saccades
towards or away from the target in the place of button presses resulted in the elimination of costs to both
accuracy and reaction time. This finding does not support the prevailing hypothesis that a portion of the
task set reconfiguration process must await the arrival of the imperative stimulus to be completed. The
differences between the saccadic and manual task switching results are discussed with respect to the
compatibility of these otherwise identical tasks.

title: Switch costs without task switching?
abstract:
A change in task initially results in detriments to perlormance. These "switch costs" have been
commonly attributed to the re-configuration of task set by way of executive control processes.
However, it is not known whether a switch in task, per se, is responsible for switch costs. In this
investigation, task instruction did not change and accordingly no switch costs should be observed if they
are only due to a re-configuration of task set. Responses were based entirely on the non-spatial identity
of targets, and spatial location was completely irrelevant to the correct perlormance of the task. Series
of spatially corresponding and non-corresponding stimulus-response ensembles alternated within an
experiment and the task set remained stable over the duration of the experiment. Although there were
no changes in task, we observed robust switch costs at two relatively long response-stimulus intervals.
The results call into question the general idea that switch costs must be the result of a re-configuration of
task set.

title: Using rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) to explore the temporal dynamics of reflexive attention shifts.
abstract:
The immediate effect of a cue in the periphery (e.g., a luminance increment) is the facilitation of
processing in the vicinity of the cue. Although it is generally agreed that this facilitative effect reflects
the consequence of an exogenously generated shift of attention to the cued location, some investigators
have claimed that the facilitation reflects a response bias rather than a genuine improvement. When the
cue in uninformative and there is, therefore, no reason to keep attention at the cued location, the
facilitation is followed by a long lasting inhibitory aftereffect at the cued location which has been called
inhibition of return (IOR). There is considerable interest in determining whether IO R's primary effect is
to delay perceptual processing, responding, or shifting of attention. The RSVP paradigm, originally
developed in the 1970's and recently popularized in studies of the "attentional blink," is ideally suited to
contribute to this debate because of its emphasis on identification accuracy (minimal demands are placed
on the motor system) and its temporal precision. The temporal dynamics of the facilitation and
inhibition that follow an uninformative peripheral cue, as obtained from the dual-RSVP-stream
peripheral cuing paradigm that we have developed for this purpose, will be illustrated.

title: Made you look - obligatory processing of faces
abstract:
Considerable converging evidence from a wide range of fields (neurophysiology, neuropsychology,
cognitive and developmental psychology) suggests that faces represent a special class of visual patterns
handled by a system separate from other visual processing systems. The present study attempts to
extend research on face perception by examining whether faces presented in the visual field capture
attention in a stimulus-driven fashion. Black and white high contrast face and non-face (inverted or
scrambled faces) images were presented in a dual stream Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP)
paradigm. The streams consisted of randomly selected letters with a single target digit placed pseudo-
randomly in one of the streams. Overall, when the digit was presented around the same time as the face
(or shortly thereafter) its identification was better when the face was adjacent to the stream with the digit
than when the face was near the opposite stream. The opposite pattern emerged as the interval between
the appearance of the face and the digit increased. This pattern corresponds roughly with the
prototypical pattern of Inhibition of Return and suggests that faces may capture attention. However,
significant laterality effects were observed suggesting that the role of attention in perceiving faces may
be differential across visual hemifields. These laterality effects are discussed in terms of recent
neuroimaging and neuropsychological data suggesting that the perception of faces is highly lateralized.

authors_raw: Mike Woodworth & Steve Porter, Angela R. Birt
title: Truth, Lies, and Videotape: An Investigation of the Ability of Federal Parole Officers and University Students to Detect Deception
abstract:
The ability of a group of Canadian federal parole officers to detect deception was investigated over the
course of two days of lie detection training. To isolate factors relating to detection accuracy, three
groups of undergraduate participants made judgments on the same 24 videotapes: (1) a feedback group
which received feedback on accuracy following each judgment; (2) a feedback+ cue information group
which was given feedback and information on empirically-based cues to deception; and (3) a control
group which did not receive feedback or cue information. Results indicated that at baseline all groups
performed at or below chance levels. However, overall, all experimental groups (including the parole
officers) became significantly better at detecting deception than the control group. The results indicate
that detecting deceit is difficult but training and feedback can enhance detection skills.
Tl 1. Individual differences in the development of face recognition: Support for a maturational change
at age eight.
Paddy McMullen, Phil Dunham and Fran Dunham
Carey & Diamond (1977) suggested that before the age of 8, children recognize a face by its individual
features. After this age, a whole-face (holistic) strategy has developed. Boys and girls aged 7 and 11
performed tests of Mooney faces (a test of holistic processing), Embedded figures (a test of feature-
based processing) and upright and inverted face recognition. Children aged 7, successfully recognized
upright faces but were at chance at the recognition of inverted faces. More importantly, upright face
recognition correlated with Embedded figures performance (r= 0.56, p<0.01) and not with Mooney face
performance. Children aged 11, recognized upright and inverted faces above change levels. Notably,
their results showed the opposite pattern to those of the younger children. Upright face recognition now
correlated with Mooney face performance (r= 0.45, p<0.05) and not with Embedded figures
performance. The maturational hypothesis of face recognition was tested from an individual differences
approach and supported.

title: The Effects of a Novel Psychoactive Drug Flibanserin on Ultrasonic Vocalizations in Rat Pups
abstract:
This study compared the effects of flibanserin, a novel 5-HTlA agonist/5-HT2A antagonist; diazepam, a
traditional anxiolytic; and imipramine, a traditional antidepressant, on separation-induced ultrasonic
vocalizations (USVs), locomotor behavior, negative geotaxis and body temperature of 7-8-day-old rat
pups. Flibanserin (5, 10, 25 and 50 mg kg -1) reduced USVs but had no effects on locomotor behavior or
geotaxic response. Low doses of flibanserin (0.5, 1, 2 and 4 mg kg -1) had no effect on any behavior.
Diazepam (0.25, 0.5, 1 and 2 mg kg -1) not only reduced the USVs but also increased rolling and
increased the latency of the geotaxic response. lmipramine (10, 15, 20 and 30 mg kg-1) reduced USVs,
increased total locomotor activity and rolling but had no effect on negative geotaxis. None of the drugs
altered body temperature. Our data showed that flibanserin is as effective in reducing the USVs as
diazepam and imipramine but has a lower incidence of motor impairment side effects. This suggests that
flibanserin might be effective for the treatment of mood disturbances such as anxiety.

authors_raw: Peggy Ruyak, Ben Rusak and Debbie Morrison
title: Recovery Sleep Patterns Following Treatment of Patients with Obstructive Sleep Apnea
abstract:
The primary purpose of this investigation was to study the effects of continuous positive airway pressure
(CPAP) on the patterns of REM sleep and Slow Wave Sleep (SWS) in patients with obstructive sleep
apnea (OSA). Analyses of baseline and CPAP treatment data from 38 patients with OSA included sleep
stage, CPAP pressure, and number of arousals during the treatment night. The study involved the re-
analysis of existing data from patients treated with CPAP on a second night following an initial baseline
night of polysomnographic recording. Data were scored to allow for examination of the detailed time
course of occurrence of NREM and REM recovery sleep during the treatment night. The results of the
experiment will further describe the phenomenon of REM and SWS rebound during the first night of
CP AP treatment.

authors_raw: Jeannette McGlone, Gregory Hirsch, Jean-Francois Legare, and Karen Buth
title: Neurobehavioural outcomes in men and women undergoing coronary artery bypass surgery with or without cardiopulmonary bypass
abstract:
Replacing the clogged arteries feeding the heart is a well-established operation for patients with
ischemic heart disease. It is called coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). The traditional methods of
placing the patient on a heart-lung machine during the operation will be compared with a newer
technique of operating on the beating heart. This talk reviews the literature and outlines a randomized,
prospective, clinical trial comparing the neuropsychological and quality of life outcomes of patients
undergoing these two techniques. The aim of this study is to evaluate longer term (six month)
psychosocial outcomes. For the first time, the goal is to investigate the outcomes of women compared
to men. Students looking for comprehensive projects over the next 3 years, persons interested in
critiquing the proposal, and the mildly curious are most welcome.
Tl 5. Effects of passive limb movement on the Visual Scanning abilities of Neglect subjects in Two
Coordinate Spaces
Beverly Butler.2, Gail Eskes 1.2,E. Harrison 1.2,& Alison MacDonald 2 , Dalhousie University 1 and Queen
Elizabeth II Health Sciences Centre2
Hemispatial neglect is a deficit in directing attention/movement toward the contralesional space.
Robertson (1995) reported that passive left limb movement did not improve visual scanning in a single
case of neglect, and suggested that active limb movement was required. Contrary to this finding, Ladavas
et al. (1997) and our own pilot study, showed that passive movement may improve visual scanning in
neglect patients in peripersonal space. The present study attempted to replicate and extend these passive
movement effects. Neglect subjects (n=6), right-hemisphere damaged non-neglect controls (n=6) and non-
brain-damaged controls (n=5) detected visual targets in peripersonal and extrapersonal space under
conditions of no movement and passive left limb movement (produced by functional electrical stimulation)
in left hemispace. We were unable to replicate the passive movement effects on visual scanning in this
group of neglect patients in peripersonal or extrapersonal space. However, the neglect group showed a
gradient of decreasing target detection from right to left, which was not evident in either of the control

title: The Influences of Child Age and Parent Informant on the Associations Between Parenting
abstract:
This project investigated the influences of child age and parent informant (mother versus father report)
on the associations between parenting practices and conduct problem behaviour in a community sample
of children aged 5 to 12. Valid sample size for the computed regressions varied from 335 to 339.
Parenting practices were assessed using a modified version of the Alabama Parenting Questionnaire
(APQ; Frick, 1991). Conduct problems were defined by DSM-IV criteria for Oppositional Defiant
Disorder (ODD) and Conduct Disorder (CD), and assessed through the use of the Assessment of
Disruptive Symptoms, DSM-IV Version (ADS-IV; Pelham, Gnagy, Greenslade, & Milich, 1992). Both
mothers and fathers acted as informants for the two questionnaires. Preliminary analyses suggest that
the parenting practices of maternal involvement, poor monitoring and supervision of children's
activities, as well as inconsistent discipline, are significantly associated with number of CD and number
of ODD symptoms in children. The magnitude of these associations varied according to whether it was
the mothers or the fathers who reported the practices. In contrast with previous research in a clinical
sample, results did not show a significant child age trend in the associations. This project has significant
implications for the parent training component of the psychosocial interventions that are currently used
for CD and ODD.
Tl 7. Time Course of Linguistic Processing as indexed by Event-Related Brain Potentials (ERPs)
Randy Lynn Newman and John F. Connolly
While most theories of visual word recognition agree that access to meaning is meditated by phonology,
some theories contend that words can access their meaning directly, without phonological mediation.
ERPs, unlike behavioral measures of word recognition, provide an on-line measure of cognitive
processing without requiring overt behavioral responses. Indeed, a number of ERP components have
been found to be sensitive to orthographic, phonological, and semantic information. The goals of the
present study were to disentangle orthographic and semantic processing, and to define phonology's role
on route to accessing semantic information. ERPs were recorded to the terminal words of visually
presented sentences. Phonological/ orthographic and/or semantic characteristics of terminal words were
manipulated in 4 conditions in which: (1) the terminal word was the highest cloze probability ending
(e.g. Larry writes with his left hand.) (congruent); (2) the terminal word was orthographically
inappropriate, but was phonologically identical to the highest cloze probability ending (e.g. The game
was called off when it started to rayne. [rain]) (Pseudofoil); (3) the terminal word was orthographically
appropriate, but was semantically incongruent to the sentence context (e.g. The pizza was too hot to
sing.[eat]) (incongruent); or (4) the terminal word was orthographically inappropriate and was
phonologically distinct from the anticipated highest cloze probability ending (e.g. The teacher wrote the
problem on the heet. [board]) (incongruent-nonword). A negative peak was observed in the 250-300
msec range (the N270) in three conditions in which the orthography was unexpected. That is, even if the
orthography was corrent, as in the congruent condition, it was orthographically distinct from the highest
cloze probability ending for that sentence. A second, left tempro-parietally distributed negative peak in
the 350-450 range (the N400), was observed in both congruent conditions. Interestingly, the N400 was
absent in the WR condition, suggesting that phonology plays an important role in mediating access to
semantic information. Overall, these findings demonstrate the ability of ERPs to measure subtle aspects
of linguistic processing.

authors_raw: Ryan C.N. D'Arcy!, John F. Connollyl,2, Frank P. MacMaster, Robert Vandorpe, Larry Gates, Sultan Darvesh
title: Transcallosal While Matter Activation Revealed by Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging During Reading
abstract:
Functional :MRI has been used to demonstrate gray matter activation associated with sensory and
cognitive functions. We provide the first evidence of f:MRI activation in ~'.h_i!e_~aJJer(1.5T). This
activation was observed in comrrtissural fiber tracts passing through the splenium of the corpus callosum
during sentence reading. The time course data from white matter voxels revealed that, like gray matter
activation, the responses were time-locked to the stimulus presentation. However, as expected, the white
matter peak amplitudes were markedly attenuated relative to the responses in the visual cortex.
Reliability of the white matter activation was established by replicating fully the effect in a participant
following a six-month interval. The active white matter regions localized the comrrtissural fibers that
connect functionally specialized visual word form areas. These findings provide the first noninvasive
identification of the neural architecture involved in sentence reading.

title: An investigation of the phonological processing in Chinese language with ERPs
abstract:
Two unique features of Chinese language draw much attention and interest in psycholinguistics. The
first is that Chinese written language is pictographic. There is an opaque relationship between the
pronunciation and physical appearance of a Chinese character. For example, homophones have the exact
same pronunciation but completely different script feature and meaning. This opacity of the script-to-
sound relationship has led to a widespread assumption that readers of Chinese can arrive at the mental
lexicon by identifying a whole character visually, without any involvement with phonological coding.
The issue of whether there is phonological code involved in Chinese reading was investigated with the
recording of ERPs. The other specific feature of Chinese language is that it is a tone language. Most
Chinese characters consist of monosyllables with an affiliated lexical tone and the tonality of a
monosyllable is lexically meaningful. Though the perception of tone plays a major role in Chinese
speech comprehension, little is known about its underlying electrophysiological basis. What's more, in
behavioral studies, there is a major issue of whether tone perception is of importance in Chinese reading
comprehension since there are more errors in tone discrimination tasks during Chinese reading. My
future experiments will mainly focus on the above issues with the recording of ERPs, the measurement
of brain electrical activity that is time-locked to the underlying linguistic processing.

authors_raw: Frank P MacMaster, Shakeela Khan, Vivek Kusumakar
title: Magnetic Resonance in Psychiatry
abstract:
In reference to the invention of the stethoscope, P.A. Piorry remarked "If this method had only a quarter
of the utility attributed to it. . .it would still be one of the most precious discoveries of medicine". The
same can be said of magnetic resonance (MR). MR technology offers exciting insight into neurobiology,
and the main modalities and their application will be reviewed here. MR is based on the application of a
magnetic field and its modulation with a radio-frequency pulse. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
offers insight into regional morphology. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) offers a non-invasive
brain biopsy of neurochemistry. Functional MRI (fMRI) offers a window into brain function via
regional brain activation and oxyhemoglobin and deoxyhemoglobin exchange. Diffusion tensor imaging
(DTI) tracts white matter coherence and will be especially useful in studies of development. MR, as it
uses non-ionizing radiation, is an especially valuable tool for the investigation of pediatric populations.
Potential applications in neural development, neuropathology and drug trials will be discussed.

authors_raw: Shakeela Kahn, Frank MacMaster, John Connolly, Normand Carrey, Gail Eskes, Larry Gates, Stan Kutcher, Vivek Kusumakar
title: Neurobiological Discriminations of ADHD and Bipolar Disorder
abstract:
Both Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder and Bipolar Disorder are characterized by behavioral and
cognitive deficits that can negatively impact on crucial aspects of life such as academic, social and
family functioning, as well as enormous financial burden on society. The goal of treatment is to
intervene as early as possible and interrupt the course of the illness. Unfortunately, childhood onset of
Bipolar disorder often presents with symptoms similar to ADHD (attention deficit hyperactivity
disorder). This can result in misinterpretation of attention deficit and impulsivity and misdiagnosis of
both conditions in childhood. The consequences of misdiagnosis can be severe. Thus, early, accurate
detection of ADHD and bipolar disorder is vital to the management of the illnesses in the long term.
The current management of this problem involves waiting and monitoring the course of the
illness; an undesirable option given the evidence for neuronal deterioration and severity that
accompanies the natural progression of bipolar disorder (Drevets et al., 1997). One focus of the Dept of
Psychiatry at the IWK Grace is to develop neurobiological diagnostic tools to distinguish between
ADHD and bipolar disorder at an early age, with the goal of intervening as early as possible. These
research directions as well as past research in the area will be discussed.

authors_raw: Deanna Braaksma, Allen Finley, John Connolly, Michael Houlihan and Patrick McGrath
title: An evaluation of the Bispectral Index (BIS) as a valid monitor of depth of anaesthesia
abstract:
The BIS index monitor (BIS) (Aspect Medical Systems Inc.) is a relatively new technique that
Anaesthesiologists might use to measure anesthetic depth. Aspect claims that BIS is the first clinically
proven, direct measure of the effects of anesthesia on the conscious state of patients. However, BIS was
not developed to assess cognitive processes. It was merely designed in reference to an observer's
assessment of depth of anesthesia (e.g., autonomic responses, movement, blood concentrations). Is the
BIS monitor a valid clinical tool for assessing consciousness during anaesthesia? Two approaches are
proposed to provide anesthesiologists with stronger, better evidence to base their clinical decisions
regarding the BIS monitor: (1) asystematic review/ meta-analysis to comprehensively synthesize the
literature on BIS index values and various outcome measures of anesthetic depth, and (2) experimental
studies examining sensory and language processing using event-related potentials during anesthesia in
adult volunteers at various BIS index values.

title: 1 bump, 2 bump, old bump, new bump: Using event-related potential maps to identify functionally independent components in ERPs.
abstract:
Event-related potentials (ERPs) are the electrical signal recorded from a scalp that has been time-locked
to a particular event and averaged across many repetitions of the event. ERPs provide a signature of the
cortical processing of an event or stimulus with excellent temporal resolution. Advances in computing
power and electronics in general have made it possible to record ERPs at many locations over the scalp,
introducing an improvement in spatial localization of cognitive processes. ERPs are analyzed by
examining the amplitude, spatial distribution and timing of the amplitude. ERP components are usually
identified visually as peaks and valleys at each location. If ERP components overlap temporally and
spatially they are difficult to impossible to separate. Several solutions to the separation of components
have been tested over the years with none adequately filling the role. Recently, an approach to "blind
separation" called independent component analysis (ICA), has been applied to neurophysiological
signals. ICA has been used with ERPs and fMRI for identifying independent patterns in the data, as
onset and duration measures and for the elimination of artifact (muscle activity; EOG; cardiac signals).
Examples of the use of ICA will be presented and discussed.

title: Model of reading aloud: computational and fMRI investigations.
abstract:
The ability to transform orthographic (letter string) information into phonological (speech sound)
information underlies our ability to read words aloud, and it is one of the most important skills we learn.
It is often argued that various facts about reading is explained by a model which assumes the existence
of a dual-route architecture (lexical and nonlexical routes from print to speech). In this talk, we present
some possible cross-fertilizations between a single-route computational model of reading aloud and
functional neuroimaging.

title: Strategy versus ability in speech perception: Behavioural and ERP studies.
abstract:
Early speech perception research centred on the impressive abilities of the human perceptual system. In
particular, such research made much of listeners' tendency to perceive gradually varying speech stimuli
in terms
of a limited number of meaningful phonological categories. Current ERP research has focussed on
determining neural correlates of this ability.
However, more recent perceptual research has shown that while normal listeners show very
similar perceptual abilities, the strategies which individual listeners employ to reach a percept will often
differ. This talk will discuss current behavioural studies, which have shown developmental differences
in perceptual strategies. The talk will conclude with a discussion of the potential for ERP studies to
further our understanding of perceptual strategy differences.

title: Does Vagus Nerve Stimulation for the Treatment of Medically Refractory Seizures Enhance Memory?
abstract:
Pharmacology and Departments of Psychology Dalhousie University & QEII HSC
Epilepsy can have a profound impact on all aspects of a person's life. A novel treatment, vagus nerve
stimulation (VNS), has recently been introduced to treat medically refractory seizures. Despite some
promising effects on seizure frequency, little information is available regarding the effect of VNS on
cognitive functioning. A recent report in Nature Neuroscience indicates that VNS enhances recognition
memory in human subjects (Clark et al, 1999). However, this report has not been substantiated by other
groups. The present study examines memory function in a group of patients being treated with the vagal
nerve stimulator for medically refractory seizures. Subjective and objective memory measures,
including the Memory Observation Questionnaire and the Weschsler Memory Sc.ale-UTan~ administered
immediately before implantation of the stimulator and againat a follow-up visit one year later.
Outcome measures from the VNS patient group are compared to two control groups, including a group
of patients who have undergone temporal resectioning, and a group of patients maintained on
antiepileptic medication.

authors_raw: Elliott Marchant, Donna Goguen, Marleen deGroot, and Ben Rusak
title: Getting Hip on the Chip •• /- ' ··: > -· ) .,?71--{.-h/:__:,;__,1
abstract: This talk will outline new technology that provides efficient access to genetic information using miniaturized high-density arrays of oligonucleotide probes. This technology is currently is being used by the Rusak lab in collaboration with Novartis Institute for Functional Genomics and Affymetrix, to explore the molecular impact of light on the mouse suprachiasmatic nucleus. ,, (~\ .r\ 1 • ~- " I• 1 ~ •·1~1 ~ '•../•I'., _)_./

authors_raw: Marleen de Groot, Elliott Marchant and Ben Rusak
title: Meals on Wheels
abstract:
Animals exposed to schedules of temporally restricted food access will begin to anticipate the arrival of
food after a number of days on the schedule, typically by increasing activity, such as wheel-running.
This anticipatory activity is regulated by a self-sustaining, endogenous pacemaker that is both
structurally and functionally distinct from the master circadian pacemaker. The identity of this food-
entrainable pacemaker and the nature of the afferent signals that affect it remain unknown. We are
investigating which brain regions may be involved in the acquisition and expression of food anticipatory
activity. We have recently assessed patterns of immediate-early gene (IEG) expression in various brain
regions in mice who have been acutely food deprived or exposed to a repeated daily schedule of food
access. The preliminary data showing the effects of refeeding on IEG expression in these two groups of
mice will be discussed. . . ,c, / J ·

title: Intentional relations and divergent perspectives in social understanding
abstract:
People are very skilled at applying folk-psychological concepts uniformly to self and other. Thus, I
understand your being in love in much the same way that I understand my being in love. But the
information I have about you being in love is hardly the same as the information that I have about me
being in love. This informational difference can sometimes lead to divergent interpretations of human
actions between actors and observers. Not surprisingly, social psychologists, always on the lookout for
differences rather than commonalities, have stressed this divergence rather than the commonality in
social understanding, naming it the 'actor-observer effect'. In the present paper, I extend a model of
social understanding developed by Barresi & Moore (1996) to account for divergent interpretations of
actions, not only between actors and observers, but also between members of different social groups.

authors_raw: C. Christensen, A. Gregus, K. Luedeman, S. MacKinnon, B. Oliver, J. Pitman, J. Taggart, Marleen de Groot & Elliott Marchant
title: Lateral Hypothalamic Lesions Disrupt Time-Place Learning in Rats
abstract:
Time-place learning refers to situations in which an animal learns to associate a particular time of day
with a particular location. The acquisition of the association between temporal and spatial information
has previously been studied in various species. In order to investigate whether the lateral hypothalamus
(LH) plays a role in time-place learning, intact (n=6) and LB-lesioned (n=4) rats were trained to obtain
food at two distinct locations, each at a different time of day. A standard two lever T-maze was used in
this study. Rats were rewarded for lever presses on the left side during morning session and on the right
side during afternoon sessions. All 10 rats learned the task, however, omission tests showed that LH
lesioned animals performed worse than intact animals, suggesting that lesioned animals were using a
different strategy. The results indicate that the LH may be an important structure involved in time-place
learning.

authors_raw: Jennifer Klages and John D. Fisk
title: Is there a relation between Apolipoprotein E gene status and neuropsychological memory test performance? An analysis of the Canadian Study of Health and Aging
abstract:
The current identified risk factors for Alzheimer's disease (AD) have insufficient predictive value. One
is the presence of ;E4allele of the Apolipoprotein (APOE) gene. Poor performance on
neuropsychologicaitestsis another. We examined the relation between these two risk factors in a
population-based sample of people over age 64. Participants were enrolled in the Canadian Study of
Health and Aging (CSHA), a nationwide study of the prevalence of dementia that involved over 10,000
people. APOE status was determined in1578 of which 1188 had no E4 allele and 390 had at least one
~le. From this group, 1123 had n~ __cog~iti~ impai~ (Ng}, 268 had cognitive impairment but
not dementia (CIND) and 187 had dementia. Neuropsycholog1cal test performance of subjects with and
without the E4 allele was c-ompared within these di~c categories. NCI subjects who had an E4
allele performed worse on two memory tests: th~ued RecaliTest(p<.004) and the Rey
Auditory Verbal Leaming Test (p<0.05). APOE status was not related to test performance for the CIND
or demented groups and was not related toperformance on any other tests. APOE status -and memory
test performance have some relationship in nondemented elderly. Together, these factors may help to
predict the development of dementia at followup.

title: Alcohol effects on Risk-Taking during Video Lottery Terminal (VLT) Play Among Pathological
abstract:
Mike Ellery, Sherry H. Stewart, Pamela Loba, James R. Blackburn, & Raymond Klein
As part of an ongoing study, four risk-taking variables were examined in order to test the hypothesis that
alcohol consumption would lead to increased risk taking during gambling play, particularly for
pathological gamblers. Participants were regular video lottery terminal (VLT) players, recruited by
newspaper advertisement in the community. An equal number of the participants run thus far were
assigned to alcohol and no alcohol control conditions (N = 10 per beverage condition). Eight of the 20
(N =4 per beverage condition) met criteria for probable pathological gambling, as assessed by the South
Oaks Gambling Screen (SOOS; Lesieur & Blume, 1987), and the remaining 12 scored below the SOOS
cutoff, indicating non-pathological gambling status (N =6 per beverage condition). Participants were
randomly assigned to either the moderately intoxicating dose of alcohol condition, or the control
beverage condition (mix only). Following beverage consumption and absorption, participants were
allowed to play a poker game on a commercially available VLT for up to 30 minutes. Four indices of
risk-taking were recorded throughout gambling play: (a) number of times participants hit the "powerbet"
button (which allows gamblers to double their bet size after having viewed only two cards of their five
card poker hand); (b) total money inserted into machine during entire 30 minutes of play (in Canadian
dollars); (c) average bet size during the last 5 minutes of play (also in Canadian dollars); and (d) total
time out of a possible 30 minute play period that subjects chose to play machines (measured in minutes).
Results of preliminary planned comparison analyses provide evidence that: (1) alcohol consumption
increases risk taking behaviour while gambling, but only among pathological (and not non-pathological)
gamblers, and (2) increased risk taking is displayed by pathological relative to non-pathological
gamblers during VLT play, but only when they are intoxicated, and not when they are sober.
Theoretical and clinical implications of the findings will be discussed.

title: Trade-offs between current and future reproduction in the cricket, Gryllus integer
abstract:
In order to maximize lifetime reproductive success, organisms must often make trade-offs between
current and future reproductive effort. We examined these trade-offs in the cricket, Gryllus integer, in
response to predation risk and infection: two factors that could potentially reduce future reproductive
output to zero. We found that G. integer females laid more eggs when the egg-laying substrate was
covered than when exposed. However, G. integer females did not lay more eggs following infection with
a potentially lethal pathogen, Serratia marcescens. These results suggest the G. integer females regulate
oviposition based on predation risk, but that it is not influenced by immune status.

authors_raw: Robin L. Patterson & Darcy A. Santor
title: Accumulation, Reactivity, and Adaptation Models of Event and Mood Relationships
abstract:
Many studies examining the relation between events and mood employ a 2-wave panel design in which
events and mood are assessed at two time points. This assumes that the effects of events accumulate and
persist until mood is assessed at follow-up. This design is inadequate to assess mood reactivity or
individual adaptation. The present study examined three models of event and mood relationships:
accumulation, reactivity, and adaptability. Participants (N=102) completed mood and event checklists
twice weekly for 10 weeks. These checklists involved recording events and tracking moods and
behaviors. The results showed little evidence for the accumulation model. Results supported models that
tested reactivity and adaptation. Both positive and negative events influenced negative moods; however,
few negative events resulted in lasting mood disturbances. Mood disturbances that were precipitated by
a negative event lasted less than six days. Results have implications for how events are assessed and the
types of proposed models.

authors_raw: Melanie McFadyen, Marsha Penner, Stephen Lewis, Normand Carrey, & Richard Brown
title: Effects of Chronic Methylphenidate Administration at Several Stages of Development in CD-1 Mice
abstract:
Although methylphenidate hydrochloride (MPH, Ritalin) has been used in the treatment of attention
deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) for several decades, research examining the potential effects of
MPH on neurobehavioural development is lacking. We have conducted several experiments that begin
to systematically detail the effects of different doses of MPH administered at different times during
development (prenatal, neonatal/infancy, and prepubertal). Mice exposed to MPH (5 mg/kg) prenatally
(embryonic days (E) 8-10 or El2-14) exhibited significant increases in spontaneous locomotion when
tested at postnatal days (PND) 3-11. No differences were observed in tests of neuromotor reflexes such
as righting, negative geotaxis, or forelimb grip strength. In adulthood, the mice exposed to MPH from
E8-10 exhibited significantly increased exploration in both the open field and the elevated plus maze, as
well as decreased anxiety in the elevated plus maze. Chronic MPH administration (5 or 20 mg/kg)
during postnatal development (PND3- l 1) resulted in significant increases in locomotion, but had no
effect on ultrasonic vocalizations when pups were tested one hour after each injection. In adulthood,
these mice performed equally well in the Morris water maze, showing no deficits in either learning or
memory in this cognitive spatial task. No doses of MPH (2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, or 80 mg/kg) administered
during prepubertal development (PND26-32) had an effect on spontaneous locomotion, exploration, fear
or anxiety in the open field and elevated plus maze when the mice were tested at PND 33. There was no
effect of any dose of MPH on learning and memory in the Morris water maze when mice were tested
from 34-37 days of age. The results of our experiments suggest that while acute administration of MPH
does elevate locomotor activity, chronic administration has no enduring deleterious effects on
neuromotor reflexes, learning or memory. Experiments examining longer administration times, MPH
exposure at different stages of development, and effects of MPH in animal models of ADHD are
necessary in order to fully establish the safety of MPH exposure during development.

title: Effects of the Benzodiazepine Receptor Agonist Chlordiazepoxide & the 5-HTlA Antagonist WAY100635 on Behaviour in the Rat Elevated Zero Maze are not Influenced by the Light/Dark Cycle.
abstract: The rat elevated zero maze is a validated model of anxiety (Shepherd et al. 1994 Psychopharm 116:56- 64). In this test, rats administered anxiolytic drugs typically show a behavioural profile of increased exploratory activity (line crosses & head dips), increased time in the open areas, and less risk assessment (stretch-attends). In mice, 5-HTlA receptor antagonists have altered profiles on the elevated plus-maze when tested during different portions of the light/dark cycle (Rodgers et al., 1998 J Psychopharm 12:A32). To investigate this further, rats housed under normal or reversed light/dark cycles were tested with chlordiazepoxide (CDP), a benzodiazepine receptor agonist or with WA Yl00635, a 5-HTlA receptor antagonist. CDP (4 mg/kg) treated rats showed a similar anxiolytic profile in both housing groups. WA Yl00635 (0.01 to 0.3 mg/kg, sc: 30min) was without effect on rats from either housing group. These data show that the time of testing relative to the diurnal cycle does not alter the profiles of either CDP or WA Y100635, and question the assumption that time of testing is a critical determinant of the anxiolytic properties of 5-HTlA receptor antagonists.

authors_raw: l . J-r-\/ Heather Schellinck, Allison Clarke, Richard Brown and Michael Wilkinson
title: The identification of pheromonal components in male mouse urine which initiate puberty acceleration in fem ale mice
abstract:
The pheromonal components of male mouse urine which accelerate puberty in female mice have not
been clearly identified. To clarify this issue, we examined the effect of a number of compounds on
puberty acceleration and neural activation of the accessory olfactory bulb (AOB). During postnatal days
21-28, juvenile female mice, were exposed to male urine, major urinary proteins (MUPs),
MUPs+ligands attached (2-sec-butyl-dihydrothiazole:SBT and 2,3-dehydro-exo-brevicomin:DHB ),
SBT/DHB alone or saline. Puberty acceleration occurred in mice exposed to male urine or
MUPS+ligands but not in mice exposed to SBT/DHB alone or MUPs alone or saline. Immediate early
gene expression (c-fos) was significantly higher in the AOB of mice exposed to male urine or
MUPS+ligands than in those mice exposed to saline, SBT/DHB or MUPS alone. These results indicate
that exposure to specific components of male mouse urine not only induces puberty acceleration but also
activates the neural pathway which initiates such a effect.

title: Evolutionary origins of morality: Is there an ancestral 'logic' to the modern mind?
abstract:
Selfish gene theory has difficulty explaining the existence of moral impulses. Evidence from
evolutionary economics suggests that individuals can forgo the temptation to cheat due to the emotions
of guilt and sympathy. However genes predisposing the development of these prosocial emotions would
be at a fitness disadvantage in a population of exploiters. How did sympathy evolve if it is costly to the
