altruist? One possible pathway is the formation of cooperator-cooperator alliances. If group formations
were based upon intrinsically unfalsifiable sympathy signals, non-kin altruism may be evolutionarily
stable. Perhaps the benefits to the genes involved were accrued at the within- and between-group levels
of selection. Preliminary evidence that humans detect an individual's likelihood to cooperate will be
presented.

title: Remote computing: Internet access from Far Away Places
abstract:
Reliable access to the internet has become a requirement for today's academics and we take it for
granted in our daily work at the university. However, when you take your work (and office) on the road
(or the high seas), you need to make special arrangements to maintain that internet access.
Technological advances of the recent years hold much promise for remote workers. Wireless
communication is particularly enticing. In an entertaining illustrated presentation, we will discuss what
worked reliably, what did not, based on our recent travels.

authors_raw: Ron Hoffman, Vivien Hannon, John MacDonald, David Patriquin
title: We do PowerPoint, we do web pages, now what? Digital Video!
abstract:
Recent technological advances in what has become known as Information Technology have changed
the way we work. Chalk and blackboards are rapidly being supplanted by PowerPoint presentations, and
we expect students to use that technology, too. A primary source for information is now the web
(although too many students seem to see it as the only source), and our graduates need to know how to
create their own web sites when they hit the job market. Now, new technologies are bringing digital
video to the desktop, and we are just beginning to see the impact. We discuss these technologies, show
some examples of what can be done, and ask if we should add another skill to our IT skill set.

authors_raw: Ray Klein, David Shore, J. Gati & R. Menon
title: Mental Geography Meets Mental Chronometry in an Exploration of the Law of "Prior Entry"
abstract:
Abstract: It has been suggested that psychology was borne from the union of interests in mind
(philosophy) and brain (physiology). Here we seek to unite two methodologies with strong connections
to mind (mental chronometry) and brain (neuroimaging) in the expectation that together they will be
able to do more than either could alone. Our study concerns Titchener's Fourth Law of Attention: "The
stimulus, for which we are predisposed, requires less time than a like stimulus, for which we are
unprepared, to produce its full conscious effect" (1908, p251). We will illustrate how this law of "prior
entry" can be explored and precisely measured psychophysically by obtaining temporal order
judgements for pairs of stimuli, one of which is attended. We will then present preliminary fMRI data
suggesting that specific brain regions are involved in performing these judgements. Finally we will
propose how event-related fMRI can be used to test Titchener's "Law of Prior Entry" and how such
neuroimaging studies of prior entry can be useful in demonstrating the potential of latency-resolved
fMRI.

title: There is More to Tactile Perception than Simply Touch
abstract:
David I. Shore*, Emily SpryA, Charles SpenceA * Rotman Research Institute; Baycrest Centre for
Geriatric Care; University of Toronto ADepartment of Experimental Psychology; Oxford University
We explored the role of body position in the perception of simple tactile stimuli. The observer was
presented with brief tactile vibrations to the left and right forefinger and was required to indicate which
hand was stimulated first (using a foot pedal response). The interval and order between the two stimuli
was varied randomly in order to produce a psychophysical function from which the Just Noticeable
Difference (JND) was derived. This measure indicates how much time is needed in order to indicate
reliably which stimulus was presented first. In three separate experiments observers adopted different
postures: Hands crossed, hands far apart, and one hand displayed in a mirror. The JND was much
larger when the hands were crossed compared to uncrossed and, when hands were close together rather
than far apart. The mirror manipulation had no effect on JND. The finding that changing hand position
resulted in a change in JND is surprising given that the same receptor surface is stimulated in both
conditions. The role of body centred representations in tactile perception will be discussed.

title: The Rising Cost of OOTLUF
abstract:
Recent studies have looked at the trade-off involved in auto-pilot systems. Although automation does
reduce the workload of the pilot in important tasks, it also seems to reduce the situational awareness,
and produce Out of the loop unfamiliarity, (OOTLUF). This present study tested this trade-off by asking
participants to navigate a set of virtual Hebb/Williams mazes, under both manual and automated
conditions. Automation was simulated on alternate mazes by having participants learn a route through
which they were passively transported using a previous participant's active exploration. The cost of
situational awareness was measured as the increase in time to complete a maze based on the method of
learning, while workload was measured through simple reaction time to random and frequent probes in
both the manual and automatic conditions. Although the OOTLUF, cost of automation was observed in
this experiment, the benefits to workload were not as clear cut.

title: The Negative Consequences of Being a Good Boy Scout
abstract:
The boy scout motto - "always be prepared" - may lead to rapid responses when a wilderness creature
attacks suddenly. But when it comes to reacting to visual targets, responses may be slowed by prior
(motor) preparation. We presented participants with two peripheral visual targets to which speeded
spatially-compatible button-press responses were required. On a random half of the trials, a Stop Signal
was presented at a variable delay following the First Target. Participants were instructed to withhold a
response to the First Target when the Stop Signal appeared. Their ability to do so varied with the
interval between the First Target and the Stop Signal appearance: The longer the delay, the less likely
the participant was to withhold the prepared response. We examined how reaction times to the Second
Target varied with the probability of canceling a response to the First Target. Although the results are
preliminary, they suggest that preparing a response to the First Target slows the speed of making that
same response to the Second Target. More importantly, the degree of slowing is related to the degree of
prior motor preparation: The more prepared the First Target response is (i.e., the more difficult it is to
cancel), the greater the inhibitory aftereffects may be.

title: What 'coon you say about brain circuits using local inactivation?
abstract:
The raccoon has proved a great model for investigating cortical reorganization after peripheral
denervations (e.g. amputation) due to their enlarged representations of the forepaw digits. In the raccoon
cortex, a large sensory evoked potential (SEP) occurs in response to electrical stimulation of the
corresponding digit - an "on-focus" response. Stimulation of a neighbouring digit evokes a smaller SEP
- an "off-focus" response. It has been hypothesized that the off-focus response is conveyed through
intracortical circuits from one digit representation to another and that these corticocortical pathways may
contribute to the patterns of cortical reorganization observed in this species. We examined this
hypothesis by recording on- and off-focus SEPs from 2 neighbouring digit cortical regions and
examining the change in SEPs during a selective, reversible inactivation of one region using lidocaine
and tetrodotoxin. Although the drugs blocked the "on-focus" response in the inactivated area, this did
not result in a significant decrease in the amplitude of the "off-focus" response from the neighbouring
cortical digit area. This indicates that changes in corticocortical connections may not play a major role in
reorganization following amputation.

authors_raw: Ivan Valdivia, Marsha Penner, Jeannette McGlone, Shirley Munk, David Clarke, and Mark Sadler
title: Quality of Life in Patients with Vagal Nerve Stimulators
abstract:
People with chronic epilepsy may experience a quality of life that is not optimal. Patients whose seizures
are resistant to medication and who are not eligible for brain surgery may now be able to use Vagal
Nerve Stimulation (VNS) as an alternative treatment. While the study of Quality of Life in persons with
epilepsy is researched, information regarding the Quality of Life of patients using VNS is not. This
study aims to examine Quality of Life in a VNS implantation group and two control groups: I-those
who have undergone temporal lobe resectioning, and 2-those who are maintained on medications alone.
Two separate measures of Quality of Life (i.e., the Quality of Life in Epilepsy-89, and the Quality of
Life Questionnaire) are administered immediately before implantation of the VNS and again at a year
follow-up. Preliminary data will be presented.

title: The evolution, history, and development of consciousness: A sketch
abstract:
In the present talk I will give an overview of the evolution, history and development of consciousness.
Obviously, it will only be a sketch. Basically, the issue is 'why consciousness'? What's consciousness
for? How did it evolve to the level we find it in the human species? How did consciousness change
historically in the human species? And, how does consciousness develop during the life of an individual
human person? I promise to give answers to all of these questions. Whether you will believe my answers
is quite another issue. But what can you expect from a ten-minute sketch?

title: Neuropharmacology and neurobiology of general anesthetics: the mystery of h1;1_faan consciousness ,.., ) ·' ;)
abstract: Can the nature of consciousness be quantified, located, and understood within the human brain? Prevention of conscious awareness is a main goal of general anaesthesia. Examining (1) the molecular mechanisms of action of diverse anesthetic agents on various sites of the brain, (2) the similarities and differences between neuronal systems of anaesthetized and non-anaesthetized brains, and (3) the effects of anesthetics on higher brain functioning using functional brain imaging may be a fundamental empirical first step toward the eventual understanding human consciousness. The current presentation will outline what is known about the neurobiology and pharmacology of general anesthesia (e.g., NMDA receptors, Ach neurotransmitters, neuronal proteins, cortical-thalamic interactions, whole-brain metabolism), and will attempt to relate it to current models of consciousness (e.g., Rosenthal's metarepresentation, 1990; Tulving's multiple systems, 1998).

title: "Now you see it, now you don't: Or did you?"
abstract:
Part of a recent experiment by John Christie and John Barresi will be presented as an example of the use
of psychophysical methods in studying consciousness. The pitfalls, and controversies around these
methods will be mentioned and hopefully incite much painful thought. Questions will be entertained but
not necessarily answered.

title: The Hard Problem of Consciousness: Bridging the Explanatory Gap
abstract:
Many (perhaps most) psychologists and neuroscientists believe that an adequate, materialistic
understanding of brain and behaviour will entail an understanding of the mind and consciousness. There
will be no irreducible or incompatible residue left to explain once our empirical investigations, and their
theoretical interpretation, are complete. Some (perhaps most) philosophers, however, are skeptical of
this optimistic outlook. They believe that the mind, and particularly consciousness, have properties
which cannot be brought within the scope of scientific explanation. There is an explanatory gap between
a complete physical story, and one which includes such conscious phenomena as qualia and values.
Although we can expect that the easy problems of perception, learning, discrimination, and so on, will
be solved through scientific methods, the hard problem of consciousness will prove to be much more
difficult, and perhaps impossible.

title: An examination of strain differences in nest building in virgin, pregnant and lactating C57BL/6 and DBA/2 mice (Mus Musculus). Tamara L.Y. Bond Richard E. Brown 1,Christianne Hawken and Paul E. Neumann 1
abstract:
C57BL/6 (B6) and DBA/2 (D2) mice differ in maternal behaviour and nest building. However, the
results on nest building have been contradictory. Although lactating B6 females spend more time nest
building than lactating D2 females (Brown et al., 1999, Physiol Behav, 67:599-605), pregnant D2
females build better pre-parturient nests (Broida & Svare, 1982, Physiol Behav, 25: 153-157). To
resolve this discrepancy, we recorded nest quality in virgin, nulliparous, pregnant and lactating females.
Virgin B6 and D2 females were housed with either a male or female of their own strain for fifteen days.
Females were then housed singly and their nests rated daily on a scale of one to four. Some of the
females of each strain that were housed with males failed to give birth and some litters died before the
pups reached four days of age. There were no strain differences in the nest ratings of virgin females or
females who did not give birth, nor did these groups differ. Therefore, these two groups were collapsed
into one group of nulliparous females. No differences were observed between strains in the proportion
of pups surviving per litter or in pup weight gain. B6 and D2 females whose litters survived built better
nests than nulliparous females. Among females whose litters survived, B6 females built better nests
than D2 females both before and after birth. Although both strains increased their nest building on the
day of parturition, there was a strain by day interaction that resulted from a dramatic increase in nest
building by D2 mice on the day of parturition. However, the increase in nest building by D2 mice at
parturition was not large enough to ameliorate the difference in nest building between the two strains.
Mothers of both strains whose litters died built better nests than nulliparous females but nest ratings did
not differ between strains. The nest ratings of D2 mothers whose litters died did not differ from those of
D2 mothers whose litters survived but B6 mothers whose litters died built poorer nests than B6 mothers
whose litters survived. One hypothesis to account for these results is that females of these two strains
differ in hormone levels during pregnancy.

authors_raw: Gail Eskes, C. Szostak and D. T. Stuss
title: The Role of the Frontal Lobes in Implicit Memory
abstract:
While the role of the frontal lobes in explicit memory is well supported, the findings for implicit tasks
are less conclusive. We investigated the role of the frontal lobes in perceptual and conceptual implicit
priming. Three memory tasks were given under both implicit and explicit retrieval instructions using
word fragment completion, picture fragment completion and category exemplar generation. Four groups
were studied: Left dorsolateral lesions, right dorsolateral lesions, medial lesions (left, right or bilateral),
normal controls. Word fragment completion priming was impaired by left dorsolateral lesions, with
other priming tests unaffected by any lesion. Explicit performance showed a different pattern, with
category exemplar cued recall impaired by all lesions. These findings support the role of the frontal
lobes in both implicit and explicit retrieval mechanisms.

authors_raw: Angela R. Birt, Stephen Porter, & Mike Woodworth
title: Criminal Career Profiles as a Function of Psychopathy and Sexual Violence
abstract:
The problem of sexual violence has become increasingly apparent in recent years. In this study, the
complete criminal and conditional release performance histories of 217 federally incarcerated sex
offenders and 101 non-sex offenders were examined. Offenders were categorized according to the
nature of their sexual violence and all offenders were rated on level of psychopathy (PCL-R, Hare,
1991). Results indicated that (a) certain sex offender groups had higher levels of psychopathy than
others, (b) psychopathy exacerbated the violent and non-violent crime committed by all offender groups,
and (c) Mixed Rapists/ Molesters were the most dangerous as indexed by their psychopathy prevalence,
criminal profiles, and conditional release performance. These findings have implications for the
management and treatment of sexual violence.

title: Effects of parental experience and the presence of the father on pup growth and development in Peromyscus californicus. Mauro L.
abstract:
This experiment investigated the effects of the presence of the father and parental experience on pup
development and behavior in California mice. Twenty-seven litters of California mice were used as
subjects. Litters housed with single experienced mothers (n=9), experienced fathers and mothers (n=9)
and inexperienced fathers and mothers (n=9) were studied. Behaviors of pups were recorded every
second day from 10 to 30 days of age. The results indicated that the presence of the father did not
influence pup behavioral development and physical growth. Pups of first time parents were more active
and had advanced locomotor activity in comparison with pups from experienced parents. It is suggested
that the parity of the parents has greater influence on the development and behavior in California mice
than the presence of father. Financial support: CAPES (Brazil) and NSERC (Canada).

title: Coding for orientation in the representation of faces
abstract:
A common debate in face and object recognition literature is whether faces and objects share similar
processing and representational systems. Experiment 1 investigated whether patterns of response to
faces rotated through multiple orientations were similar to those observed for objects. Participants
named digital images of faces presented at multiple orientations over a series of 8 blocks. The findings
suggested same effects of orientation on rotated faces and object naming during the first block of
presentations. However, unlike objects, effects of orientation required many more blocks to diminish.
Experiment 2 investigated the kind of representations underlying these practice effects. Participants
repeatedly named faces at two orientations. After training, response times were recorded for faces
rotated at the trained orientations and two "surprise" orientations. If, like objects, practice effects
transferred to faces presented at "surprise" orientations this would suggest that orientation-invariant
representations were encoded. Conversely, no transfer of practice effects would suggest that orientation-
specific representations were encoded.

title: An Investigation of Factors Influencing Accuracy at Detecting Deception
abstract:
Although empirical research has demonstrated that there are reliable verbal and nonverbal cues to
deception (e.g., Porter & Yuille, 1996), the ability to detect deceit eludes most people. The purpose of
the present study was to investigate possible factors influencing accuracy at detecting deception,
including cue usage, personality characteristics, gender, handedness, and confidence. Participants were
asked to judge the honesty of eight videotaped speakers relating an account of a personal experience.
Half of these tapes were presented audio-visually while the other half were presented with audio only.
Participants provided both a baseline report of cues they generally used in detecting deceit and cues they
actually relied upon in formulating each judgment. Major findings were that: both target attractiveness
and female gender were negatively associated with accuracy; lies were easier to identify than truths; left-
handers were better at detecting deceit than right-handers; and the number of overall cues used was
positively correlated with accuracy.

authors_raw: Elizabeth McLaughlin, Sherry Stewart & Steve Taylor
title: Subscale scores on the Child Anxiety Sensitivity Index (CASI) predict unique variance in DSM-IV anxiety symptoms beyond trait anxiety
abstract:
The CASI is a self-report questionnaire, which surveys children's fear of the physical, social, and
psychological consequences of anxiety sensations. Groups of items pertaining to each of these
consequences have been empirically shown to form three distinct lower-order factors on the adult
version of the scale. Our first goal was to validate this factor structure in the CASI. Secondly, we
wanted to determine whether scores on each CASI factor predicted symptoms of anxiety disorder
subtypes, after taking out variance due to trait anxiety. The CASI, a measure of DSM-IV anxiety
symptoms (Spence Children's Anxiety Scale), and the STAIC were administered to 349 school-aged (7-
15 years) children. Scores on each of the three CASI factors predicted unique variance in conceptually
related anxiety symptoms.

title: One or Multiple Mechanisms Underlying Orientation-Invariant Identification of Objects?
abstract:
What are the mechanisms underlying orientation-invariant identification of objects? We are
investigating the conditions under which orientation-invariant identification of objects occurs, and
whether one or several mechanisms underlie that process. Orientation-invariant recognition occurs at
the superordinate and basic levels of identity, as well as after practice with naming rotated objects. The
underlying mechanism(s) might be based upon the extraction of orientation-invariant features, or, upon
the global attributes (requiring intact spatial arrangement of the features) of objects. We presented four
blocks of feature-scrambled objects followed by a block of intact, rotated objects, in a verification task
where level of identity was controlled. If the mechanism is feature-based, superordinate- and basic-level
identification of scrambled objects should be faster than subordinate-level. Furthermore, practice at
identifying scrambled objects should diminish orientation effects in the final block. A global-based
mechanism would not produce these effects.

title: Executive Function Abilities and Prudence and Altruism in Preschoolers
abstract:
The aim of the current study was to assess the cognitive basis for emerging social behaviour during the
preschool period. A comprehensive battery of executive function tasks measuring inhibition, attentional
flexibility, and working memory, and a future-oriented prudence and altruism task was administered to
58 preschoolers aged 3;6. Age and language partialled correlations revealed that children who were
unable to inhibit copying a puppet's actions, and those who could not refrain from peeking during the
wrapping of a present were more likely to choose two stickers for later on a delay of gratification for self
task. Children who waited longer before peeking were also more likely to choose one sticker over two
for themselves in the present. There was no relationship between performance on any EF task and
future-oriented sharing behaviour. Contrary to the predicted outcome, there was a significant negative
relationship between performance on both inhibition tasks and children's choice to forego an immediate
reward. A hypothesis is put forward regarding the possibility that poor inhibitors were choosing two
stickers for different reasons than would be expected. Further longitudinal relationships between the EF
variables and performance on the future-oriented prudence and altruism task will be discussed as
children were also tested at age 4;0.

title: Effects of Chronic Ritalin Administration on Postnatal Mouse Pup Development
abstract:
Methylphenidate hydrochloride (MPH, Ritalin) is a psychostimulant that is used primarily in children
for the treatment of ADHD. Despite having been used for several decades, the consequences of chronic
MPH administration has not been well characterized in the developing nervous system. Thus, our
experiment investigated the effects of repeated administration of MPH on development using several
tests from the Dalhousie Developmental Test Battery including isolation-induced ultrasonic
vocalizations (UVs), spontaneous locomotor activity, and several tests of neuromotor development in
outbred CD-1 mouse pups. From postnatal days (PNDs) 3 to 11, mice received daily injections (s.c.) of,
1) a high dose of MPH (20mg/kg), 2) a low dose of MPH (5mg/kg), 3) an equivalent amount of saline,
or 4) no injection. Mice were tested 30-70 minutes post-injection on PNDs 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11. Both the
high and low doses of MPH resulted in significant increases in locomotor activity at all ages tested, but
had no significant effect on the number or duration of UVs emitted. Contrary to our expectations,
performance on tests of neuromotor development was not clearly affected by either dose of MPH.
Furthermore, MPH treatment had no effect on body weight. These results suggest that chronic MPH
treatment has no short-term effects on the developing nervous system.

authors_raw: Sean McCabe, Jana Podhorna, and Richard Brown
title: Effect of diazepam on the behaviour of "anxious" C57BL/6 mice in the step-down passive avoidance task
abstract:
In humans, anxiety impairs learning and memory in a variety of tasks. C57BU6 mice display higher
levels of anxiety than other strains of mice in various models of anxiety such as the elevated plus maze
and open field. Moreover, C57BU6 mice show memory impairments in models such as the passive
avoidance task that involve a fearful or "emotional" stimulus. Diazepam has been used to treat anxiety in
humans, but has been found to possess amnesic properties by impairing the acquisition process. In
animals, benzodiazepines administered before learning trials in passive avoidance tasks impair memory
retention but not acquisition. The present study examined the effects of diazepam on acquisition and
retention of the passive avoidance task in male and female C57BU6 mice. Diazepam or saline was
administered to the mice 30 min before the learning trials. Female mice appeared to be more sensitive
to the sedative effects of diazepam than males. However, diazepam did not impair acquisition or
retention of the passive avoidance task in either sex. These results suggest that diazepam does not
produce the amnesic affect in anxious C57BU6 mice that is seen in other strains of mice.

authors_raw: Randy Lynn Newman, John F. Connolly, Ryan C.N. D' Arcy, Daniel Cook
title: Event Resolution Imaging: Overcoming the limitations of signal averaging in ERP research
abstract:
The interpretation of ERPs has typically relied on signal averaging to extract information from
background noise (i.e. EEG). A new signal detection method, event-resolution imaging (ERi), has
demonstrated the ability to extract information from the EEG with little or no averaging. ERi uses a
three-step process of pattern learning, pattern recognition, and validation. During pattern learning,
multiple epochs from two distinct conditions are analyzed to detect and subsequently discriminate robust
patterns in space (multiple electrode sites), time, frequency, and phase. This information is encoded as a
template that is subsequently used to analyze unclassified, unaveraged or few-trial averaged EEG data.
We tested the feasibility of ERi by reanalyzing data recorded during performance in a
neuropsychological reading test (PALPA; D' Arey, Connolly, Eskes, 1999). Using traditional averaging,
correct and incorrect trials in the P ALP A were differentiated on the basis of a P300 response to correct
trials. For each epoch analyzed, ERI generated a high signal-to-noise waveform and subsequent
classification. Initial results on 5 trial averages indicated an average accuracy level of 72.3%, and results
for a single trial analysis indicated an average accuracy of 62.3%. Data of a learning disabled
participant, who exhibited no P300 activity in averaged data, were classified with 76% accuracy.
Interestingly, for this individual ERi found that 100% of correct trials failed to exhibit correct-trial
template features (i.e. no P300) as seen in all healthy individuals. ERi appears to be a powerful tool for
use in basic and clinical research.

authors_raw: Daniel A. Waschbusch, Ann Marie Penny --=--- - The purpose of the proposed study is to investigate whether or not recent theories of intelligence are useful in understanding children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. Participants will be children aged to, including children with and without ADHD, and with and without learning disorders. In order to assign each child a diagnosis, parents will complete a series of questionnaires assessing the child's behaviour, and each child will complete the Wide Range Achievement Test, third edition. Each child will also complete the Woodcock-Johnson Tests of Cognitive Abilities, third edition, which provides scores based on the Carroll-Horn-Cattell theory of intelligence. These scores will be analyzed for patterns within diagnostic groups, as well as differences between groups
title: Thinking About ADHD: Is a Psychoeducational Perspective Useful in Understanding Attention- Deficit / Hyperactivity Disorder?

authors_raw: Mary Ellen Large and Paddy McMullen
title: Priming effects of global and local attention on the categorization of objects: A pilot study.
abstract:
Images of objects are identified more quickly at the basic level (dog) than the subordinate level (beagle)
because subordinate level identifications require more visual processing in order to differentiate
exemplars who share many common features. The differences in speed of processing between basic and
subordinate level categorizations may be explained by global (coarse aspects of structure) and local (fine
detail) attentional differences. To investigate this possibility the priming effects of a global and local
attentional task on the categorization of objects at the basic and subordinate level were examined.
Additionally, distractors varied in their degree of similarity to the target. It was found that after priming
at the local level participants responded more quickly to both basic and subordinate categorizations
when the distractors were similar and after priming at the global level they responded more quickly to
basic and subordinate categorizations when the distractors were dissimilar. This result suggested that
an important distinction in the speed of processing for basic and subordinate categorizations is not the
level of categorization per se but the visual context in which the categorizations occurs.

authors_raw: Jingtian Wang and John F. Connolly
title: The effects of the segmental and suprasegmental processes of spoken Chinese Mandarin on the auditory ERPs
abstract:
In this study, event-related brain potentials (ERPs) were used to investigate spoken Chinese Mandarin
processes. Chinese four-character proverbs were presented auditorally to 15 native Mandarin speakers
for listening comprehension. Five conditions used proverbs that ended with: 1) the accurate fourth
character of the proverb; 2) a character with the same final vowel and tone as the correct proverb-
terminal character, but different initial consonant and meaning; 3) a character with the same initial
consonant and tone as the correct proverb-terminal character, but different final vowel and meaning; 4) a
character with the same segments as the correct proverb-terminal character, but different tone and
meaning; and, 5) a character with a totally different segments, tone and meaning from the correct ending
character. The N400 was seen in the last four conditions with the largest N400 in the fifth condition
while the smallest was seen in the fourth condition. Only a late positive component was found in the
first condition. It was concluded that tone processing does play a role although not that much as
segments in processing spoken Chinese. Before N400, there was another negativity whose peak varied
across last four conditions, which suggested that within highly constrained proverb contexts, tone was
processed earlier than vowel. In addition, the ERP scalp distribution varied not only across different
conditions but also different time periods. These data suggest that an auditory code relies upon discrete
processing stages with different neural mechanisms underlying consonant, vowel and tone processing.
The findings are integrated into current theories of spoken word recognition.

authors_raw: Elliott Marchant, K. Devarajan, and B. Rusak
title: Sillier Cilia: The Hairy Walls of the Mouse Third Ventricle
abstract:
In the course of anatomical research aimed at confirming the results of microarray gene expression
studies, we re-discovered the generally ignored fact that portions of the third ventricle in the
hypothalamus are lined with cilia. We also made the novel observation that these cilia contain two
proteins: the calcium-regulating protein parvalbumin, and the neurohormone oxytocin. Levels of both
parvalbumin and oxytocin in the cilia were regulated both by time of day and by whether or not the mice
had been exposed to light an hour earlier. The rapid responses of these proteins to light exposure suggest
a dynamic regulation that may influence the secretion of oxytocin into the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).
Oxytocin has been implicated in various affiliative behaviours and in the regulation of excitatory
neurotransmission in the brain. Our observations suggest novel mechanisms for rapid regulation of
global brain function via hypothalamic modulation of hormonal secretion into the CSF.
I
I
-I
I

title: Exchange of dye tracers across the blood-brain barrier systems around the fly's eye
abstract:
To gauge the effectiveness of the barriers around the eye, quantitative estimates of dye exchange were
made by microfluorometry, using sulforhodamine 101 (SR) or lucifer yellow (LY). Influx to the retina
from the blood was calibrated by making a known injection into the retina, allowing influx and efflux to
be measured on the same preparations. Efflux of SR averaged 2.8 µM eye-1 min-1 per mM driving
concentration of the dye injected, while the paired influx rate was significantly lower, 0.20 µM eye-1
min-1 per mM dye injected, for an exchange ratio of 14: 1. Values for LY were higher but not
significantly different, for a ratio of 8: 1. The efflux half-times for LY and SR also were not significantly
different, 2.9-3.6 h, but were ~5 times slower than previous estimates. The molecular uptake rate for
dye significantly exceeds that recently calculated for ATP usage during the maximum rate of neural
signaling by the photoreceptors, 1000 bit s-1 (55 x 109 versus 7.5 x 109 molecules ceu-1 s-1 ), and might
represent the flux rate of the corresponding transporter. Earlier estimates of dye loss from the retina also
used phenol red (PR). Photographic evidence indicates that with optimum concentrations in the eye,
clearance of PR was also slow, taking 8-17 h. To identify the dye exchange route, 20 µm fluorescent
microspheres were injected into the retina, based on reports that larger microspheres remain mobile in
the eye and eventually to leave it. Instead, they penetrated a distal zone of mechanical weakness which
opened up around the cone cells, then closed again, trapping the microspheres. The barrier sites which
rate-limit influx and efflux to and from the eye and account for the flux asymmetry remain to be
identified, and could be different neuroanatomical structures.

authors_raw: Pamela Loba, Sherry H. Stewart, Raymond M. Klein, & James R. Blackburn ------ ---------- A set of lab-based and in-vivo studies were conducted to identify game parameters that would reduce the risk of abuse of video lottery terminals by pathological gamblers, while exerting minimal effects on the behavior of non-pathological gamblers. Three manipulations of standard VLT game features were explored. Participants were exposed to: a counter which displayed a running total of money spent; a VLT spinning reels game where participants could no longer "stop" the reels by touching the screen; and sensory feature manipulations. In control conditions, participants were exposed to standard settings for either a spinning reels or a video poker game. For the lab-based study dependent variables were self-ratings of reactions to each set of parameters. A set of 2 x x (game manipulation [experimental condition vs. control condition] x game [spinning reels vs. video poker] x gambler status [pathological vs. non-pathological]) repeated measures ANOVA's were conducted on all dependent variables. The in-vivo study used behavioural ratings as the dependent measure. Finding for both studies suggest that the sensory manipulations produced the most robust reaction differences
title: Manipulations of the Features of Standard Video Lottery Terminal (VLT) Games: Lab-based and In-vivo Results
The quickest cancellation in the west
abstract:
Having the best tactic for successfully cancelling an action ensures the minimum of unwanted
movements. Normal overt responses to environmental stimuli can unexpectedly require sudden
cancellation. Strategies for movement cancellation include freezing all movement or making an alternate
movement instead. It is not known which strategy is more effective at preventing an overt action.
Participants can be instructed to cancel a response to a stimulus when presented with a 'stop-signal', or to
replace the original response with an alternate action when presented with a 'change-signal'. Past
research has investigated the effectiveness of inhibition mechanisms by plotting the likelihood of
successful inhibition along points in time. This research has used stop-signals almost exclusively.
Following a brief literature review, the present talk will outline a research proposal that is intended to
provide a direct comparison of the effectiveness of inhibiting responses to stop-signals compared to
change-signals. Preliminary data will be provided.

authors_raw: Randy Lynn Newman, John Connolly, Kimberly Mclvor, Wayne MacDonald, and Sarah Arnold
title: Identifying Phonological Awareness Deficits with Event-Related Brain Potentials (ERPs)
abstract:
Reading disabilities primarily arise from a deficit in phonological awareness, which is simply defined as
the ability to identify, think about, or manipulate the individual sounds in words. The present research
used event-related brain potentials (ERPs) to elucidate the neural correlates involved in phonological
analysis, and how these differ in individuals with and without reading disabilities. Participants heard
instructions, such as "Clap without the /cl sound", where the target word (Clap) was always
monosyllabic and began with a consonant cluster. The instructions were followed by presentation of a
correct item (lap) or one of three incorrect items: (1) wrong consonant deleted (cap), (2) consonant
cluster deleted (ap); or an (3) irrelevant word (goose). Those with efficient phonological skills elicited a
Phonological Mismatch Negativity (PMN), a component associated with phonological expectations, to
incorrect items and a P300, a component associated with memory updating, to correct items. Individuals
with the greatest deficits in phonological skills showed no differentiation between correct and incorrect
items, with the exception of a PMN to irrelevant words. Those with moderate deficits displayed a PMN
to both irrelevant words and to items with the wrong consonant deleted. Further, these individuals
elicited a P300 to both correct items, and to items with the consonant cluster deleted. Results will be
discussed in terms of the capacity that ERP techniques have for identifying reading disabilities, and for
assisting reading recovery programs.

title: Kindling-induced emotionality in both male and female rats Wintink, AJ, Young, N, Davis
abstract:
Men and women with temporal lobe epilepsy often experience interictal (i.e. between seizure) emotional
disturbances. Long-term kindling (i.e. repeated focal stimulations) in rats serves as a model to
understand the nature of seizures and of interictal emotionality (i.e. anxious behavior in novel situations)
but has not yet been used to investigate potential differences in emotionality between male and female
rats. There is substantial reason to expect sex differences in interictal emotionality including clinical
data showing that more women are diagnosed with anxiety related disorders than are men and the
expression of anxiety can be manifested differently between men and women. In the present study, both
short- (30 stimulations) and long- (99 stimulations) term kindling was used to assess differences in
emotionality in both male and female rats. Rats were assessed on behavioral measures of anxiety and
depression and sex differences were observed on some of the measures. The results provide the first
examination of kindling-induced emotionality between male and female rats .

authors_raw: Heather M. Schellinck, Matthew Darrah and Lianne Stanford
title: Long-term effects of acute and chronic pain in infancy in mice
abstract:
Exposure to pain during development has long been hypothsized to disrupt behaviour in later life.
(Hebb, 1949 Organization of Behaviour). We investigated the influence of early pain experience on
anxiety and learning and memory in mice. Neonatal mice were exposed to an acute pain, i.e., a tail snip
at postnatal day 8 or chronic pain, i.e., paw pricks on postnatal days 8-14. When tested in an elevated
plus maze on day 30, both acute and chronic pain mice showed behavioural changes indicative of
increased anxiety compared with control animals. The effects included a reduction in open arm entries,
percent time spent in the open arms and a decrease in head dips. In addition, chronic pain animals
showed a greater decrease in time spent in the open arms and in number of entries into the open arms as
well as an increase in stretch attends compared with acute pain animals. There were no differences in
the number of line crossings in the experimental and control animals. This pattern of results would
suggest that the pain-exposed animals showed an overall increase in risk assessment, a behaviour
associated with hypervigilance.in humans. In contrast, when tested in the Morris Water Maze, these
mice demonstrated an intact working memory in a three-day acquisition task as well as in a probe trial
and reversal trials. Thus, it appears that their increased anxiety did not adversely effect their
performance in a spatial working memory task. Overall, these results reflect the potential role of early
experience in influencing behaviour in adult animals.

authors_raw: Celeste Lefebvre, John Connolly and Gail Eskes
title: An event-related brain potential (ERP) study of attention/working memory
abstract:
The purpose of this study is to adapt two commonly used standardized neuropsychological measures of
attention/working memory for computer presentation and simultaneous ERP recordings. This study will
investigate the effectiveness of ERPs to determine working memory capabilities, as measured by the
Digit Span and Spatial Span tasks from the Wechsler Memory Scale-III, in a normal population of
undergraduate students. Each subject will be administered the traditional and computerized version of
one of the two tasks. We hypothesize that 1) an individual's results on the computerized version of the
Digit Span or Spatial Span tasks will be comparable to their results on the task when it is traditionally
administered 2) the ERP components will reflect attention/memory processes and can determine when a
person has reached the capacity of their attention span/working memory and 3) the differential ERP
response pattern will be observable on an individual subject level.

authors_raw: Donald E. Mitchell, Guy Gingras and Peter C. Kind
title: Initial Recovery of Vision after Early Monocular Deprivation in Kittens is Faster When Both Eyes Are Open
abstract:
The mild effects of binocular as compared to monocular visual deprivation led to the widely held view
that the plasticity observed in the mammalian visual cortex during development reflects the operation of
competitive interactions between the two eyes for synaptic space. More recent evidence suggests that
inputs from the two eyes compete for limiting amounts of a retrograde molecule such as a neurotrophin,
and active neurons can compete more efficiently than less active neurons. Competitive mechanisms,
however, cannot explain the physiological and behavioural recovery in the deprived eye observed during
a subsequent period of binocular vision in cats. Here we show, in direct comparisons of the speed of
visual recovery in the deprived eye of kittens following a 6 day period of monocular deprivation, that
recovery is initially faster in the situation where both eyes are open (binocular recovery) than with
reverse lid-suture, where the animal is forced to employ the originally deprived eye. This state of affairs,
which lasted for 4 - 8 days, is difficult to reconcile with accounts of synaptic plasticity based upon
binocular competition but is consistent with homosynaptic mechanisms such as the model proposed by
Bienstock, Cooper and Munro (the BCM Model).

title: Exogenous orienting without awareness depends on attentional control settings
abstract:
Exogenous orienting, to a sudden-onset peripheral cue is thought to occur semi-automatically.
Typically, exogenous orienting exhibits a biphasic pattern: responses to targets are initially facilitated by
the cue but later they are inhibited (inhibition of return; IOR). Exogenous orienting to a cue is not fully
automatic because it depends on the cue and target sharing a particular quality (e.g., sudden-onset).
Thus, exogenous orienting is said to depend on the establishment of an attentional control setting.
McCormick (1997) demonstrated that peripheral cues presented below a threshold of awareness attract
attention and subsequently facilitate target processing. Interestingly, at late cue-target onset
asynchronies he did not observe IOR, even though this is a robust effect often observed with exogenous
cues. In the present study, masked and unmasked sudden-onset cues were presented under two
conditions. In the first condition, the task was to respond rapidly to the onset of a target. Although the
target task was the same in the second condition, the cue was detected and localized at the end of trial.
IOR was present with and without masks in the first condition, but facilitation was not observed when
the cues were masked. In the second condition, facilitation was observed for masked and unmasked
cues, but IOR was not observed with masked cues. The results suggest that assessing cue awareness
establishes a set for searching and this set gives rise to facilitation regardless of whether or not the cue
can be consciously localized.

title: Attachment Security, Maternal Sensitivity, and Children's Response to Pain: An Internal Working Model of Pain
abstract:
This study will examine the predictive validity of attachment security and pain behaviour following both
everyday pain incidents and acute pain incidents. The present study will be conducted with a cohort of
fifty, five-year-old children, followed since 6 months of age, and their mothers. The children's faces will
be videotaped during their 5-year medically indicated inoculation shot. Children's pain reaction will be
coded using the Children's Facial Coding Scale and parent-child interactions will be assessed with the
Child Adult Medical Procedure Interaction Scale. Following the needle, both mothers and children will
provide ratings of subjective pain intensity and pain behaviour. Mothers will also complete diaries
following four everyday pain incidents that their child experiences. Included in the diary is information
concerning the child's reaction and subsequent parental response. Quality of attachment will be assessed
using a Separation-Reunion procedure, the Separation Anxiety Test, and the Pain and Relationship Task.
It is hypothesized that attachment security classification will predict children's response to and
behaviour following painful incidents. The mediating effect of maternal sensitivity, initially collected at
both 6 and 18 months of age, will be also examined. Multimodal procedures will also be used to
examine the role of third variables that may account for these potential relations. These will include
receptive language, emotional regulation, and emotional awareness.
Tl 6. Good genes, fluctuating asymmetry and human jealousy
Will Brown and Chris Moore
Ancestrally extrapair copulations (EPC) likely exerted adaptive pressures on hominids. One adaptive
solution to reducing the probability of EPC is jealous emotions. Jealousy may be an adaptive strategy
designed to retain one's mate. If jealousy was an ancestral mate retention tactic, then individuals more
likely to be philandered upon should be more jealous. A predictor of likelihood to be philandered upon
may be developmental instability. Developmental stability (DS) is a heritable indicator of an organism's
ability to buffer environmental and genetic perturbances. DS is measured indirectly via fluctuating
asymmetry (FA). FA is the deviation from perfect bilateral symmetry in normally bilateral traits. The
less deviation from perfect symmetry is believed to be an intrinsically unfalsifiable indicator of 'good
genes.' As predicted, more asymmetric individuals (as measured by digital calipers with .0lmm
accuracy) were more jealous. FA accounted for substantial proportion of variance in self-reported
jealousy. Findings are consistent with jealousy being a condition-dependent mate retention tactic in
humans.
Tl 7. Listening for chaos amid order, or order amid chaos
Susan Boehnke, Susan Hall, Torsten Marquardt, Dennis Phillips
The listening brain generates auditory images with a location by examining the correlation between the
sound waveforms that reach the two ears. If the sounds reaching the two ears are perfectly correlated
(+ORDER) we perceive a compact sound image with a distinct location. If the two ears receive
independent uncorrelated sounds (CHAOS), the perception is spatially diffuse and has no "location",
like the noise of wind rustling leaves in a forest. We can digitally generate another, unnatural, type of
perfectly correlated signal in which the signal phase at one ear is perfectly opposite to that of the signal
reaching the other ear (-ORDER). This is perceptually somewhat compact, but doesn't have an easily
specifiable "location". We have been examining the shortest detectable duration of each of these sounds
when they occur between markers of another type: What is the shortest duration of ORDER you can
detect in an ongoing stream of CHAOS, or of CHAOS in ORDER. It is much easier to detect the latter.
Tl 8. Dieting and body dissatisfaction: An epidemiological survey of health behaviours in Canadian
adolescents
Frank Elgar, Christine Clancy, Alan J.C. King
Identifying socio-demographic trends and psychological and behavioural correlates of dieting and body
dissatisfaction (BD) in adolescents helps to identify those at greater risk of developing eating disorders.
Analyses were performed on data collected from the Health Behaviours in School-Aged Children study
in which 11,272 Canadian adolescents from grades 6 through 10 participated. Both BD and dieting were
most prevalent among older and female adolescents, and BD increased the likelihood of dieting
threefold. Other factors that increased the likelihood of dieting were depressed mood and low self-
confidence. Adolescents who socialized more with peers, smoked more and consumed more alcohol
were more likely to be dieting, suggesting peer influences on eating behaviours are pervasive during
adolescence. Also, breakfast skipping was more closely related to BD and dieting than to family
income, suggesting school lunch programs targeted to adolescents from low-income families should
inform all adolescents about the risks associated with unnecessary dieting.

title: "Matt Stone looks just ravishing in his pink dress" Irony in computer-mediated conversations.
abstract:
The text-based conversations that occur in email and internet chat environments eliminate many of the
non-verbal signals, such as facial expressions and tone of voice, that we rely on to interpret a speaker's
intent. The present study assessed the impact of computer-mediated communication (CMC) on the use
of ironic forms (e.g., irony, hyperbole, understatement, rhetorical questions) in conversations between
strangers. Given the reduction in the cues available in CMC to signal irony, we expected less use of
irony in this impoverished environment than in face-to-face conversations. Comparison of 40
conversations across these two communicative environments indicated that, contrary to initial
expectations, substantially more ironic language was produced in the CMC condition. This general
finding has several implications for current theoretical accounts of the comprehension and production of
verbal irony.

title: Neurobiology of sleep deprivation effects on mood in major depressive disorder
abstract:
The goal of this pilot study is to assess the neurotransmitter changes in the brain that correlate with the
improvement of mood in depression. Sleep deprivation therapy has consistently shown an acute,
although transient, improvement of depressive symptoms in more than half of patients suffering from
major depression. A test-retest magnetic resonance spectroscopy will be performed before and after half
a night of sleep deprivation in a sample of female patients (n=12) in their twenties, suffering from major
unipolar depression. We will investigate the medial prefrontal cortex, which was already identified by
brain imaging studies as showing metabolic differences before and after sleep deprivation in responders,
and the limbic structures, which have been shown to be involved in emotional expression. We expect the
glutamatergic peak on the MRS spectra to change in association with changes in mood.

authors_raw: Catherine A. Forestell and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Using Taste Reactivity to Measure Shifts in the Palatability of Conditioned Taste Cues
abstract:
Tastes become more preferred after they have been paired with sugar reinforcement in a classical
conditioning paradigm. The mechanism involved in the formation of conditioned taste preferences is
not clear. It is possible that conditioned preferences occur because animals learn to expect calories upon
consumption of the taste cue. Alternatively, the conditioning procedure may produce an increase in the
palatability of the taste cue. Typically the taste reactivity test is used to assess the palatability of tastes.
This test involves measuring a rat's hedonic and aversive affective responses to oral infusions of taste
solutions. In this talk I will describe some of the taste reactivity experiments currently being conducted
in our laboratory to assess palatability shifts in conditioned taste cues.

title: Challenges of running research in a large laboratory class: An animal experiment where the researchers outnumber the animals by 30 to 1
abstract:
As part of the continuing evolution of the introductory psychology class (P 1001) into a laboratory class,
this past year an animal research project was incorporated into the curriculum. Eighteen mice were
assigned to either of two groups. Mice in the experimental group received caffeine in their drinking
water. The mice were then observed in an open field apparatus, and experimenters recorded several
behaviours. With over 600 potential experimenters, the logistics were challenging. In this talk, we
describe how live observations were combined with additional observations recorded on digital video
and presented over the internet. The preparations were not trivial, but the outcome was a success in
several ways.

title: Construction and Initial Validation of a Daily Version of the Fatigue Impact Scale (D-FIS)
abstract:
Introduction: The Fatigue Impact Scale (FIS) has been developed as a symptom-specific profile
measure of health-related quality of life (HRQoL). Its item content reflects subjects' attribution of
problems to fatigue in the areas of physicaCcognftive, and psychosocial functioning. The FIS was
initially developed for cross-sectional studies of medical conditions in which fatigue is a chronic
symptom. Thus, it had not been designed to be a responsive measure of daily changes in fatigue. The
present study describes the development and initial validation study of an adaptation of the FIS for use
in conditions in which fatigue symptoms are of acute onset or have significant daily variation.
Methods: Items for the Daily F_MigueImpact Scale (D-FIS) were selected from the pool of original FIS
items with the aid of Rasclianalyses of existing data. The reduced, 18-item FIS was administered to a
sample of 93 subjects with flu-like illness, some of whom were followed for a 21-day period. Rasch
analyses were used to further reduce the scale to a minimum number of items that represented a
unidimensional measure of self-reported fatigue impact.
Results: The resulting 8-item D-FIS demonstrated good relations to ratings of other flu symptoms and
to general ratings of HRQoL. It also proved to be a responsive measure, demonstrating significant
reduction in fatigue impact for those subjects that were followed longitudinally. Moreover, subjective
ratings of fatigue impact were associated with work hours lost and estimated work productivity for those
subjects who did work for pay during the study period.
Conclusions: This initial validation study indicates that the D-FIS has considerable promise as a
measure of the subjective daily experience of fatigue and as a specific HRQoL outcome measure.

authors_raw: John Fisk, Murray Brown, Luanne Metz and T.J. Murray
title: The Concordance Between Health-Related Quality of Life Utility Measures and Neurologic Disability Ratings in Multiple Sclerosis
abstract:
Introduction: Clinical trials of disease-modifying agents for MS have focused on neurologic disability,
relapse rates, and MRI measures of central nervous system lesions. While the results have been
promising, study limitations, coupled with the high costs of treatments and the limited evaluations of
their cost-effectiveness, have lead to controversies about their use. The goal of this study was to
evaluate the relation between clinical ratings of neurologic disability and generic Health- Related
Quality of Life (HRQoL) utility measures in MS patients. Methods: A total of 195 patients with MS
(47 males, 148 females), representing the full range of neurologic disability (i.e. no neurologic signs to
bedridden) were recruited in a stratified (neurologic disability) and randomized manner from two
regional MS clinics. All subjects received a standardized clinical assessment and completed the
EuroQol (EQ5D) and the Health Utility Index (HUI) Mark II and Mark III. Results: The EQSD and
both versions of the HUI all demonstrated correlations > .60 with the clinical ratings of neurologic
disability. Analyses of variance demonstrated that the HUI Mark II and, to a greater extent the HUI
Mark III, were able to distinguish between clinically distinct subgroups of patients. A surprising finding
was the marked differences in the extent and rate of decline of the various HRQoL utility measures
across the full range of increasing neurologic disability. Conclusions: HR.QoL utility measures can be
appropriate outcome measures in clinical trials for MS. Differences in item content and in the valuation
methods are evident in how the subjective health states of a specific disease population are represented
by HR.QoL utilities. The selection of a HR.QoL utility measure can have a profound effect on the cost-
utility ratios in pharmacoeconomic analyses of MS treatments.

authors_raw: Rusak, B., Darvesh, S., Hopkins, D., Goguen, D., Fice, D., Kale, K., Reid, A. and de Groot
title: Patterns of Orexin Immunoreactivity in the Brains of Alzheimer's Disease Patients
abstract:
Qrexins (hypocretins) are a class of peptides originating in cells of the hypothalamus that are attracting
grearTnterest. Orexins appear to play a critical role in regulating arousal; for example, loss of either
orexins or their receptors have been implicated as a cause of narcolepsy in animals and humans. In
addition, orexins have an important impact on food intake in animal models. Patients with Alzheimer's
Disease (AD) often show clinical symptoms related to dysregulation of both sleep and body weight.
While neuronal cell loss occurs in many brain regions in AD patients, the regions responsible for
dysregulation of feeding and sleep are not known. We have conducted immunocytochemical studies of
selected regions of the brains of AD patients and age-matched controls to evaluate the number of orexin-
immunoreactive neurons and the extent and pattern of orexin fibre staining. Preliminary results will be
presented.

authors_raw: Sarah E. Boudreau and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Mousing around with sensory preconditioning
abstract:
The ability of mice to acquire a stimulus-stimulus association was tested with odor stimuli in a sensory
preconditioning paradigm, as a means of measuring their abilities to integrate separately learned
associations. Two distinct odor stimuli, rose and lemon, were presented in buried odor pots. On the first
day of training mice in the paired group received paired presentations of odor 1 (01; lemon) and odor 2
(02; rose). Mice in the unpaired group received randomized odor presentations 15 minutes apart. On
the following day, both groups received paired presentations of 02 and a sugar reward. On the third
day, both groups of mice received one trial of 02 and sugar followed immediately by trials of 01 alone.
If separate associations were learned and integrated, then those in the paired group should spend more
time digging in 01 on the final day then mice in the unpaired group. Results to be discussed.

title: Plasmid protection rackets Will Brown and Ford Doolittle
abstract:
Bacterial plasmids are self-replicating elements that exist within a cell but are not part of the
chromosome proper. When plasmids are mobile they can laterally transmit between phylogenetically
distant lineages (e.g. E. coli and S. enterica) and confer advantages to the host organism. For example
antibiotic resistance is probably a case of adaptive host evolution due to lateral transfer. However, why
do plasmids transmit themselves laterally? Are host benefits a sufficient explanation to explain the
presence of plasmids? Benefits to the host could be an epiphenomenon of benefits to the plasmid. It is
suggested that social evolution may favour multi-level 'conspiracies' between evolutionary units and
this potentially leads to conflicts within a given level of selection (e.g. plasmid 'protection rackets').
Despite the effectiveness of conspiratorial selection between evolutionary units, the threat of mutinous
counter-adaptations may exist in all social systems (e.g. meiotic drive, maternal-effect selfish genes or
postsegrational killing in plasmids). It seems that, in contrary to intuitive 'folk wisdom,' the brain is not
necessary for social evolutionary processes to operate.

authors_raw: D'Arcy, R.C.N., Connolly, J.F., Service, E., Hawco, C., & Houlihan
title: The influences of increased working memory load on semantic neural systems.
abstract:
The effects of WM on the semantic N400 response were studied using high-resolution ERPs. Subjects
studied visual sentence primes (The man is teaching in the classroom), anticipated the superordinate
terminal word (school), and then evaluated the semantic appropriateness of the visual target sentence
(The man is in the ...school/barn). Level 1 WM load contained a single prime sentence and Level 2
contained two prime sentences. The inappropriate words elicited the N400, but the latency of this
response was delayed significantly by ~50ms due to increased WM load. Subsequent brain mapping and
source analysis techniques showed the effects of WM on N400 generators.

authors_raw: Marleen H. M. de Groot & Benjamin Rusak
title: More on Marleen's Misbehaving Mice: Maximizing the Mating of Monogamous Mutants
abstract:
California mice (Peromyscus califomicus) are studied by Richard Brown's lab because of their unusual
pattern of bi-parental care of the young. We became interested in these mice because, unlike other
species we have studied, they show high levels of activity in their home cages in anticipation of the
onset of the daily light phase. Although the circadian systems of two species of Peromyscus mice have
previously been studied (P. leucopus and P. maniculatus; Daan & Pittendrigh, 1976), circadian rhythms
of California mice have not been described in the literature. Eighteen adult male California mice were
housed in individual cages inside isolation boxes that allow for manipulation of each animal's light
exposure. Activity rhythms were recorded under a variety of lighting conditions. Although most of
these mice behaved similarly to other Peromyscus species studied previously, a few animals showed
extremely unusual patterns. Efforts to further characterize these anomalous patterns, and to selectively
breed for unusual circadian characteristics will be discussed.

authors_raw: Devoulyte, K., Laskis, S., & Sullivan, M
title: Relation between catastrophizing, depression, and the intensity of cold and influenza symptoms
abstract:
The current study examined the role of catastrophizing and depression in predicting the intensity of cold
and influenza symptoms. Participantsincluded 49 adults (35 females, 14 males) who agreed to complete
a diary ofsymptoms for 7 days after the onset of a cold or the flu. Participant swere classified as
catastrophizers or noncatastrophizers based on their scores on the Pain Catastrophizing Scale (PSC).
Results showed that catastrophizers reported significantly more symptoms, and significantly more
severe symptoms than noncatastrophizers. The relation between catastrophizing and symptom severity
remained significant even when controlling for initial symptom severity, duration of onset of symptoms,
and depression. Catastrophizers also reported using more medication than noncatastrophizers.
Catastrophizers did not differ from noncatastrophizers on an objective measure of fever, nor did they
differ in the amount of school/work time lost due to symptoms. The theoretical and practical
implications of these results are discussed.

title: The Development of the Objective Self
abstract:
The present study explored the development of the objective self. This refers to the child's ability to
perceive the self from a third person perspective. Other measures of this ability such as the mirror
recognition task have been related to important developmental changes. It was hypothesised that this
ability develops in stages consistent with the development of other cognitive abilities. Two tasks,
thought to measure this ability, were created for this study. The first task assessed children's ability to
reason about their mass while the second task assessed children's ability to reason about their size.
Children in different age groups showed significant differences in performance on both tasks. The
results of this study suggest that a child's ability to represent the objective self changes considerably
over the preschool period.

authors_raw: Gll~ Gingras and Richard E. Brown
title: Procedures for testing the sensory abilities of inbred, transgenic and mutant mice: a review of the literature
abstract:
With the increasing number of strains of inbred, transgenic and mutant mice available, it is common to
use these mice in studies of cognitive functions. However, many strains of mice have sensory deficits
and it is important to test for sensory function before using these mice in tests of cognitive function. A
number of review papers have been written on the behavioral phenotypes of mutant mice, but
unfortunately, little information is available on the sensory capacities of these mice. Moreover, online
databases on mutant mice are rather broad and contain little information on behavioral abnormalities.
Because behavioral tests are dependent on sensory abilities, basic information on sensory abnormalities
in mutant mice is important for research in the neural and the behavioral biology of the mouse. The goal
of this presentation is to describe the methods used in testing the sensory functions (vision, hearing,
taste, smell, touch, and pain) of normal and mutant mice. To our knowledge, this is the first attempt to
create a comprehensive review of the sensory abilities of mice.

title: ACh is AChing for Attention
abstract:
Acetylcholine (ACh) is important because its release in the cortex is high during EEG "activation", it
enhances sensory input and its neurons are lost in Alzheimer's disease. However, we don't understand if
this cholinergic system functions by activating the entire cortex in preparation for significant inputs or if
separate cortical regions can be activated independently.
This is partially an anatomical question (do individual neurons branch to many regions or do
different cholinergic neurons go only to each region?) that will be examined using tract-tracing
techniques .
It will also be examined functionally by measuring ACh release from two cortical areas under
different sensory conditions-can ACh be released selectively from one region and not from another? or
does it increase everywhere regardless of the input? This will be studied in anesthetized animals using
visual and auditory stimuli and then in awake behaving animals trained to carry out difficult visual or
auditory discriminations.

authors_raw: Kazue Semba, Samuel Deurveilher and Joan Burns
title: How does the circadian clock tell the rest of the brain it's time to wake up?
abstract:
Daily cycles of sleep and waking characterize the lives of various species. In mammals, the master clock
for these circadian rhythms is located in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), whereas the neuronal
system controlling sleep and wakefulness is distributed throughout the brain. To understand how these
two systems are linked, we previously electrophysiologically characterized direct projections from the
SCN to the ventrolateral preoptic area (VLPO), a major site of sleep-active neurons (Sun, Whitefield,
Rusak and Semba, submitted). However, the most of the SCN output is thought to reach its targets
indirectly. In the present study, therefore, we are using a cocktail of anatomical tracers to identify
multisynaptic SCN pathways. The potential relay nuclei so far identified include the medial preoptic
area, dorsal hypothalamic area, and dorso- and ventromedial hypothalamic nuclei. These nuclei could
relay SCN output to the VLPO, and to known wake-active cell populations, including neurons
containing hypocretins/orexins, histamine, dopamine, acetylcholine, serotonin and noradrenaline in the
forebrain and brainstem.

title: Nonpredictive Gaze and 14-Month-Old Infants: Saccadic Responses to Static and Animated Cues Varying in Validity and SOA
abstract:
Reflexive orienting to nonpredictive gaze cues has been demonstrated in adults, young children, and
infants (Friesen & Kingstone, 1998; Hood, Willen, & Driver, 1998; Ristic, Friesen, & Kingstone, in
preparation). In the present study, as in the Ristic et al study, a schematic happy face was used to present
nonpredictive central gaze cues. Infants' saccadic responses to subsequent peripheral targets were
examined. Out of 37 infants tested, 25 (mean age: 14 months; 9 days) met the inclusion criteria of 8
usable trials. The design was mixed, with cue type (static or animated) as a between-subjects variable,
and cue validity (valid or invalid), target location (left or right), and stimulus onset asynchrony (200 ms,
or 1000 ms) as within-subjects variables. At the present stage of analysis, none of the anticipated effects
are significant. A follow-up study will use more interesting and age-appropriate stimuli to examine the
effects of nonpredictive gaze cues.

title: Effects of the Swiss Air Flight 111 disaster on community-level alcohol consumption: Post- traumatic drinking? Sherry H. Stewart and the "Leaming from Experience" Research Team
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The previous literature is controversial whether communities respond to disaster with
increased or decreased alcohol consumption. Inconsistencies may stem from the different time periods
following disaster over which communities are tracked. For example, decreased drinking may occur
during the initial response to disaster, when communities are mobilizing their resources to deal with the
trauma. Increased "post-traumatic drinking" may still occur during later stages of a community's
response to disaster, as some members attempt to cope with symptoms of post-traumatic stress. By
examining liquor sales over a wider time span, this study may help bring resolution to prior
inconsistencies. OBJECTIVE: To determine whether there are changes in alcohol sales in coastal
communities affected by the 1998 airline disaster in Peggy's Cove, Nova Scotia. DESIGN: Liquor sales
records are being examined in communities affected by the disaster and control communities for the
months and years prior to, during, and after the disaster. SETTING AND SUBJECTS: Liquor stores
serving coastal communities affected by the disaster are being compared with liquor stores serving
matched control communities. RESULTS: Analyses will be performed both within affected
communities over time, and between affected and control communities. We examine whether alcohol
sales increased or decreased in the period immediately following the disaster, whether there is sustained
increased sales over time, or a delayed increase in alcohol sales well after the disaster.
IMPLICATIONS: The results could help identify whether alcohol abuse prevention needs to be
provided for communities affected by large-scale disaster.

title: What does the human genome project mean for Psychology?
abstract:
The human genome was published in Science and Nature in February 2001. The mouse genome,
Drosophila genome, C. Elegans genome and others have also been published. It is predicted that
everything we know in pharmacology, medicine and human biology will have to be revised in light of
genetic knowledge. How will these breakthroughs in genetics impact on psychology? Will everything
in psychology have to change to reflect genetic knowledge? Do psychology students understand the
language and concepts of genetics? What new theories and techniques will be required for animal
research? Will genetics manipulations, gene chip technology and gene insertion by adenoviral vectors
become standard tools in the study of brain and behaviour? Can we keep up with the demand for
neurobiological and behavioural studies on transgenic and mutant mice? What will we have to learn
about genetics to facilitate human experimental and clinical research? Finally, how can psychologists
use bioinformatics and neuroinformatics, the handling and use of large genetics databases, in their
research? This talk examines some of the emerging knowledge in genetics that psychologists will need
to integrate into their teaching and research in order to benefit from the genetics revolution.

title: Human Patient Simulator: A proposed feasibility study
abstract:
The Human Patient Simulator (HPS) is a highly sophisticated computer based mannequin used to train a
wide range of medical professionals. The HPS is extremely diverse and can provide training in acute
crisis resource management, basic and advanced medical interventions and management, basic
pharmacology, and equipment usage. This unique training tool has been used extensively in the training
of medical personnel but it has been virtually ignored for research purposes. One of the difficulties that
arise in the use of the HPS for research is the lack of standardized scenarios. The proposed study
evaluating the performance of sleep deprived paramedics would allow us to evaluate the capabilities of
the simulator, develop standardized scenarios, and establish a research program using the HPS as a
research tool.

title: What matters in personal survival: Philosophical questions and empirical answers.
abstract:
What do people care about in personal survival? If they have to change and become different from what
they are now, what do they think is most important to maintain about themselves through change? Some
philosophers have suggested that what is important to maintain in survival is one's identity. But what
does identity mean for a person? Others say that what matters in survival is that one can identify with
the person that continues even if the person that one is now no longer exists. But who decides when a
'continuer' is no longer one's self? Rather than answer these philosophical questions the present
investigation looks into which aspects of self matter to people in survival: what they care most about
preserving in change, even in circumstances in which death is one the alternatives.

title: Homicide Perpetration as a Function of Psychopathy: The Instrumental-Reactive Continuum Explored.
abstract:
The purpose of the present study was to examine the characteristics of a large sample of Canadian
homicides as a function of psychopathy. Although previous studies have investigated the relationship
between psychopathy and criminal behaviour, little is known about the relationship between
psychopathy and homicide. One hundred and twenty-five (N = 125) Canadian offenders who had
perpetrated a criminal homicide were investigated for several characteristics regarding the nature of, and
possible motivations behind, their offence. Results indicated that homicides committed by psychopathic
offenders (as measured by the Psychopathy Checklist-Revised; Hare, 1991) were significantly more
instrumental (planned, goal-oriented) relative to non-psychopathic offenders who committed more
spontaneous "crimes of passion." In addition, psychopathic offenders were found to commit more
gratuitous, sadistic, and sexual violence in the content of their homicides than non-psychopathic
offenders. This research strongly contributes to the scientific understanding of homicidal violence as a
function of psychopathy, and could have important implications for homicide investigations.

title: Investigation of the Consistency of Traumatic Memories over Time as a Function of Interview Style
abstract:
In the legal system, it is often important to evaluate the credibility of memories for distant emotional
experiences. The purpose of the proposed study will be to examine prospectively the characteristics and
consistency of both traumatic and positive emotional memories as a function of interview style.
Although previous studies have investigated the qualitative aspects of emotional memories, little is
known about the consistency of traumatic memories over extended periods of time. The goals of the
current study are to contribute to the understanding of characteristics of traumatic memory, as well as
examine the extent to which these memories are consistent over time. It is expected that this research
will reveal important findings to further our understanding of how traumatic experiences are
