remembered, in addition to potential enhancing or biasing influences of various interview techniques.

authors_raw: Sean McCabe, Stephen Porter, Angela R. Birt, & Mike Woodworth
title: Who Can Catch a Liar? An Investigation of the Effect of Motivation and Feedback on Deception Detection
abstract:
Previous research indicates that most people are not overly proficient at detecting deception, typically
performing around the level of chance. However, a recent study suggested that the provision of
immediate and accurate feedback may gradually facilitate the accuracy at detecting deceptive reports
(Porter, Woodworth, & Birt, 2000). This apparent improvement in accuracy from the feedback could be
related to either: (1) an increase in the social pressure from the feedback to elevate the participants'
motivation to make accurate judgments, or (2) the feedback may have allowed the participants to
gradually adopt more successful decision-making strategies. The present experiment was designed to
examine the validity of these competing theories. Participants (N = 151) were randomly assigned to two
experimental conditions according to motivation (High versus Low) and feedback (Accurate, Inaccurate,
or No Feedback). They were shown a series of twelve videotaped speakers providing either a true or
deceptive report. Contrary to the major hypothesis, results indicated that the highly motivated
participants (those who anticipated a reward for performing well) performed significantly less accurately
than low motivated participants (who were not motivated by or aware of any possible reward). A
significant effect for any feedback (no difference for accurate versus inaccurate) was found for the
accuracy for the honest videos and a significant interaction between motivation and feedback was seen
in the accuracy for deceptive targets. Although results indicated a general motivational impairment
effect for the detection of deception, any type of feedback appears to counteract this impairment effect
and facilitate judgements on liars.

authors_raw: Paul Angelopoulos, Angela R. Birt, Stephen Porter, & Mary Ann Campbell
title: Are Left Handers Better Lie Catchers? An Investigation of Individual Differences and Accuracy in Deception Detection
abstract:
In a recent study (Porter, Stapleton, Campbell, & Birt, 2001), it was found that left-handed individuals
were able to detect deception at a higher level than right-handed individuals. The present study was
intended to replicate this result and explore possible reasons for the difference. Participants (N = 110)
viewed twelve videotaped targets (six liars, six truth-tellers). They were asked to judge the honesty of
the video targets. The videos were presented in one of three modalities: audio only, visual only and
audiovisual. Participants were asked to complete a number of questionnaires to explore possible
information processing styles associated with accuracy in detecting deception. It was hypothesized that
left-handed participants rely more on nonverbal information such as facial cues when making credibility
judgements, and therefore, would have higher accuracy levels in the visual only and audiovisual
conditions than right-handed participants. Overall, left-handers were 14.17% more accurate in judging
deception than were right handers. As hypothesized, left handers displayed this superiority effect in
both the visual and audiovisual conditions. No significant differences in accuracy scores between left
and right handers were found in the audio only condition. Analysis of self-reported cues upon which
detection judgements were based revealed a trend for left handers to rely primarily upon cues of a visual
nature. The findings are discussed in relation to possible differences in information processing
tendencies/styles between left and right handed individuals.

authors_raw: Lesley Spencer, Stephen Porter, & Angela R. Birt
title: "Bleeding Men and Parrot Ladies": Effect of emotionality of a scene on susceptibility to false memories
abstract:
Although some level of distortion is commonly present in eyewitness memory, the relation between the
emotionality of a witnessed scene and susceptibility to false memories is less clear. In the present
investigation, participants (N = 90) were randomly assigned to one of six conditions in a 2 (misled
versus non-misled) X 3 (positive, neutral, or negative emotional scene) between-subjects design.
Participants were informed that the study was focusing on "emotional processing" and were not aware
that their memory for the scene would be tested. After viewing a highly positive, neutral, or highly
negative scene (e.g., graphic accident) from the International Affective Picture System (Lang & Ohman,
1988), half of all participants were exposed to a series of misleading questions, one of which included a
dramatic false suggestion (e.g., a large animal in the scene that did not exist). Approximately one hour
later, after a series of filler tasks, participants were given ten direct questions about the scene (five
related to the misinformation provided earlier). Results indicated that the misleading questions had a
substantial impairing effect (37% difference) on recall accuracy. Overall, emotional valence did not
significantly influence susceptibility to memory distortion. However, it was related to false memories
for the dramatic misinformation in misled participants. Specifically, misled participants in the negative
emotion condition recalled seeing the dramatic misinformation significantly more often (80%) than
participants in either the positive (40%) or neutral emotion (40%) conditions. Further reflecting the
strong impact of misleading questions, no (0%) non-misled participants in any condition recalled seeing
the dramatic misinformation. Possible implications for eyewitness memory are discussed.

title: Young Offender Recidivism: Development and Validation of a Comprehensive Risk Scheme (A Doctoral Dissertation Proposal)
abstract:
Psychopathy is over-represented in criminal offender populations, is associated with persistent and
violent offending, and is considered an important component of adult risk assessment (Hare, 1998;
Hemphill, Templeman, Wong & Hare, 1998; Salekin, Rogers, & Sewell, 1996; Serin, 1995). Although
psychopathic traits have been identified in both children and adolescents (Forth, Hart & Hare, 1990;
Frick, O'Brien, Wooton, & McBurnett, 1994), the relevance of psychopathy to young offender
recidivism risk is less clear. The main objectives of the proposed research will be to: 1) determine the
base rate of psychopathy in a large sample of young offenders; 2) retrospectively identify the
psychosocial and criminal correlates of youth psychopathy in order to identify potential early markers in
its development; 3) evaluate the contribution of an adolescent measure of psychopathy to the prediction
of past and future criminal behaviour; 4) identify the criminal and psychosocial factors discriminating
persistent offenders high in psychopathic traits from non-persistent psychopathic youths; and 5) develop
a much needed comprehensive risk/need assessment instrument based on psychopathy and other factors
contributing to the prediction of re-offending in young offenders. To meet these objectives, the
psychosocial and criminal history data previously collected from 227 male and female young offenders
(see Santor and Kusumakar, 1998) will be used. The PCL-YV (Forth, Kosson, & Hare, in press) will be
scored from this information to measure psychopathy. Recidivism will be measured from the point of
participation in the original study to the present (3-4-year follow-up). Half of the sample will be used to
develop a risk/need measure, while the other half will be used in a cross-validation. Collectively, these
results will contribute to the understanding of psychopathy in its earlier stages and provide a useful tool
for the assessment of risk/need in young offenders.

authors_raw: Keni Purdy, Paddy McMullen and M. Freedman
title: Changes to the object recognition system in patients with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
abstract:
Do patients with Dementia of the Alzheimer's Type (DAT) show category-specific deficits in object
identification, and if so do they arise from semantic or visual damage? Basic (e.g., dog) and subordinate
(e.g., collie) level identifications require a large amount of visual processing, whereas superordinate
(e.g., animal) level identification requires additional semantic processing beyond the basic level. DAT
patients, normal elderly controls and college-aged controls completed an object verification task, where
they decided whether line-drawings of living and non-living objects matched names at superordinate,
basic or subordinate levels. Superordinate and subordinate identifications were equally difficult for DAT
patients. Subordinate identifications were most difficult for all controls. A category-specific deficit for
living objects was found with DAT patients, but only at the Superordinate level. This finding supports
the notion that some differences between living and non-living objects have a semantic basis. These
findings also characterize the nature of the semantic memory deficit in DAT.

authors_raw: Darryl Houghton, Lianne Stanford & Richard E. BrO\y_n
title: A comparison of HS mice in the Barnes Maze and the Morris Water Maze
abstract:
The Monis water maze, which is frequently used as a test of learning and memory in rodents has been
criticized as being a stressful paradigm in mice (Van der Staay, 2000: Physiol. Behav. 68, 599-602 &
Brooks et al. 2000: Soc. Neurosci Abst. 30, #841.3). This stress may confound learning and memory.
The Barnes maze (BM), a visual spatial task in which mice learn to escape from the center of a brightly-
lit platform into a dark cage through 1 of 16 holes on the periphery, may be a less stressful alternative to
the MWM. If these two tasks are accessing similar cognitive processes, measures of learning and
memory in the MWM should be highly correlated with those of the BM. To test this hypothesis, we ran
34 Heterozygous Stock (HS) mice (IBG, CO, USA) in both the MWM and the BM. Mice in the MWM
showed decreased escape latencies (EL) across days and increased EL on the first day of reversal. Mice
in the BM had decreased EL in acquisition, but no increase in EL on the first day of reversal. The
number of errors committed in the BM decreased over days and did increase on the first day of reversal.
Escape latency and errors within the BM correlated for all measures of learning (.45 < r < .92, p < .0001

title: 0072). Separately, each of these tasks has measured learning and memory. In comparing the two tasks, correlations for the escape latencies of the BM and MWM were calculated and all measures of learning [all days combined (r = .24, p < .1969), day - one acquisition (r
abstract: = = .13, p - = < .4568)) or memory (r .05) were not significant. There were also no significant correlations between the BM (errors) and the MWM (EL) for any measure of learning [all days combined (r = -.15, p < .4011), day-one acquisition (r = .01, p <.9545), all acquisition (r - = -.13, p < .4965), day-one reversal (r = = -.18, p < .3004), & last day of reversal (r = .04, p < .8229)) or memory (r .10 for errors). These data suggest the cognitive processing for these two tasks may be different, and when comparing data between these tasks caution should be exercised.

authors_raw: Boris Kablar and Michael A Rudnicki
title: Information provided by skeletal muscle and lower sensory/motor neurons influence neuronal fate in the developing mouse brain
abstract:
Fetuses that develop without Myf5 and MyoD genes entirely lack skeletal myogenesis and lose spinal
motor and proprioceptive neurons. To test whether extrinsic cues provided by the lacking structures
influence neuronal precursor cell differentiation in various brain areas involved in motor control we
assessed wild-type and mutant brains for structural and molecular differences. Anatomical milestones of
the mutant brain appeared unaffected. However, the expression pattern of various molecular markers for
neural progenitors and pyramidal neurons in the mutant motor cortex was downregulated. Consistently,
some areas of the mutant extrapyramidal tract were also affected. Surprisingly, we were unable to detect
any change in proliferation or cell death in the mutant neuroepithelium. Taken together, it appears that
patterning mechanisms intrinsic to the areas for motor control specify the basic organization of its
subdivisions. However, extrinsic cues seem to change intrinsic properties of neural progenitors and
influence the ratios of the cell types they produce.

authors_raw: Yannick Marchand, Ryan D'Arcy & John Connolly
title: Linking neuropsychological and neuropsychological assessments
abstract:
There is considerable potential for the use of event-related brain potentials (ERPs) for neurocognitive
evaluation in a clinical setting. The objective of the current study was to evaluate a group of left-sided
stroke patients with varying degrees of receptive language impairment using both neuropsychological
testing and ERP assessment. It was hypothesized that ERPs will assess accurately receptive language
function and correlate with the neuropsychological data. Data were collected from ten left-sided stroke
patients, who were undergoing rehabilitation at the time of testing. ERPs were recorded during the
computerized Peabody Picture Vocabulary Test-Revised (PPVT-R), which has graded levels of
vocabulary difficulty. In the computerized PPVT, a picture is presented followed by a digitized spoken
word that is either congruent or incongruent with the picture. The results provide a comprehensive
demonstration of the efficacy of ERP neurocognitive assessment in a stroke population.

authors_raw: Allen, G.V., Chase, T., Sisca, L. and Reynolds
title: Induction of Hsp27 in the cerebellum after focal cerebellar injury
abstract:
Heat shock protein induction is thought to play a role in neuroprotection after traumatic brain injury
(TBI) or ischemic insults. Induction of the 70 kDa heat shock protein has been described in Purkinje
cells following various types of brain injury. However, information regarding expression of the small
27 kDa heat shock protein (Hsp27) after focal cerebellar injury is lacking. In this study, we used a
weight drop model of brain injury in the anesthetized rat to identify tissue damage and Hsp27 induction.
Male, Sprague Dawley rats were anesthetized with isoflurane and a craniotomy (5.0 mm) was made over
the left paravermal cerebellar cortex using manual manipulation with an auger drill bit. The bone flap
was removed (dura intact) and brain injury was produced by dropping a 20-g brass weight (severe
TBI=15 cm height, 300g/cm, mild TVI=2.5 cm height, 50g/cm) down a plexiglas guide tube onto a
plexiglas piston (3.5 mm diameter). Immediately after trauma, the bone flap was replaced and sealed
with dental cement. Control animals were treated the same except that they did not received a weight
drop injury. The animals were killed at different time intervals after injury and brain sections were
processed for the immunohistochemical detection of Hsp27 or stained with thionin. Hsp27 is not
constitutively expressed in the cerebellum. Following mild or severe weight drop injury, Hsp 27
induction was observed in Purkinje cells and astrocyte-like cells located in the molecular and granular
cellular layers. Hsp27-positive astrocyte-like cells were prominent in widespread regions of the
cerebellar fiber tracts predominately ipsilaterally. Following severe TBI only, Hsp27 labeled neurons
and glial cells were observed in the deep cerebellar nuclei. Thionin staining showed prominent
chromatolysis in the Purkinje cell layer directly under the TBI site and gliotic scarring in the deep fiber
tracts ipsilateral to the injury. The data demonstrates that induction of Hsp27 occurs in both cerebellar
neuronal and neuroglial cell populations after traumatic cerebellar injury. The role of HSsp27 induction
in promoting cell survival or providing protection against subsequent injury needs to be determined.

authors_raw: Paddy McMullen, Amelia Hunt and Kerri Purdy
title: Local Attentional Deficits, Prosopagnosia and Category-Specific Agnosia for Living Objects
abstract:
M.B., a 41 year old prosopagnosic with category-specific agnosia for living objects, was tested 10 years
after her original testing session for her ability to 1) detect configural and element-based changes in
schematic faces and non-faces, 2) process at global and local attentional levels and 3) verify the identity
of living and non-living objects at different levels of categorization. In the face task, normal controls
were more sensitive to element changes in faces and non-faces than to configural changes. M.B. showed
equivalent and low sensitivity to both element and configural changes in faces. MB was much more
sensitive to changes in nonfaces, with a normal pattern of greater sensitivity to elemental changes. She
was also impaired at local processing of Navon-type figures in a divided attention task. Finally, normal
controls showed a living advantage and more efficient decisions to basic names than subordinate or
superordinate names when superordinate, basic or subordinate names of living and nonliving objects
were matched to pictures. M.B. showed very deficient processing of subordinate names in this task.
Interestingly, her category-specific deficit was not manifest 10 years after her original deficit was found.
M.B. has a visual impairment that manifests as a general face-processing problem that may be linked to
abnormal local attentional allocation. Her category-specific problems were likely of a more semantic
nature.

authors_raw: A.C. Davis, C. Dodd, N. Young, & E.G. Marchant
title: Long-Term Kindling in Mice as a Model for Interictal Emotionality
abstract:
Patients with temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE) not only suffer from recurrent spontaneous seizures but also
show disturbances in interictal (between seizures) emotionality, i.e. depression and anxiety. Long-term
kindling is an animal model that is used to study these increases in emotionality. Most of the research to
date on kindling-induced emotionality has been done with rats, and none has been done with mice. The
advantage of using mice in this model is that the use of transgenics may allow for a greater
understanding of the neural mechanisms underlying long-term kindling-induced emotionality. The
purpose of this study was two-fold: to determine the effects of kindling on emotionality in mice, and to
determine a technique that would allow for a maximum survival rate in mice for the duration of the
long-term stimulations. Mice were given either 30, 75 or sham stimulations, and were tested on the
open field and elevated plus maze. Significant differences between kindled and control mice were found
in time in the periphery of the open field, line crosses in five minutes in the open field and open arm
entries on the elevated plus maze. Also the survival rate of the mice for the duration of the experiment
was 78%. The results from this experiment suggest that kindling in mice does produce changes in
emotionality and that it is possible to deliver long-term stimulations to mice. These results suggest that
future studies using transgenics in the long-term kindling model may be possible.

authors_raw: Naomi Doucette, Angela R. Birt, Stephen Porter
title: "If I Didn't Know Better, I'd Think You Were Lying to Me... ": Influence of Pre-judgment Biases on Credibility Judgments"
abstract:
This study examined the effect of judgment bias on the accuracy in detecting deception. One hundred
and two participants judged the veracity of 12 video taped statements (6 honest and 6 deceptive) and
were asked to make ratings of credibility. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three
conditions: no bias, honesty bias, or deceptive bias. In the deceptive bias condition, participants were
instructed that it was quite possible that the speakers in the videos were attempting to be deceptive. By
contrast, participants in the honesty condition were instructed that the speakers were likely to be telling
the truth. Participants in the no bias condition were led to believe that the purpose of the study was to
simply judge the quality of memory reports and no mention of deception or honesty was made. Overall
accuracy in detecting deception was at chance level across the three conditions. However, the
instructional manipulation did bias judgments and the degree of bias depended on the nature of the
instructions. The participants in the no bias condition were significantly less accurate than those
participants asked to focus on either honesty or deception. The frequencies with which deceptive and
honest judgments were made also varied across the conditions. The implications of such pre-judgment
biases in the context of legal and forensic settings are discussed.
Changes to In-House Program
• Richard Brown will include a reminiscence about Vern Honig's life in the department during
his introduction to the In-House.
• Additional poster for Thursday, April 26, 2001:
Switch costs without task-switching? An inter-trial examination of the Simon effect
Jason Ivanoff & Amelia Hunt
In three experiments we examined whether switching the task-irrelevant mapping between
stimulus and response results in a task-switch cost. Contrary to theories of task-switching that
maintain that the switch cost is solely due to task-set re-configuration, a cost was observed.
• Ben Rusak will be the presenter for T25) Patterns of Orexin Immunoreactivity in the
Brains of Alzheimer's Disease Patients.
• John Barresi will be the presenter for T41) What matters in personal survival:
Philosophical questions and empirical answers.
• T22) Heather Schellinck, Ron Hoffman and John MacDonald will be switching times with
T37) Amy Carr.
• And last but not least, a typo on Friday, April 27, 2001. Lunch will be from 12:30 to 2:00 pm

title: Segregating the scene: the effectiveness of sound localization cues on the formation of auditory objects in time
abstract: The goal of the auditory system is to reconstruct sources in the acoustic scene and follow them through time, i.e. the formation of separate auditory 'streams' or objects (after Bregman, 1990). Differences in frequency, pitch, ear, or timbre can promote segregation of two interleaved sounds, but little is known of the effectiveness of the auditory cues for spatial location. In experiment l, subjects rated their perceptual experience of a repeating A_ B_A_ sequence of spectrally-identical noise bursts, where A differed from B in either ear of presentation, interaural level difference or interaural time difference (ITD). In experiment 2, detection thresholds were obtained for a temporal asymmetry in each type of ABA sequence, a task sensitive to stream formation. In both tasks, ITD was the least effective spatial cue for segregation, despite the fact that it is the dominant cue used for sound localization. The reason for this apparent mismatch will be discussed.

title: Dead Thesis Revival: Reforming Graduate Studies in Canadian Universities
abstract: In PhD programs in Canada, completion rates hover around 50%, less than 10% of students who do finish do so within scheduled residencies, and mean times-to-completion has nearly doubled during the last three decades from 6 to 11 years. Such inefficiency creates significant costs to universities and to students. Reform is needed to address low completion rates and lengthy times-to-completion. This presentation includes data from US and Canadian PhD programs and a discussion about the personal, social, financial and institutional factors that influence completion rates. A study is under way to examine the relation between institutional regulations and resources and completion rates in PhD programmes. Administrators of graduate studies from Canada, the United States and the United Kingdom will participate in a mail-in survey. Implications of the results and directions for further research will be discussed.

authors_raw: Catherine A. Forestell and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Palatability Shifts in Taste and Flavor Preference Conditioning
abstract: Although rats acquire strong conditioned preferences for tastes and flavors paired with glucose, it is not clear whether this occurs because of a shift in palatability to the CS or whether the CS evokes an expectancy of postingestive reinforcement in test. The present studies sought to determine whether preference conditioning enhanced CS palatability using a taste reactivity procedure. Our results indicate that ingestive responses to taste and flavor stimuli are enhanced as a result of differential preference conditioning.

authors_raw: Donald Mitchell, Peter Kind, Bashir Ahmned, Colin Blakemore, Tobias Bonhoffer and Frank Sengpiel
title: Correlated Binocular Activity Guides Recovery From Monocular Deprivation
abstract: The mild effects of binocular as compared to monocular visual deprivation led to the widely held view that the plasticity observed in the mammalian visual cortex during development reflects the operation of competitive interactions for synaptic space between inputs from the two eyes. However, during a subsequent period of binocular vision there is substantial behavioural and physiological recovery, leading to the suggestion that absolute activity levels, or some other non-competitive mechanism, determine recovery from MD. Here we demonstrate that correlated binocular input is essential for such recovery. Both behavioural and physiological (assessed by optical imaging) recovery is far less complete if the two eyes are surgically misaligned after a period of MD. This is a powerful demonstration of the importance of cooperative, associative mechanisms in the developing visual cortex.

title: Planned Investigations of the Opiate-Mediated Reward Mechanisms Involved in Pathological Gambling-Alcohol Use Disorder Co-Morbidity
abstract: Alcohol abuse and pathological gambling are commonly co-morbid, yet little is known about mechanisms underlying this co-occurrence. In a preliminary study (Stewart, Loba, Blackburn, Ellery, & Klein, in press), we examined heart rate (HR) responses to video lottery terminal (VL T) play and alcohol intake among a sample of regular VL T players. The results of this pilot study replicated previous findings that gambling and alcohol intake alone each result in increased HR. The results also provided novel information that the combination of VLT play and alcohol further intensifies HR increase relative to either activity alone. We have hypothesized that activity in the endogenous opiate system may underlie both pathological gambling and alcohol use disorders and may help explain their co-morbidity. We describe our plans for a set of three studies that will evaluate the possible role of endogenous opiates. The first is a replication and extension of our pilot study that controls for possible confounds (e.g., controls for movement artifact, use of placebo control). The second study will evaluate levels of beta endorphin release (via blood analyses) in response to VLT play, alcohol intake, and their combination in pathological and non-pathological gamblers, and will determine the degree to which betaendorphin correlates with HR acceleration to each activity. Finally, a third study will evaluate whether naltrexone (an opiate antagonist) blocks HR increase and positive mood changes to VL T play, alcohol intake, and their combination in a sample of pathological gamblers. Theoretical and clinical implications of this planned research will be discussed.

title: The impact of female reproduction on immunocompetence in the cricket
abstract: Animals may be selected to increase energy invested in reproduction at the expense of other physiological systems such as immunity. We examined whether a trade-off was evident between reproduction and immunity in the cricket, Gryllus texensis, by comparing the immunocompetence of mated females with the number of eggs they produced. Females will adjust egg output when presented with different egg-laying substrates. Immunocompetence was measured by: 1) phenoloxidase (PO) activity in the hemolymph; and 2) a functional assay analysing survival rates following infection with the bacterium Serratia marcescens. Contrary to prediction, we found no evidence for a difference in PO activity in the hemolymph of females laying significantly different numbers of eggs. Additionally, preliminary data suggest that females laying high numbers of eggs are, in fact, less likely to succumb to infection. These initial results suggest that while reproduction may be costly, these costs are not at the expense of immunity. Future studies will investigate a larger contrast in reproductive investment with virgin and mated females.

title: Einstein meets Lenin in Brodmann's area 39
abstract: This talk is based on my research after reading two books: Michael Paterniti (2000) Driving Mr. Albert: A trip across America with Einstein's Brain, and Tilman Spengler (1991) Lenin's Brain. When Albert Einstein died on 18 April 1955, he was considered the greatest genius of his time. His brain was removed for analysis by Dr. Thomas Harvey, who kept it in a jar in a wooden box in his lab until the first research paper was published in 1985 (Diamond et al., 1985. Exp. Neurol. 88, 198-204). Since that time there have been at least 8 papers, two books, and numerous newspaper and magazine articles written about Einstein's brain. When Lenin died in January 1924, he was considered the greatest of geniuses. His brain was removed and analyzed by Oskar Vogt, who was selected by a special advisory board of the Soviet government. Vogt was a student of Haeckel and had worked for a year in Paris with the Joseph and Marie Dejerine (one of the most famous medical couples since the Curies), and from 1901-1910, Vogt and his wife Cecile (another famous medical couple), supervised the neuroanatomical research of Korbinian Brodmann, whose famous monograph "Localization in the Cerebral Cortex" was published in 1909 [English translation 1994].These neuroanatomists are only some of those who have examined "famous brains" as medical curiosities. What has been learned about the neural basis of intelligence from this "cult of genius"? This talk will examine some of the history of neuroscience related to the neuroanatomical analysis of famous brains.

title: Using speed-accuracy trade-off functions to explore the effect of inhibition of return on information processing
abstract: In two tasks - a go/no-go task and a choice-RT task - we generated speed-accuracy tradeofffunctions (SATFs) to examine the timecourse of the influence of inhibition of return (IOR) upon target processing. The SATFs were created by plotting accuracy as a function of response time. With the go/no-go task, IOR was characterized by slower responses, an increase in criterion (C), and a slight increase in sensitivity (d') at a relatively early portion of target processing. In contrast, with the choice-RT task, IOR was characterized by slower responses at an early portion of target processing and reduced accuracy at a late portion of target processing. This pattern of results suggests that IOR increases the criterion to make a response and it reduces the sensitivity of target processing during the relatively late portion of target processing, but only with choice-RT tasks.

title: Fearful Asymmetry: Genomic Imprinting and Family Conflict
abstract: Conflicts of interest may occur between mother's and father's genes within offspring due to genomic imprinting (Haig, 2002). Genomic imprinting is the differential expression of a gene depending upon the parent-of-origin in which the gene was inherited. Specifically it appears that father's genes are predominantly expressed in the hypothalamus (involved in homeostasis)/limbic system while mother's genes are predominantly expressed in the neocortex (involved in flexible decision making). Haig (2000) and Trivers (2000) have both suggested that genomic imprinting could mediate conflicts over information transmission and reception within the brain. Keverne (2001) has further hypothesized that maternal genes may have been selected for the emancipation from endocrinological determinism mediated by paternal genes. It is a tantalizingly (albeit speculative) idea that the occurrence of genomic imprinting approximately 135 million years ago (MYA) in mammals may have indirectly facilitated coevolutionary conflicts between genes and social learning. This paper will outline some reasons to suspect that there are intragenomic conflicts over fear responses (e.g. fight or flight). Neuroscientists have located specific neurotransmitters in the brain that are involved in the suppression of agonistic responses. These transmitters and neural areas appear (in part) to be maternally derived.

authors_raw: Dennis Phillips, Andrew Stuart and Michael Carpenter
title: Our Biggest Headache: The Body's Smallest Muscle
abstract: The stapedius muscle of the middle ear holds the dubious distinction of being the smallest muscle in the human body. It contracts reflexively in response to loud sounds, and the result of the contraction is a directly measurable reduction in transmission of lowfrequency vibrations to the cochlea. Now, speech sounds are inherently broadband, and at high intensities, the high-frequency content of the sounds is prone to masking by the sound's low-frequency content. This is problematic because the high-frequency content of speech carries much information on phoneme identity, so an inability to perceive the high-frequency information is highly deleterious to speech perception. The stapedius reflex thus has the beneficial consequence that at high stimulus intensities, highfrequency information is less masked than it would otherwise be by concurrent lowfrequency content in the sound. The main behavioral evidence on this view comes from patients with Bell's palsy. Bell's palsy is thought to be an isolated pathology of the facial nerve (the nerve that innervates the stapedius). In those patients, word recognition at the affected ear(s) is often profoundly poorer at high stimulus intensities than it is at low intensities. The explanation offered for this is that the high-frequency content of the speech sounds is not afforded its usual protection from low-frequency masking when the stimulus amplitudes are high. The present talk will describe two new experiments on the stapedius reflex. These experiments provide independent, converging lines of evidence on the role of the stapedius reflex in human hearing.

title: The Impact of the Interview: A Comparison of Traumatic and Positive Memory Characteristics
abstract: In the legal system, it is often important to evaluate the credibility of memories for distant emotional experiences. The purpose ofthis study was to examine the characteristics of both traumatic and positive emotional memories as a function of interview style. Although previous studies have investigated the qualitative aspects of emotional memories, little is known about the influence of differential modes of recall on memory report characteristics and ratings of the psychological impact of the event. A comparison of four commonly used police and therapeutic interview styles (free narrative, cognitive interview, guided imagery, and written narrative) was conducted on both qualitative and quantitative characteristics of traumatic and positive memories reported in a community sample. Approximately 50 participants were interviewed at two time intervals. Further analyses were conducted on the consistency of traumatic and positive memories over time intervals of two to five months on a series of consistency questions and memory ratings (e.g., quality of memory, vividness/clarity). Results have important implications for understanding memory reports in the criminal justice system, and the variations in memory reporting that occurs as a function of various interview protocols.

title: Uncovering The Mechanisms Establishing and Maintaining Sex Differences in Brain
abstract: Rodents provide a useful model system in which to study sex differences and gonadal steroid action, at a behavioural and a cellular level. The importance of gonadal steroids during development for organizing sexually dimorphic neural circuitry is well established. However, until recently the molecular mechanisms underlying steroid action in developing brain were not known. We have recently elucidated a pivotal role for GABA in the developmental process of defining a male versus female brain. Once neural circuitry is sexually differentiated during development, gonadal steroids 'activate' this circuitry in adulthood resulting in behavioural sex differences. My current research focus is studying how stress responding differs between the sexes. This research has relevance for humans as the incidence of various stress-induced psychopathologies is higher in women than men. Using a natural model of stress, I have demonstrated that male and female rats differ in ethologically relevant behavioural responses and in neural activation (using c-fos protein induction as a marker) following the stressor. Future experiments will ( 1) examine sex differences in the effects of stress on cellular processes implicated in the development of stress-induced psychopathologies, and (2) investigate a causal role for gonadal steroids, both in adulthood and during development, in mediating sexually dimorphic responses to stress.

authors_raw: Sarah E. Boudreau and Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Mousing Around With Sensory Preconditioning: Part II
abstract: Little is known about the mouse's ability to associate neutral stimuli. Our research has focused on sensory preconditioning using the albino CD- I mouse. Training consisted of three phases. During Phase 1, mice were repeatedly presented with two pairs of odors. Odors were presented in small odor pots covered in bedding. Following Phase 1 exposure, one odor from one pair was paired with a buried sugar reward (S+), and one odor from the other pair was presented without sugar (S-). Mice learned to dig in the bedding more to S+ than to S-. Finally, in Phase 3, mice were given a choice between the two odors that had not been conditioned. If a mouse had indeed formed a bond between the two odors in a pair during Phase 1, then the associate of S+ should have been the preferred odor. In several experiments, the associate of S+ was not preferred, though in a subsequent test, S+ was preferred to S-. In an effort to explain our failure to produce sensory preconditioning, two more experiments were conducted looking at the possibility of configuring and spontaneous configuring of odor compounds in Phase 1. Preliminary results from these two studies will be discussed.

title: Excuse me, your face is interfering with my Inhibition of Return.
abstract: Is inhibition of Return (IOR) affected by the biological significance of the target? IOR refers to the relative slowing of reaction times when responding to a target appearing in a cued versus uncued location. As faces are typical targets of a visual search, facial images were used as cues and targets and presented either intact as faces or scrambled as nonfaces. In Experiment 1 participants were presented with simplistic line and circle images of faces and exhibited no evidence of IOR. Experiment 2 used realistic facial images and IOR occurred only when a face was the target. Experiments 1 and 2 were repeated using a simple circle as the cue, and IOR then occurred for both face and non-face targets. Overall, IOR was affected by the complexity of the cue, and the biological significance of the target was important only when the cue and target were complex.

title: There Is No Spatial Negative Priming
abstract: All eight negative priming theories are based on a particular pattern of data, and most necessarily require this pattern. The pattern derives from a congruency hypothesis, "if items are congruent in a sequence of events then performance will be improved, else if items are incongruent then performance will be impoverished." In examining other relationships, besides those usually used to test negative priming, it was found that negative priming for spatial locations is not what it was once believed to be. It behaves more like inhibition of return, which does not follow a congruency hypothesis. This is important given the number of"negative priming" experiments that have been performed using the localization paradigm to test inhibition in populations. A reanalysis of location negative priming experiments needs to be performed given because costs in location negative priming are due to stimulation and not special treatment of the distractor.

authors_raw: Marleen H. M. de Groot & Benjamin Rusak
title: Promiscuous (and Probably Perverted) Pairings of Peromyscus Produce a Plethora of Peculiar Progeny.
abstract: Light can affect daily rhythms by entraining the endogenous circadian pacemaker that normally drives these rhythms, as well as by directly affecting (masking) the overt expression of behaviour. A novel photopigment, melanopsin, is hypothesized to mediate both of these effects, as well as pupillary responses to light, all of which survive loss of classical photoreceptors in the retina. Last year, we described a California mouse (Peromyscus ca/ifornicus) expressing a normal circadian rhythm that failed to entrain to the light/dark cycle, but whose activity was completely masked by light exposure. We have recently established that this mouse responds to visual stimuli and shows normal pupillary constriction in response to light. This animal's phenotype appears to have a genetic basis, since the progeny of selective crosses of his descendants showed similar abnormalities. These mice are the first example of animals expressing apparently normal circadian rhythms that are not entrained by light, but that still show potent masking responses to light exposure.

title: Looking for BrdU-incorporated hippocampal cells in an animal model of depression.
abstract: Various theories exist regarding the aetiology of human depression. One theory of depression is that it is due to stress-induced decreases in hippocampal neurogenesis (Jacobs, Van Praag, & Gage, 2000). Support for this theory comes from observations of decreased hippocampal volume in individuals with depression and also from data showing that various antidepressant treatments in rodents increase hippocampal neurogenesis. The present experiment was, therefore, designed to examine hippocampal neurogenesis in animal model of depression. Olfactory bulbectomy (OBX) has been shown to cause depression-like symptoms and may occur through stress-mediated mechanisms. In the present experiment, adult male rats underwent OBX or sham surgery. Six weeks after surgery rats received 4 injections of BrdU ( a marker of mitotic activity; 50mg/kg, i.p.) and sacrificed 24 hrs after the last injection. Brains were processed using immunohistochemistry for BrdU-:incorporated cell identification. The results are discussed in relation to the neurogenesis theory of depression.

authors_raw: Cheryl Birch, Sherry Stewart, Shondalee Eisnor, & Jennifer Theakston
title: The Mood-Induced Activation of Alcohol Expectancies in Internally Motivated Drinkers
abstract: This study investigated whether Coping Motivated (CM) and Enhancement Motivated (EM) drinkers differ in their activation of alcohol-related cognitions following exposure to musical mood induction procedures (MMIP). A total of 86 undergraduates who had elevated screening scores on either the CM or EM subscale of the Drinking Motives Questionnaire (DMQ-R; Cooper, 1994) participated in this experiment. Half the people in each motive group were randomly assigned to a positive mood condition and the other half to a negative mood condition. The Alcohol Craving Questionnaire (ACQ; Singleton, Tiffany & Henningfield, 1994) was administered at baseline and following the MMIP to assess phasic changes in alcohol-related cognitions. After an ANCOV A was used to adjust for motive-group differences in baseline mood state, it was evident that the MMIP's induced the intended target moods for all groups. The mood induction operated specifically to trigger different alcohol expectancies for CM and EM drinkers. Consistent with hypotheses, planned comparisons revealed that only CM drinkers in the negative (but not the positive) mood condition reported significant increases in expectancies that alcohol would relieve negative mood states. Also consistent with hypotheses, only EM drinkers in the positive (but not the negative) mood condition reported increases in beliefs that alcohol would enhance positive affective outcomes. Theoretical and clinical implications will be discussed.

authors_raw: Sherry Stewart, Martin Zack, Pamela Loba, & Ray Klein
title: In Win, Lose, and Booze: Implicit Gambling Outcome - Alcohol Associations In Problem Gamblers
abstract: Although prior research shows that most gamblers drink alcohol while gambling, the functional relationship between particular gambling events (e.g., wins, losses) and alcohol use remains unknown. This study examined priming of alcohol cognition by gambling gain and loss events among gamblers with differing histories of drinking in response to these events. A stronger tendency to drink following a particular gambling outcome (win, loss) was predicted to coincide with a stronger association between that outcome and alcohol in memory, resulting in greater priming on an implicit association task (!AT). Participants were community-recruited problem gamblers. On the IAT, gain (e.g., winnings) and loss (e.g., bankrupt) words were paired with alcohol (e.g., vodka) or non-alcohol (e.g., lemonade) words and participants made a category judgment (e.g., alcohol vs. non-alcohol) to each word by pressing one of two response keys. Participants also reported how likely they were to drink when they won and lost at gambling. Preliminary analyses indicate that history of loss-based drinking did not influence priming of alcohol concepts by exposure to loss cues on the !AT. However, the predicted association was observed between gain events and alcohol in those who reported a history of win-based drinking.

authors_raw: Laura P.R. Reynolds and Gary V. Allen
title: Effect oflnsulin-like Growth Factor-1 on Functional Recovery Following Cerebellar Injury.
abstract: Faculty of Medicine, Dalhousie University Introduction: Insulin-like growth factor (IGF-1) acts as a non-selective neurotrophic agent in the adult brain in response to injury. IGF-1 has been shown to mediate levels of Calbindin-28kD in Purkinje cells and promote climbing fiber re-innervation. Our aim was to determine whether administration of exogenous IGF-1 following severe cerebellar injury would enhance recovery of motor function. Methods: A weight drop model of brain injury was used to induce severe damage to the left paravermal cerebellar cortex in the anesthetized adult rat. Treatment animals (n=6) received 3 series of direct IGF-1 injections (l.25mg/kg) 2 mm deep into the cerebellar cortex immediately before injury and 24 hour and 48 hours post-injury. Control animals (n=6) received severe injury followed by a series of PBS injections (0.05 M, pH=7.4) into the cerebellar cortex. Sham animals (n=4) received craniotomy without injury or injection. Animals were tested for motor function using the beam-walking test. Results: Motor recovery rates were significantly enhanced in treated animals compared to controls (p < 0.01). Animals that received IGF-1 injections approached near recovery levels by day 15. In contrast, the motor function of control animals failed to reach near recovery levels by day 29. Conclusions: These findings suggest that post-injury treatment with IGF-1 is an effective means of enhancing motor function following severe cerebellar injury. Analysis of brain sections for the immunohistochemical detection of Hsp27, Calbindin-28kD, Parvalbumin and Glycogen Synthase Kinase 3~ are currently in progress to reveal the role ofIGF-1 in cell survival and to determine the intracellular mechanism of action of IGF-1. (Supported by Nova Scotia Health Research Foundation)

title: Differential Effects of Kindling Different Brain Regions on Kindling-Induced
abstract: AC Davis, V Thome, A Gregus, AJ Wintink and LE Kalynchuk. Amygdala kindling dramatically increases emotionality in rats and is considered a good model of the behavioural changes associated with temporal lobe epilepsy. Less well studied have been the effects of kindling other brain regions on changes in emotionality. One study has shown that kindling the dorsal hippocampus causes increased emotionality whereas caudate kindling does not (Kalynchuk et al, 1999). The purpose of this study was to further study the effects of kindling both limbic and non-limbic regions on kindling-induced emotionality. Rats were given either 99 kindling or sham stimulations of the left amygdala, caudate, claustrum, bed nucleus of the stria terminal is (BNST), or perirhinal cortex. After kindling the rats were tested on the open field, elevated plus maze, and forced swim test. Amygdala-, BNST- and perirhinal-kindled rats showed increases in emotionality on all tests compared to controls. In contrast, caudate and claustrum kindling did not significantly increase emotionality. These results demonstrate that kindling-induced emotionality is only produced by kindling of specific brain regions.

authors_raw: injections, vehicle injections, restraint stress, or handling every
title: Effects of Repeated Corticosterone and Restraint Stress on Anxiety and Depressionlike Behavior in Male Rats
abstract:
Gregus A*, Davis, AD, Wintink, A J, Kalynchuk, LE Repeated stress increases glucocorticoids and is associated with the development of affective psychopathology in humans and maladaptive behavioral changes in rats. It is well established that stress-induced corticosterone (CORT) impairs cognitive functioning in rats; however, little is known about its effects on affective behavior. Accordingly, the present study examined the effects of two repeated stressors, restraint and CORT injections, on changes in emotional behavior in male rats. Rats received either CORT
day for 21 days prior to and during behavioral testing. Starting on day 22, animals were tested for changes in fearful/anxious behavior in the open field, and the elevated plus maze, and for symptoms of depression in the forced swim test and sucrose consumption test. Results revealed that neither CORT nor restraint altered motor or anxiety-related behavior the open field or the elevated plus maze. However, CORT increased the frequency and time spent immobile in the forced swim test compared to vehicle injected controls. Additionally, CORT significantly decreased the proportion of sucrose-to-water solution consumed compared to vehicle injected rats in the sucrose consumption test. Restraint did not alter behaviors in the forced swim or sucrose consumption tests. These results suggest that repeated CORT injections produce behaviors associated with depression and that neither CORT nor restraint increase anxiety-like behavior in male rats.

authors_raw: Bernier, D., Marchand, Y., Rahey, S., Striemer, C., Young, G. B., & Connolly, J. F
title: Evoked Potentials In Coma Populations
abstract: A variety of electrophysiological protocols will be used to evaluate the functional state of cortical and subcortical structures of patients in coma following traumatic head injury. The population to be investigated will be patients between 18-50 years of age. Testing will start as early as possible after the injury and will be repeated on a regular basis for a period of six months. The battery of assessments includes Somatosensory Evoked Potentials, Brain Stem Auditory Evoked Potentials, as well as Event-Related Potentials associated with pre-conscious attention mechanisms(MMN) and higher level semantic functions (N400). The ultimate goal is to investigate which measures will reliably predict return to consciousness, and to investigate cognitive function in coma and at emergence.

title: Effect Of Postnatal Handling On The Emotional Behaviour Of Adult AmygdalaKindles Rats
abstract: Long-term amygdala kindling in adult rats produces high levels of anxiety, and is often used as an animal model for studying the emotionality associated with human temporal lobe epilepsy. Brief handling in early-life decreases anxiety and the behavioural responses to stressors encountered in adulthood. The present study examined whether postnatal handling would reduce the development of emotionality in adult amygdalakindled rats. Male rats were handled for the first 14 days of life or left undisturbed. At adulthood, rats were either left undisturbed in their home cage or received sham stimulations, or 10, 25 or 60 amygdalakindling stimulations and were tested for changes in anxiety and depression. Kindling produced increases in emotional behaviour, consistent with previously published findings. However, postnatal handling had no effect on kindling-induced emotionality, suggesting that variations in early environmental experience do not play a role in the development of epilepsy-induced emotionality.

authors_raw: Jeremy A. Murphy and Kazue Semba
title: Caffeine-induced activation of orexin neurons
abstract: Caffeine is the most commonly used psychostimulant. Orexins are novel neuropeptides, found in posterior hypothalamic neurons, which are suggested to participate in normal wakefulness, feeding as well as in the neuropathology of the sleep disorder narcolepsy. We investigated whether stimulant doses of caffeine activate orexin-containing neurons in rats, using c-Fos as a neuronal activation marker. We confirmed that caffeine increased locomotion at low doses (10 and 30 mg/kg), while inhibiting locomotion at a high dose (75 mg/kg); saline had no effect. In the same animals, all doses of caffeine increased the number of c- Fos expressing, orexin neurons by approximately 200%, whereas a similar degree of activation of non-orexin neurons occurred only after the highest dose. These results indicate that orexinergic neurons are selectively activated by stimulant doses of caffeine, and suggest that the activation of orexin neurons, either directly or indirectly, play a role in mediating behavioural activation by caffeine.

title: Evidence for Neural Plasticity Following Reading Remediation Training
abstract: The present study recorded ERPs as participants, with and without phonological processing deficits performed a modified phoneme deletion task. Participants in the phonologically impaired (Pl) group were enrolled in a reading remediation program (Spell Read) and were tested prior to receiving instruction and at specified time points during the program (i.e., 20 hours, 40 hours, completion of program). Participants heard instructions asking them to omit the initial phoneme from a monosyllabic word (e.g., Clap without the /Cl sound). This was followed (ISi = 2 sec) by presentation of the correct (e.g., Lap) or incorrect answer (e.g., cap). In the control group, two ERP components were elicited: the Phonological Mismatch Negativity (PMN), a component linked to phonological analysis was largest to incorrect items, and the P300, a component associated with memory recognition was largest to correct items. In the PI group, the PMN and P300 responses were atypical (i.e., small, absent, delayed) prior to receiving reading instruction. However, after only 20 hours of training, both the PMN and P300 responses began to normalize relative to the control group. These results have been observed in both children and adults indicating that changes observed in children are not solely the result of ongoing neural development.

title: "Eight Decades of Measurement in Depression"
abstract: Since 1930, some 400 instruments and measures have been developed to assess and diagnose depression. These measures differ in content, response format and in objectives. Although there are good reasons for constructing different measures of depression, the large number of often diverse measures created over the past 80 years creates its own challenge to one of the main goals of measurement, namely to standardize assessment and streamline decision-making. The proposed talk reviews some of the many important psychometric techniques and substantive developments that have impinged directly on these measurement goals, including (a) the need for impossibility equating different scales, (b) item content heterogeneity, and (c) potential difficulties associated with the use of principal components analysis for developing short forms. Many of these challenges can be resolved through the use of techniques based on item response models.

authors_raw: Bernier, D., MacMaster, F., Gates, L., Kusumakar, V., Rusak, B
title: Sleep Deprivation Effects on Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy and Mood in Major Depressive Disorder
abstract: A variety of studies using different methods of imaging brain structure and function have reported changes associated with major depressive disorder (MOD). PET and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) studies have reported decreased metabolic activity in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (PFC), concurrently with increased activity in the ventrolateral PFC. Differences in metabolic activity have also been reported in the anterior cingulate, the temporal lobe, and some subcortical structures, including the hippocampus and parahippocampus. Many of these abnormalities are reversed after successful antidepressant treatments. Pharmacological treatments generally take weeks to achieve their effects, making it difficult to identify the steps leading to these changes. Partial overnight sleep deprivation has been shown consistently to produce transient but very substantial mood improvement in more than 50% of patients suffering from MOD. This provides an opportunity to study brain changes within hours of an effective treatment. Single-proton, magnetic resonance spectroscopy (H-MRS) allows detection of some neurochemical changes in targeted brain regions that are not detected by other functional brain imaging methods. We have been using H-MRS to investigate the effects on features of the MR spectra that represent levels of choline and levels of glutamate/glutamine/GABA in several brain regions after sleep-deprivation induced mood improvement. Preliminary results demonstrating rapid changes in brain neurochemistry associated with mood improvement will be presented.

authors_raw: J. Klages, J. Ingles & G. Eskes
title: A Case Study of the Effects of Neglect on Reading
abstract: SS is a 66 year-old white male who had a right hemisphere infarct affecting the basal ganglia, posterior temporal and occipital lobes, associated with impaired left-sided sensory and motor function and left hemi-spatial neglect. Clinical neuropsychological screening with the Cognistat on admission to a Rehabilitation service showed no evidence of dementia and severe left-sided neglect on the Behavioural Inattention Test. Further testing of his neglect revealed evidence for neglect based on sensory/perceptual rather than motor mechanisms, and object-centered left-sided errors or omissions, suggesting a representational deficit. Neglect dyslexia was also seen in his reading errors and omissions on the left side of single words. Interestingly, his reading errors were significantly higher for regular words (13/30) and non-words (14/30), then for matched irregular words (2/30), suggesting different forms of lexical representations and/or processing as revealed by the differential effects of neglect. To our knowledge, this difference between regular and irregular words has not been previously reported in the literature and will be valuable in developing a fuller understanding of normal reading processes as well as lexical neglect.

authors_raw: J. A. Theakston, S. H. Stewart, M. Dawson, D. Lehman, S. Knowlden
title: What does your personality say about your reasons for drinking?
abstract: The present study sought to replicate and extend research on the relationships between five-factor personality models and motivations for drinking alcohol. Undergraduate students (n = 571) completed the Drinking Motives Questionnaire-Revised (Cooper, 1994) and the 100-item International Personality Item Pool questionnaire (Goldberg, 1999) measuring the Big-Five personality domains. Multiple regression analyses revealed that personality domains predicted both external (Conformity and Social), and particularly, internal (Coping and Enhancement) drinking motives after accounting for demographics and alternate drinking motives. Replicating previous findings with the NEO personality scales, Coping motives were predicted by low Emotional Stability, and Enhancement motives were predicted by high Extraversion and low Conscientiousness. High Intellect/Openness and low Agreeableness also predicted Enhancement motives, and low Extraversion predicted Coping motives. High Emotional Stability predicted Conformity and high Agreeableness predicted Social motives. Low Intellect/Openness also predicted both external motives. Thus, converging evidence suggests the presence of personality risk factors associated with risky reasons for drinking.

title: An Investigation of the Ability of Individuals to Detect Deception in Criminal
abstract: Because nearly all studies examining the detection of deceit have focused on university student samples, the generalizability of the findings to forensic settings is questionable. Given the importance of detecting deception in forensic settings, the present study examined how well people were able to detect deception in a sample of incarcerated criminal offenders. Undergraduate students (N = 103) viewed a series of eight videotaped segments in which offenders related truthful or deceptive autobiographical events of a positive or negative valence. The results illustrated that, contrary to the main hypothesis, the participants were able to make accurate judgments about the veracity of the statements at a level that was significantly above chance (M = 66%) and higher than that seen in previous studies with non-offenders. In addition, it was found that participants were most accurate at judging positive deceptive statements (76%).

title: Strain differences in locomotion, anxiety and spatial learning and memory performance between C57BL/6J and 129S3 mice.
abstract: This study examined strain and sex differences in locomotion, anxiety and spatial learning and memory in male and female C57BL/6J and 129S 1 mice using a behavioral test battery. The elevated zero and elevated plus maze, light-dark box test and open field indicated that the C57BL/6J mice had higher levels of locomotor and exploratory behavior that the 129S1 mice, while the 129S1 mice demonstrated higher levels of anxiety than the C57BL/6J mice. The Barnes maze and Morris water maze were used as tests of spatial learning abilities. In the Morris water maze, there were no strain or sex differences in latency to or distances to reach the platform, however the C57BL/6J swam substantially faster than the 129S1 mice. During the probe trial, both C57BL/6J and 129S1 strains displayed more time spent in the correct quadrant, demonstrating that learning had occurred, though there was no strain or sex difference noted. Strain and sex differences in both neuromuscular function were shown as the C57BL/6J mice remained on the rotating rod significantly longer than did the 129S 1 mice, while the females performed better than the males. This study indicated that the 129S1 strain exhibit greater levels of anxiety than C57BL/6J however can perform equally well as the C57BL/6J mice under appropriate test conditions as shown in motor and spatial learning. Nonetheless, more general conclusions on the abilities of the 129S1 strain cannot be drawn, since variability between the 129 substrains exists and only one was tested here.

authors_raw: Jill E. Hatchette, MSc, Patrick J. McGrath, PhD & Michael Murray, PhD
title: Pain and pain management: Attitudes and practices of adolescents.
abstract: Objective: To describe adolescents' attitudes toward pain and pain management strategies, knowledge of over-the-counter analgesics (OTCs) as well as the social influences and gender differences apparent in pain management practices, attitudes and knowledge. Design: Focus groups. Setting: Junior high school and the meeting room of a local mall. Main Outcome Measures: Demographic data collected from participants via questionnaire prior to focus group discussions. Content analyses of transcripts to interpret statements within the context of categories formulated a priori from existing literature. Participants: Junior high school adolescents (grades 7, 8 and 9) participated in the sessions. Results: Adolescents' attitudes toward OTCs ranged from negative (e.g. unnatural, over-used, largely placebo) to positive (e.g. a preference to be pain free). Females indicated independence in self-administering OTCs - a gender difference not attributable to onset of menstruation -both male and female adolescents cited sports injuries as their most frequent pain-related complaint. With respect to knowledge adolescents expressed importance of reading the label, but also based dosing on irrelevant factors like perceived effect, size of pill and age estimation. Parental role in OTC use included approval seeking, availability & talk (particularly to mother). Peer role in OTC use included availability and talk. Gender differences were apparent in both parental and peer roles; parents encouraged males to use physical methods of pain relief & females to take OTCs. In discussing pain with peers males indicated more qualifiers; where females were likely to talk about their pain "all the time", males tended to minimize pain that resulted from their own mishaps. Conclusions: This study is the first of a series designed to understand adolescents' attitudes toward pain & pain management strategies. The findings yield additional research questions to be investigated in a second study addressing the emergence of attitudes within the family dynamic.

authors_raw: Mohammad R. Inanlou, G. Andrew M. Reid and Boris Kablar
title: Contractile Activity Of Skeletal Musculature Involved in Breathing is Essential for Normal Lung Organs Genesis
abstract: Mouse fetuses that develop without both Myf5 and MyoD entirely lack skeletal myogenesis (i.e., amyogenic fetuses) and as a consequence perform no fetal breathing movements (FBMs). By employing amyogenic fetuses we tested the hypothesised influence of FBMs on cell fate and differentiation in the lung. To study cell differentiation, we performed immunohistochemistry against surfactant protein (SP)-A, B, -C and AD.We also examined cell cycle kinetic, using immunohistochemistry against proliferating cell nuclear antigen (PCNA) and in situ detection of programmed cell death (TUNEL). Mutant lung tissue was examined at pseudoglandular (embryonic day, E, 15.5), canalicular (E16.5) and terminal (E17.5-19.5) stage of lung development. We found: (a) an underdeveloped alveolar system, (b) increased cell death, (c) decreased cell proliferation and (d) down-regulated SP-D. In conclusion, our preliminary data suggest that FBMs influence development of the terminal part of the bronchial tree, where the gas exchange normally occurs, by controlling cell differentiation and functional maturation and by affecting cell cycle kinetic through unknown mechanochemical signal transduction pathways (Supported by an NSERC operating grant to B. K.).

authors_raw: Sandra Sparkes, Frank P. MacMaster, Normand Carrey and Vivek Kusumakar
title: Neuroimaging Studies of Pediatric Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder
abstract: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder is a prevalent and debilitating illness affecting 3-6% of children. Past studies have implicated the cerebellum in the pathophysiology of the illness. The corpus callosum has also been implicated in ADHD, although not consistently. Methods: 13 ADHD children (7-11 years, 8 males, 5 females) and 13 healthy controls (7-11 years, 9 males, 4 females) underwent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans. Results: Body I in the corpus callosum was significantly smaller in ADHD children as compared to controls. No other corpus callosum region differed between groups. Cerebellum vermis was also reduced in ADHD subjects as compared to controls. Conc!usions: Reductions in Body I and the vermis may reflect the abnormalities noted in motor behavior and inhibition respectively.

authors_raw: Amanda Arnold and Gary V. Allen
title: Glutamate Injection into the Rat Motor Cortex Conditions Against Subsequent Brain Injury Induced Motor Dysfunction
abstract: Introduction: Repetitive mild injury to the rat primary motor cortex has been shown to produce a conditioning effect, so that subsequent severe injury to the same site has no effect on motor function. The present study investigates whether local glutamate release following repeated mild injury is a mechanism for this conditioning effect. Methods: Adult male, Sprague-Dawley rats were trained on a beam-walking task, and received a series of glutamate or phosphate buffered saline (PBS) injections, spaced three days apart, into the rat hindlimb motor cortex. A second group of trained animals received either glutamate or PBS injections and a subsequent severe brain injury three days after the last injection. Results: Animals with repeated PBS or glutamate injections showed no motor deficits in the beam-walking task. Animals with repeated PBS injections followed by severe brain injury had significant motor deficits, whereas animals with repeated glutamate injections prior to severe brain injury had no significant motor deficits in the beam-walking task. Groups of animals with either repeated glutamate or PBS injections and severe brain injury had similar necrotic cavity volumes in the hindlimb region of the primary motor cortex. Animals with repeated glutamate injections showed patterns of heat shock protein 27 and glial fibrillary acidic protein expression that was similar to that seen following repeated mild brain injury. Conclusions: This data suggests that the conditioning effects on motor function seen after repetitive mild brain injury may involve local glutamate release from injured neurons and subsequent activation of secondary motor control sites. (Supported by the Nova Scotia Neurotrauma Society)

authors_raw: Christianne Hawken, Krista Luedemann & Richard Brown
title: The Jax Phenome Project: Anxiety, Locomotion and Spatial Memory in A/J, Balb/cByJ and Molf/ei mouse strains
abstract: The purpose of this research was to create a comprehensive database of behavioral phenotypes for inbred mouse strains. We examined the behaviour of twenty-four A/J, Balb/cByJ and Molf/ei mice on measures of exploration, anxiety, learning and memory in a standardized test battery (Brown et al., 2000). Anxiety-related behaviors were measured using the elevated zero maze, elevated plus maze and light-dark box test. Exploration and locomotor ability were tested in the open field and Rota Rod. Spatial memory was examined using the Morris water maze and Barnes maze. There were significant differences among the three strains. The A/J strain was the most anxious in the elevated plus maze, elevated zero maze and the light/dark box. The Molf/ei had the greatest locomotor ability and explored to a greater extent in the open field than the other strains. In addition, the Molf/ei strain had the best spatial ability of the three strains in both the Barnes maze and Morris water maze. Pl 1. Effect of Methylphenidate Hydrochloride (Ritalin) Administration on Locomtor Activity, Emotional Behaviour and Spatial Learning in C3H/HeSnJ Mice Tera M.Mosher & Richard E. Brown Attention Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is a neuropsychological disorder prevalent in 3-5% of children. The three major characteristics of ADHD are hyperactivity, impulsivity and inattention. Currently, ADHD is treated through a variety of methods, including the use of psychostimulants. Ritalin is the treatment used by the majority of those diagnosed with ADHD because it decreases hyperactivity allowing subjects to concentrate more effectively. There are, however, few animal studies concerned with the effects of Ritalin on behaviour. In particular, it is important to ensure that drugs which alter hyperactivity do not impair cognitive function (e.g. Learning and Memory). The focus of the present study is to determine the effects of Ritalin vs. saline injections on locomotor activity, emotional behaviour and spatial learning in C3H/HeSnJ mice, which are not hyperactive. These mice were tested in the elevated plus maze, open field and Morris water maze. In the elevated plus maze the Ritalin-treated mice spent more time in the open arms than saline-treated mice. In the open field, mice treated with Ritalin were significantly more active than those treated with saline. In the Morris water maze there was no effect of Ritalin treatment on the latency to reach the platform. From these results we conclude that Ritalin increases locomotor activity, and decreases emotionality, but does not effect spatial learning and memory. Our next experiment will compare the effects of MPH on the hyperactive coloboma mouse with those of the control mice. We expect that MPH will reduce locomotor activity in these mice.

title: Elucidating the link between bullying, mood and self-injury among young people
abstract:
Self-injurious behaviour (SIB) can be best understood as intentionally harming oneself (e.g.
cutting) in order to provide rapid, albeit ephemeral, relief from psychological turmoil (Briere &
Gil, 1998; Favazza, 1996, 1998). Typically, SIB onsets during adolescence and persists well into
adulthood if left untreated. The plight of SIB carries with it many risks, including serious injury,
and accidental or intentional suicide, all of which accentuate the psychological pain of the self-
injurer (Favazza, 1998). Though precise aetiologies for SIB remain elusive, several correlates
have been identified. For instance, it is well accepted that abuse associates with SIB (Lipshitz et
al., 1999). It is also well accepted that abuse associates with mood problems (Andrews, 1995;
Browne & Finkelhor, 1986). Taken together, it is interesting to speculate how abuse and mood
are associated with SIB. Specifically, it is interesting to consider bullying as a form of abuse due
to its ubiquity amongst young people (Bond et al., 2001; Nansel et al., 2001) and its association
with mood disturbances (Bond et al., 2001). Despite this relationship, little work has been done
to investigate bullying and SIB. The dearth of work completed suggests that bullying may be a
correlate of SIB (Bhugra et al., 2002; Davies & Cunningham, 1999). In sum, little is known
about the link between bullying, mood and SIB. As a result, the present study aims to elucidate
this link by regressing these variables in order to construct a mediational model. Findings,
clinical implications and directions for future research will be discussed.

authors_raw: Sherry Stewart, Cheryl Birch, Shondalee Eisnor, & Jennifer Theakston
title: Music, Mood and Booze: Activation of implicit alcohol cognition in coping and enhancement motivated drinkers.
abstract:
Cooper's (1994) model of drinking motivations distinguishes between two groups of
drinkers at risk of drinking problems - those who drink to enhance positive mood and
those who drink to cope with negative mood. We investigated whether these groups
differ in their activation of implicit alcohol cognition following mood induction.
Participants were 86 undergraduates with elevated scores on the Enhancement or Coping
Motives (EM or CM) subscale of the Drinking Motives Questionnaire. Participants in
each motive group were randomly assigned to a musically-induced positive or negative
mood condition. A modified Stroop (1935) task, involving color-naming of alcohol and
non-alcohol control words, was administered following mood induction. As
hypothesized, EM drinkers in the positive (but not negative) mood condition showed
significantly longer color-naming speeds for alcohol vs. control words indicating
interference in color-naming by the content of the alcohol words. Even though CM
drinkers in the positive mood condition did not show evidence of selective processing of
alcohol cues (as expected), CM drinkers in the negative mood condition also failed to
show evidence of alcohol interference. Overall, CM drinkers showed significantly longer
color-naming latencies than EM drinkers suggesting possible ceiling effects in the CM
group. Theoretical and clinical implications will be discussed.
New poster
Pl I) How do different mouse strains differ on measures of locomotion,
exploration, anxiety, learning and memory?
Dirk Luchtman and Richard Brown
Transgenic mouse models of human diseases provide an animal model for understanding
the underlying mechanisms of these diseases. It is hoped that, eventually, these models
will translate into new treatments of human (neurological) diseases. Researchers are
assembling test batteries in order to compare behavioral expressions of transgenic and
mutant mice. The data that is produced in these experiments can be used to build up
electronic databases, like the Mouse Phenome Database of the Jackson Laboratory.
As part of the Phenome Project, we are investigating the behavioral differences in 5
strains of mice: SPRET/Ei, MOLF/Ei, C3H/HeJ, SJL/J and A/J. We are testing these
mice for anxiety-like behaviors on the elevated plus maze, and light-dark box;
locomotion and exploration in the open field; motor learning on the rotarod; and spatial
learning in the Barnes maze and Morris water maze. We are also testing these mice for
olfactory discrimination learning and visual acuity. Our poster compares the 5 strains of
mice on these behaviors and examines the influence of body weight, anxiety, exploratory
behavior and visual acuity on performance in tests of learning en memory. Thursday, Mayl, 2003
Room 4258, Life Sciences Centre
9: 15 Introduction and brief history of convention (Vin LoLordo)

title: The influence of Parental Factors on Recurrent Headache in Adolescents
abstract:
Functional headache or headache not due to an identified organic cause is one of the most
common somatic complaints in adolescents. Recurrent functional headache in adolescents is
associated with a number of factors. These include child mental health factors (i.e., depression,
anxiety), child demographic factors (i.e., gender, age), and familial factors (i.e., socioeconomic
status, family headache history). Parental factors associated with recurrent functional headache
in adolescents include parental encouragement of child illness behavior and parental modeling of
illness behavior. It is not known how these parental factors impact on headache-related
impairment (pain intensity), functional disability (restriction in performing social, academic, and
physical tasks), or handicap (inability to fulfill social, academic, or physical roles). In the
proposed research, an Internet-based study will be used to evaluate a model of headache in
adolescents. The model will examine hypothesized relationships between parental factors and
headache-related impairment, functional disability, and handicap in adolescents.
