This effect was independent of whether the stimulus location was to the left or right of fixation and is believed to be a motor programming deficit only in the parietal neglect patients. We have replicated their experiments in normals and imaged the behaviour using fMRI. This presentation will focus on the methods, new performance findings, and adventures in functional imaging.

authors_raw: Jonathan M. Fawcett & Tracy L. Taylor Reaction time was measured in response to visual probes embedded within a standard item-method directed forgetting task. Study words were presented sequentially, each followed ms later by a cue to remember or forget. RT probes were presented at stimulus onset asynchronies of
title: Relative Attentional Load in an Item-Method Directed Forgetting Task

authors_raw: Autumn P. Mochinski and Heather M. Schellinck
title: Early Postnatal Pain Results in Test Apparatus Anxiety
abstract: Pain during development is believed to permanently alter neurobiology and behaviour in both humans and rodents. Behavioural studies in animal models have primarily focused upon measuring the effects of early pain on subsequent anxiety in standard tests such as the elevated plus maze and the open field. The results have not been consistent either within or between labs and appear to depend on such variables as the type of pain, the age of the animals when they experienced the pain, the age when tested for anxiety and the type of apparatus in which they were tested. In the current study, CD-1 mice received injections of either 1% formalin, physiological saline or a sham treatment on Post Natal Days (PND) 3-5 and were exposed to a rat as a predator stressor on PND 30. Formalin injected animals scored significantly differently than controls on several measures of anxiety during habituation to the test apparatus but not during the test phase when the rat was present. These results would suggest that being in the test apparatus itself created such anxiety in the formalin injected animals that their levels of anxiety did not increase in the predator-stress situation.

authors_raw: Nicola Hoffman, Kazue Semba, Jennifer Stamp, Richard Brown
title: Selective cholinergic basal forebrain lesions using anti-murine-p75-saporin (mu p75-saporin): a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease?
abstract: Degeneration of cholinergic neurons in the basal forebrain (BF) is a prominent characteristic of Alzheimer's disease (AD). One current animal model developed to mimic BF cholinergic neuron atrophy observed in human AD is via injecting neurotoxins into the mouse brain. Our long-term research aims are to selectively lesion mouse BF cholinergic neurons and then examine the cognitive and other functional deficits associated with this cell loss. Mu p75-saporin is an immunotoxin that has been shown to selectively destroy cholinergic neurons in the medial septum (MS), but demonstrates concurrent cell loss in cerebellar Purkinje cells. In the present study we examined the efficacy of the recently developed, presumably improved mu p75-saporin in selectively lesioning BF cholinergic neurons in the mouse brain. One of three doses (1.0, 1.5, or 2.0 μg/μl) of the immunotoxin or vehicle was infused bilaterally into the lateral ventricles of male C57BL/6J mice. Two weeks following injections, mice were intra-cardially perfused, the brains removed and sectioned for immunohistochemistry to visualize cholinergic and a subtype of GABAergic BF neurons, as well as acetylcholinesterase (AChE) histochemistry. Quantitative analysis revealed a reduction of cholinergic neurons in the MS/VDB following intracerebroventricular injections of mu p75-saporin, but only a trend for a decrease of cholinergic neurons in the nBM. The density of AChE-postive fibers in the hippocampus and somatosensory cortex was not significantly altered following neurotoxin injection, although there was a trend for a decrease in the hippocampus. No loss of noncholinergic neurons in the MS or nBM was apparent, nor was there a loss of noncholinergic cerebellar Purkinje cells. The results indicate that the most recently developed mu p75-saporin has an increased selectivity to cholinergic MS neurons, compared with the previous formula, and may be useful as a selective cholinergic immunotoxin for mice. Mu 75-saporin may be a useful tool to create an animal model of AD and expand research concerning the progression and neuropathology of the disease.

authors_raw: Christopher Mushquash, Sherry Stewart, Patrick McGrath and Nancy Comeau
title: The Structure of Drinking Motives in Mi'kmaq Adolescents
abstract: When psychological measures are used with cultural groups that are different from the sample the measures were developed with, it is important to evaluate the psychometric properties within the new group. The Revised Drinking Motives Questionnaire (DMQ-R; Cooper, 1994) is a 20-item self-report measure designed to quantify adolescents' reasons for drinking alcohol. Specifically, it was designed to tap levels of the four drinking motives in Cooper's model (i.e., social, enhancement, coping, and conformity). Respondents rate their relative frequency of drinking for each of the indicated reasons on a 5-point Likert scale. The factor structure of the DMQ-R was examined in a sample of First Nations (i.e., Mi'kmaq) adolescents. Exploratory factor analysis was used because the factor structure of the DMQ-R has not been examined previously in this group. Analyses indicated a three-factor structure, with enhancement and social motives not separating into two distinct factors. Moreover, community informants (e.g., school personnel) anecdotally indicated possible problems with some of the social motive items in this cultural group. A qualitative methodology will be used to determine potential reasons for the discrepancy from previous findings in the majority culture (i.e., measurement problem vs. real difference in the structure of drinking motives in the Mi'kmaq culture).

authors_raw: Sherry Stewart, Kaitlin English and Nancy Comeau
title: Relations between personality variables and risky drinking motives in aboriginal adolescents.
abstract: 164 high school students from two Aboriginal communities in NS completed a measure of personality risk for substance abuse - the Substance Use Risk Profile Scale [SURPS] - which taps anxiety sensitivity (AS), hopelessness (HOP), sensation seeking (SS), and impulsivity (IMP). The 96 drinkers also completed the Drinking Motives Questionnaire - Revised, which taps coping, conformity, enhancement, and social drinking motives. AS was correlated with conformity drinking motives. HOP was marginally positively correlated with coping drinking motives and significantly negatively correlated with enhancement and social motives. SS was correlated with enhancement and social motives. IMP was correlated with all four drinking motives. The SURPS appears a valid measure for tapping personality variables related to risky drinking motives in Aboriginal teens. Similarities and differences between the present findings and those observed in a sample of teens from the majority culture will be discussed with emphasis on possible cultural factors contributing to the observed differences.

authors_raw: Mandy Emms, Denise Bernier, Frank MacMaster, Benjamin Rusak & Normand Carrey
title: MRI-based anatomical analysis of the caudate nucleus in children with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a childhood onset behavioural disorder that affects 3-5% of school age children and is characterized by inattention, impulsivity and hyperactivity. Anatomical studies using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) have reported differences between controls and children with ADHD in the size and structure of the caudate nuclei, but results have not been consistent across studies. In this study, two observers, blinded with respect to group assignment, quantified the size and shape of the caudate nuclei of 6 boys (6-8 years of age) and 6 age-matched controls using manual tracing of the whole caudate in MR images. Analysis included detailed volumetric measurements of the caudate in each section through the nucleus, studied in the sagittal, coronal and horizontal planes. Analysis revealed that while the ADHD children did not differ in total caudate volume from controls, they showed a significant localized volume loss. The main finding of the study was that the central region of the left caudate was significantly smaller in the children with ADHD, as well as the caudal region of the left caudate and the rostral region of the right caudate. These findings of localized volume loss on both the left and right caudate nuclei help elucidate why previous neuroimaging studies have found such varied results when assessing total caudate volume.

authors_raw: Antonina Kuznetsova, Denise Bernier, Martin Alda, T. Hajek, Benjamin Rusak and John Fisk
title: Tissue Type Classification of Brain Regions of Interest in Magnetic Resonance Images
abstract: Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) is a method for examining concentrations of metabolites in circumscribed areas of the living brain (regions of interest; ROIs). Any given ROI may contain different tissue types (white matter, grey matter and cerebrospinal fluid), the proportions of which will affect interpretation of MRS data. The purpose of this study is to compare the ability of two commonly used software packages (Brain Voyager QX v. 2 and AFNI) to perform automated tissue-type classification of ROIs accurately and reliably. We will use twenty anatomical (T1-weighted) MR brain images collected using a 1.5 Tesla General Electric scanner and containing 60 ROIs in the in the left medial temporal lobe, in the right and left dorsal pre-frontal cortex, and in the right and left medial pre-frontal cortex. Two experimenters familiar with these methods will independently use the standard AFNI and Brain Voyager procedures for tissue type classification on these ROIs. Intra-rater reliability of 95% and above and inter-rater reliability of 90% and above will be considered acceptable. Validity of classification results produced by each method will be assessed by a blind expert rater who will be asked to visually assess each ROI and to determine which software provided the most accurate representation of tissue type proportions in each one. The outcome measure will be the frequency with which each method is chosen.

title: Validation of High-field MRI Volumetric Assessment of Morphological Changes in Olfactory Structures in Humans
abstract: Recent neuroimaging studies have reported significantly smaller than normal olfactory bulb volumes associated with impaired olfactory performance in patients with schizophrenia and related psychotic disorders. The small dimensions of the olfactory bulb and tract, however, make in vivo volumetric assessment of these structures difficult. The present study was undertaken to investigate the feasibility of using of high field (4T) structural MRI to perform volumetric analysis of the olfactory bulb and tract in patients with psychotic disorders in comparison with healthy volunteers in order to determine parameters that will generate optimal signal quality. A second objective of the study was to determine reliability and validity of performing volumetric assessments. A replica of the adult human olfactory bulb and tract was constructed and three-dimensional, high-resolution imaging was performed. Our findings indicate that volumetric assessment of human olfactory structures can be achieved with high field MRI in order to examine putative differences between patients with psychotic disorders and control subjects.

title: Do individual differences in inhibition of return and single target discrimination ability affect visual search efficiency?
abstract: We investigated whether amount of inhibition of return (IOR: a tendency for slower responses to targets presented at previously attended locations) and discrimination ability can predict individual differences in visual search efficiency. There is suggestion that IOR can act as a 'foraging facilitator' in visual search by working as a type of implicit memory system that discourages re-inspection of items. Also, it was suspected that search efficiency should vary with individual discrimination ability, as search difficulty can vary with the similarity between targets and distractors. Three tasks were used: a single-item discrimination task (target/non-target discrimination), a visuo-spatial attention task (to measure IOR), and a visual search and probe task (to measure search efficiency and IOR to a probe presented following search). Amount of IOR following search and discrimination ability were each found to be significantly correlated with search efficiency: increasing amount of IOR and increasing discrimination ability predicted increasing search efficiency. Findings support the proposal that IOR is a foraging facilitator, and suggest that discrimination ability is a rate-determining factor in visual search.

authors_raw: Frederick Li, Samuel Deurveilher, Christina Morgan, and Kazue Semba
title: "Doing the Locomotion" With the Rat Perifornical Hypothalamus: Who's excited about glutamate?
abstract: The present study examined the role of glutamatergic inputs to the perifornical lateral hypothalamus PeFLH in the regulation of various arousal-related behaviors. Rats received a unilateral microinjection of vehicle or glutamate receptor agonists, AMPA (1 or 2mM) or NMDA (10mM), and were placed into an open field for a 90-min video recording. Rats were then perfused and processed for double-label immunohistochemistry for c-Fos, a marker for neuronal activation, and orexin, a neuronal phenotype exclusive to this region. As compared to vehicle, AMPA and NMDA injections increased the total distance traveled within 90 min. The number of cells immunoreactive for both c-Fos and orexin increased at the injection site. These results suggest that glutamatergic inputs to orexin neurons in the PeFLH may play an important role in locomotor activity.

title: Neuronal or glial origin for the electroretinogram (ERG) in insect eyes?
abstract:
An insect's CNS is isolated from its circulation by a blood-brain barrier, derived from which is a blood-eye barrier (BEB). Because visual neurons cross this BEB, their electrical activity should generate responses across it. This is the conventional explanation for the unusually large extracellular waves recorded in the eye as the electroretinogram (ERG). The ON- and OFF-responses of the ERG are supposed to come from monopolar neurons directly postsynaptic to the photoreceptors, in the first synaptic zone, the lamina. The ERG is employed widely to gauge whether the first synapse is working normally, following genetic perturbations aimed at dissecting the molecular mechanisms underlying cycling of the photoreceptors' neurotransmitter, histamine.
Do monopolar neurons really generate the ERG transients? Simultaneous recordings of the ERG and intracellular recordings from visual neurons suggest not. A high resistance barrier proximal to the lamina (likely the marginal glial cells) appears to generate the ERG transients. Monopolar neurons cross this glial zone so their responses should also contribute to the ERG, and candidate wavelets exist. Their small size indicates that the ERG currents from the glia are much larger than those coming from the neurons. These glial cells are obviously light-responsive: the same histamine release may activate both glia and monopolar neurons. At least for those interested in presynaptic mechanisms, ERG transients from glia merely substitute a glial origin for a response so far assumed to be neuronal.

authors_raw: Yoko Ishigami, Ray Klein and John Christie
title: Using illusory line motion to explore attentional capture
abstract:
Uninformative peripheral cues (figure-8 markers) and targets (an 8 changing to a
2 or 5) were combined with illusory line motion (ILM) probes to explore attentional capture. Markers were presented above, below, left and right of fixation. Any marker could brighten but for a particular participant targets only appeared on the horizontal or vertical axes. Occasionally, a diagonal line was presented connecting two of the 8s. Such lines are seen to be drawn away from a cue when the cue is adjacent to the line. We used ILM and digit identification performance to measure how endogenous attention (allocated to the 8s that could change to targets) modulates the cue‚s signal strength. Digit identification reaction time and accuracy revealed that attention was captured by exogenous cues even when they were presented at task irrelevant locations. Whereas the reported magnitude of ILM away from an adjacent cue was not significantly affected by endogenous attentional control settings, ILM was weakly experienced in lines not adjacent to a cue in a manner suggesting endogenous generation of ILM.

authors_raw: Mathew S. Grandy and David A. Westwood
title: Opposite representations of object mass by the perceptual and sensorimotor systems in the size-weight illusion
abstract: Goodale and Milner's (1992) duplex vision model suggests that separate visual pathways underlie object perception and the control of object-directed actions. Virtually all the research behind this model is based on tasks for which (1) vision is the primary or exclusive sensory input, and (2) grasping is the motor output. Recently, Flanagan & Beltzner (2000) showed that perceptual and motor responses to the size-weight illusion (in which a smaller object is perceived to weigh more than a larger object of identical mass) can also be dissociated. Using faulty logic, they claimed to have shown a dissociation between the effect of the SWI on perceptual reports of object mass and on the scaling of lifting forces used to pick up the object. We show in the present study using superior methods that the sensorimotor and perceptual systems clearly generate separate (and indeed opposite) representations of object mass in the SWI over repeated lifting episodes. This finding has clear and important implications for understanding multi-sensory integration for perception and action.

title: The Affect of Short Musical Phrases with Different Melodic Contours
abstract:
The affect (emotional content) induced by music has been assessed in longer pieces of music, mainly by reference to constructs such as modality, pitch height, tempo, and tonality. Specifically, major keys sound happy, minor keys sad, higher keys (pitches) more uplifting, faster tempos more exciting, and tonal pieces more pleasant to listen to. Note that terms like modality tempo and tonality refer to broad qualities of music leading to broad affective experience. Less is known about the affective quality of melodic contour, possibly because it is so hard to define. We propose to explore the relationship between induced affect and the attributes of the melodic contour on a note-by-note basis. Using short (8-14 notes) simple phrases of different melodic contours, the primary goal is to tease out the specific aspects of contour that lead to affective responses to music. The second goal is to create computational models of structures that lead to induced affect.
POSTERS

authors_raw: Lisa Boutilier, Daniel Clark, Julie Longard, Sarbjeet Singh
title: The History of Psychology at Dalhousie
abstract: Psychology at Dalhousie has a rich past, filled with interesting personalities, and dedicated and driven researchers. In some ways, it is a microcosm of the development of disciplinary psychology over the past century and a half, yet with a unique Nova Scotian flavour. This is the story of an unwelcome Freudian and a Prince whose connections to the President allowed him to move the department into the White House. It is also the story of an award-winning architectural wonder and how respectability was brought to "snake oil salesmen." And it is the story of a chairman who brought wolves to Nova Scotia, and another who added a seal tank to the LSC. And it is also a lament that Richard Brown's exile to Sable Island failed. The class interviewed past and present faculty and staff, including six department chairs. Students conducted extensive archival research, and began the construction of an extensive report on the development of this leading psychology department in Canada.

title: We've Come a Long Way in 35 years
abstract: In September of 1971, the Life Sciences Centre opened to house the departments of Psychology, Biology and Oceanography (in order of importance, although you could argue the last two) at Dalhousie. Just four years earlier, the Tupper Medical building had opened, complete with basement television studio, bringing home-grown moving imagery to modern teaching at the university. The Psychology Department enthusiastically embraced this new technology for both teaching and research, and pushed to have a television studio included in the new LSC. The first consumer-level video recording equipment appeared at the same time, and our initial occupation of the LSC was accompanied by much video making. Most of that early work has been lost, and much of what remains is nearly irredeemable due to deterioration of the media and changing standards in video technology. This presentation will give you a taste of the historical records we are losing and suggest that perhaps we should be doing a better job of documenting the history of what arguably has been one of the most influential Psychology departments in Canada.

authors_raw: Erin L. Mazerolle and Ryan C.N. D'Arcy
title: Determining neuro-cognitive connectivity: An event-related fMRI study at 4T. OR Beyond phrenology: Functional connectivity of the human brain
abstract: There is a growing trend in neuroscience to study the nature of the connections between brain regions, as these connections are vitally important to understanding brain function. The well-studied visual and motor systems provide an excellent foundation for investigations of functional connectivity. Interhemispheric connections between the visual and motor systems have been previously studied using unimanual reaction times (RTs) to lateralized light flashes. When the light is flashed in the hemifield contralateral to the responding hand, the visual information must cross to the hemisphere that controls the hand. While behavioural studies have yielded differences between crossed and uncrossed RTs, it is likely that light flashes can be processed by relatively low-level systems. We propose to use lateralized cognitive processes (face and word processing) to elicit interhemispheric transfer between distributed cortical systems. Furthermore, we plan to vary the extent of the network recruited by the task by manipulating the number of interhemispheric transfers. This model will allow for testing of basic hypotheses about 'neuro-cognitive' connectivity using non-invasive functional imaging.

authors_raw: Jodie Gawryluk, Jeannette McGlone, Mark Sadler, and Ryan D'Arcy
title: Is emotional intelligence affected by right more than left temporal lobe resections?
abstract: The right amygdala may have a distinct role in emotional intelligence. This research will examine emotional intelligence after left and right temporal lobe resections that include the amygdala. There are several approaches to evaluate emotional intelligence. Critically, this study will use Bar-On's Emotional Quotient inventory. It is hypothesized that individuals with right temporal lobe resections will score lower on the EQ-i, than those with left temporal lobe resections. Conversely, individuals who have had left temporal lobe resections are expected to have lower verbal memory and naming scores than those with right-sided resections. These groups are not expected to differ on descriptive information or neuropsychological measures such as intelligence, speed of reading, or visuospatial skills.

authors_raw: Martha E. Carmichael and Dennis P. Phillips
title: Ear asymmetries in auditory temporal gap detection
abstract: There is a long history of interest in the functional specializations of the two cerebral hemispheres in man. In particular, there is interest in the reasons why language processing is often lateralized to the left hemisphere. One hypothesis is that the left hemisphere, and perhaps especially the auditory part of it, is in some way a "faster temporal processor" than is the right. The intended connotations of that expression are not always clear, but they typically include auditory temporal acuity, i.e., the temporal "grain" with which the stream of sound is perceptually resolved. In this regard, high temporal acuity is advantageous for resolving the sounds of speech. The present study employs temporal gap detection paradigms, with and without two kinds of contralateral masking, to measure temporal acuity through the left and right ears in normal adult listeners. Differences in acuity through the two ears may be informative about the temporal resolution supported by the two cerebral hemispheres.

title: Orienting attention in visuospatial neglect: How limb movements affect the disengage deficit Or "Limbs…Cueing…Attention" (read as "Lights…Camera…Action")
abstract: Visuo-spatial neglect from right hemisphere damage is associated a difficulty disengaging attention from the right side to move it to the left side (disengage deficit). Neglect has been shown to be reduced with both active and passive left limb movements, but the mechanisms of this positive effect is unknown. In the current study a cuing paradigm was used to assess the effect of active (AM) and passive (PM) left and right limb movements, compared to a no movement condition (NM), on the disengage function in right hemisphere stroke patients with and without neglect and in older healthy controls. Results showed that the disengage deficit was related in size to neglect severity in stroke participants and orienting was affected by limb movements in the older control group and in two neglect subjects with a severe disengage deficit. These data suggest that left limb movements can directly affect the orienting of attention, although the direction and consistency of the effect remains yet to be resolved.

authors_raw: Mike Lawrence, Beverly Butler and Gail Eskes
title: Patterns of attention in visual search after stroke
abstract: Visual search performance was measured in healthy subjects and right hemisphere stroke survivors, including patients with and without neglect. These groups were compared on traditional measures of global and spatial attention, as well as new measures that quantified how attention moved through space during the search task. It was discovered that neglectful stroke survivors fared worse than their non-neglectful and healthy counterparts not only in where they allotted their attention, but how they moved it as well. These results reveal a novel pattern of performance that may shed light on the mechanism of the neglect dysfunction.

authors_raw: Tessa J. Craig, and Christine T. Chambers
title: Expressive and Receptive Pain Language and Its Relationship to General Language Development Among Healthy Children
abstract: Recent research has shown that young children tend to rely on a select number of words to describe pain. The purpose of the current study was to extend previous research by examining receptive as well as expressive pain language in children, and to examine the relationship between pain language and children's general vocabulary skills. Sixty children (30 boys; 30 girls) aged 3-6 years (M = 4.17, SD = 1.03) and a parent (93% mothers) participated in the study. Parents completed a questionnaire assessing the degree with which their children used and understood seven words for pain, and the age at which they first started to use and understand these words. Children's general vocabularies were assessed using two standardized measures of vocabulary development: the PPVT-III and the EVT. Results indicated that 'hurt' was used most frequently and was best understood by the children, appearing in their vocabularies at 28 months of age. 'Pain' and 'ache' were rated as being infrequently used and poorly understood, and did not appear in children's vocabularies until around 40 months of age. There were no significant relationships between children's expressive and receptive pain language and their scores on the measures of general expressive and receptive vocabulary (r's = .06 to .22). These results have implications for developmentally appropriate assessment of pain in young children.

authors_raw: Jennifer A. Parker and Sophie Jacques
title: Equating Secondary Tasks: A Dual-Task Investigation in 5-Year-Old Children Or… Doing Two Tasks at the Same Time Should be Harder Than Doing Just One (But Apparently It Isn't)
abstract: Two experiments were performed with 5-year-old children to establish equivalent verbal and nonverbal secondary tasks for use in future dual-task studies. Children's response time on a simple primary task was measured while they were asked to perform a series of concurrent secondary tasks. The primary task in the first experiment required children to touch a target on a computer screen, while in the second experiment, they were asked to draw a continuous path. Children in both studies participated in five secondary task conditions. They were asked to repeat one word continuously in two verbal secondary tasks (i.e., "ball" vs. "candy"); they were asked to make a facial movement in the two nonverbal secondary tasks (i.e., smack their lips vs. make a kissy face); and they were not asked to perform any secondary tasks in a control condition. Interestingly, it was found that children's response times in the control condition were not faster than those in secondary task conditions. These findings are consistent with Luria's theory on the verbal regulation of motor behaviour.

authors_raw: Meredith Pike, Jennifer Mullane and Penny Corkum
title: Using the Attention Network Test to Measure Three Types of Attention in Children with Attention Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder
abstract: The Attention Network Theory (Posner & Peterson, 1990) proposes that there are three functionally and anatomically independent networks of attention: Executive, Alerting and Orienting. The Executive and Alerting networks are thought by some researchers to be impaired in children with ADHD. Recently, the Attention Network Test (ANT) was developed to measure these three networks simultaneously. To date, the ANT has never been used in children with ADHD. In the current study, the ANT was given to 20 children with ADHD. The results validate the utility of the ANT as a new measure for studying attention in children with ADHD. As well, further evidence for the independence of the three attention networks was found which provides further evidence of support for the Attention Network Theory.

authors_raw: Laura Knickle, Melissa McGonnell and Penny Corkum
title: The Influence of Subtype on Academic Difficulties and Cognitive Profiles in Children with ADHD
abstract: It has been proposed that the subtypes of ADHD differ in their cognitive and academic profiles, as well as in their symptom profiles and demographic characteristics. However, the results of previous research has been inconsistent, most likely due to a number of limitations, including poorly diagnosed ADHD samples and lack of standardized assessment of cognitive and academic skills. The present study included 64 children who received a diagnosis of ADHD-Combined Type or ADHD-Inattentive Type based on a rigorous, interdisciplinary assessment process. It was found that the ADHD subtypes did not differ in terms of their age of diagnosis, sex distribution, mean IQ scores or academic performance, but that the ADHD-Inattentive Type had less comorbid mental health problems and higher socio-economic level. The results question the clinical utility of the subtypes in helping to understand cognitive and academic performance in elementary school-aged children.

authors_raw: Amy C. MacPherson, Patricia A. McMullen, and Phillip J. Dunham
title: Individual Differences in the Development of Face Recognition
abstract: Children at seven (n = 32) and eleven (n = 30) years of age participated in a project examining individual differences in face recognition and the processing strategies children use to recognize faces. Children took part in two tasks including choosing the familiar face from a series of upright or inverted photographs of human faces and determining which of two abstract images (i.e., Mooney face stimuli and scrambled counterparts) contained a face. The goal of this project was to provide further evidence of a switch during childhood from feature-based to configural processing in the recognition of upright faces. Results are combined with those from adults (n = 62) tested under similar conditions to tell a complete developmental story.

title: Studies in visual-proprioceptive integration
abstract: Integration of information over visual and proprioceptive modalities is an important means for establishing a sense of self in early development. Recently, we have been studying this process in adults through two approaches. First, we examine the temporal parameters of visual-proprioceptive integration by showing adult participants delayed video (from 0 to 5 video frames) of their own arm and facial movements. Our results show a discrimination of live from delayed video for both hand and facial movements at delays of over 2 video frames (66 msec). Second, we have been investigating whether the rubber hand illusion first described by Botvinick & Cohen (1998) for visual-tactile information extends to visual-proprioceptive information. In our study, participants have a rubber hand attached to their own hand with a brace. They watch the rubber hand as they move their own hand behind a screen and then report on whether they experience the movement in the rubber hand. Our results to date indicate that the rubber hand illusion does extend to visual-proprioceptive integration.

title: The Affect of Short Musical Sequences with Different Melodic Contours
abstract: This research examined the relationship between affect (emotional content) and the shape of 16 equi-temporal eight note musical sequences designed to differ only on melodic contour (ascending, descending, arch, inverted-arch, etc.). Two groups of participants rated the intended affect of each sequence on ten bipolar affect scales (contentment-joy, delicacy-strength, excitement-boredom, passivity-aggression, etc.). Modeling of melodic contour was done using fourth-order polynomial equations. Of which, the first term captures the average pitch height. The second term captures the ascending / descending aspect of the shape. The third term captures the arch aspect of the shape. Higher order terms capture nuances. The analysis indicated that pitch height was a primary predictor on eight of the affect scales. However, the analysis also indicated that the ascending/ descending aspect was important for five affect scales, while the degree of arch was important for eight affect scales. Different terms were important for different affect scales.

title: Do the information processing differences in figure and ground reported by Wong & Weisstein (1983) generalize to a speeded orientation discrimination task?
abstract: Wong and Weisstein (1983) reported that sharp line targets were detected better within a visual region perceived as figure while blurred line targets were detected better within a region perceived as background. They interpreted these results as evidence for two modes of visual processing: 1) high spatial-frequency channels that facilitate figure analysis and 2) low spatial-frequency channels that facilitate ground analysis. These results have not been replicated and until now, no published work has demonstrated whether they generalize beyond the narrow range of methods used in the original study. The current investigation examined the generalizability of their findings using similar figure/ground and target stimuli and a better choice of target locations within the figure/ground drawing. The task was also changed from one of detection to one of horizontal/vertical line discrimination. The findings failed to replicate Wong and Weisstein. As such, their results do not generalize after minor methodological alterations.

title: Mechanistic Issues in Human Coitus: Implications to Sexual Recovery after Iatrogenic Impotence
abstract:
Tens of thousands of men become impotent each year as a result of either the surgery or radiation used to treat prostate cancer. For approximately a third of these men, phosphodiesterase 5 (PDE5) inhibitors, such as Viagra, are ineffective. Many studies indicate that this loss of sexual function undermines the men's self-esteem and lowers their overall quality of life.
In this talk I will review options for these patients. I will discuss the limitations of current treatments for erectile dysfunction and introduce a counter-intuitive work-around for recovery of rewarding sex that completely circumvents the issue of penile erectility. I will provide a hypothesis, based on neural activity in cortical sensory fields, for why this approach works. I will also discuss the psycho-social challenges to making novel approaches to sex therapy acceptable to men and their partners, the majority of whom are senior citizens.

authors_raw: Nancy Bandstra, L. Skinner, C. LeBlanc, C. Moores, D. Brennan, and C. Chambers
title: The Role of Child Life in Pediatric Pain Management
abstract: There is now strong support in the use of non-pharmacological approaches for reducing children's pain and distress during painful medical procedures. Although an exponential growth in knowledge in these techniques has occurred in the past decade, studies continue to suggest that children are undertreated for their pain. This research project is being conducted to examine child life specialists' role in pediatric pain management. While it is generally known that child life specialists make an important contribution in terms of pediatric pain management, no research has described the specific pain management strategies used by child life specialists. The purpose of the current study is 1) to describe the strategies currently being used by child life specialists to manage pediatric pain 2) to describe how effective child life specialists find these strategies to be, and finally, 3) to determine how much training experience child life specialists have in pediatric pain management and assess the level of interest in receiving more training. Child life specialists from hospitals across Canada will be invited to complete an online questionnaire concerning their use of various non-pharmacological pain management strategies to help pediatric patients cope with pain.

authors_raw: Steve Porter, Ron Leslie and Matthias Schmidt
title: Investigations of Affective Processing and Psychopathy using fMRI
abstract: Antisocial Personality Disorder (APSD) is a personality disorder characterized by a major affective deficit accompanied by a disregard for societal rules in general (e.g., Hare, 1996). A subset of those with APSD can be characterized as manipulative, callous, remorseless, irresponsible individuals (e.g., Hare, 1996, 1998), or as having psychopathic traits. According to Patrick (1994), a central feature of psychopathy is the deviant affective response. Since individuals with psychopathic characteristics appear to have emotional deficits, the question remains as to whether or not these deficits have a neurological basis. The limited existing studies that have used neuroimaging techniques implicate brain abnormalities in psychopathy and antisocial behaviour. The main objective of this program of research will be to determine how the brain processes affective visual and audio information in normal controls and in individuals with psychopathic characteristics. This research will shed light on the underlying neural deficits postulated to be associated with psychopathy, which may in turn help explain the antisocial behaviour that is characteristic of this disorder.

authors_raw: Kerry Lynn MacSwain and Margo C. Watt
title: Gender differences in implicit and explicit cognitions pertaining to anxiety
abstract: Previous research has found that women (vs. men) yield higher anxiety scores on both explicit and implicit measures of cognitions related to trait anxiety and anxiety sensitivity (AS). In addition to this, the correlation between implicit and explicit measures of trait anxiety is higher for women, suggesting that explicit measures reflect this underlying personality construct more accurately in women than in men. The present study proposes to further investigate these gender differences in cognitions related to anxiety. Participants will complete two explicit measures and two implicit measures of trait anxiety and AS, including a newer implicit measure known as the Extrinsic Affective Simon Task (EAST). It is hypothesized that women (vs. men) will yield higher anxiety scores on implicit and explicit measures of cognition. It is also expected that the correlations between implicit and explicit anxiety measures will be higher for women than men.

authors_raw: Jennifer Vriend, Jason Ivanoff, Angeline Vaughan, John Christie, and Ben Rusak
title: Sleep on it
abstract: The Visual Discrimination Task (VDT) is a sensory learning task, performance on which has been reported to depend on sleep intervening between initial acquisition and later testing. We studied VDT performance in 21 subjects (19 F, 2 M, aged 20-27), each in two conditions: once with, and once without, a 2 h nap opportunity (13:30-15:30) intervening between morning training (08:30) and evening testing (19:00). Performance improvement was assessed as the difference between morning and evening performance during each session. Subjects were divided into good versus bad nappers based on whether they showed at least 2 min of REM and 2 min of Slow Wave Sleep during the nap opportunity. Good nappers improved more during the day in the nap session than did bad nappers. This improvement, however, depended entirely on good nappers performing poorly during the morning of the nap session. The results suggest the hypothesis that sleep may improve newly acquired cognitive skills only in people who are relatively sleep deprived.

title: The Effects of Positive and Negative Mood States on Mood-Regulation Gambling Outcome Expectancies
abstract: This study aimed to clarify how positive and negative mood states influence the mood-regulation gambling outcome expectancies of undergraduate gamblers, as self-reported on a recently developed questionnaire (the Gambling Expectancies Questionnaire; GEQ). Participants were 91 undergraduate psychology students, aged 18 or older, who reported regular gambling. Participants were randomly assigned to a positive or negative musical mood induction procedure (MMIP) or to a neutral mood control. The GEQ was administered at baseline and after the MMIP to determine changes in reward and relief expectancy strength. The hypotheses of mood specificity of the activation of reward and relief expectancies were only partially supported. The trend of increased reward expectancies following positive mood induction was expected; however, the trends of increased relief expectancies following positive mood induction and decreased reward expectancies following negative mood induction were unexpected. Further research and potential clinical implications are discussed.

title: The Effect of Outcome Expectancy on Depression and Quality of Life in Rural Stroke Survivors
abstract: Patient recovery expectations are related to health outcomes and have been found to be influence the success of treatment. The relationship between outcome expectancy and overall functioning has potential implications for the rehabilitative process of patients with a chronic illness; however this relationship has not yet been explored in stroke survivors. The purpose of the present study is to examine the relationship between outcome expectancies and depression and quality of life in rural stroke survivors. A measure of outcome expectancy was designed to assess 13 different domains of outcome within 6 broad categories of memory, physical functioning, social relationships, leisure and occupational activities, dependence, and overall expectations. This measure was included in the Yarmouth Stroke Project (YSP) interviews, which are currently being conducted and include qualitative and quantitative questions that address psychosocial functioning. The development and efficacy of the expectancy measure will be discussed with reference to preliminary results if available.

authors_raw: Alana R. Paul and Steve Barnes
title: Are my eyes playing tricks on me? Evidence of inhibitory neurotransmitter actions of β-alanine in the salamander retina
abstract: β-Alanine is a putative inhibitory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system. Using calcium imaging, the responses of neurons in living retinal slices were investigated during exposure to β-alanine, glycine, GABAA agonist, muscimol, and GABAC agonist, cis-4-aminocrotonic acid. At 1 mM, β-alanine caused inhibition of depolarization-induced calcium responses in both photoreceptors (PRs) and inner nuclear layer (INL) cells, similar to results seen with 500 μM muscimol and glycine. However, 200 μM β-alanine caused enhancement (possibly due to disinhibition) of calcium response in PRs, and an ambivalent response in INL cells. Neither glycine nor muscimol caused this enhancement. It is possible that β-alanine can inhibit both PRs and INL cells, but that it has more potent effects in the INL and thus at lower concentrations, β-alanine selectively inhibits cells in the INL. This response to β-alanine could provide evidence for a novel β-alaninergic signalling pathway in the retina involving inhibition of cells presynaptic to PRs.

title: Non-Phosphorylated Neurofilament Labeling is Regulated by Visual Experience in the Kitten Lateral Geniculate Nucleus: Implications for Anatomical Remodeling During the Critical Period.
abstract: Monocular deprivation (MD) during early development causes rearrangement of neural connectivity that can produce severe and permanent vision impairment. Neurons in deprived layers of the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) exhibit smaller somata and reduced geniculocortical terminal fields compared to neurons in non-deprived layers. The mechanisms underlying these deprivation-induced changes are unknown. We have demonstrated a correlation between structural change and cytoskeleton alteration in visually deprived cats. Following 4 days of MD, neurons within deprived layers of the LGN were smaller than those in non-deprived layers, and this structural change was accompanied by a spatially and temporally coincident reduction in neurofilament labeling. Recovery of cell-size and neurofilament labeling was observed after 4 days of post-deprivation binocular visual experience. The recovery appeared to be complete after 8 days of binocular vision. The results from our study support the hypothesis that cytoskeleton alteration provides a substrate for deprivation-induced neuronal restructuring.

authors_raw: Donald E. Mitchell and Frank Sengpiel Continuous monocular deprivation during early postnatal life results in reduced cortical representation and visual acuity of the deprived eye. However, loss of visual function in one eye after a brief period of compromised vision would appear to be maladaptive. Previously we have reared cats with a daily regimen in which they received variable amounts of MD and BE, and subsequently assessed the visual capabilities of the two eyes. Here we report on visual cortex function on the basis of optical imaging of intrinsic signals. As with behaviour, we show that daily amounts of binocular vision as short as 0.5 h allowed normal ocular dominance architecture and visual responses to develop despite much longer periods of MD. However, unlike vision where the outcome appears to be dictated by the proportion of total exposure that is normal, cortical architecture appears to be determined by the absolute amount of binocular vision rather than its relative share. As with behaviour, continuous binocular experience was more effective for cortical development than an equivalent exposure split into two epochs. Normal experience is clearly more efficacious in driving visual cortical development and vision than abnormal experience
title: Not all visual input is created equal: Normal input is more efficacious for development of the visual cortex and for vision.

authors_raw: Heather-Dawn Wood, Jacob Lingley and Simon Gadbois
title: The Swim of the Mummichog
abstract: The present experiment sought to determine if Fundulus heteroclitus (the common killifish or mummichog) would use distal visual cues or proximal landmarks to complete an innovative place preference paradigm. A plus maze consisting of four empty beige saucers and one enriched darker saucer was used to exploit the mummichog's innate preference for concealment. In condition 1, 10 fish were trained with a high-contrast, extra-maze distal cue, situated over the enriched saucer. In condition 2, 10 fish were trained with proximal landmarks situated in the entrance of the arm leading to the enriched saucer. Experimental trials consisted of displacing the visual cues and recording accuracy. Error rate for condition 2 was found to be significantly lower than condition 1, indicating fish using proximal landmarks had significantly fewer errors than fish using distal cues. These results promote the present paradigm as a useful fish cognitive task for research across the fields of ecotoxicology, ethology, and psychology.

authors_raw: Oliver Braubach, Alan Fine and Roger Croll
title: Learning about Learning from Zebrafish
abstract: The zebrafish olfactory system is emerging as an important model for studying neural processing of chemosensory information. This is largely due to its accessibility for in vivo physiological studies and also the ease of genetically manipulating this system. While our knowledge about the neurophysiology of zebrafish olfaction is advancing rapidly, these fish have not yet been widely used in studies of olfactory behavior. We therefore sought to develop various assays to study olfactory-driven behaviors and learning in zebrafish. We have succeeded in conditioning zebrafish to respond to specific odorants in order to obtain a food reward, while at the same time conditioning them to ignore other odors of similar quality. Physiological recordings in the zebrafish olfactory system will enable us to pinpoint regions in the olfactory bulb that are responsive to specific odorants. Combining these approaches allows us to induce behavioral plasticity that can be correlated with detailed physiological data to reveal neural substrates activated and modified in olfactory learning.

authors_raw: Jennifer Vriend, Jason Ivanoff, Angeline Vaughan, John Christie, and Ben Rusak
title: Sleep on it
abstract: The Visual Discrimination Task (VDT) is a sensory learning task, performance on which has been reported to depend on sleep intervening between initial acquisition and later testing. We studied VDT performance in 21 subjects (19 F, 2 M, aged 20-27), each in two conditions: once with, and once without, a 2 h nap opportunity (13:30-15:30) intervening between morning training (08:30) and evening testing (19:00). Performance improvement was assessed as the difference between morning and evening performance during each session. Subjects were divided into good versus bad nappers based on whether they showed at least 2 min of REM and 2 min of Slow Wave Sleep during the nap opportunity. Good nappers improved more during the day in the nap session than did bad nappers. This improvement, however, depended entirely on good nappers performing poorly during the morning of the nap session. The results suggest the hypothesis that sleep may improve newly acquired cognitive skills only in people who are relatively sleep deprived.

title: "I Sleep Therefore Can I Think": Effects of Obstructive Sleep Apnea on Neurocognitive Function
abstract: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is characterized by disrupted breathing during sleep, leading to hypoxemia and fragmented sleep at night, daytime sleepiness, and changes in cognition and mood. Individuals treated with CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) report improved fatigue and better sleep, yet commonly continue to have complaints of persistent cognitive dysfunction and reduced performance at work and daily activities, particularly those that require executive functions. The purpose of the study is to investigate the executive function of individuals with OSA on treatment with CPAP. OSA patients and matched controls will be compared on experimental working memory tasks, standardized neuropsychological battery, measures of mood, daily functioning and quality of life. All patients and some controls will also receive an overnight polysomnographic sleep study to characterize their current sleep. It is hypothesized that while CPAP may improve sleep and thus attentional capacity related to the excessive daytime sleepiness, performance on tasks measuring executive control will continue to be impaired.

title: The Effect of Mood on Susceptibility to Misinformation Regarding Emotional Photographs
abstract: The current study will investigate the influence of mood and the emotional valence of stimuli on accuracy of recall and susceptibility to the misinformation effect. A negative, positive, or neutral mood will be induced in participants using a musical mood induction technique. Participants will then be shown three emotional photographs: one negative, one neutral, and one positive, and after a delay will be asked accurate or misleading questions regarding details contained in the photographs. After a second delay, half of the participants will answer open-ended questions about certain details that were in the photographs that they saw, and the other half will be shown two photographs (one original and one altered to incorporate the misinformation in the misleading questions) and will be asked to select the original photograph that they saw.

authors_raw: Lindsay S. Uman, Sherry H. Stewart, Margo C. Watt and Amber Johnston
title: Differences in High and Low Anxiety Sensitive Women's Responses to a Laboratory-Based Cold Pressor Task
abstract: Anxiety sensitivity (AS) or the fear of anxiety-related sensations has been recently linked to a variety of disabling chronic health conditions including pain-related conditions (Asmundson et al., 2000). In the present study, 90 undergraduate university women were selected for inclusion in one of two AS groups (high or low) based on their screening scores on a 16-item measure of AS. Participants were tested individually on a lab-based cold pressor task using a variety of self-report and observer-measured variables. Data analyses revealed that, as expected, the high AS participants reported significantly more fear in response to the cold pressor on the McGill Pain Questionnaire - Short Form (SF-MPQ) than did the low AS participants. Also as expected, the high AS participants reported more pain in response to the cold pressor on the Present Pain Index (PPI) of the SF-MPQ than did the low AS participants. High AS participants did not differ from low AS participants on other aspects of the cold pressor response (e.g., pain threshold, pain tolerance, pain recovery). Results also provide support for the role of pain-related fear as a mediating variable between AS and increased perceived pain intensity.

title: ?I didn?t forget it, I just remembered something else instead!?: Examining possible attentional allocation in directed forgetting.
abstract: A standard item-method directed forgetting task presented sequential word pairs consisting of two differently colored words. Participants were instructed to attend or ignore designated words based on color. Immediately following the presentation of each word pair, a remember or forget memory cue was presented aurally. Following study, a recognition test assessed memory of both forget and remember-cued words, regardless of attention instruction (attend or ignore). As expected, percentage correct scores for attended words illustrate superior recognition performance on remember-cued as compared to forget-cued words, indicative of a robust directed forgetting effect. In contrast, memory performance on ignored words shows an interesting potential trend for a reverse directed forgetting effect. It appears as though withdraw of attention from forget-cued words is succeeded by attention to irrelevant information, such as ignored words. Support for attention models of directed forgetting and applications will be discussed.

authors_raw: Jonathan Fawcett and Tracy L. Taylor
title: The Relationship between Attention and Forgetting in Humans
abstract: Reaction time (RT) was measured in response to visual detection probes embedded within an item-method directed forgetting paradigm. Study words were presented individually followed by an instruction to remember (R) or forget (F). Probes were presented at stimulus onset asynchronies (SOAs) of 1400, 1800, or 2600 ms in relation to the study word or the memory instruction. No-probe catch trials and a no-word control condition were included to evaluate false alarms and provide a subtractive baseline measure. At the end of all study trials, a yes-no recognition task measured retention of R and F words. The results demonstrated a tendency for post-F instruction probe RTs to be longer than post-R instruction probe RTs at the two earliest SOAs; this effect was marginally significant at the 1400 ms SOA and statistically significant at the 1800 ms SOA. Recognition data indicated a significant directed forgetting effect, with better recognition of R than F words. These findings suggest that intentional forgetting requires more attentional resources than remembering.

title: Impact of Methylphenidate on Sleep and Daytime Alertness in Children with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder.
abstract: Changes in sleep quality related to treatment with methylphenidate and potential daytime sequelae of sleep disturbances were evaluated across a randomized placebo-controlled medication trial in 15 children, rigorously diagnosed with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Data were analyzed with a repeated measures ANOVA and contrast analyses. Parents and teachers reported an improvement in ADHD symptoms on medication. Parent questionnaires and actigraphy indicated increased sleep disruption on medication. Significant changes in sleep duration, time to fall asleep, and wake episodes were recorded, with no significant changes in sleep efficiency or nocturnal activity. No changes in daytime alertness were reported, but preliminary evidence of hypersomnolence after discontinuation of medication is presented. Results and implications of the study for treatment of children with ADHD are discussed.

authors_raw: Adrienne Junek, Ben Rusak and Kazue Semba
title: Effects of sleep deprivation on hippocampal plasticity in adult rats
abstract: Sleep deprivation for 96 h decreases hippocampal cell proliferation in rats (J Physiol 2003 549:563). We are currently investigating whether sleep deprivation for 6, 12, 24, and 48 h can produce the same effect. We are also investigating any changes in hippocampal PSA-NCAM expression after sleep deprivation. Adult male rats were sleep deprived using a modified Wahmann running wheel, injected with bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU, a marker for cell proliferation) 2 h before the end of the SD, and perfused at the end of SD. Blood samples were also collected for plasma corticosterone analysis in order to assess the stressfulness of SD. Preliminary counts of BrdU-immunoreactive cells in the dentate gyrus show a small but significant increase in cell proliferation after 12 h SD, and a trend for decreased cell proliferation after 24 and 48 h SD.

authors_raw: Erin L. Mazerolle, Ryan C.N. D'Arcy, and R. Bruce Bolster
title: Event-related potential methods for evaluating functional status in epilepsy
abstract: Neuropsychological assessment in temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE) can be augmented by event-related potential (ERP) methods that evaluate the functional status of tissue adjacent to medial epileptogenic foci. Anterolateral temporal regions have been linked to visual object recognition (VOR) in healthy controls. We propose an ERP method for assessing the functional integrity of anterolateral temporal regions using a word-picture matching task. The semantic level of the word (basic versus superordinate) and the object category (natural versus artifactual) were manipulated in order to identify components that are sensitive to intact VOR. Distributed sources were modeled to determine whether these components were localized to anterolateral temporal lobe regions. ERP components that were sensitive to both semantic level and object category manipulations were identified in healthy controls. Furthermore, one of these components was localized to the anterolateral temporal lobes. The findings demonstrate that the spatiotemporal information provided by ERPs can potentially be used to evaluate the functional status of the anterolateral temporal lobes. This approach can be applied in epilepsy assessment to, for example, guide surgical intervention in TLE.

authors_raw: Kim Brewer, Jodie Gawryluk, James Rioux, Chris Bowen, Ryan C.N. D'Arcy and Steven Beyea
title: Spiraling into Control: Overcoming fMRI susceptibility to artifacts in the inferior temporal lobe
abstract: We are developing a site directed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) approach to guide pre-surgical mapping in patients with temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE). Susceptibility effects are the basis of functional imaging, however, susceptibility induced signal loss also occurs at numerous tissue-sinus interfaces. This signal loss creates artifacts in the inferior temporal lobes and orbitofrontal area, thereby limiting the ability to detect functional activation in these regions. The current investigation utilized a 4 tesla imaging system to examine alternative fMRI techniques with the objective of reducing artifacts caused by susceptibility. Specifically, the commonly utilized technique of echo-planar imaging (EPI) was compared to spiral out and spiral in methods of image acquisition. Compared to EPI and spiral out, the spiral in technique has produced promising results. Utilizing spiral in, it is possible to gain functional information at the tissue-sinus interface. To our knowledge, this represents the first demonstration of spiral in methods at 4 tesla. Application of this novel technique will allow for advanced pre-surgical functional mapping research on temporal lobe epilepsy.

authors_raw: Timothy P. O'Leary and Richard E. Brown
title: Learning and memory in C57BL/6J mice on the Barnes maze with a fixed or randomly located escape hole.
abstract: The Barnes circular maze is a visuo-spatial learning and memory task that was originally designed for rats, and has been adapted for use with mice. In the Barnes maze, animals use extra-maze visual cues to locate a hole that provides escape from aversive bright light. To determine if mice use extra-maze visual cues in the Barnes maze, male and female C57BL/6J mice were trained with the escape hole in a fixed location, or moved randomly across trials, so that extra-maze visual cues could not predict the location of the escape hole. Mice trained with the escape hole in a fixed position made fewer errors, traveled shorter distances and searched more accurately during probe trials than mice trained with a randomly positioned escape hole, indicating that mice may use extra-maze visual cues in the Barnes maze. Sex differences were found for some training phases but not others, and will be discussed.

authors_raw: Lisa Wright, Rahia Mashoodh, Kimberly Hébert and Tara Perrot-Sinal
title: The Effects of Repeated Exposure to Cat Odour on Behavioural and Endocrinological Stress Responding in the Rat
abstract: Acute exposure of a rodent to a predatory odour is an ethologically appropriate stressor that induces robust and quantifiable behavioural and physiological responses. Predator odour (PO) exposure therefore models psychological stress experience and may be used to study the long-term consequences of continued or repeated activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and hypersecretion of glucocorticoids (GCs), which are linked to the development of various anxiety and depression-related neuropsychiatric illnesses in both men and women. The goal of the present study was to characterize changes in behavioural and hormonal responses to predation threat across a period of chronic odour exposure, and also to examine sex differences in such changes. This was achieved by exposing groups (n=12) of adult male and female Long Evans rats to a PO or control odour (CO) stimulus for one hour/day over a period of 22 days. Behavioural responses were examined on the first exposure day and every fourth day thereafter. Corticosterone (CORT) levels were assayed from blood samples collected during a baseline condition, and following exposure conditions at the beginning and end of the chronic odour exposure period. Interestingly, patterns of behavioural change across the exposure period were similar for PO and CO exposed rats, although the magnitude of behaviours remained very different. There were also sex-specific alterations in GC release, with both CO and PO exposed females showing higher levels of plasma CORT at the end of the chronic odour exposure period relative to the beginning. In a subsequent study, we will characterize the temporal profile of GC release following initial exposure to PO.

authors_raw: Gillian M. Alcolado & Vincent M. LoLordo
title: Flavour-Flavour Learning in a Devaluation Procedure Controlling for Post-Ingestive Effects.
abstract: We were looking for evidence of flavour-flavour learning across long delays between flavour presentations. Twenty-six male rats received pairings of a neutral flavour with a delayed, differently flavoured carbohydrate, and pairings of a second neutral flavour with another delayed, differently flavoured carbohydrate. A third neutral flavour was presented alone. Then one of the flavoured carbohydrates was devalued by pairing it with an injection of lithium chloride. All rats avoided the devalued flavoured carbohydrate in a test pitting it against the non-devalued flavoured carbohydrate, supporting the effectiveness of the devaluation procedure. Most rats failed to avoid the neutral flavour that had been paired with the devalued carbohydrate when it was pitted against the neutral flavour that had been paired with the non-devalued carbohydrate. However, one subgroup that didn't have mint as one of the flavours paired with a carbohydrate learned the association, suggesting that replication with truly neutral flavours is merited.

title: Utilizing Caffeine Dependence To Condition An Avoidance To A Novel Flavor
abstract:
Male Sprague-Dawley rats were given chronic exposure to caffeine in their drinking water for 21 days in an effort to establish caffeine dependence. They were then given daily 30-minute trials with a Kool-Aid/Saccharin flavor (Maintenance CS), after each of which the caffeinated drinking water was returned. Rats were then given daily 30-minute access to a different Kool-Aid/Saccharin flavor (Withdrawal CS), followed by tap water allowing the rats to go into caffeine withdrawal. All rats were then given a 24-hour two-bottle test pitting the Maintenance and Withdrawal CSs against each other. The rats overwhelmingly avoided the Kool-Aid/Saccharin flavor paired with caffeine withdrawal symptoms, and drank more of the Maintenance CS. Based on these results, we can conclude that caffeine dependence was established, and that dependence can be used to condition an avoidance to a flavor paired with caffeine withdrawal.
POSTERS

title: Richard Brown - The life and Work of Bruce Moore at Dalhousie
Vin Lolordo - Bruce Moore's Contributions to Conditioning
John Christie - Doves in Space
Trish Cole - Parrots and Mynahs and Crows, Oh My!
Chris Moore - Bruce Moore's Contributions to the Phylogeny of Learning 5:00 Closing Comments & Awards Recognition 5:15 Dinner/Reception in the 2nd Floor Lounge

authors_raw: Sherry H. Stewart, Brigitte Sabourin, Margo C. Watt,3 Jaye Wald
title: How does interoceptive exposure work in the treatment of anxiety sensitivity?
abstract:
Anxiety sensitivity (AS) is implicated in the development/maintenance of anxiety disorders. A brief cognitive behavioural (CB) intervention is effective in decreasing fear of anxiety in high AS women (Watt et al., 2006). This process-based study explored the specific role of the interoceptive exposure component (i.e., running) in explaining intervention efficacy. Female undergraduates, selected as either high or low in AS, participated in three 1-hour CB group sessions that included the running component on the third day. Participants also completed ten 10-minute running sessions between the third day of therapy and the 10-week follow-up. Running was effective in increasing heart rate. Compared to low AS participants, high AS participants displayed greater somatic, affective, and cognitive reactions to running, and showed a greater decrease in affective and cognitive reactions over sessions. These preliminary results suggest that interoceptive exposure may exert its effects in decreasing AS by reducing catastrophic thinking about arousal-related sensations.
Note: This talk will be jointly presented by Sherry Stewart and Brigitte Sabourin.

title: Preserving Our History with Digital Video: The Snapshot Project
abstract: Video records documenting the history of the Dalhousie Psychology Department are few in number, and in too many cases rapidly deteriorating. The old analog recordings on magnetic tape will be a difficult challenge to recover. On the other hand, modern digital video recording technology holds promise for increased longevity of archival records, and offers relative ease in recording. In the winter term this year, students in the Psychology/Neuroscience 3010 class began a project to record on digital media a video "snapshot" of the department as it exists today, by interviewing faculty members on camera and then producing a short edited version of each interview. In this presentation, we will describe the project and the training given the students, and show some examples of the resulting material that will go into the departmental archives.

authors_raw: Ana B. Chica, Tracy L. Taylor
title: Two mechanisms underlying Inhibition of Return
abstract: Inhibition of Return (IOR) refers to slower reaction times to respond to targets presented at previously stimulated, inspected, or attended locations (Klein, 2000). Taylor (1999; Taylor & Klein, 2000) showed that IOR can affect both input and output processing, depending on whether the oculomotor system was in a quiescent or in a prepared state, respectively. The motoric flavour of IOR was revealed by the finding that IOR delayed spatial responses even when there was no possibility of an effect on input processing (because there was no target at the location that was supposedly inhibited; the target was a centrally presented arrow). That the "motoric" flavour of IOR did not affect input processing was implied, but not directly demonstrated by Taylor's experiment. If the motoric flavour of IOR is truly non-perceptual and non-attentional, then targets presented at supposedly inhibited locations should not show evidence of this inhibition when the responses to these targets are not spatial. We combed the literature for any studies in which IOR was generated by an eye movement and its effects tested with a non-spatial discrimination task. Finding none, we conducted such a test. Strongly confirming the implication of Taylor's findings, we found that when the eyes move to the peripheral cue and back to the centre before the target appears (to emphasize the motoric component), IOR is generated in a detection task (in which motor preparation is important) but not in a colour discrimination task. The same colour discrimination task showed IOR when eye movements to the cue were prevented (and thus the motoric component of IOR was not activated).

title: Manipulability Affects the Time to Name Objects when they are in Graspable Orientations
abstract: Previous research has shown that during object naming tasks, participants are slower to name objects they could normally pick up with one hand (manipulable) compared to objects they could not pick up. The current research explores whether this effect of manipulablity on object naming is sensitive to the ability to grasp the object as assessed by its orientation. Participants were asked to name both manipulable and nonmanipulable objects shown in three different orientations (rotated 0º, 60º, and 300º clockwise). Preliminary results with twelve right-handers replicate previous research with participants being slower to name manipulable objects and slower to name objects rotated in the picture plane. Further, these results suggest an interaction, with the effect of manipulablity bigger at graspable orientations (300º relative to 60º degrees). This interaction appears to be strongest for the first block of trials, and disappears with practice. These results confirm that effects of manipulability on object naming are related to their ability to be grasped.

authors_raw: Brigitte Sabourin, Dr. Sherry H. Stewart, Dr. Margo C. Watt
title: Sex Differences in Treatment Outcomes of Patients with Comorbid Depression and Substance Use Disorders
abstract: Substance use disorders (SUD) and depression are two prevalent and highly comorbid disorders. It is believed that comorbid depression in women with SUD's tends to be more independent of the SUD and less likely to remit following SUD treatment than comorbid depression in men with SUD's. On the other hand, there remains much uncertainty regarding prognosis of the SUD for individuals with comorbid depression, with studies showing either worse, equal, or better treatment outcomes for comorbid versus non-comorbid individuals. As part of the present study, clients entering treatment at Addiction Prevention and Treatment Services in Capital Health will be assessed for SUD's and depression upon entering treatment and again after three to four months of treatment. Sex differences in rates and in the presentation of comorbid depression will be determined. The study also aims to compare SUD treatment outcomes for women and men with and without comorbid depression, as well as to assess depression outcome after SUD treatment.

authors_raw: Heather Fulton, Sean Barrett and Sherry Stewart
title: Genetics of Early Psychosis and the Influencing Effects of Adolescent Drug Use
abstract: Many argue that schizophrenia develops through a gene by environment interaction. Interestingly, most individuals with schizophrenia abuse drugs making drug use a possible environmental trigger. More specifically, when individuals with an underlying genetic vulnerability use certain drugs at a critical developmental window, an interaction may occur where they develop the disorder. A recent study by Caspi et al. (2005) implicated a particular genetic polymorphism; individuals who had this polymorphism and used cannabis in adolescence were at five-fold increased risk for the development of schizophrenia. However, the investigators did not control for use of several other substances (e.g., tobacco). Thus, the results may not be specific to cannabis. This study will investigate the role of genes in NS Early Psychosis Program patients and matched control participants, and then relate this information to each individual's substance use history to test the gene x environment interaction theory and its specificity to cannabis.

title: Impact of Methylphenidate on Sleep in Children with ADHD
abstract:
Introduction: Children with ADHD are five times more likely to have sleep problems than typically developing children. Sleep problems can cause serious behavioural, learning/cognitive, emotional and social impairments. Our previous research has suggested that the primary treatment for ADHD (i.e., stimulant medications such as Ritalin) may be one of the underlying causes of sleep disturbances in children with ADHD.
Methods: Participants (n=28) were diagnosed with ADHD based on an assessment conducted in a multisystem, multidisciplinary ADHD Clinic. All children from this clinic who were diagnosed with ADHD and were considered appropriate for a trial of stimulant medication were recruited to participate in the study. Sleep was measured using subjective (questionnaires, sleep diaries) and objective (actigraphs) measures during a one week baseline period and a three week randomized stimulant medication trial (placebo, low dose, moderate dose).
Results: Results of repeated measures ANOVA indicated that sleep was negatively affected by stimulant medication. Based on actigraphy, the average change in sleep duration was a decrease of 63 minutes and sleep onset increased by an average of 40 minutes. However, the number of night time awakenings decreased and overall sleep efficiency was not affected. Similar results were found based on the sleep diary data, but not the questionnaire data.
Conclusions: Children with ADHD had typical sleep before starting stimulant medication, however, during the acute medication trial there was a statistically and clinically significant change in their sleep. There was some evidence of increased consolidation of sleep, perhaps a compensatory mechanism for the sleep disruption caused by stimulant medication. Further prospective research examining both the short and long term implications of stimulant medication on sleep in children with ADHD is needed.

authors_raw: Nicolle Vincent, Christine Chambers, Penny Corkum, Ben Rusak
title: Self-reported sleep difficulties in a community-based sample of children and adolescents with recurrent pain
abstract: Objectives: In clinical samples of children and adolescents who experience recurrent pain, there is evidence of more disturbed sleep compared to those without chronic pain complaints. This study examines self-reported sleep difficulties in a community-based sample with recurrent pain in comparison to a pain-free control group. Possible mediating variables are also explored. Methods: Participants were ninety children and adolescents, 8-15 years of age, who experienced recurrent pain or were healthy and pain free. Participants completed self-report measures of their sleep, pain, functional disability, depression, coping style, and family functioning. Results: Participants with recurrent pain reported significantly more sleep difficulties than did those in the pain-free control group. There was a significant positive association between increased reports of sleep difficulties and higher scores for pain (frequency, duration, intensity). Further analyses will be conducted to identify possible mediating variables. Conclusions: These results suggest that sleep disturbance is associated with pain in a community-based sample of children and adolescents with recurrent pain.

authors_raw: Lau E.Y.Y., Eskes G.A., Morrison D.L., Rajda, M., Spurr K.F
