title: Effects of Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea on Daily Functions, Mood and Quality of
abstract: Introduction: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is characterized by disrupted breathing during sleep, leading to hypoxemia and fragmented sleep at night, daytime sleepiness, and changes in cognition and mood. Here, we report preliminary analyses of an ongoing study of the effects of CPAP treatment on sleepiness, sleep quality, daily functions, mood, and quality of life (QoL). Methods: Thirty CPAP-treated patients with moderate to severe OSA were studied with an overnight polysomnographic sleep study, measures of sleepiness and sleep quality, mood, functional outcomes, cognition, and QoL. Pre-treatment respiratory disturbance index (RDI), mean oxygen saturation (SpO2), sleep quality, and sleepiness, and retrospective ratings on functional outcomes and QoL were also available. Results: CPAP improved breathing disruptions, sleep quality, daytime sleepiness, functional outocmes, and QoL. Post-treatment mood, functional outcomes, and quality of life are predicted by sleepiness and sleep quality. Conclusion: CPAP is an effective treatment for OSA in ameliorating breathing disruption and oxygen desaturation during sleep. However, there are residual dfficulties in daytime outcomes associated with sleepiness and sleep quality.

title: Sleep is the Best Remedy for Sleep Loss, But How are our Hormones Affecting our Ability to Recover Sleep? --A Case for Females
abstract: We investigated the effects of ovariectomy with or without estrogen and/or progesterone replacement on spontaneous sleep and recovery sleep after 6 h of sleep deprivation in female rats. Male rats were also included for comparison. Females were ovariectomized and implanted subcutaneously with capsules containing oil, estrogen, progesterone or both hormones. Males received oil-filled capsules. All were implanted with EEG and EMG electrodes. During the baseline recording, ovariectomized rats without hormone replacement tended to spend more time in both non-rapid eye movement (NREM) and REM sleep than those with estrogen and/or progesterone replacement and males. Following 6-hour sleep deprivation, ovariectomized rats without hormone replacement were much slower in recovering REM sleep (but not NREM sleep) than the other groups, whereas ovariectomized rats with the highest dose of estrogen showed the lowest NREM EEG delta power (a measure of sleep intensity). These results indicate that ovarian hormones can significantly affect REM sleep and NREM delta power recovery in female rats.

title: The Role of Superordinate Labels on Categorical Flexibility in 3- and 4-year-olds
abstract: The purpose of the current study was to investigate the effect of superordinate labels on categorical flexibility in 3- and 4-year-olds using two match-to-sample tasks that assess categorization and cognitive flexibility. It was expected that both 3- and 4-year-olds would perform better on the categorization task than on the cognitive flexibility task. In addition, 4-year-olds were expected to improve in a label condition on both the categorization task and the cognitive flexibility task, but 3-year-olds were not. As predicted, 4-year-olds performed better than 3-year-olds on both versions of both tasks and performance was better on the categorization task than on the cognitive flexibility task for both age groups. Surprisingly, however, 4-year-olds did not benefit from labels on either task but 3-year-olds did. Results are discussed in terms of the role that different labels might have on categorical flexibility.

authors_raw: E.L. Laur, E.Y.Y. Lau, G.A. Eskes, D. Morrison, M. Rajda
title: Improvements in Quality of Life in Patients with Obstructive Sleep Apnea using Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) Treatment is Related to Decreased Sleepiness and Improved Mood.
abstract: Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) is a disorder characterized by brief breathing disruptions during sleep. Symptoms include excessive daytime sleepiness, depression, and decreased memory, attention and quality of life (QoL). Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) is the standard treatment for OSA. CPAP keeps the airway open to eliminate disrupted breathing and is effective in restoring sleep and improving daytime symptoms. The current study aimed to investigate the role of sleepiness and mood on QoL in patients with OSA. We hypothesized that both mood and residual sleepiness are associated with QoL after using CPAP. Thirty patients with moderate to severe OSA were asked to complete questionnaires to measure sleepiness, mood, and QoL. CPAP was found to significantly improve QoL (t(24)=-8.977, p=0.00) and decrease sleepiness (t(28)=6.323, p=0.00). QoL was negatively correlated with sleepiness r(29)=-0.605, p=0.00) and depressed mood r(29)=-0.629, p=0.00). Our findings suggest that the effects of CPAP treatment on sleepiness and depression are associated with improvement in QoL.

authors_raw: Jennifer C. Mullane, MA, Jenna L. Bower, BSc, & Penny V. Corkum, PhD
title: Sleep Problems and Performance on a New Test of Attention in Children with and without ADHD
abstract: Few studies have examined the relationship between sleep disturbances and attention in children and even fewer have investigated this association in children with ADHD. This pilot study examined differences in sleep problems between children with and without ADHD and explored the effects of sleep disturbances on three networks of attention as measured by the Attention Network Test (ANT). Participants were 24 children with ADHD-Combined Type (Mage = 110.58 mths), 17 children with ADHD-Inattentive Type (Mage = 116.12 mths), and 45 typically developing children (Mage = 115.32 mths). Children with both subtypes of ADHD had significantly more sleep problems than controls, as measured by the Children‚s Sleep Habits Questionnaire. Partial correlation analyses found that sleep disturbances were related to less efficient performance on the ANT‚s measure of executive attention, but only in the ADHD-Combined Type group.

title: Children's Attributions for Ambiguous Intentions: Hostile or Outcome Driven?
abstract: The current study examined three possible reasons for why aggressive children make hostile attributions of intent in ambiguous situations: social learning deficits, theory of mind deficits, or innate predispositions. Thirty-five typically developing children between the ages of 44 and 87 months were assessed on their tendency to make hostile attributions using an extended version of the hostile attribution bias interview. This extended interview included vignettes with both positive and negative outcomes as well as hostile, ambiguous, and benign intentions. Results indicated a main effect of intent and a trend toward a significant age group effect, but no effect of story outcome and no significant interactions. Results are discussed in terms of existing theories and limitations of the current study.

authors_raw: Sarah E. Dreumont-Boudreau & Vincent M. LoLordo
title: The Role of Caffeine Exposure and Dependence in a Conditioned Flavor Avoidance Paradigm
abstract: Two experiments assessed the value of a flavor that signaled 24 hrs of caffeine exposure (Signal CS) in male Sprague-Dawley rats. In Experiment 1, naïve rats were given a Kool-Aid flavor followed by 24 hr access to caffeine. Several days later, they were given a different flavor followed by water (Neutral CS). When given a choice between the two flavors, rats avoided the Signal CS and consumed more of the Neutral CS. Avoidance of the Signal CS was maintained even after the rats were made dependent on caffeine. In Experiment 2, Group 1 was maintained on 21 days chronic caffeine, while Group 2 was maintained on water. Both groups were then given a Signal CS followed by 24 hrs of caffeine, and several days later, a Neutral CS followed by water. Group 2 avoided the Signal CS, and consumed more of the Neutral CS. However, Group 1 consumed equivalent amounts of both the Signal and Neutral CSs.

title: Caffeine dependence in rats: Effects of dose and exposure on conditioned flavour avoidance.
abstract: In order to examine the parameters of oral caffeine dependence, groups of rats were chronically exposed to caffeine-tainted water (1.0 g/L) for 5, 10, 15 and 20 days. Upon removal of the caffeine, rats were exposed to one of two novel Kool-Aid flavours (CS Withdrawal). Twelve days later, rats were exposed to the second Kool-Aid flavour (CS Neutral). A 24-hour 2-bottle test showed that rats that had been exposed to caffeine for 20 days, but not 5, 10 or 15 days, strongly preferred the CS Neutral. In a second experiment, groups of rats were given caffeine-tainted water for 21 days at one of four concentrations (1.0 g/L, 0.5 g/L, 0.25 g/L, and 0.125 g/L), after which withdrawal and neutral CSs were established. Only rats that drank the 1.0 g/L caffeine solution strongly preferred the CS Neutral. This suggests that long exposure to a strong caffeine solution is required in order to induce dependence in rats such that a CS associated with the withdrawal of caffeine becomes avoided.

title: The Effect of Consumption of Kola Nut (Cola Nitida) Diet on Locomotor Behaviour and Anxiety in Mice
abstract: Kola nuts are eaten in Nigeria (mostly in the Northern part) during ceremonies and as stimulants. The long term effect on behaviour is yet known. Following chronic consumption (4 weeks feeding) of kola nut (cola nitida) diet (50%w/w), patterns of locomotor behaviour and anxiety were studied in 16 male Swiss white mice (18-32g body weight), using the open field, elevated plus maze and the light/dark transition box. Mice in the control group (n = 8) were fed normal rodent chow while those in the kola group (n = 8) were fed kola diet (50% kola, 50% rodent chow) for 4 weeks. All animals were allowed free access to clean drinking water. The frequency of line crossing in the open did not differ between the kola and control groups. The frequency of rearing against the wall did not differ, but the frequency of rearing in the open field was lower in the kola group (p< 0.05). Grooming and genital licking were also lower in the kola group compared to control (p< 0.001, 0.05 respectively). In the elevated plus maze the duration in the open arm in the kola group was higher than the control group (p< 0.01). The frequency and duration of head dips did not differ. The frequency and duration in the closed arm were lower in the kola group compared to control (p< 0.05). The duration of grooming, genital licking in the kola group was higher in the closed arm compared with control (p< 0.01). The kola fed animals spent more time in the light region of the light/dark transition box (p< 0.01), showed more rearing (p< 0.05), and spent less time in the dark region when compared with controls. These results indicate that consumption of kola nut diet decreases anxiety-related behaviour in mice in the elevated plus maze and the light/dark transition box but does not affect locomotor activity in the open field.

authors_raw: Jennifer Stamp, Danny Mendelsohn, Michael Wong, David Cyr, & Heather Schellinck
title: Anxiety and Risk-Taking in a Mouse Model of Sensation Seeking
abstract: Sensation-seeking is a personality trait characterized by the desire to experience novelty. Sensation seeking is typically associated with low anxiety, high risk-taking and vulnerability to drug abuse. One potential animal model of human sensation-seeking is high response to novelty in rats. High responders (HRs) choose novel environment over a familiar one and show an increase in locomotor activity in the new environment. These rats also display lower levels of anxiety and an enhanced propensity to self-administer drugs of abuse. This project had two main aims: 1) to determine whether the low anxiety in HR rats is also demonstrable in C57 mice and 2) to use two tests of anxiety (open field and elevated plus maze) as well as a test to specifically assess the dimension of risk taking (predator exposure). HR mice were distinguished from their LR counterparts based upon the amount of activity in an open field. In the open field, HRs spent more time in the "risky" center and less time in the "safe" corners compared to LRs. There were no significant differences between HRs and LRs in any of the measures in the elevated-plus maze but HR mice spent significantly more time than LRs in an area of a predator arena which was in proximity to a food-deprived rat. These data suggest that attributes of humans sensation-seeking are demonstrable in mice.

title: What route do visual currents take in generating the fly's electroretinogram (ERG)?
abstract:
The ERG is an easily recorded, generic visual response, neglected since the 1960s but recently resurrected as an assay tool for investigating the molecular biology of synaptic transmission in Drosophila. Using the blowfly Calliphora, I'm attempting to determine the real origin of the ERG, mistakenly believed to have been solved long ago.
Transient ERG currents generated in the first synaptic zone add arithmetically to those generated by the retina, but the direction of current flow uncovered here is unconventional. Currents move out from the synapse into the retina, surprisingly so as the retina appears to be a cul-de-sac surrounded by impervious cuticle. That the cornea is actually a good conductor has been confirmed by tracking responses over it with a new fangled surface electrode, after current has been injected alternately on the cornea then just inside it. ERG currents behaved similarly: the cornea has low internal resistance, though a barrier exists at its intersection with head cuticle.
By tracking the angular response of ERG components over ~250°, ERG currents have been found to enter the cornea only where the light source illuminates active neurons underneath. Some of this current leaves again from 'inactive', unilluminated zones, while the rest loops out around the eye, giving rise to the recorded ERG.

title: Plasticity of microcircuits in the insect central nervous system
abstract: The goal of our project is to study cellular components of neuronal plasticity and their outcomes in the behavioural plasticity of two model insect species (the honeybee Apis mellifera and the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster). In these species, synaptic complexes within the first optic neuropil and within the synaptic input region of the mushroom body (a higher sensory integration centre required for olfactory learning and memory) facilitate the investigation of fundamental principles of neuronal structural plasticity. My previous work revealed specific, environmentally induced and age-dependent structural plasticity within the brain of female honeybees, but there is still little information about the underlying cellular, subcellular and molecular events underlying such changes. Using electron microscopy and immunofluorescence labelling of filamentous (f-) actin, which is enriched in dendrites, my proposed project seeks to narrow the gap between two hypotheses that: a) developmental and adult plasticity of these synaptic circuits in the insect brain may underlie behavioural plasticity; and b) the source of dendritic structural plasticity is based on the dynamics of motile f-actin.

authors_raw: Shini-ya Takemura, Zhiyuan Lu and Ian Meinertzhagen
title: Synaptic circuits formed by input neurons to the fly's optic medulla
abstract: The visual system of a fly is intricate, precise, and supports rapid vision. Photoreceptor inputs are received at the two outermost neuropiles of the optic lobe. Many classes of optic lobe neuron, each with a unique morphological signature, are reported from Golgi impregnation. Their synaptic circuits are known completely in the first neuropile, or lamina, but remain a formidable challenge in the second, the medulla. The shape of a neuron is only a guide to how it may connect with other neurons, which must ultimately be investigated by electron microscopy (EM). Most synaptic sites are divergent dyads, and much of the neuropile comprises interneurons and their local microcircuits. Using: a) unlabelled EM series of the distal medulla; and b) the Gal4/UAS system to label specific neurons at EM level, we are analysing synaptic connections of lamina input terminals. Spectral sensitivity is encoded by photoreceptors R7 and R8, and motion sensitivity by lamina cells L1 and L2. Remarkably, R8 provides input to R7 and also to L1, structural evidence for a pathway between colour and motion-sensitive channels.

title: Immunocytochemical localization of synaptic and other proteins in the photoreceptor terminals of the fruit fly, Drosophila melanogaster.
abstract: The fruit fly, Drosophila melanogaster, is a useful model organism to study the localization of the proteins that contribute to synaptic function because it is genetically manipulable and because its neurons have a distinctive synaptic ultrastructure that includes a specialized pre-synaptic organelle at the release site, T-bar ribbon. The latter comprises a platform surmounting a pedestal. I examined the protein composition of the presynaptic T-bar ribbon and its associated synaptic organelles, at photoreceptor terminals of D. melanogaster. After validating candidate antibodies by confocal microscopy, pre-embedding immuno-EM methods showed that four monoclonal antibodies all clearly label distinct synaptic structures: nc82 (Bruchpilot) and SUK4 (which labels a kinesin epitope) label the T-bar ribbon, nc82 at the edge of the platform, and SUK4 close to its pedestal; anti-discs large recognizes the photoreceptor head of capitate projections that invaginate from surrounding glia; while aa2/2 (raised against a homogenate of D. melanogaster brains) localizes to the synaptic vesicle pool.

title: Ectopic synapses in the visual system of the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster
abstract: The visual system of the fruit fly is remarkable for its numerical and spatial determinacy, especially at its identified photoreceptor synapses. In Drosophila we can examine ectopic synaptogenesis by genetic means, by targeting photoreceptor axons to an incorrect neuropile in the brain. Photoreceptors are of two types, R1-R6 that innervate the first optic neuropile, the lamina, and R7-R8 that innervate the second, the medulla. Target cells within the lamina persist and maintain expression of cell-specific markers, even when their normal R1-R6 photoreceptor inputs terminate in the medulla. Analysis of lamina ultrastructure reveals that the axons of these mis-targeted photoreceptors, even though they penetrate the lamina, nevertheless form reciprocal synaptic inputs with their normal lamina targets. In addition, these mis-targeted R1-R6 photoreceptors also form ectopic synapses within the medulla, thus providing input to two neuropiles. A subset of these ectopic synapses are characteristic of photoreceptor synapses in the lamina, tetrads with four postsynaptic partners, which are otherwise not found in the R7-R8 photoreceptors that normally terminate within the medulla.

title: The influence of heavy metals on the morphology and plasticity of neurons in the fly's visual system
abstract: The insect's brain provides an excellent, expendible model to study basic processes occurring in neurons. Using the housefly's brain, I have previously examined the toxic effects of heavy metals (lead, cadmium, zinc and copper) on neuronal morphology and plasticity. My analysis of neurotoxicity concentrated on the influence of these metals on the plasticity of two classes of monopolar cell, L1 and L2, in the lamina of the optic lobe. Changes in the volume of these interneurons depended on the type of metal and its concentration, and revealed that heavy metals penetrate the haemolymph-brain barrier. Rare genetic errors in metal metabolism, or accumulation of metals through environmental or occupational over-exposure can also elevate brain metals to cause neurodegeneration. When they degenerate, neurons take up heavy metals, probably because fail to pump them. With workers at the Canadian Light Source, we will use X-Ray absorption spectroscopy to image the metal profile of fly brains undergoing neural degeneration. For this, we are examining a fly model of Alzheimer's Disease, mutant for the human gene for human Aβ42 amyloid peptides, a major component of senile plaques, and are developing methods to image the brains of these flies non-invasively, as a model for the progression of neurodegenerative disease in humans.

authors_raw: Janusz Borycz, Jola Borycz, Rita Kostyleva and Ian Meinertzhagen
title: Drosophila mutants white, scarlet and brown differ not only in their eye colour but also in the distribution and head content of biogenic amines
abstract:
The white, scarlet and brown genes in Drosophila melanogaster are named because their mutants have these abnormal eye colours. white mutant flies also have a behavioural phenotype that indicates a neural action. The genes encode ABC transporters involved with the uptake and storage of precursors of pigment granules. We find additional effects on head biogenic amines: all three have about half the normal head histamine (HA), a neurotransmitter at photoreceptor terminals, as well as reduced 5-HT; and white also has reduced dopamine (DA).
The mutants also have abnormal cellular distributions of HA, 5-HT and DA. In centrifugates of wild-type brain homogenates, a vesicle-enriched pellet fraction contains ~75% of head HA with 25% in the supernatant. In white, brown and scarlet these proportions are reversed. Similar determinations reveal a reversed ratio between supernatant and pellet fractions for 5-HT and DA as well, and mutant photoreceptor terminals have reduced numbers of synaptic vesicles. We suggest that these three genes may influence the intravesicular contents of the neurotransmitters histamine, serotonin and dopamine in neurons, so as to affect transmission by these neurons.

authors_raw: Birgit Greiner, Nicole Delaney, Nancy Butcher, Rorbert M. Marshall and Ian A. Meinertzhagen
title: Drosophila synapses: protein complexes viewed using EM tomography
abstract: Genes programme the physiology of synapses by coding for the synaptic proteins that regulate synaptic function. However, conventional electron microscopy of thin sections is unable to visualise the organisation of such protein complexes because these overlap each other within the depth (~50nm) of an ultrathin section. Recent developments in EM tomography solve the problem of overlap, deriving depth information from wide-angle image tilt series, and revealing structure at the sub-nanometre resolution inherent in an electron beam. Using EM tomography methods we have reconstructed the T-bar ribbons and other synaptic organelles at the release sites of two types of Drosophila wild-type synapse, the neuromuscular junction and photoreceptor tetrad synapses of the fly's lamina. These reconstructions provide a basis against which we will compare structural perturbations in synapses of flies mutant for the genes, such as endophilin or bruchpilot, coding for synaptic proteins.

authors_raw: Stephen Porter, Michael T. Woodworth and Marcus Juodis
title: That's what Friends are For: A Comparison of the Qualities of Single- and Dual-Perpetrator Murders
abstract: To date, there has been no investigation of possible qualitative differences between homicides committed by perpetrators acting alone versus those who acted with an accomplice. In this presentation, the findings from the first empirical study of single- versus dual-perpetrator homicides will be described. This study involves a thorough examination of the official crime descriptions of 124 Canadian homicides, 84 involving a single perpetrator and 40 involving two perpetrators. The crimes are being compared in terms of perpetrator motive, instrumental/ reactive violence, sadistic violence, gratuitous violence, sexual elements, and victim characteristics. Preliminary findings suggest that dual-perpetrator homicides are associated with a higher level of instrumental aggression (i.e., premeditation, external gain) than single-perpetrator homicides. Further, murders by single perpetrators were significantly more likely to display sadistic and sexual violence than murders by dual-perpetrators. Single perpetrators were more likely to target female victims, whereas dual perpetrators targeted both males and females.

title: Conflict in Dating Relationships
abstract: The present study assessed the relationship between dating abuse and irrational thinking and the hostile attributional bias. This was done using the Conflict in Adolescent Dating Relationships Inventory (CADRI) as the measure of past abusive behavior, the Survey of Personal Beliefs (SPB) as the measure of irrationality, and the rating of a video depicting an ambiguous interaction between two triangles as a measure of the hostile attributional bias. It was hypothesized that greater levels of abuse would be significantly related to higher levels of irrational thinking, and greater incidence of the hostile attributional bias. Hierarchical regression analysis indicated a small, but significant, relationship between abuse and irrational thinking (particularly, the inability to tolerate frustration). Limitations and future directions are discussed.

authors_raw: Marc Zahradnik, Sherry H. Stewart, Doreen Stevens, Christine Wekerle, & Christopher Musquash
title: A collaborative, community based exploration of how post-traumatic chronic arousal acts as a mediator between exposure to violence and subsequent alcohol use in a sample of school attending Mi'kmaq adolescents
abstract: This study is part of a larger ongoing collaborative mental health initiative between our lab and a Nova Scotian Mi'kmaq community. Given the strong relationship that post-traumatic stress (PTS) symptoms have with both childhood exposure to violence (EV) and substance misuse, particularly alcohol, we hypothesized that--based on the "self-medication" hypothesis--in a sample of school attending Mi'kmaq adolescents, PTS symptoms will mediate the relationship between EV and subsequent alcohol use (AU). From a total sample of 166 (52% female, 42% male, & 6% unknown) adolescents (minimum age 14), individual regression analyses (Baron & Kenny, 1986) yielded empirical support for chronic arousal PTS symptoms, total PTS symptoms, and depressive symptoms as "partial mediating" variables that help explain the relationship of EV with later AU. However, a regression analysis that included all relevant predictors--EV, each of the 3 PTS symptoms, total PTS symptoms, and depressive symptoms--resulted in a final model that accounts for 28% of the variance in predicting AU, and in which only the chronic arousal symptom cluster of PTS and EV remained significant.

authors_raw: Jonathan Fawcett and Tracy L. Taylor
title: Incidental forgetting as a by-product of intentional forgetting
abstract: The present study explored the consequences of item-method directed forgetting on incidental learning of probe words. During the study phase, study words were presented individually followed by an instruction to remember (R) or forget (F). Pink and blue probe words were presented at stimulus onset asynchronies (SOAs) of 1400, 1800 or 2600 ms in relation to each memory instruction; participants were required to make a speeded colour discrimination of each probe word. After the study trials, a yes-no recognition task measured retention of R and F study words and incidental learning of probe words. Recognition data indicated a significant directed forgetting effect, with greater recognition of R than F study words. Importantly, F instructions increased discrimination RTs and impeded later recognition of probe words presented at the earliest SOA. These findings demonstrate that intentional forgetting interferes with the formation of incidental memories. These results are inconsistent with the dominant hypothesis in the literature, which suggests that forgetting is accomplished by allowing F items to passively decay from working memory.

title: Temporal Order Judgement in Regards to First Base Plays in Baseball
abstract:
Sports fans, players and coaches can get very heated about important games, often disagreeing with the umpire's calls. Basic research on the effect of attention on temporal order judgments suggest that these disagreements may occur because the spectators and umpires are attending different regions of space. We sought to determine if attending to different regions in space would cause a difference in the decision of an individual to call a play "safe" or "out". In E1 participants were instructed to attend to the ball or the foot. The results showed that attention
mattered: when attending the foot there were more "safe" calls than when attending the ball. In E2, when participants were instructed to attend to the glove or the base, no significant effect of attention was found. It was hypothesized that the participants may have been able to attend both the base and the glove due to their proximity. In E3 participants were instructed to attend both stationary objects, or both moving objects. It was found that the data was similar to that of E2 and there was no significant difference between the attend stationary and the attend moving conditions. Across all trials there was a bias to call "safe" more often than "out".

title: Psychophysical Quantification of Configural Face Processing Using Just Noticeable Differences
abstract: To quantify the degree to which upright faces are processed with a 'configural processor' relative to inverted faces and objects, psychophysical, just-noticeable differences (jnds) were determined for displacement of critical features (e.g. eyes, windows) within upright and inverted faces and houses and other objects. Stimuli were closely matched on low-level visual features of size, luminance, and shape. Observers were most sensitive to displacement of critical features in upright faces. Sensitivity was equivalent for inverted faces, upright houses and inverted houses. This technique provides quantified differences in the degree to which configural processing is used.

title: Low-Level Processes Contribute to the Production of the Inhibition of Return Effect
abstract: The ability to detect and orient to unexpected novel changes in our environment is one of the primary functions of attention. Inhibition of return (IOR) is thought to be a functional after-effect of visuo-spatial orienting, defined as an increase in response times to a target when it is presented at a previously stimulated location compared to when it appears at a new location. Recently it was shown that IOR is correlated with the decrement in the visual response of superior colliculus neurons to a target after responding to a cue at the same location (Dorris et al, 2002). Furthermore, for a sequence of visual stimuli, a smaller visual response occurs with each subsequent stimulus presentation, likely through a process of adaptation and/or habituation (Boehnke et al, 2006). This suggests that the IOR effect should grow as a function of the number of times a cue is presented prior to the target. To investigate this, we used multiple cue presentations (blocked by number of cues) and multiple cue identities in a Posner cueing task requiring a manual response to a target. The results of the experiments demonstrate that habituation and/or adaptation can occur based on a stimulus' location and affects the timing of subsequent behavior, providing further evidence that low-level sensory processes play a role in producing the IOR effect.

title: Black and White Like Me
abstract: John Griffin's classic on racism, Black Like Me, provides an interesting text in which to investigate the development of a dialogical self. Griffin becomes a black man for only a short period of time, but during that time he develops a black social identity that contrasts radically with his former white identity. He develops a schizoid sense of self. He is white and he is black. When he looks into a mirror on several occasions he engages in a dialogue with himself, as both a black and a white person. At first these two identities are so different that there is no "sympathy" between them. But through his experience, he eventually overcomes the dichotomy of two opposing selves, and acquires a personal identity, neither white nor black, but just human. In this talk, I will trace the development of these dialogical selves and the emergence of this new human identity.

title: The development of 'third-party' gaze following
abstract: By 9 months of age, infants will typically follow the gaze of an interactive partner to a target outside of the immediate visual field. We know from recent research (e.g. Floor & Akhtar, 2006) that infants are only able to learn words and actions in purely observational (or 'third party') contexts from about 18 months. In this study we examined when infants would follow the gaze of someone with whom they are not interacting. Infants of 12 and 18 months observed an interaction between their mothers and an experimenter during which the two adults periodically turned to look at an interesting sight. Coding of infants' tendency to follow the adults' gaze showed that whereas 12-month-olds did not follow gaze, 18-month-olds did. This difference was not due to age-related differences in the tendency to look at the adults. These results are consistent with the idea that young infants learn best in interactive joint attentional settings but by the end of infancy can start to learn purely through observation.

title: THE RUBBER HAND ILLUSION: Intermodal Perception and Self-Ownership in Adults
abstract: Mental representation of the body arises from the intricate relationship between intermodal sensory input and a continually changing conceptual diagram. Any interference to this process, such as when a person views a rubber hand being stimulated in synchrony with the occluded real hand, will lead to a misperception as tactile sensations are falsely referred to the non-body part. Our research is focused on further analyzing the nature of the rubber hand illusion using conditions of intentional (goal directed) and random movement. We predict that intentional movement will generate a stronger rubber hand illusion based on previous research that links volition with body perception. Results failed to produce a robust rubber hand illusion but revealed a possible attentional component to the process of body identification. In the present study we plan to replicate the previous experiment with the inclusion of a goal-directed movement condition and measure of attention.

title: I feel the same way: Recognizing facial emotion as appropriate to context.
abstract: Almost all research in emotion recognition to date focuses on categorizing exaggerated facial displays of primary emotions. Little research has focused on how we recognize more complex emotional expressions in their natural contexts - in particular in contexts in which we share emotions with others but do not explicitly label them. The main purpose of our current project is to develop a paradigm in which natural emotional expression to given contexts are used in research on emotion recognition. We focus on the ability to recognize when someone is sharing an emotional reaction similar to self with respect to a stimulus context, rather than on the more cognitive/linguistic task of labelling that emotion. We expect that the development of such a paradigm will open up new ground for investigating the basic processes involved in understanding shared emotional states, where articulation through use of emotion category labels is not involved.

authors_raw: Leanne ten Brinke & Dr. Steve Porter
title: Reading between the Lies: An examination of cues to deception in emotional facial expressions
abstract: Since the 1960s, Ekman and colleagues have argued that certain aspects of facial communication are not controllable and that inconsistent emotional expressions, including "micro-expressions", can betray a deceiver's true emotion. Despite the widespread popularity of this idea, it has received little empirical attention. This study compared genuine and deceptive (simulated, neutralized, masked) facial expressions. Each 1/30th second frame of the videotaped clips then was coded for the presence and presentation time of the universal emotional expressions in the upper and lower facial regions. Results indicated that inconsistent expressions occurred significantly more frequently in masked than in genuine expressions. Additionally, inconsistent expressions were more frequent and longer in falsified negative (fear, disgust, and sadness) than happiness expressions. Micro-expressions occurred in 21.95% of participants in 2% of all expressions (14 micro-expressions in total), and all involved the upper or lower face only (unlike the traditional "full face" conceptualization). Implications for identifying concealed emotions in forensic and security settings will be discussed.

title: Parenting Stress and Maternal Depression in Rural Mothers
abstract: Over the past 10-15 years, a consensus has emerged indicating that the causes of parental stress, dysfunctional parenting and child outcomes are best understood using dynamic multivariate models that acknowledge the interaction between variables and the changes in behaviours and cognitions in the parent and child as a result of recurring interactions (Abidin, 1990). Accepted models of parenting stress encompass child characteristics, parent characteristics, environmental characteristics and parent child interactive stress. Previous research has identified strong links between maternal psychological distress/ functioning and parenting stress, dysfunctional parental behaviours, and the social and emotional well being of the child. The stress of parenting is great for all parents. Unique aspects of rural life can compound the factors of parental stress. These include social isolation, employment issues and a general lack of available resources, such as access to health care and childcare. The connections between these sources of rural stress and parenting stress have not been studied. In addition, the mediating role of maternal depression on the direct pathway from general stress to parenting stress has not been investigated. A conceptual model for understanding these connections between rural experiences of stress, maternal depression, and parenting stress will be discussed in this presentation.

authors_raw: Hilary D. Duncan, S. H. Deacon, Jean Saint-Aubin
title: How Does the Reader Read -er: Using the Missing Letter Task to Investigate Lexical Access of Morphologically Complex Words
abstract: Dual-route models of complex word processing posit two access routes to the lexicon: a direct-access route, wherein the word's full-form representation is accessed via the whole word; and a decompositional route, wherein the component morphemes are accessed separately. Evidence for a dual-route model comes from lexical decision tasks and eye-movement monitoring during fluent reading. These studies have shown that the time needed to identify morphologically complex words is influenced by whole-word and component frequencies. The aim of the current study is to investigate the ability of the Missing Letter Effect task to replicate these results. Sixty-three university students were asked to search for the target letter -r while reading a prose passage containing high and low frequency one-morpheme and derived words. More omissions were made on one-morpheme than derived words when they were low-frequency, but there was no difference when they were high-frequency. Results support recent eye-movement studies that posit a dual-route model of lexical access.

authors_raw: Jaime Williams Jordan Green, and Jeannette McGlone Capitol District Health Authority
title: Does the risk of memory loss influence decision making by physicians and patients who contemplate temporal lobe resection for control of seizures?
abstract: Temporal lobe resection (TLR) is a successful, although underutilized treatment for complex partial seizures. One reason for this underutilized may be the perceived risk of severe and permanent memory loss. However, patients' and physicians' perceptions of the risk of memory loss following TLR have not been empirically investigated. In this presentation, we propose a series of studies using a triangulation of methodologies examining whether fear of memory loss contributes to decision-making about elective brain surgery. First, we propose conducting focus groups with non-surgical, pre and post TLR patients to uncover their thoughts and attitudes towards TLR surgery and the risk of memory loss. Second, we will survey epilepsy specialists querying their thoughts regarding memory loss including how they communicate the risk to patients and what role the risk has in recommending and proceeding with TLR. Finally, we will explore information available on the Internet regarding TLR and the risk of memory loss, including the accuracy and consistency of the information. Implications of the research will be discussed including the importance of effective epilepsy-related knowledge transfer and critical comments will be invited.

authors_raw: Nicola Hoffman, Kazue Semba, Jennifer Stamp, Richard Brown
title: Selective cholinergic basal forebrain lesions using anti-murine-p75-saporin (mu p75-saporin): a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease?
abstract: Degeneration of cholinergic neurons in the basal forebrain (BF) is a prominent characteristic of Alzheimer's disease (AD). One current animal model developed to mimic BF cholinergic neuron atrophy observed in human AD is via injecting neurotoxins into the mouse brain. Our long-term research aims are to selectively lesion mouse BF cholinergic neurons and then examine the cognitive and other functional deficits associated with this cell loss. Mu p75-saporin is an immunotoxin that has been shown to selectively destroy cholinergic neurons in the medial septum (MS), but demonstrates concurrent cell loss in cerebellar Purkinje cells. In the present study we examined the efficacy of the recently developed, presumably improved mu p75-saporin in selectively lesioning BF cholinergic neurons in the mouse brain. One of three doses (1.0, 1.5, or 2.0 μg/μl) of the immunotoxin or vehicle was infused bilaterally into the lateral ventricles of male C57BL/6J mice. Two weeks following injections, mice were intra-cardially perfused, the brains removed and sectioned for immunohisto-chemistry to visualize cholinergic and a subtype of GABAergic BF neurons, as well as acetylcholinesterase (AChE) histochemistry. Quantitative analysis revealed a reduction of cholinergic neurons in the MS/VDB following intracerebroventricular injections of mu p75-saporin, but only a trend for a decrease of cholinergic neurons in the nBM. The density of AChE-postive fibers in the hippocampus and somatosensory cortex was not significantly altered following neurotoxin injection, although there was a trend for a decrease in the hippocampus. No loss of noncholinergic neurons in the MS or nBM was apparent, nor was there a loss of noncholinergic cerebellar Purkinje cells. The results indicate that the most recently developed mu p75-saporin has an increased selectivity to cholinergic MS neurons, compared with the previous formula, and may be useful as a selective cholinergic immunotoxin for mice. Mu 75-saporin may be a useful tool to create an animal model of AD and expand research concerning the progression and neuropathology of the disease.

title: "Don't Look at a White Bear!" Action Suppression in Adults
abstract: The purpose of this study was to develop an objective measure of action suppression. In this task, participants were required to inhibit looking at the content of a picture cue, while still using its location to inform them about the location of target words to which they had to respond. Although reaction time data showed few effects, accuracy data showed that participants were unable to ignore the content of cues at SOA of 300 ms. Results are discussed in relation to prior thought suppression research and findings in the attention literature (Wegner et al., 1987; Wegner & Zanakos, 1994; Jonides, 1981; Danziger & Kingstone, 2005).

title: Memory complaints after temporal lobe resection are the exception, not the rule.
abstract: Temporal lobe resection (TLR) is an efficacious and cost effective treatment for medically intractable complex partial seizures. A risk of mesial TLR is memory loss. Three converging methods asked, "Are patients aware of memory loss in their daily lives one year after surgery?" Method I examined whether 64 TLR patients from Nova Scotia reported group changes in self-reported and verbal memory ability pre- to post-operatively. Mean verbal memory ability declined significantly after left TLR; however, patients and their relatives rated the patients' memory to be significantly better after than before surgery. Method II examined the magnitude of change in subjective ratings for each individual (i.e., Reliable Change Index), based on control data obtained from unoperated epilepsy patients who were medical managed over the year. The majority of TLR patients were classified as unchanged or improved in memory self-ratings whereas fewer than 10% fell into the significant deterioration classification. Method 3 was a meta-analysis that synthesized outcomes of six publications on self-reported memory change pre- to post-op in persons with TLR. Twenty-two effect sizes were calculated based on a total sample of 450 patients and 171 relatives. The weighted mean effect size was +0.25. Hence the consensus is that memory is judged to be significantly better following TLR than before. We conclude that objective and subjective estimates of memory are dissociable. Both risks need to be discussed prior to elective surgery.

authors_raw: Jaime Williams Jordan Green, and Jeannette McGlone, Capitol District Health Authority
title: Does the risk of memory loss influence decision making by physicians and patients who contemplate temporal lobe resection for control of seizures?
abstract: Temporal lobe resection (TLR) is a successful, although underutilized treatment for complex partial seizures. One reason for this underutilized may be the perceived risk of severe and permanent memory loss. However, patients' and physicians' perceptions of the risk of memory loss following TLR have not been empirically investigated. In this presentation, we propose a series of studies using a triangulation of methodologies examining whether fear of memory loss contributes to decision-making about elective brain surgery. First, we propose conducting focus groups with non-surgical, pre and post TLR patients to uncover their thoughts and attitudes towards TLR surgery and the risk of memory loss. Second, we will survey epilepsy specialists querying their thoughts regarding memory loss including how they communicate the risk to patients and what role the risk has in recommending and proceeding with TLR. Finally, we will explore information available on the Internet regarding TLR and the risk of memory loss, including the accuracy and consistency of the information. Implications of the research will be discussed including the importance of effective epilepsy-related knowledge transfer and critical comments will be invited.

authors_raw: Jodie Gawryluk, Ryan D'Arcy and David Clarke
title: A multimodal imaging approach for surgical planning in temporal lobe epilepsy
abstract: Functional brain mapping has tremendous potential to guide neurosurgical planning. Preliminary studies have used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) and event related brain potentials (ERPs) to map the temporal lobe in a healthy control sample and have highlighted the importance of a multimodal imaging approach. The proposed program of research will combine (fMRI) and (ERPs) to focus on resective surgery for temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE). TLE is the most common type of seizure disorder and surgical intervention is often required for medically intractable cases. Functional MRI will allow for a spatial analysis of task related activation (targeting the temporal lobe), whereas ERPs will allow for analysis of specific processes related to that activation. The current program of research consists of two components: 1) fMRI and ERPs will be used to characterize medial and lateral temporal lobe function in TLE patients and healthy controls; 2) pre- and post-surgical comparisons will determine whether multimodal mapping can improve functional outcome.

authors_raw: Erin L. Mazerolle & Ryan C.N. D'Arcy
title: Interhemispheric transfer of functionally lateralized visual stimuli: An fMRI study at 4T
abstract: Split-brain patients are generally unimpaired in daily life, but demonstrate deficits on tasks that require interhemispheric transfer. We studied interhemispheric transfer in healthy individuals using 4-Tesla functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI). In addition to visual interhemispheric transfer (examined previously), the experiment was designed to explore transfer of motor information. We hypothesized activation differences as a function of the type of cross (visual or motor). Word and face stimuli were presented to elicit lateralized brain processing in the left and right hemispheres, respectively. Visual transfer was manipulated via hemifield of presentation, and motor transfer was manipulated via response hand. Decreased behavioural performance was observed for crossed conditions. Both visual and motor transfers were associated with activation near and in the corpus callosum, but visual cross activation was more posterior (around the splenium), whereas motor cross activation was more anterior (around the body). This study highlights the benefits of high field fMRI for studying interhemispheric transfer across the corpus callosum.

title: A framework for novelty detection, pattern completion and memory consolidation in the hippocampus and cortex
abstract: A large volume of data support the hypothesis that the hippocampus rapidly encodes representations that bind distributed cortical memories until cortex can reactivate its own memories. We provide a mechanistic framework for this hypothesis, accounting for novelty detection, pattern completion and memory consolidation in the basal ganglia, hippocampus and cortex. Under this framework, everything subject to attention is encoded by early LTP in the hippocampus, but only novel, goal-directed events are subject to late LTP (l-LTP) in subfield CA1, the site of pattern completion under the framework. Attention and novelty detection emerge from hierarchical, bidirectional cortical processing, corresponding to coherent and incoherent activity respectively. Incoherent activity creates unique hippocampal representations, learned by synapses mediating coherent activity and generating a strong response in CA1. This response converges with goal-directed prefrontal activity in the basal ganglia, leading to dopamine release and l-LTP in CA1. Subsequently, coherent activity regenerates the CA1 representation, activating associated cortical areas. The rate of cortical consolidation depends on interference with existing representations.

authors_raw: Nathan Saruk, Gail Eskes, John Christie, Hilary Duncan, and Ray Klein
title: Sex, Drugs, and Abrupt Onset Distractors: Made you Look!
abstract: Visuo-spatial neglect is a failure to attend, detect or respond to stimuli that appear in contralesional space after a stroke. Neglect is considered to be a disorder of attention resulting from impairment in exogenous control of orienting while endogenous orienting is believed to remain relatively intact. We examined the interaction of exogenous and endogenous control of orienting in an occulomotor capture paradigm in 3 groups: young and older healthy controls, and stroke patients with spatial neglect. Dependent ! variables included saccadic latency from target onset to saccade initiation and proportion of trials in which the eyes initially moved to an abrupt onset distractor (attention capture) before proceeding to the target. Consistent with previous findings, saccadic latencies were longer in the older group compared to the younger group, but both groups showed equivalent rates of overt attentional capture. In contrast, patients with neglect showed increased capture to right-sided distractors and decreased capture to left-sided distractors.

title: Of guns and geese: A general review of the 'weapon focus' literature
abstract: A frequently cited factor in the accuracy of eyewitness testimony is weapon focus, defined as a deficit in later memory for an event or visual scene containing a weapon. Weapon focus has been attributed to interference from the arousal/threat created by the weapon (arousal/threat hypothesis) or by attentional focus on the weapon (unusual item hypothesis). Laboratory, simulation and field studies are separately reviewed, with emphasis on their implications for both of these hypotheses. It is concluded that weapon focus is a small effect observed primarily in laboratory studies, and not easily generalized to real-world scenarios. This talk is a prototype for a symposium presentation submitted to BBCS and a review article in preparation; therefore, questions or comments are encouraged.

title: The Role of Self-regulation in Pro-environmental Behaviour
abstract: A major challenge in the encouragement of pro-environmental behaviours is demonstrating long-term maintenance of desired actions. Recent research suggests long-term health-related behaviour change may be linked to self-regulation skills (Hall et al., 2006). Like health behaviour, establishing new pro-environmental behaviours often involves problem solving and long-term planning that require self-regulation skills. The proposed study will examine the relation between self-regulation skills and pro-environmental behaviour in adults. Using Ajzen's Theory of Planned Behaviour, self-regulation skills will be assessed to help examine the degree to which "actual behavioural control" explains differences in pro-environmental behaviours at the individual level. Three dimensions of self-regulation will be examined according to a problem-solving framework in the literature on early child development of executive function: problem representation, planning, and execution. It is expected that individuals with better self-regulation skills will show more pro-environmental behaviours. Additionally, actual behavioural control should help to explain significant variance in pro-environmental behaviour.

title: Is a hands-free phone safer than a handheld phone?
abstract: The nature of the differences in driving-related performance between hands-free (HF) and handheld (HH) phone drivers is not well understood. One purpose of this review is to evaluate the effects of the phone type on driving (-related) performance across different levels of driving fidelity in the published literature. Talking on a phone, regardless of the phone type, has negative impacts on driving (-related) performance especially in detecting and identifying events. Moreover, the HF phones were rarely found to be necessarily safer than the HH phones. These patterns are similar across different levels of fidelity in studies.

authors_raw: Lisa Wright, Kimberly Hébert, Katherine Muir, and Tara Perrot-Sinal
title: Stress Responding and Adolescent Development in a Rat Model System: Ally cats and hood rats
abstract: Recent studies demonstrate that the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis can be 'programmed' by experiential factors in early life, tying mature stress response output to past environmental conditions. Mesocorticolimbic dopamine function also contributes significantly to adult stress response regulation, and this system undergoes major developmental modifications during the adolescent period. In two experiments, we manipulated levels of adolescent stress using repeated exposure of rats to ethologically relevant cat odour stimuli (periadolescent predator odour; PPO) or a control condition. In Experiment 1, adolescent rats showed a clear avoidance response upon initial exposure to the cat odour. By the final PPO exposure, however, they showed several measures of hyperactivity and spent significantly more time near the odour relative to control rats. Furthermore, long-term effects on defensive behaviours persisted into adulthood, and adult expression levels of the D2 dopamine receptor were lower in prefrontal regions. In Experiment 2 specific sensitivity of the adolescent period was examined by comparing outcomes of PPO with identical manipulations administered in early adulthood. Adult endocrine and behavioural parameters were uniquely altered by manipulations in adolescence, but only behavioural effects were specific to cat odour priming. PPO enhanced adult responses to novelty but increased activity during cat odour stress testing. These findings indicate that adolescence is a sensitive period for stress response programming, and a potential mechanism for this could involve developmental alterations in mesocorticolimbic dopamine function.

authors_raw: Rahia Mashoodh, Stephanie Nelson, Chris J. Sinal, Tara S. Perrot-Sinal
title: Effects of environmental manipulations on maternal behaviour and implications for the development of the rodent limbic system during the first week of life.
abstract: Differences in expression of rat maternal behaviours are associated with differential development of endocrine, emotional, and cognitive responses to stress in offspring. It is hypothesized that plasticity of maternal behaviour serves as the interface between offspring development and the current environment demands. Consequently, offspring neural and behavioural profiles will be optimally programmed to suit the environment in which they are reared. In the first condition dams were exposed to a predation threat (cat odour) on the day of birth. To further gauge the extent to which environmental complexity modulated mother-infant interactions, pregnant dams were introduced to an enriched environment on GD16. Dams and pups lived in this environment until PND7, when pups were sacrificed, brains removed and hippocampi dissected. Using quantitative PCR, changes in excitatory GABA and expression of the transcription factor NGFI-A were used to measure the developmental consequences of these maternal environments.

title: Four sources of error in the neuro-behavioural analysis of inbred, mutant, and transgenic mice.
abstract:
When we began to develop behavioural test batteries for mice over 10 years ago, we were naïve and, like many others, we thought that you just bought mice and tested them, using the apparatus and procedures published in journal articles. But we soon found out that this is not as simple as it sounds. We have discovered four major sources of error in our experiments and I will discuss how we have dealt with these problems. They are:
The mouse problem. Mice have a mind of their own and do not want to do what you want them to do. Mouse strains differ in many unexpected ways. How do you determine the problems with each strain?
The apparatus problem. Many types of apparatus for mice do not do what people claim in their papers, and modification of the apparatus gives different results. How do you determine which version of an apparatus to use?
The procedure problem. There are many ways of using an apparatus, and different procedures give different results. Which procedure should be used?
The experimenter-error problem. Experimenters make mistakes. How do you detect them, correct them, and eliminate them?
POSTERS

title: Disattenuation by Simulation: An estimation and inferential tool for correlation amidst estimable measurement error
abstract: Few empirical measurements can be made without some degree of measurement error, and when estimating the correlation between two variables it is well known that this error tends to lead to raw correlation values that underestimate the true correlation. Empirical estimates of measurement error, calculated across repeated measurements of the same entity, can be used to correct these attenuated correlation estimates. A general purpose tool is presented for calculating correlation estimates and corresponding inferential intervals in the context of estimable measurement error. The new method, dubbed disattenuation by simulation (DS), is founded on a simple simulation method that lends a combination of flexibility of application and inferential capacity heretofore unknown in traditional correction procedures. Monte Carlo simulation reveals that DS outperforms traditional approaches to correlation estimation and inference.

authors_raw: Chelsea Quinlan, Jonathan Fawcett, & Tracy Taylor
title: Directed Forgetting: Comparing Pictures and Words
abstract: The present study used an item method paradigm to examine whether or not a directed forgetting effect would generalize from words to pictures and vice versa. Participants were assigned to one of two study-test conditions: word-picture or picture-word. Either words or pictures were presented one at a time at study followed by a remember or forget memory instruction. Following the presentation of all the study trials, a yes/no recognition test measured memory performance for both the remember and forget items in the opposite form as study. Results showed that memory performance was significantly greater for to-be-remembered than to-be-forgotten items in both the word-picture and picture-word conditions, which indicates a directed forgetting effect. However, contrary to the predictions of various theoretical views of the picture superiority effect, there was no significant difference in the magnitude of the directed forgetting effect between the two study-test combinations (picture-word vs. word-picture). These findings have important implications for the underlying encoding mechanisms of words and pictures.

title: Development of normal coherent motion perception in kittens requires visual experience in the first 6 weeks of postnatal life
abstract: Coherent motion perception (CMP) is a complex form of motion that is believed to be processed in primates in the middle temporal cortex (MT) and in cats in PMLS that are both visual cortical area beyond the striate cortex. Here we report that kittens reared in darkness to 6 weeks acquire only rudimentary abilities to perceive CMP despite recovering normal visual acuity. Data from animals deprived for different durations suggest that the critical period during which kittens require visual input is very short, lasting only to 6 weeks. The results suggest that higher visual cortical areas require light early in postnatal life for normal development and are also of interest with respect to the controversial viewpoint of Rosa and colleagues that MT (and possible PMLS in cats) may actually be primary visual cortical areas.

authors_raw: McGlone, Jeannette, Carter, Sherri, and Williams, Jaime
title: Memory outcomes after temporal lobe epilepsy surgery: selective amygdalohippocampectomy vs anterior temporal lobe resection
abstract: There is no consensus whether cognitive effects such as memory loss are minimized if neurosurgeons remove a smaller than larger region of the temporal lobe. We present retrospective data on 16 patients who underwent selective amygdalohippocampectomy (SAH) versus 48 with anterior temporal lobe (ATL) resections for control of temporal lobe epilepsy. Demographics and medical histories were similar (i.e., age of onset, seizure frequency, medications, side of surgery, seizure outcome). There was no significant between-group difference on objective memory scores, except trends for SAH to have lower post-op Wechsler Paired Associate scores than ATL ( p <.10). There was no difference on subjective memory ratings using the Memory Observation Questionnaire (MOQ) pre-op or post-op. Within-group ANOVAs (pre to one-year post surgery) showed that objective and subjective scores did not decline significantly after surgery. Interestingly, ATL patients rated memory change more positively than SAH patients (i.e., mean difference on MOQ-SA t score of 8.8, T2,18.2, = 2.1, p < 0.06); a finding that was also seen in the relatives' ratings (MOQ-RA, MD = 9.2, T2,51 = 2.3, p <0.05; MOQ-RB, MD = 22.4, T2,52 = 2.0, p < 0.06). In this non-randomized sample, subjective memory outcomes favoured the larger ATL than smaller SAH resections.

authors_raw: Carter, Sherri, Williams, Jaime, and McGlone,Jeannette
title: Predicting subjective memory decline following temporal lobe epilepsy surgery
abstract: Memory ratings decline for <10% of temporal lobe epilepsy patients after surgical resections (see Williams, Martin and McGlone, submitted). This retrospective study examined 5/ 64 surgical epilepsy cases who reported significant postoperative memory decline on the Memory Observation Questionnaire (MOQ) determined by reliable change indices. Better pre-surgical memory performance and absence of mesial temporal sclerosis (MTS) were predicted. Cases were matched on age, sex, and full scale IQ (Average range), and then compared on objective memory, mood, and psychosocial adjustment. Wilcoxon tests revealed trends for higher pre-surgical memory performance and greater objective memory loss for those with increased memory complaints post-surgery, although findings did not reach .05 level of significance in this small sample (i.e., WMS Percent Delayed Recall - Stories, p = .08 and Verbal Paired Associates, p = .07). Contrary to expectations, neuropathology revealed 4/ 5 cases with MTS. Pairs did not differ on depression or psychosocial adjustment yet case review revealed adverse clinical outcomes for those with more memory complaints. Taken together, these findings concur with Lineweaver et al. (2004), suggesting that for those with normal pre-surgical memory abilities, greater complaints following surgery may coincide with actual memory decline and be a marker of poorer post-surgical outcome.

authors_raw: Doucette, S., Sherry, S. B., Stewart, S. H., Hussman, J., Sherry, D. L., & Vincent, N.
title: The mediational role of reassurance-seeking in the relationship between anxious attachment and hypochondriacal alienation: A preliminary test of the Interpersonal Model of Hypochondriasis
abstract: Hypochondriasis involves a persistent fear of having a serious illness (American Psychiatric Association, 2000). This common difficulty is debilitating to the sufferer and costly to the healthcare system (Barsky & Borus, 1995). We conducted a preliminary test of the Interpersonal Model of Hypochondriasis (IMH; Noyes et al. 2003). The IMH proposes reassurance-seeking behaviours typical of individuals with hypochondriasis are fuelled by anxious attachment. These reassurance-seeking behaviours lead to instances of conflict with, and feelings of alienation from, other people. Such feelings of alienation are also believed to exacerbate health-related worry among persons with hypochondriasis. We hypothesized that reassurance-seeking would mediate the link between anxious attachment and interpersonal alienation. Fifty-nine undergraduates completed measures of anxious attachment, reassurance-seeking, and interpersonal alienation. Results suggest hypochondriacal reassurance-seeking is a marginally significant partial mediator in the anxious attachment - hypochondriacal alienation connection. This study advances understanding of how hypochondriasis is maintained which may inform future interventions.

authors_raw: Antonina Omisade1, Benjamin Rusak1,2,3, and Orfeu Buxton4.
title: Impact of Partial Sleep Deprivation on Cortisol Patterns in Young Women
abstract:
Departments of Psychology,1 Psychiatry2 & Pharmacology,3 Dalhousie University, Halifax, NS, Canada.
Harvard Medical School4, Harvard University, Boston, MA, USA.
This pilot study examined the effects of partial sleep deprivation on the daily rhythm of salivary cortisol in young women across a range of Body Mass Index (BMI) values. Twelve healthy women (ages 18-25; BMIs 18.3-51.9) spent 3 nights and 2 days at the Chronobiology Laboratory at the QEII HSC. During the first 2 nights (Habituation and Baseline), participants were permitted to sleep for up to 10 h. During the third (Experimental) night they slept for 3 h (05:00-08:00). Saliva samples were collected every 2 h from waking to 21:00 during Baseline and Experimental days. The participants also completed inventories assessing mood and cravings for various food groups at the end of each day. Sleep loss elevated afternoon/evening cortisol levels relative to baseline and attenuated the normal morning peak in salivary cortisol levels. Changes in cortisol patterns were not associated with BMI scores. There was an association between increased evening cortisol levels after sleep deprivation and increased hunger on the Experimental day, elevated cravings for most food groups, and lower mood. There was also an association between elevated evening cortisol on the Experimental day and time since the end of menses. These are the first reported effects of sleep loss on cortisol rhythms in women.

authors_raw: Jonathan M. Fawcett and Aaron J. Newman
title: The perception of biological form and motion: An ERP Study
abstract: Previous research reporting event-related potentials (ERPs) sensitive to biological motion have not distinguished between stimuli depicting static human forms and motion. In the present study, we addressed the specificity of these ERP effects to biological motion by presenting static point-light displays of upright and inverted human forms that began to move after 400-600 msec. Pre-motion onset, equivalent N170 and N300 amplitudes were observed for upright and inverted human forms; post-motion onset, amplitudes of both N170 and N300 components were larger for upright stimuli. Results suggest that these components are not biological motion specific, but are enhanced by upright biological motion.

authors_raw: Jonathan M. Fawcett and Tracy L. Taylor
title: Start Forgetting or Stop Remembering? Commonalities Between Directed Forgetting and Stop-Signal Inhibition
abstract: By combining an item-method directed forgetting paradigm with a stop-signal task, the current study explored commonalities between directed forgetting and stop-signal inhibition. Following each study phase memory instruction, the current study presented a centralized green circle (Go-Signal) requiring a speeded detection response that was sometimes replaced by a red circle (Stop-Signal) cancelling that response. Although stop-signal reaction times were not affected by the preceding memory instruction, an instruction to Forget did facilitate stopping by delaying responses. These findings provide support for the view that intentional forgetting is an active cognitive process.

authors_raw: Michael A. Lawrence & Raymond M. Klein Dalhousie University
title: Disentangling exogenous and endogenous temporal attention
abstract: The allocation of information processing resources over time, otherwise known as temporal attention, may be achieved by relatively automatic or controlled mechanisms. Loud noises, for example, may automatically elicit attention whilst learned strategies may be used to prepare resource allocation to impending important events based on previously detected temporal contingencies. With analogy to systems of allocation in spatial attention, these theorized mechanisms are termed exogenous and endogenous temporal attention, respectively. Where past research has confounded these theoretically distinct dimensions of temporal attention, the present work seeks to ameliorate this oversight, including a novel application of Rescorla's "truly-random control" procedure.

title: What am I?
abstract: "What am I?" is a question that has intrigued thinkers in all cultures at all times. My focus will be answers to this question given by thinkers in the western tradition. Transformations in answering this question have occurred, but the issues remain the same. What is my metaphysical nature? Am I a physical thing, for instance an individual living being, or an immaterial, and immortal substance? Am I even a persistent 'thing' at all? Perhaps I'm a process of some other thing, such as neural activity in the brain? Maybe, instead of being a thing or a material process I'm constituted by being conscious of my self or being self-aware. Or am I merely a concept constructed by the brain or mind but constituted by social processes? Indeed, perhaps, there is no single notion that satisfies all our intuitions about our selves and that I am essentially fragmented in nature.

authors_raw: Kate Thompson, Jonathan Fawcett and Tracy L. Taylor
title: Tag, you're it: Tagging as an alternative to Y/N recognition in item-method directed forgetting
abstract: In item-method directed forgetting, memory performance is commonly measured using yes/no recognition. Such a task asks the participant whether or not a word was presented during the study phase regardless of memory instruction. The present study proposes another, possibly more valid measure of directed forgetting. Following a standard item-method directed forgetting task, study words were tested using either a standard yes/no task or a tagging task. In the tagging task participants 'tagged' each word as remember, forget, or new. The benefits of the tagging method over a yes/no distinction are discussed.

authors_raw: Noel, M. E., Chambers, C. T., McMurtry, C. M., & McGrath, P. J.
title: The Role of Pain in Influencing Children's Recalled Procedural Anxiety
abstract: Aim of Investigation: Children are required to repeatedly undergo medical procedures over the course of their early lives and many find these experiences to be both painful and frightening. Children's memories of painful medical procedures have important implications for coping during subsequent procedures. Previous research indicates that children's memory for sensory aspects of pain is surprisingly accurate. We examined whether the level of pain children report experiencing immediately after a medical procedure is related to their recalled anxiety 2 weeks following the procedure (above and beyond the anxiety that children initially experience). Methods: Participants consisted of 27 children (12 males, 15 females) aged 5-10 years (M = 7.52, SD = 1.53) who had undergone venepuncture. Children completed a measure of pain (Faces Pain Scale-Revised: FPS-R, scores range from 0 to 10) immediately following the procedure and a measure of anxiety (Faces Anxiety Scale: FAS, scores range from 0 to 4) at two time points: immediately and 2 weeks following the procedure. Memory scores were calculated by taking the difference between anxiety ratings at each time point. Results: After controlling for initial anxiety, the level of pain that children experienced immediately following the procedure was related to their recalled anxiety ( r = .54, p < .05 ); children who experienced greater pain had higher levels of recalled anxiety. Children who initially reported lower levels of pain (FPS-R = 0-4) tended to accurately estimate their level of procedural anxiety 2 weeks following the procedure. Conversely, children who reported higher levels of pain (FPS-R = 6-10) immediately following venepuncture tended to report higher levels of procedural anxiety at follow up. Conclusions: These preliminary findings suggest that children's initial procedural pain experience may be related to how they recall their level of experienced procedural anxiety.

authors_raw: Jillian H. Filliter, MHSA and Shannon A. Johnson, PhD
title: Self-Awareness of Autistic Symptoms, Empathy, and Systemizing in High-Functioning Children and Adolescents with an Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract: Studies of theory of mind in Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD) typically focus on the ability to perceive and understand others' mental states. It has been proposed that the mechanisms that underlie understanding of others' mental states are similar to those involved in attributing mental states to the self. We compared parent and self-report scores on the Autism Spectrum, Empathizing, and Systemizing Quotients (AQ, EQ, & SQ). Participants were 15 individuals with ASD and 16 age- and IQ-matched controls, ages 9 to 19 years. Comparisons of parent- and self-report scores revealed that ASD participants reported fewer autism symptoms and higher empathy levels relative to their parent, but there was no difference on the SQ. In contrast, parent- and self-reports for the control group did not differ for the AQ and SQ measures, but parents reported more empathizing than participants. These results suggest that those with an ASD have less self-awareness than their typically developing peers.

title: PROGRAMMING THE STRESS RESPONSE:  INVESTIGATING THE ROLE OF SEX DIFFERENCES IN EARLY HPA AXIS DEVELOPMENT
abstract:
Males and females are known to differ in their responses to stressors, and these differences may be related to the observed sex differences in stress-related diseases. In mammals, differences between the male and female brains are thought to be primarily the result of gonadal hormone secretions, acting during development to 'organize' the wiring of the male and female brain, and in adulthood to 'activate' these differences in neural circuitry. In rodent models, the early postnatal period is a critical time for the sexual differentiation of the brain, and is also a time of significant plasticity in the developing stress system, though little work has directly focused on interactions between the two.
We propose to characterize potential sex differences in the development of several brain structures involved in the modulation of the stress response, as well as investigate some of the potential mechanisms that underly these differences.

authors_raw: Debbie Johnson Emberly, MSc, Dalhousie University; IWK Health Centre, Ian D. Graham, PhD, VP Knowledge Translation Branch, CIHR & Jacqueline Tetroe, Senior Policy Analyst, Knowledge Translation Branch, CIHR.
title: The who, what, when, where and how of knowledge translation: A content analysis of funded CIHR operating grants.
abstract: Objective: To undertake a content analysis of funded Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) open access grant proposals to examine the types and budgets of knowledge translation (KT) activities proposed. Methods: A random sample of funded grant proposals (n=50 from funding years 2000/01 and 2005/06) was generated from the total funded operating grants in each of the four CIHR themes. Variables abstracted included characteristics of the grant, investigators, and KT activities. Grants were analyzed for the amount budgeted directed to end of grant KT activities, e.g., conferences, publishing and integrated KT activities, e.g., focus groups with research stakeholders. Results & Discussion: Overwhelmingly, the KT activities identified were end of grant diffusion activities, e.g., conference presentations, publication in peer-reviewed journals. The percentage of grants which identified KT as a specific objective within the proposed research program increased from an average of 10% of all funded applications in 2000/01 to an average of 30% in 2005/06.
