Innovative KT activities are emerging from the health systems/services and population health themes, e.g., involvement of stakeholders, website dissemination which have the potential to reach more stakeholders for less cost than traditional methods of dissemination. Implications of these findings are discussed in the context of the mandate of CIHR and the science of KT.

authors_raw:
Megan E. McLarnon1, Jessica Meisner1, Christine Darredeau2, and Sean P. Barrett1, 2
1Department of Psychology, Dalhousie University
2Department of Psychiatry, Dalhousie University
title: Personality, Motivation and Patterns of Misuse of Prescription Anxiolytics and Sedatives
abstract: Anxiolytic and sedative medications have important therapeutic uses and are widely prescribed in the treatment of anxiety and insomnia. In addition to their beneficial effects, these substances have psychoactive properties that render them vulnerable to misuse. Despite indications that recreational use of anxiolytics and sedatives is growing, there is a dearth of information available regarding the factors related to their misuse. Existing research has suffered from inconsistency in the operational definition of misuse. Specific personality characteristics and differing motives for use are thought to be related to vulnerability for problematic substance use. The current study investigates anxiolytic and sedative misuse in an adult Canadian sample using a battery of structured interview and self-report measures. Misuse will be operationally defined as the use of the medication for something other than its intended psychiatric purpose. It is expected that distinct clusters of anxiolytic and sedative users will be characterized based on patterns and contexts of use, demographics, personality, psychiatric symptoms, and motivation for use. Identification of the predictors and correlates of anxiolytic and sedative misuse will be essential in understanding this important public health issue.

authors_raw: Susan R. Battista, Sherry H. Stewart, Heather Fulton, Dan Steeves, Christine Darredeau, & Dubravka Gavric
title: A Further Investigation of the Relations of Anxiety Sensitivity to Smoking Motives
abstract: Prior research has shown that anxiety sensitivity (AS) is associated with smoking to reduce negative affect. However, given previous limitations in the measurement of smoking motives, it has yet to be definitively established that AS is specifically related to negative reinforcement smoking motives. The present study examined a sample of 119 smokers attending a tobacco intervention program who completed the Anxiety Sensitivity Index and the Reasons for Smoking Scale (RFS) at pre-treatment. In a principal components analysis of the RFS items, stringent parallel analysis supported a two factor solution (negative and positive reinforcement smoking motives). ASI total scores were significantly positively correlated with both RFS factors in bivariate correlational analyses. In partial correlations, the relation between the ASI and the RFS negative reinforcement factor remained significant when controlling RFS positive reinforcement smoking motives, but the correlation of ASI with RFS positive reinforcement motives was not significant after controlling for RFS negative reinforcement motives. These findings have implications for designing targeted tobacco interventions for high AS smokers.

authors_raw: Keiko Fujise and S. Hélène Deacon
title: How language background affects spelling: The role of first language in spelling English plurals
abstract: The current study examines whether and how one's first language affects the spelling of the English plural -s. This is examined by comparing the extent to which English learners utilize frequency-based approach (learning through frequent exposure) or rule-based approach (learning through explicit rules) when spelling the English plural -s. The two experimental groups will consist of late learners of English (i.e., exposure to English after age 12) whose first language is French or Japanese. English monolingual control group will also be included. Whereas French shares the notion of plurality and the writing system with English, Japanese shares neither with English. Consequently, French participants' greater amount of exposure to alphabets and to the notion of plurality may lead them to utilize frequency-based approach in spelling English plurals. On the other hand, Japanese participants may utilize rule-based approach because they do not have the luxury to resort to knowledge built up over time by exposure.

title: Are individual differences in absentmindedness correlated with individual differences in attention?
abstract: In recent years, absentmindedness in everyday life has been assessed using the Cognitive Failures Questionnaire (CFQ) (Broadbent et al., 1982). We used the CFQ and the Attentional Network Test (ANT) (Fan et al., 2002, Callejas et al., 2005) to examine the relationship between absentmindedness and attention (which has been little explored to date and with inconsistent findings). Using orthogonal subtractions of performance in selected conditions (see Klein, 2002) the ANT measures the efficacy of three isolable components of attention: alerting, orienting, and executive control. Two different versions of the ANT were administered in two experiments with 100 participants in each. Among three networks whose efficiency is assessed by this test, alerting and orienting, but surprisingly not executive control, were correlated with absentmindedness. The opposite correlations between the CFQ and orienting with the two versions of the ANT suggest that their measures of orienting tap separate constructs.

title: Development of Smiling in Early Infancy: Relations across age and context
abstract: Infants seem designed to not only orient to but also attract the social elements in their environment that will ultimately aid in constructing knowledge. One effective means for securing adult attention is through the use of facial expressions. Infants display a variety of smiles that differ in terms of their use in social interactions and connection to feelings of enjoyment. Smiles more associated with sleep are termed 'reflexive' because they are believed to occur in the absence of known stimulation. Smiles more associated with alert waking states are termed 'elicited' because they become increasingly more contingent on particular external stimuli. Unfortunately, little is known regarding the function of reflexive smiles and their relationship to elicited smiles. Further, the developmental outcome of individual patterns of smiling during early infancy remains to be discovered. We intend to address the above issues (among others) using a longitudinal design by determining the type, frequency and duration of smiles occurring during active sleep and waking states (both social and non-social contexts).

authors_raw: Penny Corkum, Trish Pottie, & Patrick McGrath
title: Better Days Better Nights: Evaluation of a Behavioural Sleep Intervention for Children
abstract: Objectives: Sleep difficulties are one of the most common concerns reported by parents to family physicians and pediatricians (Owens, et. al., 2002), with approximately 15% to 37% of school-aged children being affected by some type of persistent sleep disturbance (Owens, et. al., 2000). Insufficient sleep can have pervasive negative effects on a child?s day-to-day functioning and development (Reid et al., 1999), on the family unit (Kataria, et al., 1987; Richman, 1981) and on child-parent interactions (Stores, 1996). Unfortunately, treatments for sleep problems are not widely implemented (Owens & Mindell, 2002). Although there is research support for the efficacy of behaviourally-based sleep interventions for typically-developing infants and preschool children (Ramchandani, et. al., 2000), there is a paucity of research examining these interventions for elementary school-aged children. Methods: A two-arm, randomized control trial is currently being conducted in which children (5-12 years old), with or without disruptive behaviour disorders, are assigned to a behaviourally-based sleep intervention or a waitlist control condition. The intervention program consists of five sessions, delivered from a distance using written materials and a non-professional telephone coach. The treatment effectiveness is being evaluated through multiple sleep methods (questionnaires, sleep diaries and actigraphy) and assesses change in sleep disturbance symptoms, daytime behavioural problems, and parental stress. Satisfaction ratings are also gathered from parents. The poster will provide an overview of the study design and treatment, as well as preliminary results from our administrative database regarding treatment effectiveness in terms of impact on sleep disturbances. Results: The preliminary analyses were based on a total of 24 children (12 treatment and 12 control). The treatment group demonstrated significant improvement in the overall sleep disturbances scale (F(2,21) = 4.20, p = .03). More specifically, there was a significant change in sleep onset (F(2,21) = 4.07, p = .03), but no difference in terms of bedtime resistance (F(2,21) = 2.30, p = .12), although the means changed in the expected direction. Parental satisfaction was very high in the treatment group. Conclusions: These results, although preliminary, suggest that the behaviourally-based sleep intervention was effective in improving some sleep patterns. The goal is to have 30 children in each arm who have completed their assignment. After the trial is completed, we will conduct analyses to determine if the results are similar based on actigraphy and whether improved sleep patterns results in improved daytime functioning. Support: Nova Scotia Health Research Foundation, Halifax, Nova Scotia

title: Energy Drinks: What Have You Binge Drinking??
abstract:
Research has shown that substance use does not occur in isolation, but frequently in combination with other substances (e.g. Barrett et al., 2006). There is a growing concern that many young people are combining energy drinks (ED) with alcohol (ALC) despite labels warning against this practice. Sixty university students (M 26, F 34, age17-29, mean = 20.4, SD = 2.4) were recruited who were current or recent ED users. Participants were interviewed to elicit information about their motivations for, and patterns of, ED use, their lifetime substance use and detailed information regarding combined substance/ED use in the seven days preceding the interview. All participants completed self-report questionnaires (demographics, ADHD checklist, and a personality inventory). Analyses (t-tests) revealed significant differences between those who combine ED/ALC and those who do not, on mean drinks per session, total lifetime substance use, age of first use of ALC and the introversion/helplessness personality scores. Binary logistic regression provided a model for the prediction of ED/ALC co-use.
Presentation: Talk

title: The Temporal Parameters of Visual Proprioceptive Perception in Infancy
abstract:
In humans, differentiating between one's own body movements and those of others is
critical. Bahrick and Watson (1985) proposed that the ability to recognize oneself in a
mirror and/or live video is aided by the detection of the visual proprioceptive contingency
between self and the display. The current study aims to determine at what point children
can discriminate between a delayed and non-delayed image of their face. Five to 12-month-old infants will be randomly assigned to one of three delays (1, 2, 10 seconds) and view two computer monitors; one of which displays a live transmission of the infant's face while the other displays a delayed image. It is expected that infants will show a preference for the delayed images, indicating that they can discriminate between the live and delayed feeds. These results will allow us to determine the age at which infants can integrate visual and proprioceptive information.

title: Effects of Socializing and Environmental Enrichment on Alzheimer's Mouse Models
abstract: The effects of environmental enrichment and group housing are currently being investigated. The study includes transgenic mouse models of Alzheimer's Disease (B6C3-Tg(APP695)3DboTg(PSEN1)5Dbo/J) and their wildtype littermates. Mice are being housed in either an enriched environment (rat cage with tubes, plastic house, nesting material, visual stimuli, and other toys) or an impoverished environment (mouse cage with pine chip bedding only). Within these two environments mice are either singly housed, or housed in groups of 2-4. At 3 weeks of age mice were placed into the experimental housing conditions. Mice will be assessed on a battery of tests at both 6 and 12 months of age. The test battery includes the open field test (exploratory behaviour and anxiety), rotarod test (motor ability), Morris Water Maze (visual learning and memory), olfactory digging task (olfactory learning and memory), and cue and context fear conditioning (learning and memory).

authors_raw: Leanne ten Brinke, B.Sc. (Hons.) (presenting), Steve Porter, Ph.D., Chantal Gustaw, B.A. (Hons.)
title: Dangerous Decisions: Examining the Influence of Facial Trustworthiness Assessments on Decisions of Guilt
abstract: Despite the common assumption that judges and jurors can accurately assess witness credibility, research suggests that they cannot detect deception above the level of chance. According to the Dangerous Decisions Theory (DDT; Porter & ten Brinke, 2008), intuitive evaluations of trustworthiness based on the face greatly influence interpretation of subsequent information about the target. In a courtroom setting, ensuing assessments of evidence may be highly flawed, as evidence is interpreted in a manner consistent with the initial (unreliable) impression of trustworthiness. A recent study by Porter, ten Brinke and Gustaw (2008) presented participants with vignettes describing severe or petty crimes, accompanied by a photograph of the supposed defendant, previously rated as highly trustworthy or untrustworthy in appearance. Participants required less evidence to arrive at a guilty verdict and were less likely to be influenced by exonerating evidence for the untrustworthy-appearing defendants. The current evidence is supportive of DDT and has implications for legal decision-making practices.

title: The Executive and the Environmentalist: Do Executive Function Skills Help Predict Pro-environmental Behavior?
abstract:
Recent research into health-related behaviour change suggests that executive function skills may help explain the inconsistency between people's intention to behave in a particular way and their actual behavior (Hall et al., 2006). Like health behaviour, successfully establishing new pro-environmental behaviours may also require executive function skills. Using Ajzen's Theory of Planned Behaviour as a framework, this study examined the extent to which executive function skills helped to explain above and beyond intention the variation in recycling and composting behaviour by undergraduate students. Executive function skills failed to explain significant variation in behavior.
However, performance on the Stop Signal task showed a trend towards significance suggesting that the ability to inhibit a dominant response may be associated with successful behavior. Results are discussed in the context of improving pro-environmental behavior change strategies.

authors_raw: Evan R. Fairn and Shelley A. Adamo
title: Effects of immune activation on behaviour in the cricket Gryllus texensis
abstract: As in vertebrates, activating an immune response in insects can result in a suite of behavioural changes called 'sickness behaviour' which are believed to function to increase resistance to infection. We tested whether immune activation causes a change in feeding, agonistic, or shelter-seeking behavior in the field cricket Gryllus texensis. Male crickets injected with heat-killed bacteria showed decreased feeding compared to controls but did not differ from controls in the amount of time spent in shelter. Immune challenged females did not differ from controls in amount of time spent feeding but spent less time in shelter than controls. Immune challenged male crickets generally lost agonistic encounters with control males and immune challenged males were less likely to engage in agonistic behavior.

authors_raw: Sherry H. Stewart, Raymond M. Klein, Sean P. Barrett, and Daniel S. McGrath (presenter)
title: Self-generated motives for gambling: Relations to gambling involvement, gambling problems, and co-morbid psychopathology in two population-based samples of gamblers
abstract: Motivational models have proven useful in understanding certain addictive behaviours, such as alcohol use; however, they have not been extensively investigated in the gambling area. In the present study, the utility of classifying gamblers into gambling motives groups was examined based on self-generated responses to a question about primary reason(s) for gambling. Secondary data analyses were performed on two gambling prevalence studies. Participant responses were coded in the context of Coopers' (1994) motivational model for alcohol use, adapted for gambling. Categories for primary gambling motives included: coping, enhancement, social, and other. Preliminary results indicate that coping-motivated and enhancement-motivated gamblers are characterized by a greater severity of gambling problems on the CPGI problem gambling index than socially-motivated gamblers. The results also suggest that additional categories may be required to address gamblers primarily motivated by recreation or financial incentives. Motive group differences in demographic variables, psychiatric symptoms, gambling activities, and other substance use will also be discussed.

title: Exhibitionist flasher uses wing mirrors with fluorescent fusilli for sexual entrapment.
abstract: A long-legged dolichopodid dipteran from local sea cliffs carries on his wings terminal spots that flash in sunlight over a wide angle, a display aimed to attract the opposite sex. Unusually, the spots behave as mirrors to deflect light incident upon them following the double angle mirror-galvanometer rule, but the wing spot zone is too thin (1.5 µm) to contain any reflector crystals. Instead, broad-band reflecting surfaces are formed from the flattened sides of ~5300 ultrathin, colourless hairs ("microtrichia"), extensions of the surface cuticle that jut out at 90° from the wing surface. The puzzle is how their action as mirrors can combine with wide-angle emission. The hairs are actually spiral mirrors, shaped a bit like fusilli pasta and packed like sardines, so an incoming fusillade of light has to undergo multiple randomizing reflections within the tightly packed fusilli. This generates a broadly diffused output beam (measured normal distribution, ±2 SD ≈ 88°). The microtrichia also fluoresce bright blue in ultraviolet light, prominent in sky-light. While their reflections are mirror-like, microtrichia appear to act as leaky waveguides for this blue fluorescence. Presumably, the flasher-receiver has blue receptors concentrated in part of her retina to take advantage of this parallel, fluorescent visual display.

authors_raw: Antonina Omisade1, Benjamin Rusak1,2,3, and Orfeu Buxton4.
title: Impact of Partial Sleep Deprivation on Cortisol Patterns in Young Women
abstract: This pilot study examined the effects of partial sleep deprivation on the daily rhythm of salivary cortisol in young women across a range of Body Mass Index (BMI) values. Twelve healthy women (ages 18-25; BMIs 18.3-51.9) spent 3 nights and 2 days at the Chronobiology Laboratory at the QEII HSC. During the first 2 nights (Habituation and Baseline), participants were permitted to sleep for up to 10 h. During the third (Experimental) night they slept for 3 h (05:00-08:00). Saliva samples were collected every 2 h from waking to 21:00 during Baseline and Experimental days. The participants also completed inventories assessing mood and cravings for various food groups at the end of each day. Sleep loss elevated afternoon/evening cortisol levels relative to baseline and attenuated the normal morning peak in salivary cortisol levels. Changes in cortisol patterns were not associated with BMI scores. There was an association between increased evening cortisol levels after sleep deprivation and increased hunger on the Experimental day, elevated cravings for most food groups, and lower mood. There was also an association between elevated evening cortisol on the Experimental day and time since the end of menses. These are the first reported effects of sleep loss on cortisol rhythms in women.

title: Forget about the flankers
abstract: In the item-method of directed forgetting, decreased memory for forget cued words is generally attributed to lack of encoding when memory instructions are presented after each item. The present study examines the possibility of inhibitory mechanisms associated with forget-cued items by adding a flanker task using forget-cued, remember-cued and novel words as flankers in between the study and recognition phase of an item-method directed forgetting task. Flanker trials require a speeded decision about a target word (living or non-living) flanked by words from the same or opposite category. Typically, opposite-category flankers slow reaction times and increase identification errors of the target word. It is hypothesized that, if inhibition is involved in item-method forgetting, the typical flanker effect will be dampened or eliminated when the flanker words had previously been forget-cued, as compared to remember-cued or novel words. Results and implications are discussed.

title: The Psychophysics of configural face processing
abstract: The present study investigated observer sensitivity to the 2nd order configural relations of features (distances between features) within face and non-face stimuli. Using Just Noticeable Differences (JNDs) we have shown that observers are more sensitive to 2nd order relational information in face stimuli than house stimuli. Contrary to existing theories, inverting the stimuli reduced sensitivity to 2nd order relational information in both faces and houses when both types of stimuli were presented in the same task. However, when only the house stimuli were presented without the face stimuli, inversion failed to reduce sensitivity to 2nd order relational information in houses. Together these results suggest that 2nd order relational processing may not be face-specific, and can be flexibly applied to non-face objects when they are presented in the context of faces.

title: Face perception and the processing of race
abstract: Using Event-Related Potentials, researchers have revealed an early negative component?the N170-- that is larger in response to faces than to other classes of object. It is thought that this component reflects processes that encode the structure of the face. Importantly it has been shown that the N170 is not sensitive to the age or gender of the face, suggesting that this type of information is extracted at later stages. However, it is unclear whether this stage is sensitive to the race of the face. In the present experiment, event related potentials were recorded while participants viewed faces of three different races. It was shown that race had no effect on the N170, a finding consistent with the notion that race is extracted from a face image after the basic structure has been encoded.

authors_raw: Heather G. Fulton, Sean P. Barrett, Sherry H. Stewart, Kimberley Good, Ron Leslie, Alissa Pencer, David Whitehorn and Heather Milliken
title: Patterns and Predictors of Tobacco use in Early Psychosis Patients and Controls
abstract: Psychosis is a severe disorder affecting more than 1% of the population. Smoking is highly prevalent in this disorder, especially in comparison to the general population. However, little research has examined how individuals with psychosis use tobacco in comparison to controls. Early psychosis patients' patterns of current and lifetime tobacco use were compared to controls. Patients reported their heaviest use of tobacco to be later in life and reported using tobacco more frequently than controls. As different proportions of patients and controls currently used tobacco, subsequent analyses were conducted only with current smokers. Current smokers with psychosis smoked more cigarettes per day and reported greater tobacco dependence levels than current smoker controls. In regression analyses, patient status predicted tobacco dependence levels over and above other variables (e.g., demographics). Results provide insight into how patients with psychosis use tobacco and how to tailor cessation programs to better serve this population.

authors_raw: E.L. Mazerolle, R.C.N. D'Arcy & S.D. Beyea
title: Detecting fMRI activation in white matter: Interhemispheric transfer across the corpus callosum
abstract: SUMMARY: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) activation is believed to be restricted to gray matter (GM). Both hemodynamic and neurophysiological evidence support this assertion. In spite of this, fMRI activation is not neatly restricted to GM, and an increasing number of studies are explicitly reporting WM activation. In the current study, we investigated WM activation using 4T fMRI. To elicit WM activation, an interhemispheric transfer task was employed using lateralized cognitive processing. The role of the corpus callosum (CC) in this type of task has been previously established in split brain patients. The analysis revealed CC activation in14/24 subjects. Of these 14 subjects, four had activation centered in the CC. Interestingly, this activation was consistently localized to the posterior body of the CC. In contrast, CC activation in the remaining 10 subjects was likely attributable to partial volume effects (i.e., contamination by GM signal). Ultimately, characterization of fMRI in WM may open new avenues of clinical research in which functional assessment of WM is critical.

authors_raw: Nancy J Butcher, Tara N Edwards, Birgit Greiner, and Ian A. Meinertzhagen
title: Detection of fMRI activation in white matter
abstract: The synaptic vesicle cycle predicts the movement of vesicles through a synaptic terminal so that a reciprocal process of vesicle recovery and neurotransmitter pumping matches the shedding of synaptic vesicles during transmission. The visual system of Drosophila melanogaster provides an excellent model for the investigation of the structure and identity of presynaptic organelles and proteins involved in the synaptic vesicle cycle; not only do photoreceptor terminals in Drosophila have a regular structure but their synapses are genetically manipulable. In the present study, novel insight into the ultrastructure and identity of proteins and organelles involved in the synaptic vesicle cycle and vesicle endocytosis was provided. New advances in EM tomography enabled the resolution of a filamentous vesicle network within the photoreceptor terminal, and substantiated claims that the T-bar ribbon is a site of vesicle docking and shedding. These reconstructions of synaptic terminals may serve as a basis of comparison for synaptic mutants at a level of detail not previously available using conventional EM methods. Investigation of the two junctional proteins, Crumb (Crb) and Discs Large (Dlg), show that Dlg, but not Crb, is localized to photoreceptor terminals and facilitates the development of the endocytotic organelles known as capitate projections. Unexpectedly, dense core vesicles were found in mutant photoreceptor terminals, potentially uncovering a new function of Dlg in the photoreceptor terminal.

title: Do Jugglers show Enhanced Processing of Peripheral Visual Stimuli?
abstract:
Action video game players show enhanced performance on a number of attentional
tasks. In addition, those who learn a visual spatial language such as American
Sign Language show enhanced attention to peripherally presented moving targets.
The current research considered whether or not juggling, another visual spatial
task, confers the same benefits as learning sign language or playing video
games. Jugglers were matched to non-jugglers of the same age, gender, and
handedness and tested on a number of attentional tasks (Multiple Object
Tracking, Flanker-Compatibility, and Useful Field of View) as well as having
event-related potential recorded during an attention-to-motion task.
Preliminary results (N=12) suggests that juggling may indeed confer some
benefits measurable by visuo-spatial tasks. However, the size of the effects
for jugglers may not be as large as those found for video game players and
signers, and may be limited to only certain tasks.
Presentation: TALK
Date: No preference.
Equipment Needs: None, beyond a PC computer (preferably).

authors_raw: Sean P. Roach an Leslie S. Phillmore
title: Neural response of male and female black-capped chickadees (Poecile atricapillus) to normal and altered conspecific song
abstract: The purpose of the present study is to establish neural correlates for previous behavioural studies illustrating which particular aspects of the highly stereotyped song of the black-capped chickadee (Poecile atricapillus) are necessary for conspecific recognition by male and female chickadees. Birds will be exposed to synthetic song stimuli of four types corresponding to those used in the behavioural studies: normal songs, and songs with eliminated glissando, altered internote pitch ratio, or both. After sacrifice, levels of ZENK expression in the caudomedial nidopallium (CMM) and caudal medial nidopallium (NCM) will be examined (previous research has established ZENK expression as a reliable indicator of song perception and recognition). It is expected that ZENK levels in these areas will correspond to behavioural responses to normal and altered songs in male and female chickadees. It will be especially interesting to see the neural response of male chickadees to songs with altered internote pitch ratio, as males are able to perceive such differences but do not discriminate based upon them.

authors_raw: Lisa D. Wright, Katherine Muir, Holly McDonald, and Tara S. Perrot-Sinal
title: Adolescent endocrine and behavioural responses to predatory stimuli in a rodent model: Alley cats and hood rats, part II.
abstract: Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal function and defensive behaviours can be 'programmed' by external factors during early life in rats. Emerging evidence indicates that the adolescent environment is also important in this context, as prefrontal brain regions mature at this time and are responsible for cognitive regulation of stress responding. In order to identify potential mechanisms underlying long-term consequences of adverse adolescent conditions, we conducted a series of experiments designed to examine adolescent responses to stressor exposure. In Experiment 1, we examined responses to two different cat odour stimuli, following repeated adolescent exposures. We also compared adolescent and adult responses to repeated cat odour presentation (Experiment 2), and, finally, we compared adolescent responses to predation threat with responses to a social isolation stressor. Results underscore the importance of the nature of stressor stimuli in determining effects on adolescents, providing support for the hypothesis that adult stress responding will be optimized for predicted environmental conditions.

authors_raw: Justin Patterson, B.A. , Shannon Johnson, Ph.D.
title: Recognition of Emotion in Faces and Voices in High-Functioning Individuals with an Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract:
It has been proposed that individuals with an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) are impaired at recognizing the emotions exhibited by others. However, previous studies of emotion recognition in ASD have yielded conflicting results. In addition, there has been minimal work addressing the recognition of emotions across modalities in ASD. In two separate, but similar studies, we examined recognition of facial and vocal emotions in a total of 26 children and adolescents diagnosed with Asperger's Disorder and 21 typically developing controls. Participants viewed a series of faces depicting emotional expressions and selected the emotional label for each stimulus. Participants also listened to a series of nonsensical vocalizations expressing emotions and selected the correct emotional label. In Study 1, the Asperger's group was less accurate than controls for the recognition of sad facial expressions and the vocal expressions of both anger and interest. There were no group differences in Study 2.
SUMMARY: Emotion recognition abilities of individuals with Asperger's Disorder

authors_raw: Sherry, S. B., Sherry, D. L., Hewitt, P. L., and Flett, G. L.
title: Does Perfectionism Impede Research Productivity? A Study of Psychology Professors
abstract: Amid the "publish or perish" field of academic psychology, personality traits that influence research productivity (i.e., the number of and the impact of journal articles, book chapters, books, edited books, symposia, and posters) may represent important contributors to career outcomes. In the present study, self-oriented perfectionism (i.e., demanding perfection of oneself) and socially prescribed perfectionism (i.e., perceiving that others are demanding perfection of oneself) were hypothesized to impede research productivity. Psychology professors (N = 1,258) completed measures of demographics, personality traits, and research productivity. Results indicated higher levels of self-oriented perfectionism were associated with lower levels of research productivity above and beyond demographics, conscientiousness, and neuroticism. Socially prescribed perfectionism was unrelated to research productivity. The present study suggests psychology professors who demand perfection of themselves struggle to disseminate their research. Publications and presentations generated by psychology professors high in self-oriented perfectionism may be seen as occurring in spite of--and not because of--their perfectionistic behavior.

authors_raw: Evan R. Fairn and Shelley A. Adamo
title: Effects of immune activation on behaviour in the cricket Gryllus texensis
abstract: As in vertebrates, activating an immune response in insects can result in a suite of behavioural changes called 'sickness behaviour' that are believed to decrease the impact of disease on fitness. We tested whether immune activation causes a change in feeding, agonistic (i.e. aggressive), or shelter-seeking behavior in the field cricket Gryllus texensis. Male crickets injected with heat-killed bacteria showed decreased feeding compared to controls but did not differ from controls in the amount of time spent in shelter. Immune challenged females did not differ from controls in amount of time spent feeding but spent less time in shelter than controls. Immune challenged male crickets generally lost agonistic encounters with control males and immune challenged males were less likely to engage in agonistic behavior.

authors_raw: Aaron Newman, Shannon Johnson, & Kevin Duffy
title: Envisioning the Future: Building a Clinical Neuroscience Program at Dalhousie
abstract: Neuroscience research has revolutionized our understanding of relationships between brain, behaviour, thought, and emotion. Tools such as genetic screening and brain imaging will eventually play a central role in the diagnosis and assessment of psychopathology, and evidence from neuroscience already has uses in everyday practice. Although clinical psychologists have a solid understanding of the biological bases of behaviour, a deeper knowledge of the neurophysiological underpinnings of normal and pathological psychological function will be essential in the future. To provide clinicians with this knowledge, and the skill set to use it, training programs must keep pace. Dalhousie, with its exceptional Clinical Psychology program and internationally-recognized strength in neuroscience, is perfectly poised to play a leading role in integrating clinical psychology and neuroscience. In this forum we will outline a 5-year plan to develop a program in Clinical Neuroscience at Dalhousie.

authors_raw: Tristan Neal, Chris Moore, Cherie Collicot
title: Interactive and Observational Learning in Infants:
abstract: Research in the past 20 years has established infant learning of novel words and social actions in joint attention (see Moore, 2006, for review). Recently, 18 month-olds have shown this learning by observing two people who are socially aloof from the infant interact (Akhtar, 2005; Floor and Akhtar, 2006) and 24 month-olds by observing one socially aloof person perform alone (Nielson, 2006). As of yet, no comparison of learning between these two observational contexts and that of joint attention has been made with the same children. This study compared novel word and action learning between these contexts in a group of twenty-two 14-26 month olds. The results supported previous study of novel action learning, however no evidence for novel word learning was shown. Sample size, children's age, task familiarity and motivation may have influenced the degree of learning demonstrated.

authors_raw: Kate S. Kalousek, Christine T. Chambers, Patrick J. McGrath, Natalie L. Yanchar, Chris Moore, Carl L. von Baeyer
title: EVERYDAY PAIN INCIDENTS IN CHILDREN AGED 12 TO 36 MONTHS
abstract: Aim of Investigation: Everyday bumps and hurts are a common occurrence for children. These minor yet frequent incidents may have considerable influence on children's development of pain coping strategies. While several studies have documented the frequency and nature of everyday pains in preschoolers (3 to 5 years), little is known about everyday pain in younger children. We examined the frequency and nature of everyday pain incidents in a sample of toddlers during active play. Methods: An observational checklist (Dalhousie Everyday Pain Scale - Revised) was used by trained coders to document the nature of minor painful incidents (e.g., body location, cause of hurt, severity of incident, child distress) that took place during the play session. Results: 35 children (18 boys, 17 girls) aged 13-31 months (M = 22.5, SD = 5.2) and their parents (32 mothers, 3 fathers) were observed at an indoor play facility for up to 4 hours during 2 separate 2 hour play sessions (N= 17 for session 1; N=18 for session 2). Analyses will examine how child variables (i.e., sex, age, temperament) influence the nature of the painful incidents as well as parental response to such incidents. The influence of parental reaction (comfort/sympathy, distraction, ignoring, punishment) on the intensity of child distress will also be examined..

title: I remember your smile: Recognizing facial emotions from a brief acquaintance.
abstract:
Does familiarity with a person's emotional responses in particular situations enhance an observer's ability to correctly recognize that person's responses in other situations? Dynamic facial displays of two people were individually paired with images to which they were responses, or paired with other images. For each display, observer's task was to judge whether a person was looking at the accompanying picture or not. Observers (N = 60) were exposed to a pre-test 'acquaintance' period for one of the stimulus faces. Acquaintance improved performance on paired displays previously seen (p <.001). Surprisingly, performance on previously unseen expressions did not differ from those for a complete stranger. Familiarity with the person's facial responses did not generalize to unfamiliar situations.
PRESENTATION: Talk
AVAILABLE: Either day.

title: Epigenetics: The future of psychology
abstract: One of the most enduring dichotomies in psychology is the "nature vs. nurture" debate. The human genome project allows us to identify every gene in the human (and mouse, rat, fruit fly, etc) and to understand the contributions of genes to neural and behavioural development. We are also able to identify the physical, social and chemical factors which influence neuro-behavioural development. Epigenetics is the study of how environmental variables regulate gene expression. The action of environmental factors on neurochemical systems and intracellular transduction pathways of neural cells modify the structure of histone proteins in the chromatin of the cell nucleus and these histone proteins regulate gene expression. Thus, epigenetic processes provide the mechanism for environmental regulation of gene expression and the solution to the nature-nurture problem. This talk gives a brief outline of epigenetics and describes epigenetic approaches to the study of behavioural development, aging, learning, memory, social behaviour and neuropsychiatric disorders. Already epigenetic research is changing the way that psychological processes are understood.

title: The acute effects of exercise or mindfulness therapy on affect, fatigue, and pain in patients with Multiple Chemical Sensitivities
abstract:
Multiple Chemical Sensitivities (MCS) is a condition characterized by sensitivity to low levels of food and chemical exposure, which results in a variety of physical symptoms including pain and fatigue. Symptoms may be caused by a number of common substances such as perfume, detergents, and exhaust. MCS has a large impact on the Nova Scotia health care system, as well as on the quality of life of those with the illness, and research into the effectiveness of treatments is needed.
The Nova Scotia Environmental Health Centre (NSEHC) is a medical facility dedicated to research and treatment of MCS and other environmental illnesses. The NSEHC currently offers mindfulness and graded exercise therapy programs for MCS patients. The purpose of the proposed study is to compare the acute and chronic effects of these two treatment programs on the outcomes of pain, fatigue, mood, and arousal. In doing so, this study intends to delineate the impact of these programs on the MCS patients and examine whether they are the same. A further goal is to determine at what point within each of these programs patients tend to show the most improvement.
The results of the present investigation may improve upon existing MCS treatment programs, and allow for the development of cost-effective, individualized programs. Preliminary results will be presented.

authors_raw: Shannon A. Johnson, Leslie M. Blaha, Vanessa A. Bruce, & James T. Townsend
title: Lack of Local Bias during Selective Attention Global-Local Processing in Autism Spectrum Disorders
abstract: Previous studies of global-local (GL) processing in autism spectrum disorders (ASD; Rinehart et al., 2000; Plaisted et al., 1999) suggest a lack of both global advantage and global interference. However, methods employed in these studies have serious limitations that raise questions about the validity of the results. In the current study, we used traditional cognitive science methods to examine GL processing in ASD during selective attention tasks. Sixteen high-functioning individuals with an ASD and 16 age- and IQ-matched controls completed eight selective attention GL tasks using hierarchical letter and number stimuli. Repeated measures ANOVAs revealed a main effect of attended dimension with faster response times for the global versus local dimension for both groups on all tasks. Contrary to previous ASD studies, global interference was demonstrated by both groups and neither group demonstrated local interference. Overall, findings indicate typical GL processing in high-functioning ASD participants during selective attention conditions.

authors_raw: Roisin M. O'Connor, Sherry H. Stewart, Margo C. Watt, & Kerry MacSwain
title: Alcohol Use and Gambling are Associated with Different BAS Components
abstract: Motivational models attribute behaviour to individual differences in the Behavioural Inhibition (BIS) and Activation (BAS) systems. BAS sensitivity has been linked to risk for addictions. Factor analyses suggest that BAS is comprised of drive, fun-seeking, and reward-responsiveness components. Research suggests that the fun-seeking and drive components are most strongly related to alcohol misuse and other externalizing behaviours. We examined the relative contributions of each BIS/BAS subscale to the prediction of 533 undergraduates' self-reported alcohol use, smoking, and gambling behaviour. Factor analysis supported the expected 4 BIS/BAS factors. Controlling for sex and age, hierarchical logistic regressions revealed that only elevated fun-seeking was a significant predictor of any drinking or smoking in the past month, while only low BIS was a significant predictor of any gambling in the past month. Controlling for sex and age, hierarchical linear regressions revealed that only elevated fun-seeking was a significant predictor of alcohol use frequency and quantity, while only elevated drive was a significant predictor of gambling frequency and money spent. Findings suggest that different components of BAS sensitivity contribute to alcohol use and smoking vs. gambling (i.e., the tendency to seek out new potentially rewarding experiences vs. the tendency to strongly pursue appetitive goals, respectively).

authors_raw: Haralambos Lavranos, Leslie Phillmore and Simon Gadbois Department of Psychology/Neuroscience Institute, Dalhousie University, Halifax, NS B3H 4J1
title: Characterization of Electrocommunicative Signals of Elephant Nose Fish (Gnathonemus petersii)
abstract: Elephant nose fish use weak electric signals for both electrolocation and electrocommunication. In this study, we sought to characterize electrocommunicative signals in two ways: how they change over distance and in response to conspecifics. The signals follow the inverse square law and fall off in maximum, minimum, and root mean square amplitude. The size of the fish was not related to signal power. Small sample size and low signal resolution prevented us from concluding that individual fish discriminate between familiar and non-familiar conspecifics, however there is preliminary evidence that the fish discriminate between an environment with or without conspecifics.

authors_raw: Laura Robblee, Sean Roach, and Leslie Phillmore Department of Psychology, Dalhousie University, Halifax, Nova Scotia B3H 4J1
title: BEHAVIOURAL RESPONSE TO PLAYBACK OF VOCALIZATIONS BY BLACK-CAPPED CHICKADEES (Poecile atricapillus)
abstract: Black-capped chickadees have a well studied and large repertoire of vocalizations used for communication; the production of two of these vocalization types, fee bee song and chick-a-dee call vary with season: song is produced primarily in spring, whereas call is produced year-round (ref). We examined the behavioural responses of black-capped chickadees to playback of three types of vocalizations: (1) conspecific fee bee song, used by males for territorial defense and mate attraction, (2) conspecific chick-a-dee calls, used by both males and females as contact and alarm calls, and (3) heterospecific (Song Sparrow, Melospiza melodia) song. We measured the number of vocal responses, the average distance from the sound source, and general activity before, during, and after playback. Overall, there were not many vocal responses to playback of any kind. However, birds appeared to spend more time closer to the speaker during playback of conspecific vocalizations than heterospecific vocalizations, and birds that heard conspecific calls were more active than birds that heard either heterospecific songs or silence. Results are discussed in terms of seasonal influence on behavioural response to vocalizations, and have implications for neural processes underlying perception of vocalizations.

authors_raw: Sherry H. Stewart, Tony Toneatto, Martin Zack, Nigel E. Turner, and Valerie V. Grant
title: Developing and Testing a New Gambling Motives Questionnaire
abstract: Summary: We developed and evaluated a 15-item Gambling Motives Questionnaire (GMQ). In Study 1, principal components analysis of GMQ items completed by 193 gamblers revealed Enhancement (EM), Coping (CM), and Social (SM) Motives factors. Probable pathological gamblers (PPGs) scored higher across scales than non-pathological gamblers (NPGs) with larger differences for CM and EM. EM predicted gambling behavior, and CM and EM predicted gambling problems. CM predicted gambling problems more strongly in women; EM predicted gambling problems more strongly in men. With gambling behavior controlled, only CM remained associated with gambling problems. 243 problem gamblers, representing six primary gambling activities (e.g., bingo, sports betting), participated in Study 2. GMQ subscales were differentially associated with demographic variables (e.g., CM correlated negatively with being married, working, and income). Gambling motives also varied by gambling activity (e.g., bingo was associated with CM and sports betting with EM). The GMQ appears a promising clinical research tool.

title: It Is What It Is! (Or Is It, "It Is Where It Is!"?)
abstract: The present study aimed to clarify the relative contributions of object and location information to children's awareness of the relation between a symbol and its referent. Three- and 4-year-old children were tested using a version of the DeLoache (1987) model-room search task. Performance on two types of models (congruent vs. conflict) and two types of trials (same-location search vs. different-location search) were compared as a function of whether object properties and object location matched or not. While there was no effect of age, children were able to use object property information but not object location information to search for hidden objects, and when they erred, they appeared to do so by using object-based information.

authors_raw: Jordan Scantlebury, Richard E. Brown, & Tara S. Perrot-Sinal
title: The potential role of fetal amyloid-β in the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease among parous women: Are the kids really driving you crazy?
abstract: Epidemiological research suggests that parous women (those with children) are more likely to have a diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease (AD) than nulliparous women (those without children), perhaps due to a decrease in the average age at disease onset. Parity has not been associated with AD in men. Recent discovery of a moderate increase in amyloid plaque (AP) density with reproductive experience in women, but not men, corroborates these reports. The relationship between parity and AD has been attributed to changes in estrogen and progesterone exposure accompanying pregnancy, but compelling evidence indicates that the role of sex hormones in AD has been exaggerated. The purpose of the proposed research is to investigate a parsimonious alternative mechanism whereby parity may enhance AD pathogenesis, namely, the possibility that amyloid-β (Aβ), which is developmentally expressed, is transported from the fetus to the mother during pregnancy. Aβ is the putative pathogenic peptide in AD, and the predominant hypothesis of AD etiology predicts that the presence of fetal Aβ will increase the rate of AP formation and neurodegeneration.

title: An habituation account of inhibition of return
abstract:
Inhibition of return (IOR) is an effect whereby reaction times (RTs) to a target presented at a location where a stimulus had recently been presented are increased compared to when a target is presented at a new location. Early reports that habituation is not responsible for the IOR effect are shown to be ill conceived. More importantly, a large portion of the literature can be accounted for using the habituation framework. The current paper reviews some of the support for this theory, and explores some of the predictions that this theory suggests.
Behavioural Consequences of Preferred Direction Coding in Human Motor Cortex
Cowper-Smith, C.D.1, B.Sc Westwood, D.A.2, Ph.D.
To examine the behavioural relevance of the directional tuning preferences for neurons in primary motor cortex (M1), we analyzed the reaction time for consecutive, endogenously cued arm and eye movements (E1: arm-arm; E2: eye-eye). We also explored the existence of a common spatial coordinate system for arm and eye movements by including both types of movements in the same experiment (E3: hand-eye; E4: eye-hand). Participants fixated a central target that was surrounded by 8 equidistant peripheral targets. When the prime cue (a directional arrow) was displayed at fixation, participants responded with a joystick or eye movement to the indicated target and then returned to centre. The probe cue was delivered 1.5 seconds after the onset of the prime cue, and required a second response (joystick or eye movement); this movement could be to the same target or any of the other targets. The results suggest that arm and eye movements utilize different coordinate systems that do not map onto the known directional tuning curves of neurons in a straightforward manner. The results are broadly consistent with past research on motoric inhibition of return.

authors_raw: Gail Eskes, Ray Klein, David Shore
title: Using Temporal Order Judgments to Study Spatial Neglect.
abstract: Spatial neglect after right hemisphere stroke is common and results in difficulties with orienting, detecting or responding to left-sided stimuli. Investigating the underlying mechanisms of neglect can be challenging, due to spontaneous improvement, learned compensatory strategies by patients, and heterogeneity of the neglect syndrome, both within a patient on different tests, as well as between patients. In this talk, we'll describe our approach to studying neglect using temporal order judgments (TOJs) and the preliminary results from a series of studies examining both bottom-up and top-down mechanisms controlling attention in neglect.

title: Using eye movements to answer basic and applied questions
abstract: Helmholtz demonstrated that attention and gaze direction could be dissociated and more recently Posner developed the cuing paradigm as a model task for exploring the nature of attention shifts in the absence of shifts in gaze direction. Yet, normally attention and eye movements are closely coordinated. Scholars have, therefore, used oculomotor behavior as a tool for answering basic science questions about cognitive processes and for generating data to shed light on real-world problems. Some of the basic and applied research in the Klein laboratory that uses these strategies will be briefly described.

title: Preserving Our History with Digital Video: The Snapshot Project, Year 2
abstract: In the winter term last year, students in the Psychology/Neuroscience 3010 class began a project to record on digital media a video "snapshot" of the department as it exists today, by interviewing faculty members on camera and then producing a short edited version of each interview. Copies of the interviews on DVD have been enthusiastically received by the university archivist, and may some day be available in a departmental museum. This year's 3010 class completed an additional nine interviews. In this presentation, we will briefly describe the project and the training given the students, and show the resulting material that will go into the departmental and university archives.

authors_raw: Luiza Radu, Esther Lau, & Gail Eskes
title: Identifying Risk of Obstructive Sleep Apnea in a General Population using the Berlin Questionnaire, the Sleep Apnea Scale of the Sleep Disorder Questionnaire, and Polysomnography
abstract: Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) is a common sleep disorder which has been linked to serious health issues, cognitive disabilities and driving risk, but is often under-diagnosed in adult populations. The Berlin questionnaire (BQ) and the Sleep Apnea scale of the Sleep Disorders Questionnaire (SA-SDQ) are screening tools used for identifying persons at risk for OSA, although validation studies using lab polysomnography in healthy populations are limited. We examined how well the BQ and the SA-SDQ predicted the presence of sleep apnea in a group of healthy individuals (n=34), with no previous history of sleep disordered breathing. Individuals (ages 33 to 85, 13 Males, 21 Females) were assessed for risk of OSA using the BQ and SA-SDQ followed by overnight in-laboratory polysomnography. Sleep apnea was defined by the Respiratory Disturbance Index (RDI; number of breathing disruptions/hour) and designated as mild (RDI = 5-15; 9 individuals) or moderate (RDI > 15; 7 individuals). Of those 16 with OSA symptoms, four were designated as OSA risk by the BQ. Additionally out of the 18 individuals with no OSA symptoms (RDI < 5), 14 were considered to be at a low risk for OSA by the BQ. These findings indicate that the BQ has low sensitivity. Our analysis further showed that the SA-SDQ was not an accurate predictor for sleep apnea (RDI > 5). Recommendations for BQ and SA-SDQ use will be discussed.

authors_raw: Fiona Davidson, Sarah Ironside, & Penny Corkum
title: Changes in Circadian Rhythm Evident During an Acute Randomized Placebo-Controlled Trial of Methylphenidate
abstract:
Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a highly prevalent disorder in school-aged children. Stimulant medications are commonly used to treat ADHD, however, it has been suggested that they may alter the timing or regularity of circadian motor activity levels. The current project aimed to investigate the impact of stimulant medication on circadian rhythms in children with ADHD. Twenty medication-naïve children with ADHD were monitored for changes in motor-activity during a blinded placebo-controlled medication trial to examine the impact of immediate-release methylphenidate hydrochloride (MPH-IR). Motor activity was measured by actigraphy and activity profiles were analyzed to identify changes in circadian rhythms. MPH-IR was related to a measurable phase-delay in the timing of the daily rhythm and was found to increase night-time motor activity during sleep-onset latency. In this sample, strong and stable daily rhythms over each week of the medication trial were observed.
Children taking MPH-IR have been found to experience prolonged sleep-onset latency.

authors_raw: S. Allen, A. Graham, D. McGrath, E. Breen, J. Tonet, D. Sherry, S. Stewart, & S. Sherry
title: Testing the Existential Alienation Model: A Cross-sectional Study in an Undergraduate Sample
abstract: Research suggests persons high in socially prescribed perfectionism (SPP; i.e., perceiving others are demanding perfection of oneself) are at risk for depressive symptoms. The present study examines mechanisms believed to explain the SPP-depression relationship. Specifically, the present study tests the Existential Alienation Model (EAM). This model hypothesizes that SPP contributes to cognitive dysfunction (e.g., catastrophic thinking) and to existential problems (e.g., difficulty accepting the past) which, in turn, contribute to depressive symptoms. To test these hypotheses, a cross-sectional study was conducted wherein undergraduates completed self-report measures assessing SPP, catastrophic thinking, difficulty accepting the past, and depressive symptoms. Path analyses provided support for the EAM such that catastrophic thinking and difficulty accepting the past mediated the relationship between SPP and depressive symptoms. Persons high in SPP may be vulnerable to depressive symptoms because they experience catastrophic thoughts about everyday events and encounter difficulties making sense out of their experiences.

authors_raw: Leona Chu, Ben Smith, & Patrice Cote
title: Spontaneous Nav1.6 Rescue (snr): Discovery of a Modifier Allele that Attenuates the Motor Phenotype in a Voltage-Gated Sodium Channel Mutant
abstract: The voltage-gated sodium channel Nav1.6 is concentrated at the nodes of Ranvier in central and peripheral neurons and is important for the propagation of the nerve impulse. Mice with a mutation in Nav1.6 are born normal but quickly deteriorate due to muscle weakness and paralysis. These mice are termed dmu (degenerating muscle). Here we report the discovery within our colony of dmu mutants of mice that do not display this motor phenotype. We have called these mice snr for spontaneous Nav1.6 rescue. These mice do however have abnormal electroretinogram (ERG) tracings that are identical to the ones typically seen in dmu mutants. These findings suggest that an independent gene affects the severity of the motor phenotype. Purpose: To characterize snr, a recently discovered spontaneous mutation within the dmu Nav1.6 mutant mouse strain. Methods: First, the genotype of dmu mutant mice is determined. Homozygous snr mice are then identified based on their phenotype and ERG measurements. ERG measurements will be performed at developmental stages to determine retinal morphology changes associated with age. Preliminary work were made in behavioral monitoring, sensorimotor coordination, overall motor function, and sensorimotor integration focusing on hindlimb function. Results: Data gathered from these experiments indicate that there is a rescue of the snr motor phenotype without recovery of visual acuity. Conclusions: Initial results strongly suggest that snr mice are a new substrain within the dmu mouse strain. Further genetic characterization of both dmu and snr mice will reveal the mode of inheritance of this new modifier allele.

title: Expressions of CRH in Postnatal Rat Pups: Sex Differences in the Development of the HPA Axis
abstract: The importance of circulating gonadal hormones in adult stress responding has been well documented. However, little research has focused on the role of early life gonadal hormones in permanently organizing these dimorphisms. In rodents, the early postnatal period is critical for the sexual differentiation of the brain, and is also a time of plasticity in the developing stress system; thus, significant interactions between the two processes may occur. This study focuses on the developmental timeline of corticotrophin releasing factor (CRF) across early postnatal development in brain regions associated with the HPA axis, including the PVN, BNST, CeA and hippocampus. Preliminary results suggest a role of both sex and age in the number of CRF-ir neurons in the PVN, CeA and hippocampus. This research may contribute to our understanding of "normal" HPA axis development, as well as how/why early life stressors might disrupt HPA axis function in a sex-specific manner.

authors_raw: Simon Gadbois, Meredith Flannery, & Deborah Austin
title: Wildlife Conservation Canines in Kejimkujik National Park
abstract: Conservation canines have been used for decades to find endangered or threatened species in the wild. Dogs have been used to find a wide range of species at risk. Together with Parks Canada, we are interested in using dogs to find Eastern Ribbonsnakes and Pine Martens in Kejimkujik National Park. Both species will benefit from accurate population density information and habitat use data. Our lab brings a synthetic and integrative approach to the use of canines in the field and the lab: knowledge of the wildlife habits and physiology of the target species and knowledge of animal learning principles and animal psychophysics. We are interested in field applications, methodologies and training techniques, including approaches never used with scent detection canines. Two main training techniques (errorless discrimination training and traditional discrimination training) will be compared. Once we demonstrate the effectiveness of our methodology, we may expand this work to other local and problematic species.

authors_raw: J. Gawryluk, E. Mazerolle, K. Dillen, K. Brewer, S. Beyea, & R. D'Arcy
title: White Matter fMRI in the Corpus Callosum
abstract: Although most fMRI studies restrict their focus to gray matter, an increasing number of studies have observed white matter (WM) activation. Our group recently reported the first prospective WM fMRI studies at 4T using interhemispheric transfer tasks. The initial study used a visual word/face task, which elicited activation in the isthmus of the corpus callosum. A subsequent study used optimized WM fMRI using an asymmetric spin echo (ASE) spiral imaging sequence and a visual-motor checkerboard task, which elicited activation in the anterior corpus callosum. The current study examined whether varying task type differentially activates WM within the same subjects, thereby allowing for the assessment of functional differentiation in the corpus callosum. The results show corpus callosum activation for both tasks, with location of activation varying according to task type. These findings provide further support for WM fMRI which may lead to advanced clinical applications for WM disorders such as multiple sclerosis.

authors_raw: Jeannette McGlone, Kaitlyn Lee, Bernd Pohlmann-Eden, Mark Sadler, & Jacqueline Gillespie
title: Developing a Comprehensive Measure of Perceived Stigma for Use with Literate Adults who have Epilepsy
abstract: Perceived stigma has been associated with poorer self-management of seizures, lower quality of life, and negative psychosocial consequences in persons with epilepsy. Measuring level of perceived stigma in epilepsy is critical because of its high prevalence (~50%) and potential for intervention. One available instrument, the Felt Stigma Scale (Jaccoby, 1994) has good internal reliability and cross cultural applications, but its properties of brevity (only 3 items), one dimensionality, and being interview-based all limit application in treatment studies. This project adapts two new measures derived from the mental health literature for use with persons diagnosed with epilepsy. The Stigma Scale (TSS; King et al., 2007) and the Internalized Stigma of Mental Illness scale (ISMI; Ritsher, Otilingam, & Grajales, 2003) were selected because of their length, multidimensionality, strong psychometric and conceptual properties. Each contains a subscale that measures thriving despite adversity, hence we examined if those subscales correlated significantly with the total stigma score or with a traditional measure of Resilience. The results are promising.

authors_raw: Heather Phelan, Jillian Filliter, & Shannon Johnson
title: Relationships between Memory Performance and Intellectual Ability in Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract: Previous studies have found that individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) experience some difficulties with learning and memory, particularly the use of mnemonic strategies. Proponents of the Task Support Hypothesis assert that, when external supports are provided, individuals with ASD perform more similar to typically developing peers (Bowler et al., 1997). We administered the California Verbal Learning Test, Children's Version (CVLT-C) to 14 high-functioning youth with ASD and 14 matched controls. The control group had a significantly steeper learning slope and significantly higher total recall than the ASD group. For the ASD group, but not controls, recall performance improved significantly from free recall to cued recall, providing further support for the Task Support Hypothesis. Correlational analyses suggested significant associations between better performance and higher IQ for the ASD group. Findings indicate that task support, along with a higher IQ, is related to memory performance in children and adolescents with ASD.

authors_raw: Erin Moon, Penny Corkum, & Isabel Smith
title: A Case Series Evaluation of a Manualized Treatment Program for Sleep Problems in Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract: A preliminary investigation of the effectiveness of a manualized behavioural sleep intervention program for children with was conducted with three participants (2 males; 1 female; aged 8-9 years) with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and dyssomnia. The primary objective of the program was to reduce sleep onset latency. The intervention consisted of a treatment handbook for parents; a distance treatment approach was used in which parents had weekly telephone contact with a trained facilitator. Within a case series design, both subjective (parent-report questionnaires and sleep diaries) and objective (actigraphy) measures were used to record changes in children's sleep and daytime behaviour. For all three children, mean sleep onset latency was reduced substantially following the intervention. These improvements were maintained after 12 weeks, providing initial support for the effectiveness of this intervention program.

authors_raw: Matthew Ryan, Ray Klein, & Gail Eskes
title: The Effects of Prism Adaptation on Recovery of Functional Skills in Unilateral Neglect Patients - A Proposal
abstract:
Right Hemisphere stroke patients often present with Unilateral neglect, characterized by rightward bias in eye, head, and hand movement, and deficits exploring the left hemi-space. Prism adaptation has shown promise for uncovering neglect's underlying mechanisms and ameliorating spatial deficits. Patients wear goggles shifting vision rightward, then undergo leftward visuo-motor compensation while pointing at visual targets. Following adaptation, enduring improvements have been found in tests of neglect and spatial attention.
	Evidence for prism adaptation's efficacy in recovering activities of daily living is sparse. Experiments addressing improvements in this area have been anecdotal reports, shown null effects, and were small sample or case studies without control groups. The proposed study will assess Prism Adaptation's effect on a validated index of daily life activities by comparing two groups neglect patients: one receiving adaptation versus a control group. It is hypothesized the adaptation group will show greater progress in recovering functional skills.

authors_raw: Nicole Ouellette, Cindy Hamon-Hill & John Barresi
title: Determining the Existence of Separate Neural Networks for Static and Dynamic Facial Expression via Embodied Simulation
abstract: This study attempted to give a behavioural correlate to recent neurological evidence for separate neural networks for processing of static and dynamic facial expressions. Ninety-six adults were engaged in an emotional recognition task. A dissociation of neurological processing was tested by utilizing a non-invasive behavioural procedure, which engaged the cortico-bulbar pathway, to interfere with embodied simulation. It was hypothesized that the static stimuli, relying on more motor mimicry along the cortico-bulbar route for an affective representation of the emotional state, would result in decreased performance (accuracy and reaction time) on the emotional recognition task. Dynamic stimuli, relying on a separate, extrapyramidal pathway for an affective representation of the emotional state, would not be affected. The results of this study support separable processing of static and dynamic stimuli. These findings highlight an important distinction between processing of static facial expressions and their more ecologically valid dynamic counterparts.

authors_raw: Jonathan Harris, David Westwood, & Chris Cowper-Smith
title: Inhibition of Return for Vertically-Directed Saccades
abstract: A great deal of research has examined IOR for saccades that are parallel with the horizontal but not vertical meridian. Thus, we used a saccade-saccade paradigm to investigate potential differences in the magnitude of IOR between the horizontal and vertical axis. In each trial, participants made two saccades in response to either endogenous or exogenous signals (S1 and S2). Gaze was returned to centre between S1 and S2. Trials were further characterized by S1 direction (left, right, down, or up) and S2 direction relative to S1 (same or 180° opposite). Our results show that within the horizontal axis, IOR was present and did not differ in magnitude between right and left. For the vertical axis, IOR was revealed for saccades made in response to downward but not upward S2s. These results are interpreted in the context of differences in visual search/spatial attention between upper and lower visual field.

title: Motoric Inhibition of Return and the Coordination of Voluntary Arm and Eye Movements
abstract: The current view of motoric inhibition of return (IOR) depends critically upon the activation and subsequent inhibition of oculomotor processes. However, recent evidence suggests that skeletomotor activity can directly affect the magnitude of IOR. In order to address this possibility further, we analyzed the gradient of reaction times of consecutive voluntary arm and eye movements (E1: eye-eye; E2: arm-arm; E3: arm-eye; E4: eye-arm) that were offset by 0°, 45°, ±90°, 135°, or 180°. Participants fixated a central target that was surrounded by 8 equidistant peripheral targets. When signal 1 (S1; a directional arrow) was displayed at fixation, participants responded with a joystick or eye movement to the indicated target and then returned to centre. Signal 2 (S2) was delivered 1.5 seconds after the onset of S1 and required a second response (joystick or eye movement); this movement could be to the same target or any of the other targets. Our results show that different gradients of IOR occur depending upon the relative involvement of the eye and hand. These results are interpreted by positing that different task-specific forms of motoric IOR may operate to promote effective visual search and object interaction behaviours.

title: Oculomotor Inhibition of Return (O-IOR)
abstract: Discovered by Posner & Cohen (1984) using a simple cue-target paradigm, inhibition of return (IOR) was originally conceptualized as an aftermath of orienting that discouraged attention and gaze from returning to previously attended locations and objects. Interest in IOR has grown steadily in the 25 years since it was discovered, partly because it is easy to study and partly because of its putative function as a foraging facilitator. Beginning with Taylor-Helmick's dissertation (Taylor, 1997) evidence has accumulated for two flavours of IOR: one flavour is elicited when the oculomotor system is tonically inhibited by the task requirement to remain fixated while the other flavour (Oculomotor-IOR) is elicited when the oculomotor system is engaged by some aspect of the task. Research from our lab, and others, focussing on the role of oculomotor processes in generating IOR and the effects of IOR, however generated, upon oculomotor processing, will be presented.

authors_raw: Kristie Dukewich, Mike Lawrence, & Ray Klein
title: Testing the 'IOR as Habituation' Proposal using Stimulus Intensity
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) is an effect whereby people are slower to respond to a target at a previously cued location compared to a target that is presented at a previously uncued location. Dukewich (2009) proposed that the IOR effect is a product of habituation based on repetitive spatial stimulation and outlined several predictions regarding the IOR effect that were based on the parametric characteristics of habituation. We tested the prediction, rooted in the habituation literature, that low-intensity stimuli would produce more IOR compared to high-intensity stimuli. Our results are not entirely consistent with the original prediction. Is there something wrong with our experiment? Is the prediction we have derived from the habituation literature incorrect? Or is the proposal simply disproved?

authors_raw: Janine Olthuis, Brigitte Sabourin, Margo Watt, & Sherry Stewart
title: Brief CBT for High Anxiety Sensitivity: Effects on Implicit and Explicit Catastrophic Misinterpretations of Arousal Sensations
abstract: Anxiety sensitivity (AS) is a fear of anxiety and anxiety-related sensations (e.g., increased heart rate). This fear occurs because individuals with high AS tend to misinterpret and catastrophize these physiological sensations, believing that they can have serious physical, psychological, or social consequences. Research suggests that AS may be a cognitive risk factor for anxiety disorders. Past studies show that a brief cognitive behavioural therapy intervention, with a physical exercise interoceptive exposure component, is effective in decreasing AS in (non-clinical) high AS individuals. To better understand the mechanisms through which this intervention works, and the role played by catastrophic misinterpretation of anxiety-related arousal sensations, this study will test the effectiveness of this intervention in decreasing both AS levels and catastrophic misinterpretations (assessed implicitly and explicitly) of anxiety-related arousal symptoms. The research plan and expected findings will be presented. The results will help improve available interventions and prevention for anxiety disorder.

authors_raw: N. Hartling, S. Doucette, K. Fossum, A. Hamilton, S. Sutherland, D. Sherry, S. Stewart, & S. Sherry
title: Testing the Interpersonal Model of Health Anxiety: Interpersonal Neuroticism, Health-Related Worry, Health-Related Reassurance-Seeking, and Health-Related Alienation in Romantic Couples
abstract: This study involves a preliminary test of two mediational models hypothesized in the Interpersonal Model of Health Anxiety (IMHA; Noyes et al., 2003; Sherry et al., 2008). According to the IMHA, health-related worry mediates the relationship between interpersonal neuroticism (i.e., anxious attachment and emotional instability) and health-related reassurance-seeking; and health-related reassurance-seeking mediates the relationship between interpersonal neuroticism and health-related alienation. Eighty-two couples were recruited to test these hypotheses and both self-reports and informant reports were collected. Mediated regression analyses partially supported one hypothesized model: Consistent with the IMHA, health-related worry partially mediated the relationship between interpersonal neuroticism and health-related reassurance-seeking. Support for this mediation model was specific to women and to self-reports. Interpersonal neuroticism may represent a dispositional personality structure that underlies and drives key symptoms of health anxiety (e.g., excessive reassurance-seeking). Health-related reassurance-seeking may function as a maladaptive form of interpersonally-focused emotional regulation among women high in interpersonal neuroticism.

authors_raw: A. Graham, S. Allen, D. McGrath, E. Breen, J. Tonet, D. Sherry, S. Stewart, & S. Sherry
title:
Perfectionistic Concerns, Catastrophizing, Difficulty Accepting the Past, and 
Depressive Symptoms: A Short-Term, 4-Wave Prospective Study
abstract: Persons high in perfectionistic concerns (PC; i.e., hyper-sensitivity to failures, concern over evaluations, and doubts about abilities) appear vulnerable to depressive symptoms. This study tests a new model, which aims to explain why persons high in PC are vulnerable to depressive symptoms. The proposed model represents a dual pathway mediational model where catastrophizing and difficulty accepting the past are hypothesized to mediate the relation between PC and depressive symptoms. A 4-wave longitudinal study, involving 240 undergraduates, was conducted to test these hypotheses. Consistent with the proposed model, catastrophizing and difficulty accepting the past mediated the relation between PC and depressive symptoms (after controlling for baseline depressive symptoms). Persons high in PC appear to appraise stressful events in a catastrophic manner and to experience difficulty in negotiating a sense of purpose and meaning in their lives.
