Amid such cognitive and existential difficulties, persons high in PC appear vulnerable to depressive symptoms.

title: The Effect of Academic and Social Integration in Real and Virtual Groups on Self-Efficacy among First-Year Students
abstract: Retention of university students is a major issue for Canadian universities. Low academic and social integration, along with career goals and intention to continue in university, influence attrition. The College Self-Efficacy Instrument (CSEI) is a good predictor of retention and attrition. This study measured first-year students' feelings of self-efficacy and then randomly assigned participants to one of three intervention task conditions (academic, school spirit, and control) and one of three social groups (social, virtual, and individual). Measuring participants' feelings of self-efficacy before, immediately after, and two weeks after the experiment showed a significant effect for the social group on the final measure of the CSEI, which suggests the importance of face-to-face, social interaction in the university environment.

title: Disrupted Embodied Simulation while Judging Appropriateness of Facial Emotion to Context
abstract: By having participants hold or bite a pen in their mouth for two of four blocks of trials, we tested whether inhibition of or interference with embodied simulation would affect one's accuracy for judging when a target's facial expression was appropriate to context. Dynamic facial displays of 4 targets were individually paired with images to which they were responses (match) or paired with other images (mismatch). Participants holding the pen improved on mismatch trials and worsened on match trials. Participants biting the pen performed better initially than hold participants on mismatch trials and did not exhibit a reversed pattern of accuracy. These results are indicative of a disruption in normal embodied simulation. The typical process of matching the emotional state of self to other may be disrupted when embodied simulation is interfered with. Due to the motor activation with the pen, the mismatched state of the self to given contexts may influence searching for a mismatch state in other.

title: An About-Face in Lie Detection: An Investigation of Spontaneous and Posed Facial Expressions
abstract: Facial deception research is frequently applied to high-stakes security and forensic settings; however, the breadth and quality of the field is limited. Recent studies have asked participants to pose facial expressions while viewing emotional images, assuming that this technique is analogous to examining spontaneous expressions. In this investigation, we attempted to elicit spontaneous facial reactions with commonly used visual stimuli, and compared them to posed expressions. Spontaneous reactions were rare. In a second experiment, we modified the stimuli by simultaneously presenting emotional images and sounds. The majority of participants demonstrated spontaneous facial expressions, which they were unable to conceal when instructed to do so. Our findings suggest that existing stimuli are inadequate for facial deception research, and that supplementing posed for spontaneous expressions conceals some cues to deception.

title: A Test of Explanations for the Preference of Concordant Binocular Visual Experience in Early Visual Development
abstract: Kittens that receive mixed early visual experience comprised of adjacent episodes of normal (binocular) and abnormal (monocular) visual exposure show a decided preference for the former. So long as about 30% of the total visual experience is normal, animals develop both normal visual acuity in both eyes and cortical architecture in terms of the dimensions of ocular dominance domains in V1. We tested whether this preference for binocular exposure arose because it matched the cortical architecture that existed at the time that mixed visual exposure was first imposed. To test this idea we induced an abnormal early cortical architecture by monocularly depriving animals from near birth so that now, at the time that mixed visual exposure was initiated, monocular exposure was the closest match to the existing cortical architecture. However, despite the match to the cortical architecture, monocular exposure did not appear to be preferred; indeed short daily episodes of binocular exposure were still very effective.

title: Lateral Adaptation Pooling in the Vision of White-Eyed Flies
abstract:
In insect eyes, convention holds that the ON- and OFF transient waves of the electroretinogram (ERG) come from first-order interneurons, lamina monopolar cells (LMCs): these occupy the position of bipolar cells in the vertebrate retina. Ease of recording the ERG has led to its resurgence as a diagnostic tool for understanding synaptic transmission in the model organism, Drosophila. The presence of ERG transients is used to assay whether the first synapse in the eye is working, in genetic rescue missions. These transients cannot come from the LMCs, however: one suggestive piece of evidence is that the transients are involved in extensive lateral adaptational pooling. This is believed not to be a property of LMCs, thought to be strictly local processors. The structure of the eye predicts that a LMC wave ought to be present in the ERG: a contender wave has been detected, but is small and hard to isolate in normal flies.
	White-eyed mutant flies are missing both of the eye's two main screening pigments, and light rays entering from a narrow angle experience increased internal scatter. Correspondingly, the mutant eye discloses a larger, more easily measured version of the supposed LMC wave. Surprisingly, tests show that this wave too is subject to lateral adaptational interactions, though nothing like this has been detected so far in direct intracellular recordings from LMCs. This discrepancy might be resolved if lateral interaction acted presynaptically at the level of the photoreceptor terminals via amacrine cells, before these release their neurotransmitter to activate the postsynaptic LMCs.

title: Ultrastructure and Synaptic Characteristics of Neurons of the Mushroom Body Calyx in Drosophila Melanogaster
abstract: Synaptic circuits form the functional basis of behaviour. To understand behaviour, we must first investigate the wiring and properties of neurons in these circuits. The fruit fly Drosophila provides an excellent genomic species to study neural circuits because it has a relatively simple nervous system and generates readily quantifiable behaviours. The mushroom body (MB) is a high-order integrative centre of the insect brain, which in Drosophila is required for olfactory learning and memory. The input neuropil, the MB calyx comprises three types of neurons: projection neurons (PNs) from the antennal lobe; Kenyon cells (KCs) intrinsic to the MB; and extrinsic neurons (ENs) from various sources. Using serial-section electron microscopy, we have characterized the morphology of these elements from computer 3-D reconstructions, and their synaptic organization. We find that each PN terminal bouton forms twenty or more output synapses per KC, and confirm that KCs have claw-shaped dendritic endings which encircle the PN bouton. The KC dendrites and PNs also receive inputs from EN elements, and ENs also receive occasional inputs from PN terminals. Thus PN and EN elements connect reciprocally. We also find evidence for extensive connections between ENs. The implications of these findings will be discussed.

title: From DNA to Protein - the Cricket's Tale: Part One
abstract: Molecular and proteomic approaches can provide information on the cellular responses to stressful stimuli. Genetics can identify novel genes while a proteomic approach can identify proteins that play a role in an animal's stress response. We have used these methods to identify the presence/absence of receptors to the neurohormone octopamine in the tissues of the cricket (Gryllus texensis). Octopamine plays a critical role in the cricket's stress response. Once we identify the target tissues in the insect's immune system (eg. fat body, haemocytes) we will test for changes in protein expression following physiological challenges. We will use this information to explore how and why stress alters immune function.

authors_raw: Donald Mitchell, Katelyn MacNeill, & Jan Kennie
title: Cecilia, You're Breaking My Heart!
abstract: We have previously documented severe deficits of global motion perception in kittens that are binocularly deprived of vision in the first 6 weeks. In addition, we have noted informally, an apparent deficit in their ability to follow the motion of laser pointer lights. To explore this quantitatively we have exploited robotic technology with the assistance of North Knight Software to construct CECIL (Cat Entertaining Computer Instructed Laser). We will recount our attempts to train cats to follow moving laser spots using Cecil (at first they appeared as interested as students in a class listening to an account of The Principle of Univariance in relation to colour vision). In addition we will explain our ideas with respect to the existence of possible critical periods for combining the perception of motion with action.

authors_raw: Nicole White, Jonathan Fawcett, & Aaron Newman
title: Biological Motion Perception: An ERP Study on the Functional Role of the N300 Component
abstract: Previous event-related potential (ERP) studies of biological motion perception have identified two ERP components reliably observed in response to point-light stimuli: the N170 and the N300. The N300 has been hypothesized to reflect neural processing specific to biological motion, but in a recent study, static point-light stimuli were found to elicit an N300 component similar to that of biological motion. Thus, it is unclear this component indexes recognition of the human form, or processing of biological motion information. In order to further clarify the role of the N300 component, ERPs were recorded from 24 subjects while viewing three types of point-light stimuli: static point-light frames, stick figures, and classic biological motion. The N300 component was elicited in response to both static frames and stick figures, as well as to biological motion. The amplitude of the N300 was affected similarly by inversion and scrambling across all three stimulus types, indicating that the brain processes these stimuli in the same way. We therefore suggest that the N300 component indexes human form recognition and is not specific to biological motion.

authors_raw: Andrew Trerice, Yoko Ishigami, Matthew Ryan, Luiza Radu, & Gail Eskes
title: Pointing Right to See Left
abstract: Visuomotor adaptation to a prism-induced lateral displacement of the visual field to the right has been found to improve symptoms of spatial neglect due to right hemisphere damage. Whether the positive effect is based on bottom-up visuomotor plasticity or on top-down spatial representations is unclear, however. We tested a patient with neglect due to damage to subcortical regions (primarily basal ganglia), on a series of tests of perceptual or visuo-motor bias. These measures were administered before and after the patient adapted to a prism-induced rightward visual shift. Despite visuo-motor adaptation as verified by a midline pointing test, performance on tests of neglect was generally unchanged. Outcome measures included star cancellation, landmark, chimeric faces, temporal order judgment, and visual search tasks. The specific pattern of results will be discussed in relation to previous studies.

title: Letter Names and Alphabet Book Reading by Senior Kindergarteners: An Eye Movement Study
abstract: In this study, we monitored eye movements of 20 five-year-old children while reading an alphabet book to examine the manner with which the letters, word, and pictures were fixated and the relationship of attention to print to alphabetic knowledge. Children attended little to the print elements, took longer to first fixate print than illustrations, and labelled fewer letters than they did when presented with letters in isolation. After controlling for scores on a receptive vocabulary test, regressions revealed that children knowing more letters were quicker to look at the featured letter on a page, and spent more time looking at the featured letter, the word, and its first letter. Thus alphabet books along with letter knowledge may facilitate entrance into the partial alphabetic stage of word recognition.

title: Children's Spelling of Vowel Sounds: The Use of Morphological Strategies
abstract: English orthography encodes both phonological and morphological information, necessitating the use of multiple spelling strategies. The phonological quality of vowels depends in part on lexical stress patterns; vowels in stressed syllables are phonologically salient, whereas vowels in unstressed syllables reduce to the toneless phoneme schwa. Morphological derivations offer an additional source of vowel spelling information when the addition of a suffix to a stem word drives a lexical stress shift (and consequently changes the phonemic vowel quality; MAjor-maJORity), making the use of the relationships between words a potentially helpful spelling strategy. This talk will offer evidence for a trend toward children's use of the information provided by morphological manipulation when spelling unstressed vowels, as well as a planned research that will clarify whether children independently employ this strategy, and whether a simple intervention can facilitate its use.

authors_raw: Hélène Deacon, Diane Leblanc, & Chantal Sabourin
title: Double or Nothing: Children's Integration of Letter- and-Meaning-Patterns in Spelling
abstract: Learning to spell in English can be challenging because there are several levels of regularities. As an example, long and short vowels in English are, respectively, spelled with a vowel followed by a consonant singlet and doublet (e.g., diner and dinner). This letter-pattern regularity sometimes conflicts with meaning patterns, through which the spellings of root words are often, but not always, retained in related forms (such as dine - diner, but knit - knitter). The present experiment is designed to determine the relative influences of these two sources of information on children's spelling. We found that children's sensitivity to letter-patterns emerged early and was very robust, but, by grade 2, their sensitivity to meaning-patterns emerged in a manner that sometimes lead them to errors. This intriguing pattern of results will be discussed.

authors_raw: Sarah Massoud, Steve Aiken, & Aaron Newman
title: Binaural Integration in Asymmetric Hearing Loss
abstract: It remains unknown whether the failure of binaural integration in patients with asymmetric hearing loss is primarily governed by the absolute hearing threshold in the more severely impaired ear or by the level of asymmetry between the two ears. In order to tease apart these causes, this study characterizes binaural integration as a function of interaural level difference in controls. ASSR recordings were made while participants listened to short blocks of Gaussian noise with an interaural correlation (IAC) alternating between 0 and +1 at 4 Hz. Stimuli were presented with sound levels varying in asymmetry from 0-40 dB with reference levels of 80, 60, and 40 dB. Significant neural responses at 4 Hz and 8 Hz indicated that binaural integration occurred at almost every level of asymmetry. Main effects of asymmetry or reference level were seen only on 8 Hz responses. Binaural integration appears to break down around an interaural asymmetry of 40 dB.

title: Classification of Keystroke Dynamics
abstract:
Developed mainly to improve password security, keystroke dynamics is a biometric technique aimed at identifying users on the basis of their typing patterns. Features such as the time between key presses are inputted into machine learning, data mining, and/or pattern recognition algorithms in order to develop classifiers that can identify a genuine user versus an imposter.
	Another potential, but as of yet unexplored, application of keystroke dynamics is the study of psycholinguistics. As a first step, this study will evaluate whether word features (e.g., word frequency) can be identified on the basis of keystroke dynamics. An existing algorithm for classifying users will be implemented and modified in order to classify words on the basis of their linguistic features. This work will provide insight into the mechanisms of typed language production, which is important given the increased prevalence of computer-based communication.

title: An Introduction to Statistics in R
abstract: R is free computer software that can be used for everything from very simple to the most advanced data analysis. It is the language of statisticians. This will be a live tutorial of where to get started using R. It will begin with addressing the reasons you should use R. Advice on how to learn about the software as a user rather than as a programmer or statistician will follow. I will present some demonstrations of the simplicity and ease of use for basic analysis and visualization of data. This is everything you need to get started with R. See the next talk for more advanced capabilities of R.

title: R for Psychology: A Live Demonstration
abstract: This will be a live demonstration of using R for the analysis and visualization of data. Prepare to be impressed with how a free piece of software with a simple interface can address your need to understand your data. Please attend the prior talk for an introduction to R first.

title: Models of Spatial Hearing Mechanisms in Man
abstract: There are two models of the neural mechanisms serving sound localization in the horizontal plane. One is based on a population of neural channels, each of which is narrowly tuned to its own particular azimuthal range, and is independently responsible for indicating the presence of a stimulus in that range. The second model assumes the existence of only two, hemifield-tuned channels, and it is the relative activity of these two channels that specifies source location. We present our recent psychophysical evidence that distinguishes which of these models is active in man.

authors_raw: Rachel Dingle, Susan Hall, & Dennis Phillips
title: A Frontal Channel in Human Spatial Hearing?
abstract: While the discovery of an organized, topographical map of auditory space in the barn owl popularized the 'multi-channel' model of sound localization, this model fails to generalize to other animals. We now have compelling evidence that mammalian sound localization, at least in the horizontal plane, is based on the relative activity of two populations ('channels') of neurons that are broadly-tuned to contralateral auditory space. However, there is also some evidence from physiological realms that a third, 'frontal' channel exists, made up of neurons that respond to sounds situated at the auditory midline. Here we present a simple psychophysical task designed to probe the existence of this frontal channel.

title: Auditory integration of temporally desynchronised channels in speech perception.
abstract: Although speech perception is highly robust in the face of temporal distortion, strong claims have been made regarding the importance of preserving low-frequency modulations (e.g., 3-8 Hz) for accurate speech perception. For example, local time reversal in the order of 250 ms results in a severe drop in intelligibility [e.g., Saberi and Perrott (1999), Nature, 398, p.760]. This study examines the perception of stimuli that were processed by four 1/3-octave filters, the outputs of which were desynchronized by a series of fixed delays. Intelligibility was found to be dependent on both speech rate and degree of temporal distortion. One possible explanations for this is that, when bands are desynchronized, integration across bands results in perceptually errors. However, perceptual segregation may also occur at very long delays resulting in two separate percepts which may explain the recovery of intelligibility at such delays. Several further experiments were conducted to test this hypothesis. It was found that, when the fundamental frequency was different in different bands, there was a relatively small reduction in intelligibility with increase in across-band asynchrony. However, if the fundamental frequency was flattened to a constant monotone for all bands, intelligilibilty decreased precipitously with increasing across-band asynchrony and there was no recovery for long delays. The results of these experiments suggest that the intelligilibility of sentences distorted in this manner depends critically on listeners' ability to integrate spectral information across channels and that the relationship between temporal distortion and the range of low-frequency modulation rates in speech is spurious.

authors_raw: Ryan Wilson, Janet Roberts, Manohar Bance, & Aaron Newman
title: Auditory Spatial Attention in Cochlear Implant Recipients: An ERP Study
abstract: The success of cochlear implant (CI) surgery to treat hearing impairment is quite variable, particularly in post-lingually deaf adults. The current study investigated how well individuals with CIs can allocate auditory spatial attention in the context of competing stimuli, relative to normally hearing controls. We compared the event-related brain potentials (ERPs) of adult cochlear implant recipients with normally hearing controls during a "swinging story" task. Two stories were presented simultaneously from different speakers located to the left and the right of the participant respectively. Every 30-90s the stories switched location and participants had to maintain attention to one story. Linguistic probes ("ba" and "da") were presented from attended and unattended locations. ERP responses from 7 CI and matched control participants were compared for attended and unattended probes. We predicted that better auditory spatial attention ability would be associated with greater differences in N1 ERP amplitudes between attended and unattended probes.

title: Brain Activity following Cochlear Implant Treatment for Hearing Loss
abstract: Functional outcomes following cochlear implant (CI) treatment for hearing loss are variable in post-lingually deaf adults. Therefore, a greater knowledge about the effect of auditory experience on the processing abilities of the auditory system could have relevant applications in providing individualized treatments for hearing loss. Experiments testing congenitally deaf individuals have shown that deaf participants demonstrate a different pattern of neural activation in response to visual information than normally hearing control participants. The present study used the event-related potential (ERP) technique with a visual refractory period paradigm to see whether a similar effect was observed in CI participants with good and poor functional outcomes. Although no statistically significant group effects were found between CI and control participants, an interesting trend between the two groups of CI participants was observed, which provides additional motivation for recruiting more CI recipients in the future.

title: On the Origin of Specious: Victorian Society and Galton Science
abstract: Sir Francis Galton, the distinguished Victorian scientist, was a polymath to whom much is owed by many disciplines. Galton first distinguished himself as a geographer and tropical explorer; however, with the publication of Darwin's On the Origin of Species in 1859, he became almost exclusively concerned with heredity. Although Galton's work in this area produced firm contributions to science, such as the discovery of correlation and regression and the debunking of Darwin's theory of pangenesis, Galton often produced questionable science in which specious claims and spurious inferences trumped objectivity. These lapses in scientific judgment can be attributed to Galton's near-religious zeal for his theory of eugenics, which was presented as a scientific panacea for the issues faced by Victorian society. However, rather than changing the fundamental class structure of Victorian society, Galton's eugenics sought to improve the quality of individuals in the society, particularly those with what Galton deemed the greatest promise. While protecting and promoting the status and interests of the elite, eugenics also advocated removing supports from the lower classes. Galton's eugenics, and relatedly, his Procrustean treatment of data, make sense only in the context of his own place in Victorian society.

title: Speeded Object Recognition, Categorization, and the Influence of Object Manipulability
abstract: The time to name and recognize objects is influenced by whether or not the object is manipulable (graspable by one hand). In particular, non-manipulable objects are matched faster (in a picture-word matching tasks) than manipulable objects, when object familiarity is controlled for (Filliter, McMullen, & Westwood, 2005). The current study investigated whether or not the effect of manipulability would still be present when the Age of Acquisition (the age at which the vocabulary was acquired) was also controlled for. Participants were asked to categorize objects as manmade or natural. Identification was made more difficult with a backward mask, and short exposure durations (ED) of either 20ms or 100ms. Consistent with previous research, non-manipulable objects were categorized more quickly than manipulable objects (F(1,39)=12.451, p = .001). Objects shown at long ED (100ms) were categorized more quickly. Natural objects were also categorized more quickly than manmade objects. In addition, a significant three way interaction indicated that for natural objects the manipulability effect was only present at short EDs, but for manmade objects the manipulability effect was present at both EDs (F(1,39)=1.055, p = .042).

title: Specialized Visual Processes for Faces of Different Races
abstract: It is well established that people are better at recognizing faces of races they are more familiar with. In two studies we explore the visual processing differences between faces of different races. We employed 2 tasks that have been shown to elicit face-specific processing in the visual system and provide evidence that one, but not the other, is sensitive to experience with faces of different races. This suggests that disproportionate experience with faces of a single race leads to changes in specific face-processing mechanisms within the visual system, and provides evidence that memory for faces of different races is affected by differences in visual encoding.

title: Assessing Response to Novelty as a Predictor of Risk-Taking and Reward Sensitivity in Outbred Mice
abstract: Animal models can help elucidate neurobiological etiologies of human addictive behaviour, as well as individual differences in addiction vulnerability. Locomotor response to a novel environment has been shown to be a reliable predictor of drug self-administration (SA) acquisition in rats, and is used to model a human personality trait associated with risk-taking. Rats showing high levels of activity in a novel environment, labeled high responders (HR), acquire SA to a variety of drugs more readily than rats with relatively lower levels of activity, or low responders (LR). HR rats show many physiological differences from LRs that may explain their propensity for drug seeking, namely high stress reactivity and mesolimbic reward pathway activity. Although mice are more commonly-used model organisms in genetics research than rats, the HR/LR model has not been adopted by mouse researchers. In order to determine whether the HR/LR model is suitable for use with mice, this study tested outbred CD-1 mice for some hallmark characteristics of HR and LR rats. The open field maze and elevated plus maze were used to assess locomotor response to novelty and risk aversion. Reward sensitivity was tested using a two-bottle sucrose preference test under free-feeding conditions and diet restriction stress. Thymus and adrenal glands were examined post-mortem for weight changes after the chronic diet-restriction stressor. Overall, the expected behavioural and physiological differences between HRs and LRs were not observed.

authors_raw: Tracy Monaghan, Megan McLarnon, Jessica Meisner, & Sean Barrett
title: Patterns and Predictors of Anxiolytic Diversion in Currently Prescribed Adults
abstract: Sedative and anxiolytic medications such as Ativan, Valium and Xanax are important for the treatment of anxiety disorders. However, the qualities which render these drugs useful may also contribute to misuse. Prescribed users diverting their medication to those without a prescription is one form of this abuse. The current study investigated the patterns of diversion in currently prescribed adults from Halifax Regional Municipality. Structured interviews and questionnaires detailed substance use and personality traits. Diverters differed from non-diverters in several important ways. Those who diverted were more likely to misuse their own prescription, mix medication with alcohol and receive diverted anxiolytics, stimulants or analgesics. Diverters further broke down to those who were motivated therapeutically or non-therapeutically. Non-therapeutic diverters were more likely to have used a greater number of lifetime substances and have higher sensation seeking scores. Findings could aid physicians with identification and prevention of those at greater risk to divert.

authors_raw: S. Sherry, A. Graham, D. McGrath, E. Breen, A. Allen, J. Tonet, D. Sherry, & S. Stewart
title: The Perfectionism Model of Binge Eating: A Short-Term, 4-Wave Longitudinal Study of Vulnerability, Pathoplastic, and Complication Effects
abstract: Perfectionism is often considered central to binge eating. The Perfectionism Model of Binge Eating (PMOBE) hypotheses perfectionism is a vulnerability for binge eating, but not a complication (or consequence) of binge eating. The PMOBE also hypotheses perfectionism has a pathoplastic effect on binge eating by generating both pre-binge antecedents (e.g., interpersonal problems) and post-binge consequences (e.g., depressive affect) that maintain binge eating. A 4-wave longitudinal study involving a sample of 240 undergraduates was used to test these hypotheses. Participants completed self-report questionnaires once every 7 days for 4 consecutive weeks. Consistent with the PMOBE, vulnerability and pathoplastic effects were observed, but not complication effects. Perfectionism may represent a vulnerability for binge eating that encapsulates several core attributes of, or key concerns for, people who binge eat. Perfectionism may also have a pathoplastic effect on binge eating by generating pre-binge antecedents and post-binge consequences that prolong the course and influence the expression of binge eating.

authors_raw: Jeannette McGlone, Kaitlyn Lee, Jacqueline Gillespie, Bernd Pohlmann-Eden, & Mark Sadler
title: Emotional Intelligence and Epilepsy
abstract: Two studies found that persons with average cognitive intelligence and long standing (> 20 years) temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE) displayed low average range emotional intelligence(EI). To investigate medical and non-medical contributing factors, the Bar-On Emotional Intelligence Questionnaire: Short Form was mailed along with measures of seizure frequency, severity, duration, perceived stigma, and resilience to persons whose epilepsy was either temporal lobe originating or not. The response rate was 37% (n = 27/73). Mean EI fell within the Average range (92.1, sd = 15.5). ANOVA revealed no effect of diagnosis (TLE vs Non TLE) or duration (mean = 11.9m, sd = 12.9). EI correlated significantly with seizure frequency (r = .6), severity (r = .6), perceived stigma (r = -.5) and resilience (r = .7). This sample experienced shorter durations and good outpatient management, i.e., medical factors that might enhance EI. Non-medical factors such as low perceived stigma and high resilience were linked to EI.

title: Measuring the Components of Attention Across the Lifespan using the Attention Network Test (ANT)
abstract: The Attention Network Test (ANT) measures the efficacy of the alerting, orienting, and executive networks. Ten sessions of the ANT (Fan et al., 2002, Callejas et al., 2005, or Rueda et al., 2004) were administered to younger children, younger adults, and older adults. Participants indicated the direction of a target (e.g., arrow), accompanied by congruent or incongruent distractors. The targets were preceded by visual or auditory cues. The results of the younger adults indicate that, despite some practice effects, all network scores remained highly significant even after nine previous sessions. The relatively poor reliability of network scores with one session of data rises to respectable levels as more data is added. The specific pattern of results of older adults and younger children will be discussed in relation to the younger adults' results.

title: Selective Control of Attention to Emotionally-Salient Stimuli
abstract: Emotion regulation is essential for successful functioning in daily life. Along with influencing subjective experience and mental health, the ability to regulate emotions is crucial in interpersonal relations. Two key cognitive emotion control strategies have been identified: reappraisal and attention deployment. The latter of these techniques was the focus of the current study. As a means of affect regulation, attentional control involves consciously monitoring attention to emotion-evoking stimuli. The purpose of the current study was to objectively measure emotional inhibition by having participants complete a computerized action suppression task using three different cue valences. Relevant background material, study predictions, and preliminary data will be presented.

title: Theory of Mind and Anosognosia in Huntington's Disease
abstract: The clinical features of Huntington's disease (HD) include a broad range of motor, cognitive, and psychiatric symptoms; including chorea, cognitive impairment and gradual personality changes. In addition to these symptoms, Huntington's patients often demonstrate a lack of awareness regarding their own symptom severity (i.e., anosognosia). Research on anosognosia in HD has focused exclusively on self-report of motor symptom severity. However, diminished self-awareness may have a negative impact on a patient's ability to interact socially with family members and friends. In fact, behavioural symptoms such as social incompetence and psychiatric disturbances are reported to be more distressing than motor symptoms for patients and family members. The proposed study will examine the cognitive, psychological, and disease correlates of anosognosia in HD, as well as investigate the relationship between this phenomenon and theory of mind.

title: On Forming a Concept of Person
abstract: Human beings may be the only organisms capable of thinking of self and other in equivalent ways - as selves and persons. Most organisms think about their own activities differently than they do the activities of others. In a few large-brained organisms like chimps and dolphins they sometimes think of the activities of self and other in the same way. But, only humans think quite generally in this manner. In this talk I will trace the early development of this uniform concept of self and person and interpret it's meaning with respect to human social life and moral capacities.

authors_raw: Shannon Johnson, Jillian Filliter, Tim Pleskac, Sarah Queller, Susan Bryson, & Isabel Smith
title: Modeling Decision-Making Processes in Autism-Spectrum Disorders
abstract: Mathematical models of complex cognitive tasks offer a powerful method for understanding atypical cognition and behaviour in clinical populations. In the current study, formal cognitive models of decision-making tasks (e.g., Iowa Gambling Task) were employed to examine the psychological and cognitive processes that underlie previously reported atypical decision-making performance in high-functioning children and adolescents with Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD). Overall, task performance and mathematical modeling results converged to reveal a risk-averse decision-making pattern in the ASD group across tasks. These findings demonstrate a link between cognitive processes and core symptoms of ASD, including preference for sameness and aversion to change.

authors_raw: Laura Goodman, Jodie Baker, & Shannon Johnson
title: Relationships Between Executive Functions and Temperament in High-Functioning Youth with an Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract: In typically developing (TD) individuals, there are relationships between executive functioning (EF) and temperament. There is evidence for differences in EF and temperament in ASD, but associations have not been examined. Eleven youths with an ASD and 11 controls completed the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test (WCST) and Stroop Task. A temperament questionnaire was completed by the participant or a parent. We examined relationships between EF and attentional, inhibitory, and activation control. No group differences on EF performance were found. Compared to controls, temperament scores for the ASD group revealed less control of attention (t = -2.32, p < .05) and inhibition (t = -2.55, p < .05). For the TD group, better WCST performance was associated with more behavioural control of activation and attention. For the ASD group, better Stroop performance was related to higher behavioural inhibition scores. Understanding temperament in ASD may inform our understanding of variability in executive functions.

title: Is Item-Method Directed Forgetting the Active Suppression of Rehearsal?
abstract: In an item-method directed forgetting task study words were presented, one at a time, each followed by an instruction to 'Rehearse' (R+), 'Not Rehearse' (R-) or 'Forget' (F). Reaction time (RT) was measured in response to visual (E1) or auditory (E2) detection probes presented 1400 ms, 1800 ms or 2600 ms following each instruction. RTs were longer following R- and F instructions than following R+ instructions; furthermore, participants subsequently exhibited greater yes-no recognition of R+ words than of R- or F words. These results support the notion that item-method directed forgetting is achieved by the active suppression of rehearsal.

title: Inhibition in Item-Method Directed Forgetting: A Modification of the Independent Probe Technique
abstract: The item-method directed forgetting paradigm has been widely used to experimentally assess the characteristics of intentional forgetting. The present study explores the involvement of inhibition after the instantiation of a Forget memory cue in directed forgetting. This was done by modifying the Independent Probe Technique developed by Anderson (Anderson & Spellman, 1995). In 3 experiments, the effects of inhibition were assessed by comparing memory for words that were semantically related to another study category (given the opposite memory cue) to words that were not semantically related to another study category. It was predicted that, if instantiation of a Forget memory cue caused inhibition of the word, this inhibition would spread to related words. Therefore, there would be decreased memory for R items that were related to F items compared to those R items that were not related to F items. Results are discussed.

title: Chromometric Explorations of Mind: Colour as an Index of Information Access and Accuracy
abstract: This talk will summarize published and unpublished work employing a new chromometric approach to the measurement of information access and accuracy. Thus far this approach has been applied fruitfully to the study of visual working memory, attention, and the author will discuss proposals for further application to the study of directed forgetting.

title: Two Types of Manipulability and Their Effects on Picture-Naming and Picture-Word Matching
abstract: Object recognition studies have observed a manipulability effect wherein manipulable objects are slower to be named and matched than non-manipulable objects. These effects have been found with control for familiarity and manipulability. However, age of acquisition (AoA) has also been shown to have an effect on naming, and has not been controlled in previous studies. Previous studies also have not controlled for two aspects of manipulability: functionality and graspability. The current study conducted picture naming and matching tasks controlling for functional and graspable manipulability, familiarity and AoA. A manipulability effect was observed for graspable manipulability in the naming condition where highly manipulable objects were named more slowly. A reverse effect was found in both the naming and matching conditions for functional manipulability, where highly functional objects were named faster than less functional objects. These results support two different effects of manipulability on object processing and have implications for models of object recognition.

title: What's Up at NRC's Neuroimaging Labs
abstract:
This talk will provide an overview of activities and recent developments at NRC's Institute for Biodiagnostics (Atlantic). Basic and clinical research highlights involving human and animal MRI along with emerging EEG and MEG applications will be presented.
5:00	Closing Remarks
5:30	Dinner and Reception--2nd Floor Lounge

title: The Cricket's Tale - Neurohormones and Mimics
abstract: Octopamine and its precursor tyramine are biogenic amines with essential roles in the stress response of insects. Though octopamine's function as a neuroactive chemical has been clearly demonstrated it has only recently been established that tyramine can also act as a neuromodulator in insects. Differentiating the roles of these different amines has been difficult. Identification of octopamine, tyramine and octopamine/tyramine receptors in four different tissues of the Texas field cricket (Gryllus texensis) has been demonstrated using molecular techniques. The presence of three different receptor classes in the neural tissues of crickets is interesting and suggests novel pathways in the stress response of insects. This information will help show what is happening at the cellular level during a host's stress response. Identification of molecules and mechanisms involved in disease susceptibility with a particular interest in the interplay between stress and immunity will be explored.

authors_raw: C. D. Cowper-Smith, G. E. Eskes, & D. A. Westwood
title: What's Your Next Move? Directional Biases for Sequential Limb and Eye Movements
abstract: The purpose of our study was to determine, independent of the movement effector system, if movement results in a directional reaction time bias for subsequent movements. Participants either made two consecutive eye or arm movements in directions indicated by arrows presented at fixation. In experiment 1, only two possible target locations were shown during a trial. Here, movements were faster when offset by 90 or 180 degrees from the first movement. This pattern is consistent with inhibition of return (IOR) typically found for repeated movements made in the same direction. Unlike previous studies requiring repeated movements, in experiment two we presented four possible target locations. Here we found that eye and arm movements were faster only when offset by 90 degrees from the initial movement; the well-established advantage for movements offset by 180 degrees was eliminated. Our results reveal an effect of target set size on the spatial gradient of RTs for consecutive movements.

authors_raw: Jeffrey MacLeod, Susan Bryson, & Raymond Klein
title: Visual Search in Autism: A Consistent Islet of Ability?
abstract: Four published empirical studies have demonstrated that children and adults with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) perform more efficiently than typical controls on difficult visual search tasks. Recently we (MacLeod, Bryson, & Klein, unpublished) attempted to replicate this result while investigating whether greater inhibition of return (IOR) in the aftermath of visual search can explain enhanced visual search efficiency in people with ASD. No differences in visual search efficiency or amount of IOR were detected between the ASD and control groups. Potential reasons for this non-replication of previous results are discussed, and a future study proposed.

title: What is Unique about Learning to Read Chinese Words?
abstract: This study evaluated the contribution of morphemic and submorphemic sensitivity to Chinese word reading and vocabulary with development in children. We tested 453 Cantonese-speaking kindergartners, second graders, and fifth graders (151 per grade) on tasks of morpho-lexical compounding and radical-semantic relatedness, focusing on morphemic and submorphemic sensitivity, along with other standard measures of Chinese character recognition, vocabulary knowledge, phonological awareness and nonverbal IQ. Results of this study showed that morphemic and submorphemic sensitivity both uniquely contributed to Chinese word reading and vocabulary knowledge, even after controlling for other reading-related measures. However, the extent to which each of these constructs contributes to Chinese word reading and vocabulary differs across grades. Findings of this study demonstrate that morphemic sensitivity and submorphemic sensitivity are independent predictors of Chinese word reading and vocabulary, and the developmental shift of the importance of these constructs in learning to read and comprehend Chinese.

title: The Effects of Faces, Emotion, and Context on the Mere Exposure Effect
abstract: The mere exposure effect refers to the finding that the simple repeated exposure to a stimulus results in an enhanced preference for that stimulus. The purpose of this study was to explore the effects of both emotional faces and context on the mere exposure effect. The current study used a between-subjects design, which represented the factorial combination of the emotional face presented during the exposure phase (happy, angry) and the emotional face presented during the rating phase (happy, angry). Although there was no effect of emotional face on the mere exposure effect, there was an effect of context such that a mere exposure effect was present when the context matched (happy-happy, angry-angry), but not when the context mismatched (happy-angry, angry-happy). These findings are discussed in terms of both a cognitive and affective perspective of the mere exposure effect.

title: Shifting in Autism - The Cue is The Key
abstract: Individuals with autism also exhibit difficulties shifting visual attention on endogenous but not on exogenous orienting tasks. Experiment 2 demonstrated that the difficulty with endogenous shifting is a function of delayed response to the cue. Experiment 3 demonstrated that endogenous orienting can be elicited in response to arbitrary cues on the basis of cue-target contingency in typically developing children and adults. Individuals with autism, however, did not show orienting to arbitrary cues.

authors_raw: Kate Thompson, Jeff Hamm, & Tracy Taylor
title: Exploring the Differential Withdrawal of Attention From F and R Items in Directed Forgetting
abstract: Previous research has shown that, in item-method directed forgetting, attention is more readily withdrawn from F items than R items (as evidenced by a greater magnitude of IOR after F compared to R items). The present experiments attempt to test the hypothesis that this F>R IOR difference occurs only when the target localization response is made specifically to the location at which the word was presented. Participants were presented with four peripheral locations at which words and targets could appear. Participants localized the target with either two responses (i.e. general localization: 'right' vs. 'left') or four responses (specific localization: one for each location). The F>R IOR difference emerged only when participants made a specific response, and not when a general localization was made. These results are consistent with a recent characterization of the F>R IOR difference as reflecting a reluctance to respond to the source of unreliable (F) information.

authors_raw: Sean Roach, Jacquelyn Cormier, Andrew Veysey, & Leslie Phillmore
title: Seasonal Variation in Response to Playback of Vocalizations by Black-Capped Chickadees (Poecile atricapillus)
abstract: Songbirds exhibit dramatic seasonal changes, including changes in vocal communication. The black-capped chickadee (Poecile atricapillus), a common songbird with a large repertoire of vocalizations, has a song (fee bee) that is sung primarily by males in the spring, and a chick-a-dee call that is produced year-round but most frequently in the fall and winter. In order to compare responses in breeding (spring) and non-breeding (fall/winter) birds, we captured male black-capped chickadees from the wild during those two seasons. We measured their behavioural (activity and vocalizations) and brain (expression of the immediate early gene ZENK in the auditory areas NCM and CMM) responses to lab playback of conspecific song, conspecific call, heterospecific (song sparrow, Melospiza melodia) song, or silence as a control. Neural responses differed between seasons, most notably with much more response to heterospecific song during the non-breeding (fall/winter) season. Thus, neural perception of conspecific and heterespecific vocalizations varies seasonally.

authors_raw: Beverly Butler, Gail Eskes, Renée Lyons, & Patricia Ebert
title: 'Making the Most of Your Memory': A Pilot Study of a Cognitive Skills Training Group for Community-Dwelling Stroke Survivors
abstract: Problems with attention, memory and executive functions have been shown to significantly interfere with physical recovery, independence, community re-integration, and quality of life after stroke. Rehabilitation through individualized training of compensatory strategies and/or cognitive skills-based training can be effective, but is time-intensive, and access to health care professionals with the necessary specialized training is often limited. Thus, we have undertaken the first randomized, controlled, cross-over trial of a group-based cognitive rehabilitation program for community-dwelling stroke survivors. Participants (n=8) who were enrolled and randomized to either the Early Intervention or Late Intervention group were at least six months post-stroke, were living in the community, and had subjective cognitive complaints. Outcome measures including subjective measures of cognitive functioning, mood, and quality of life, as well as objective tests of cognitive performance were collected at baseline and at three month intervals for nine months. Feasibility and outcome data from this pilot trial will be presented.

authors_raw: Jodie Gawryluk, Erin Mazerolle, & Ryan D'Arcy
title: White Matter fMRI Activation in the Internal Capsule: Co-Localization with DTI Tractography
abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) in white matter is controversial. The current study had two objectives: (1) to determine whether task-related white matter activation could be detected in the internal capsule; and (2) to determine whether diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) tractography would illustrate structural connections between the internal capsule activation and primary motor cortex activation. A motor task was used. Internal capsule activation was examined at the individual level and functionally-guided DTI tractography was performed. Tracts were seeded such that they had to pass through activated regions in both the primary motor cortex and the internal capsule. Analyses revealed that internal capsule activation was present in 90% of participants. Furthermore, 100% of these participants had tracts connecting the internal capsule activation to activation in the primary motor cortex, confirming structural connections between activated white and gray matter regions. This finding has implications for fMRI studies of brain connectivity.

title: The Effects of Attentional Control Sets on Eye Movement Latencies in IOR
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) has typically been conceptualized as a bias against returning attention to previously inspected locations. This bias is typically indexed by slowed responses times to cued locations. In recent years, a debate has centered on which levels of processing are affected by IOR. On one hand, some have argued that IOR is fundamentally an attentional phenomenon. On the other hand, it has been argued that there may also be an oculomotor component to IOR in some instances. Experimental results are mixed on this matter with some researchers presenting evidence that IOR is a strictly attentional phenomenon in some instances and a motoric phenomenon in others (Taylor & Klein, 2000). Other patterns of results have suggested that motoric and attentional components of IOR may operate simultaneously (Abrams & Dobkin, 1994). Consideration will be given to experimental methods to explain the divergent findings.

title: Who Said Pedestrian Means Dull: Observations on Street Crossing Behaviour Stephanie Allen, Richard Brown, Tim O'Leary and the Students of Measuring Behaviour (3161)
abstract: Pedestrians represent the largest group of road users yet an alarming 20- 35% of the population crosses the street illegally at intersections (Keegan and O'Mahony, 2003; King et al., 2009). The high rates of illegal pedestrian activity increase to the potential for pedestrian-car crashes. Most pedestrian-car collisions occur when a pedestrian is crossing the street (Bungum et al., 2005), and as many as 32%-44% of car-pedestrian crashes involve illegal pedestrian behaviour (Austroads, 2000). A series of six observational studies were conducted in Halifax, Nova Scotia to explore pedestrian behaviour at various intersections throughout the city. Study one found that pedestrians crossed the street faster in the presence of a countdown timer than a simple walk symbol. Study two examining the effect of age (young/old) and shoe type (heels/no heels) found that older people and women wearing high heeled shoes crossed the street most slowly. Study three found that jaywalking and improper crosswalk use was more likely to occur on-campus than off. Study four found that individuals who talked/texted on a cell phone, or listened to headsets took longer to cross the street than individuals who where not engaging in such behaviours. Study five found that younger pedestrians and pedestrians in groups committed more crosswalk violations than older pedestrians and pedestrians walking alone. Consistent with study two, older pedestrians crossed more slowly than younger pedestrians, and females crossed more slowly than males. Furthermore, groups containing members of both sexes were slower to cross the street than same-sex groups. Study six examined the effect of high and low traffic and distracters on crossing behaviour. Overall significantly more individuals exhibited safe crossing behaviours than risky crossing behaviours, however at low-traffic time periods a higher percentage of individuals exhibited risky crossing behaviours than safe crossing behaviours. Consistent with study four, pedestrians that were distracted exhibited the most dangerous crossing behaviours. Results indicate that pedestrians that were either younger, in groups, on-campus, or distracted were significantly more likely to engage in risky crossing behaviours.

authors_raw: Jason Satel, Zhiguo Wang, Raymond Klein, & Thomas Trappenberg
title: A Computationally Explicit Neurocognitive Theory of Inhibition of Return (IOR) Based on Short-Term Depression of Exogenous Signals to the Superior Colliculus
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) is an orienting phenomenon characterized by slower behavioral responses to targets that have been spatially cued, relative to uncued locations. There has been extensive discussion in the literature regarding the neural generators and stages of cognitive processing involved in IOR. There is, however, general agreement that at least a subset of IOR-like phenomena are mediated at the level of the superior colliculus (SC). Recent investigations have demonstrated that IOR is accompanied by reduced responses to exogenous inputs in the SC. Here, we present a neuroscientifically based computational model of the SC which implements an early sensory habituation account of IOR. A variety of key findings in the literature are generated by this model, including the biphasic pattern of facilitation at short stimulus onset asynchronies (SOAs) and IOR at long SOAs, vector averaging of IOR, and the co-existence of IOR and endogenous orienting at the same location. Quoting Neuroscience Institute <HYPERLINK "mailto:neurosci@Dal.Ca"neurosci@Dal.Ca>: in their labs.

title: Visual and Non-Visual Learning and Memory Performance in the APPswe/PS1dE9 Mouse Model of Alzheimer's Disease
abstract: The APPswe/PS1dE9 mouse model of Alzheimer's disease develops amyloid beta plaques in the retina and begins to show retinal degeneration by 12 months of age (Perez et al. 2009, Invest Ophthalmol Vis Sci, 50:793-800). As mice are commonly tested for learning and memory deficits using visually dependent tasks, we studied the relationship between visual ability, learning, and memory in transgenic and wildtype control mice at 6-8 (young) and 20-25 (old) months of age. In the visual water box, we found that young mice performed better than old mice on visual detection (F(1,41)=23.44, p<.0001) and pattern discrimination (F(1,41)=31.15, p<.0001), but there was no effect of genotype. Young mice had better visual acuity than old mice (F(1,41) = 9.43, p<.01) and transgenic mice tended to have worse visual acuity than wildtype mice (F(1,41)=3.26, p = .078). In the Morris water maze (MWM), a visually dependent test of learning and memory, old mice took longer than young mice to reach the platform (F(1,41)=44.35, p<.0001) and transgenic mice tended to take longer to reach the platform than wildtype mice (F(1,41)=3.8, p=.0583). Old mice took longer to reach the visual platform (F(1,39)=27.49,p<.0001), had slower swim speed (F(1,40)=43.20,p<.0001), and spent less time in the correct quadrant during the probe trial (F(1,40)=14.64,p=.0004), than young mice. There was no effect of genotype on these measures. Wildtype mice tended to cross the annulus more frequently then transgenic mice during the probe trial (F(1,40)=3.99, p=.0526) and young mice crossed the annulus more often than old mice (F(1,40)=8.67, p <.01). In the conditioned odour preference task, all mice spent more time digging in the CS+ than the CS- odour (F(1,20)=28.84, p<.0005), and there was no effect of age or genotype on amount of liquid consumed. On the conditioned taste aversion test there was no effect of age or genotype. These results demonstrate an effect of age and genotype on visual acuity and on performance (latency) in the MWM, a vision-dependent task. There was, however, no effect of genotype or age on memory in the non-visual tasks. It appears that amyloid plaques in the retina effect visual acuity and memory performance in the MWM, but not in non-visually dependant tasks. This indicates that there is a confound between sensory and cognitive deficits during aging and that non-visual tests should be used to ensure that the learning and memory deficits in aging are due to decrements in cognitive rather than sensory function. We are now examining the eyes and brains of these mice for amyloid plaques. Supported by grants from NSERC of Canada and the Alzheimer Society.

authors_raw: S. Fitzpatrick, S. P. Mackinnon, N. Hartling, S. Allen, B. Orchard, K. Miller, S. H. Stewart, D. L. Sherry, & S. B. Sherry
title: Understanding the Origins and the Consequences of Perfectionistic Demands in Romatic Relationships: A 2-wave, 28-day Longitudinal Study
abstract: Socially prescribed perfectionism is conceptualized as involving a dispositional tendency to perceive perfectionistic demands from others. Theory suggests early experiences with hyper-critical, demanding others lead to enduring mental representations of others as imposing perfectionistic demands upon the self. These mental representations may generalize, often inaccurately, to a romantic relationship. This position is termed the "dispositional hypothesis." Socially prescribed perfectionism may also arise from current, actual, and direct exposure to a hyper-critical, demanding romantic partner. This position is termed the "exposure hypothesis." It is unclear if depressive symptoms among persons high in socially prescribed perfectionism are better understood in terms of the dispositional or the exposure hypothesis. To test these hypotheses, 222 romantic couples were studied with a 2-wave, 28-day longitudinal design. Partial support for the dispositional and the exposure hypothesis was observed, with evidence most clearly implicating the dispositional hypothesis in depressive symptoms. Socially prescribed perfectionism may represent an admixture of dispositionally-based social perceptions and transactions with demanding others. 1:30-3:00 Session 3 Chair: Chris Cowper-Smith

title: Hebb and Cattell: The Genesis of The Concepts of Fluid and Crystallized Intelligence
abstract: Donald Hebb was hired as a Lecturer in Psychology at Queen's University in 1939, and while he was there, he wrote up the work that he had done at the Montreal Neurological Institute with Wilder Penfield. In one of his papers (Hebb, 1942), he was critical of the concept of "Intelligence" and stated that: "Intellectual development involves (A) the development of direct intellectual power, by neural maturation, and (B) the establishment of routine modes of response to common problems, or of perceptual and conceptual modifications leading to qualitative modifications of behavior." (page 289). He called these Intelligence A and Intelligence B. Hebb (1941) presented these ideas at the APA meeting in Evanston, Illinois, in a symposium which included Raymond B. Cattell, a professor at Harvard University. Two weeks after the symposium, Cattell sent Hebb his manuscript, which contained a description of Hebb's ideas on Intelligence A and Intelligence B, but these were now termed "crystallized" and "fluid" intelligence. Hebb and Cattell exchanged many letters about this, as did their heads of department, George Humphrey (Queen's) and Edwin Boring (Harvard), before Cattell's paper was rewritten in such a way that everyone was satisfied. This talk examines the ideas of Hebb and Cattell on intelligence, their correspondence, the development of the ideas of fluid and crystallized intelligence, and how the following sentence came about: "Hebb has independently stated very clearly what constitutes two thirds of the present theory" (Cattell, 1943, page 179).

authors_raw: Sean Roach, John Batt, & Leslie Phillmore
title: Social Context and Electrocommunication in the Weakly Brown Nose Knifefish, Apteronotus Leptorhynchus
abstract: Weakly electric fish use electric signals both for electrolocation and for electrocommunication. The brown nose knifefish generates a quasi-sinusoidal wave-type signal that is sexually dimorphic (600-800 Hz for females and 800-1000 Hz for males). Modulations in this signal (chirps and rises) seem to serve communicative functions that are socially mediated, although their specific roles (e.g., aggression, courtship, etc.) remain poorly understood. The first objective of these experiments is to further elucidate these roles via behavioural testing of free-swimming fish paired in varying arena sizes and with conspecifics of differing familiarity, and of fish exposed to altered playback signals. The second objective is to examine the possible relevance of the gene FoxP2, known to play an important role (often related to social modulation) in other vertebrate communication systems. This will be done by housing fish in different contexts (alone or with same- or opposite-sex conspecifics) and then examing neural FoxP2 immunoreactivity.

title: Top-Down Modulation of Inhibition of Return
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) is an orienting phenomenon characterized by slower responses to spatially cued than to unced targets. In three experiments, we utilized a digit identification technique (Posner & Cohen, 1984) in which a small digit that required identification was presented following a peripheral cue. Around the time of the cue, the digit could appear in a cued peripheral box or in the central box, thus guaranteeing saccadic response to the cue in one condition and no saccade in the other condition. An IOR effect was observed only when a saccadic response to the cue was required. These findings suggest that behaviorally manifested IOR effects are modulated by top-down processes, such as attentional control setting (ACS). Our method for testing this hypothesis will be presented.

title: White Matter Connectivity in Bilaterally Deaf Individuals vs. Hearing Controls
abstract: In the event of sensory deprivation, such as hearing loss, cross modal plasticity may occur as brain areas are reallocated for other functions, such as visual processing. Previous studies have reported functional differences, such as auditory areas responding to visual stimuli, as well as differences in diffusivity along white matter pathways between individuals with sensory impairments and controls (Finney et al., 2001; Shu et al., 2009). Varied white matter connectivity is expected between persons with long standing deafness, and normally hearing participants. The ongoing research project employs Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) to infer the integrity and orientation of white matter tracts noninvasively and in vivo. In deaf individuals compared to controls, we predict reduced connectivity in the ascending auditory pathway and between A1 and auditory association areas. As well we predict stronger connections between auditory association regions and multimodal polysensory regions such as posterior STS and parietal cortex. Preliminary findings regarding these differences will be presented.

authors_raw: Laura Goodman, Heather Phelan, Julie Rouette, Michelle Kerr, & Shannon Johnson
title: Physiological Arousal, Gaze Direction, and Face Recognition
abstract: The relationship between physiological arousal and recognition for faces with direct versus averted eye gaze was examined in a sample of forty-nine typical adults. Participants were tested on face discrimination and recognition tasks and their physiological arousal was measured via skin conductance responsivity (SCR). Counter to our hypothesis, there was no significant difference between SCR for direct gaze faces and averted gaze faces; however, this finding is in keeping with some previous research. As expected, participants correctly recognized more male direct gaze faces compared to male averted gaze faces. As well, male participants showed a significant and unexpected relationship between increased physiological arousal and poorer overall facial recognition. In addition to being the first study to simultaneously test gaze direction, arousal, and recognition, our results suggest complex relationships between arousal and face processing that interact with the sex of the stimulus and viewer.

title: The Influence of Attention on the Probability and Fidelity of Memory
abstract: Typical studies of memory employ discrete stimuli and yield dichotomous measures of performance: Ss either remember or forget each stimulus. An alternative approach employs stimuli that can be represented on a continuum, requiring Ss to identify the position of each stimulus on the continuum when memory is later tested. Where early application of this approach confounded multiple sources of performance error (the probability, fidelity, and bias of memory) more recent advances (Lawrence, in press) provide statistical tools to isolate and quantify these sources for analysis as dependent variables. Applying these tools to the study of how spatial attention affects memory, it is found that while exogenous spatial attention affects only the probability of memory, endogenous spatial attention affects both the probability and fidelity of memory.

title: Front-End Visual Adaptation: Presynaptic Inhibition at Photoreceptor Terminals in an Insect Eye
abstract: In natural scenes at any one time of day, the visual operating range is restricted by the available pattern contrast, less than 100:1. This 100:1 has to be accommodated within a total brightness scale of >10,000,000:1, as bright sunlight graduates down to dusk. The mismatch requires a range-setting mechanism at the visual front end, to continuously re-scale the operating range as the average ambient light intensity falls and rises -- 'light adaptation'. At the first synaptic zone (lamina) in the visual system of flies, the output terminals of photoreceptors R1-R6 receive multiple feedback synapses from a network of lamina intrinsic neurons, amacrine (Am) cells, themselves driven directly by the same photoreceptors: R1-6 Am. Results here reveal a powerful adaptive system that acts omni-directionally across the lamina zone, swiftly suppressing the output from the photoreceptor terminals -- presynaptic inhibition. The amacrines form the only feedback network poised to accomplish the job of rapidly adapting the visual output from the photoreceptors: they are present at the appropriate level, have the required lateral connectivity, are driven directly from the R1-6 terminals and make large numbers of feedback synapses on to them.

title: A Comparison of Sleep and Sleep Lab Adjustment among Children Who Are Typically Developing and Children with ADHD
abstract: Sleep plays an integral role in health, yet approximately 30% of typically developing (TD) children and 50% of children with ADHD are reported by their parents to experience sleep problems. Interestingly, subjective reports (e.g. parent reports) of sleep problems are not always consistent with objective measures of sleep (e.g. polysomnography data gathered in sleep labs). One reason why there may be inconsistent findings between subjective and objective measures may be related to children's responses to the sleep lab environment. This research will examine if children with ADHD, compared to TD children, are differentially affected by the sleep lab environment. This study will first compare how sleep changes from the home environment to the sleep lab environment, and will then determine if children with ADHD are differentially affected by the sleep lab experience. The proposed research project has potential to account for the inconsistent findings in the ADHD-sleep literature.

authors_raw: K. Ryan Wilson, Heather O'Rourke, Linda Wozniak, Ellina Kostopoulos, Yannick Marchand, Ryan D'Arcy, & Aaron Newman
title: The Clinical and Neuropsychological Impact of Intense Aphasia Treatment
abstract: Intensive speech-language therapy can lead to significant improvements in language ability, even in chronic aphasia. One such program is the Intensive Residential Aphasia Communication Therapy program (InteRACT), an intensive 4-week long program with over 100 hours of speech-language language therapy. Our goal was to characterize the effects of intensive therapy on the N400, an ERP effect associated with lexical processing. Eight aphasic patients and 8 matched controls performed a task in which they had to determine whether a spoken word was a 'match' or a 'mismatch' for a presented image. Each aphasic completed the task immediately before and after the InteRACT program; controls performed the task twice with a 4-week interval between sessions. Seven of the 8 aphasics showed clinically significant gains on standardized language testing from the beginning to the end of InteRACT. Post-therapy, the aphasic and control groups showed different changes in the scalp distribution of the N400. Aphasics showed and a more left-lateralized scalp distribution while controls showed a posterior N400 in addition to an anterior positivity.

authors_raw: Cindy Hamon-Hill, Annabel Cohen, & Raymond Klein
title: Both Music and Video Contribute to the Parsing of a Short Motion Picture Animation
abstract: The current study investigated how music modulates the interpretation of animation (Heider & Simmel, 1944) by analyzing the parsing of visual and musical stimuli when processed individually and together. Forty-eight participants (mean age = 21(3.6) years) segmented the animation (92 sec), independent orchestral music (92 sec), and the animation and music combined. Temporal patterns of segmentation for the music and combined conditions were strongly correlated, r(93)= .454, p <.001. However, a significantly stronger correlation was obtained between the segmentation patterns for the animation and combined conditions, r(93)= .846, p <.001. Information from both modalities contributed to the parsing of the film, with greater dominance of the visual information.

title: Stimulus-Response Compatibility in a Simulated "Real-World" Driving Task
abstract: The performance advantage when stimulus (S) and response (R) locations correspond during non-spatial discrimination tasks is called the Simon effect. Laboratory studies demonstrate that this effect occurs across horizontal, vertical and diagonal S-R orientations. Here we investigate whether the Simon Effect will be observed in a more "real-world" situation akin to driving. With their hands placed on the left and right sides of a steering wheel and with response buttons under each thumb participants navigated a micro-ship through a virtual artery. During navigation, hand position changes from horizontal to vertical (clockwise and counterclockwise) while green and blue targets (pathogens) appear unexpectedly requiring a 2-choice discrimination response made by pressing an appropriate button. As effector location (thumb) varies while steering, so too will the relative S-R orientation. If the Simon effect generalizes to this driving task, RT and accuracy will improve as the relative distance between the S and R decreases.

title: The Cognitive Failures Questionnaire: A Fuller Exploration of Dimensions and Rotations
abstract:
The Cognitive Failures Questionnaire (CFQ) is widely used to assess frequency of everyday slips/errors even though its factor structure is unclear. Using data from 2394 female and 921 male undergraduates, the present study compared the principle components structures for 3 through 7 factors, using both orthogonal and non-orthogonal rotations. The best structure was a 4-factor solution with non-orthogonal rotation. For the males, the best was a non-orthogonal 4-factor solution while for the females, the best was a non-orthogonal 3-factor solution. A subset of 484 students completed the CFQ more than once. It demonstrated good test-retest reliability at 1 year.
5:00-6:00 What is MEG and How Can I Use It?
Aaron Newman
Halifax is now home to Canada's most advanced magnetoencephalogrphy (MEG) brain imaging system. MEG is a noninvasive tool that offers the temporal resolution of EEG/ERP and spatial resolution than can rival fMRI. However, compared to those techniques it is faster to set up, less affected by head movements, more comfortable for the subject/patient, silent, and does not involve large magnets. This makes it an ideal tool for use with children and various patient populations. Through a partnership between the IWK Health Centre and the National Research Council Institute for Biodiagnostics, Elekta Neuromag OY has installed a 306 channel MEG imaging system and will be situated their North American R&D Headquarters at the IWK Health Centre. This presents a fantastic opportunity for researchers to do cutting-edge work with first-class support. However, as with any technique MEG is not exactly "plug 'n play" and requires some understanding to get up and running. In this talk I will briefly describe what MEG is and how it works, and then walk the audience through the process of designing a MEG study, moving a project through the approval process at IWK/NRC, implementing the study, and analyzing the data.
Friday, April 30th

authors_raw: Janine Olthuis, Sherry Stewart, Margo Watt, & Brigitte Sabourin
title: Anxiety Sensitivity and Negative Interpretation Biases: Implications for Anxiety Symptoms
abstract: Anxiety sensitivity (AS) is an established cognitive risk factor for anxiety disorders. The present study explored whether individuals high in AS display a similar negative interpretation bias as that identified in those with anxiety disorders. Furthermore, we investigated whether the association between AS and both panic and generalized anxiety symptoms could be accounted for by negative interpretation biases of ambiguous panic-related body sensations, general events, social events, and/or other body symptoms. Eighty-nine female undergraduates (44 low AS; 45 high AS) completed self-report measures of AS, negative interpretation bias, and panic and generalized anxiety symptoms. Findings showed that women high in AS had significantly greater negative interpretation biases. In addition, negative interpretations of ambiguous panic-related body sensations partially mediated the association between AS and panic symptoms while negative interpretations of ambiguous general and social events partially mediated the association between AS and generalized anxiety symptoms. Clinical implications are discussed.

title: The Effects of Maternal Care on Offspring Fitness in the Zebra Finch (Taeniopygia guttata)
abstract: Variations in maternal care can cause adverse effects later in life for humans and rats (Liu et al. 1997). This study examined the role of maternal care on offspring fitness in the zebra finch (Taeniopygia guttata), a species that provides biparental care to their offspring (Zann, 1996). Offspring were raised in cages with either both parents or a single mother. Parental investment was assessed at days 8 and 13 post-hatch; behaviourally, single mothers compensated for the missing father in most measures. Attempts at assessing physiological stress responsivity provided inconclusive data. Discussion of results focuses on improving future data collection to provide a better picture of parenting behaviour and offspring outcome in the zebra finch.

title: Understanding the Relationship Between Morphological Awareness and Vocabulary
abstract: Although a strong association between morphological awareness (MA) and vocabulary has been well-established, we know very little about the direction of their relationship. In this longitudinal study, we investigate the possibility of a bidirectional relationship between MA and vocabulary, with a focus on the distinction between inflectional and derivational morphology. Across grades 1 to 3, 100 children completed measures of vocabulary and of past tense, inflected, and derived MA, along with measures of phonological awareness, word reading, pseudoword reading, and nonverbal reasoning. Hierarchical regression analyses revealed that the direction of the relationship between vocabulary and MA depended on morpheme type. Vocabulary predicted subsequent growth in past tense MA, whereas broader measures of inflected and derived MA predicted subsequent vocabulary growth, with derived MA emerging as a stronger predictor of vocabulary. These findings suggest a dynamic relationship between vocabulary and MA that is sensitive to the morphological distinctions within a language.
