After controlling for all other variables, affective inhibitory control and aspects of display rule knowledge uniquely predicted ER in the disappointing gift paradigm. These findings suggest that ER requires 1) knowledge of contexts requiring ER and 2) inhibitory skills required to execute ER.

title: Introducing an Endogenous Attention Systems Test
abstract: The Attention Network Test (ANT) has become a popular research tool for quantifying several phenomena of attention. However, non‐optimal design of this test limit it's power and interpretability. This talk will present a new attention test, designed to obviate the limitations of the ANT and extend its measurement to further phenomena of attention. Pilot experiments will be presented that validate its composition and applicability to both basic and clinical research.

authors_raw: Moragh Jang, Rachel Dingle, Susan Hall & Dennis Phillips
title: Human Sensitivity to Auditory Temporal Irregularity
abstract: The human auditory system is extraordinarily sensitive to temporal periodicities in sounds, and to differences in the periodicities in sounds. What is far less clear is human sensitivity to deviations from perfect periodicity. This is important, because the failure of the auditory system to encode periodicities (e.g., in listeners with central auditory pathologies) is devastating perceptually, and experimental "jitters" of stimulus time structure in normal listeners is deleterious to speech perception. Detection thresholds for this kind of temporal jitter have not previously been presented. We report an experiment in which we directly measure sensitivity to the temporal irregularity of otherwise perfectly periodic click trains. Our data suggest that thresholds for the detection of temporal irregularity are not those expected from a simple Weber's Law, and that two perceptual operations may be contributing.

title: Sensitivity to Morphological and Orthographic Stress Cues When Reading Multisyllabic English Words
abstract: In English words, certain letter patterns are associated with stress placement (e.g., age; trochaic stress), and research suggests that adults are sensitive to these stress cues. Our study investigates whether this sensitivity differs when a cue is orthographic vs. morphological, and links stress cue sensitivity to reading ability. 104 participants completed a lexical decision task; its items were grouped into sets ending in the same letter pattern. These endings represent either a(n) orthographic cue, consistent with stress (COURage); orthographic cue, inconsistent with stress (corSAGE); or morphological cue, consistent with stress (SHORTage). 40 of the participants also completed measures of word reading and morphological awareness. We expect fewer errors when an orthographic cue is consistent vs. inconsistent, fewer errors for morphological vs. orthographic cues, and that the magnitude of the latter effect will be related to morphological awareness. Moreover, we expect better readers to be more sensitive to all stress cues.

authors_raw: Dennis Phillips, Chelsea Quinlan & Rachel Dingle
title: Visual and Auditory Factors in Sound Localization Acuity
abstract: Historically, sound localization acuity was thought to be correlated with head size. Heffner and her colleagues (1992‐2007) have dispelled this notion by showing that sound localization acuity is highly (and inversely) correlated with the species' width of field of best vision, and that when this factor is partialed out, the relation of acuity to head size vanishes. Her hypothesis is that one function of the auditory system is to provide source location data to the visual system so that the latter can bring the field of best vision into alignment with the auditory target. Thus, if one has a narrow width of field of best vision, then one must be a highly accurate sound localizer to achieve that end. But through what mechanism would this evolutionary pressure exert its effect on sound localization acuity? This talk provides one answer to that question.

title: Exploring Attention in ItemMethod Directed Forgetting
abstract: In the item‐method directed forgetting paradigm, participants are presented with study items one at a time, which are each followed by an instruction to either remember or forget. Typically, a directed forgetting effect is seen, where subsequent memory is better for R items compared to F items. Interestingly, research suggests that the allocation of attention differs after R and F memory instructions, as well. For example, Taylor (2005) found a greater magnitude of IOR (longer reaction times to targets presented in the same, rather than a different location than the word) after F instructions compared to R instructions. The present experiments used similar attentional paradigms to continue investigating the attentional effects of directed forgetting in order to determine when they occur, what their effects are, and attempt to understand what their purpose might be.

title: The Relationship Between Inflammation and Neurotrophin Function in Depression
abstract: Monoamine deficits, hyper‐HPA axis, inflammation and neurotrophin dysfunction may contribute to the etiology of depression. Most proinflammatory cytokines can activate the HPA axis and reduce monoamine syntheses. However, the relationship between inflammation and neurotrophic system, and between the HPA axis and neurotrophic system is unknown. In a depression model, olfactory bulbectomized rats, the microglia were activated, which was correlated with increased concentrations of proinflammatory cytokines interleukin (IL)‐1, IL‐6, tumor necrosis factor‐a, and interferon‐g in the hippocampus, and elevated CRF mRNA expressions in the hypothalamus. However, the expressions of brain‐derived neurotrophic factor and nerve growth factor were down‐regulated. TrK receptors were also down‐regulated, whereas receptor p75 was up‐regulated in the the model when compared to controls. An omega‐3 fatty acid reduced inflammation and the HPA activity, normalized neurotrophic system and improved animal behavior. These results suggest that inflammation may contribute to the dysfunction of the HPA axis and neurotrophic system in depression.

title: Is tobacco addiction nicotine addiction?
abstract: Both the positive (e.g. incentive motivation) and negative (e.g. withdrawal relief) reinforcing properties of tobacco smoking are often attributed to a single factor: nicotine. However, there is surprisingly little unequivocal evidence that nicotine per se has either negative or positive reinforcing properties in the absence of other pharmacological (e.g. psychoactive effects of non‐ nicotine tobacco ingredients) and non‐pharmacological factors (e.g. expectancy effects; sensory‐ motor aspects of tobacco use). In a recent series of studies, my research group has found that many of the negative reinforcing properties of smoking appear to be attributable to non‐nicotine tobacco constituents and/or the sensory‐motor aspects of smoking, while many of tobacco's positive reinforcing properties appear to be attributable, at least in part, to psychological factors such as expectancy. In this presentation the roles of nicotine and various non‐nicotine smoking factors for tobacco addiction will be discussed.

title: Spatial and Temporal Dynamics of Visual Attention
abstract: Snyder (1972), using visual search, and McLean et al. (1973), using rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP), examined illusory conjunctions of features (Treisman & Gelade, 1980) in the domains of space and time, respectively. Seeking to develop a methodology for exploring whether "slippage" (failure to bind features) in space and time are related, the purpose of our pilot study was to replicate the methods and findings of these two classic studies. Participants searched for a coloured letter presented in a circular array all at once (visual search) or in a rapidly presented stream of single letters presented at fixation (RSVP). After each trial in each paradigm, they performed two tasks: identifying and localizing (in either space or time, depending on the paradigm) the target letter. Replicating previous findings, errors came predominantly from items adjacent to targets. Details of error patterns will be described and implications of future studies will be discussed.

authors_raw: Meredith Bessey, Aimee Coulombe & Penny Corkum
title: Validating a Measure of Parental Sleep Beliefs in the SchoolAged Population
abstract: Sleep problems are commonly reported in typically developing children, and are even more prevalent in clinical populations, such as children with ADHD and autism. Often times sleep problems in children go untreated, and parental beliefs about sleep may be a contributing factor. Developing a measure of these beliefs is an important first step in adapting and modifying these beliefs in order to improve treatment utilization and adherence. A newly developed sleep beliefs questionnaire was administered to parents of typically developing children (n=179) and of children with ADHD and autism (n=176). The goal of the current study was to begin to test the validity and reliability of the measure. A four factor structure emerged accounting for approximately 50% of the variance. The results indicate promise for this measure to be used in both typically developing and special needs populations, as a way to explore parents' beliefs about sleep and sleep problems in their children.

authors_raw: Katie Goodine, Nancy Butcher & Ian Meinertzhagen
title: Development of Intrinsic Neurons in the Mushroom Bodies of Drosophila melanogaster
abstract: In insects, the mushroom bodies are paired, higher‐order integrative brain structures implicated in olfactory learning and memory, courtship behaviors, spatial learning, and context generalization in visual learning. Extensive research on the anatomy and function of the mushroom bodies in the ant and honeybee has been conducted, and there is now growing interest in the mushroom bodies of the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster because of its relatively simple organization and genetic manipulability. We investigated the use of genetic tools to label cells of the main input region of the mushroom bodies, the calyx, and to identify the number of mushroom body intrinsic neurons, called Kenyon cells, at three different developmental stages; the first prepupal stage (P0%), the eighth phanerocephalic pupal stage (P50%), and the freshly emerged adult stage (P100%). The implications of our findings will be discussed.

title: The Effects of Alcohol on Smoking Cue Reactivity
abstract: Alcohol appears to alter smoking behaviour through physiological and psychological means. To tease these factors apart, experimental manipulation is required to understand the motivational and physiological roles alcohol has on tobacco use. 30 male and 30 female non‐dependent smokers who are moderate drinkers will be assigned to an alcohol, placebo group, or a control group. They will watch four 2‐minute videos, two during the ascending limb of alcohol absorption, and another two during the descending limb. Half the participants will watch smoking cues during the ascending limb and the other half during the second video of the descending limb. After each video presentation, participants will rate their desire to smoke on both the positively reinforcing and the negatively reinforcing dimensions of tobacco on questionnaires. This research will help determine if differences exist between psychological and physiological aspects of alcohol on smoking cues.

title: Autism as a Cognitive Style: Visual Orienting and the Broader Autism Phenotype
abstract: Autism is characterized by severe impairments in social communication and behaviour. In my model of autism, later emerging impairments are a function of early emerging differences in attention, how and where infants direct their attention. There is compelling evidence that autism is caused by genetic factors; not only is there a higher incidence of autism within families, but there are also numerous autistic‐like traits exhibited by non‐impaired family members, referred to as the Broader Autism Phenotype (BAP). I will present recent data examining the relationship of the BAP to visual orienting.

title: Testing the Embodied Account of Object Recognition
abstract: Mounting evidence demonstrates that passively viewing images of tools both potentiates motor responses and activates frontoparietal networks responsible for programming action. To account for these findings, embodied theories of cognition propose that the same sensory‐motor neural networks are involved in encoding and retrieving object knowledge. We investigated whether motor programs play a necessary role in the retrieval of object knowledge. Participants performed an object naming task while squeezing a sponge with either their right or left hand. The objects were either man‐made (e.g. hammer) or natural (e.g. horse). We hypothesized that, if activation of motor programs are necessary to retrieve object knowledge, this arbitrary motor act should interfere with object naming, especially for categories that are associated with motor acts. Across two experiments we show that sensory‐motor simulations are involved in the retrieval of both man‐made and natural objects, suggesting that both are embodied.

authors_raw: Zhiguo Wang, Jason Satel & Raymond Klein
title: Sensory and Motor Mechanisms of Oculomotor Inhibition of Return
abstract: We propose two mechanisms underlying inhibition of return (IOR) in the oculomotor system: sensory and motor. Sensory mechanism: Repeated visual stimulation results in a reduction of visual input to the superior colliculus (SC); consequently, saccades to targets which appear at previously stimulated retinotopic locations will have longer latencies than those which appear at unstimulated locations. Motor mechanism: The execution of a saccade results in asymmetric activation in the SC; as a result, saccades which reverse vectors will have longer latencies than those which repeat vectors. In the IOR literature, these two mechanisms correspond to IOR effects observed following covert exogenous orienting and overt orienting in the absence of peripheral stimulation, respectively. We predict that these two independent mechanisms will have additive effects; a prediction that is confirmed in a behavioral experiment. We then discuss how our theory and findings relate to the IOR literature.

title: Sleep Architecture in Children with ADHD and Their Typically Developing Peers
abstract: Over the last 30 years, there have been highly inconsistent findings across studies which used polysomnography (PSG) to compare sleep architecture profiles of children with ADHD to their typically developing (TD) peers (e.g., Sadeh, 2007). These inconsistent findings could be related to methodological issues (e.g., participant medication status, co‐morbidities). Therefore, the current study used PSG technology to analyze sleep architecture variables of a medication‐naïve, rigorously diagnosed sample of children aged 6 - 12 years with ADHD (n=15), compared to a sex‐ and age‐ matched sample of their TD peers (n=15). Multivariate analysis (MANOVA) revealed significant differences between ADHD and TD groups in terms of sleep architecture. Specifically, children in the ADHD group had a significantly longer latency to REM sleep, significantly fewer REM cycles, and less Stage 1 sleep than TD peers. Also, children in the ADHD sample had significantly longer sleep onset latencies, but significantly shorter sleep duration than TD peers.

authors_raw: Simon Gadbois, Kate Thompson, Brittany Hilton, John Christie & Vincent LoLordo
title: Odour Matching in Dogs: Applications and Challenges
abstract: Odour matching is used with forensic canines (dogs used as «expert witnesses») and wildlife conservation dogs (to match individuals from scats). Unfortunately, we believe that the most common technique (even standardized in Europe), the scent line-up, is fundamentally flawed as it makes a perceptually difficult task even more challenging by adding an unnecessary element of memory. In the last year we have been investigating a number of other approaches including «same/different» responses to pairs of odours, and short delayed-matching-to-sample procedures. A comparison of all three approaches and the discussion of a true perceptual challenge (odour detection in matrices) will be presented.

title: The Role of Faces in ItemMethod Directed Forgetting
abstract: This study examined whether or not the specialized system used to process, encode, and recognize faces contributes to memory processes, such as the ability to remember and intentionally forget. Each experiment in this study consisted of an item‐method directed forgetting paradigm. In the study phase, facial expressions were presented one at a time, each followed by either an instruction to Remember or an instruction to Forget. Following the presentation of all items in the study phase, memory performance was measured using a yes/no recognition test. Although a directed forgetting effect (greater memory performance for Remember‐cued items than Forget‐cued items) was found for Neutral facial expressions across all of the experiments, this was not the case for emotional facial expressions (Angry and Happy facial expressions). Critically, it was found that there was a link between timing and the directed forgetting effect for emotional facial expressions such that when the stimulus presentation duration was decreased (i.e., really short), there was no effect of emotional valence on directed forgetting; however, when the inter‐trial interval was increased (i.e., really long), there was an effect of emotional valence (both negative and positive valence) on directed forgetting.

authors_raw: Ben Corrigan, Jonathan Fawcett & Tracy Helmick
title: Wait a Minute, Forget That: Using Natural Speech Cues to Induce Directed Forgetting
abstract: To examine directed forgetting due to natural speech cues, participants were asked to rate audio presentations for their quality. The audio clips included mistakes (Errors), which were acknowledged (F instruction) and then corrected (Correction), as well as other items (Reference). During a surprise two‐alternative forced choice recognition task, participant's accurate recognition for Correction and Reference items was significantly higher than for Error (F) items. It is hypothesized that because participants were not intentionally attempting to memorize the material, the forget instruction initiated a cognitive process that withdrew attention from the Error items.

authors_raw: MarieEve Couture, Sherry Stewart, E. Kuntsche, M. Cooper & Roisin O'Connor
title: Drinking Motives, Alcohol Use, and AlcoholRelated Problems: A CrossNational Comparison and Validation of the Drinking Motives QuestionnaireRevised Among College Students
abstract: The aims of this study were to explore cross‐national similarities and differences in the structure of the Drinking Motives Questionnaire‐Revised (DMQ‐R), in the mean endorsement levels of social, enhancement, coping, and conformity drinking motives, and in the links between drinking motives and both heavy drinking and alcohol‐related problems among college students. Confirmatory factor analysis, analysis of variance, and structural equation modeling were performed on data from Canada, the United States, Hungary, Spain, the United Kingdom, Israel, Ireland, and Brazil. Results indicate that the structure of the DMQ‐R did not consistently yield adequate fit to the data across countries; however, the four‐factor structure of the measure was superior to an alternate three‐ factor structure across countries. Further similar drinking motives predicted heavy drinking and alcohol‐related problems across countries. Similarities were also found in the rank order of drinking motive endorsement across countries but some cross‐national differences emerged in the mean levels of endorsement of particular motives.

title: Aladdin's Cave: New Lamps for Old in Illuminating the Tiny World of the Fly's Eye
abstract: The question of the origin of the several electrical responses recorded extracellularly from the eyes of flies has gained prominence with the adoption of this cellular model for molecular genetic studies of neural transmission, in Drosophila. In particular, the origins and dynamics of the electroretinogram's ON‐ and OFF‐responses are not understood and have been widely misinterpreted. An accidental discovery here has suggested that the usual method of free‐field illumination is of limited utility in exposing the properties of the ON and OFF signals. Introducing a new method of illumination to limit these deficiencies has allowed separation of the ON‐OFF system from the more elemental responses of the photoreceptors and monopolar neurons of the first synaptic zone, exposing these clearly for the first time in the electroretinogram. Currents from different monopolar neuron do not interact and as expected, sum independently and linearly. In contrast, the ON‐OFF system collects light from many facets across the eye in an apparently cooperative, non‐linear summative fashion.

authors_raw: Amanda Green, Kal Mungovan, Meaghan MacLean & Tara Perrot
title: The Effects of Prenatal Psychological Stress and Postnatal Environmental Enrichment on the Development of Anxiety and Depressive Behaviours and Neural Changes in the HPA Axis
abstract: We investigated the impact of a novel prenatal psychological stress (PPS) on sex‐specific ontogeny of behaviours related to social interaction, anxiety and depression, and the role of postnatal environmental enrichment (EE) to prevent/rescue these changes. Activity was decreased in juvenile EE offspring compared to standard cage. Juvenile females showed greater anxiety‐like behaviour than males and EE removed this difference. Juveniles exposed to EE also showed less depressive‐like and antisocial behaviour, although antisocial behaviour increased in males with exposure to both PPS and EE. In adulthood, PPS females displayed more depressive‐like behaviour compared to all other groups, and this correlated with their juvenile levels of depressive‐like behaviour. Interestingly, there were sex‐specific effects of PPS and EE on CRH‐ir cells in the PVN of the hypothalamus, with both PPS and EE affecting males to a greater extent. The implications of these findings will be discussed.

title: Reading for Meaning: Investigating Children's Sensitivity to Morphological Structure During Contextual Reading
abstract: Children utilize multiple avenues of information in order to accurately read and understand written words. One such avenue is presumed to be the morphological structure of complex words (e.g., bold + ly). In particular, the frequency of the base morpheme (e.g., bold in boldly) appears to have a privileged role in children's reading. Complex words with high frequency bases (e.g., questionable) are read more accurately and understood to a greater extent than words with low frequency bases (e.g., conceivable). The current investigation stems from the fact that children appear to be sensitive to the morphological structure of words presented in isolation. Yet, it remains unclear to what extent the effects of base frequency are influenced by contextual reading. Thus, the purpose of this experiment is to explore the relationship between base frequency (high vs. low) and context (isolation vs. sentence) by examining their combined effect on word reading and comprehension.

authors_raw: Therese Chevalier, Aaron Newman, Vanessa Lim, Heather Maessen & Manohar Bance
title: White Matter Connectivity in Bilaterally Deaf Individuals vs. Hearing Controls
abstract: In the event of sensory deprivation, such as hearing loss, cross modal plasticity may occur as brain areas are reallocated for other functions. Previous studies have reported functional differences, such as auditory areas responding to visual stimuli, as well as differences in diffusivity along white matter pathways between deaf and control participants. We used Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) to infer the integrity and orientation of white matter tracts noninvasively and in vivo. Tractography was conducted from regions of interest, which included the primary auditory cortex, planum temporale, and V5 of the visual cortex in both hemispheres. Significant differences in connectivity were found between V5 and the planum temporale in the left hemisphere in deaf patients as compared to controls. Similar differences were noted at trend levels in the right hemisphere. These findings support the hypothesis that structural changes occur to facilitate cross modal cortical reorganization in response to sensory deprivation.

title: Motivation and Data Quality Across the Term: How to Get Cleaner Data in Just Five Minutes
abstract: We present a questionnaire currently in development to measure attitudes towards research following completion of a variety of experimental tasks. The questionnaire itself is available in both computerized and paper form and requires between two and five minutes to complete. It is our prediction that our questionnaire could be developed to allow researchers to predict the quality of the data a subject will return, thus allowing researchers to account for that in their models. Analyses of the pilot project we ran this year will be presented. We are actively seeking research laboratories in our department willing to help recruit subjects for this project.

authors_raw: Daniel Ikpi, Richard Brown & Sunday Bisong
title: The Effects of Eating Dennettia tripetala fruit on locomotor and anxietyrelated behaviours in CD1 mice
abstract: Dennettia tripetala fruit induces a mild stimulating effect when ingested; it is used to spice foods in Southern Nigeria. It is believed to reduce social anxiety and increase sociability. The present study investigated the stimulatory effects of D. tripetala on locomotor and anxiety‐like behaviours in CD1 mice in the open field, elevated plus maze and light‐dark transition box. Thirty singly housed male CD1 mice were divided into 3 groups of 10. The Control group were fed normal rodent pellets. The Low‐dose group were fed a 10% D. tripetala diet (10g of D. tripetala mixed with 90g of rodent pellets). The High‐dose group were fed 15% D. tripetala diet (15g of D. tripetala mixed with 85g of rodent pellets) daily for 4 weeks before testing. Both doses of D. tripetala reduced the number of line crosses and the frequency of centre square entries in the open field while the high dose reduced the number of line crosses in the light‐dark box and the frequency of rearing in the open field. The low dose of D. tripetala increased the time in the open arms in the elevated plus maze, while the high dose decreased the number of stretch attend postures in the light‐dark box. The results indicate that chronic ingestion of D. tripetalareduced locomotor and exploratory behaviour in CD1 mice at both doses while the low dose may have an anxiolytic effect. Future studies will examine the effect of D. tripetalaon motor learning, motor control and sociability.

authors_raw: Mark Jaime, Julie Longard, & Chris Moore
title: An assessment of the visual-proprioceptive integration threshold in children with and without autism spectrum disorder
abstract: We investigated a group of 5- to 8-year-old children and a group of 8- to 11-year-old children with high functioning autism on their awareness of self-generated bodily movements by assessing their ability to integrate information provided through the visual and proprioceptive sensory modalities. Children were presented with a task in which they had to make judgments on whether their self-generated hand movements appeared live or delayed on a video monitor in the following 4 different delay conditions: a (1) 0 ms, (2) 100 ms, (3) 200 ms, and (4) 300 ms delay. We were interested in the threshold of time at which children detected a temporal asynchrony between visual and proprioceptive information, from here on called the visual-proprioceptive integration threshold (VPIT). In addition, we assessed their accuracy of delay judgment for each condition. Preliminary results suggest that, in typically developing children, the VPIT decreases and the accuracy of delay judgment increase as a function of age. Further, the VPIT for the older children with ASD appears to resemble that of the 7- to 8-year-old typically developing age group. These results suggest that as children develop, and are exposed to greater amounts of integrated first and third person information, they become more sensitive at detecting visual-proprioceptive temporal asynchronies of self-generated bodily movements. However, children with ASD may have a different developmental pathway in regards to visual-proprioceptive integration.

authors_raw: Matthew Hilchey, Jason Satel, Z. Wang, R. Story, & Raymond M. Klein
title: When spatiotopic and retinotopic coordinates are dissociated inhibition of return is primarily spatiotopic while P1 reductions are primarily retinotopic
abstract: Although the visual coordinate system is initially retinotopic, it has long been known that the representation of IOR can be observed in spatiotopic coordinates when an eye movement is made between the cue and a target calling for a manual response (Posner & Cohen, 1984; Maylor & Hockey, 1985). Neuroimaging studies using event-related potentials (ERPs) have generally revealed that early sensory components (P1) are reduced by IOR (for a review, see Prime & Ward, 2006). All such studies, to date, have confounded retinotopic and spatiotopic reference frames. In Experiment 1 we investigated whether the target-elicited P1 component would be modulated by IOR while we unconfounded these reference frames by interposing an eye movement between cue and a target calling for a manual response. With seemingly conflicting results, recent behavioral studies have investigated the frame of reference for IOR using saccadic responses to targets (Mathot & Theeuwes, 2010; Pertzov, Zohary & Avidan, 2010). Experiment 2 seeks to determine whether oculomotor IOR is spatiotopic (Pertzov et al.) or retinotopic (Matthot & Theeuwes) immediately following an intervening eye movement. In Experiment 1, P1 reductions at the cued location were greater in retinotopic, than spatiotopic, coordinates even though IOR showed the reverse pattern. Similar behavioral results (stronger spatiotopic IOR) were obtained in Experiment 2 when probed with eye movement responses. These results show that immediately after a saccade IOR is coded in spatiotopic coordinates and that the magnitude of P1 reductions does not provide an accurate reflection of IOR's behavioural manifestation.

authors_raw: Mahmoud Hashish, Matthew Hilchey, Jason Satel, Jason Ivanoff, Raymond M. Klein
title: Speed-accuracy trade-offs in inhibition of return during active oculomotor inhibition
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) refers to the phenomenon of increased response times (RTs) to targets at previously cued locations when the interval between a to-be-ignored spatial cue and a target exceeds ~ 300 ms. Recent work has revealed that this phenomenon can be expressed differently depending on the extent to which the oculomotor system is active during a task, resulting in either a perceptual/attentional "flavour" or a motoric "flavour" of the IOR effect. There are several studies with results consistent with the motoric "flavour" of IOR (a speed-accuracy trade-off) in which the eye movement system was not explicitly engaged (but may not have been actively inhibited either). The purpose of this investigation was to repeat one of these experiments (Ivanoff & Klein, 2001) while ensuring that the eye movement system was actively inhibited. In this project, eye movement was monitored through the usage of eye tracking equipment and software. If the motoric patterns reported by Ivanoff and Klein (2001) were generated because they did not monitor eye movements and their participants were not actively inhibiting the oculomotor system, then when we did encourage such active inhibition, a perceptual/attentional flavour of IOR should be obtained (i.e., the speed-accuracy trade-off will be eliminated). This is precisely what we found.

authors_raw: Ralph J. Redden & Raymond M. Klein
title: Calling close plays at first base in baseball
abstract: This project investigated the law of prior entry in a practical real-world setting. We examined close plays at first base in baseball, a scenario in which accurate performance demands that the umpire monitor two events simultaneously - both the ball's arrival at the glove and the runner's arrival at the base. However, umpires are trained to attend the ball when making calls at first base, risking potential bias. To explore this scenario empirically, we placed participants in the role of an umpire while watching video clips of close plays at first base taken from MLB games during the 2007 and 2008 seasons. Further, different groups of participants were provided different focus instructions: some were instructed to attend the ball/glove, some were instructed to attend the runner/base and others were not provided any focus instructions.

title: An investigation of the possible reciprocal relations between alcohol consumption and impulsivity in university students: A four-wave longitudinal study
abstract: This study seeks to investigate the possibility of reciprocal relations between alcohol consumption and impulsivity in university students using a four-wave longitudinal study design. Participants are 150 male and female university students who completed a number of online questionnaires, which included the Substance Use Risk Profile Scale (SURPS) to assess for impulsivity and a Lifestyle Questionnaire to assess for alcohol consumption. Cross-lagged analyses are being used to determine the relationship between alcohol consumption and impulsivity levels, which is appropriate since this is a statistical technique used for non-experimental longitudinal research designs. The results from this research will potentially be beneficial in the treatment of alcohol use disorders to determine if impulsivity is a personality dimension that should be targeted in treatment.

authors_raw: Jason Satel, Z. Wang, & Raymond M. Klein
title: Sensory and motor mechanisms of oculomotor inhibition of return
abstract: We propose and simulate, with a dynamic neural field model, two mechanisms underlying inhibition of return (IOR) in the oculomotor system: sensory and motor. Reflecting sensory adaptation (or habituation), repeated visual stimulation results in a reduction of visual input to the superior colliculus (SC); consequently, saccades to targets which appear at previously stimulated locations will have longer latencies than those which appear at unstimulated locations. In contrast, the execution of a saccade results in asymmetric activation in the SC; consequently, saccades which reverse vectors will have longer latencies than those which repeat vectors. In the IOR literature, these two mechanisms correspond to IOR effects observed following covert exogenous orienting and overt orienting in the absence of peripheral stimulation, respectively. We predict that these two independent mechanisms will have additive effects; a prediction that is confirmed in a behavioral experiment.

title: Unloading: The effect of task externalization on memory load and subsequent working memory performance.
abstract: For most of us, work and life are fraught with seemingly endless upcoming tasks. According to David Allen, a popular productivity guru, incomplete tasks, whether we are consciously reflecting on them or not, overload our memory. These thoughts consume cognitive resources and negatively impact our performance in everyday life. He proposed that in order to maximize our productivity, we should externalize, or "unload," these tasks that weigh on our minds by writing them down. The present study tests this idea by examining whether writing down our planned tasks (unloading) improves performance on working memory exercises. 36 undergraduate students participated in this study, with 12 participants in each of the 3 conditions: full unload, partial unload, and no unload. All participants completed baseline working memory measures, and then were given a few minutes to think about all of their personal tasks that must be completed in the next two weeks. Then, participants in the full unload condition were instructed to make a list of their upcoming tasks with the understanding that they could keep their list for future reference. Participants in the partial unload condition also wrote out their tasks, but were told that they would not be able to keep the list. Finally, participants in the no unload condition were not asked to do anything further and thus did not have an opportunity to "unload." All participants then completed a second set of working memory tasks. The results showed that participants who wrote things down showed improved working memory, regardless of whether they were allowed to keep the list. Participants who did not write out their upcoming tasks did not show changes in working memory scores. This supports our hypothesis that writing things down positively influences working memory. The finding that no difference was found between the full unload and partial unload conditions suggests that it is the act of removing thoughts that weigh on one's mind that affects performance, not the creation of a reliable memory aide. While discussion focuses on outstanding issues to be addressed, the present findings suggest that individuals could achieve significant cognitive benefits by engaging in a very simple and convenient practice: writing down their upcoming tasks.

authors_raw: Kim Good, Magda Wojtowicz, D. Baladi, Denise Lewis, L. Burns, H. Robertson, John Fisk
title: Changes in Attention Network function in early Parkinson's Disease
abstract:
Attention difficulties have been noted in Parkinson's disease (PD), although the exact nature and extent of these deficits, over and above slowed response speed, remain unclear. The Attention Network Test (ANT) is a relatively brief computerized task that measures the functioning of three networks: alerting, orienting and executive control. Abnormal performance in all three attention networks has been previously reported in PD patients. However, because of its design, the ANT does not allow examination of the interactions among attention networks. Moreover, the exogenous and endogenous components of orienting are confounded in this task. In the current study, we investigated the attention network performance in PD patients using a modified version of the ANT, the Attention Network Test of Interactions (ANT-I), which remedies these methodological limitations.Twelve (12) individuals with mild PD (Hoehn and Yahr stages 1-2) and 12 healthy age and education comparable controls completed the ANT-I in addition to other Neuropsychological tests of verbal memory and executive functions.
PD participants' overall response speed on the ANT-I was not significantly slower compared to controls (t(22)=1.75, p=ns) although they did demonstrate greater within-subject variability in response speed (t(13.3)=2.45, p<0.04) and more errors (t(12.1)=2.49, p<0.04). A mixed model ANOVA revealed significant main effects for the orienting (F(2,44)=19.2, p<0.0001) and executive networks (F(1,22)=138.5, p<0.0001), but not for the alterting network. In addition, a significant group by orienting by executive network interaction (F(2,44)=4.7, p<0.02) was found with the PD participants demonstrating smaller flanker compatibility effect in the presence of spatial cues.
This is the first study to identify attention network dysfunction in individuals with early stage PD using the ANT-I. PD participants did not demonstrate significantly slower overall response speed but did demonstrate greater response speed variability and more errors. In addition, PD participants demonstrated a smaller flanker compatibility effect in the presence of uninformative spatial cues with relatively slower response on congruent flanker trials under these conditions. These findings illustrate the important of investigating interactions between attention networks when attempting to understand the behavioral consequences of neurodegenerative disorders.

authors_raw: Jodie R. Gawryluk, Eva Gunde, Erin Mazerolle, Steven D. Beyea, & Ryan C. N. D'Arcy
title: Exploring the value of white matter functional magnetic resonance imaging
abstract: Structural MRI is not sensitive to the full scope of white matter (WM) damage in multiple sclerosis (MS), which may explain why lesion volume is poorly correlated with clinical symptoms. To accurately access the total burden of MS, it is necessary to assess functional changes in the WM in addition to structural measures. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is the dominant approach for examining brain function. To date, however, fMRI has only directly been able to measure neural activation in gray matter, and WM function/dysfunction was thought to be inaccessible. Our research group developed techniques that allow for direct measurement of WM activation using fMRI. As an important next step, we determined whether WM activation can be linked to tests measuring WM function. We used an adapted Symbol Digit Modalities Test in a 4T fMRI study. Results from 17 healthy individuals revealed WM activation in the corpus callosum. The findings link our new techniques in fMRI to an established clinical test of WM function.

authors_raw: Caroline Reiss, Jason Satel, Matthew Hilchey, & Raymond M. Klein
title: Investigating inhibition of return-related modulations of early sensory event-related components with central and peripheral cuing
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) is a behavioral phenomenon indexed by increased response times to targets presented at previously cued locations, so long as the interval between the cue and the target exceeds ~300 ms. IOR can be generated by uninformative peripheral onsets (exogenous) or central arrow (endogenous) cues. This experiment replicates previous behavioural work which has demonstrated IOR in these two cue conditions (Taylor & Klein, 2000), with the advantage of also saving and analyzing electroencephalographic (EEG) recordings. We investigate a paradigm with saccadic responses to cues (mixed central and peripheral) followed by manual responses to peripheral targets. In addition to behavioral IOR, previous EEG studies have demonstrated that the target-elicited early P1 event-related potential component (ERP) is reduced in magnitude at peripherally cued, relative to uncued locations. Here, we observed significant IOR and P1 modulations for peripherally cued targets, replicating previous work. However, trials with central endogenous cues produced IOR (as expected), but no P1 modulations.

authors_raw: Christopher T. Dean & Raymond M. Klein
title: Performance versus paper: Can typography save trees without sacrificing reading speed and comprehension?
abstract: The environmental impact of typography is under-recognized. While traditional typographic decision making is primarily based on aesthetics, intuition, and convention, we believe that a scientific approach can be used to save paper. Two experiments were conducted in which participants read eight passages, either silently or out loud. The passages were set in two different formats: standard, and paper-saving which reduced paper consumption by approximately 28%. The reading of each passage was timed and followed by a set of comprehension and subjective questions. Statistical analysis of our data revealed: 1) Reading silently was significantly faster than reading out loud while comprehension accuracy and time to answer the comprehension questions was affected by this variable. 2) Format had no significant effect on time to read, comprehension accuracy, or time to answer the comprehension questions. 3) Participants preferred standard over paper-saving parameters despite the fact they found paper-saving parameters more appropriate for a variety of contexts. The primary finding of this research demonstrates that with no loss in speed and comprehension, millions of trees a year can be saved if the paper-saving format we tested were adopted by most word processing softwares.

authors_raw: Jason Satel, Matthew Hilchey, Ross Story, Zhiguo Wang, & Raymond M. Klein
title: The effects of ignored versus foveated cues upon inhibition of return and early event-related potential components to targets calling for manual localization responses
abstract: Using 24 different variations of Posner's cue-target paradigm, Taylor & Klein (2000) discovered two mutually exclusive "flavors" of IOR: When the oculomotor system is inhibited, IOR affects input processes (perception/attention flavor); when the oculomotor system is not inhibited, IOR affects output processes (motor flavor). Studies of brain activity with ignored cues and manual responses to targets (for a review, see Prime & Ward, 2006) have typically reported that IOR reduces an early, sensory component (P1) of the brain's response to the target. Since eye movements are discouraged in these experiments, this P1 reduction might be a reflection of the perception/attention flavor of IOR. If, instead of ignoring the cue, participants make a prosaccade to the cue (and then return to fixation) before making a manual response to the target, then the motor flavor of IOR should be generated. In the present study we compared these two conditions while monitoring eye position and recording ERPs to the targets. If the P1 modulation is related to IOR, there should be no IOR modulation of the P1 component when the motoric flavor of IOR is generated by a prosaccade to the cue. Whereas target-elicited P1 reductions were similar, and behavioral IOR significant, in both conditions, behavioural IOR was significantly larger in the saccade condition. These findings suggest that either: 1) perceptual/attentional mechanisms of IOR are recruited whether the oculomotor system is inhibited or activated, or 2) P1 reductions do not provide an accurate reflection of IOR's behavioral manifestation.

authors_raw: Chelsea K. Quinlan & Tracy L. Taylor
title: The Musicality Effect
abstract: The purpose of this study was to explore whether or not singing a word would result in greater memory than simply reading a word aloud and/or reading a word silently. In order to explore this research question, we conducted three experiments, each of which consisted of a study phase followed by a test phase. In the study phase, participants were presented with words that they were instructed to sing, read aloud, or read silently. In the test phase, participants were tested using yes/no recognition. When the instruction condition (sing, read aloud, read silently) was manipulated within-subjects, we found that there was greater memory for words that were sung compared to words that were read aloud as well as words that were read silently. Also, we found that there was greater memory for words that were read aloud than words that were read silently (i.e., production effect). However, when the instruction condition was manipulated between-subjects, we found that there was no memory difference for words that were sung, read aloud, or read silently. Together, these findings suggest that while singing may be an effective memory strategy, it may require that words that are sung be intermixed with words that are read aloud and/or words that are read silently. We will discuss these findings in terms of relative distinctiveness.

authors_raw: Megan E. McLarnon & Sean P. Barrett
title: Misuse of prescription anxiolytics and sedatives in the community and among clients of substance use treatment programs in the HRM
abstract: Misuse of prescription drugs is a widespread phenomenon in Canada and a topic of increasing concern among health care providers. While anxiolytic and sedative medications have important therapeutic uses in the treatment of anxiety and insomnia, these substances have psychoactive properties that render them vulnerable to misuse. Understanding the correlates and contexts of misuse is essential for developing targeted treatment and prevention strategies. This presentation will summarize a series of studies conducted with adults in the Halifax Regional Municipality, including community samples and those drawn from local substance use treatment programs. Collectively, these studies show that anxiolytic and sedative misuse is a heterogeneous phenomenon, with varying patterns of use linked to individuals' motivations for misuse. Furthermore, these investigations demonstrate that prescription anxiolytic and sedative misuse is linked to individual-level factors (e.g., personality dimensions, past history of substance use) as well as to medication-related variables (e.g., type of anxiolytic or sedative, prescription status). By improving our understanding of prescription anxiolytic and sedative misuse and providing a more comprehensive characterization of this important public health issue, these findings have practical implications in both clinical and research contexts.

title: Validating the behavior rating inventory of executive functioning for children with ADHD and their typically developing peers
abstract: Many children with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) display impairments in executive functioning (EF), particularly working memory and response inhibition. The Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Functioning (BRIEF) has been widely used both clinically and in research for measuring EF in children with ADHD, despite the fact that it has not been validated against objective neuropsychological measures of EF. Therefore this study will examine the concurrent validity of the BRIEF (both parent and teacher ratings) compared to performance-based neuropsychological measures of EF. This will be examined for both children with ADHD and their typically-developing (TD) peers. The four EF domains of interest are: working memory, planning, inhibition, and set shifting. The sample will include children with ADHD and typically developing children matched for age and sex, between the ages of 8 and 12 years. It is expected that children with ADHD will be rated by parents and teachers as having more EF problems, and will also demonstrate more EF impairments than TD children on objective measures of EF. Additionally, it is expected that ratings from the BRIEF will correlate with scores from the performance-based measures of EF. It is hoped that the proposed study will establish the concurrent validity of the BRIEF for measuring EF in children with ADHD and their TD peers.

authors_raw: Kate M. Rancourt, Jillian H. Filliter, Michelle Kerr, Patricia A. McMullen, & Shannon A. Johnson
title: Semantic memory structure in children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorder
abstract: Performance on categorization and prototype formation tasks in people with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) suggests that their semantic memory structure may be atypical, and that categorization performance might be related to variations in central coherence (i.e., 'big picture thinking') and everyday functioning. Data from characterization and semantic memory tasks administered to 20 youths (8-18 years of age) with ASD, and 20 age-, IQ-, and gender-matched, typically-developing comparison participants are being collected. Participants will complete two measures of central coherence (the Embedded Figures Test and a Homograph Reading Task), and parents will complete a measure of adaptive functioning (Adaptive Behaviour Assessment System-II). Semantic memory tasks include: 1) Match-mismatch task: participants decide if a simultaneously presented picture and word are a match or mismatch (the level of identify of matches and mismatches is varied throughout the task), and 2) Triadic comparison task: three nouns are presented simultaneously and the participant decides which two are most similar. On the match-mismatch task, we expect youths with ASD will be faster to identify objects at more specific (i.e., subordinate; e.g., 'blue jay') levels of categorization, compared to controls who will be fastest at the basic level (i.e., general category; e.g., 'bird'). On the triadic comparison task, we expect 1) less efficient category structure in youths with ASD, and 2) that objects will be organized based on concrete rather than abstract features, whereas the opposite will be true for controls. Finally, we anticipate that faster performance at the subordinate level on the match-mismatch task, and less efficient category structure on the triadic comparison task, will be related to poorer central coherence performance and poorer adaptive functioning. Results of this research will provide information about the ways that youth with ASD learn, which could inform intervention research and practice.

authors_raw: Kevin R. Duffy & Donald E. Mitchell
title: Complete darkness promotes instability of the neuronal cytoskeleton
abstract: A short period of monocular deprivation in early postnatal life can alter the function and structure of neurons within the visual system. Such deprivation can also reduce labeling for a predominant component of the cytoskeleton, neurofilament, which acts to stabilize the gross structure of mature neurons. In a previous study that examined recovery from monocular deprivation we showed that elimination of visually-driven activity by dark-rearing not only fully blocked recovery of neurofilament in deprived regions of the thalamus but additionally promoted its overall reduction even within non-deprived regions. In this study we have examined directly the influence of dark-rearing on the level of neurofilament in the primary visual cortex. In tissue slices and in immunoblots we show a reduction of neurofilament within the visual cortex of animals that were kept in complete darkness for at least 10 days. We reason that removal of structure-providing protein by a pulse of dark-rearing may promote a more flexible neuronal state that raises the capacity for recovery from visual deficits consequent to amblyogenic rearing.

authors_raw: Donald E. Mitchell, Kevin R. Duffy, Katelyn MacNeil, & K. Holman
title: Substantially enhanced visual recovery from early monocular visual deprivation in kittens promoted by short periods of darkness
abstract: Short periods of darkness reduce labeling for a cytoskeleton that supports neuron structure by forming a stable intracellular scaffold (Duffy et al , In-House Conference 2012). Could this reduction of cytoskeleton protein increase plasticity in the visual cortex and thereby enhance the capacity for recovery for recovery from amblyopia? We measured the consequences for the vision of the two eyes of a 10 day period of darkness (DR) imposed at different times following early monocular deprivation (MD) in kittens. The period of DR prevented development of amblyopia when it occurred immediately after MD, and also promoted rapid recovery (in just 5-7 days) when imposed later. Although the close temporal similarity of the changes in cytoskeleton proteins and the behavioral effects of 10 days DR suggest a close link between the two, DR may have other effects including changes in the cortical excitatory-inhibitory balance. As an initial step in therapy, complete elimination of light to both eyes for 10 days may facilitate current treatment procedures for human amblyopia.

authors_raw: Mike Lawrence, Brittony Osler, Megan Fisher, Michelle Kerr, Stephanie Allen, & Shannon Johnson
title: Introducing a combined attention systems test
abstract: The Attention Network Test (ANT) and its variants (ANT-I, LANT, ANT-R, etc) have become popular research tools for quantifying several phenomena of attention. However, non-optimal design of these tests, limit their power and interpretability. This talk will present a new attention test, the Combined Attention Systems Test (CAST), that was designed to obviate the limitations of its predecessors and extend measurement to further phenomena of attention.

title: Acoustic startle response, prepulse inhibition, and dopamine D2 receptor levels in the rate medial prefrontal cortex after chronic predator odour exposure and social isolation stressors in adolescence
abstract: Stress and glucocorticoid release are implicated in programming the developing brain and the prefrontal cortex is particularly vulnerable to the programming effects of stress in adolescence, a period during which the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) undergoes significant development. Additionally, alterations in prefrontal dopaminergic activity are implicated in stress-related psychopathologies. Previous research demonstrated a reduction in dopamine D2 receptors in the rat medial prefrontal cortex after chronic predator odour exposure during adolescence. No previous work has examined the effects of chronic adolescent stress on both D2 receptor level and dopaminergic activity in the mPFC. In the current study, chronic predator odour and social isolation were used as naturalistic models of chronic psychological stress during the rat adolescent period (PND 21 to 59). Changes to acoustic startle response (ASR) and prepulse inhibition of startle (PPI) were assessed concurrent to stressor exposure in adolescence and adulthood. D2 receptor levels were also measured in the mPFC using western blot procedures. The results of this study indicate that ASR and PPI change in adulthood compared to adolescence and are affected by chronic predator odour and social isolation stressors in adolescence. No changes in the level of mPFC D2 receptor were observed. The results of this study provide further knowledge of how chronic stress during adolescence affects mPFC dopaminergic activity, possibly allowing for a better understanding of the etiologies of psychopathologies such as schizophrenia.

authors_raw: Charlene Parker, Chris Moore & Petra Hauf
title: Using mimicry to increase prosocial behaviour in preschoolers
abstract: The effects of mimicry on children's social and non-social behavior were tested in the current study. Children were randomly assigned to 2 conditions. During the priming phase the experimenter and child each played with an identical set of toys and the experimenter interacted with the child in a specific way. In the mimicry condition, the experimenter played with the same toy at the same time and in the same manner as the child. In the control condition, the experimenter played with a different toy in a different manner than the child at all times. During the testing phase each child performed two tasks in a counterbalanced order: a resource allocation task, and a delay of gratification task. Effects of mimicry on pro-social and non-social behaviors were determined by performance on the resource allocation task and the delay of gratification task, respectively. It was expected that children in the mimicry condition would perform better on the resource allocation task, but that children's performance would not vary between conditions on the delay of gratification task. Results will be discussed with regard to the effects of mimicry on pre-school children.

authors_raw: Erin L. Mazerolle, J. R. Gawryluk, K. Dillen, S. D. Beyea, & Ryan C. N. D'Arcy
title: Understanding why white matter fMRI is scarcely reported: Sensitivity to white matter activation increases with field strength
abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) activation in white matter is controversial. Given that many of the studies that report fMRI activation in white matter used high field MRI systems, we investigated the field strength dependence of sensitivity to white matter fMRI activation. Data were acquired during a motor task (finger tapping) at 1.5T and 4T. Group and individual level activation results were considered in both the motor cortex and the posterior limb of the internal capsule. In addition, we also evaluated the temporal signal to noise (tSNR) of the different tissue types as a function of field strength. We found that field strength dependent sensitivity increases were greater for white matter than gray matter. A similar pattern of results was observed for tSNR. We propose that, because physiological noise is lower in white matter than gray matter, field strength related increases in physiological noise might be less problematic for white matter. We further posit that the smaller T2* differences between gray and white matter at 4T compared to 1.5T allow for more uniform sensitivity across tissue types at high field, and conclude that high field MRI may be critical for fMRI studies aimed at investigating activation in both gray and white matter.

authors_raw: Thérèse M. Chevalier, Garth Stewart, Meg Boddington, Monty Nelson, Norman Brodie, & Robert McInerney
title: Differences in Canadian and American norms for the WAIS-IV
abstract: Large discrepancies have been noted between Canadian and American normed scores on the WAIS-IV, particularly for lower functioning individuals. This creates the potential for bias in diagnosis and decision making. To date there has been little research on discrepancies between Canadian and American norms on the WAIS and the validity of the normative data that has been developed from the respective standardization samples. Based on data from 338 individuals who underwent routine neuropsychological testing in Western Canada, this study will examine differences between test scores based on Canadian vs. American norms, and whether demographic variables such as age and education affect this discrepancy. It is expected that the magnitude of difference between American and Canadian IQ scores will be greater for those with IQ scores at the lower extreme than those with average IQ scores, and that other demographic or person variables will contribute to the variance between scores. Preliminary results will be presented.

title: Optical emissions by insect wing spots
abstract: Butterfly wing spots reflect incident light back out, as visual signals to observers. A small group of flies also developed wing spots in males but these act differently, redirecting sunlight passing through the wing out towards female observers. These spots also fluoresce, but fluorescence is a photon-inefficient process: whether fluorescence emission could be bright enough to be seen above the general background reflection from the wing surface seemed problematic. With UV excitation, blue fluorescence emission in fact added ~44% extra photons atop background (ΔI/I ratio ≈ 0.44); green fluorescence emission was much weaker and red emission, even feebler. The next question, though, is whether fly photoreceptors can even detect this blue emission above background photon noise, at the low levels of illumination present where these flies live -- on north-facing marine cliffs. At the head-to-head distance at which courtship display takes place, a females's view angle is reduced to barely 1°, dictating that only six identically aligned photoreceptors can view his wing spot. In low-level blue illumination, recordings from single photoreceptors in Calliphora to a 1° source establish that a response ΔI/I of only 0.42 can be attained above photon noise. The six aligned photoreceptors have axons that converge into the same synaptic column, however, where they become strongly electrically intercoupled through multiple gap junctions. Electrically averaging six visual responses via these junctions could improve the signal/noise ratio in each axon by up to √6 (2.45-fold). A ΔI/I → 0.17 then might be achievable for the electrical response, good enough to allow reliable detection of a larger optical fluorescence ΔI/I of ~0.44.

title: Pneumatology and the birth of psychology as an empirical science in eighteenth century Britain
abstract: During the 18th century, science and religion were often partners in the advance of knowledge and enlightenment. An example of this cooperation occurred in the development of an early modern version of psychology. After the collapse of Aristotelian metaphysics of soul as living principle in the seventeenth century and its replacement with Cartesian metaphysics where the soul became conscious mind, room was made for a novel approach to the investigation of soul. Traditionally, pneumatology, or the science of spirits, focused on metaphysical and religious notions of the human soul, angels & demons, and God. But right from the start in 18th century, there was an understanding that the human soul could be approached naturalistically. Teachers of pneumatology at dissenting religious academies in Britain tracked the development of this new empirical approach to soul as mind throughout the century, and through publications, they also participated in its development.

authors_raw: Matt A. MacNeil, Aislin R. Mushquash, Simon B. Sherry, Sherry H. Stewart, Sean P. Mackinnon, Martin M. Antony, & Dayna L. Sherry
title: Heavy episodic drinking among dating partners: A longitudinal actor-partner interdependence model
abstract: Heavy episodic drinking (HED) is a serious health problem for many young adults. For about two decades, rates of HED have remained consistently high among young adults. Although research has identified a variety of intrapersonal, interpersonal, and environmental contributors to HED, much of the research focuses on intrapersonal factors. Thus, more research is needed to test the role of specific interpersonal factors in HED. This study tests the partner influence hypothesis, which suggests romantic partners influence each other's HED over time. A sample of 208, non-married, heterosexual dating couples completed HED measures at baseline and again 28 days later. Actor-partner interdependence analyses showed significant actor effects, which suggests HED is stable within each partner over time. Additionally, results showed significant partner effects, where HED in both young men and women in romantic relationships positively influenced their partners' future HED over 28-days. Results also suggest both women and men are affected more by their own prior levels of HED than by their partner's prior levels of HED. Nevertheless, small partner effects were observed for both women and men. Results support the partner influence hypothesis, suggesting HED is a behavior maintained, in part, by a pattern of interpersonal influence. These results suggest the importance of considering both intrapersonal and interpersonal factors when developing prevention and intervention programs for HED in young adults.

authors_raw: Jason Satel, Matthew Hilchey, Zhiguo Wang, Caroline Reiss, Ross Story, & Ray Klein
title: Uncovering the neural mechanisms of inhibition of return using event-related potentials
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) is a cognitive phenomenon whereby response times are slower to cued as compared to uncued targets at cue-target onset asynchronies greater than ~300 ms. A number of studies have investigated IOR using a traditional cue-target paradigm (where the cue is ignored), while recording electroencephalographic activity. Event-related potential analyses have revealed that amplitudes of the early sensory P1 and the later Nd components are reduced for cued, as compared to uncued targets. In a series of studies expanding on this line of research, we have replicated previous results showing that cueing modulates P1, both when peripheral cues are ignored, and when they are foveated. However, IOR scores only correlated with P1 modulations when peripheral cues were ignored. When central, endogenous cues were mixed with peripheral cues, IOR was only observed for foveated central cues, while P1 modulations were only observed for ignored central cues. Mixing central with peripheral targets eliminated the P1 modulations, but IOR was observed for both target types. Finally, when an eye movement was interposed between cue and target appearance, P1 modulations were only observed for retinotopically cued targets, while the later Nd component was reduced only for spatiotopically cued targets. We propose that P1 modulations are the result of repeated activation in retinotopic maps, while Nd modulations may be a reflection of spatiotopic IOR.

authors_raw: Richard E. Brown, Rhian K. Gunn, Tim P. O'Leary
title: What are we really measuring in tests of anxiety in mice?
abstract: Anxiety in mice is measured in the elevated plus maze (EPM; Hogg, 1996, Pharm Biochem Behav, 54, 21-30); open field (OF; Prut & Belzung, 2003, Eur J Pharm, 463, 3-33); and light/dark box (LDB; Bourin & Hascoet, 2003, Eur J Pharm, 463, 55-65), and studies often use all three (Kliethermes et al., 2005, Neurosci Biobehav Rev, 28, 837-50). We examined anxiety and locomotor behavior in 10 week old male and female mice of 15 inbred strains (129S1/SvImJ, A/J, AKR/J, BALB/cByJ, BALB/cJ, BTBR T+ tf/tf, C3H/HeJ, C57BL/6J, CAST/EiJ, DBA/2J, FVB/NJ, MOLF/EiJ, SJL/J, SM/J and SPRET/EiJ) on the EPM, LDB and OF. Across all three tests we found that (1) measures of locomotion are significantly positively correlated; (2) frequency of defecations are highly positively correlated; (3) behavioral measures of anxiety are poorly correlated, and; (4) within each test, measures of anxiety and locomotion are correlated. In a factor analysis locomotor measures accounted for 22.2% of the variance, whereas anxiety-related measures accounted for only a small proportion of the variance. This analysis casts doubt on the concept of genetically determined strain differences in a "trait" of anxiety and indicates that locomotor behaviors are the most reliable measures of strain and sex differences in these apparatus. This suggests that we must reconsider the concept of strain-specific "anxiety" traits and focus on activity levels. Whether or not newly designed apparatus for measuring anxiety such as the triple-test (Fraser et al., Psychopharm, 2010, 211, 99-112) will help us resolve this issue is being examined.

authors_raw: Meredith Bessey, Jennifer Richards, & Penny Corkum
title: Sleep lab adaptation in children with ADHD and their typically developing peers
abstract: There are inconsistent findings as to the type and frequency of sleep problems in children with ADHD. High rates of sleep problems are consistently found on parent report, but are not always verified on polysomnography (PSG). It is hypothesized that this discrepancy may be due to differential sleep lab adaptation (i.e., how well or how poorly children adjust to a sleep lab environment). Research has not investigated this specific question, although one study examined changes in sleep patterns from the first to second night of PSG collection and found that children with ADHD demonstrated a stronger first night effect compared to typically developing (TD) children. In the current study, actigraphy variables were compared between the home environment and the sleep lab during PSG collection for children with ADHD (n = 25) and TD children (n = 25). Further, sleep lab adaptation reports from parent, child and PSG Research Assistant (RA) were compared between groups. Actigraphy variables revealed that both groups of children slept for significantly less time in the sleep lab than at home. Additionally, TD children, but not children with ADHD, had improved sleep efficiency in the sleep lab. It was also found that all children had significantly less wake after sleep onset in the sleep lab than at home. Sleep onset latency did not differ across environments and there was no interaction with group. Parents of children with ADHD reported that their children's sleep onset at the sleep lab was atypical. PSG RAs noted more sleep difficulties for children with ADHD, but the children themselves did not report any difficulties. For some sleep variables, data collected in the sleep lab may not be representative of children's sleep in the home environment. Based on actigraphy data there was no differential impact on sleep for children with ADHD, and therefore, sleep lab adaptation is not a likely reason for the discrepancy between parent report and PSG findings.

authors_raw: Sean R. McWhinney, T. Bardouille, Aaron J. Newman, D. DeBay, R. M. Sadler, D. B. Clarke, M. J. Esser, & Ryan C. N. D'Arcy
title: Spatial MEG laterality maps for language
abstract: Functional neuroimaging has been used with increasing prevalence in recent years as a non-invasive method of language laterality assessment. Historically, laterality of language and memory has been assessed using the intracarotid sodium amobarbitol test (i.e., the Wada test). This is an invasive investigation and provides spatially limited measures of laterality. Despite advances in neuroimaging, recent laterality assessments suffer similar limitations, such as a one-dimensional laterality index (LI). This oversimplification has neglected the potential value of functional neuroimaging. We demonstrated a language laterality assessment that uses magnetoencephalography (MEG) to produce "complex laterality vectors". These values were used to create "laterality maps" that indicate both the strength of activity and degree of laterality for a function. To demonstrate, language was assessed in healthy controls and in patients with epilepsy. It was shown that laterality maps provide information about the spatial characteristics of language activation, and that this information would be unavailable using the traditional LI. Importantly, it was possible to differentiate between weak LI scores that resulted from weak vs. strong bilateral activation, as well as strong LI scores that resulted from weak vs. strong unilateral activation. The ability to clarify these ambiguous situations, and to display complex patterns of activation, make laterality maps an ideal representation of language dominance.

title: Assessment of olfactory learning and memory, reversal learning, and olfactory sensitivity in the 5xFAD mouse model of Alzheimer's disease
abstract: As a result of continued advances in genetic engineering techniques, an ever-increasing number of mouse models of Alzheimer's disease (AD) are being developed. Before these animals can be used to assess the potential of novel drug therapies, the validity of the models must be tested. Despite the well-known capacity of rodents for learning olfactory tasks, the majority of memory tests rely upon visual cues. Here we report on the use of an operant-olfactometer to assess olfactory learning and memory in 6 month old 5xFAD mice. The transgenic mice showed no deficits compared to wildtype littermates at this age in either an olfactory discrimination task, or a reversal learning task. An olfactory sensitivity task found impaired performance in transgenic mice, as well as greater sensitivity in female mice than male mice.

title: Effects of training on a dual n-back task on the components of working memory and fluid intelligence
abstract:
Objective: To investigate the specificity and generalizability of working memory training. Design: Randomised, single blind, controlled design. Setting: University research laboratory and community. Participants: 24 healthy participants (ages M = 20.7, SD = 2.5 yrs; 5 male, 19 female). Five withdrew due to time constraints.
Interventions: Dual n-back training group: Five daily training sessions each week for three weeks. Sessions consisted of 20 blocks of an adaptive dual (visual and auditory) n-back task. Active control group: Same training program without adaptive progression to harder levels. Main Outcome Measures: Cattell's Culture Fair Intelligence Test (IQ); Monty Hall problem and Stroop task (executive function); Operation Span and Symmetry Span (verbal and spatial working memory span). Results: Operation Span and Symmetry Span: Only the training group showed a significant improvement, p < .0001, 95% CI [11.68, 30.17] and p = .0465, 95% CI [0.18, 26.76], respectively. No other measure showed a significant benefit from active training. Conclusions: Neurological disease and injury frequently affect working memory and dual task performance, leading to reduced quality of life and creating a need for effective cognitive interventions. In this study, dual task coordination benefited from training, but these benefits did not generalise to executive functions or fluid intelligence as previously reported. However, individual differences in performance on the training task has been shown to impact transfer of training benefits. More research is needed to identify critical factors that underlie generalizability of working memory training. Investigation of individual differences in the study is ongoing.

authors_raw: Amanda D. Green, Kal Mungovan, Meagan MacLean, Michael J. Esser, Tara S. Perrot
title: Prenatal psychological stress and postnatal environmental enrichment have age and sex specific effects on the development of anxiety/depressive behaviours and changes in glucocorticoid receptors
abstract: Stressful events during gestation can cause sex-specific effects on brain and behavior, and may contribute to some of the differences observed in adult stress responding and psychopathology. We investigated the impact of a novel prenatal psychological stress (predator exposure - PE), in the rat, 3x daily during the last week of gestation on juvenile (PND15) and adult behaviours related to social interaction (play behaviour), anxiety (open field test - OFT) and depression (forced swim test - FST and sucrose preference test), and the role of postnatal environmental enrichment (EE) to prevent/rescue these changes. Adult animals were sacrificed and brains were collected for western blot analysis of glucocorticoid (GR) and mineralocorticoid receptor (MR) levels. In the juvenile OFT, PS females spent less time than males in the open field center and EE removed this difference. In the juvenile FST, PE or EE pups showed less immobility than controls. Juveniles exposed to either PE or EE also showed less antisocial behaviour, but with both PE and EE, anti-social behaviour increased. In the adult sucrose preference test, PE females consumed significantly less sucrose than all other groups, and this correlated with increased immobility in the juvenile FST. Analyses of hippocampal GR and MR levels are ongoing. Both PE and EE altered anxiety and depressive behaviours, but the effects were highly age and sex specific. Some alterations indicative of pathologies (OFT anxiety in males or adult female depressive behaviors) caused by the early environment did not manifest until adulthood, while changes caused by early EE (decreased OFT anxiety in males, decreased FST immobility in females) were lost in adults. Together, these results suggest an important, though complex, role for early life psychological stressors and early life behaviours in creating an adult vulnerability to anxiety or depressive disorders.

authors_raw: Evan R. Fairn & Shelley A. Adamo
title: Do immune-challenged crickets change behaviour to conserve resources and avoid predators?
abstract: Vertebrates mounting an immune response often exhibit a suite of behavioural changes (i.e. sickness behaviour) that can include a decrease in food intake, water intake, reproduction, locomotion, and exploratory behaviour. It is believed that sickness behaviour is adaptive, possibly functioning to conserve resources required to survive infection and reduce exposure to predators when in a debilitated state.
