Little is known about whether immune-challenged invertebrates exhibit similar behavioural changes. Here, we investigated sickness behaviour in the Texas field cricket Gryllus texensis. Consistent with a previous study, we found that this species decreases feeding, but not drinking, during an immune response. We found no evidence that immune challenge affects locomotion, exploratory behaviour/boldness in a novel environment (with or without simulated predator), or propensity to engage in courtship. Decreased motivation to feed during an immune response could result in reduced energy expenditure and decreased exposure to predators by decreasing foraging. However, our results suggest that this species does not employ a broad predator avoidance and energy conservation strategy when immune-challenged.

title: The effects of distracters on working memory
abstract: Working Memory (WM) is used to hold and manipulate information in an active, short-term store while performing a range of daily tasks such as reading, mental arithmetic and problem solving. These tasks must be completed in the face of distractors, which appear to become increasing difficult to ignore as we age (e.g. Gazzaley et al., 2005). The purpose of this study was to investigate the effect of distractors on both the maintenance and manipulation components of WM performance. We predicted that WM performance would be most disrupted by distractors when the task required continuous updating of the active short-term store. Ten healthy young adults (mean age = 24.2; 6 female) completed a series of n-back WM tasks designed to assess the effects of task-irrelevant distractors on WM performance. Analysis of both reaction time (RT) data and accuracy was conducted using mixed effects modeling with updating (maintenance vs updating) and distractors (present vs not present) treated as fixed effects. Accuracy was high across all conditions (94-96%). The presence of task-irrelevant distractors increased RT in both maintenance (RT=514ms vs 634ms for absent vs present distractors respectively) and updating n-back tasks (679 ms vs 769 ms, respectively), although the increase was smallest during the updating tasks. Contrary to our prediction, these results suggest that increased task difficulty can reduce attention to task irrelevant distractors, perhaps due to the need for greater attentional control. These data suggest that the role of distractors in WM deficits that accompany aging or brain damage deserve investigation.

authors_raw: Cameron D. Hassall, Katharine Holland, Timothy J. Pleskac, Olave E. Krigolson
title: What do I do now? Exploration and exploitation in a sequential risk-taking task?
abstract: Effective decision making requires exploring new options and exploiting previous learning. Many experimental tasks fail to capture the exploration/exploitation trade-off, either because learning occurs too rapidly, or because they lack valid operational definitions for these behaviours. The Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART: Lejuez et al., 2002) requires sequential gambles from participants. Each subsequent gamble involves more potential payout, but also more risk. Although BART responses may become somewhat automatic with learning, participants will occasionally pause to assess whether or not they should continue to gamble. These and other behavioural findings suggest that the BART may be a good candidate for studying the exploration/exploitation trade-off (Wershbale & Pleskac, 2010). Here, we reproduced earlier results by Pleskac et al. (2009) showing a decrease in exploration over time. In other words, participants became less likely to deliberate over their decisions (explore) as they became more familiar with the BART. Furthermore, we observed an event-related potential (ERP, specifically P300) difference between exploration and exploitation trials. Finally, we found that the feedback error-related negativity (fERN), an ERP component sensitive to rewards and punishments, was enhanced for higher-valued gambles, indicating that participants maintained an accurate representation of potential reward. Taken together, these results suggest a possible neural marker of exploration that is validated by both behavioural and neuroimaging data.

authors_raw: Erin Sparks, Helene Deacon, Rebecca Tucker, & Sally Carver
title: Lexical stress and the Missing Letter Effect: The roles of grammatical and orthographic stress cues during reading
abstract: Although English does not explicitly encode stress in its writing system, it does contain regularities that probabilistically cue stress placement. One regularity is grammatical: nouns tend to be trochaic (DOCtor); verbs tend to be iambic (enJOY). A second regularity is orthographic: certain letter patterns are associated with either trochaic or iambic stress. In two experiments, we explore the role of these stress cues in skilled reading using the Missing Letter Effect (Corcoran, 1966). Test words are embedded in passages that participants read while simultaneously detecting a target letter. We then compare letter detection rates in words whose stress cues are either consistent (trochaic nouns/iambic verbs; trochaic words with trochaic cues) or inconsistent (iambic nouns/trochaic verbs; trochaic words with iambic cues) with their stress patterns. We expect fewer letter detections in the consistent conditions, reflecting more efficient processing of these words than those with inconsistent stress cues.

authors_raw: Namrata Joshi, Tara Perrot, & Ron Leslie
title: The impact of repeated stress on memory and models of affect in male and female rats reared in isolation for a short period during adolescence
abstract: Repeated stress causes enduring effects when it occurs during post-embryonic development. Brain regions undergoing rapid maturation during this period are particularly impacted and the problem is believed to be exacerbated in case of limited or no social support. The rearing of animals in isolation during this period of development has been used to model such an absence of social support and has been shown to cause enduring changes in response to stress including behaviour representative of certain affective disorders. I have investigated the effect of isolating rats for a brief period of time during adolescence upon the changes produced by the stress of repeated exposure to cat odour administered during the same period (i.e. predator odour stress). Previous experiments with this model of chronic stress indicated that some effects were produced on acoustic startle modulation, while memory and behaviours representative of affective disorder were largely unaffected. I hypothesize that rearing animals in isolation produces more of an increase in stress-related disruption compared with the effects of predator odour stress alone. Performance in the elevated plus maze was examined in order to determine levels of anxiety-like behaviour. The sucrose preference test was used to assess depression-like behaviour and a novel object recognition test was used to assess memory in order to monitor hippocampal function. Preliminary results indicate little or no effect of isolation on the changes brought on by stress exposure.

authors_raw: Raymond M. Klein, Matthew Hilchey, & Jason Satel
title: On the flavors of inhibition of return (IOR)
abstract: In her 1997 dissertation, using variants of the model task pioneered by Posner, Tracy Taylor (Taylor & Klein, 2000) discovered 2 distinct "flavours" of IOR. When saccadic responses were precluded, the effect of IOR was confined to the input pathway (attentional/perceptual favour); yet, when saccadic responses were executed (either to the cue or target, or both) IOR was confined to the output pathway (decision/motor flavour). Recent work from our laboratory validates this important distinction, explores its implications, and identifies the motor flavour as the type of IOR that has been hypothesized to be a novelty seeking foraging facilitator.

authors_raw: Josh Pereira, Cindy Hamon-Hill, & John Barresi
title: Does ADHD affect perception of emotions in others?
abstract: According to Barkley (1997), impaired social skills associated with Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) are due to higher levels of emotional reactivity, with no disruption in their perception of others' emotions. However, in recent reports adults with ADHD displayed less accuracy than control participants in recognizing facial affect (Friedman et al. 2003; Rapport et al. 2002) and a negative association between accuracy in emotion recognition and experienced emotion (Rapport et al. 2002). We tested emotion recognition in adults with ADHD using an ecologically valid paradigm and observed no difference in accuracy between the control and clinical group. We did observe slower reaction times for the ADHD group, p = .003, on static but not dynamic facial stimuli, p = .01. Higher reported levels of experienced emotion for the ADHD group, relative to controls', were negatively associated with reaction time, r =-.452, p = .01, but not with accuracy. Instead, decreased accuracy was associated with difficulty in identifying emotions for the ADHD group, r = -.417, p = .04. We suggest our results can be explained by testing with naturalistic stimuli that do not require emotional labeling.

authors_raw: Zachary Stahlschmidt, Robbin McKee, & Shelly Adamo
title: Food availability and the tradeoff between reproduction and immune function
abstract: Reproduction and immune function are both integral fitness components in many taxa--from plants to humans. Life history theory predicts investment into one of these traits comes at a cost to the other trait (e.g., high fecundity is associated with reduced immune function). Two hypotheses have been proposed regarding the proximate mechanisms underlying this tradeoff. According to the classic "resource allocation" hypothesis, organisms with a finite number of resources can optimize either reproduction or immune function, but not both. The "pleiotropic effects" hypothesis asserts that each trait involves mechanisms that are detrimental to the other trait independent of resource availability (e.g., oxidative bursts used during immune activation destroy pathogens but may also damage reproductive tissue). To address these hypotheses, we used adult female Texas field crickets (Gryllus texensis) and a two-factorial design: immune challenge (chronic injections of heat-killed bacteria, chronic sham treatment, or unhandled control) and food availability (ad libitum or intermittent access to food). We discuss the effects of these two factors on body mass, fecundity (number and size of eggs, hatching success, and hatchling phenotype), and the ability to withstand oxidative stress. Together our results clarify the importance of resource availability and pleiotropic effects in the widespread tradeoff between reproduction and immune function.

title: Is there a 'crow' in 'crown'? Evidence for direct and phonologically-mediated connections between orthography and semantics in developing readers
abstract: An important goal of reading development is the ability to access meaning quickly and efficiently from orthography. In the current study, we explored whether children are sensitive to embedded sub-word orthographic patterns via a semantic competition paradigm. Using this paradigm, 108 children in grades 2 and 3 were asked to make "yes/no" category decisions to items. Participants' responses to the same words were compared across congruent ("is battery related to the category vehicles?") vs. incongruent conditions ("is battery related to the category animals?"), both of which require a "no" response, yet differ in respect to whether the embedded sub-word is related to the given category (bat in battery is related to animals but not vehicles). Semantic competition effects were observed; children were less accurate to categorize words when the embedded word was related in meaning to the category (incongruent items) as opposed to when the sub-word shared no meaning with the category (congruent items). These findings demonstrate that connections between orthography and semantics are formed in the early stages of reading development and that, even in young readers, semantic information is activated from sub-word orthographic patterns. Furthermore, semantic interference effects were most evident when sub-words shared the same pronunciation as their carrier words (e.g., bat in battery), supporting the notion that phonology plays an important role in the orthography-to-semantics connections of developing readers.

authors_raw: Marcelo F. Salviano, B. E. Goad, Rhian K. Gunn, & Richard E. Brown
title: Effects of social separation on depression, anxiety, learning, and memory in the 5XFAD mouse
abstract: In humans, loneliness increases the risk of depression and of late-life dementia (Wilson et al, 2007, Arch. Gen. Psychiatry 64, 234-40). Social isolation also increases depressive-like behavior in female C57BL/6J mice (Martin & Brown, 2010, Behav. Brain Res. 207, 196-207). The aim of this study was to investigate the effects of social separation on behavior of male and female 5XFAD mice and their B6SJL wildtype (WT) littermates. Mice were housed in same-sex littermate groups until five months of age and then half were separated for one month before testing. Separated females, but not males, had reduced weight gain and increased wildness scores. Separation increased the frequency of bouts of immobility in the tail suspension test for both males and females. Separation did not affect measures of activity in the elevated plus maze or light-dark box but female 5xFAD mice showed less anxiety than WT females. There were no effects of separation on learning or memory in the Morris water maze, but separated mice showed less freezing in the contextual memory test. These results indicate that social separation results in an increase in depressive-like behavior in female mice but does not affect measures of activity, anxiety, spatial or conditioned fear memory. Although females seem more sensitive to the effects of social separation than males, the 5XFAD mice do not seem more sensitive than their WT littermates.

title: Effect of L2 proficiency on modulating oculomotor control in Hindi-English bilinguals
abstract: The cognitive impact of bilingualism has recently attracted attention of many researchers. Bilinguals' performance on several non-linguistic interference task , using manual response, have indicated that bilinguals posses better executive control as compared to monolinguals. We wanted to determine whether similar bilingual advantage would result in an overall saccade response using an oculomotor non-linguistic Stroop task and how proficiency modulates such advantage. The high proficient bilinguals showed overall faster saccade latency on all the trial types than the low proficient bilinguals thus providing evidence for domain -general advantage extending to the oculomotor control in the bilinguals

title: The impact of nicotine lozenges and stimulus expectancies on cigarette craving
abstract: Cigarette craving plays an important role in the outcome of cessation attempts. Craving arises during nicotine abstinence, yet non-nicotine factors such as stimulus expectancies, beliefs about the effects of a substance, also play an important role in craving and cessation. The current study will use a balanced placebo design to examine the impact of nicotine and stimulus expectancies on cigarette craving. 52 male and 52 female smokers will be divided into two groups, a nicotine lozenge and a placebo lozenge group. Within each group, half the participants will be correctly informed of the lozenge type received, while the other half will be deceived about lozenge type received. Following lozenge consumption craving will be assessed, participants watch two 2-minute video clips (a neutral and a smoking cue), and craving will be reassessed. A two-way mixed measures analysis of variance will be conducted to determine whether nicotine and stimulus expectancies have differing effects on cigarette craving. It is hoped that this research will lead to a better understanding of factors, such as craving, impeding and facilitating successful smoking cessation, even when aided with a nicotine replacement therapy, such as the nicotine lozenge.

authors_raw: Franziska Kintzel, Yoko Ishigami, & Gail Eskes
title: Ready, set, point! The effects of alertness on prism adaptation in healthy adults
abstract: One therapeutic approach for patients with spatial neglect is prism adaptation (PA), which targets the orienting deficit underlying neglect. Evidence suggests that alerting and orienting functions interact. Alerting tones reduced patients' abnormal orienting bias, measured by temporal order judgments. We examined the interaction of alerting with orienting during a PA procedure. Ten healthy young participants each in 3 conditions were tested: PA only, PA + alerting tones, and control group. Outcome measures included 3 subjective-midline tasks (SMTs), and visual search (VS). During the adaptation period, participants pointed towards targets while wearing clear or prismatic goggles that shifted the visual field rightwards. Warning tones were presented on 25% of trials in the PA + alerting condition. With ANT-I as pre-test, we validated the alerting effect by using similar warning cues. Preliminary results showed typical after-effects of PA in PA conditions on SMTs, but not on VS. Alerting cues either increased or decreased SMTs after-effects, depending upon whether the task was visually guided. Thus, interaction between alerting and orienting appears complex, and alerting seems to enhance responses similar to ones given during PA.

title: Interactions between the immune response and stress response in insects (cricket, Gryllus texensis): Conflict and collaboration
abstract: Immune responses and stress responses often conflict when they occur simultaneously, leading to immunosuppression. In this study we examine some of the molecular underpinnings of these interactions. We examine the effect of both the immune response and stress response on the expression of heat shock protein 90 (Hsp90), glutathione-s-transferase (GST) and nitric oxide (NO) synthase gene expression using quantitative PCR. We examine the effects on gene expression in the fat body, a tissue important for both the stress and immune responses in crickets. Hsp90 is a protective chaperone molecule and increases in response to heat stress 100 fold compared to control animals. GST is part of a class of enzymes involved in detoxification of oxidative stress products. It is upregulated 50 fold, compared to non-injected animals, when crickets are injected with paraquat, a toxic pesticide. Following an immune challenge with heat killed Serratia marcescens Hsp90 is upregulated 21 fold at 3 hours and 100 fold at 12 hours and drops to 60 fold at 24 hours compared to non-challenged animals. There is a 2 fold upregulation of GST at 3 hours but no change at 1, 6, 12 or 24 hours. The enzyme NO synthase, which produces NO, is upregulated 5 fold at 24 hours compared to non-injected controls. The effects of acute stress, and acute stress in combination with an immune challenge, on gene expression will be discussed.

authors_raw: Kim Tan-MacNeil, Hélène Deacon, & Annie Noble
title: An individual differences approach to understanding predictors of first-year undergraduate students' reading ability
abstract: There is a need for research with adults that explores the factors that determine individual differences in reading ability. This would offer a key test of the lifespan-applicability of reading development models (e.g., Ehri, 2009; Share,1995) from an individual differences perspective. We examine how three key reading-related variables -- phonological awareness, morphological awareness, and orthographic processing -- predict first-year undergraduates' performance on word reading, reading comprehension, and ecologically valid measures of reading. 120 students completed a range of standardized and experimental measures of reading outcomes. Hierarchical multiple regression will be used to evaluate how phonological awareness, morphological awareness, and orthographic processing independently and together predict reading ability in adults. This research will evaluate, for the first time, the combined and independent effects of these variables on adult reading ability, and contribute to our understanding of reading across the lifespan.

authors_raw: Logan J. Nealis, Matthew MacNeil, Martin Smith, Stephane MacLean, Cynthia Ramasubbu, Dayna L. Sherry, Sherry H. Stewart, & Simon B. Sherry
title: Narcissistic perfectionism: A framework for understanding grandiose, entitled, demanding and critical people
abstract: Some types of perfectionism (e.g., self-critical perfectionism) are well-defined by theory and well-supported by evidence (McGrath, Sherry, et al., 2012), while others are poorly understood. Though the connection between narcissism and perfectionism is occasionally discussed in passing (Sorotzkin, 1985), sustained theoretical commentary or empirical research on this topic is scarce. The present study tested the factorial validity of the narcissistic perfectionism construct, a hypothesized constellation of traits involving narcissistic grandiosity, a sense of entitlement, unrealistic demands for others, and intense criticism of others. Participants (N = 323) completed questionnaires measuring traits of interest (e.g., Hewitt & Flett, 1991; Raskin & Terry, 1998). Confirmatory factor analysis indicated the hypothesized measurement model for narcissistic perfectionism fit the data adequately, thereby supporting the factorial validity of the narcissistic perfectionism construct. Discriminant validity analysis also indicated narcissistic perfectionism is meaningfully distinct from existing measures of self-critical perfectionism. The present study advances understanding of the narcissistic style of perfectionism by articulating and by quantifying the underlying structure of narcissistic perfectionism, including what factors contribute to this constellation of traits and the relative importance of each trait.

authors_raw: Austin Korgan, Amanda Green, Tara Perrot, & Michael Esser
title: Effects of prenatal stress and environmental enrichment on juvenile temporal lobe epilepsy
abstract: Epilepsy is prominent in the general population, affecting ~1-2% of the population worldwide. Febrile seizers, provoked by fever, are common among children, though normally these episodes are mild and do not result in prolonged deficits. However, prolonged or atypical febrile convulsions can have a long-term impact, including increased risk of medial temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE) and damage to limbic system structures. The objectives of the current study will attempt to identify interventions that minimize the effects of febrile convulsions. Using an ethologically relevant predator exposure, we subjected pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats to regular elevations in prenatal glucocorticoid levels. Postnatal environmental enrichment was then utilized as a potential moderator of any prenatal effects. Then, on PND 14, LPS and kainic acid induced febrile seizures were observed for behavioral measurement. Initial results show strong dam dependent effects of seizures on behavior, Fos immunoreactivity and glucocorticoid levels.

title: Impact of menstrual cycle phase on metabolic effects of sleep restriction
abstract: Spontaneous short sleep and imposed sleep restriction are linked to altered metabolic and endocrine function. Sleep restriction has been associated, for example, with increased cortisol and ghrelin levels, decreased leptin levels and increased appetite. Most of these studies have used male participants, but a few recent studies have confirmed similar findings in women. One such study showed effects of sleep restriction of cortisol and leptin levels, but a retrospective analysis of the data also suggested that menstrual cycle phase was a strong modulator of these effects of sleep loss. These preliminary results suggest that reproductive hormones may have a significant impact on how sleep loss affects the endocrine system in women. In the proposed study, we will recruit ten women at two different stages of their menstrual cycle (mid-follicular & mid-luteal, n=20) and examine whether menstrual phase affects the impact of sleep restriction (3 hrs of sleep for one night) on levels of the hormones cortisol, ghrelin and leptin . A secondary goal is to examine how sleep loss affects self-reports of mood, hunger and food preferences, and whether menstrual cycle phase also modulates these effects. It is hoped that this research will shed light onto information about how normal hormonal patterns during the life cycle of women affect their response to sleep loss. The results will also be important for a better understanding of how sleep loss affects risks for weight gain and hormonal disruption, a well as the development of associated chronic diseases in women.

title: Women in Science, Women in Medicine: Why the difference in retention rates from trainee to practioner?
abstract: Although as many women as men receive a Ph.D. in the biological sciences, more men than women are successful at obtaining a tenure-track position. A number of reasons have been suggested for these 'leaks in the pipeline'. One prominent theory suggests that women drop out of science because of the incompatibility between a scientific career and raising a family. Supporting this hypothesis are data showing that while unmarried women are as likely as men to achieve tenure, women with children were 1/3 less successful. Having children has no apparent effect on the odds that men will gain a tenure-track position. This paper presents a preliminary comparison between the fields of science and medicine in their ability to attract and retain women. The reasons for the difference will be discussed.

authors_raw: Hélène Deacon, Jenna Benere & Anne Castles
title: What children learn as they learn to read
abstract: Children bring a number of skills to the process of learning to read, and they learn a great deal from their reading. It has been argued that one of the skills that children use as they read is orthographic processing skill, or the ability to form, store and access representations of words and letter-patterns. We examined this assumption in an empirical study designed to clarify the direction of the relationship between orthographic processing and reading. We conducted a three-year longitudinal study of children from Grades 1 to 3. We included standard measures of orthographic processing skill, at both the lexical and sublexical level, and word reading accuracy, as well as controls of vocabulary, non-verbal reasoning, and phonological awareness. In all analyses, word reading predicted progress in acquiring orthographic processing skill, regardless of grade level or orthographic processing measure. In contrast, orthographic processing skill did not predict progress in word reading. Our results suggest that, between Grades 1 and 3, children acquire orthographic processing skill through their reading and that this ability, as characterized by the most common tasks used to date, does not play an independent role in supporting reading acquisition. We discuss the implications of these findings.

authors_raw: Amanda Williams, Kelly O'Driscoll, & Chris Moore
title: The Influence of empathy on prosocial behaviour in children
abstract: Although empathy has been linked to prosociality and generosity in adults, the relationship between empathy and prosocial behavior in children is not well understood. It was the objective of the current study to explore the influence of empathy on prosocial behavior in children, and more specifically investigate effects of empathy on sharing and envy. Children between the ages of 5-6 years were randomly assigned to one of two conditions before engaging in a sticker sharing task. In the empathy condition children watched a video of a young girl upset that her dog had gone missing (distressing situation), while in the control condition children watched a video of the same girl preparing for a yard sale (neutral situation). A manipulation check showed that children who had viewed the distressing video rated the emotional state of the child in the video, as well as their own emotional state, more negatively than those who had viewed the neutral video. When asked to allocate resources between self and the child in the video, it was found that children in the empathy condition made more prosocial allocations; more often sharing, and less often withholding benefits, than those in the control condition.

authors_raw: Chris D. Cowper-Smith, Gail E. Eskes, & D. A. Westwood
title: Inhibition of return: A simple motor execution bias
abstract:
Inhibition of return (IOR) is thought to improve the efficiency of visual search behaviour by biasing attention, eye movements, or both, toward novel stimuli. Previous research suggests that IOR might arise from sensory, attentional or motor programming processes. In the present study, we were interested in determining if IOR could instead arise from a simple late-stage motor output bias, independent from effects on earlier processes. Participants made consecutive saccades (from a common starting location) to central arrowhead stimuli. We removed the possible contribution of sensory/attentional and motor preparation effects in IOR by allowing participants to fully prepare their responses in advance of an execution signal. When responses were delayed to allow response preparation, we continued to observe IOR. Our data therefore provide evidence that IOR can result from a simple motor execution bias that may arise from inhibitory effects on motor output neurons controlling the extraocular muscles directly.
DEBATE: Open vs traditional peer-reviewed publishing in science
Aaron Newman, Jon Fawcett, Mike Lawrence, Heath Matheson, and more
The world of publishing, both within academia and more broadly, has been revolutionized by digital technology. With the technology necessary to distribute one's work available to virutally everyone, it is clear that the old paradigms cannot be maintained. At the same time, scientific publishing has a long and proud tradition of curation through rigourous peer review. While few would argue that peer review is a bad thing, we are also aware that peer review has a dark side: irrational reviewers (positive or negative, though we focus more on the latter); coldly rational reviewers who deliberately sabatoge manuscripts to prevent being scoped; editors who lack the expertise to evaluate arguments properly; and the fact that journals deliberately restrict themselves to the publication of the "best" papers in order to improve their reputations, rather than because all rejected papers are "bad" science. As well, in moves reminiscent of the music industry over the last 15 years, we have witnessed academic publishers take coprporate and legislative steps to protect their profit margins at the expense of academic freedom, for instance by requiring libaries to purchase "bundles" of journals at great expense, including journals that the libraries do not want, as well as publishers' support of recent legislative moves in Canada and the US to radically strengthen copyright protection at the expense of academic freedom. Against this draconian landscape, however, academics are faced with the reality that our careers, including hiring, promotion, and funding decisions, hinge not only on how much we publish, but where. In this debate, one side will argue in favour of open publishing and highlight current best practices, while the other side will argue in favour of a relatively unchanged status quo. Arguments and rebuttals will be followed by an open discussion with the audience.

title: Seeing the forest on the left and the trees on the right
abstract: Using a novel method that could be applied quite broadly to both normal and neurologically damaged subjects we assess right and left biases in global stimuli across hemifields. In the same experiment, without changes in instruction, we also assess local laterality effects across hemifields and across objects within a hemifield. Findings are as expected in normal participants but with larger effect sizes than previously found in similar studies. A lone neurological patient is presented who shows a unique laterality difference from the healthy controls for local items.

authors_raw: Greg MacLean, Matt Hilchey, & Ray Klein
title: Picking a flavor: Do perceptual processing strategies drown out motoric IOR effects?
abstract: At short cue-target onset asynchronies, irrelevant cues can "capture" attention, resulting in a response benefit when they cue the location of a upcoming target, and incurring a response cost when they do not. However, participants can selectively filter task irrelevant cues if they differ predictably from task-relevant stimuli on some salient dimension such as color (Folk, Remington & Johnston, 1992) or spatial location (Ishigami, Klein, & Christie, 2009). This selective filter, termed an "attentional control setting", has been shown to modulate the early facilitative effects of cueing, but not the inhibitory aftereffects (Often referred to as Inhibition of Return or "IOR"; Pratt & McAuliffe, 2002). In the present study, a manipulation similar to that of Ishigami et al (2009) was used in order to induce a spatial ACS in participants, and responses to central arrow targets were used to measure the cueing effects of irrelevant non-informative cues. Evidence also suggests that IOR can be separated into two semi-distinct "flavors"; one perceptual and the other motoric (Taylor & Klein, 2000) but activation of the motor system does not seem to affect expression of the perceptual flavor (Chica, Taylor, Lupianez & Klein, 2010). By mixing saccadic and manual target trials within blocks and varying the relative probability of those trials it was expected that participants might invoke their perceptual flavor of processing, shutting down the oculomotor system and eliminating saccade-measured IOR. Results are discussed with focus on implications for spatial attentional control and the divergent typologies of IOR.

authors_raw: Sunday A. Bisong & E. E. Osim
title: Preliminary findings on the anti-epileptic and anti-anxiety effects of leaf-extracts of Chinese lantern tree (dichrostachys glomerata) in albino mice
abstract: The leaves of the Chinese lantern tree (Dichrostachys glomerata) are used as a spice and for medicinal purposes in Africa. The use of the leaf-extracts of this plant as an anti-epileptic drug in traditional medicine has left more questions than answers. The aim of this study was to test the anti-epileptic and anti-anxiety effects of the ethanol leaf-extracts of D. glomerata in mice. Ethanol leaf-extracts of D. glomerata (DG) were phytochemically analyzed and lethality studies done. The phytochemical constituents of ethanol leaf-extracts of D. glomerata included 10.60% alkaloids, 0.02% saponins, 2.55% tannins, 4.2% flavonoids, 2.16% cardiac glycosides, traces of moisture, ash, protein and some other reducing compounds and its LD50 was 13.5mg/kg, from which an ED50 of 4.5 mg/kg was calculated. To test anti-epileptic effect of the extract, seizures were induced in 20 mice using 80 mg/kg of penthylene tetrazol (PTZ). Ten mice were given PTZ alone (80mg/kg, i.p.) and the other 10 mice given PTZ 30 minutes after pre-treatment with ethanol leaf-extract of D. glomerata (4.5mg/kg i.p.). The latency to onset of seizures was significantly longer and the duration of seizures was significantly shorter for mice treated with ethanol leaf-extract of D. glomerata + PTZ when compared to the PTZ alone group (p<0.001). The effect of the ethanol leaf-extract of D. glomerata on anxiety-like behaviour associated with epilepsy was studied in another 40 male albino mice, divided into four groups. Mice were either given 0.9% normal saline (0.1ml/10g body weight, i.p. - control), saline + PTZ , ethanol leaf-extracts of D. glomerata (4.5mg/kg i.p.) alone or ethanol leaf-extracts of D. glomerata extract 30 minutes before giving PTZ. Anxiety-like behaviours were scored in the elevated plus maze (EPM), light/dark box (LD) and open field (OF) tests within 1 hour after drug treatment. The percentage time in the open arm of the EPM differed between drug treatments (F(3,36) = 3.872; p<0.05). It was lower in the PTZ treated mice (P< 0.05) than controls, but did not differ in the other groups of mice. The frequency of stretch attend postures (SAP) in the EPM test also differed between treatment groups (F(3,36) = 9.032; p< 0.001). SAPs were higher in the PTZ treated mice than in control (p< 0.001), DG and PTZ + DG treated mice (p< 0.01), but did not differ between control, DG and PTZ + DG treated mice. The number of head dips in the EPM also differed between the treatment groups (F(3,36) = 7.745; p< 0.001). PTZ treated mice did more head dips compared to controls (p< 0.05), DG alone (p< 0.01) and PTZ + DG treated mice (p< 0.01). The number of head dips were also lower in the PTZ + DG treated mice than control (p< 0.001). Percent time in the light in the LD test and the frequency of SAP did not differ between treatments. However, the trend is similar to that observed in the EPM test. The frequency of grooming differed between the treatments (F(3,36) = 2.704). Mice treated with PTZ groomed more than controls (p< 0.01), mice treated with DG (p< 0.05), and PTZ + DG (p< 0.001). Mice treated with PTZ + DG also groomed less than controls (p< 0.05), mice treated with PTZ (p< 0.001) and mice treated with DG (p< 0.01). Centre square entries in the OF test differed between treatments (F(3,36) = 4.596; p< 0.01), with PTZ treated mice making fewer entries than controls (p<0.001). While DG treated mice did not differ from controls in centre square entries, mice treated with PTZ + DG entered less than controls but more than PTZ treated mice (p< 0.05). The time spent freezing differed between treatments (F(3,36) = 6.795; p< 0.01). Mice treated with PTZ froze more than controls, DG and PTZ + DG treated mice (p< 0.01), which did not differ. These results indicate that D. glomerata decreased epileptic seizures and decreased anxiety associated with PTZ-induced epilepsy in mice.

authors_raw: Emily E. Ledue, Michelle Y. Zou, & Nathan A. Crowder
title: Spatiotemporal tuning in mouse primary visual cortex
abstract: Humans can discriminate the speed of a moving visual stimulus; however, how speed is processed by the visual system is not well understood. In primates motion processing occurs most extensively in the middle temporal area, but a portion of complex cells in macaque primary visual cortex (V1) also show speed tuning. Conversely, simple cells respond preferentially to a certain temporal frequency rather than speed. The speed of a sine-wave grating is defined as the ratio of the temporal and spatial frequencies. The mouse has become a popular model of cortical processing due to its ease of use for in vivo imaging and because genetic manipulation of the mouse is well established. Unlike in the macaque, mouse V1 neurons do not appear to show speed tuning and preferred speed changes in proportion to the spatial frequency of a stimulus. To confirm these findings and further evaluate the mouse as an animal model to study motion processing, we used single unit in vivo electrophysiology to measure spatiotemporal tuning in mouse V1. We presented neurons with drifting sine-wave gratings of various spatial and temporal frequency combinations to generate spatiotemporal response functions. We quantitatively assessed the speed tuning of neurons using two methods: 1) we fit our spiking responses with a two-dimensional Guassian function and 2) we performed a partial correlation analysis to determine whether model responses selective for speed or temporal frequency better described our data. Our results substantiate earlier findings that very few neurons in mouse V1 code for stimulus speed.

authors_raw: Leslie Phillmore, Heather MacGillivray, Stephanie Martin, & Ryan Wilson
title: Sex on the brain: do gender and breeding condition affect FoxP2 expression in chickadees?
abstract: Seasonal plasticity in behaviour, such as increased song production in spring, are mirrored by corresponding plasticity in the brain in many songbird species. Regions of the brain important for song, such as HVC, increase in volume when birds are in breeding condition, while specificity of response in NCM and CMM, regions important for song perception, decreases. FoxP2 is a gene associated with language expression and comprehension in humans, and is also important for song learning in songbirds. FoxP2 expression in Area X, a region important for song learning and maintenace, is high during development in male songbirds, while they are learning songs, and fluctuates seasonally in seasonal learners such as canaries, who change their vocal repertoire between breeding seasons. We wanted to examine whether FoxP2 changed in male and female chickadees, a species in which only males reliably sing, but both males and females produce learned vocalizations at different rates across the year, and exhibit plasticity in those vocalizations. We found that in males but not females, HVC volume was larger in breeding than non breeding birds, and that as expected, males had larger HVC than females. We also found no changes in FoxP2 expression across breeding condition, suggesting that levels of FoxP2 in chickadees may not change due to year-round production of learned vocalizations.

authors_raw: Yoko Ishigami, Mary E. MacIsaac, David Shore, Raymond M. Klein, & Gail Eskes
title: Does phasic alerting of neglect patients overcome their spatial deficit in visual awareness?
abstract: Temporal order judgement (TOJ) tasks can be used to measure spatial orienting biases (quantified by the Point of Subjective Simultaneity, PSS) in visual-spatial neglect. Robertson et al. (1998) demonstrated that warning tones could improve the right-sided bias in neglect, suggesting an interaction between the alerting and orienting networks. We attempted to replicate this finding using 2 TOJ tasks that required either a spatial- or colour-discrimination judgment to look at the potential effects of response bias. We tested eight neglect patients and nine controls. Participants indicated which stimulus (left/right or red/blue for the spatial and colour conditions, respectively) was presented first. Warning tones were presented 25% of the time. Two participants with typical right-sided bias without tones showed improvements with tones in the PSS in the spatial condition, replicating Robertson et al. Four participants with paradoxical left-sided bias without tones showed improvement with tones in the PSS in both spatial and colour conditions. These results suggest that alertness manipulation may be effective for some patients, and that effects may depend on directions of initial bias without tones and/or response options.

authors_raw: Stephanie Allen, Mike Lawrence, Daniel Rainham, & Shannon Johnson
title: Directing our attention back to nature: An exploration of the relationship between exposure to nature and attention in typically developing children
abstract: A growing body of research indicates that contact with natural environments positively affects our ability to focus and direct our attention. However, while the media has begun to speculate about the relationship between nature exposure and attention in children, few studies have empirically examined this relationship. Understanding if and how exposure to nature affects attention in children is of particular importance given that demands on children's attention are increasing and time spent in nature is decreasing. Current theory and existing research will be discussed, as will the design of our initial study that will examine the effects of nature exposure on attention in children.

authors_raw: Tara M. Gralnick and Simon Sherry
title: Is Perfectionism a Vulnerability Factor for Depression After Controlling for Neuroticism?
abstract: Extensive evidence suggests neuroticism (i.e., a dispositional tendency to experience negative emotional states) is a higher order personality trait that shares substantial variance with perfectionism dimensions and depressive symptoms (Graham et al., 2010; Sherry et al., 2012). Such evidence raises an important question: are perfectionism dimensions vulnerability factors for depressive symptoms after controlling for neuroticism? The present meta-analysis examined the existing evidence testing if perfectionism dimensions (i.e., self-oriented perfectionism, socially prescribed perfectionism, concern over mistakes, doubts about actions, and perfectionistic attitudes) predict changes in depressive symptoms after controlling for neuroticism. A comprehensive meta-analysis of 10 longitudinal studies involving 1858 participants from undergraduate, community, and psychiatric samples indicated all five perfectionism dimensions significantly predicted changes in depressive symptoms after neuroticism was taken into account. Perfectionism dimensions consistently predicted changes in depressive symptoms across a wide range of samples, methods, and measures. Observed effect sizes were small to moderate in magnitude. Results lend credence to and bring coherence to theoretical accounts, case histories, and empirical studies suggesting perfectionism dimensions are part of the premorbid personality of people vulnerable to depressive symptoms. Results also suggest perfectionism dimensions are unique lower order personality traits that add incrementally to understanding of depressive symptoms above and beyond neuroticism. Perfectionism dimensions appear neither redundant with nor captured by neuroticism. Findings from the present meta-analysis are incongruent with a view of self-oriented perfectionism as an adaptive personality trait associated with positive outcomes. In contrast, results indicate self-oriented perfectionism may represent a form of counterproductive overstriving that confers vulnerability to depressive symptoms. Although self-oriented perfectionism is often labeled as adaptive, such assertions appear premature.

title: Addictive Personalities
abstract: Research has discovered that there are four personality risk factors, as well as other factors such as resilience, that affect how individuals are susceptible to addictions. Addictions, being harmful and dangerous for those affected, are very difficult to treat and yet it is very important to do so. The purpose of this experiment is to determine how individuals with different risk and protection factors respond to a quitting smoking program. Smoking habit and risk factor surveys, as well as piCO measurements, were completed by a sample population of adolescents. The data was then evaluated for correlation values and p-values using statistic software. It was discovered that individuals with high risk personality types (Anxiety Sensitive and Impulsive) and high risk resilience levels as a whole did not respond particularly differently to the quitting smoking program compared to those with lower risk. Individuals with high risk Hopeless Introvert personality type did respond notably less to the program than those with lower scores. It was also discovered that individuals receiving treatment did not reduce their smoking significantly compared to those with no treatment, suggesting the treatment program was not effective in inducing a reduction in smoking habits.

authors_raw: Richard E. Brown, Tim O'Leary, Kurt Stover and Leanne Fraser
title: Searching for dissociations among multiple memory systems in mouse models of Alzheimer's disease
abstract: We have examined age-related changes in learning and memory in different mouse models of Alzheimer's disease (AD). This brief report examines age-related changes in spatial learning and memory (acquisition and 24 hour memory in the Morris water maze reference memory task), procedural learning and memory (accelerating rotarod), and olfactory memory, in male and female 3x-Tg AD and 5XFAD mouse models of AD and their appropriate control strains between 3 and 18 months of age. The 5xFAD mouse has two transgenes; the APP transgene with the Swedish (K670N/M671L), Florida (1716V) and London (V7171) mutations and a presenilin transgene with M146L and L286V mutations, all of which are controlled by the murine Thy-1 promoter. The controls are wild-type littermates. The 3xTg-AD mouse has three transgenes, the human amyloid precursor protein (APPswe), a mutated mouse presenilin-1 (PS1M1461), and a transgene associated with tau pathology (Tau301L). The B6129S1F2 mice are used as controls. Data will look at age, sex and strain differences on each test to examine the effects of the transgenes on different memory systems. Confounding effects of background strain, behavioural measures and housing conditions will be discussed.

authors_raw: Jocelyne C. Whitehead and Richard E. Brown
title: Analysis of Neurobehavioural Development in the 5xFAD Mouse Model of Alzheimer's Disease
abstract: Introduction. The 5xFAD mouse model of familial Alzheimer's disease has two mutant transgenes with five mutations [the Swedish (K670N/M671L), Florida (1716V) and London (V7171) mutations are expressed in the APP transgene, while the presenilin transgene has mutations (M146L) and (L286V)] that are all controlled by the murine Thy-1 promoter. The 5xFAD mouse develops ?-amyloid plaques by 2 months of age, with synapse loss and deficits in long-term potentiation, spatial learning and memory and contextual fear memory at 4 months of age. The phenotypes expressed in the 5xFAD mouse model are consistent with symptoms of FAD patients. Since these genes are expressed at an early age, it is possible that the 5xFAD mice differ in their early development from littermate controls. Objectives. We therefore used a test battery to examine neurobehavioural development of male and female 5xFAD and wild-type littermates from postnatal day 2 to 24. Methods. The test battery assessed developmental milestones, sensory-motor reflexes, motor and cognitive abilities using behavioural and reflex tests that are reliable measures of the development of the central nervous system. Results. There were no differences between pup genotypes on any of the developmental tests. There were, however some sex differences as males weighed more than females, opened their eyes earlier and developed the hind-limb grasp reflex earlier than females. Males also reached the home cage bedding faster than female pups during the homing test. Female pups performed better than males in the forelimb grip strength test, which may be due to their lower body weight. Female transgenic pups weighed less than female wild-type pups, and did not habituate to a novel object. This suggests that the transgene may be affecting pup development in a sex-dependent manner. Maternal behaviour was observed from postnatal day 1 to 15 in mothers whose pups were included in the neurodevelopmental test battery and mothers whose pups remained undisturbed. No differences in levels of maternal care were found, indicating that testing the pups did not reduce maternal behaviour. Conclusion. This is the first study to examine ontogeny in the 5xFAD mouse model of Alzheimer's disease and the results indicate that the transgenes do not affect pre-weaning development in these mice.

authors_raw: Jasmyn Cunningham, Yaeesh Sardiwalla, Denise Bernier and Benjamin Rusak
title: Tossing and turning: the effects of overnight sleep restriction on women with unipolar depression
abstract:
It has been firmly established that mood in approximately fifty percent of depressed patients improves after being subjected to sleep restriction. As pharmacological and cognitive treatments for depression are known to be limited in effectiveness, a greater understanding of the mechanisms behind positive response to sleep restriction could lead to novel, individualized treatments. This pilot study aimed to better understand the effects of sleep restriction in responders (participants whose moods respond positively to overnight sleep restriction), as well as the correlation between baseline diurnal variation and responsiveness to sleep restriction. This was done through the analysis of self-reported mood and chronotype questionnaires filled in before, during, and after overnight sleep restriction by both healthy controls and depressed participants. In addition, the study monitored the levels of activity of the participants using a wrist actigraph for the entirety of the study period. Results showed no overall responders to sleep restriction, however several participants were negative responders to certain items of the Hamilton Depression Inventory (mood symptoms worsened). No conclusions could be made regarding links between baseline diurnal variation and response to sleep restriction. Depressed subjects showed reduced levels of activity prior to sleep restriction (baseline) compared to control subjects. The levels of activity during sleep restriction in this study do not show a positive percentage change for any participant, merely less negative percentage changes in certain individuals. The main limitation of the study was the very small sample size, but it would be quite feasible to conduct further study in this area with several modifications.
Friday's Posters

authors_raw: Franziska Kinzel, Yoko Ishigami, and Gail Eskes
title: Can alerting cues enhance prism adaptation?
abstract:
The identification of appropriate and effective interventions for spatial neglect is critical for improving outcomes and overall quality of life post-stroke. One promising rehabilitation technique is prism adaptation (PA). PA involves adapting to goggles fitted with prisms that shift the visual field rightward by pointing at a target. After goggle removal, a leftward shift of orienting is observed. However, PA effects are inconsistent across studies and outcome tasks, possibly due to the multidimensional nature of the impairments (i.e. both orienting and vigilance/sustained attention deficits) underlying neglect. For example, alerting cues can target sustained attention deficits and directly improve orienting in neglect.
Therefore, the purpose of this research was to examine the impact of combining alerting cues with PA in healthy controls, as a first step in determining the feasibility of this technique in patients with spatial neglect. Method: In two studies, healthy young participants (n=60) were randomly assigned to one of 3 conditions: PA only, PA + alerting tones on 25% of trials (PA+alerting), or sham prism group. Outcome measures included 2 Subjective Midline Tasks (SMTs), Proprioceptive Straight Ahead pointing (PSA: midline pointing with eyes closed) and Visual Straight Ahead pointing (VSA: visually guided midline target pointing), as well as Visual Search (VS). The SMTs were administered immediately after PA and after a delay of 30 minutes. During the adaptation period, participants pointed toward line targets while wearing sham (clear) or prismatic goggles that shifted the visual field rightwards. Auditory alerting cues (400 Hz, 89 db) were presented 300-1100 ms before the target in the PA+alerting. Results: Expected aftereffects were obtained in all the PA conditions on the SMTs in terms of leftward deviations in pointing, but not on VS. The impact of alerting depended on the outcome test. In SSA, alerting tones decreased the after-effects at the 30 minutes delay (3.3 vs 2.5 degrees, p< .05, PA vs PA +alerting, respectively). In VSA, alerting tones increased after-effects at both immediate and delayed test (7.4 vs 8.4 degrees and 5.1 vs 5.7 degrees, p's< .05; PA vs PA +alerting, respectively). Conclusions: The impact of alerting tones is not always positive and may depend on similarities between exposure task and outcome tasks. Adding alerting tones to PA as a rehabilitation technique may potentially improve some functional outcomes for patients with spatial neglect.

authors_raw: Stephanie A. H. Jones, Beverly Butler, Raymond Klein, Gail Eskes
title: Measuring the components of attention in the young healthy adult population
abstract: Behavioural and neurological research has supported a three-component model of attention whereby separate, but interacting, brain networks are responsible for alerting/vigilance, orienting and executive attentional abilities (Posner & Petersen, 1990). Alerting or vigilance refers to our ability to maintain a state of arousal over time, orienting involves directing our attention to a particular task, location or stimulus, and executive attention includes the higher order conscious processing required to use the detected information (e.g. decisions and memory). These networks are commonly impaired in many brain diseases. As correct measurement of attentional abilities is fundamental to the goal of understanding and treating attention deficits, we developed a theoretically-driven computerized test battery for measuring attentional abilities, presumably reflecting these networks: the Dalhousie Computerized Attention Assessment Battery (DalCAB). Ninety-two healthy participants from 18 - 30 years of age completed the automated battery on two separate occasions. The purpose of the present study was to examine the factor structure of DalCAB in the healthy young adult population. Specifically, we sought to determine if these data fit the proposed three network model of attention using exploratory factor analysis (EFA). EFAs of nineteen reaction time sub-scores and five accuracy sub-scores (for a total of 24 variables) will be conducted. Results will be discussed relative to the three component model of attention.

authors_raw: Joshua P. Salmon, Graham C. Wilson, Raymond M. Klein, & Gail A. Eskes
title: A step towards building better video "games" for rehabilitation: A survey investigating entertainment preferences in adults
abstract: As the healthcare system becomes increasingly burdened with demands for rehabilitation services, the need for accessible therapies is growing. Video game approaches offer a promising supplement to existing therapy programs. Research has shown that video games can improve such cognitive functions as memory, processing speed, executive function and attention in both young and older adults. The current challenge however, is finding (or designing) video game approaches that simultaneously match the therapeutic needs of the patient population while remaining fun and engaging. As part of a larger research program, to develop better rehabilitation software or "games" for patients recovering from Stroke or Parkinson's disease, the current study conducted a nation-wide survey of adult entertainment preferences. Measures of favorite television, book, film and movie genres were also collected, as well as information about frequency of use of various technologies, devices and video games. The results from this research offer a window into how adults spend their time with technology, and what approaches in designing new software for older adults might be most effective. The ultimate goal of this research is to apply this information to design more engaging game-like therapies that will encourage and motivate patient rehabilitation.

authors_raw: Sally Carver, Laura Ovens, Dennis P. Phillips
title: Auditory Temporal Processing Across the Lifespan: Gap Detection and Temporal Regularity
abstract: As people age, many tend to 'lose' their hearing (presbyacusis). Historically, the hearing science community thought that presbyacusis was attributable entirely to peripheral deficits. The more recent view is that there is also a central auditory nervous system deficit, specifically a temporal processing deficit that cannot be explained by an absolute sensitivity loss. The purpose of the present study was to investigate the nature of those 'central' auditory processing deficits while controlling for peripheral hearing loss using a multiple regression analysis, and to do so in both musicians and non-musicians. Our preliminary findings suggest that there is little negative effect of age on any of four temporal processing tasks when absolute sensitivity to sound is controlled for. Our data also hint that musicians might actually improve in their temporal processing capabilities with age. Our data support the view that lifelong musical practice might protect against age-related impairments in temporal processing.

authors_raw: Richard E. Brown, Zoltán Molnár, Damian Young, John Mason and Pietro Corsi
title: Creating a Living History of Neuroscience
abstract: Much of the history of neuroscience is being lost or discarded. We have instituted a plan to save and use historical objects for teaching and research. We started with a wooden box of glass microscope slides belonging to Sir Charles Sherrington and labelled "Histology Demonstration Slides" found in the University Laboratory of Physiology at the University of Oxford, and have now included glass slides from Le Gros Clark and historical equipment. We have identified a number of slides used in publications by Sherrington and Le Gros Clark and equipment used in classical experiments in the history of neuroscience at Oxford. Of particular interest is the slide box of Sherrington. This box contains 21 drawers of slides from Sherrington's years at St Thomas Hospital (1888-1895), Liverpool University (1896-1914) and Oxford University (1914-1935). It also contains slides presented to him by other leading contemporary neuroscientists (Ruffini, Brown, Saunderson, Fritsch). We recognized a slide of intestine from a Cholera case (Sherrington 1887, Proc Royal Soc, 42, 474). Slides of blood (amphibian, dog, human) and preparations from bone marrow indicate that Sherrington maintained his interest in haemathology and microbiology after working in the laboratories of Virchow and Koch. There are numerous serial sections through normal peripheral nerves and nerves in which the sensory or the motor roots were sectioned and stained after degeneration. There are preparations where nerves can be followed to muscle spindles (cat), but there are also slides where no spindles can be found at all (e.g. in the eye muscle of a monkey). The collection includes 11 spectacular slides from Gustav Fritsch from 1891. These include slides from Fritsch's 1881 publication "Vergleichend-anatomische Betrachtung der elektrischen Organe von Gymnotus electricus" Veit, Leipzig. These slides are accompanied by similar preparations on electric organs of fish made by Sherrington. We are documenting these objects on a website [HYPERLINK "https://learntech.imsu.ox.ac.uk/historyofmedsci/"https://learntech.imsu.ox.ac.uk/historyofmedsci/] which can be used to integrate historical information into teaching and research programs. This poster demonstrates new ways of presenting historical research in neuroscience for teaching and research.

title: Introduction
abstract: A historical overview of the people and events in our department from at least 1970. This year I will focus on the contributions of Jeannette McGlone and Heather Schellinck, who will retire this year.

title: Physical and Mental Fatigue
abstract: Fatigue happens either during a prolonged time or with sustained efforts. Pathological fatigue is a symptom that has been identified in many neurological conditions, such as multiple sclerosis (MS) and stroke. Despite of the prevalence of fatigue, the underlying mechanism of this phenomenon has not yet been revealed. To date, most of fatigue research used a dichotomous definition of fatigue based on one's subjective perception and behavioural performance. This could be helpful in general clinical practices, but it seemed insufficient to explain the nature of fatigue since the subjective and behavioural measures often do not correlate. The fatigue-related brain activity was another route to integrate different measurements and to formulate a unitary theory of fatigue. The neurophysiological measures of physical fatigue attributed the decline of motor output to peripheral, spinal, and supraspinal origins. Two models have been proposed to explain the proportion of fatigue due to a suboptimal brain function -- the homeostasis hypothesis and the central fatigue hypothesis. Each of them proposed a neural mechanism for fatigue: the imbalance between facilitatory and inhibitory processes (e.g. cognitive control vs. sensory inhibition) or the alteration in the striato-thalamo-frontal feedback loop. Both models emphasized the role of frontal lobe in physical fatigue, where most structures have been associated with cognitive functions, such as goal maintenance, inhibition, and conflict reconciliation. The role of frontal lobe in fatigue can be illustrated by the gradual decline of frontal-related cognitive function, such as inhibition, during effortful physical or cognitive task.

title: The influence of working memory load and task modality on the Simon effect Andrew Clouter
abstract:
In a typical Simon task, participants make task-relevant spatial responses that differ in spatial location (e.g., different response keys) to stimuli with task-irrelevant spatial locations. For example, a participant may have to press a right key for blue squares and a left key for red squares; the location of the squares is task-irrelevant. The Simon effect refers to the finding that responses are both slower and less accurate when the spatial location of the stimulus and that of the response key are incongruent. As a result of the conflict between the task-irrelevant spatial location of the stimulus and the relevant spatial location of the response, Simon tasks are commonly used as measures of executive attention, as Simon tasks allow a measure of the ability of participants to resolve the conflict that is generated.
Executive attention and working memory are seen increasingly as overlapping cognitive constructs. In fact, one model of working memory suggests that working memory is a combination of short-term memory and attentional control.
Some studies suggest that higher working memory loads reduce attentional control when the contents of the working memory task and the task-irrelevant content overlap. One may suppose that in the spatial tasks, where both the spatial location of the task-relevant stimuli and the spatial response mapping are encoded in working memory, a higher working memory load may reduce the Simon effect.
A common finding in studies of the Simon effect is that the effect generally decreases with higher response times. One explanation for this finding is that a spatial stimulus activates a tendency to respond in the direction of that stimulus. Thus, when the spatial location of the stimulus and the appropriate response key are incongruent, response times are increased. However, the tendency to respond in the direction of the stimulus decays over time.
To address these issues, in this study we manipulated working memory load (0-back and 2-back) and task type (spatial and verbal working memory tasks).
Results suggest Simon effect differences in the two working memory load conditions. Simon effects were significant in the low-load condition, but not in the high-load condition. In addition, Simon effects differed in the two tasks. The verbal tasks show a Simon effect that decreases with higher reaction times, while the spatial tasks show the opposite trend. Analysis of the effect of working memory load by task type shows that the spatial tasks show a Simon effect that increases with higher reaction times in both load conditions; the verbal task shows the expected decrease in the effect with higher reaction times in the low-load, but not the high-load, condition. An important lesson to be drawn from this analysis is to examine the effect of interest over the response time distribution; the Simon effect differences by task would not have been apparent here otherwise.

title: T4) Impact of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Metabolic Effects of Sleep Restriction
abstract: There is extensive evidence that spontaneous short sleep and imposed sleep restriction are linked to altered metabolic and endocrine function. Sleep restriction has been associated, for example, with increased cortisol and ghrelin levels, decreased leptin levels and increased appetite, especially for carbohydrate- and fat-rich foods. While most of these studies have been conducted using male participants, a few recent studies have confirmed some of these findings in women. Parallel laboratory and epidemiological studies have shown a link between chronic short sleep and body weight gain. A retrospective analysis of results from one study in women suggested that menstrual phase was a strong modulator of these metabolic effects of sleep loss. In the current study, we recruited young healthy females of reproductive age (n=18; Mean age: 22.4; Mean BMI: 21.7) to examine whether menstrual cycle phase modulates the impact of sleep restriction on levels of the metabolic hormones cortisol and leptin. A secondary goal is to examine how sleep loss affects self-reports of mood, hunger and food preferences, and whether menstrual cycle phase also modulates these effects.

title: Synaptic circuits in the lobula of the fly's visual system
abstract:
I have conducted a neuroanatomical approach to reveal functional circuits in the visual system of the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster, especially focusing on a neuropil called the lobula, the highest-order component of the optic lobe. The fly receives light stimuli at the compound eyes. Visual information is subsequently processed in the primary visual centre of the brain, or the optic lobe. The optic lobe comprises four neuropils including the lamina, medulla, lobula and lobula plate. The lobula receives inputs mainly from the medulla, and sends outputs to the lobula plate as well as to the higher-order visual centres in the central brain. Recent behavioural and live-imaging studies have revealed some lobula neurons signal features of colour, movement and shape. Although these neurons have been identified using light microscopy, low-resolution images with light microscopy are not sufficient to investigate synaptic connections of these cells. Therefore, in this study, I recruited serial-section transmission electron microscopy (ssTEM) to analyse neuronal morphology and synaptic connections of the lobula in a higher-resolution.
Relay neurons from the medulla, transmedulla (Tm) cells, account for most cell types connecting the medulla and lobula. Among them, Tm1, Tm2 and Tm9 cells have presynaptic terminals in the first (Lo1) and second (Lo2) strata of the lobula. First I reconstructed these terminals and confirmed their columnar distributions. Lo1 also contains dense arbors arising from T5 cells. T5 cells provide outputs to the lobula plate tangential cells (LPTC), which are known to signal motion. To trace T5's highly branched arbors, I used Gal4-driven membrane-targeted horseradish peroxidase (HRP) with a T4/T5 driver. T5 turned out to receive inputs from all the Tm1, Tm2 and Tm9 terminals, as well as other Tm subtypes which terminate deeper in the lobula. Previous anatomical, functional and behavioral studies reveal that Tm1 and Tm2 are responsible for motion and shape detection, while Tm9 is associated with color discrimination. The result indicates that T5 not only conveys motion information but also some aspect of spectral information to the lobula plate. Other neurons and pathways, including colour-processing pathways, will be focused using both unlabeled and HRP-labeled ssTEM section series.

title: Genito-pelvic pain during pregnancy and postpartum: The prospective impact of pain-related anxiety and hypervigilance to pain
abstract: Background. Genital and pelvic (genito-pelvic) pain is common at three months postpartum (Barrett et al., 1999). However, there is limited knowledge regarding the course and specific symptom profile (e.g., onset, location) of this pain in pregnancy and after childbirth. Further, pain-related anxiety and hypervigilance to pain have been associated with increased genital pain intensity in women, unrelated to childbirth (Desrochers et al., 2009). These relationships have not been examined in postpartum populations, or prospectively. Research Questions. The following research questions were examined: (1) What is the prevalence and symptom profile of genito-pelvic pain during pregnancy and at three months postpartum? (2) What is the impact of pain-related anxiety and hypervigilance to pain in pregnancy on genito-pelvic pain quality and intensity at three months postpartum? Method. One hundred forty-three first-time expectant mothers completed measures of pain-related anxiety and hypervigilance to pain. Those reporting genito-pelvic pain in pregnancy and/or at three months postpartum completed measures of the quality and average intensity of their genito-pelvic pain. Results. Of 143 women, 70 (49%) reported genito-pelvic pain in pregnancy (88% reported developing the pain during pregnancy). Genital pain was reported in 21% of women, pelvic pain in 24%, and both genital and pelvic pain in 47%. At three months postpartum, 29 (27%) women reported genito-pelvic pain. The pain resolved for 30/70 (43%) women, persisted for 21/70 (30%), and 8/29 (28%) women reported a new onset of genito-pelvic pain after childbirth. At postpartum, 52% of women reported genital pain, 22% pelvic pain, and 26% both genital and pelvic pain. Controlling for breastfeeding, higher pain-related anxiety and hypervigilance to pain during pregnancy accounted for 40% and 33% of the variance in the quality and average genito-pelvic pain intensity at three months postpartum, respectively. Only pain-related anxiety was an independent predictor for both the quality, β = .43, p = .06 (trend) and intensity, β = .47, p = .04, of the pain. Conclusions. Results suggest that about half of women may develop genito-pelvic pain during pregnancy, but that this pain will resolve for a majority, persist for about a third, and a significant subset of women will newly develop pain after childbirth. Pain-related anxiety in pregnancy may increase the risk for greater postpartum genito-pelvic pain. An improved understanding of the course and predictors of genito-pelvic pain could help health care providers identify and offer early intervention to women that are at risk for experiencing this pain postpartum.

title: We don't lose it all as we age: Filtering visual distraction across the lifespan
abstract: Background: Working Memory (WM) is used to hold and manipulate information in an active, short-term store while performing a range of daily tasks such as reading, mental arithmetic and problem solving. These tasks must be completed in the face of distractors, which appear to become increasing difficult to ignore as we age (e.g. Gazzaley et al., 2005). The purpose of this study was to investigate the effect of distractors on both the maintenance and manipulation components of WM performance. Method: 20 healthy younger and 20 healthy older adults completed a series of n-back WM tasks designed to assess the effects of task-irrelevant distractors on WM performance. Analysis of reaction time (RT) data and error rates (ER) was conducted using mixed effects modeling using Age Group (young vs older), Load (1 vs 2 targets), Task Type (maintenance vs manipulation) and Distraction (present vs not present) treated as fixed effects. Hypotheses: We predicted that WM performance (ER and RT) would be most disrupted by distractors when the task required continuous manipulation of 2 targets in the active short-term store at and that older adults would be more adversely affected by the presence of distractors than would younger adults. Results: ER and RT were adversely affected in both young and older adults as Load increased and as the Task Type switched from a maintenance to a manipulation task and this effect was more pronounced in older adults. Older adults were no more affected by the presence of distractors than were younger adults and the addition of a second target during the manipulation task resulted in reduced distractor effects. Conclusion: It may be the case that, as a way of coping with distraction, particularly when the primary task is difficult, individuals apply a more stringent attentional control setting, thereby limiting the amount of information that can enter WM. Contrary to our predictions these data also suggest that this ability is maintained in healthy aging. Differences between current results and the existing literature will be discussed.

authors_raw: Antoine Tremblay, Anne Johnson, Elissa Asp, Tim Bardouille, Malgorzata Zarzycka and Aaron J. Newman
title: More Than Just Words: A Crowded Mental Dictionary
abstract: We attempt to gain better insight into the question of what is stored in our mental dictionary and what is put together from smaller parts. Many linguistic theories posit a separation between a system that stores individual words and their meanings (a lexicon) and a system of rules for combining lexical items into phrases and sentences (a grammar). However, we have previously found behavioural evidence that multi-word sequences that frequently occur together (e.g., in the middle of) may in fact be stored as \chunks" in the lexicon rather than being computed on-line from grammatical rules every time they are produced. Using the magneto-encephalography (MEG), independent components analysis (ICA), hierarchical clustering, and generalized additive mixed-effects modeling (GAMM), we characterized the effects of the frequency of use of single words (this; man; looks; like), bigrams (this man; man looks; looks like), trigrams (this man looks; man looks like), and quadgrams (this man looks like) on brain activity in specific brain regions during the pre-production stage of unconstrained overt picture description, in adults over a range of ages and educational backgrounds. Our findings suggest that (1) very low to low frequency bigrams, trigrams, and quadgrams may be computed, more or less quickly, from smaller parts, and (2) very high frequency bigrams, trigrams, and quadgrams may coalesce into single units, similarly to words such as dog, strawberry, and bicycle (i.e., they may be stored as wholes rather than computed from smaller units).

authors_raw: Austin C. Korgan, Amanda D. Green, Michael J. Esser and Tara S. Perrot
title: Effects of environmental enrichment on the HPA-axis in a convulsive rat model
abstract: Environmental enrichment (EE) has been associated with decreased vulnerability to neurological and psychological disorders in several species. Following prenatal stress, EE can reduce anxiolytic behavior and restore normal hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal (HPA)-axis functioning. One approach to restoring this functioning is through the regulation of corticotropin releasing hormone (CRH). CRH has been shown to be a powerful convulsant in animal models and is up regulated following febrile seizures in humans. In this study, we subjected pregnant dams to daily predator exposure stress from gestational day 13-20. Following birth, subsets of these dams and their pups were housed in a larger, 'enriched' environment. On postnatal day 14, rat pups were given an injection of lipopolysaccharide, followed by a subthreshold dose of kainic acid.
