Behavior recordings of the ensuing seizures allowed us to classify them based on severity (mild, moderate, or severe). Perfusions and blood collections the following day allowed us to observe CRH-immunoreactivity (-ir) in the hypothalamic paraventricular nucleus, as well as measure plasma corticosterone (cort) levels. Overall, EE decreased CRH levels in all pups, independent of seizure status. Post hoc analysis revealed a trend between plasma cort levels and pre- and post-natal conditions in pups experiencing moderate seizure. Correlations among plasma cort levels, CRH-ir, birth weight, and total seizure score for each group suggests differential HPA-axis function dependent on early life exposure.

authors_raw: Namrata Joshi, Ron Leslie and Tara Perrot
title: Effect of a combination of isolation rearing and predator odour stress on measures of startle in male and female rats
abstract: The purpose of our work is to examine the effects of adolescent stress exposure on prefrontal cortex (PFC) function by examining pre-pulse inhibition (PPI), startle amplitude (SA) and habituation using a novel model of stress in rats which combines isolation rearing with repeated exposure to predator odor. Our previous work established that repeated predator odour stress alone during adolescence does not significantly affect these measures (unpublished data). In the present study, Long-Evans rats of both sexes were given a combination of isolation rearing and repeated predator odour stress to determine if the removal of the social support offered by a cagemate could result in reducing the animals' resilience to odour stress. Animals were isolated for most of the adolescent period (PND28-54); six odour exposures were given during this period (on PND34, 38, 43, 47,53 and 69). Measures of SA and PPI were assessed repeatedly throughout adolescence and early adulthood. Preliminary analysis of the data has revealed a reduction in PPI following exposure to a combination of isolation and predator odour exposure. Females showed a transient effect of stress that emerged during adolescence itself, while among males, stress reduced PPI only after the final exposure in early adulthood.

title: Choices mothers make: Tradeoffs of reproductive decision-making in crickets
abstract: Reproductive decision-making is important across taxa, and post-fertilization decisions, such as oviposition-site selection (OS), can have profound effects on parent and offspring fitness. Egg-laying females must weigh their own needs (e.g., predation avoidance or foraging) against those of their offspring (e.g., a suitable developmental environment), which can lead to tradeoffs among these factors. We used several experiments with the Texas field cricket (Gryllus texensis) to examine how females navigate such tradeoffs during OS. Specifically, we investigated tradeoffs associated with predation risk. For example, does increased predation risk alter females' thermal preferences for OS or result in reduced egg-laying? We also manipulated food availability to examine a tradeoff between feeding and egg-laying and to determine whether food limitation promotes risky OS. Last, we manipulated the number of oviposition sites to investigate if females "put all of their eggs into one basket", which likely benefits females, or if they prefer to oviposit into multiple sites, which may benefit offspring. We discuss our results and show that widespread environmental factors (e.g., food availability, temperature, and predation risk) can exert complex interactive effects on important reproductive decisions.

authors_raw: Chelsea K. Quinlan and Tracy L. Taylor
title: Enhancing the Production Effect in Memory
abstract: The current literature demonstrates that reading aloud is the most effective form of production; however, the distinctiveness account suggests that other forms of vocal production that include an additional distinct element should produce an even greater production effect. In three experiments, we found that reading items aloud loudly and singing items at study resulted in a greater production effect than reading items aloud in a normal voice, with the largest benefit for singing. Our findings support the distinctiveness hypothesis by demonstrating that other forms of production can have a more pronounced effect on subsequent memory than reading aloud at study.

title: Implicit Sequence Learning in Normally Hearing Adults: Implications for Predicting Success with Cochlear Implants
abstract: Cochlear implants (CIs) are a type of medical prosthesis that requires extensive learning to use effectively, and success rates vary widely in both adults and children. Importantly, the variables predicting success with CIs have yet to be determined, although researchers have investigated many possible predictors. One cognitive ability that may relate to success with CIs is the ability to learn and remember sequential patterns of information. Conway et al. (2010) found that implicit sequence learning ability is related to language processing abilities under challenging acoustic conditions. Specifically, they found that participants' ability to implicitly learn sequences that were based on an artificial grammar (compared to their ability to learn ungrammatical sequences) was positively correlated with their ability to understand speech in noise. The current study proposes to build on Conway et al.'s (2010) study by using fMRI technology to examine the relationship between the neural processes underlying implicit sequence learning in normally hearing adults and their ability to perceive speech in noise. A larger overall study will examine these processes in deaf CI candidates and examine relationships between their pre-implant cognitive abilities and post-implant language outcomes.

authors_raw: Thérèse M. Chevalier, S. Helene Deacon, Rauno Parrila, Krista Ritchie
title: Which reading, learning, and organizational strategies are associated with successful academic performance in students with a history of reading difficulties?
abstract: Research has shown that individuals who report a history of reading difficulties have similar reading performance to those with diagnosed reading disabilities, and both groups have more difficulty than their peers in adjusting to the demands of reading and writing at a university level. Despite academic challenges, these students are pursuing post-secondary education more frequently than in the past. Through a joint project with Helene Deacon's lab and Student Services at Dalhousie University, all incoming students in the fall of 2010 were invited to complete self report surveys designed to identify students with a history of learning difficulties and to evaluate their use of reading, learning, and organizational strategies. Based on the academic achievement of the 473 students who completed the surveys, this study will identify which strategies are most highly associated with academic success in students with and without a history of reading difficulties. Preliminary results will be presented.

authors_raw: Cameron D. Hassall and Olav E. Krigolson
title: Wake up and smell the shifting probabilistic outcomes
abstract: Real-world decision making can be challenging due, in part, to changes in an underlying reward structure: what was the best option last week might be less rewarding today. Here, we discuss our ability to adapt to shifting stimulus-response-outcome rules in the context of a gambling game known as a "two-armed bandit". Participants made gambles in simulated environments known to be either certain (an "honest" casino) or uncertain (a "dishonest" casino). Our electroencephalographic and behavioural data suggest that humans are able to adapt to subtle changes in underlying reward structures, especially when given cues as to which environment they are in. Finally, we suggest that reward-processing areas within medial-frontal cortex may be partially responsible for detecting (and adapting to) these shifting probabilistic outcomes.

authors_raw: Sarah Dolan, Tracy Taylor, and Jonathan Fawcett
title: Controlling Prosody in Directed Forgetting of Speech Errors
abstract: Past research has shown that corrections made by a speaker following commission of an error (e.g. "oh no, I meant…") permit the listener to intentionally forget the erroneous information while improving memory for the correction itself. However, prosodic cues such as emphasis of the corrective statement could be responsible for the improvement observed for corrections. To explore this possibility, we transcribed and recorded five-minute popular scientific podcasts using monotonic computer-generated voices, which contained no prosodic cues. Each podcast contained occasional factual errors followed by an immediate self-correction. After listening to the clips, participants were given a surprise test of recognition memory for erroneous, corrective, and reference statements. Unlike in past research, performance for erroneous and corrective statements were both below performance for reference statements; errors and corrections did not differ. We propose that this discrepancy resulted from the lack of prosodic elements in our stimuli, which, in natural speech, differentiate errors and corrections.

title: Emotion Regulation: Global Construct or Valence-Dependent Skill?
abstract:
Emotion regulation, described for our purposes as the process by which individuals control overt emotional displays, has received increasing attention over recent years. However, the majority of this research has focused on children's ability to control negative emotions, and investigations of positive emotion regulation have been relatively lacking. The current study aimed to relate 5- to 10-year-olds' regulation of negative emotions (namely disappointment) with their ability to regulate positive emotions (happiness/excitement) to examine whether emotion regulation is robust across contexts or whether it fluctuates depending on situational demands (i.e., regulation of positive vs. negative emotions). A secondary objective involved comparing cognitive and socioemotional correlates of positive and negative emotion regulation.
To measure regulation of negative emotions, we used a disappointing gift paradigm in which children received an attractive gift bag containing a disappointing gift (a plastic cup). To examine regulation of positive emotions, a novel measure of positive emotion regulation was devised in which children had to cease playing an exciting game to help the experimenter clean up a staged accident. Correlational analyses revealed a significant negative correlation between regulation of negative emotions in the gift paradigm and regulation of positive behaviors during the staged accident. In other words, children who best regulated negative emotions appeared to experience more difficulty regulating positive emotions, and vice versa. These findings suggest that emotion regulation is not a generic skill, but one that varies systematically depending on the valence of the elicited emotion. In addition, children's positive and negative emotion regulation correlated differently with measures of theory of mind and inhibitory control, adding strength to the notion of emotion regulation as a valence-dependent skill.

authors_raw: Jonathan Oore, Leanne Fraser and Richard Brown
title: Age-related changes in motor ability and motor learning in triple transgenic (3xTg-AD) and control (B6129SF1/J) mice on the accelerating Rotarod
abstract: The motor performance of the triple transgenic (3xTg-AD) mouse model of Alzheimer's disease (AD) is still largely uncharacterized. Male and female 3xTg-AD and control (B6129SF1/J) mice were tested in a cross-sectional design at 2-, 6-, 9-, 12-, and 15-months of age on the accelerating Rotarod to look at motor ability and motor learning. At all ages, transgenic mice had a steeper motor learning curve and performed better on each day than controls. Female mice showed increased motor ability compared to males, while male mice had a steeper learning curve than female mice. Age on its own did not have a meaningful effect on Rotarod performance, though age and genotype were found to interact; however, the precise trend is not clear. It was concluded that the behavioural differences seen in the 3xTg mice may shed light on the neuropathological development of AD.

title: A randomized controlled trial of a novel motivation-matched treatment for problem gamblers: "BEAT Gambling"
abstract: Our previous work has suggested that subtyping gamblers according to their underlying motivations for gambling has clinical utility in terms of predicting gambling behavior and problems. We recently developed a novel 6-session treatment for problem gamblers, called "BEAT gambling" (Brief Escape and Action Treatment for Gamblers), in which distinct interventions are provided to meet the needs of Escape (i.e., coping-motivated) vs. Action (i.e., enhancement-motivated) problem gamblers. We conducted a randomized controlled trial where 70 problem gamblers (25 Action; 45 Escape) were randomly assigned to either the BEAT gambling intervention or to a Treatment as Usual (TAU) control provided by problem gambling therapists in the community. Both treatments led to significant improvements on a variety of gambling and associated mental health outcomes. Consistent with hypotheses, our novel BEAT gambling intervention was superior to TAU on several outcomes including gambling frequency, readiness to change, gambling craving, problem gambling "caseness", and treatment retention.

title: The Effects of Alcohol and Energy Drink Co-Administration: Preliminary Results from a Randomized-Controlled Study.
abstract: Introduction: Mixing alcohol and energy drinks has become increasingly popular among young adults. Given the novelty of this trend, only a small body of research presently exists. There appears to be an academic division as to whether or not it is safe to consume these two substances in tandem. Of the research that exists, it appears that mixing alcohol with energy drinks can lead to negative repercussions, including increases in risk-taking behavior and increased alcohol intake. Furthermore, research suggest that users may have an altered perception (i.e. underestimation) of how intoxicated they are, leading to poor decision-making. Objectives: The present study wished to address three questions. (1) Do energy drinks cause a subsequent increase in alcohol intake? (2) Do energy drinks affect latency to administer alcohol. (3) Do energy drinks cause an alteration in the subjective reporting of mood. Hypothesis: We predicted that energy drinks would cause an increase in alcohol intake as well as a decrease in latency to administer a first drink of alcohol, relative to caffeine only and control conditions. We predicted an analogous effect on the subjective measures (SRS & BAES) we assessed. Method/Results: In a randomized double-blind study using a validated measure of human self-administration, it was found that energy drinks caused a significant increase in total alcohol intake relative to an inert substance. Furthermore, it was determined that a beverage containing only caffeine caused a significant decrease in the latency to obtain alcohol, relative to an inert substance. No significant effect of condition was found on the subjective reporting of various mood descriptors. Conclusion: This study hopes to give preliminary insight into the physiological and behavioral processes which effect alcohol consumption when co-used with energy drinks.

authors_raw: Amelia Moffatt, Denise Lewis, Mackenzie Armstrong, Leonie Balter, Hera Schlagintweit, Sean Barrett and Kim Good
title: Subjective craving measures and neural correlates associated with tobacco cue reactivity in former, current and never-smokers
abstract:
Through classical conditioning, smoking-related cues become strongly associated with the reinforcing effects of tobacco and elicit craving behavior in addicted smokers. These cue-induced cravings have been shown to activate the mesolimbic dopamine reward circuit. Individuals who have demonstrated the ability to cease smoking (former smokers) are a neglected research group. It is unclear what are the effects of smoking cues are on those individuals who were once strongly dependent on cigarettes.
The current study aimed to examine both the neural activity and subjective cravings in response to smoking-related cues in former, current and never-smokers. Blood oxygen level dependent functional magnetic resonance imaging (BOLD fMRI), using an observation-response task, was employed. During the FMRI scan, subjects were required to watch two stimulus videos (one with neutral cues, the other with smoking-related cues), and respond to craving and mood questionnaires following each stimulus video.
Our behavioral data revealed that current smokers had significantly higher craving levels following smoking cue presentation, compared to our never and former smokers. The neuroimaging data showed that current smokers displayed significant brain activity within the mesolimbic reward circuit during smoking cue presentation. Former smokers demonstrated no appreciable activity in the reward pathway; however, did show increased activity in prefrontal brain regions. These results suggest that despite former smokers showing little craving behavior, the regions of the brain required for top down processing (willpower), may be engaged in these individuals.

authors_raw: Kristina Taylor, Anne Sophie Champod, Gail A. Eskes
title: Attention and Gaming: Development and validation of a game-like prism adaptation procedure in healthy participants
abstract: Prism adaptation is a promising rehabilitation technique for visuo-spatial neglect. In this two part study we first investigated if a computerized game-like prism adaption procedure (i.e., Peg-the-Mole) can induce aftereffects in a sample of 30 healthy adults (aged 18 to 23). A second study was then conducted to assess the validity of Peg-the-Mole and to determine if the aftereffects would generalize to a throwing task in 40 healthy adults (aged 18 to 30). Study 1 determined that Peg-the-Mole with deviating goggles is effective in inducing both proprioceptive only (p = 0.003) and proprioceptive visual aftereffects (p = 0.000) when compared to non-deviating goggles. Study 2 determined that Peg-the-Mole with deviating goggles is not different from the standard prism adaptation procedure with deviating goggles on measures of proprioceptive only (p = 0.754), visual only (p = 0.072), and proprioceptive visual (p = 0.115) aftereffects and generalization of aftereffects to a throwing task (p = 0.351) and both conditions with deviating goggles are significantly different than without deviating goggles on all measures (p = 0.000). Furthermore, Peg-the-Mole was more enjoyable than the standard prism adaptation procedure (p = 0.001). This study demonstrates that alterations can be made to the standard PA procedure to make it more feasible for use in patients' homes as well as making it a more enjoyable rehabilitation technique.

authors_raw: Tamara Speth, Penny Corkum and Aimée Coulombe
title: A Usability Study for the Better Nights, Better Days Web-Based Intervention for Pediatric Insomnia
abstract:
Introduction and Objectives: Pediatric insomnia is a highly prevalent and pervasive problem that affects both children and their caregivers. While effective behavioural treatments exist, they are generally inaccessible and therefore used by a small number of parents. The Better Nights, Better Days web-based intervention for pediatric insomnia was designed to help overcome the accessibility issues related to behavioural treatments.
Before the intervention is ready for evaluation through randomized control trials (RCTs), the current usability study is necessary to ensure parents' satisfaction and comfort with the intervention. Methods: Data will be collected in two cycles. Cycle One will involve an evaluation of the intervention by the Better Nights, Better Days Team, as well as a group of front-line health care professionals, and a group of parents who have a child with insomnia. This cycle of usability testing will take place during the development process, as a way to make improvements to the intervention before the website is fully constructed. As the proposed study will be taking place during the development process, participants will be asked to provide feedback on the intervention as the sessions are developed. There will be three waves of data collection in total. Cycle Two will be a pilot study with a new group of parents who have a child with insomnia, and will involve a full run-through and testing of the intervention. Results: We have not begun data collection for the current study. Conclusion: The current study will collect feedback on the Better Nights, Better Days intervention, both during and after the development process. The study will help to refine the intervention before beginning RCTs, and will allow researchers to ensure an enjoyable and effective user experience, which will help to improve the sustainability of the intervention.

authors_raw: Sean P. Mackinnon, Susan R. Battista, Simon B. Sherry, Marla Korecky, Laura Lambe, and Sherry H. Stewart
title: Perfectionistic self-presentation predicts social anxiety: A 21-day diary study
abstract: Perfectionistic self-presentation is theorized to maintain social anxiety from moment-to-moment. Despite this theorized relationship, no research has tested this relationship using experience sampling methodology. The present research tested whether perfectionistic self-presentation predicted social anxiety beyond numerous key covariates using a daily diary design. Undergraduates (N = 165; 75.6% women; 84.8% White) completed questionnaires each day for 21-days using palm pilots. Generalizability theory and multilevel factor analyses suggested daily measures of perfectionistic self-presentation, social anxiety, perfectionism cognitions, and depressed mood were reliable and valid daily measures. Moreover, perfectionistic self-presentation was a robust predictor of social anxiety at the between-subjects and within-subjects levels, even when controlling for socially prescribed perfectionism, perfectionism cognitions, and depressed mood, extending prior cross-sectional research on this topic. By understanding daily personality processes, we can better understand the processes that perpetuate and maintain social anxiety, and ultimately how to devise more effective ways to treat social anxiety.

authors_raw: Eva Gunde, M. Stevens, X. Song, Steven Beyea, Ryan D'Arcy
title: Linking neuropsychology to brain imaging: reliability of the Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT)
abstract: The SDMT is reliable and sensitive neuropsychological tool used to evaluate psychomotor and information processing speed and cognitive dysfunction in Multiple Sclerosis (MS). Reliability of functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has been reported with a range of 21% - 78% and a mean of 29% with regards to several tasks, including those applied for pre-surgical planning. The objective of this study is to test reliability of the SDMT using fMRI with the ultimate goal of developing the test as a clinical imaging tool to detect early changes in brain function and follow functional decline in MS. Here, we present preliminary findings of an ongoing study. Seven participants with remitting relapsing type of MS attended three visits a week apart with no experience of a relapse or a worsening of clinical symptoms. Functional maps of the SDMT were created for cortical and subcortical areas. Reliability of the SDMT was tested by applying the ROC-r analysis developed for pre-surgical mapping. This method allows for individualized thresholding of each functional map and provides the best overlap of active clusters at test-retest sessions. Overlapping cluster coefficients were calculated using the Rombouts method. Comparing the individual functional maps of the three visits, the average test-rest Rombouts coefficients show overlap of 45% for cortical, 36% for subcortical, and 40% for both, cortical and subcortical regions. Reliability of the SDMT is promising, as it is well above the overall mean cluster reliability reported in the literature and comparable to reliability (~46%) of tasks requiring higher order processing. Measuring reliability is a critical step towards developing the SDMT as a clinical imaging tool. We are currently increasing our sample size as well as recruiting healthy participants to obtain more accurate and representative information of the reproducibility of this test.

authors_raw: Kristen Bailey, Sally Bird, Jessica Fralic, Patrick McGrath and Jill Chorney
title: Preparing Parents to be Present for their child's Anesthesia Induction
abstract: Background: Surgery is a painful and anxiety provoking experience for thousands of children who are admitted to Canadian hospitals each year. Recent literature suggests that preoperative anxiety is a significant predictor of postoperative pain and postoperative analgesic use (Kain, Mayes et al., 2006). Thus, managing children's preoperative anxiety is an important aspect of children's perioperative care. Parental presence during anesthesia (PPIA) is one of the most commonly used but controversial ways to help children manage their anxiety. A recent Cochrane review concluded that PPIA did not change child or parent outcomes as compared to no PPIA (Yip et al., 2009); however, studies included in this review did not take into account baseline characteristics (i.e., parent anxiety) nor did they prepare parents to be in the operating room which are both factors thought to be related to children's preoperative anxiety (Kain et al., 2006; 2007). Objective: Therefore the purpose of the present study is to compare the effectiveness of a brief family-centred video-based preparation intervention for PPIA (PPIA prep) to a PPIA with standard logistical preparation (PPIA std). Participants & Setting: The present study will recruit 100, (2-10 year old) children scheduled to undergo elective surgery at the IWK Health Centre and their parents. Design & Procedure: Parents will be randomized to receive either PPIA prep or PPIA std just prior to their child's surgery. Children of parent's randomized to each group, will be compared on measures of anxiety, pain, analgesic use and time required to recover from the surgery in hospital. Hypotheses: It is expected that children with parents in the PPIA prep group will have lower anxiety before surgery and less pain and analgesic use after surgery and will be discharged sooner than children with parents in the PPIA std group.

authors_raw: I.A. Meinertzhagen, S. Takemura, D. Chklovskii, L. Scheffer, Z. Lu and J.A. Horne
title: But that's just anatomy: The search for synapses, circuits and wiring diagrams in the Drosophila brain.
abstract: Animal behavior arises from computations in neuronal circuits, but our understanding of these computations has been frustrated by the lack of detailed maps of synaptic connections, or connectomes, of identified neurons. For example, despite intensive investigations over half a century, the neuronal implementation of local motion detection in the insect's visual system remains elusive. A semi-automated pipeline developed at Janelia Farm has used serial-section electron microscopy to reconstruct a connectome, comprising 377 neurons and 14,798 synapses, within the largest neuropile of the Drosophila brain, the optic medulla. By matching reconstructed neurons to examples from light microscopy, we assigned neurons to cell types and assembled a connectome of the medulla's repeating module. Within this module, we identified cell types constituting a motion detection circuit compatible with the elementary motion detector (EMD) first identified as a cybernetic model over 50 years ago, and showed that the connections onto individual motion sensitive neurons in this circuit were consistent with their direction selectivity. Our results identify cellular targets for future functional investigations, and demonstrate that connectomes provide key insights into neuronal computations.

title: Sniffer dogs as non-invasive wildlife monitoring research assistants: The Wood Turtle (Glypetmys insculpta)
abstract: The Canid Behaviour Research Team has been working with Wildlife Conservation Canines since 2009. Following work with Ribbon Snakes (Thamnophis sauritus) and Eastern Coyotes (Canis latrans) we present data from a pilot study conducted in Middle Musquodoboit, Nova Scotia. Like most of the turtle species in Nova Scotia, wood turtles (Glyptemys [formerly Clemmys] insculpta) have experienced significant population decline in recent decades. Two dogs were assessed as potential "turtle dogs". Preliminary results, seasonal challenges in detection and search, and training considerations will be discussed.

authors_raw: Catherine Floyd, Meghan Macdonald, Elizabeth Cummings, Elizabeth McLaughlin and Simon Gadbois
title: Detection and Discrimination of Hypoglycemic States by Dogs
abstract: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is a life long-long chronic disease that requires constant blood glucose monitoring. Hypoglycemia, or low blood sugar, is a serious complication of diabetes and often occurs at night when little if any glucose monitoring occurs. This is a particularly serious problem in children as they are often unable to recognize and wake during low glucose levels. Continuous glucose monitors that sound an alarm when abnormally low or high blood sugar is detected are available, however, these devices are expensive to operate, may lead to infection, and can be inaccurate, often failing to detect true hypoglycemic states. There are many anecdotal reports that pet dogs alter their behaviour in response to low blood sugar in their owners. This occurs during both waking and sleeping states of the owner and dogs often show alert responses before the person detects their low sugar. American companies are claiming to train diabetes alert/assistance dogs, however there has been no empirical research to date that indicates whether dogs are able to detect and discriminate hypoglycemic states from normoglycemic states. To empirically test the ability of dogs to detect hypoglycemia via olfactory cues, the current study collected saliva, breath, and sweat samples from children and youth with Type 1 diabetes in normoglycemic and hypoglycemic states. The samples were presented to three volunteer dogs using a match-to-sample procedure, errorless discrimination training, and habituation/dishabituation. Data collection is currently underway.

title: Anonymity & Sharing Behaviour in Children
abstract: Background Despite abundant research on sharing behaviour in children, there is a paucity of research investigating whether the sharer's anonymity impacts sharing. More surprisingly, previous studies (that have assumed that young participants can understand anonymity) have failed to verify that young children understand this complex concept. Method We are assessing 3- and 5-year-olds to examine at what age children understand the concept of anonymity by asking forced-choice questions about their status as a 'known' or 'anonymous' sharer within a dictator game paradigm. From there, children share stickers as 'known sharers' or 'anonymous sharers'. Results Preliminary data analyses (N=40) suggest that 3-year-olds do not understand the concept of anonymity; however, 5-year-olds do. It also seems that being a 'known' or 'anonymous' sharer does not influence 5-year-old's sharing. Conclusion Given that these results indicate that 3-year-olds do not understand anonymity, previous research findings using this age group in paradigms featuring anonymity should be interpreted cautiously. Likewise, preschool-aged children's sharing may be driven by something other than their status as an 'anonymous' or 'known' sharer (such as a desire for equal sharing, regardless if they are anonymous).

authors_raw: Julie Longard, Susan Bryson, & Chris Moore
title: Early Expressive and Receptive Language Trajectories in Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract: The early expressive and receptive language trajectories of children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) are poorly understood. The current study was conducted as part of a multi-site prospective study examining the early development of high-risk infant siblings of children with ASD (hereafter, high-risk infants). We investigated expressive and receptive language trajectories based on corresponding domain standard scores on the Mullen Scales of Early Learning (MSEL) at four time points (6, 12, 24, and 36 months). In the combined sample of high-risk (n=371, 56% male) and low-risk (n =152, 52% male) infants, expressive and receptive language trajectories were examined using semi-parametric group-based modeling. A 3-group solution provided optimal fit to the variation in both expressive and receptive language. The three trajectories were characterized by the following performance across time: (1) increasing from average to above average, (2) maintaining average, and (3) declining from average to below average. A diagnosis of ASD at 36 months was predictive of membership in the declining trajectories for both expressive and receptive language. The current findings have important implications for early intervention in ASD.

title: Inhibition of Return: Two causes investigated using speed-accuracy trade-off functions
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) can be described as an inhibitory aftermath of orienting seen in the form of slower response times in the direction or location that was previously attended. First discovered by Posner and Cohen (1984) the phenomenon has been shown to exist in two mutually exclusive forms (Taylor & Klein, 2000): an effect on motoric processes (an output form) and an effect on attentional/perceptual processes (an input form). The factor proposed to modulate which form of IOR is observed is the state of activation of the oculomotor system: output based when the system is not suppressed; input based when it is suppressed. Whereas Chica, Taylor, Lupianez, and Klein (2010) discovered that when caused by an eye movement to an uninformative peripheral cue, the delay in responding to targets at the originally cued location (the IOR effect) was accompanied by more accurate responding. By generating speed-accuracy tradeoff functions using response-signal methodology as proposed by Wickelgren (1977) and implemented by Ivanoff and Klein (2006) and Zhao, Heinke, Ivanoff, Klein and Humphreys (2011) under conditions of eye monitoring, we determined the output form of IOR is characterized by a criterion shift, as compared to the input form, which is characterized by a change in performance.

title: Shaping adult phenotypes through early life environments
abstract: A major question in the biology of stress and environmental adaptation concerns the neurobiological basis of how neuroendocrine systems governing physiological regulatory mechanisms essential for life (metabolism, immune response, organ function) become harmful. The current view is that a switch from protection to damage occurs when vulnerable phenotypes are exposed to adverse environmental conditions. In accordance with this theory, sequelae of early life social and environmental stressors, such as childhood abuse, neglect, poverty, and poor nutrition, have been associated with the emergence of mental and physical illness (i.e., anxiety, mood disorders, poor impulse control, psychosis, and drug abuse) and an increased risk of common metabolic and cardiovascular diseases later in life. Evidence from animal and human studies investigating the associations between early life experiences (including parent-infant bonding), hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal axis activity, brain development, and health outcome provide important clues into the neurobiological mechanisms that mediate the contribution of stressful experiences to personality development and the manifestation of illness. This talk will summarize essential aspects of our current molecular understanding of how early environment influences brain development in a manner that persists through life and highlight recent evidence from rodent studies suggesting that maternal care in the first week of postnatal life establishes diverse and stable phenotypes in the offspring through epigenetic modification of genes expressed in the brain that shape neuroendocrine and behavioral stress responsivity throughout life.

authors_raw: Meghan G. Schinkel, Erin C. Moon, Christine T. Chambers & Patrick J. McGrath
title: A Comparison of Maternal versus Paternal Non-Verbal Behaviour during Child Pain
abstract: Introduction and Aims: Parental behaviour is known to play a significant role in children's response to pain. However, the majority of research to date has focused solely on maternal behaviour during child pain. A recent study conducted by Moon, et al. (2011) found generally no differences between mothers' and fathers' verbal behavior during child pressor pain. The present study aimed to build on this work through comparing mothers' and fathers' non-verbal behaviour during child pain. Methods: The non-verbal behaviour of mothers (N = 20) and fathers (N = 20) of 20 children (10 boys, 10 girls) who participated in the cold pressor task as part of the study by Moon et al. (2011) were coded from video-tape. Children had completed the CPT once with their mothers present and once with their fathers present in a counterbalanced order. Parental non-verbal behaviour was coded across 13 categories of behaviour, including distraction, humor, physical comfort and reassurance, facial and behavioural sympathy, procedure related attending behaviour and fidgeting. Coding was completed by two research assistants. Percent agreement ranged from 88% to 100% across the behaviour codes. Results: A series 2 (child sex) x 2 (parent sex) mixed analyses of variance (ANOVAs) revealed generally no significant differences in mother vs. father non-verbal behaviour, with the exception of fidgeting (i.e. any form of fidgeting or behaviours expressing agitation such as feet tapping, tapping fingers, bouncing knee), F(1,18) = 6.57, p < .05, with fathers engaging in significanly more fidgeting than mothers (fathers: M = 0.39, SE = .08; mothers: M = .23, SE = .06). There were no significant effects of child sex or parent by child sex interactions. Discussion: Concurrent with findings on parental verbal behaviour, mothers and fathers non-verbal behaviour generally did not differ during their child's pain experience. However, fathers appeared to fidget more often than mothers during child pain, perhaps indicating a greater amount of restlessness or discomfort in response to watching their child in pain or uncertainty about how to respond.

authors_raw: Donald Mitchell, Katelyn MacNeill, Kaitlyn Holman, Matthew Smithen and Kevin Duffy
title: More light from darkness: new results on the recovery from early visual deprivation in kittens following 10 days of complete darkness.
abstract: Last year we reported on the fast visual recovery from early monocular deprivation (MD) promoted by a 10 day period of complete darkness imposed either immediately or 5-8 weeks after a one week period of MD. In this update we show that the effects are no less dramatic following much longer periods of MD (from eye opening to 6-8 weeks of age) and as well report on the consequences of shorter or interrupted periods of darkness. Measurements of depth thresholds indicate that only about one-third of animals acquire stereoscopic vision despite recovering normal visual acuity in both eyes.

title: Origin of the sustained negative response in the ERG of Drosophila SNR mutants
abstract: The electroretinogram (ERG) in the fruitfly Drosophila melanogaster is an important diagnostic tool for the molecular genetics of synapses. The presence or absence of two of its three components, the ON- and OFF-transient responses, allows diagnosis of the working of the synapse from the photoreceptors to their postsynaptic follower neurons, following some genetic manipulation. In mutants where this synaptic outlet is blocked, the ON- and OFF-transients disappear, but a third component still remains: a Sustained Negative Response (SNR) that is believed to originate only in the photoreceptors themselves or their axons. The SNR remains in mutants ebony and tan that interrupt synaptic transmission by disrupting re-use of the photoreceptors' neurotransmitter, histamine, through enzymatic defects. Experimentally, if the SNR does come from the photoreceptors, a bright local flash delivered to ~1/10th of the ~770 facets of the eye should elicit a larger SNR than a dimmer flash from a 1° point source that illuminates only a few facets maximally, while the rest get very little stimulus. This proposition has been tested in three alleles of ebony and one of tan, always yielding the opposite result: the brighter 72-facet flash gives a much smaller response than the 1° flash that stimulates only 1-3 facets maximally. Confirming the calibrations, in the white-Hdc mutant in which the synapse again is blocked, the 72-facet input gives the larger responses, because the brighter local flash is now able to scatter widely throughout the transparent white eye. The results can be explained if the SNR does not originate in the photoreceptors, but instead comes mostly from a wide-field integrating cell system: this prefers tiny inputs delivered over many facets to saturating inputs aimed into only 1/10th of the eye.

authors_raw: Mike Lawrence, Stephanie Allen, Shannon Johnson, Raymond Klein
title: Psychometric evaluation of a brief but comprehensive computerized attention test
abstract: We present a brief computerized test that achieves measurement of a variety of phenomena of attention, including psychometric data from typically-developing child and young adult populations.

authors_raw: Roxanne Sterniczuk, Olga Theou, Benjamin Rusak, & Kenneth Rockwood
title: Non-traditional risk factors predict dementia and mortality in healthy individuals
abstract: As age-related health deficits accumulate over time, so does the risk for adverse health outcomes. Previous work re-analyzing data from the Canadian Study of Health and Aging suggested that several risk factors which are not traditionally associated with dementia, may impact an individual's level of frailty and their risk of Alzheimer's disease (AD). We investigated whether an index consisting of such non-traditional factors, could predict future reports of incident AD and dementia, as well as mortality, in a similar manner to traditional risk factors, in a larger, multi-national cohort. Secondary analyses were conducted on data from the Survey of Health, Ageing, and Retirement in Europe and consisted of cognitively healthy individuals 50 years or over, from 12 European countries (N = 11,817). Three AD risk factor indices (RFIs) were constructed to predict a ~4-year risk for a self or informant report of AD, dementia and survival; a 31-item non-traditional RFI, a 6-item traditional RFI, and a 37-item combined RFI. While controlling for age, sex, education, and traditional risk factors for AD within our regression risk models, the non-traditional RFI significantly predicted the risk of dementia and mortality after an average of 4.3 years. The combined RFI exhibited the strongest prediction of dementia and mortality. Although non-traditional risk factors may not individually contribute to dementia or mortality, a decline in overall health status is strongly associated with an increased risk for these adverse outcomes. Health professionals should place greater importance on the examination of overall health decline, rather than just assessing traditional risk factors for illness.

title: The Effects of Alcohol and Snus on Dependent Smokers? Cigarette Craving & Cigarette Self-Administration
abstract: Dependent smokers use cigarette and alcohol widely together; however, factors affecting the impact of alcohol on smoking behavior are not known. This study assessed the effects of snus (smokeless powder tobacco) and alcohol on cigarette craving and smoking behavior in 19 dependent smokers. Randomized placebo controlled double blind design was used and each participant was assessed in 2 sessions. A progressive ratio task was used over 60 minutes as a behavioral measurement. Mood and cigarette craving were assessed through subjective measurements. Tobacco craving increased significantly after drinking alcohol and placebo beverages. Snus significantly reduced individuals' motivation to start smoking. Snus and alcohol together did not have any significant effect on cigarette craving and smoking behavior. In conclusion, the effect of alcohol on cigarette craving involves its interaction with nicotine; however, when used with snus, its interaction with nicotine alone will not have any considerable impact on smoking behavior.

authors_raw: Talisa Tims, Helene Deacon and Anne Castles
title: The Relationship Between Orthographic Processing and Word Reading
abstract: Orthographic processing is an important skill for word reading in children. Orthographic processing refers to the ability to form, store, and retrieve orthographic representations. As a construct, orthographic processing can be broken into orthographic knowledge (access to existing orthographic representations) and orthographic learning (formation and storage of new orthographic representations). Previous research has consistently found a relationship between orthographic knowledge and word reading. However, studies attempting to determine the directionality of the relationship between orthographic processing and word reading have found conflicting results. Thus, in this study, we propose to use measures targeting orthographic learning instead of orthographic knowledge in order to better elucidate the nature of this relationship. The proposed study measures orthographic learning in second and third grade students at three time points over the course of one and a half years. Children will complete various measures of word reading, phonological awareness, reading comprehension, orthographic knowledge, and orthographic learning. Data collection for the first and second testing points are complete and data collection for the third and final testing point is proposed to be completed by June 2013. This presentation will focus on the theoretical background and predictions for this study.

authors_raw: John Christie, Matt Hilchey and Ray Klein
title: Rescuing Eye Movement Modellers From a 40 Year Rut
abstract: For 40 years it has been established that when two stimuli are presented close to one another eye movements toward those stimuli tend to land between them. This is the global effect of stimuli on eye movements. In contrast, when two stimuli are presented far apart eye movements land at one of the stimulus locations--a local effect. Contrary to conventional wisdom, by measuring the effect through priming, we find a strong global effect for stimuli very far from each other. This effect is even stronger than the local effect at all stimulus spacings. Researchers need to reconsider one of the fundamental "principles" they use in modelling human and primate eye movements

authors_raw: Lisa D. Wright, Austin Korgan, and Tara S. Perrot
title: Hood Rats and Alley Cats: Potential for Protective Effects of Prenatal Stress in an Animal Model of Seizure
abstract: There is a large body of literature demonstrating detrimental effects of prenatal stress on various measures of neural development and long-term health outcomes in rat offspring. Much of this work, however, has involved stressing pregnant rat dams using restraint or a combination of unnatural disruptions to the homecage environment. The present project sought to examine the effects of repeatedly exposing pregnant rat dams to a naturalistic stressor, predatory threat, every day during the third trimester. After giving birth, their male and female offspring were raised either in standard housing conditions or in an enhanced environment that was larger and contained a burrow. Outcomes that were measured in the adult offspring included seizure susceptibility and corticotrophin-releasing-hormone (CRH) production in a subregion of the thalamus, as well as in an overlying subregion of cortex. We found that adult offspring of dams exposed to cat stress exhibited less seizure behaviour and had lower levels of CRH-producing cells in cortex, relative to offspring from control dams. However, these differences were ameliorated in offspring raised in an enhanced environment, suggesting that any protective effects garnered through prenatal stressor exposure of rat dams could also be acquired later on, through post-natal environmental enhancement.

authors_raw: Jennifer Stamp, Dawn Peters, & Lynda MacAllister
title: Comparison of Risk and Protective Personality Traits in Smoking and Non-Smoking Teens
abstract: Research has identified a number of distinct personality dimensions that place an individual at risk for substance misuse. The Substance Use Risk Profile Scale (SURPS) assesses four of these: hopelessness-introversion (HI), anxiety sensitivity (AS), impulsivity (IMP), and sensation-seeking (SS). Psychological resilience is the ability to overcome challenges (measured by the Connor-Davidson Resilience Scale, or CD-RISC), and although not well studied in the context of substance misuse, predicts favourable outcomes in individuals experiencing trauma. The present study aimed to compare these personality constructs in smoking and non-smoking teens in rural high schools in Nova Scotia. We sought to: 1) compare measures on the SURPS and CD-RISC in smoking and non-smoking teens and 2) determine the relationship between personality and response to a school-based quitting smoking program. Overall smokers had higher significantly higher scores on the HI and SS dimensions of the SURPS, and lower scores on the CD-RISC. CD-RISC scores were positively correlated with age of first use, and negatively correlated with self-reported smoking at baseline. Amongst the smokers, there were no significant relationships between personality risk factors and baseline smoking or response to treatment.

authors_raw: Amanda Williams, Katie Isenor, & Chris Moore
title: The influence of social comparision on sharing and envy in children.
abstract: Though prosocial behaviour emerges early in development, there are numerous factors that influence its manifestations. This study explored how social comparison influences prosocial behaviour in five and six-year-old children. Children first played a computer game against a fictional child, and were randomly assigned to a winning condition- in which they won nine out of ten trials against their partner, or losing condition- in which they lost nine out of ten trials. Next, they were asked to distribute resources between themselves and their partner from the computer game in a sticker sharing game that included sharing, and envy trials. Though we expected to see more prosocial behaviour for the winning condition in both trial types- more sharing, and decreased envy, children in the winning condition showed more prosocial in sharing trials alone. We suspect the competitive nature of the game increased envy across both conditions, and plan to follow-this up by including a non-competitive condition.

title: Effects of housing conditions on neurogenesis in black-capped chickadees (Poecile atricapillus)
abstract: In chickadees, it has been shown that captivity leads to suppressed neurogenesis in the hippocampal complex compared to free-flying birds. It is not known whether differential housing condition influences the degree to which neurogenesis is suppressed. We captured birds during winter and subjected them to different housing conditions: Outdoor aviary, indoor aviary, and indoor cage. Birds were injected with BrdU and observed for six weeks after which time their brains were assessed for neuronal proliferation and survival. Results from this study will allow us to determine the effects of housing condition on neurogenesis while considering associated factors including behavioural differences and climate. These results will provide insight to what factors underly differences in neurogenesis between captive and free-flying birds. Further, results will aid in understanding the influence of environmental context in field and laboratory studies using songbirds.

title: The connectome of the larval central nervous system (CNS) of Ciona intestinalis
abstract: The complete wiring diagram (connectome) of any CNS has been reported only for C. elegans. Using serial-EM, we report the connectome for a Ciona larval CNS with 166 neurons, 4 ascending neural tube axons, and 70 non-neuronal cells. It incorporates the elements of sensory integration along with a central pattern generator for larval swimming, with outputs through paired motoneurons in the visceral ganglion (VG) and proximal tail, a region containing 11 ascending and 20 descending neurons. Information in the sensory vesicle (SV) arises from: photoreceptors with outer segments (23 in the pigment cup, 7 in the lumen of the SV); 2 otolith associated antenna cells; 16 enigmatic, anaxonal coronet cells; and axons from rostral trunk epidermal (RTEN) and papillar (PN) neurons. Information is integrated in both the posterior SV by 31 interneurons and within the VG where an intrinsic circuitry allows for further integration of signals. This pathway constitutes the means of control of initiation of motor response as well as its modulation. The input provided to motor neurons within this region in turn determines their activity at cholinergic neuromuscular synapses and results in a variety canonical behaviours. This connectome will be presented via circuit diagrams that will be essential both to interpret functional studies in Ciona larvae as well as to predict those functions. Inherent in this discussion of circuitry is a critical analysis of the nature of synaptic connections and functional cell types in this basal Chordate species.

authors_raw: Randii-Lynn Sullivan, Heather Milliken, Heather Schellinck, Richard Brown, Jordan Fisk, David Macalindon, Denise Lewis, Mackenzie Armstrong and Kimberley Good
title: An Investigation of the Neural Correlates of Impaired Olfactory Identification Ability in Patients with First Episode Psychosis: An FMRI Study
abstract:
Olfactory identification deficits (OID) are present in a proportion of patients with first episode psychosis and may be prognostic for a poorer outcome. The neural underpinnings of this impairment have not been identified. The purpose of this study was to examine the neuronal correlates of OID in patients with first episode psychosis by comparing a subgroup of patients who have OID (n=6) to a subgroup of patients with intact (normal) olfactory identification (NOI n=6) and contrast their functioning to a group of healthy control subjects (n=16) using BOLD FMRI while being passively presented with odours or clean air.
Using a region of interest (ROI) approach, BOLD activation was examined across groups. The first contrast compared healthy controls to the two patient groups during odour presentation. Healthy control subjects activated the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) and anterior cingulate during odour presentation whereas patients did not. Comparing the two patient groups, the NOI group activated insular regions (more right than left) and right amygdala while OID patients did not. These results support the notion that brain activation is indeed compromised in secondary olfactory brain regions in patients with psychosis, whereas in those patients who have olfactory identification deficits, the abnormality may lie in primary olfactory cortex network. In patients with intact olfactory identification, compensatory regions may be invoked.

title: The Effect of Alcohol on Smoking Cue Reactivity in Non-Dependent Smokers
abstract: Introduction: Non-dependent smokers report greater urge to smoke in response to smoking cues and alcohol. Moreover, smoking cue-craving is perceived as more intense in females and as blood alcohol levels are rising. This study examined the role of alcohol and sex on tobacco craving induced by smoking cues in non-dependent smokers on the ascending limb of the blood alcohol curve. Methods: Seventy-five non-alcoholic non-dependent smokers (38 male; 37 female) were recruited from the general population. During one double-blind placebo-controlled session, participants assessed the effects of smoking cues on cigarette craving as measured by the QSU-B, following the administration of a moderately intoxicating dose of alcohol or a taste-matched placebo beverage. Results: The results revealed that acute alcohol consumption lead to an increase in factor 1 and 2 desire to smoke in females in response to a non-nicotine stimulus on the ascending limb of the blood alcohol curve. Discussion: The findings suggest that females may be more susceptible to craving in response to smoking-related cues particularly early in the alcohol absorption period.

authors_raw: Kevin R. Duffy, Kaitlyn Holman, and Donald E. Mitchell
title: Monocular deprivation alters the size of FoxP2 (X cells) cells in the cat lateral geniculate nucleus
abstract: A central characteristic of the visual system is the streaming of information through parallel pathways. In the cat lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN), parallel processing streams derive from two principal neuron types, namely X and Y cells. It is well known that disruption of visual experience by monocular deprivation has a profound impact on the structure and function of Y cells, but the extent to which deprivation influences X cells remains unclear. Recently the FoxP2 protein has been shown to selectively label X cells in the ferret LGN, providing an opportunity to examine whether X cells are modified by monocular deprivation. In this study, FoxP2 labeling was examined in the LGN of normal (n=2) and monocularly deprived (n=3) cats. We first determined that the labeling characteristics of neurons reactive for FoxP2 were consistent with it being a marker for X cells in the cat LGN. Monocular deprivation for either a short or long duration did not change the number of FoxP2-positive neurons between non-deprived and deprived LGN layers. However, cell measurements revealed that in deprived layers, X cells were reduced in size by 20% after 7 days of deprivation, and by 30% after 7 weeks of deprivation. The observed alteration to X cells in the context monocular deprivation is in agreement with the associated impairments to vision.

title: Bayes in brief: philosophy and application
abstract:
While quantitative methods in psychology and neuroscience have traditionally been dominated by the "null hypothesis significance testing" (NHST) philosophy of statistics, recent advances in computing have permitted alternative approaches, most notably Bayesian methods. This talk will briefly distinguish the philosophical backgound of NHST and Bayesian methods followed by a walk-through of a simple Bayesian analysis. The talk will conclude with discussion of the power of Bayesian methods to address more advanced analyses.
Final Symposium
Psychology and Neuroscience: Why our name without "and neuroscience" would not smell as sweet
Descartes made a sharp distinction between conscious processes of the mind or rational soul and mechanical material processes of the body. This distinction was effective and had long-term consequences that included separating the disciplines of psychology and neurobiology. Why this happened and why we are now able to reintegrate psychology and neuroscience is the subject of this symposium.
Why psychology and neuroscience separated as sciences
John Barresi
For Aristotle psychology was an integral part of the biology of animate beings. But, with the rise of Christianity and its emphasis on Plato's earlier idea of the immortality of the rational human soul, Western thinkers were ready for Descartes' revolutionary theory, which distinguished sharply between the conscious rational soul of humans and body-machines. While subsequent thinkers rejected Descartes' idea that all other animals were mere automata, without conscious minds, the majority of 18th and 19th century scientists continued to distinguish conscious processes of minds and purely material processes of animate bodies, including the brain. A consequence of this historical development has been that psychology and neurobiology have been separate disciplines until quite recently.
How Donald Hebb brought psychology and neuroscience back together
Richard Brown
In the 1940's, there were many "Schools" of psychology, such as Gestalt Psychology, Structuralism, Functionalism, Behaviourism, etc., each of which looked at behaviour in a different way. There were also a plethora of "physiological" theories of behaviour proposed by Pavlov, Lashley, Morgan, and others. Each theory focused on a different aspect of behaviour: learning, memory, development, motivation, emotion and clinical problems. What Hebb did, with two simple concepts, the modifiable synapse and the cell assembly, was to provide a neural mechanism which could account for all of the different categories of behaviour that psychologists were struggling to explain with their separate theories. The result was to change Psychological theory and reintroduce the idea that a neural mechanism was the basis for all behaviour, normal and abnormal and that one of the aims of Psychology was to understand that physiological mechanism. Hebb's aim was to show that the psychological study of the "mind" required a biological approach.
A neo-Hebbian blueprint for the integration of neuro-psychological science and the mission of our Department
Raymond M. Klein
With Hebb's ideas providing a backdrop, the recent history of our discipline will be related to a (somewhat) personalized history of the department. Divisions based on workplace (laboratory versus clinic), type of work (studies of brain versus behavior; animals versus humans), sub-discipline (development, perception, personality, learning, etc.) and favorite causal mechanism (nature vs nurture) characterize these histories. Importantly, conceptual, methodological and technological innovations have made it possible for us to implement Hebb's neuropsychological vision of the discipline through an integrated effort to understand how nature and nurture shape the neural networks that underlie the organization of our overt and covert behaviors.
The future of psychology and neuroscience
Olav Krigolson
Our increasing ability to see into the mind has helped scientists adapt theory to match the computations that are actually performed within the brain. For example, rational choice theory would posit that when faced with a decision, one should always choose the response option with the highest utility, or value. However, that is not always the case. When humans play the "ultimatum game" frequently people make irrational decisions - they reject proffered money in the name of "fairness". Functional imaging has given us insight into why this occurs, and has forced a reconciliation of rational choice theory with psychological theory. As we head into the future, technological advances in human neuroimaging will only allow us to see further into the processes of the mind and explain other phenomenon. For instance, recent advances in neuroimaging have taken us into the realm of mind reading - a technology that knows what we are thinking as we are thinking it. Not only does research like this give us better insight into why we do and say the things we do, but it also puts us right in front of Pandora's Box - forcing to us to an understanding that we might not want to have.
POSTERS
Thursday's Posters

title: fERN differences within Subclinical Psychopaths
abstract:
Psychopathy is a personality disorder distinguished by a characteristic lack of affect,
superficiality, spontaneous and impulsive behavior, and a parasitic lifestyle. Clinical and
subclinical psychopathy differ only in the severity of dysfunction within the individual. One
may wonder if psychopathy may impact reward processing within the medial-frontal cortex? The
feedback error-related negativity (fERN) is a component of the human event-related brain
potential (ERP) that is sensitive to reward processing. In the present study we sought to
investigate the relationship between fERN amplitude and subclinical psychopathy; using the
Self-Report Psychopathy Scale Revised III to measure levels of subclinical psychopathy within
an undergraduate population. Given that, by nature, Psychopaths do not typically learn from
their mistakes, we expect to find a deficit in reward processing as reflected in an overall decrease
in fERN amplitude for people who score high on psychopathy. Our data supports this
contention.

authors_raw: Matthew P. Lowe, Jillian L. King, Nathan A. Crowder
title: Contrast adaptation in mouse V1 depends on spatial frequency but not firing rate
abstract:
It is well known that the visual system adjusts it function based on prevailing viewing conditions.
For example, prolonged viewing of high contrast gratings produces contrast adaptation in which
both the perceived contrast of test stimuli and the responsivity of visual neurons can be altered.
Contrast adaptation has been well documented in cats and primates, however its underlying
neural mechanisms are not well known. Therefore, we examined this phenomenon in V1 in mice,
a genetically manipulable animal model. Previous research in mice has shown that contrast
adaptation depends on the spatial frequency (SF) of the adaptor, and that the strength of
adaptation can diminish when non-preferred stimuli are used. The purpose of the current study
was to determine whether the selectivity of the adaptation effect is due to the SF of the stimuli or
merely the sub-maximal spike rates elicited by non-preferred SFs. To do this, we used a top up
contrast adaptation protocol where two different adaptors flanked the preferred SF and elicited
similar spike rates. The test stimulus either matched the adaptor or was different than the
adaptor. Similar to previous studies, we found that contrast adaptation was strongest when the
test SF matched the adaptor. It appears that contrast adaption is mediated by similar underlying
neural mechanisms in different animals, which suggests that genetic tools available in mice could
be leveraged to investigate these mechanisms.

authors_raw: Malik Abouleish, Kevin J. Frank, Jillian L. King, Nathan A. Crowder
title: Retinorecipient areas in the diurnal rodent Octodon Degu
abstract:
The degu (Octodon Degu) is a diurnal rodent that has been used in neuroscience research to
study circadian rhythms. However, degus may also be worth considering as a model in visual
neuroscience because they have a far greater density of cone photoreceptors than traditional lab
rodents (rats and mice). We used biotinylated dextran amine anteriograde labeling to explore the
retinofugal projections in the degu for the first time. We documented anteriograde labeling in
twenty-eight subcortical visual nuclei, but focused the majority of our analysis on the dorsal
lateral geniculate nucleus and the superior colliculus as two regions that may have adapted to
diurnal activity. We found that the degu's dorsal lateral geniculate nucleus lacked distinct
stratification, and was morphologically similar to the rat. The greatest density of labeling we
observed occurred in the superior colliculus, with far greater contralateral bias than observed in
rats. These differences may reflect the cone-rich retinofugal signal in degus, but overall the
morphology of visual projections in this member of the family Octodontidae resembles that in
members of the family Muridae.

authors_raw: Josh Bowdridge and Dr. Kevin Duffy
title: Investigating a Role for Matrix Metalloproteinases in the Cortex of Visually Deprived Cats.
abstract: Occlusion of one eye in early life can produce an intractable impairment of visual acuity in the deprived eye that persists through life. Monocular deprivation (MD) in kittens has been studied as a model of human amblyopia, an analogous condition that arises due to abnormal development of the visual system during early postnatal life. Recently, it has been demonstrated that a short period of 10 days of complete darkness is sufficient to reverse the anatomical and functional changes that result from MD. In the present study we chose to examine levels of matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) activity in the visual cortex of animals reared with various forms of abnormal visual experience in an attempt to elucidate the molecular mechanisms that underlie deprivation-induced neural changes, and also those that underlie recovery from MD. Our examination of MMP activity revealed an increase in MMP-2 activity in V1 of kittens after 7 days of monocular deprivation that was coincident with a loss of the MMP substrate, aggrecan.

authors_raw: Matthew J. Smithen, Kaitlyn Holman, Donald Mitchell & Kevin Duffy
title: Recovery from monocular deprivation in kittens induced by ten days of darkness depends upon the acuity in the non-deprived eye and requires a period of binocular vision
abstract: Amblyopia is a condition that results in a deficit in vision and is modelled primarily in kittens by depriving one eye of patterned input for a defined period. Recently, it was discovered that ten days of darkness could promote the recovery of vision in kittens with experimentally induced amblyopia. Experiments were designed, first, to test whether ten days of darkness could promote recovery when the monocular deprivation was extended to eight weeks of age. Second, whether the recovery of vision in the deprived eye was boot-strapped by the neural connections established with the non-deprived eye. Third, if a period of binocular vision was necessary for the recovery seen following ten days of dark rearing.

authors_raw: Stephane Maclean, Yoko Ishigami-Doyle, Cameron Hassall, Olave Krigolson & Gail Eskes
title: ERP Components Underlying Prism Adaptation (PA)
abstract: Prism adaptation (PA) is an experimental technique used to treat spatial neglect after stroke, but the effects are inconsistent over studies, suggesting a need to understand better the underlying mechanisms. Redding and Wallace suggested that the adaptation process involves two components: An immediate strategic recalibration of visuomotor aiming (top-down) and a slower visuo-motor spatial realignment process (bottom-up). The purpose of our study was to examine whether we could detect these components by measuring event-related potentials (ERPs) that are generated during the visuomotor adaptation process induced by exposure to visual field-displacing goggles while reaching. The findings identified two ERPs associated with movement completion that dissociate over the time-course of adaptation: an invariant ERN response sensitive to error commission that could reflect realignment and a P300 response that decreases over time that could reflect conscious visuomotor re-calibration. Implications of these findings for understanding prism adaptation and treatment of neglect will be discussed. Friday's Posters

authors_raw: Yoko Ishigami, Stewart Longman, Amanda Tyndall, Grazyna Burek, Melanie Denheyer, Marc Poulin, Gail Eskes
title: Relationship of the components of the Attention Network Test-Interaction (ANT-I) to standardized measures of attention and executive function
abstract: The Attention Network Test is a useful computer-based tool for measuring the three attention networks (alerting, orienting, and executive) within a single 20-miniute session. We examined relationships between the attention networks and the global performance measured with the Attention Network - Interaction (ANT-I) and standardized neuropsychological tests clinically used to measure different domains of cognitive functioning: attention, processing speed, memory, executive function with healthy older adults (N=181). We found that better executive network scores and better global performance from the ANT-I were associated with processing speed, memory, and executive function as measured by the standardized neuropsychological tests. The results provide insights into the psychometrics of the ANT-I and its potential utility in clinical research settings.

authors_raw: Luke Burke, Laura Alsip, Nicole Hodder, Sarah Vokey, Cameron Hassall, and Olav Krigolson
title: Neuroeconomics: Changes in neural activity during learning
abstract: The purpose of our research was to investigate the brain basis of what it means to be an expert. We recorded EEG while participants completed a perceptual discrimination task and examined two particular components of the event-related potential: the N250 and the reward positivity. Participants showed an enhancement of the N250, thought to index familiarity, for familiar stimuli (previously-learned, computer-generated "blobs") compared to unfamiliar stimuli (novel blobs). We also observed a decrease in the reward positivity, a positive deflection in response to unexpected rewards, for familiar stimuli compared to unfamiliar stimuli. Thus, in addition to perceptual familiarity, one of the hallmarks of expertise may be that feedback type (correct/incorrect) is more expected, evoking a diminished reward response.

authors_raw: Joshua P. Salmon, Graham C. Wilson, Raymond M. Klein, & Gail A. Eskes
title: A step towards building better video "games" for rehabilitation: A survey investigating entertainment preferences in adults
abstract: As the healthcare system becomes increasingly burdened with demands for rehabilitation services, the need for accessible therapies is growing. Video game approaches offer a promising supplement to existing therapy programs. Research has shown that video games can improve such cognitive functions as memory, processing speed, executive function and attention in both young and older adults. The current challenge however, is finding (or designing) video game approaches that simultaneously match the therapeutic needs of the patient population while remaining fun and engaging. As part of a larger research program, to develop better rehabilitation software or "games" for patients recovering from Stroke or Parkinson's disease, the current study conducted a nation-wide survey of adult entertainment preferences. Measures of favorite television, book, film and movie genres were also collected, as well as information about frequency of use of various technologies, devices and video games. The results from this research offer a window into how adults spend their time with technology, and what approaches in designing new software for older adults might be most effective. The ultimate goal of this research is to apply this information to design more engaging game-like therapies that will encourage and motivate patient rehabilitation.

title: Drosophila Melanogaster
abstract:
Mitochondria produce energy, in the form of ATP, to carry out cellular work. Our experiment
hypothesized that larger mitochondrial volumes are present where greater energy demands exist
in visual neurons of fruit flies (Drosophila melanogaster). We predicted that L1 neurons, the
more energetically intensive neurons, would have greater mitochondrial volume and surface area
densities than L2 neurons. We predicted that mitochondrial volume would be greatest in the
presynaptic area, where neurotransmitter cycling occurs. Using Fiji Image J we traced
mitochondria in electron micrograph stacks of four L1 and six L2 neurons to reconstruct the
three-dimensional mitochondrial distributions for comparison between neuron types and boutons.
Z-tests demonstrated L2 neurons have significantly greater mitochondrial volume and surface
area densities; therefore, greater energy requirements. Greater mitochondrial volumes in the
postsynaptic terminals of both neurons indicated greater local energy demand. Further research
is required to extend this hypothesis to neuron classes in other strata.

authors_raw: Ashley Howse, Marcel Peloquin, S. Doherty, Cam Hassall & Olav Krigolson
title: The Impact of Socioeconomic Status on Reward Learning in the Medial-Frontal Cortex
abstract: While there is strong evidence that a system involved in reward processing exists within the human medial-frontal cortex, there is still a lot to learn about the factors that impact the sensitivity and functional efficacy of this system. Behavioural studies have indicated that children from low socioeconomic (SES) backgrounds may be more sensitive to rewards than those from privileged backgrounds. While children of higher SES outperform their disadvantaged counterparts on cognitive tasks, children of lower SES show greater improvement when rewards are offered for task performance compared to those of higher SES (Sweet & Ringness, 1971; Johnson, 1974). In the present study, we sought to examine the effect of childhood SES on the reward processing system within the human medial frontal cortex. Participants completed a learnable gambling task while electroencephalographic data were recorded. In line with previous work, we found that reward and loss feedback elicited a feedback error-related negativity (fERN; Miltner et al., 1997). Further, and importantly, we found that that the amplitude of the fERN is reduced for participants from low SES compared to high SES backgrounds--a result that suggests individuals from low SES backgrounds are more sensitive to rewards than their privileged counterparts. Additionally, a moderate correlation was observed between SES and fERN amplitude suggesting the reward sensitivity decreases as SES increases.

title: Personality Differences Among Smokers and Non-Smokers
abstract:
Past Research has shown there is a clear link between some personality traits and substance use.
Our research will tackle the question of whether there is a measureable difference between the
personalities of students that smoke cigarettes and those who do not. We accomplished this by
comparing personality dimensions in 432 high school smoking and non-smoking students with
the Substance Use Risk Profile Scale (SURPS) and the Connor-Davidson Resilience Scale 10
(CD-RISC). The SURPS measured hopelessness (HI), anxiousness (AS), impulsivity (IMP) and
sensation seeking (SS). The CD-RISC measured resiliency. Smokers were found to have higher
hopelessness and sensation seeking as well as lower resiliency scores than non-smokers, and
within the smokers, those with high resiliency started smoking later in life than those with low
resiliency. Smokers with high HI attended fewer smoking cessation sessions, and those who
completed the program were found to have lower HI and SS scores. This suggests that
individuals with different personality traits would benefit from different treatment types, for
example, smokers with high HI may benefit from a focus on self-worth in their treatment.
Furthermore, high resiliency appears to delay or prevent the onset of smoking, so increasing
resiliency in our children is a step that can be taken in the future.

title: Introduction
abstract:
A historical overview of the people and events in our department from at least 1970. This year I
will focus on the contributions of Jeannette McGlone and Heather Schellinck, who will retire this
year.

authors_raw: Sean Mackinnon, D.F. Selterman, E. Chagnon and M. Furda
