title: Mate preferences in speed-dating: Do women really prefer men with higher earning prospects?
abstract: As part of a large-scale, collaborative research project to test the reproducibility of scientific results in psychology, we sought to replicate key findings from Eastwick and Finkel (2008). Specifically, we hypothesized - contrary to evolutionary theory - that women would not display a preference for mates with higher perceived earning prospects in a live-attraction (speed-dating) paradigm, compared to men. Participants (N=307) went on brief, 4-minute "dates" with potential romantic partners. Participants rated dates on attraction and relationship initiation variables after speed-dating. We also followed participants for 30 days following the speed-dating to explore actual dating experiences. Results showed the perception of greater earning prospects was associated with greater romantic interest in potential partners, r=.17 [.05, .29], but this effect was not moderated by gender χ2(1)=0.89, p=.46. We successfully replicated the findings from Eastwick and Finkel (2008), suggesting both men women and men prefer partners with higher earning prospects, contrary to evolutionary theory.

title: The squeeze is on: Increased competition for faculty positions has ramifications for the recruitment of women into academia.
abstract: The number of women faculty in the sciences has increased steadily over the last 40 years. However, relatively fewer women than men make the transition from doctoral student to faculty member. The reason for the loss of these highly skilled individuals from academic science remains unclear. Both motherhood and a bias against women in science have been hypothesized to be the main culprits. I present evidence that excessive competition for faculty positions disproportionately affects women with children. The ratio of academic positions available, given the number of doctoral students trained, has been falling over the last decade in Canada. Studies in the US have shown that women doctoral students and post docs with children are less likely to obtain a faculty position than single women or men. A comparison with medicine demonstrates how motherhood need not be a barrier to womens' participation in academic science.

authors_raw: Rebecca Tucker, Patrick McGrath, Anna Huguet, Darlene Inglis, Marsha Campbell-Yeo, Michelle LeDrew
title: Breastfeeding in the NICU: An exploratory study of barriers and facilitators from the perspectives of mothers and health-care professionals.
abstract: Breastfeeding is known to have health benefits for both infants and their mothers. Based on the evidence of these benefits, the World Health Organization's recommendation is to exclusively breastfeed infants for their first six months. Despite this, we continue to see low numbers of Canadian mothers that are still exclusively breastfeeding their infants at six months of age. For infants who have been admitted to the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU), the number of mothers who are still exclusively breastfeeding at six months appears to be even lower. In this study, we will be talking to both mothers and health-care professionals in an attempt to understand their experiences and get their perceptions of the challenges to, and facilitators of, breastfeeding infants who have been admitted to the NICU. We are also interested in what they suggest as possible solutions and supports that would have helped them (or their patients, in the case of health-care professionals). Ideally, this research will provide important information that can then be used to develop an interactive web-based instructional program aimed at supporting sustained breastfeeding for mothers who have had infants admitted

authors_raw: Amanda LeRoux, Lisa Wright, Tara Perrot, Benjamin Rusak
title: Impact of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Endocrine Effects of Partial Sleep Restriction in Healthy Women
abstract: There is extensive evidence that sleep restriction alters endocrine function in healthy young men, increasing afternoon cortisol levels and modifying levels of other hormones that regulate metabolism. Recent studies have confirmed these effects in young women, but have not investigated whether menstrual cycle phase influences these responses. The effects on cortisol levels of limiting sleep to 3 h for one night were assessed in two groups of women at different points in their menstrual cycles: mid-follicular and mid-luteal. Eighteen healthy, young women, not taking oral contraceptives (age: 21.8 ± 0.54; BMI: 22.6 ± 0.63 [mean ± SEM]), were studied. Baseline sleep durations, eating habits and menstrual cycles were monitored. Salivary samples were collected at six times of day (08:00, 08:30, 11:00, 14:00, 17:00, 20:00) during two consecutive days: first after a 10 h overnight sleep opportunity (Baseline) and then after a night with a 3 h sleep opportunity (Sleep Restricted). All were awakened at the same time of day. Women in the follicular phase showed a significant decrease (p =0.004) in their cortisol awakening responses (CAR) after sleep restriction and a sustained elevation in afternoon/evening cortisol levels (p =0.005), as has been reported for men. Women in the luteal phase showed neither a depressed CAR, nor an increase in afternoon/evening cortisol levels. Menstrual cycle phase dramatically altered the responses of healthy, young women to a single night of sleep restriction, implicating effects of spontaneous changes in endocrine status on metabolic responses to sleep loss.

authors_raw: Adria Markovich, Melissa Gendron and Penny V. Corkum
title: Validating the Children's Sleep Habits Questionnaire Against Polysomnography in School-Aged Children
abstract: Sleep is a vital physiological behaviour in children's development, and as such it is important to be able to efficiently and accurately assess whether children display difficulties with sleep quality and quantity. The Children's Sleep Habits Questionnaire (CSHQ; Owens, Spirito, & McGuinn, 2000) is one of the most commonly used diagnostic tools for pediatric sleep problems. However, this instrument has never been validated directly against the gold standard of sleep measurement (i.e., polysomnography; PSG), and studies comparing it to actigraphy are limited. The current study assessed the validity of the CSHQ via direct comparison with PSG for 30 typically developing school-aged children (ages 6 to 12). Significant correlations were expected between the four CSHQ subscale scores that were analyzed (i.e., Sleep Onset Delay, Sleep Duration, Night Wakings, and Sleep Disordered Breathing) and related sleep parameters as measured via PSG. Furthermore, these CSHQ subscales were predicted to accurately identify 80% of children meeting related PSG criteria for a sleep disorder. Surprisingly, Pearson's correlation analyses revealed no significant correlations between CSHQ subscales and PSG variables. In addition, sensitivity and specificity analyses of the CSHQ subscales revealed consistently low sensitivity and high specificity. Combined, the CSHQ Sleep Onset Delay, Sleep Duration, Night Wakings, and Sleep Disordered Breathing subscales showed low construct validity and diagnostic validity. These results demonstrate weak support for the overall clinical utility of the CSHQ as a comprehensive screening tool for sleep difficulties in school-aged children.

authors_raw: Roxanne Sterniczuk, Benjamin Rusak, Kenneth Rockwood
title: Sleep disturbance and daytime sleepiness increase dementia risk in healthy individuals independent of overall health status
abstract: This study examined the relationship between sleep disturbance and overall health status, in the development of incident dementia. Secondary analyses were conducted on cognitively healthy older males who participated in the Honolulu-Asia Aging Study (N = 3,852). A 9-item 'sleep disturbance index' consisting of factors related to nighttime insomnia and daytime sleepiness, was compared to a 60-item 'frailty index' (FI) consisting of various health-related factors. Controlling for age, education, body mass index, and FI, the 'sleep disturbance index' was significantly predictive of incident dementia after an average of 2.9 years. Individual items 'waking up several times at night' and 'sleepy most of the day', were strong predictors in risk models that included the FI; however the FI was not a significant predictor in our models. Sleep disturbance appears to be an important risk factor in the preclinical stage of incident dementia (i.e., prior to the manifestation of cognitive decline).

authors_raw: Sherry Stewart, N. Castellanos-Ryan, F. Vitaro and P.J. Conrod
title: Why my Friends Drink is Why I Drink
abstract:
Peers may influence one another's drinking behavior through social influences on drinking
motives. A sample of 2,659 adolescents (median age = 14.5 at baseline) in 20 schools across 11
boroughs of London, UK participated. At each of 4 waves, spaced at 6 month intervals,
participants completed measures of their own risky internal drinking motives and their own
binge drinking frequency. At each wave, students also nominated up to five students who were
their closest friends. We created two scores for each individual representing coping motives and
enhancement motives in the peer group by averaging the coping motives and enhancement
motives scores of his/her nominated friends. Using cross-lagged analyses we showed that, over
time, the internal drinking motives of the peer group influenced individual binge drinking by
way of influencing individual internal drinking motives. Moreover, peer influences on drinking
motives occurred later in adolescence for coping motives than for enhancement motives.

title: Early Life Models of Anxiety: The Role of Age, Sex and the Environment
abstract: The early environment (prenatal and postnatal) shapes stress response systems, ultimately affecting adult brain and behaviour. We have developed a novel model of prenatal stress coupled with postnatal enrichment to investigate the expression of anxiety-like behaviours in adult rats. Moms were exposed to a live cat 3 times a day for each day (prenatal stress; or a control condition) during the last week of gestation and then either housed in standard cages or enhanced home cages. Offspring behaviour was examined in the juvenile period as well as in adulthood. Anxiety was reduced by prenatal stress in juvenile males, but not females, but effects were gone by adulthood. Prenatal stress also reduced play behaviour in the juvenile period. The combination of prenatal stress and postnatal enrichment produced complex results.

authors_raw: Ashley Howse, Marcel Peloquin, Cameron Hassall and Olav Krigolson
title: Is my hangover affecting my ability to learn? Neurological evidence from electroencephalography
abstract: While there is strong evidence for a reward processing system involved with learning within the human medial-frontal cortex, a lot remains unclear about the factors that influence the functional efficacy of this system. Behavioural evidence suggests that alcohol hangover impacts motor control and cognitive functioning (Cherpitel et al., 1998; Verster, 2007). In the present study, we aimed to provide the first neurological evidence of an impairment of the reward processing system of the human medial fontal cortex during alcohol hangover. Participants completed a learnable gambling task while EEG data were recorded. In line with previous work, we found that the feedback error-related negativity (fERN; Miltner et al., 1997) was elicited by wins and losses during this paradigm. Further, and importantly, a reduction in fERN amplitude was observed between hangover and control participants. Additionally, a moderate correlation was observed between fERN magnitude and hangover severity. These data indicate the magnitude of the impairment increases with hangover severity. The results of the present study are important for public education and safety, as an impairment of the reward processing system may implicate individuals' ability to execute corrective behaviours--processes necessary for successfully executing potentially dangerous tasks such as driving a motor vehicle.

authors_raw: Cynthia Ramasubbu, Simon Sherry, Chantal Gautreau, Dayna Sherry, Logan Nealis, Heather Hadjistavropoulos, and Sherry Stewart
title: Do Catastrophizing of Bodily Sensations and Health-Related Reassurance-Seeking Maintain Health-Related Anxiety?
abstract: The impact of catastrophizing of bodily sensations (i.e., rumination about, overconcern with, and intolerance of bodily sensations) and health-related reassurance-seeking (i.e., seeking care from others regarding one's perceived health problems) on health-related anxiety (i.e., anxiety about perceived health problems) is unknown. We hypothesized catastrophizing of bodily sensations and health-related reassurance-seeking maintain health-related anxiety, and catastrophizing of bodily sensations predicts health-related reassurance-seeking. A sample of 155 young adults was studied using a 28-day daily diary study with longitudinal follow-up. We found results consistent with two hypotheses: Catastrophizing of bodily sensations maintained health-related anxiety, and catastrophizing of bodily sensations predicted health-related reassurance-seeking. A cyclical, self-perpetuating pattern was observed wherein catastrophizing of bodily sensations and health- related anxiety contribute to one another over time.

authors_raw: Annie Chinneck, Sean McKinnon and Sherry Stewart
title: Investigating Possible Reciprocal Relationships between Symptoms of Depression and Pathological Gambling in Emerging Adults
abstract: Based on literature linking emotional disorders and problem gambling, the present study tested bidirectional relationships between depression and pathological gambling in emerging adults. It was hypothesized that depressive and pathological gambling symptomology would be positively related at baseline, and that depression would confer risk for increased gambling over time, and vice versa. A sample of 517 Manitobans aged 18 to 20 years completed a series of questionnaires over four waves and five years (2007 to 2011) as part of the Manitoba Longitudinal Study of Young Adults. Data were analysed using inflated zero univariate growth curves. Depression and pathological gambling were positively related at Wave 1. Furthermore, having symptoms of depression at Wave 1 was related to a significant increase in pathological gambling symptoms over time. The reverse was not supported, however, and engaging in pathological gambling at Wave 1 was not related to changes in depression symptomology over time. Depression may lead to escalations in gambling problems over time via self-medication. This has treatment implications in that, while depression affects changes in problem gambling, the reverse is not true. Young adults who are depressed may therefore be a subgroup who should be targeted for problem gambling prevention.

title: A longitudinal exploration of advantageous and disadvantageous inequity aversion in children
abstract: How children make decisions in situations of disadvantageous inequity, (DI) where one can split resources equally or allow one's partner to receive more, has received little attention. Though research has explored decisions made in situations of advantageous inequity, (AI) -such as sharing- more thoroughly, little is known regarding how the developmental trajectories of these two forms of inequity aversion compare, and whether they are related to one another. In this longitudinal study, two forms of AI, and two forms of DI (cost and no cost) were explored in children at three time points; 4.5 years, 5 years, and 5.5 years of age. Using a forced choice resource allocation task, relationships between the two forms of each type of inequity aversion were investigated, and relationships across the three time points for AI, and DI were examined independently. Finally, the relationship between AI and DI was explored. For both AI and DI, cost and no cost variables were correlated at each time point. Correlations across all time points were observed in AI trials, and grew stronger with age, while only one relationship was observed over time in DI trials (between times one and two). A positive relationship (indicating a preference for equity) was observed between AI and DI at time one, whereas no relationships were observed later in development. These results suggest that AI and DI do indeed have distinct developmental trajectories, and that the relationship between them changes with age

title: "Give It Away, Give It Away, Give It Away Now": Children's Sharing Generosity & Receiving Preferences in Contexts of Anonymity
abstract: People engage in prosocial behaviours, such as sharing, from a very young age. Numerous factors, including anonymity, can influence sharing. While anonymity has been shown to decrease adults' generosity, it remains unknown whether anonymity influences children's generosity. Two experiments examined the impact of anonymity on children's sharing behaviour. Experiment 1 investigated if children understood anonymity, and whether their generosity was influenced when they shared as anonymous or identified donors. Experiment 2 examined whether children preferred to receive from donors who wished to be anonymous, or from donors who wished to be identified to the child. Findings from these experiments suggest that children understood the concept of anonymous sharing, and preferred to receive from identified donors. However, unlike adults, children's generosity did not decrease when sharing anonymously.

title: Assessing the Relation Between Subjective Cognitive Impairment, Neurological Functioning, and Psychosocial Symptoms in Multiple Sclerosis Patients
abstract: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) is a degenerative neurological disorder that impacts quality of life. Among the symptoms, 65% of patients experience cognitive impairments (e.g., working memory, processing speed) throughout the course of their illness. Complicating this, many MS patients experience subjective mood symptoms of depression and anxiety that can distort accurate knowledge of cognitive impairments, resulting in patients concluding that their cognitive impairments are more severe than has been objectively measured. The current study will investigate subjective and objective cognitive impairments and how subjective mood can moderate this relationship. Participants of the study will be 354 adults with clinically definite MS from the Dalhousie MS Research Unit database. Moderation analysis will determine if anxiety or depression ratings act as a moderator between subjective and objective cognitive performance. Further analysis will employ structural equation modeling to determine whether subjective mood or other assessment measures of disability will be effective in accounting for the variability between subjective and objective cognitive impairment ratings.

authors_raw: Stephanie Sobey, Dr. Patricia McMullen, Dr. Heath Matheson
title: Face Processing and Gender Recognition: An Eye-Tracking Study
abstract:
Purpose: During social interactions, the eyes provide information that can be used to facilitate
effective communication, and even play a role in identifying gender. While some research has
demonstrated that mutual eye contact facilitates gender processing, others report conflicting
results. It is also widely accepted that inverted faces are harder to identify, and this Face
Inversion Effect is caused by a lack of expertise. This lack of expertise causes a shift to featural-
based processing as opposed to the holistic processing applied to upright faces. The current study
incorporates eye-tracking technology to further investigate orientation and gaze direction effects
on gender processing.
Methods: Participants completed a gender categorization task in which face orientation
(upright/inverted) and gaze direction (direct/averted) were manipulated. An eye-tracking device
recorded saccades and fixations within specified interest areas as well as reaction time and
decision accuracy. It is well established that humans are highly efficient at identifying gender;
therefore, faces were morphed into varying degrees of masculinity/femininity to make the task
more difficult and ensure rich eye-tracking data.
Preliminary Results: Upright faces were indeed processed faster and more accurately than
inverted faces and eye-tracking showed a strong preference for the upper portion of the face.
Gaze direction influenced reaction times such that averted gaze was processed faster than direct
gaze - an effect that was modulated by the degree of masculinity/femininity of the face. These
results suggest that mutual eye contact may interfere with gender processing and this interference
is most pronounced in highly masculine faces.

title: A Comprehensive Review of the Neural Correlates, the Mechanisms, and the Involvement of the Primary Motor Cortex (M1) in Motor Imagery
abstract: Motor Imagery (MI) is the mental rehearsal of a movement without the actual overt execution. MI and motor execution (ME) activate overlapping brain regions. Therapeutic interventions targeting lost motor function from neural damage capitalize on ME. In other words, ME drives activation of central motor pathways, which rewires the damaged networks and rehabilitates lost motor function. Given the overlapping neural activation during ME and MI, it follows that MI also has the potential to rehabilitate compromised motor function. The following review explores the similarities and differences between MI and ME. It focuses on the theories for understanding MI --specifically, its neural correlates and mechanisms. Our understanding of MI, however, is greatly hindered by the debated findings regarding the involvement of the primary motor cortex (M1) during MI. This review therefore explores MI theories with an emphasis on the contribution of M1 during MI.

authors_raw: Sarah Kraeutner, A. Gionfriddo, T. Bardouille and Shaun Boe
title: Motor imagery as a modality of skill acquisition: Assessing the neural correlates of motor imagery
abstract: Motor imagery (MI) is a form of practice in which an individual mentally rehearses a motor task, driving plastic changes in the brain and facilitating skill acquisition in the absence of physical practice. Previous research suggests that MI recruits similar brain regions as motor execution (ME), however, this data remains controversial due to methodological limitations. The current research seeks to further establish MI as a secondary modality of skill acquisition by providing electrophysiological evidence, using MEG, of an overlap between brain areas recruited for ME and MI. Participants performed both execution and imagery of a button press task. We demonstrate that regions recruited during ME were also recruited during MI. In contrast with ME, however, spatial extent of activation was greater and additional areas were recruited during MI, suggesting that MI may be a learned skill in itself. These findings further establishes MI as a secondary form of skill acquisition.

authors_raw: Yoko Ishigami, Franziska Kintzel & Gail Eskes
title: Effects of alerting tones on prism adaptation in healthy older adults
abstract:
One therapeutic approach for patients with spatial neglect is prism adaptation (PA), which targets
the orienting deficit in spatial neglect. In the current study, we examined whether combining the
PA technique with alerting tones (to enhance vigilance) increases the PA effects in older adults.
Forty healthy older participants were randomly assigned to one of four conditions that varied in
the presence of PA and frequency of alerting tones during the adaptation period. During the
adaptation period, participants pointed towards targets while wearing clear or prismatic goggles
shifting the visual field rightwards with alerting tones occurring on 0 - 50% of trials. Outcome
measures included subjective-midline tasks (SMTs) and visual search. The greatest after-effects
were observed with the highest frequency of the tones (50%) in an SMT involving both visual
and proprioceptive guidance. We conclude that alerting tones may improve some aspects of PA
treatment in patients with spatial neglect.

authors_raw: Stephanie A. H. Jones, Janet Green and Gail Eskes
title: Beyond the Physical: Can We Improve Thinking and Memory in Parkinson's?
abstract:
Individuals with Parkinson's Disease (PD) experience problems with working memory (WM),
even in the early stages of the disease. However, no standard treatment for WM deficit is
available. We sought to investigate the feasibility and preliminary effectiveness of an intensive
and focused computerized WM training program for individuals in the early stages of PD. A
controlled single blind randomized crossover design was used. One group receives a 'dual n-
back' working memory training task first, and the second group receives a control visuospatial
computer task first. Training includes at home 25 minute sessions, 5 days/week for 5 weeks.
After 5 weeks, the training conditions are crossed over for phase 2. Outcome measures include
WM, fluid intelligence and self-report functional outcomes immediately after each phase, and
after 3 months. Preliminary pre and post training results on selected outcome measures obtained
from 3 participants (2 additional participants are currently enrolled) will be presented.

title: Compromised Attention After Stroke? Maybe Not!
abstract:
Deficits in attention control, an aspect of working memory (WM), are considered one of the most
prominent impairments caused by stroke, but few studies have examined which specific aspects
of attention control are affected. This study tested the ability of 15 stroke survivors and 20
healthy control subjects to ignore distracting information while completing the n-back WM task
under increasing levels of load. We hypothesized increased distractor processing, i.e., longer
reaction times (RT) and higher error rates (ER), for stroke survivors and healthy controls as WM
load increased, and that stroke survivors would be more adversely affected by increased WM
load than the healthy controls. Contrary to our hypotheses stroke survivors and healthy controls
evidenced equivalent distractor processing, suggesting perseveration of some aspects of attention
control after stroke. Furthermore, both groups showed reduced distractor processing during the
most difficult task compared to all other tasks. Implications will be discussed.

authors_raw: Simon Gadbois & Catherine Reeve Training dogs and other animals for scent detection, discrimination
title: Applications of scent processing with canines and other animals 1: Implementation and deployment challenges.
abstract: identification, trailing, and tracking is an emerging field of research with its own challenges. Our lab has explored this field of research for eight years and worked with private and public institutions on specific problems (mostly environmental and medical). We will present the implementation challenges of this research, and the conflicts between the academic and R&D approach. We will then discuss future opportunities and applications and explain why we are expanding our research program to other scenting species.

title: Applications of scent processing with canines and other animals 2: Methodological challenges of canine olfactory psychophysics
abstract: After eight years of trial and error, fine-tuning of procedures and training protocols, our lab has recently focused on a few techniques. We are challenging some common approaches based on psychophysical (stimulus saliency), cognitive (working memory), and neuroscience (olfactomotor activation) principles. Data will be presented to support our new approaches especially in relation to the detection of hypoglycaemia in breath samples from individuals with Type 1 diabetes.

authors_raw: Jordan Fisk, Sean Aitken, Tara Perrot, Leslie Phillmore
title: Song Learning Under Stress: Does single parenting affect song development in zebra finches?
abstract: Young male songbirds such as zebra finches learn their song from listening to and memorizing their father's song and then practicing until they produce a "good copy". Memorization begins at about 25 days post-hatch, before the birds can produce song, active practice begins at about 35 days, and song crystallizes at adulthood - about 90 days. We were interested in whether having a single father throughout development, when typically both the mother and the father contribute to offspring care, would affect a son's ability to produce a good copy of adult song. To do this we removed the father or mother four days after eggs hatched, allowed birds to mature, and then compared recordings of adult offspring to their father. We found that birds reared without a model (single mother) had poor copies of fathers song as expected, but birds reared with a single father were not as good at copying father's song on several measures compared to birds reared with both parents.

title: Human Sensitivity to Single Temporal Irregularities
abstract:
There is remarkably little evidence on human sensitivity to auditory temporal irregularities. This
is important because deficits in neural timing are implicated in poor perception in auditory
pathologies. Building on previous work using the jitter paradigm, experiments will measure
human sensitivity to single temporal irregularities within 25-click trains having inter-click
intervals (ICI) between 5 and 160 ms. Thresholds will be measured as a function of ICI in four
different conditions which vary across two dimensions; direction of click shift (backward or
forward in time), and position of subsequent clicks (block shifted or not). Block shifting of
subsequent clicks results in a stimulus with only one aberrant ICI. Stimuli will be presented in
two forms; as wide-band clicks, as well as high-pass clicks in the presence of a low frequency
noise masker. It is expected that performance will vary as a function of ICI, as has been seen in
previous work. It is further expected that thresholds will vary between conditions, with higher
thresholds on conditions containing only one aberrant ICI.

authors_raw: Kaitlyn Holman, Kevin Duffy and Donald Mitchell
title: In contrast to the effects observed in kittens, 10 days of darkness does not restore neural or visual plasticity in adult cats.
abstract: Recent investigations show that 10days of darkness imposed on kittens can rejuvenate cortical plasticity and promote fast recovery of vision in kittens with stable, experimentally induced amblyopia. Here, we report effects of darkness on neural and visual plasticity in adult cats. First, we report the effect of darkness on the vision of adult cats with stable, experimentally induced amblyopia. Second, we report the effect of darkness on the vision of a normal adult cat when imposed before a period of monocular deprivation (MD). Third, we report effects of darkness on recovery of soma size and neurofilament immunolabeling in the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) of adult cats that had experienced long-term MD. Finally, we report effects of darkness on LGN soma size and neurofilament immunolabeling in normal adult cats when imposed before MD. In all cases, 10days of darkness did not promote restoration of neural or visual plasticity in adult cats.

title: Orientation Plasticity in Mouse Primary Visual Cortex
abstract:
Information processing in the visual system is shaped by recent stimulus history, such that
prolonged viewing of an adaptor can alter the perception of subsequently presented stimuli and
modify the visual response properties of neurons in multiple brain areas. In the tilt-aftereffect,
the perceived orientation of a grating is often repelled away from the orientation of a previously
viewed adaptor grating. A potential neural correlate for this tilt-aftereffect has been described in
cat and macaque primary visual cortex (V1), where adaptation produces repulsive shifts in the
orientation tuning curves of V1 neurons. In these animals, columns of V1 neurons are clustered
into iso-orientation domains and pinwheel centers, and there is evidence that orientation
adaptation is more prevalent near pinwheel centers. We investigated orientation adaptation in
mouse V1 with the aim of establishing a genetically tractable model to study this form of
plasticity. We were also curious about how orientation adaptation might differ in a species
known to lack pinwheel columnar organization and have a lower proportion of orientation tuned
neurons. We used stimulus protocols that were readily comparable with previous studies, and
found that orientation adaptation caused repulsive shifts in the majority of orientation tuned
neurons, and a general decrease in firing in neurons that were not selective for orientation.

authors_raw: Kazunori Shinomiya and Ian A. Meinertzhagen
title: Candidate neural substrate of a motion detection circuit in the visual system of Drosophila
abstract: In the fruit fly's visual motion pathways, two cell types - T4 and T5 - are the first known relay neurons to signal small-field direction-selective motion responses. I report that multiple types of transmedulla (Tm) neurons provide inputs to T5 at their terminals in the third neuropil, or lobula. From L2 cells, the OFF-channel neuron in the lamina, motion information comes from three types of Tm cells, including Tm1, 2 and 4. Additional input to T5 comes from Tm9, the medulla target of a lamina interneuron L3. Input synapses from Tm2 and Tm9 are spatially segregated on T5's dendritic arbor, providing candidate anatomical substrates for the two arms of a T5 EMD circuit. Transcript profiling indicates that T5 expresses both nicotinic and muscarinic cholinoceptors, qualifying T5 to receive cholinergic inputs from Tm9 and Tm2, which both express ChAT. I hypothesize that T5 computes small-field motion signals by integrating multiple cholinergic Tm inputs using nicotinic and muscarinic cholinoceptors.

authors_raw: Austin Korgan, Tara Perrot, Ian Weaver
title: Prenatal paternal stress and postnatal enhanced home cage affect maternal care and anxiety like behaviour in juvenile rats
abstract: Paternal effects have recently drawn interest from neuroscientists, within the context of epigenetic transmission, largely following evidence from behavioural ecology and epidemiology. Recent studies have demonstrated offspring effects of paternal age, obesity, enrichment, and psychological stress. These outcomes may be partially manifested through differences in maternal care. For the current study, we used predator odour exposure to stress male Long-Evans rats for 7 days, immediately preceding a partner preference test, followed by breeding. On the day of parturition, dams were transferred to enhanced home cages (EHC), which are approximately twice the size of a standard cage (SC) and contain a burrow, or SC. Maternal care was observed for 10 days in both conditions. At P25,two male and two female offspring were observed for social play behaviour for 5 days. Anxiety-like behaviour was examined using the elevated plus maze and open-field test on P32-35. Then, between P35-40 offspring were exposed to a predator odour for 30-minutes and sacrificed for immunohistochemical detection of GH and NPY.

title: How do student-generated questions relate to comprehension, self-efficacy and achievement in an undergraduate chemistry course?
abstract: Since questions can both indicate knowledge deficits and facilitate knowledge construction, it is unclear how student-generated questions relate to comprehension and ability beliefs during classroom learning. This study examines the relations among characteristics of students' questions, perceived comprehension of lectures, self-efficacy beliefs, and exam scores in an undergraduate chemistry course. Participants (N =103) recorded questions (n = 980) in written logs across 8 consecutive lectures. Questions were coded for various qualities. Results from path analyses indicate that different question characteristics have different relations with perceived comprehension and achievement. The percentage of unanswered questions was associated with lower perceived comprehension. Questions that clarified or indicated narrow knowledge deficits were positively associated with perceived comprehension and exam scores. Perceived comprehension predicted situated efficacy beliefs more strongly than did general self-efficacy beliefs for the course. Findings are discussed in light of implications and on-going research.

authors_raw: Laura Lambe, Sean MacKinnon, L. Cartwright, M. Korecky, M. Orr, S.J. Park, D. Song, and Sherry Stewart Stewart
title: Dyadic conflict, drinking to cope, and alcohol-related problems: A 4-week longitudinal study
abstract: The motivational model of alcohol use posits that individuals may consume alcohol to cope with negative affect. Conflict is a strong predictor of coping motives, which consistently predict alcohol-related problems. The current study tested the link between dyadic conflict, coping motives, and alcohol-related problems in romantic couples. It was hypothesized that (a) the association between dyadic conflict and alcohol-related problems would be mediated by coping- depression and coping-anxiety motives, (b) that this would be true for actor and partner effects, and (c) when controlling for enhancement motives. Participants (N=70 couples) completed weekly questionnaires for four weeks. Data was analyzed using a longitudinal Actor Partner Interdependence Model. Only coping-depression motives independently mediated the conflict- alcohol problems relationship. This held for actor effects, but not for partner effects. Results remained significant when controlling for enhancement motives. Overall, results suggested certain individuals may be coping maladaptively by turning to alcohol following relationship

authors_raw: William Gendron, Dr. Richard E. Brown, Dr. Younes Anini
title: Alzheimer's Disease and 5XFAD Mice: A Study of Hormonal Abnormalities and Metabolism
abstract: The 5XFAD mouse is a double transgenic model of Alzheimer's disease (AD), which carries an amyloid precursor protein (APP) transgene with three mutations, and a presenilin-1 transgene with two mutations. These mutant transgenes act additively to produce massive increases in beta amyloid (AB)-peptides, and the development of AB-plaques by two months of age. Therefore, the 5XFAD mouse is useful for investigating AB-pathology in AD and for assessing the efficacy of novel therapeutics in reducing AB-plaque load. In our lab, we have previously observed age- related weight-loss in the 5xFAD mouse. Weight-loss is also a common problem in human AD patients. Our objective is to investigate feeding behaviour and feeding-related hormone levels in 5xFAD mice and their WT controls. Several hormones may be involved with this characteristic weight loss, including ghrelin leptin, insulin, thyroid hormones, testosterone, and estrogen. In this study we will collect blood and measure the concentration of the hormones previously stated. Muscle tissue will be collected to evaluate whether mice suffer from sarcopenia (muscle loss), and fat tissue to identify whether weight loss may be attributed with fat loss. We hypothesize that there will be feeding and weight differences, hormonal abnormalities, sarcopenia, all of which indicate an abnormal metabolic state. This project will clarify the mechanisms underlying the feeding abnormalities and weight loss in the 5xFAD mouse model, which parallel human AD symptoms.

title: The moral bases of John Woolman's opposition to slavery: A comparison of qualitative and quantitative analyses.
abstract: How John Woolman came to play a seminal role in the movement to abolish slavery that began in the mid-18th century has recently become an issue of philosophical discussion. My interest is in a psychological account of the moral bases of Woolman's role in this movement. Thus far I have focused on a qualitative analysis of Woolman's moral development using Sylvan Tomkins' Script Theory to interpret entries in his Journal, an autobiographical document that traces his moral life history and includes discussion of his activities in opposition to slavery. For this talk, I will compare this analysis to findings from a statistical analysis of words used in the Journal that correspond to Jonathan Haidt's Moral Foundations Theory. The comparison of the two theoretical approaches and empirical methods can provide independent light on the value of each theory as well as a deeper understanding of the origins for Woolman's moral opposition to slavery.

title: You Can Take a Peek Under the Covers Without Taking a p.
abstract:
Several recent papers have discussed various problems with the way statistics are commonly
done including data peeking. It can result in inflation of the Type I error rate to unknown, and
sometimes large, amounts. It is strongly discouraged. I discuss how data peeking can be done
safely and without any of the typical issues that argue for its abolishment. On the contrary, I
argue that proper and disciplined data peeking should be encouraged.

authors_raw: Kiera O'Neil, Max Hauser, Vivian Eng, Cheryl Frenck-Mestre and Aaron J Newman
title: Second-language vocabulary training strategies and their impact on learning outcomes and brain function
abstract: The goal of the proposed study is to assess the efficacy of two second-language (L2) vocabulary training strategies on learning outcomes and brain activation. Words in a language novel to participants (Spanish) will be taught using LANGA (Copernicus Studios, Halifax, NS), a set of computer games using automated speech recognition. A vocabulary of 84 words (none of which were English cognates) will be taught over 9, half hour sessions. Half the words will be taught via paired association (i.e. visual representation paired with individual written Spanish word) and half will be taught contextually (i.e. visual representation paired with written Spanish sentences), with word assignment balanced across participants. Participants are to be assessed on Spanish vocabulary knowledge before and after training using both a picture naming task and a picture- word match/mismatch task. ERPs (event-related potentials) will be recorded during the latter task, with the expectation of a larger N400 ERP component post-training, as the N400 is an index of lexical access. After training, subjects should able to name significantly more items than before training. Mismatched picture-word pairs should elicit an N400 effect after, but not before training. The N400 will greater for words taught contextually than for those taught by paired association. This research will demonstrate the efficacy of automated speech recognition in teaching vocabulary in a new language, and suggest that contextual teaching strategies appear to foster better second language retention.

title: Investigating the factors that influence lexical stress processing in English disyllabic words.
abstract: The current study examined adults' processing of letter strings as cues to lexical stress. In English orthography, lexical stress is often associated with patterns of letter strings, typically found in word endings. Adult participants completed a lexical decision task. We selected items that varied in their number of stress friends, i.e. words sharing the same word ending and the same lexical stress. For example, label and novel are stress friends because they end in -el and take first-syllable stress. Using by-items linear regression, we analyzed the impact of the proportion of stress friends, the absolute number of stress friends, and the frequency of stress friends on reaction time. We determined that only the proportion of stress friends predicted reaction time. These findings suggest that adult readers are sensitive to spelling regularities that predict lexical stress and make use of these regularities in their lexical stress processing. We interpret these results within the context of statistical learning theory.

authors_raw: Thérèse M. Chevalier, Aaron J. Newman
title: Understanding Cortical Activation Associated with Second Language Acquisition
abstract:
Although transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) has been proposed as treatment for
individuals with aphasia following a stroke, the function of the brain regions that are the
proposed targets of TMS are not well understood. The present study aims to understand cortical
activation associated with effortful phonetic processing of a second language in order to add to
our understanding of language learning circuitry and provide a basis for further research on
treatment of language disorders such as aphasia. Thirty-four healthy adults underwent three
computer-based Spanish language-learning sessions, an fMRI scan, and a session of inhibitory
TMS, which targeted regions of the left or right hemisphere that were active during fMRI
naming tasks. Consistent with our hypothesis, similar patterns of activation were found at the
group level during picture naming tasks in English and Spanish. However, no clear patterns
emerged following TMS based on stimulation site. Implications for further research will be
discussed.

authors_raw: Alexandria Muise-Hennessey, Sean McWhinney, and Aaron J. Newman
title: The relationship between implicit sequence learning, speech in noise perception, and working memory.
abstract: Language processing involves manipulation and encoding of sequential patterns, and some work has indicated that sequential memory plays an important role in spoken language processing. Indeed, the ability to implicitly learn sequences is correlated with sentence perception in degraded listening conditions (Conway, C. M., Bauernschmidt, A., Huang, S. S., & Pisoni, D. B. (2010). Cognition, 114, 356-71). We investigated whether the relationship between implicit sequential pattern (a Markovian finite-state artificial grammar) learning and speech-in-noise perception (AzBio) is mediated by cognitive functions such as memory, attention, and executive function. Replicating previous work, we found that implicit sequence learning performance was correlated with speech perception in noise. As well, operation span (a working memory measure) was strongly correlated with speech-in-noise perception but not with implicit sequence learning. suggesting that the relationship between sequence learning and speech-in-noise perception is not mediated by working memory. Additional studies are underway to characterize the relationship between speech-in-noise perception and implicit sequence learning as a function of the signal-to- noise ratio of the speech.

authors_raw: S. Helene Deacon, Shelley Tong, & Kate Cain
title: Morphological and Syntactic Awareness: Understanding the Deficits in Children with Poor reading Comprehension
abstract: Poor comprehenders have intact word reading skills but struggle specifically with understanding what they read. We investigated whether two metalinguistic skills, morphological and syntactic awareness, are specifically related to poor reading comprehension. We compared two groups of children in grade 4 matched on multiple skills: poor comprehenders (n = 15) and average comprehenders (n = 15). The two groups performed comparably on a morphological awareness task that involved both morphological and syntactic cues. However, poor comprehenders performed less well than average comprehenders on a morphological task in which there was no additional syntactic information, and also less well on a syntactic awareness task, in which there were no morphological manipulations. Clearly, poor comprehenders struggle, in comparision to average comprehenders, on tasks targeting each of morphological and syntactic skills separately, but not when the two are combined. Future research needs to identify precisely in what ways these specific metalinguistic difficulties connect to challenges with reading comprehension.

authors_raw: Ralph S. Redden, Cameron D. Hassall & Olave E. Krigolson
title: Champ vs. Chump: Knowledge of Opponent Ability Modulates Reward Processing in Medial-Frontal Cortex
abstract: A series of recent studies (Krigolson & Holroyd, 2006, 2007a, 2007; Krigolson et al., 2008) suggest that a reinforcement learning system within medial-frontal cortex may underlie motor learning. Here, we sought to further that work by examining how expertise modulated reward evaluation by the medial-frontal system. More specifically, we were interested in whether learned knowledge of an opponent's skill level would impact reward processing (i.e. amplitude of the neural response to the presentation of the opponent). In the present study we had participants play rock, paper, scissors against virtual "opponents" while electroencephalographic (EEG) data was recorded. In the key manipulation, participants played three opponents - one who was skilled highly (won 60% of games), skilled poorly (won 20% of games), and skilled moderately (won 33% of games). A post experiment analysis of the EEG data revealed that a reward positivity was elicited when participant wins were contrasted with losses (Holroyd et al., 2008). Furthermore, and importantly, after participants had learned their opponents' ability, viewing "good" opponents faces relative to "bad" opponents faces also yielded a reward positivity. This supports our theory that individuals process opponents differently based on their history of wins and losses against them. These outcomes also support the theory that reward positivity is not only evoked by outcomes, but also modulated by stimuli that predict outcomes, as indicated by a differentiation in medial-frontal activity at the earliest indication of potential reward.

title: Learning what matters most
abstract:
Decision making in a complex world is made possible by heuristics, or "mental shortcuts". One
such heuristic, sparsity, guides our choices by focusing learning on only a small subset of
available features (such as choosing a graduate program based on how much free food is
available). Here, we replicated work suggesting that humans use sparsity when making complex
choices, and found electroencephalographic (EEG) evidence implicating selective attention in
this process. In particular, we observed an increase in the N2pc (a marker of attention) in
response to high-valued relevant features compared to low-valued irrelevant features. Thus,
selective attention may be one of the mechanisms behind the use of sparsity in decision making.

title: The Effect of Visual Noise on the Magnitude of the Directed Forgetting Effect
abstract:
The present study determined whether the amount of attention paid during initial item processing
influenced the strength of the intentional forgetting effect. Using an item-method directed
forgetting paradigm, study items in high or low background visual noise appeared one at a time
in the centre of a computer screen, each followed by an instruction to remember or forget the
previous item. After all study items were presented, participants completed a recognition task in
which they indicated whether they recognized the items, regardless of their memory instruction.
Results revealed a higher accuracy for to-be-remembered items than to-be-forgotten items. There
was no significant difference in the magnitude of the directed forgetting effect between high and
low noise conditions. This suggests that the amount of attention paid during encoding is not a
significant indicator of the magnitude of the directed forgetting effect. Future research should
address both attention withdrawal and cognitive load in the context of directed forgetting effect.

authors_raw: Joshua P. Salmon, Stephanie A. H. Jones, Chris Wright, Michael Lawrence, Beverly Butler, Ray Klein, Gail Eskes
title: How good is your reaction time data? Lessons learned from the Dalhousie Computerized Attention Battery (DalCAB)
abstract: The speed at which a task is performed can be a valuable research instrument in research and clinical assessments. However, standard computer hardware employed for measuring reaction and response times (e.g. a standard computer monitor or mouse) can add noise to the data collection of reaction times, potentially compromising the precision of measurements and subsequent conclusions drawn from the data. Therefore, an assessment of the precision of measurement should be included in the development of computerized tasks and assessments. This talk will outline different methods for assessing the precision of collected reaction time data (with and without independent chronometry). In particular, using example data collected from the Dalhousie Computerized Attention Battery (DalCAB), this talk will discuss the detection, measurement, and correction of non-representative noise in reaction time measurement. This talk should act as a cautionary tale to any researchers who have built their own software, use reaction time as a dependent measure, but have not yet employed methods for verifying the chronometry of their software.

title: Covert measurement of covert orienting: An electroencephalographic and pupilometric investigation
abstract: Covert orienting of spatial attention is typically measured by overt motor responses (keypress, eye movement, etc) made by participants to a peripheral stimulus. This presentation will discuss attempts to simultaneously deploy two measures of covert orienting that do not require such a response, one based on steady-state visual evoked potentials elicited by flickering peripheral stimuli, and another based on a relatively recently discovered effect of covert attention on pupil size when participants view differentially luminous scenes.

authors_raw: Sarah Dolan, Tracy Taylor, Jonathan Fawcett
title: Prosodic Factors in Directed Forgetting of Speech Errors
abstract:
Past research has shown that when a speech error is followed by a forget instruction (e.g., " No,
sorry, I meant…") and then by a correction to the error, participants are able to intentionally
forget the error, while performance for the correction to the error is improved as compared to
baseline. However, previous research has not controlled for prosodic emphasis of corrections,
which is commonly used in natural speech and has been shown to improve memory. We
manipulated prosodic emphasis of corrections within auditory narratives containing
speech errors. After listening to these narratives, participants were tested for recognition
memory. Results showed that recognition performance for errors and control statements was not
significantly different, however, performance for corrections was significantly higher than
performance for both errors and control statements. We found no impact of prosodic emphasis of
corrections on recognition performance. It appears that performance for corrected information
benefits from listeners' increased attention after an error occurs, but that prosodic emphasis does
not facilitate this process.

title: The directed forgetting effect: The processes associated with intentionally forgetting and remembering.
abstract: The processes involved in successfully remembering target information have long been a focus of research in cognitive psychology. Equally important, however, is our ability to intentionally forget irrelevant or misleading information. Investigation of intentional forgetting has progressed through the use of directed forgetting paradigms. In particular, research in the Taylor-Helmick lab has focused on the item-method directed forgetting paradigm. Participants are presented with study items one at a time, each followed by an instruction to Remember or Forget. When tested for memory of ALL items, participants reveal a directed forgetting effect: better memory for items they were told to Remember (R-items) compared to those they were told to Forget (F- items). This difference is the result of a number of cognitive processes -- some that lead to increased memory for R-items, and some that minimize memory for F-items. These processes will be summarized, and the empirical findings that support them will be discussed to provide a complete account of how we remember and forget in item-method directed forgetting.

title: The Dalhousie Computerized Attention Battery: Validation against traditional neuropsychological testing instruments
abstract: The Dalhousie Computerized Attention Battery is a new battery of experimentally validated tests of attention, developed to measure the vigilance, orienting, and executive control networks of attention as described in a model by Posner and colleagues. The proposed study aims to evaluate the convergent and discriminant validity of the DalCAB with fifty healthy older adult participants (55 years and older) by comparing their performance on the DalCAB to their performance on "gold standard" neuropsychological tests of attention and other cognitive functions using regression analyses. The purpose is to evaluate how well findings from experimentally derived attention measures (i.e., DalCAB tasks) converge with results from clinical measures of attention. Results of this study will further the development of the DalCAB as a tool for the assessment and treatment of attention disorders by clinicians.

authors_raw: Loriann Williams, Isabel Smith and Nancy Garon
title: Effects of child temperament and parenting on toddlers' executive functioning
abstract:
The objective of the current study is to investigate how child temperament and parenting
discipline style independently and jointly influence early executive functioning in typically
developing preschoolers, observed at two years of age and again, a year later, at three years of
age. Specifically, the study addresses: Does a positive parental disciplinary style promote the
development of executive function skills in toddlers rated high in temperamental positive affect?
It is hypothesized that a) toddlers who are temperamentally positive will display larger gains in
executive function abilities at three years of age than toddlers with other temperament profiles
and b) that toddlers whose parents engage in positive discipline will exhibit larger executive
function gains at three years of age, compared to at two years of age. We also hypothesize that c)
parental discipline will mediate the relationship between temperament and executive functioning
such that positive discipline during toddlerhood will be related to greater executive functioning
for temperamentally positive children at age 3 years. Identifying factors in toddlerhood that may
promote subsequent executive function abilities has important implications for social, emotional,
and behavioral development. Identification of these factors is also important in the context of
community and family-level prevention or early intervention programs aimed at preschoolers. Special Adjunct Talks

title: Laterality of Motor Imagery-Related Brain Activity is Modulated by Real-Time
abstract: Motor imagery (MI) is the mental performance of a movement in the absence of actual movement. As in physical practice, the repetitive activation of neural pathways during MI can drive short- and long-term brain changes that underlie skill acquisition. However, the effectiveness of MI may be limited by the lack of feedback from this endogenous process. Real- time feedback about MI-related brain activity (i.e., neurofeedback) has the potential to overcome this limitation. I will present a two-group longitudinal study, wherein participants performed MI across repeated sessions with (group 1) or without (group 2) neurofeedback. Participants who received neurofeedback were able to generate a more contralateral pattern of MI-related brain activity in primary sensorimotor cortex. This indicates that the provision of real-time neurofeedback during MI allows healthy individuals to modulate regional brain activity. This finding has the potential to improve the effectiveness of MI to support skill acquisition and neurorehabilitation.

title: Integrating methods from neuroscience, experimental psychology, and computer science in the study of cognitive science Jason Satel Cognitive science is widely considered to be the last frontier of scientific inquiry. In order to adequately investigate difficult questions in cognitive science, we must begin integrating techniques from multiple disciplines. Although my work so far has focused primarily on the study of visual attention, I am interested in using multiple interdisciplinary methods to investigate questions in cognitive science pertaining to many different topics, including, for example, attention, learning, memory, language, and disorders associated with such functions. In this talk, I will demonstrate how I have integrated methods from neuroscience, psychology, and computer science to investigate a cognitive phenomenon, termed inhibition of return, as an example of how such techniques can be combined to provide insight on difficult topics. I believe this work can be used as a model for the integration of multiple techniques throughout cognitive science and revolutionize the field.
Spelling Matters Too! How spelling practice can benefit reading skill in children
abstract:
Many theoretical models of reading propose that fluent and skilled reading results from the rapid
recognition of letter sequences as a single unit within a word. This rapid recognition depends on
previously established memory representations of letter patterns that occur frequently within a
language (i.e., orthographic representations). Much research has been devoted to how this
orthographic information is acquired incidentally during reading. However, as spelling requires
the production rather than recognition of words, spelling may establish higher quality
orthographic representation due to the increased thoroughness of processing. During this
presentation, I will present studies, in varying stages of completeness, that illustrate gains in
spelling can transfer to gains in reading, and that begin to shed light on the mechanisms that
enable the acquisition of orthographic knowledge. POSTERS
Thursday's Posters

authors_raw: Ghislain d'Entremont, RJ Redden, Mike Lawrence & Raymond Klein
title: Looking at endogenous prior entry effects in baseball judgements at first base
abstract:
To examine whether Tichener's law of prior entry holds in a baseball context, participants
were presented with close plays at first base. After each play, participants made a "Safe" or
"Out" judgement, or reported the perceived colour of a disk flashed briefly over either the base
or the glove. The biased presentation of coloured disks at one location or the other within a block
was intended to endogenously orient attention towards that location. A novel colour wheel
analysis was implemented and compared with the conventional analysis. The attentional
manipulation was successful, but there was no visual prior entry effect.
Poster 2 moved to Friday

authors_raw: Roxanne Sterniczuk, Setareh Lahasee and Ben Rusak
title: Exploring sleep and circadian rhythm disturbance in an animal model of early stage Alzheimer's disease
abstract: Sleep disturbance and impairment to the sleep-wake cycle, are commonly reported by individuals in the early stages of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Using a triple transgenic mouse model of AD (i.e., 3xTg-AD), this study examined whether there are alterations to the circadian pacemaker, the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), prior to the development of the hallmark neuropathological changes that are characteristic of the disease (i.e., plaques and tangles). Previous work of ours found that 3xTg-AD mice show changes in neuropeptide profiles in the SCN when the brain exhibits AD neuropathology. Prior to the development of AD neuropathology however, there are already alterations in the activity/rest cycles of these mice. It is not known at what stage of disease progression changes within the SCN occur. Preliminary results indicate decreased clock gene protein product Period1 in the SCN prior to neuropathology. Findings from this study may help to identify novel biomarkers that could lead to the earlier and more accurate diagnosis of AD

authors_raw: NESC: R. Kindellan, J. Morrow, A. Shahisavandi, H. Thorpe, K. Trotman, A. Hidaya, M. Barbaro, J. Blacklaws, A. Brown, J. Greenan, S. Kwiatkowski, L. Meisner, A. Pieterse, S. Shehzaad, M. Taylor, JA Horne, Kevin Duffy
title: Investigation of glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) in the primary visual cortex during normal development and in the absence of visually-driven activity.
abstract: Neurons within the mammalian primary visual pathway exhibit a high capacity for neural plasticity early in postnatal development. This critical period of neural development eventually closes so that little plasticity remains in adolescence and adulthood. The natural reduction of plasticity that occurs with age appears to be partly rooted in the accumulation of proteins - called molecular brakes - that stabilize neural circuits refined by early visual experience. Astrocytes are among the most plentiful cells within the central nervous system and they have been implicated as facilitators of neural plasticity. In this study we examined a prinicpal constituent of the astrocyte cytoskeleton, glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), to determine if it is developmentally regulated within the visual cortex in a manner consistent with a molecular brake. In the second part of our study we examined whether dark-rearing, a condition known to enhance neural plasticity, alters GFAP labeling in visual cortex. While GFAP levels appeared not to be developmentally regulated within the developing visual cortex, labeling for GFAP was altered when visually-driven activity was removed through dark-rearing.

authors_raw: Emre Fertan and Dr. Richard E. Brown
title: Age related changes in anxiety, motor performance and spatial learning in the 5xFAD mouse model of Alzheimer's Disease.

title: Using the SpyTrain videogame as a means to improve visual search in the older adults
abstract: The capacity for our brains to perceive information and generate knowledge is known as cognition. With age and the onset of health problems associated with age, this ability gradually begins to decline. Videogames have shown some promising effects on cognition in past studies, so the "SpyTrain" game was created to train visual search skills. We used SpyTrain to measure the effect of age, videogame and computer experience on reaction time and accuracy. Fifteen older and fifteen younger adults participated, and were asked about their weekly videogame and computer use. These participants then individually played "SpyTrain" while their reaction time and accuracy was being monitored. We found strong correlations between age and reaction time in the old group and computer use and error in the young group, suggesting that visual search declines with age and that frequent computer use can improve accuracy. Trends were also found between age and error and computer use and reaction time in the young group, implying the older participants might be putting in more effort and those who often use computers are sacrificing time to reduce error. We were unable to find any correlations with videogame use due to a lack of gamer participants and we found no correlation between computer use in the old group, suggesting seniors do not often use computers in an engaging way.

authors_raw: Megan Hodgin, Amanda Hudson, Roisin M. O'Connor, Sherry H. Stewart
title: Examining mood, drinking motives, and alcohol use over the menstrual cycle
abstract:
Alcohol use tends to vary over women's menstrual cycles, with increased intake occurring
(pre)menstrually - a phase also characterized by enhanced negative mood. Together, these
findings suggest that women may drink for particular emotion-focused reasons at specific points
in their cycles. In the current study, normally-cycling women (Mage = 22.58 years, SDage = 3.43)
completed daily dairy measures (via Smartphone surveys), with questions on mood, drinking
motives, and drinks consumed each day for the course of a full cycle. Results thus far (n = 26)
indicate that women felt less positive during menstruation and drank most heavily in the
ovulatory phase, followed by menstrual and premenstrual phases, and lastly by follicular and
luteal phases. Motives also varied by cycle phase, with women endorsing enhancement motives
most in the ovulatory phase, and coping motives most (pre)menstrually. Social motives were
commonly reported in both premenstrual and ovulatory phases.

authors_raw: H. Hamodat, M.K. Cash, S. Darvesh
title: Anatomy and Pathology of the Human Olfactory Bulb in Alzheimer's Disease
abstract:
Background: Clinically, there is evidence for olfactory dysfunction in neurodegenerative
disorders, like Alzheimer's disease (AD), preceding typical cognitive and behavioural
symptoms. In AD, olfactory impairment may result from neuronal loss, neuroinflammation and
pathological deposition of β-amyloid (Aβ) plaques and tau neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) in the
olfactory bulb (OB). However, the laminar organization of the human OB is poorly understood
and observations of AD pathology have been inconsistent. Purpose: the goal of this study was
to discern the laminar organization of the human OB and determine structural and pathological
changes in the AD OB. Methods: AD and normal control OB tissues were obtained from the
Maritime Brain Tissue Bank in Halifax, Canada. Adjacent OB tissues were stained with Nissl to
examine laminar distribution and immunohistochemical techniques to examine neuronal, AD
pathological and neuroinflammatory markers. Results: Herein, we provide a detailed map of the
anatomical distribution of the human OB. Aβ plaques and tau NFTs were observed in specific
layers of the AD OB, predominately in the anterior olfactory nucleus. A lack of pathology was
noted in the olfactory tract. A neuroinflammatory response was observed through the presence of
activated microglia, particularly in regions with noted AD pathology. Conclusions: The specific
pattern of AD pathological changes observed in the AD OB, provides support that impaired
olfactory functions may be related to the peripheral olfactory system. Further investigation of the
AD OB is warranted for the potential use of olfactory dysfunction as an early, definitive
biomarker and diagnostic target for AD diagnosis.

authors_raw: Kylie Duncan, Miranda Frison, Michelle Lehman, Hannah Wood
title: Amyloid-beta plaque load, cell density, and soma size in the 3xTg-AD mouse model of Alzheimer's disease
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) leads to the deterioration of thinking, memory, and motor function. The triple transgenic mouse model of AD (3xTg-AD) mimics the spatial and temporal progression of AD's neuropathological hallmarks - amyloid-beta (Aβ) plaques and neurofibrillary tangles. Previous studies found that 3xTg-AD mice showed superior motor coordination relative to controls (B6129SF2/J) on the rotarod apparatus. Given that motor deficits in human AD arise from Aβ plaque-induced neurotoxicity in the primary motor cortex (M1), the current study was conducted to determine if a relationship exists between Aβ plaque load, soma size, and cell density in layer V of M1 of 3xTg-AD mice. We used ImageJ to quantify Aβ plaque load, soma size, and cell density in two- and 15-month-old male and female 3xTg-AD and control mice. We found that 3xTg-AD females expressed significantly higher plaque loads than genotype- and age-matched males at both ages. Although a main effect of genotype on cell density or soma size was not observed, a main effect of sex on soma size was found, with females showing larger cells than males, and a main effect of age on cell density was found, with two-month-old mice having higher cell density than 15-month-old mice. Plaque load was significantly correlated with soma size and cell density. These results suggest that further examination of the motor system of 3xTg-AD mice may uncover a neuroanatomical or neurophysiological mechanism that preserves their motor functioning, which could have implications for the treatment of motor disturbances in human AD.

authors_raw: Kaitlyn M. Tagarelli, Aaron J. Newman, Michael T. Ullman
title: Understanding second language in the brain with a mini-language model
abstract:
How does an adult brain learn a second language (L2)? The complex nature of L2 learning -
such as the amount of time it takes to learn a language, the variety of conditions in which people
learn languages, and the vast individual differences between learners - has made it difficult to
completely answer this question. In order to address some of these issues, research in psychology
and applied linguistics has turned toward artificial linguistic systems, like word lists, artificial
grammars, and artificial languages, which can be learned quickly in highly controlled settings.
However, the extent to which results from this research can be generalizable to natural languages
is often called into question. This presentation evaluates the utility of a more natural language
model, a mini-language, and presents a study in which learners were exposed to a mini-language
while continuous behavioral and fMRI measures were acquired. The results from this study
support dual-mechanism models of language learning and processing, in which the neural bases
of language change as a function of exposure and proficiency, with different patterns for
lexical/semantics and grammar.

authors_raw: Emily Patrick, Emily McGuire, Aaron Newman
title: Brain Activation Associated with American Sign Language Learning
abstract:
Studying sign languages gives the opportunity to explore a language that is unique in terms of
the form of communication. Neuroimaging studies have shown that signed and spoken languages
engage similar neural networks in native learners of each. Experience in visual-manual language
affects how people process not only linguistic signs, but non-linguistic gesture as well. It is not
known, however, how much sign language knowledge is necessary for these changes in brain
activation patterns to occur. In the proposed research, we will use functional MRI to investigate
the changes in brain activation patterns in response to signs and gestural stimuli, as hearing
English speakers learn American Sign Language (ASL). We hypothesize that when responding
to ASL stimuli (and potentially gestures) patterns will shift from bilateral activation of areas
responsible for biological motion and human action perception, towards left lateralized activation
of networks associated with linguistic processing. This research will add to the current
understanding of how ASL knowledge affects processing of not only signs but also non-
linguistic gestures. In addition, by examining novice ASL learners, we will gain a better
understanding of how people learn a second language in a new modality, something that has yet
to be determined.

authors_raw: Sreejith P G, Graham Flick, Antoine Tremblay, Aaron J Newman
title: Visualize your imagination
abstract:
Communication is one the most important skills that is required to be successful in life. There are
so many creative people around the world who do not have the ability to speak and are unable to
showcase their talents to the full extent. Through my research I am developing a technique to
project what we imagine onto a computer screen. Using this technique, people who do not have
the ability to speak may eventually be able to convert what they want to convey into visuals,
making communication easier for them.
My research focuses on harnessing the power of machine learning to build a system that can
decode visual imagery from neural activation, with the goal of predicting what people are
imagining based on their patterns of brain activity. To do this, Naïve Bayes and C-forest
classification models were trained on magnetoencephalography (MEG) recordings acquired
while subjects viewed a series of images, thus establishing profiles of neural activation
associated with visual components of the imagination. These models were then used to predict
what images the subjects saw based on their observed neural activation. Ensemble techniques in
machine learning were used to make the model more general and less prone to over-fitting.
Initial results indicated good success in predicting stimulus features including shape, orientation,
and colour based on brain activation alone. Different brain regions contributed most to correct
classification of different features, and classification accuracy was improved by using data
exclusively from the temporal and occipital lobes, compared to using data form the whole brain.
If these models are successful, they will constitute an initial step towards reconstructing
authentic human visual experiences.

title: The Effect of Cueing on the Directed Forgetting Effect
abstract:
This study explored the possible effects of cueing on the Directed Forgetting Effect (DFE).
Thirty-six participants were shown words followed by a 'remember' or 'forget' instruction and
then given a yes/no recognition memory test. In order to test whether cueing would decrease the
DFE, the memory test was blocked into Remember Cued, Forget Cued, and Uncued sections.
The proportion of corrected yes responses were calculated to find the results. The results showed
a DFE as there was a main effect for memory instruction [F(1,35)=89.780, MSe=.030, p<.001]
with Remember greater than Forget (Mr=.5293, Mf=.2566, SEr=.04006, SEf=.02209), but there
memory instruction and cueing. From this study, it was found that cueing has no effect on the
DFE.

title: The evolution of persons
abstract:
In contrast to other animals, human beings are aware of themselves as selves and persons. How
and why did this level of self-consciousness come about? I argue that it came about to meet the
challenge for hominins to work together cooperatively in the complex physical and social
environment of the Pleistocene. Through cooperation that involved both foraging and breeding,
our ancestors became cognitively and emotionally bound to each other in order to engage in
temporally extended joint activities that provided a fitness advantage for all members of the
collective. Individuals became aware of themselves and others equally as persons involved in a
variety of roles that were potentially reversible. The agent-neutral concept of person made
possible uniform calculations of costs and benefits to self and other that could be used to
maintain equity across cooperative activities and provide roughly equal fitness gains for all
members in the collective.

title: Individual differences in attentional control: The case of action video game players
abstract:
The past couple of decades have seen a marked increase in the popularity of video game playing,
especially games within the action genre. Given how cognitively complex action video games
are, coupled with the time people spend playing these games, interest has grown in how
extensive experience with action video games may affect those who play them. To date, studies
comparing action video game player (AVGPs) and non-player performance on a variety of
cognitive tasks, many which engage aspects of selective attention, have revealed that AVGPs
commonly outperform non-players. To date, the collective evidence has suggested that action
video game playing provides players with greater or more flexible control over the spatial
