allocation of attentional resources. I will present recent work that compared AVGPs and non-
player performance in attention and oculomotor capture tasks to assess whether AVGPs are able
to engage such superior attentional control to better resist visual distraction.

authors_raw: Jasmyn E. A. Cunningham, Stephanie A. H. Jones, Joshua P. Salmon, Franziska Kintzel, Gail A. Eskes, Benjamin Rusak
title: Effects of Sleep Restriction on Components of Attention: Interim Results
abstract: Many patients with psychiatric illnesses have both cognitive problems and disturbed sleep. These may be causally related since sleep loss is known to disrupt many aspects of cognition. Attention is a complex function that includes several separate, but interacting neural systems guiding vigilance (readiness to respond), orienting (searching for and the selection of stimuli for further processing) and executive control (attention resource allocation) abilities. However, it is not clear which of these underlying component processes of attention are most affected by sleep loss. The Dalhousie Computerized Attention Battery (DalCAB, 8 computerized reaction time tasks purported to measure aspects of vigilance, orienting and executive control) was administered on two occasions (within subjects design) to healthy participants (women aged 19-25 years), once after a nine hour overnight sleep opportunity and a second time after a three hour overnight sleep opportunity (sleep deprivation condition). Self-ratings of sleepiness and mood states were also obtained following each sleep condition. Interim results evaluating which attentional systems are affected by acute sleep loss will be reported.

authors_raw: Yoko Ishigami, Jason Satel, Shaun Boe, Raymond M. Klein, and Gail Eskes
title: Finding physiological indices of alertness in healthy adults using electroencephalography (EEG)
abstract: The alerting network plays a special role in the systems of attention. In our previous work, we determined that the alerting network interacts both with the orienting and executive networks of attention. However, these interactions depends on the modality of alerting (visual and auditory) and age. The goal of the current study is to examine the physiological indices of the alerting effects previously identified in an identification task as a function of alerting cue modality. The task for the participants (n=15/ modality condition, mean age = 20.8 yrs) is to indicate the direction of an arrow in the middle of the screen following a visual or auditory alerting cue in half of the trials while electroencephalography was recorded. Cue-elicited event-related potentials (ERPs), including the P1 and P300 ERP components, will be examined for each modality. Details of the relationship between alerting effects in ERPs and behaviour will be discussed.

authors_raw: Andrew Beattie, Kate Thompson and Tracy Taylor-Helmick
title: Directed Forgetting: Consequences of a Forget Instruction
abstract: This study aimed at replicating the Directed Forgetting Effect through the use of videos of people saying a word. Typically, research has used written words to test this Effect, although more recently faces have been used in conjunction with written words to make the Directed Forgetting Effect more generalizable and ecologically valid. With reference to the Mere Exposure research, familiarity of stimuli, even when presented unconsciously, has led to an increase in liking for those stimuli. The aim of the current study was to combine these two areas to determine if the Directed Forgetting Effect can be replicated with more complex and ecologically valid stimuli, namely videos, while determining whether the instruction to Remember or Forget the word presented in the video will affect the likeability ratings of the faces shown in the videos. The hypothesis was that there would greater likeability of the faces associated with a Remember instruction compared to a Forget instruction, since Remember words would be more familiar, as verified through the Directed Forgetting Effect. Results of the study showed a very strong Directed Forgetting Effect of the videos, with higher recognition accuracy of Remember than Forget words. However, strong evidence was found against the difference between ratings for faces associated with Remember and Forget instructions.

title: From gestures to gaming: Visible embodiment of one's cognitive state
abstract:
The field of embodied cognition is based on the idea that cognition does not occur in isolation
but operates within, and interacts with, a body. Consistent with this notion, research has
demonstrated how bodily states can influence affective and perceptual processes. However, it is
also clear that internal cognitive states can influence bodily states (e.g., to generate goal-directed
actions); therefore, the observation of overt behaviour can be used to gain insight into one's
cognitive state. Interestingly, we often engage in overt behaviour that does not appear deliberate
or even appear to serve any clear purpose (e.g., gestures, leaning behaviour). A series of recent
investigations have employed a natural observation approach to study these spontaneous
behaviours in contexts ranging from a letter reading task to video game playing (teleoperation). I
will present the results of this work and discuss how spontaneous behaviours can represent the
visible embodiment of one's cognitive state.

authors_raw: Stewart, S. H., Mackinnon, S. P., Lambe, L., & Pearson, R
title: The Mediating Role of Gambling Motives in Explaining the Prospective Relations of Personality to Gambling Outcomes in Emerging Adults
abstract: We tested relations between the five-factor model traits of Neuroticism and Conscientiousness with gambling outcomes, as mediated through coping and enhancement gambling motives. Four-hundred-and-eighty-seven emerging adult gamblers (52.6% female; mean age = 22.2) participated as a part of the last two waves of the Manitoba Longitudinal Study of Young Adults (Manitoba Gaming Control Commission, 2011). Participants completed the NEO Five Factor Inventory (Costa & McCrae, 1992), the Gambling Motives Questionnaire (Stewart & Zack, 2008), and the Canadian Problem Gambling Index (CPGI; Ferris & Wynne, 2001). Multiple mediator analyses, using bootstrapping, revealed the hypothesized significant indirect effects of low Conscientiousness on money spent, gambling frequency, and gambling problems mediated through enhancement motives, and significant indirect effects of high Neuroticism (expected) and low Conscientiousness (unexpected) on money spent gambling that were mediated through coping motives. Findings suggest two relatively distinct pathways to excessive/problematic gambling among emerging adults. Prevention implications will be discussed.

title: Don't Stumble Tumble: A Videogame to Reduce the Use of General Anesthesia in Pediatric Magnetic Resonance Imaging
abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is distressing for many children, as they must remain immobile and endure loud noises, while lying in a narrow, enclosed tube. Some children even require general anesthesia (GA) in order to complete the scan successfully. Currently, there is no effective and accessible preparation tool available for MRI; as such, this research aims to test the efficacy of a MRI-preparatory videogame for children. This videogame is designed to prepare children for the sensory experience of MRI, while also shaping skills to enhance their capability of completing the scan without GA. Beta testing will test the game with children, in order to identify functional issues and incorporate feedback into remodelling. Implementation testing will provide the opportunity for children referred for MRI with GA, to use the videogame and attempt MRI without GA, thereby determining the proportion of families willing to use the videogame and whether the videogame is successful in reducing the necessity of GA. This tool has the potential to increase healthcare efficiency by reducing the time and resources necessary to acquire quality scans, as well as provide children with an enhanced MRI experience.

title: Real Time Neurofeedback During Imagined Imitation of Arm Movements: A Pilot Study
abstract: While various forms of mental exercises to promote motor recovery--specifically Action Observation (AO) and Motor Imagery (MI)--can be performed at home and have been proven efficacious, at the moment they are rarely used. This pilot project will combine AO and MI, where an individual watches movements whilst imagining they are executing those same movements, while providing real-time neurofeedback via electroencephalography (EEG). This protocol of Imagined Imitation (II) has previously been shown to engage the motor network to a greater degree than either AO or MI alone. Furthermore, while MI with neurofeedback has been shown to more effectively engage the sensorimotor areas required to induce motor recovery from stroke than MI alone, this has not yet been attempted using II. In this pilot project healthy subjects will perform II of a unilateral upper-limb movement, while receiving information about the symmetry of their sensorimotor activity. This project will provide valuable information for those interested in designing an optimal at-home adjunct to physiotherapy to promote motor recovery following stroke.

authors_raw: Marie-Eve Couture, Sean P. Barrett, Hera Schlagintweit, Phil Tibbo, Sherry H. Stewart, Heather Milliken, & Kimberley P. Good
title: Exposure to tobacco-related cues does not enhance cravings in patients with psychotic disorders relative to control smokers
abstract: Rates of tobacco smoking in patients with psychosis are remarkably high, and are accompanied by low rates of successful smoking cessation. Exposure to smoking-related cues is shown to reliably enhance cigarette cravings in dependent smokers recruited from the general population. In contrast, it remains unclear whether or smoking-related cues affect smokers diagnosed with psychotic disorders to the same degree as they do non-psychiatric populations. In addition, it is unknown whether the smoking motives of patients with psychosis differ from those of non- psychiatric smokers. A greater understanding of cue reactivity and reasons for smoking in smokers with psychosis may lead to enhanced interventions and increased rates of successful smoking cessation in this population. The present study compared the smoking motives, and the cigarette cravings of patients with psychotic disorders to those of healthy control participants after completion of a cue reactivity paradigm. Results revealed patients with psychotic disorders tended to be less sensitive to the cue-reactivity paradigm and suggests that patients may not be affected by tobacco cues to the same degree, if at all, as their healthy control counterparts. Further, comparison of smoking motives suggests no differences between the reasons for smoking reported by patients and those reported by healthy controls. Findings have implications for future studies aimed at identifying the source of the wide discrepancy in rates of tobacco smoking and smoking cessation across these two populations, and for the development of effective smoking cessation interventions for smokers with psychosis.

authors_raw: Stephanie A. H. Jones, Elaine Skene, Janet Green, Gail A. Eskes
title: A pilot study to examine the effects of computerized working memory training on working memory and attention in people with Parkinson's disease
abstract: Working memory (WM) is the ability to actively hold and manipulate information in one's mind and is needed for many everyday tasks such as reading and following a conversation. More than half of people with Parkinson's disease (PD) exhibit cognitive impairment, including WM deficits (Kehagia et al. 2010). We examined whether using a WM computer activity benefits WM and attention in people with PD compared to a control group. PD participants were randomly assigned to one of two computer activity groups: WM or control (CT). Each activity was used five days per week (30 minutes/day) for five weeks. Age-matched healthy adults were randomly assigned to use one of the computerized activities. WM activity: an adaptive dual n- back activity in which participants had to monitor a series of visual and auditory stimuli and press computer mouse buttons when the current stimulus matched a previous stimulus 'n' items ago. The 'n' was adaptive in that it adjusted according to the performance of the individual. CT activity: participants moved and rotated blocks to form horizontal lines (non-adaptive). All participants completed a computerized battery of attention tasks before and after the five weeks of activity use. We examined baseline differences in reaction time and accuracy for each independent variable within each of the attention tasks. Hedges' g was used to quantify the change in reaction time and accuracy before and after computerized activity use in the WM and CT groups. Preliminary results indicate that at baseline, PD participants show attention and WM deficits similar to previous reports (problems with initiation, inhibition, response interference, monitoring and WM). After training, the WM training group shows particular improvement on tasks requiring initiation, response interference and verbal WM compared to the control group.

authors_raw: Kiera O'Neil, Aurélie Lagarrigue, Aaron J. Newman, Cheryl Frenck-Mestre
title: Building a cortical network of semantic concepts in a second language: ERP evidence of fast learning via computerized games
abstract: What does it take to acquire a semantic network in a second language? The present ERP study shows extremely rapid instantiation of both learned words and related concepts, via computerized games ("LANGA"). Participants served as their own control. Electrical activity of the brain, recorded at the scalp, was examined prior to exposure to the second language and 8 days later, following a 6 day training session (preceded and followed by orientation and consolidation, respectively). Participants learned 12 words per day (nouns and verbs), for a total of 72 words over 6 consecutive days. Results show rapid changes in cortical activity, associated with learning. Prior to exposure, no modulation of the N400 component was found as a function of the correct match vs. mismatch of audio presentation of words and their associated images. Post training, a large N400 effect was found for mismatch trials compared to correctly matched audio-visual trials. More importantly, images that were semantically related to learned words (eg. for the learned word "horse" the image of a saddle was presented), produced a reduction of the N400 compared to mismatched pairs (eg. the same learned word "horse" paired with the image of a building). This semantic priming effect suggests that the words in the new language were rapidly integrated with existing semantic networks, rather than existing as an isolated second language lexicon. Our results attest to the plasticity of adult learners' brains and provide evidence for rapid onset of a semantic network in a late learned language.

authors_raw: Francesco Usai and Aaron J. Newman
title: Virtual reality environments for aphasia rehabilitation
abstract:
Current online applications for aphasia rehabilitation show high rates of attrition in the early
stages of the training process, limiting the potential for optimal recovery. The main limit of these
applications is a design overlooking important factors for a successful therapy, such as the level
of immersion and motivation the users experience while doing the exercises. In this regard, this
project aims at creating a cost-effective, easily accessible framework for aphasia rehabilitation
that overcomes the limits of current online therapies. To achieve this goal, we will use principles
of serious gaming and virtual reality to design evidence-based therapies that can engage and
motivate the user to attain constant and intensive training required for a successful rehabilitation.
Product design will be driven by evidence from relevant literature and basic research done in our
lab, together with feedback from subjects with aphasia taking part to the process of validation.

title: Diffusion Tensor Magnetic Resonance Imaging and Assessment of Olfactory Function as Early Indicators of Parkinson's Disease and Alzheimer's Disease
abstract: Alzheimer's Disease (AD) and Parkinson's Disease (PD) are the two most common neurodegenerative disorders amongst Canada's geriatric population. It has long been understood that olfactory impairments are manifested in both AD and PD and that these deficits are often present before the cardinal symptoms appear. A robust literature has shown that those afflicted with AD and PD perform poorly on the University of Pennsylvania Smell Identification Test (UPSIT) when compared to age-matched controls. The olfactory bulbs and tracts are shown to be abnormal when assessed using diffusion tensor magnetic imaging (DT-MRI), but only in PD patients. No study has yet combined objective olfactory ability with brain imaging techniques to compare those diagnosed with AD with those diagnosed with PD. The current study aimed to assess olfactory function in AD patients and PD patients along with a group of age-matched healthy controls. Olfactory function was assessed with the UPSIT, while DT-MRI techniques were used to examine the integrity of white matter in olfactory tracts and in the parahippocampal gyri. Results from the present study indicated that patients with AD and patients with PD suffer significant olfactory impairments relative to healthy controls. No significant differences were found in integrity of white matter tracts in either the olfactory bulbs or the parahippocampal gyri.

title: Inducing Spike Timing Dependent Plasticity in the Human Brain Through Paired Associative Stimulation.
abstract: In the mid 20th century Hebb famously proposed that the timing of neuronal discharge underlies synaptic plasticity. This phenomenon is now referred to as spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP). Two forms of plasticity, long-term potentiation and depression, occurs when a postsynaptic neuron discharges within ~20 ms after the presynaptic neuron (potentiation) or when it discharges ~20-100 ms before (depression). Recent studies indicate that STDP-like changes can be induced similarly on a larger scale -- between brain areas -- in humans using a paradigm known as paired associative stimulation (PAS). PAS involves stimulating two areas of the brain in close succession, allowing for facilitation of either long-term potentiation or depression between the targeted brain areas. Thus PAS may prove to be a powerful tool in research and clinical practice by aiding in functions like learning and memory. This talk will outline the existing literature on this approach, and propose applications for facilitating motor learning using PAS.

title: Sequential actions: why do upcoming perceptual - but not motor - tasks affect grasping?
abstract: Past research has shown strong evidence supporting the notion that the visual control of action and the visual perception of objects are mediated by two functionally and anatomically distinct visual systems. Little is known about how each visual system interferes with the other when performing a sequential task. However, it is known that the kinematics of an action can be affected by a subsidiary attention task. In the current study participants were presented with two rectangular objects placed one in front of the other. Participants were instructed to grasp the first object and place it on a specified target area and then either grasp, make a perceptual judgment about, or ignore the second object. The results revealed that preparing an action to the second object does not produce interference to the first action, but attending to its size for verbal judgment does. Specifically, as the size of the second object increased, the amplitude of peak grip aperture towards the first object also increased when performing the perception condition.

authors_raw: Donald Mitchell, Kevin Duffy, Paige Northrup, Ming-fai Fong and Mark Bear
title: Is there a pharmacological intervention to replace darkness? Preliminary results from a collaborative study (M.I.T) of the use of intraocular injection of tetrodotoxin (TTX) to recover vision in animal models of amblyopia
abstract: We previously demonstrated in kittens that the profound deficit in vision following an early period of monocular deprivation (MD) can be blocked or eliminated by 10 days of complete darkness (Duffy & Mitchell Curr Biol. 2013). The loss of visually driven neural activity associated with darkness may reset the levels of certain plasticity braking molecules in the visual cortex to low levels typical of an earlier stage of development. The increased plasticity conferred by reduced levels of braking molecules following immersion in darkness may promote the fast recovery from the anatomical and behavioural effects of a prior period of MD. We have investigated the possible benefits of the use of intraocular injections of TTX, a potent marine neurotoxin that selectively binds sodium channels and temporarily blocks action potentials. We examined whether blockage of all retinal activity (not just visually driven activity) with intraocular TTX recapitulates the beneficial effects of darkness.

authors_raw: Stephane MacLean, Yoko Ishigami, Cameron Hassall, Olav Krigolson, Gail Eskes
title: Feedback content can alter ERPs and perceptual realignment during a prism adaptation task
abstract: Prism adaptation (PA) is a demonstration of perceptual-motor plasticity, but is also a promising rehabilitation tool for visuo-spatial neglect (VSN). During PA, participants perform a visuomotor accuracy task while their visual field is shifted laterally by prism goggles. The visual shift initially causes large accuracy errors, but adaptation takes place after repeated trials. Upon returning to normal vision, participants show an aftereffect - a visuomotor bias in the opposite direction of the preceding prism shift. For VSN patients, it is important to develop PA paradigms that create robust aftereffects because these are what ameliorate their symptoms. Not all forms of "adaptation", however, result in strong aftereffects. The present study investigated the effect of presenting different forms of accuracy feedback to healthy young participants while they performed a PA task. We compared magnitude of aftereffects and event-related brain potentials evoked by onset of different feedback events. Results suggest that visual feedback of upper limb position at the end of each trial leads to stronger aftereffects compared to feedback that only indicates size of error on each trial. Visual feedback of upper limb also evokes a parietal ERP that may serve as a marker of perceptual "realignment" processes that are critical to producing strong aftereffects.

authors_raw: Jillian L. King, Kurt R. Stover, Matthew P. Lowe, Nathan A. Crowder
title: Optogenetic silencing of mouse primary visual cortex affects contrast adaptation in V1 but not the LGN.
abstract: Contrast adaptation is induced by prolonged viewing of a high contrast gratings, and perceptually manifests as a decrease in perceived stimulus contrast. Neurons in the primary visual cortex (V1) show characteristic changes in their contrast response functions during contrast adaptation, but the cellular mechanisms behind this phenomenon are largely unknown. Our lab has previously measured contrast adaptation in mouse V1 and found close similarities to higher order mammals; therefore we have taken advantage of the genetic tools available in mice to probe the mechanisms of contrast adaptation. We performed extracellular recordings in both V1 and the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) in transgenic mice that express channelrhodopsin-2 (ChR2) in GABAergic neurons while optogenetically silencing V1 to investigate the role of spike rate on adaptation. We found that silencing the cortex while the adapting grating was present reduced the magnitude of adaptation in V1 cells, and also caused an increase in sensitivity to low contrasts. This latter effect had never before been observed in non-perturbed circuits. Adaptation changed the responses of LGN neurons very little except for an increase in sensitivity to low contrasts. These results have allowed us to estimate what proportion of contrast adaptation observed in V1 can be attributed to inheritance from LGN and intrinsic processes from the spiking of V1 neurons themselves. We hypothesize residual adaptation not accounted for by these two factors arises from synaptic depression at genicluo-cortical synapses.

authors_raw: Sunghan Jung and William H. Baldridge
title: Horizontal cell inhibition of cones via ATP hydrolysis
abstract:
Retinal horizontal cells (HCs) modulate the output from photoreceptors via inhibitory feedback.
It has been suggested that feedback is due to changing pH in the synaptic cleft between
photoreceptors and HCs. One model proposes that phosphate, produced by extracellular
degradation of ATP released through pannexin-1 channels of HC dendrites, acidifies the synaptic
space. To test this model we studied the effect of drugs that interfere with the proposed pathway
on an indicator of feedback, the rollback of the HC response to light. The effect of the pannexin-
1 blocker, probenecid, and the ecto-ATPase inhibitors, ARL 67156 and BGO-136, were tested.
ARL 67156 reduced HC rollback but neither BGO-136 nor probenecid had a significant effect.
The action of ARL 67156 on HC rollback is consistent with the ATPase/proton model of
feedback. The lack of an effect of probenecid and BGO-136 on HC rollback is not consistent
with the model suggesting that additional work is necessary to characterize feedback.

authors_raw: Jordan Fisk, Christine Tsang, Simone Falk, Leslie Phillmore
title: Do zebra finches speak Russian? An operant task to test discriminability of mothers' song and speech to their infants.
abstract: Discrimination between human song and speech can be difficult when mothers are communicating with their infants because "motherese" is song-like in delivery. In previous studies, Tsang et al. used a preference test to demonstrate infants aged 6-9 months were able to discriminate between audio clips of mothers singing and mothers speaking to their babies in Russian. However, in a different study by the same authors, adults had difficulty discriminating some ambiguous English stimuli; they could not accurately categorize them as song or speech. In this project we used an operant task to test whether zebra finches could discriminate between audio clips of human mothers singing to their babies in Russian and English from clips of mothers speaking to their babies in Russian and English. We also presented birds with the ambiguous English stimuli to see how they categorized them. Finches were able to accurately discriminate song from speech in both languages.

authors_raw: Ralph S. Redden, Matthew D. Hilchey & Raymond M. Klein
title: The dynamics of inhibition in the aftermath of a cue is contingent on reflexive oculomotor activation.
abstract: Inhibition of return (IOR) is considered an inhibitory aftermath of visuospatial orienting, expressed in the form of slower responding toward the locations of prior orienting responses. As shown by Taylor and Klein (2000), the form of IOR depends on the saccadic response demands imposed by the task and, hypothetically, whether the oculomotor response system for reflexively generated saccades is in an active state. To test this hypothesis, we evaluate IOR by requiring a manual discrimination response preceded by reflexive and non-reflexive eye movements in separate conditions. The findings suggest that the state of the oculomotor system determines the form of IOR.

title: Three optical mechanisms in wing spots of a large dolichopodid fly, Liancalus genualis. Steve Shaw Most flies have plain or darkly mottled wings, but Liancalus males have wing spots that redirect and diffuse sunlight using ~1515 tiny upright hairs, forming a bright signal for conspecifics. When the sun is obscured, a second signal is still possible because the spots fluoresce bluish- white in UV light, an important component of skylight. There is much blue in skylight though, raising the question of whether the fluorescence could even be seen over reflection from the rest of the wing. Reflection, measured with collimated blue light was surprisingly low. Quantification of fluorescence required several steps, and indicated impressive efficiency: only 12 UV photons are required to displace one fluorescent photon from the wing spot. The wavelength distribution in 5000K skylight then dictates that the natural fluorescence-to- reflection ratio will be high, ~19:1. Fluorescent spots should be easy to see. Accordingly, photographs made under natural skylight revealed a spot visual contrast of ~15:1. However, when sky UV was selectively filtered out, spot brightness dropped by only 14-18%. This
abstract: exposes a third signaling mechanism: the spot's refractile hairs act as waveguides and pick up skylight but reflect about 17% back. Fluorescence scores at only ~1/6 of this.

authors_raw: Changes Ken Sullivan Sick animals typically change their behaviour, becoming lethargic, disinterested in food, water
title: A Comparative Approach to Understanding Sickness Mediated Behavioural
abstract: and social activities and run a fever. This change in behaviour, termed sickness behaviour, is considered an adaptive response and is thought to enhance immune function. Sickness behaviour is observed not just in mammals, but in animals across all phyla, suggesting this change in behaviour is a highly conserved evolutionary adaptation. If this is true, then understanding how sickness behaviours have been adapted over time in different species is crucial in understanding and controlling its positive (and negative) influence. A great deal of previous work has focused on studying sickness behaviour in mammals. We have used the Texas field cricket Gryllus texensis to study sickness mediated behavioural changes in order to fill in the gaps in our understanding of the adaptive nature of sickness behaviour.

authors_raw: Kurtis Turnbull, Ilya F. Kovalko, Gillian Davies, Russell H. Easy and Shelley A. Adamo
title: Coping with constraints: nutritional stress elicits changes in immune function in the caterpillar, Manduca sexta
abstract: It is thought that nutrition may reconfigure the immune system to optimize immune function when resources are constrained. However, few studies provide empirical evidence that immune resources are redistributed during nutritional limitation. We examined the effect of short-term nutritional stress on the immune function and energy availability of fifth instar Manduca sexta larvae. Insects reared on a low-nutrition diet (25% nutrient) for two days exhibited pathogen- specific declines in resistance, but did not show changes in energy availability, immune measures or immune-related gene expression. In contrast, the nutrition-absent diet (0% nutrient) caused declines in overall pathogen resistance, infection tolerance measures and energy availability. Surprisingly, the M. sexta larvae reared on the nutrition-absent diet upregulated both resistance mechanisms and immune-related gene expression. We hypothesize that the cost of inducible immune responses (i.e. immune resources synthesized in response to a pathogen) may increase during nutritional stress and therefore, constitutive immunity (i.e. immune resources present even when there is no infection) is upregulated. Together, these results suggest that assumptions about the costs and benefits of different immune components may not hold under nutritional constraints.

title: Coping with stress early in life to build resilience for later: Further evidence for the negative effects of helicopter parenting.
abstract: Environmental enrichment (EE) produces a host of effects in mammals that are associated with a shift toward the resilient end of the vulnerable-resilient continuum that defines individual differences in the development of psychiatric conditions. The mechanism by which environmental enrichment, a non-drug and non-surgical manipulation, is associated with such robust effects remains speculative. One recent hypothesis states that EE is a form of inoculation stress - successfully coping with the chronic mild stress associated with EE produces protective phenotypes. Our unique model of EE in which mother rats are housed in enhanced home cages (EHC) with their young produces long-lasting changes in emotional and social development of the young. More recently, we have been investigating how the EHC alters maternal behaviour as one means for altering offspring phenotype. I will summarize our recent findings using the EHC and discuss them within the context of the inoculation stress hypothesis.

authors_raw: Michelle E Tougas, Christine T Chambers, Penny Corkum
title: A scoping review of information about child health shared over social media
abstract:
Social media facilitates information exchange and is increasingly being used by professionals
and general public. Little is known about child health and social media interaction.
Aim: 1) Adapt systematic review methodology to explore social media interaction about
pediatric pain and pediatric sleep; 2) identify and categorize main themes, 3) assess user
interactions across social media platforms, and 4) assess consistency of posts with research
evidence. Methods: Twitter and YouTube will be searched for posts about pediatric pain and
pediatric sleep. Themes will be categorized and extracted. Category frequencies and user metrics
will be analyzed. Netlytic will be used to summarize and discover social networks. Post content
will be compared to research to identify the quality of information shared on social media.
Results: This presentation will outline the novel methodology and planned stages of research
that will be undertaken. Conclusions: This work will evaluate an approach for describing
health-related social media, will examine how this information is being shared, what questions
are being asked, and what information is being disseminated.

authors_raw: Ivy-Lee Kehayes, Sean P. Mackinnon, Ken Leonard, Ron Fraser and Sherry H. Stewart
title: Gender Differences in the Mutual Influences of Drinking Motives in Couples
abstract:
Drinking is related to various negative consequences (e.g., memory loss, injury). Drinking
motives in the five-factor model (enhancement, conformity, social, coping-with-anxiety, and
coping-with-depression) have been linked to different drinking patterns. Interpersonal factors are
also important predictors of drinking, as couples enter into a "drinking partnership," where one
partner's drinking influences the other's. Additionally, gender influence models suggest men
hold power over women and influence women more than vice versa. This project sought to
integrate drinking motives, drinking partnerships, and gender influence models to explain how
romantic relationships influence drinking motivations over time. One hundred heterosexual
couples were assessed once a week for four weeks. It was hypothesized that motives would be
influenced by both the individual and the partner, and that men would influence women more
than the reverse. Such results would demonstrate the importance of gender influence in romantic
relationships and their effects on drinking motives. Preliminary results will be shared.

authors_raw: Mackinnon, S. P., Pieterse, A., Keyhayes, I., Leonard, K., Fraser, R., & Stewart, S. H
title: Relationship conflict and drinking to cope: An actor-partner interdependence model
abstract: Past research on couples has demonstrated that relationship conflict is a risk factor for alcohol- related problems through drinking to cope (Lambe et al., submitted). It was theorized that conflict increases negative emotions (i.e., anxiety and depression) which in turn leads people to drink as a means of coping with those emotions. However, our past work did not incorporate symptoms of anxiety and depression. The present study tests whether anxious/depressed affect mediates the conflict-coping relationship using a 4-wave, 4-week longitudinal study of romantic dyads. A sample of 103 romantic dyads participated, all of whom were pre-screened to have consumed 12+ alcoholic drinks in the past year. Significant indirect effects were found, with anxious/depressed affect mediating the relationship between conflict and coping motives. Significant actor effects (i.e., own affect predicting own motives) but non-significant partner effects (i.e., own affect predicting partner's motives) were found. Implications and future directions will be discussed.

authors_raw: Esmot A Begum, Amy Goodday, Kathy F. Spurr, Malgorzata Rajda, Penny Corkum
title: Periodic limb movements during sleep in children with Attention- Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder: Baseline frequency and impact of psycho-stimulant medication.
abstract: To determine the prevalence of periodic limb movements during sleep (PLMS) in children with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and the impact of stimulant medication on PLMS, 25 medication naïve children with ADHD and 25 typically developing (TD) children, 6- 12 years of age, were evaluated using overnight polysomnography (PSG) in three phases: baseline, stimulant treatment and placebo treatment. Compared to the TD children, children with ADHD had significantly higher rates of PLMS (ADHD=24%, TD=4.0%, p<0.05) with longer sleep latency (p<0.05). Children in the ADHD/PLMS group shifted their sleep stages more frequently than children in the ADHD/Non-PLMS group (p<0.001). Stimulant medication was not associated with PLMS; however, children in the ADHD/PLMS group showed significantly longer sleep latency and decreased sleep efficiency during stimulant treatment compared to the placebo. The result of this study reveals that PLMS in children with ADHD is associated with more severe sleep problems and more side- effects on medication.

authors_raw: Amanda Hudson and Sherry H. Stewart
title: Attentional biases in problem and non-problem gamblers
abstract:
Although attentional biases have been widely studied in substance use disorders, relatively few
studies have looked at attentional biases in gamblers. The current study examined attentional
biases for gambling stimuli, relative to emotional and neutral stimuli, in problem and non-
problem gamblers (n = 57). To do so, we used a Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP)
paradigm, which decomposed different components of attention, namely attentional capture and
maintenance/ disengagement, by employing different time lags between distractors and targets.
Problem gamblers showed attentional biases for gambling stimuli, relative to all other distractors,
and these biases were observed only at the level of attentional maintenance/ disengagement.
Non-problem gamblers showed some evidence of non-specific attentional biases (to both
negative and gambling pictures). These findings highlight important differences in attentional
processes between problem and non-problem gamblers, and suggest that sustained attentional
biases among problem gamblers might be worthy targets for clinical interventions.

authors_raw: Lynn MacKenzie, Rudolf Uher and Shannon Johnson
title: Emotional Decision-Making and Psychotic-Like Experiences
abstract: Background: Emotional executive functioning has previously been labelled hot executive function as this process involves an emotional component. Performance on emotional executive functioning has been linked to the ventromedial prefrontal cortex of the brain (Bechara et al, 2000). Hot or emotional executive function has yet to be explored in individuals at high risk for SMI and may yield valuable results to the field of research. Recent research has shown promising results for improving executive functioning abilities through early intervention (Diamond & Lee, 2011). Identifying an association between executive functioning deficits and psychotic-like experiences could provide useful information for clinicians selecting assessments and possible early intervention in high-risk populations. Exploratory analysis of emotional decision-making and psychotic-like experiences could also be meaningful to support further inquiry into testing feasibility and effectiveness of executive function interventions specific to this population. Methods: Children and young people aged 8 to 21 will be selected from the existing Families Overcoming Risks Building Opportunities for Wellbeing cohort. The FORBOW is an accelerated cohort study enriched in risk of severe mental illness, which started recruiting participants in January 2013. FORBOW recruits the sons and daughters of people suffering from SMI and matched comparison controls. Overall, 75% of FORBOW participants have a family history of SMI in one or both parents, putting them at a 1-in-3 risk for developing a SMI themselves by early adulthood (Rasic et al., 2013). The remaining 25% are offspring of parents without SMI of similar age and form the same neighbourhood, referred to as comparison offspring (CO). FORBOW recruits participants at a young age (5 to 21) and follows them up in yearly intervals. The annual follow-ups include a comprehensive assessment of psychopathology and antecedents to SMI: cognitive impairment or delay,(Reichenberg et al., 2010) affective instability,(Egeland et al., 2012) anxiety, (Duffy et al., 2010; Kim-Cohen et al., 2003; Kovacs et al., 1989; Copeland et al., 2009) basic symptoms (Schimmelmann et al., 2013) and psychotic-like experiences.(Downs et al., 2013; Kelleher and Cannon, 2011; Polanczyk et al., 2010) Implications and Directions: Since FORBOW is a prospective study, the scores on the emotional decision making test will further be used to predict emergence and persistence of PLE on follow-up assessments. The results from these investigations will inform the planning and design of early interventions. If impaired emotional decision making is associated with PLE, a training in emotional decision making may be included as part of interventions for young people with PLE.

title: Why study the history of neuroscience?
abstract:
This presentation has 3 parts. First, what is history? Second, why study the history of
neuroscience? In the third part, I will show some of the work we are doing at Oxford on our
"Saving the history of neuroscience" project using examples from our history of neuroscience
website [https://history.medsci.ox.ac.uk/]. The general purpose of the presentation is to show
how history is embedded in each phase of scientific research and teaching.

title: Representing the Brain: Modeling neuroanatomy from the Early Modern to the
abstract: This presentation will survey the history of anatomy modeling in reference to the brain and nervous system. Important developments include whole-body wax modeling in the 18th century, "moulages" as teaching models in the 19th century, and contemporary computer modeling. Given their visual nature, models engage non-experts and exert a strong influence on the popular imagination. Neuroanatomical models therefore reveal the tenor of broader ideas about the brain and the mind.

title: Golgi versus Cajal and the Neuron Doctrine
abstract:
With Robert Hooke's (1665) discovery of cells using his new microscope, biologists were able to
study cells as the building blocks of living organisms. This led to the development of the cell
theory by Matthias Schleiden and Theodor Schwann (1838). Due to the difficulties of studying
neurons, they were excluded from the cell theory. The invention of the microtome (1770) and the
discovery of the tissue fixing properties of formaldehyde (1859) enabled neuroanatomists to
prepare brain tissue slices to study under the microscope. The turning point for cytoarchitecture
was the discovery and usage of different dyes to stain the slides. Golgi developed his famous
staining technique using silver and made neuronal bodies and extensions visible, but he could not
identify the small gaps between the neurons. Santiago Ramon y Cajal, modified Golgi's method
to stain different nerve tissues and defined the neuronal doctrine, which states that neurons do
not form continuous webs, but are separated from each other by small gaps. This led to the
famous arguments between Golgi and Cajal when they were jointly awarded the Nobel Prize in

title: Wilder Penfield's lasting contributions to neuroscience
abstract:
Dr. Wilder Penfield is perhaps the most well known Canadian neuroscientist. Penfield's surgical
work on epilepsy was ground-breaking. However, it is his work on cerebral localisation which
put his name into introductory psychology textbooks across the globe. What is often forgotten, is
his incredible educational experience as a Rhode's scholar, working with greats like Sir William
Osler and Sir Charles Sherrington, to name a few. As founder of the Montreal Neurological
Institute, Penfield played a pivotal role in creating a world famous destination for neuroscience
research in Canada. Penfield's brilliant work, and the creation of the MNI, has influenced many
other famous Canadian neuroscientists such as Donald Hebb and Brenda Milner. His influence
not only lives on in the famous homunculus drawing but also in the long lasting impression he
has made on neuroscience research in Canada and the world.

title: I'm significant and you're not
abstract:
The title may not sound meaningful to you because, alone, it's not. But worse, if I relied on it
alone to argue about meaningful differences I would be making a serious error. This talk was
inspired by watching our honours conference and how our students are trained to interpret
statistical tests.
Special Adjunct Talks

title: Do children with dyslexia have deficits in spoken sentence comprehension? Erin Robertson
abstract:
POSTERS
Thursday's Posters

title: Parental Management of Pediatric Cancer Pain at Home
abstract:
Pain is one of the most problematic aspects of a pediatric cancer diagnosis and persists throughout
the course of the disease. Improvements in pediatric cancer prognoses and shifts towards models
of outpatient cancer care mean that parents have become increasingly responsible for assessing
and treating their children's cancer pain at home. However, literature on what parents do to
manage their children's cancer pain outside of a medical setting is sparse.
The current study will investigate how parents assess and treat their children's cancer pain at home
and seeks to identify how parental beliefs, misconceptions, and characteristics may impact their
ability to optimally manage their child's cancer-related pain. 200 parents of children with cancer
across North America will be recruited to complete an online survey through a partnership with the
Cancer Knowledge Network (www.cancerkn.com), an online forum for those living with cancer and
their caregivers. Data collection will begin in April 2016. The study's background, design, goals,
outcome measures, and implications will be presented.

title: Modifying an Online Intervention to Treat Sleep Problems in Children with Neurodevelopmental Disorders
abstract:
The goal of this study, which is currently underway, is to transform an online sleep intervention,
Better Nights, Better Days, which was designed for typically developing children so that it is
appropriate and effective for use with children who have Neurodevelopmental Disorders (NDDs). A
Delphi study will be used to survey experts in the area of sleep and NDDs for suggestions for
developing an online intervention for sleep problems in children with NDDs. Their answers will be
compiled, combined with suggestions drawn from the literature, and fed back to the experts who
will rate, on a 7-point Likert scale, how important they believe each modification to be. Delphi
rounds will continue until consensus is reached. The information gathered from this Delphi study, in
combination with results from the literature, will ensure the modifications that are made to the
Better Nights, Better Days intervention are based both in research and in expert opinion.

authors_raw: Ioan T. Mahu, Sean P. Barrett, Lacey R. Peters, Heather G. Fulton, Sherry H. Stewart
title: Personality traits and methadone dose and compliance independently predict concurrent substance use in clients undergoing methadone maintenance treatment for opiate addiction
abstract: This study aimed to examine the relationship of personality and methadone dose/compliance to concurrent substance use in a sample of methadone maintenance therapy (MMT) clients. Sixty- eight clients (63.2% male; mean age = 38.5 years, SD = 9.8) were recruited from a low-threshold MMT clinic in Halifax, Canada (mean methadone dose = 105.4 mg, SD = 41.1). Substance use was assessed using an adapted version of the Drug List Questionnaire (Barrett, Gross, Garand, & Pihl, 2005). Demographic variables and methadone dose/compliance were collected via self-report. Personality dimensions were measured using the Substance Use Risk Profile Scale. Prescription opioid misuse was predicted by lower methadone compliance (B = -.606, OR= .545, 95% CI [.342- .869], p= .01), higher hopelessness (B= .259, OR= 1.30, 95% CI [1-1.67], p= .048) and anxiety sensitivity (B= .568, OR= 1.77, 95% CI [1.13-2.75], p= .012). Injection drug use (n=51) was predicted by lower methadone dose (B= -.043, OR= .96, 95% CI [.93-.98], p=.003), higher impulsivity (B=.398, OR= 1.49, 95% CI [1.04-2.14], p= .031) and hopelessness (B=.426, OR= 1.53, 95% CI [1.04-2.25], p= .03). In contrast, neither personality nor methadone variables predicted alcohol, cannabis, cocaine or benzodiazepine use. In sum, methadone variables and personality independently predicted opiate use and injection drug use patterns within a clinical sample of clients at a later stage of addiction. Results caution that personality may be targeted in treatment as a supplement to MMT.

authors_raw: Kathleen Merwin, BScH & Natalie O. Rosen, PhD
title: We need to talk: The influence of disclosure of female sexual problems on sexual and relationship well-being
abstract:
Disclosure (sharing personal thoughts, feelings, and information) is an essential part of
relationships. General disclosures in romantic relationships and sexual disclosures (e.g., disclosing
personal sexual likes and dislikes) in particular are related to higher sexual and relationship
satisfaction. Sexual problems are common in romantic relationships, with research showing that
43% of women have experienced one or more sexual concerns in the previous 18 months. Despite
the high prevalence of sexual problems, the relational and sexual consequences of disclosing
sexual problems to a romantic partner remain unknown. The present study examines the rate of
disclosure of sexual problems and whether disclosure is related to sexual and relationship well-
being. Women (N = 280) completed online questionnaires about their sexual problem(s), and their
sexual and relationship well-being. Women that disclosed sexual problem(s) to their romantic
partners (n = 194, 69%) reported greater sexual functioning and relationship satisfaction and lower
depression than those that did not disclose sexual problem(s) to their partners (n = 86, 31%).
Findings may identify factors that could be targeted in sex and couples' therapy, such as increasing
intimate disclosure, for the many intimate relationships that encounter sexual problems.

authors_raw: Cowie, E. M., Salmon, P. J., de Jong, D., Boffo, M., Salemink, E., Wiers, W. R., & Stewart, H. S
title: Automatic Approach Biases toward Gambling Stimuli in Canadian and Dutch Gamblers.
abstract:
Implicit association research has provided considerable insight into the cognitive
mechanisms underlying addictive behavior (Reingold & Ray, 2005). The "automatic approach bias"
(automatic tendency to approach stimuli; Wiers et al., 2009), has increased our understanding of
the cognitive influences on addiction. A gambler's automatic approach bias toward gambling
stimuli, however, has yet to be studied. The present study adapted Wiers' Alcohol Approach
Avoidance Task to the gambling context, testing its validity among problem and non-problem
gamblers in Canada and the Netherlands. The Gambling Approach Avoidance Task was developed
with Dutch images and adapted for Canadians. As hypothesized, Dutch problem gamblers
displayed a stronger approach bias toward gambling stimuli than non-problem gamblers, which
was related to greater gambling severity. Canadians displayed no approach bias toward either
stimulus. Moreover, gambling approach bias scores were negatively correlated with gambling
severity suggesting an unexpected aversion to gambling stimuli. Reasons for these discrepancies
are discussed.

authors_raw: Farmer, J. R., Cowie, M., & Mackinnon, S. P
title: Perfectionism and life narratives: A qualitative study
abstract:
Perfectionism has been conceptualized as a multidimensional personality trait involving striving for
flawlessness, placing exorbitantly high standards for performance, and the tendency to be
excessively critical regarding self-evaluations of behaviour. However, there is almost no research
examining the life narratives of perfectionistic people. The present study sought to examine how
perfectionistic people conceptualize perfectionism, and how they narrate their significant life
events. We tested a sample of 22 university students who qualify as highly perfectionistic (N = 8
adaptive and N = 14 maladaptive perfectionists). Using thematic analysis, we identified 11 themes
within participant responses to questions about their conceptualization of perfectionism. These
themes supported previous conceptualizations of perfectionism, but also revealed novel facets.
Within the life narratives interview, we identified 5 major themes (relationship success, relationship
problems, agentic redemption, agentic contamination, and academic success), which provided an
intriguing perspective as to how perfectionistic students comprehend their lives through narratives.

title: Gender and homophily in naturalistic seating patterns: A teaching activity
abstract:
A new field research activity was tested as a teaching tool for PSYO 3082. Students observed
seating patterns in naturalistic environments such as busses, cafeterias, and computer labs.
Specifically, they looked for gendered seating patterns: That is, do men tend to sit by men, and
women by women more often than chance? Students first conducted statistical power analysis for
their study, striving to reach 80% power. Then, students collected data in pairs, generating a total of
13 datasets (234 observations in total; range 7-40). Students analyzed data using Hernandez's
(2014) index of adjacency calculator, and one-sample t-tests. Overall, only 5 of 13 students
supported the hypothesis within the individual datasets. However, when data were combined
together into a mega-analysis, hypotheses were clearly supported, t(233) = -6.28, p < .001.
Presenting data as a forest plot served as a useful teaching moment on the importance of
replication and cumulative science.

authors_raw: Jarvis, E. M., Newman, A. J., & Frenck-Mestre, C
title: First Language Effects on the Learning of Grammatical Gender in a Second Language
abstract:
Some languages contain categorization systems for nouns called grammatical gender, while others
do not. Learning a second language with grammatical gender can pose different obstacles for
individuals from different linguistic backgrounds. This study aims to address two questions:
1. Does having grammatical gender in one's native language make it easier or harder to
learn gender in a new language, compared to not having grammatical gender in one's native
language?
2. Do explicit and implicit training methods differ in their efficacy for teaching grammatical
gender in a new language, and does this differ depending on whether or not one's native
language has grammatical gender?
To achieve these aims we will use a between-groups design, training English and French native
speakers on a small artificial language that has a gender system wherein half of the nouns have
the same lexical gender as in French, and the other half have gender that conflicts with French.
Half the participants in each native language group will receive explicit training; the other half
implicit training. Using a match/mismatch paradigm paired with EEG, we will compare participants
in each language group and training group on their ability to learn grammatical gender in the
artificial language.

authors_raw: O'Neil, K., Newman, A. & Frenck-Mestre, C
title: Cortical Changes Linked to Semantic Integration
abstract:
Previous work has shown that adult second language learners can rapidly acquire second
language vocabulary, and newly acquired vocabulary words are integrated into existing semantic
networks in the brain. However, prior research has focused on vocabulary learning, while the
current study will be expanded to focus on sentence processing and will provide information on a
higher level of integration. Of particular interest is how well adult second language learners can
integrate and process newly learned linguistic materials at the sentence level. Participants will
complete a 5-day video-game-based second language training program, and their brain activity
(measured with electroencephalography, or EEG) will be measured pre and post-training. Eye-
tracking will also be used to assess how participants naturally read sentences containing newly
acquired second language vocabulary. Crucially, we will examine cortical changes across sessions
to novel sentences (i.e. those not learned during training), which will allow us to determine if
participants are able to generalize their vocabulary knowledge gained through training to new
sentences.

title: Optimizing Conditional Inference Random Forest Parameter Settings for the Analysis of Complex Brain Imaging Data
abstract:
Conditional inference Random Forest modelling (CForest) is a non-parametric modelling approach
where a great number of conditional inference regression trees (CTree) are grown, typically
between 500 to 2,500. Each CTree recursively partitions data into nodes and branches by way of
hypothesis testing. The CForest modelling approach has numerous advantages including (i) the
ability to attenuate the effects of random vaiables (e.g., repeated measurements and testing sites)
and outlier data points, (ii) can model high-order interactions, (iii) doesn't over-fit the data, (iv)
allows predictor variables that would otherwise be suppressed by stronger competitors to be
expressed in the results, and (v) are reliable, generalizable, and easily interpretable. Five
parameters are associated with CForest namely, the number of CTrees to grow, the number of
independent variables to pre-select before performing an omnibus test at each tree node, the
proportion of the data that is randomly selected for each CTree, whether the random sampling is
done with or without replacement, and the alpha value for significance.
Aiming to use the CForest approach to model complex brain imaging data, we sought to
determine the optimal combination of parameter settings. A simulated whole-brain MEG data set in
source space comprising 278 virtual electrode time courses was created, which was used to
investigate 864 possible combinations of parameter settings. Results suggest that a random
sampling of 92% of the data with replacement, without variable pre-selection yields the best results,
at an alpha level of 0.05, irrespective of the number of CTrees grown.

title: Exploring potential new approaches to rehabilitation in aphasia
abstract:
Aphasia is a devastating communication disorder in which an individual's ability to comprehend
and/or produce language is severely impaired or altogether lost, and this can have widespread
negative impact on an individual's overall quality of life. Current treatments for aphasia, most
notably intensive speech-language therapy (SLT), are extremely effective in terms of improving
patients' language functionality. However, many individuals with aphasia face barriers to obtaining
the necessary level of treatment required to gain functional improvements. These barriers are
primarily related to accessibility and financial cost of SLT. As such we aim to develop an
inexpensive, accessible and scientifically validated computer-based treatment for aphasia, in the
form of a serious online gaming platform. Our program will feature gamification of validated SLT
drills that are known to be highly effective in traditional standard-of-care treatment. Initial
prototypes of our program will be piloted on patients who have previously received/are currently
receiving standard SLT, with a view to make a finalised version of the program commercially
available. Predicted outcomes of this treatment approach and future directions will be discussed.

authors_raw: Yasmin Beydoun, Kaitlyn Tagarelli, and Aaron Newman
title: Studying Morpho-semantic and Morpho-syntactic Anomalies in Modern Standard Arabic through ERPs
abstract:
Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) is the literary standard used by Arabic speakers. Its words are
composed of two overlapping morphemes: a semantic morpheme that is a 3-consonant root
containing the meaning of the word, and a syntactic morpheme that is the sets of letters inserted
into the roots to form new words. The objective of the study was to understand better how
overlapping morphology is processed using semantic and syntactic neurocognitive distinctions. We
tested native Arabic speakers using EEG as they read sentences that contained either a correct
final target word, a morpho-semantic anomaly or one of two morpho-syntactic violations
(semantically related and unrelated). Two (ERPs) were examined: The N400 associated with
semantic violations and the P600 associated with syntactic anomalies. We hypothesized that when
reading a morpho-syntactic anomaly an N400 will be elicited and that both types of morpho-
syntactic anomalies will elicit a P600 in addition to small N400 responses (larger in the unrelated
condition). The majority of the results were as predicted: the morpo-semantic anomaly elicited a
prominent N400 effect in the centroparietal regions while the semantically related morpho-syntactic
anomaly elicited a significant P600 peak in the posterior regions in addition to a smaller, but
significant, N400 effect. The unrelated morpho-syntactic condition elicited a smaller, but significant
P600 as well as an N400 response. Results point to a distinction in processing for each morpheme
in MSA, with a plausible interaction between them when considering the general semantics.
Results have implications for better understanding sentence processing in under-studied
languages.

authors_raw: Raymond M. Klein, Ralph S. Redden and Matthew D. Hilchey
title: How "inhibition of return" biases orienting depends on the state of the reflexive oculomotor system
abstract:
Since 1988, when we discovered itʼs function as a foraging facilitator, the Klein lab has been
preoccupied by efforts to understand inhibition of return (IOR). In her 1997 dissertation, and while
varying the response modality (manual versus eye movements), Taylor compared the effect of IOR
on localization response times when targets were stimuli presented in the periphery at the cued or
uncued location or were centrally presented arrows. Taylor discovered two forms of IOR: When
only manual responses were made, IOR affected inputs or their ability to capture attention; when
eye movements were made, IOR affected outputs or the decision to respond in a certain direction.
In his 2003 dissertation, Ivanoff explored the effects of IOR in speed-accuracy space. He obtained
evidence for two different kinds of effect: a genuine effect upon the accumulation of information
about the target and a speed-accuracy tradeoff with little or no change in information processing
dynamics. Only in the last few years, have we put these two distinctions together demonstrating
that when the reflexive oculomotor system is suppressed, IOR affects information processing
dynamics, while when this system is not suppressed IOR operates as a response bias.

authors_raw: Horne, J.A.1, Langille, C.1, McLin, S.1, Wiederman, M.1, Borycz, J.A.1, Tarnogorska, D.1, FlyEM Project Team, Plaza, S.2, Xu, S.2, Hess, H.2 and Meinertzhagen, I.A.1
title: FIB-SEM connectome reconstructions of an antennal lobe glomerulus from the Drosophila brain
abstract:
PRESENTERS:
Sari McLin, Meagan Wiederman, Jola Borycz, Dorota Tarnogorska
The antennal lobe of the fruit fly is similar to the olfactory bulb in the mammalian brain in that it is
divided into individual glomeruli each of which has it own set of receptor neuron inputs that encode
a particular olfactant. We have undertaken dense reconstruction on glomerulus VA1v in the
Drosophila antennal lobe and sparse tracing beyond, from an isotropic 8nm stack of segmented
FIB-SEM images. The image stack encompasses the entire right antennal lobe of a 6-day female
fly viewed in a frontal plane. This fruitless-positive, sexually dimorphic glomerulus is responsive to
fly odour (Sakurai et al. Nat. Commun., 4:1825, 2013) and was chosen because it is large,
enclosing a region much larger than any densely annotated antennal lobe volume to date. Three
main classes of neurons populate the glomerulus. We have reconstructed and blindly annotated
the synapses of the following: olfactory receptor neurons (ORNs), projection neurons (PNs), and
local interneurons (LINs). In aggregate the 183 cells we have identified represent ~70% of the
glomerular synaptic connections with more work yet to be done.

authors_raw: Shaunda-Lynn Jesso, Amanda Hudson, Roisin O'Connor, and Sherry H. Stewart
title: Drinking Motives and Sex Motives Vary Differently Across Women's Menstrual Cycle
abstract:
Drinking behaviors differ depending on a person's drinking motives (Grant et al., 2009). Similarly,
sexual behaviors differ depending on a person's reasons for engaging in sexual activity (Ball et al.,
2014). Preliminary work suggests that sex and drinking motives may vary across the menstrual
cycle (Hudson et al., 2015; Sheldon et al., 2006), but no study to date has studied how cyclical
patterns in these variables overlap or diverge. This study examined cyclical changes in
enhancement, coping and social motives across behaviors (sex; drinking). We predicted that
reasons for sex and for drinking would vary similarly depending on phase of the menstrual cycle,
consistent with perspective on motives as "traits". Participants were 48 normally-cycling women (M
= 22.7 years, SD = 5.2) of reproductive age who were regular drinkers. Daily diary surveys were
completed for a full cycle and motives for sex and for drinking were assessed in signal contingent
surveys sent each afternoon to participant's smartphones. Phase means were calculated for the
menstrual, follicular, ovulatory, luteal, and premenstrual phases of each woman's cycle for each
category of sex and drinking motive. ANOVAs were conducted to test whether enhancement,
coping, and social motives for sex and drinking changed in a parallel manner over women's
menstrual cycles. Results revealed that drinking and sex motives each showed significant changes,
but divergent patterns of change, across the menstrual cycle. Findings suggest that motives for
these particular behaviors change in a domain-specific way across the menstrual cycle. Clinical
and nonclinical implications will be presented.

authors_raw: Estelle Cochingyan, Caitlyn Macgillivray, Hayam Mahmoud-Ahmed, Sarah Schwartz, and Caleb Sher, Aaron J. Newman & Antoine Tremblay
title: Investigating the Effect of Lexical Frequency on the N400m in Unconstrained Conversation
abstract:
The magnetic N400 (henceforth N400m) is a magnetic field occurring roughly 400 ms post stimulus
onset with a negative and a positive field at left anterior and left posterior scalp regions,
respectively. The amplitude of the N400m is known to be modulated, among other things, by the
frequency with which a word is used. More specifically, low frequency words exhibit greater N400m
amplitudes than high frequency words. The N400m has been studied in the context of well
controlled experimental paradigms, which have little to do with how we actually use language in
everyday settings. Here we investigate whether the N400m effect generalizes to such settings. We
gathered four recordings of free, completely unconstrained conversation between a participant and
an experimenter while the participant was sitting in an MEG scanner. The event-related fields data
for each participant was analyzed individually using generalized mixed-effects modeling (GAMM).
The results show that low frequency words exhibited greater amplitudes than high frequency ones
roughly 400 ms after being heard in a manner consistent with previous, less ecologically valid
studies. This suggests that the lexical frequency effect on the N400m generalizes to more
ecologically valid settings. In addition, these results illustrate that research can be carried out using
experimental paradigms that are completely uncontrolled and more in line with actual language use.

authors_raw: Emily Howes Vallis & Dr. Ray Klein
title: A Comparison of Engagement within the Attention Trip and the Attention Network Test
abstract:
The Attention Network Test (ANT) is a frequently used stimuli task that measures the networks of
attention (Fan et al, 2002). However, prior research has cautioned against using this test with
specific populations due to possible boredom effects (Ishigami & Klein, 2014). The Attention-Trip
(A-Trip) is a video game inspired task designed by the Klein lab to measure the networks of
attention (Klein et al., 2011). No previous literature has compared the ANT and the A-Trip in the
context of participant engagement. The primary objective of this study involved utilizing two
subtests of the User Engagement Scale (O'Brien, 2010) to compare the ANT and the A-Trip tasks.
The objective was achieved by administering both the ANT and the A-Trip to the same sample of
28 participants recruited through Dalhousie University. After each task was performed participants
completed a version of the User Engagement Scale in response to the task that was just
completed. Results show that participants reported the A-Trip to be more engaging in comparison
to the ANT. Both the ANT and the A-Trip generated typical attention network scores in all three
attentional networks (alerting, orienting, and executive function).

authors_raw: Lauren D. Petley, Timothy Bardouille, Antonina Omisade, Steven D. Beyea, Aaron J. Newman
title: Networks of Visual Attention as Revealed Using Magnetoencephalography
abstract:
Attention is a complex construct that encompasses many of the neurological capacities that
support purposeful, directed behaviour. Posner and Peterson delineated three functionally and
neuroanatomically distinct networks of attention, including an alerting network, an orienting
network, and an executive network. The Attention Network Task (ANT) is a popular test that
measures the efficiency of these networks. This task has been noninvasively studied in time (EEG)
and space (functional MRI) in healthy individuals, but these techniques have different physiological
correlates. By contrast, magnetoencephalography (MEG) measures neural events with high
resolution in both time and space. We studied the ANT using MEG with a distributed source model
in 12 healthy young adults. The alerting network was associated with activation in visual areas and
the parietal lobe, with a right hemisphere bias. The orienting network consisted of regions
classically associated with both voluntary and involuntary spatial attention-switching. Activation
related to the executive network differed from the results observed in previous studies and may
reflect sequence effects on cognitive control.

title: The Effect of Directed Forgetting on Embodied Cognition in an Item-Method Paradigm
abstract:
People form associations between objects and where they are typically found in space: When a
word with a spatial association such as "bird" is presented, the detection of a target is faster when it
appears subsequently in a congruent ("up") location rather than an incongruent ("down") location.
We determined whether an instruction to forget a word with a spatial association reduces or
eliminates this spatial congruency effect. We presented participants with words one at a time, each
of which had an association to upper space, lower space, or no spatial association. Each word was
followed by an instruction to remember or forget and then by a target that appeared above or below
the word location; participants made a speeded keypress to localize the target. Participants were
tested for their recognition of all words presented. In addition to finding a directed forgetting effect,
with worse recognition of to-be-forgotten words than to-be-remembered words, we observed a
smaller spatial congruency effect for trials with a forget instruction rather than a remember
instruction. Forget instructions thus impact encoding as well as spatial associations, likely due to
changes in attention.

title: Effectiveness of Tetrodotoxin in promoting anatomical recovery from monocular deprivation in kittens
abstract:
Monocular deprivation (MD) by closure of one eyelid during the critical period of visual
development precipitates neuronal alterations in the dLGN of kittens that include a significant
decrease in neuron somata size within its deprived layers. Brief periods of complete darkness
during the critical period have been shown to be effective in restoring a high capacity for neural
plasticity and promoting recovery from the anatomical effects of monocular deprivation. The
cytoskeleton of neurons consists of a multitude of filamentous proteins, of which neurofilament acts
to enhance stability of neural circuitry during maturation. The consequence of this increased
stability is a proportional reduction in the capacity for neural plasticity. In this study examined the
effectiveness of binocular retinal inactivation to promote recovery from the effects of MD. Following
binocular retinal inactivation with intravitreal injections of TTX, we observed significant anatomical
recovery from the effects of 7 days of MD that was complete by ten days. These results indicate
that binocular retinal inactivation recapitulates the plasticity-enhancing effects of dark exposure.

authors_raw: Jillian L. King, Austin C. Korgan, Emily Papsin, Nathan A. Crowder
title: Contrast adaptation in mouse primary visual cortex is not generated by parvalbumin interneurons
abstract:
Contrast adaptation can be produced by prolonged viewing of a high contrast stimulus, and
induces a perceived fading of the adapting stimulus. Neural correlates of contrast adaptation have
been described throughout the visual system, including the primary visual cortex (V1). We recently
found that optogenetically silencing V1 during contrast adaptation changed the cortical pattern of
adaptation to resemble that observed in the dorsal lateral geniculate nucleus (dLGN). Thus,
adaptation in V1 is firing rate dependent and adds onto inherited thalamic adaptation. How does
this activity-dependent V1 adaptation come about? To test a previous proposal suggesting
parvalbumin expressing (Pvalb+) interneurons produce adaptation by inhibiting neighboring cells,
we optogenetically activated Pvalb+ cells during adaptation. Our preliminary findings indicate that
activating Pvalb+ cells reduces adaptation rather than enhancing it, which suggests Pvalb+ cells
are not themselves driving contrast adaptation in V1, but instead regulate cortical response gain.

authors_raw: Philip Bobbie-Ansah, Kevin Duffy & Donald Mitchell
title: A crucial role for the fellow eye in the darkness-induced recovery of visual acuity in the amblyopic eye of kittens.
abstract:
Following a short period of monocular deprivation (MD), there is a profound reduction of the vision
of the deprived eye. Although the vision of this eye improves somewhat afterwards, a substantial
deficit in visual acuity remains with characteristics similar to those observed in human
amblyopia. However, we have shown that a ten day period of total darkness can induce recovery
of the visual acuity of the amblyopic eye to normal levels in just 5-7 days. The speed and
magnitude of this recovery suggest that neural connections with the fellow eye, that support normal
vision and are retained following the period of darkness, may play a crucial role in recovery of the
amblyopic eye. We investigated the possibility that activation of the connections from the fellow
eye may serve to guide darkness-induced recovery. This was assessed by occluding the fellow
(stronger) eye for 10-14 days immediately after the period of darkness. This simple manipulation
blocked any improvement of the acuity of the amblyopic eye.

authors_raw: Jad Sinno, Kevin Duffy, Josh Bowdridge
title: Extended Periods of Darkness Facilitate Anatomical Recovery in the Dorsal Lateral Geniculate Nucleus of Juvenile Felines
abstract:
Amblyopia is a condition characterized by a permanent reduction of visual acuity in one eye that
cannot be attributed to abnormalities within the eye itself. It becomes irreversible after the closure
of the critical period (CP) of the central visual pathway, when the brain's plasticity has decreased,
and current treatments are ineffective at restoring complete binocular vision in many clinical cases
(Sun and Zhang, 2011). A period of complete darkness has been observed to reintroduce
heightened levels of neural plasticity in rats (He et al., 2007) and kittens (Duffy and Mitchell, 2013)
by removing molecular brakes on plasticity, such as neurofilament (NF), in the lateral geniculate
nucleus (LGN; O'Leary et al., 2012). The following study explored the anatomical profile of
darkness-facilitated recovery, concerning neuron structure and NF density. The remedial effects of
10 days of darkness were found to diminish with increasing duration of monocular deprivation (MD)
and was not sufficient to promote complete neural recovery in juvenile cats that received 21 days
of MD at postnatal day 30 (P30). Alternatively, 15 days of darkness was enough to promote neural
growth in deprived layers to normal levels, suggesting that increasing periods of darkness
treatment can be effective in animals that have aged beyond the peak of the CP. However, only
partial recovery of NF density was observed with and was attributed to an insufficient duration of
treatment. These findings were the same for equal durations of binocular vision, which has a
similar anatomical recovery profile as DR. Overall, these findings suggest that complete anatomical
recovery is possible for animals that have matured beyond the peak of the CP, but may not be
necessary given the literature on behavioural recovery.

authors_raw: Kevin Duffy, Alexander Lingley, Kaitlyn Holman, Donald Mitchell
title: Susceptibility to monocular deprivation following immersion in darkness either late into or beyond the critical period.
abstract:
An extended duration of darkness starting near the time of birth preserves immature neuronal
characteristics and prolongs the accentuated plasticity observed in young animals. Brief periods of
