complete darkness have emerged as an effective means of restoring a high capacity for neural
plasticity and of promoting recovery from the effects of monocular deprivation (MD). We examined
whether 10 days of darkness imposed in adulthood or beyond the peak of the critical period could
rejuvenate the ability of MD to reduce the size of neuron somata within deprived layers of the cat
dorsal lateral geniculate nucleus (dLGN). In adult cats subjected to 10 days of darkness before 7
days of MD, we observed no alteration in neuron size or neurofilament labeling within the dLGN. At
12 weeks of age, MD that followed immediately after 10 days of darkness produced an enhanced
reduction of neuron soma size within deprived dLGN layers. At this age we observed that 10 days
of darkness also enhanced the loss of neurofilament protein within deprived dLGN layers. These
results indicate that while 10 days of darkness in adulthood does not enhance the susceptibility to
7 days of MD, darkness imposed near the trailing edge of the critical period can restore a
heightened susceptibility to MD more typical of an earlier developmental stage. The loss of
neurofilament in juveniles exposed to darkness prior to MD suggests the enhanced capacity for
structural plasticity is partly rooted in the ability of darkness to modulate molecules that inhibit
plasticity.

title: Imagine that! Motor imagery for skill acquisition
abstract:
Motor imagery (MI), the mental rehearsal of movement, is a useful adjunct to physical practice (PP)
in numerous domains. However, to use MI when one is unable to engage in PP, such as post-
stroke, we must establish whether MI alone can produce learning. Further, brain damage resulting
from stroke may impact the effectiveness of MI. Thus, we must identify brain regions critical to MI.
The objective of this work was to characterise MI-based learning independent of PP and
investigate the role of parietal regions, commonly affected post-stroke, in MI. Findings demonstrate
that MI alone facilitated learning. However, inhibition of parietal regions impaired MI-based learning.
Therefore, while MI can facilitate learning in the absence of PP, the integrity of parietal regions are
likely critical for MI performance and thus MI-based learning. Ultimately, these findings support the
use of MI as a form of practice and inform applications of MI for neurorehabilitation.

authors_raw: Sean R. McWhinney, Antoine Tremblay, Thérèse M. Chevalier, Vanessa K. Lim, Heather D. Maessen, Manohar Bance, Aaron J. Newman
title: Age-related decline in white matter integrity differs in male and female healthy adults
abstract: Gaining a better understanding of how white matter integrity changes over time will be beneficial in contributing to mitigation of health care challenges associated with an aging population. Healthy aging has been associated with a global reduction in white matter integrity, which is thought to reflect cognitive decline. The present study aimed to investigate this reduction over a broad range of the life span, using diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) alongside conditional inference random forest modeling (CForest). This approach is sensitive to subtle and potentially non-linear effects over the age continuum, and was used to characterize the progression of decline in greater detail than has been possible in the past. DTI data were collected from 45 healthy individuals ranging in age from 19 to 67 years. Fractional anisotropy (FA), a quantification of water diffusion and indicator of tract integrity, was estimated using probabilistic tractography for sixteen major tracts across the brain to provide widespread coverage. CForest was used to recursively partition data into subsets at the point of division yielding the most significant difference along a random selection of variables (including age, sex, handedness, and tract). This process was completed 10,000 times to create a distribution of FA estimates for each unique combination of variables, and subsequently estimate their impact on changes in FA. Age coincided with a general non-linear decrease in FA, with onset beginning at approximately 30 years of age and the steepest declines occurring later in life. However, several tracts showed a transient increase prior to this decline, notable until 36-38 years of age. The age of onset and progression of decline varied by tract, with steeper but later decline occurring in more anterior than posterior tracts. In general, males showed higher FA than females, though this was only evident for certain age-ranges that were tract-dependent, and while significant, sex differences were small. Lastly, strongly right-handed individuals demonstrated relatively preserved FA until more than a decade following the onset of decline of others. These results demonstrate known reductions in FA in healthy aging while providing new insight into the onset and progression of decline, while new evidence suggests increases in integrity continuing into adulthood.

authors_raw: Joshi, N., Rolheiser, T., Fisk, JD., Good, KP., Khan, MN., Phillips, G., McKelvey, JR and Robertson, HR
title: Detecting an olfactory-based biomarker for early stage Parkinson's disease using Diffusion Tensor Imaging
abstract:
Diagnosis of Parkinson's disease (PD) requires the presence of motor symptoms although changes
such as olfactory loss precede the emergence of motor symptoms. We sought to find diffusion
tensor imaging (DTI) based biomarkers related to the loss of olfactory ability and substantia nigra
dopaminergic neurons that might differentiate early-stage PD from healthy controls (HC). We used
a Region-of-Interest approach to determine if anterior olfactory structures (AOS: olfactory bulb &
tract) as well as the substantia nigra exhibit microstructural changes among PD and HC subjects.
Twenty-three early-stage (Hoehn and Yahr stage I or II) PD and thirty-three age-matched HC were
compared. Olfaction was tested using the University of Pennsylvania Smell Identification Test
(UPSIT). Magnetic resonance imaging employed a 1.5T GE scanner and sequences such as a
coronal T2 FRFSE sequence as well as an axial DTI sequence. Both regions were independently
traced (manually) on each side of the brain of every subject using the T2 images, and the tracings
registered to the DTI images. Measures generated included: Volume of the tracing on the T2
image, Fractional Anisotropy (FA), Mean Diffusivity (MD), Axial Diffusivity (AD) and Radial
Diffusivity (RD) which were compared between the two groups using a two-way ANOVA with side
and disease state as independent variables. Expectedly, UPSIT scores revealed poorer olfaction in
PD subjects and DTI-variables also revealed group differences. These findings indicate that DTI
measures of AOS and substantia nigra could differentiate early-stage PD patients from HC and
support their use in the development of multimodal biomarkers to identify premotor stages of PD.

authors_raw: Mohammad Habibnezhad, Laura Cutmore, and Gail Eskes
title: Evidence-based attention rehabilitation after stroke: A review
abstract:
It is estimated that one Canadian has a stroke every 10 minutes, and mini-strokes happen every
minute. Attention deficits are among the most commonly reported cognitive problems after stroke.
Although attentional problems can improve over time, 20-50% of persons experiencing a stroke will
have long-lasting impairments. Negative outcomes are common with attentional problems (e. g.
longer hospital stays, limited independence, etc.); thus identification and/or development of
effective interventions is crucial. We aimed to summarize the evidence for the effectiveness of
cognitive rehabilitation in treating attention problems after stroke. We surveyed articles from Web
of Science, Cochrane Register of Controlled Trials, PubMed (and PubMed Central, PMC), OT
seeker, CINAHL (included databases: Academic Search Premier, Alternative Press Index,
PsycARTICLES, PsycINFO, SPORTDiscus). Articles were included if they provided results from
randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of cognitive rehabilitation (i.e. attention, working memory,
executive function training) for people after stroke. Articles were excluded if they did not have at
least one control condition, stroke patients did not comprise at least half of the study population, or
the methods did not provide an adequate description of training procedures. Preliminary results of
the quality of the evidence and overall results will be provided.

authors_raw: Christiane E. Whitehouse, Gail A. Eskes, Richard E. Braha, Joy Boyce, Anne Sophie Champod and Myrna King
title: Effectiveness of Implementing Spatial Neglect after Right Hemisphere Stroke: A Clinical Trial
abstract:
Spatial neglect is a common attention disorder following right hemisphere stroke, and is associated
with decreased independence and quality of life. Despite its high prevalence, there is currently no
standard of care for the treatment of neglect. Prism adaptation (PA) is a promising rehabilitation
technique, however incorporation into clinical care has been limited.
We have developed a computerized PA procedure designed to better engage patients and
assist clinicians. We aim to evaluate the effectiveness of the PA procedure in a typical health care
setting. Twenty-six stroke inpatients will complete 10 sessions of either the PA procedure or an
active control over two weeks.
We hypothesize that those patients who complete the PA procedure will show a greater
decrease in neglect severity and better functional outcomes compared to those in the control
condition. An effective rehabilitation procedure for neglect patients would allow for faster recovery
and increased independence in daily living.

title: Does size really matter? Exploring the interference effects of size and orientation within a sequential grasping task.
abstract:
Previous research has suggested that the orientation of an object might be more influential in
sequential tasks than size information. However, recent evidence from our lab has shown that
preparing an action to the second object does not produce interference to the first action, but
attending to its size for verbal judgment does. Specifically, as the size of the second object
increased, the amplitude of peak grip aperture towards the first object also increased when
performing the perception condition. The current study was designed in order to incorporate both
size and orientation within a sequential task. Participants were required to grasp a cylinder (5cm in
diameter) place it on an identified target area and than either grasp or make a perceptual judgment
to the second object. The second object was either 3cm or 7cm in length and was either presented
on a 45degree angle towards the left or the right. The results revealed that orientation information
affected grip posture more so than size information in both conditions.

title: Children's awareness of sentence structure and their reading comprehension.
abstract:
We test theoretical predictions as to how syntactic awareness, or awareness of word order within
sentences, contributes to reading comprehension. We conducted a longitudinal study of 100
children followed from Grade 3 to 4. Children completed measures of syntactic awareness, word
reading, and reading comprehension, and reading-related control variables. Path analyses at each
of Grades 3 and 4 show a unique contribution of syntactic awareness to reading comprehension,
with no mediation through word reading skills. Longitudinal analyses reveal that syntactic
awareness at Grade 3 predicts gains in reading comprehension between Grades 3 and 4.
Children's syntactic awareness has a direct and potentially causal influence on their reading
comprehension.

authors_raw: Austin Korgan, Elizabeth O'Leary, Jessica Bauer, Ian C.G. Weaver and Tara Perrot
title: Male Experience, Female Investment and Offspring Anxiety Behaviour: Where Father's Nature Meets Mother's Nurture…
abstract:
Programming of reproductive strategies and defensive (stress) responses in the adult rat is
programmed early in life by maternal care. The detection of a potential mate's past experience with
predators could inform the dam about the stress level of a potential mate, the likelihood of paternal
effects in offspring, and potential ecological threats. We examined whether a male's previous
exposure to predator (cat) odor influences a female's attraction toward these males, subsequent
mother-infant interactions and the development of long-term emotional (defensive) responses in
the offspring. Our results show for the first time that variation among males in their predator
encounters may contribute to stable behavioural variation among females in courtship and
maternal care, even when the females themselves are not directly exposed to a predator.
Furthermore, paternal predator experience is associated with long-term effects on chromatin
plasticity and anxiety-like behaviour of the young adult offspring. These results, together with our
previous findings, suggest that paternal predator exposure can influence offspring development
both directly and indirectly, through altering maternal behaviour.

title: The Impact of Wealth and Social Comparison on Sharing in Children
abstract:
Prosocial behaviours (e.g., sharing, helping) are known to be influenced by the process of social
comparison. Wealth is one variable that can lead to social comparison; people will describe
themselves as rich or poor in comparison to another individual. The purpose of the proposed
project is to investigate the influence of wealth and social comparison on prosocial behaviour in
children aged four to eight years old. Children will spin a wheel and win more (wealthy condition),
the same (control condition), or fewer (poor condition) stickers than a partner. Children will then be
asked if and how many stickers they would like to share with their partner. It is hypothesized that
children in the wealthy and poor conditions will share more and less, respectively, than children in
the control condition. Understanding the factors that influence prosociality in children is necessary
for encouraging and supporting the development of this important behaviour.

title: Social behaviour and aggression in the 5xFAD mouse model of Alzheimer's disease
abstract:
Background: Social dysfunctions in Alzheimer's disease (AD) are reported in a significant number
of the patient population, and greatly contribute to the overall societal burden of the disease. While
social withdrawal and aggression have been clearly documented in the AD patient population, they
have not been well assessed in transgenic mouse models of AD.
Methods: 5xFAD and control mice were housed in same-sex same-genotype pairs and were
tested in behavioural tests aimed at quantifying measures of sociability and aggressive behaviours.
Tests included a homecage interaction test, a newly-developed odour plus maze, and the three-
chamber sociability test.
Results: 5xFAD mice spent similar time interacting in their homecage, tended to spend less
time in an area containing an unfamiliar social odour (U=13, p=0.07), but spent more time in an
area containing a social stimulus (U=10, p=0.03) compared to control mice.
Conclusions: These results suggest a complex interplay between social avoidance and
approach behaviours that are dependent on the quality of the social stimuli. Although 5xFAD mice
avoid a social odour, they approach a social stimulus. It is expected that this phenomenon may be
due to apathy exhibited by these mice. These findings suggest that 5xFAD mice share key aspects
of social dysfunctions observed in AD patients.

authors_raw: Emre Fertan, Nicole Woodland, Dr. Richard E. Brown
title: Determining the age-related changes in spatial learning and memory in the 3xTG mouse model of Alzheimer's disease using the Hebb-Williams maze
abstract:
SUMMARY: The 3xTG mouse model of Alzheimer's disease develops intracellular plaques at 3
months, extracellular plaques at 6 months, and tau tangles by 12 months of age. We used the
Hebb-Williams maze with food reward to assess learning and memory in 3xTG mice and their
B6129SF2 wild type controls at 4 and 7 months of age. In the acquisition (Long-Term Memory)
phase, 3xTG mice reached the learning criterion significantly faster than the WT mice and males
reached criterion faster than the females. In the test phase (Working memory), 3xTG mice had
significantly longer latencies in the hard mazes than the easy and medium mazes, but there was
no such difficulty effects for the WT mice. The 4 month old male WT mice had significantly shorter
latencies then female WT mice but there was no sex difference for the 3xTG mice. At 7 months of
age male and female mice of both genotypes had longer latencies in the hard mazes. Our results
show that (1) 3xTG and male mice have better long term memory compared to the WT and female
mice. (2) Working memory deficits in the 3xTG mice start as early as 4 months but they can be
detected only in difficult tasks. (3) Anxiety and motivation may be affecting the cognitive
performance of 3xTG mice and WT controls.

title: The importance of the seemingly unimportant.
abstract:
Laboratory studies involving rodents are designed to control for as many extraneous variables as
possible. This generally results in the inclusion of at least one 'control' group, the goal for whom is
exposure to all of the non-manipulated variables of the experiment, in a fashion identical to that of
the experimental group, except for the manipulated variable. A number of reports over the years
that shown that seemingly less important variables such as sex of the experimenter, exposure to
routine lab procedures, and prior testing experience, can have experimental effects. We
demonstrate that repeated exposure to a series of events that included experimenter handling,
transport to and exposure to a novel room and arena had strong effects on anxiety-related
behaviour in pair-housed rats. Interestingly, repeated exposure did not have the same effects in
animals raised in isolation. These results will be discused within a stress inoculation framework.

authors_raw: Colin R. McCormick, Ralph S. Redden & Raymond M. Klein
title: Exogenous Eye Movements and Inhibition of Return: Is Environmental Context Essential for the Maintenance of IOR?
abstract:
Inhibition of return (IOR) functions by inhibiting orienting back to previously attended and
disengaged-from locations and through this process we are more likely to attend to new areas. The
mechanisms and properties of IOR are explored in this study, specifically looking at whether
background maintenance is essential for viewing IOR generated by exogenous eye movements. A
modification of Klein and MacInnes (1999) "Where's Waldo?" search experiment is used to do this.
But instead of allowing self-generated eye movements (in the service of searching for Waldo) we
guided the participant's oculomotor behaviour with a sequence of salient fixation stimuli. The
reaction times to orient to the final saccade were recorded using an eye tracker. There were six
different and equiprobable locations of the target on the screen at varying degrees away from the
previous fixation, or the fixation two before. There were 3 background conditions: 1) a Waldo
picture that remained present for the whole trial; 2) a Waldo picture that was removed at the time of
the appearance of the final target; 3) the series of eye movements was made on a blank
background. Results reflect those of Klein and MacInnes (1999) in that participants were slower to
respond to targets at locations that were previously disengaged from compared to targets at
previously unviewed locations, but only in trials where the background remained consistent
(conditions 1 and 3). Implications of this study for understanding IOR are discussed.

authors_raw: Alexis Wheeler and Dr. Richard Brown
title: The Effects of Exercise on Memory and Learning in 3xTg Mice and Their Wild Type Controls
abstract:
Exercise has shown to improve neurogenesis and reduce the onset of dementia in Alzheimer's
affected patients. Female triple transgenic Alzheimer's disease model mice and female B6129SF2
wild type mice were given access to either a functioning running wheel or a stationary running
wheel for a period of three weeks. After three weeks of voluntary running the mice were tested for
spatial learning and memory in the Morris Water Maze. Mice given access to a running wheel had
a significantly shorter swim distance to reach the hidden platform, more percent time spent in the
correct quadrant during the probe trials and more annulus crossings during the probe trials than
their stationary counterparts. Transgenic mice given a running wheel also performed comparably to
wild type mice with access to a running wheel. These results indicate that short-term exercise has
a significant effect on spatial learning and memory in Alzheimer's model mice and can help to
reduce the onset of dementia and other Alzheimer's symptoms. Testing other age groups, shorter
or longer running periods, as well as testing male mice would give further insight into the
prevention of Alzheimer's symptoms and the effects of short-term exercise on learning and
memory.

authors_raw: Ying Zhang, Julie Longard and Chris Moore, PhD
title: Does the discrepancy between self and other ratings of affect predict sharing in children?
abstract:
We examined if the discrepancy between children's mood and their empathic concern for another
child in distress would predict more sharing. Eighteen boys and 12 girls (M = 58 months, SD = 10
months) were asked to rate their self-mood using a Facial Affect Scale (FAS) before watching an
empathy-inducing video, then they were asked to rate their empathic concern toward the
protagonist using the FAS. For each participant, a discrepancy score between their self-mood and
empathic concern rating was calculated. Participants' sharing behaviours were then measured
using a resource allocation task, in which they were given opportunities to share stickers with the
protagonist. Spearman's correlational analysis between children's discrepancy scores and sharing
was not significant. Limitations of the study, including the ubiquitously high ratings of self-mood
using the FAS, are addressed. Retesting the correlation in future research may help to improve our
understanding of the mechanisms underlying children's generosity.

authors_raw: Laura Cutmore, Lotta Pries, Tracy Taylor
title: Let Me Give You a Hint: Recognition Cues Don't Alter Directed Forgetting in an Item- Method Task
abstract:
In an item-method directed forgetting paradigm, words are presented one at a time, each followed
by an instruction to Remember or Forget; on subsequent tests of explicit memory, performance is
better for Remember words than Forget words - a directed forgetting effect. The dominant view is
that the directed forgetting effect arises during encoding due to selective rehearsal of Remember
over Forget items. We attempted to falsify a strong view that the directed forgetting effect is due
only to encoding mechanisms. Following a typical item-method study phase, we presented yes-no
recognition test items in one of three colors: 1) green to indicate that the test item might have
received a Remember instruction at study; 2) red to indicate that the test item might have received
a Forget instruction at study; and, 3) black, which provided no cue to study instruction. In
Experiment 1, recognition color cues were mixed within-blocks; in Experiment 2, they were
manipulated between-blocks. We found no evidence that the directed forgetting effect is influenced
by recognition cueing

title: How to find the sexy secrets of the fruit fly's Va1v glomerulus.
abstract:
For the last 6 years in collaboration with the Janelia Research Campus of HHMI we have been
undertaking dense reconstruction of major parts of the Drosphila brain to produce wiring diagrams
of synaptic networks. In the last two years we densely reconstructed the Va1v glomerulus in the
antennal lobe. Dense reconstruction is an evolving technique that depends on advances in imaging
hardware, imaging automation and processing, tissue preparation, manual tracing, computer vision
and machine learning. In this presentation I will present the evolution of some of the techniques
that lead to dense reconstruction and where we hope to go from here.

title: Connectomic approaches to a simple brain
abstract:
The brain of the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster is unique in the range of genetic methods that
can target transgenes to the identified neurons of its nervous system, and remarkable for the
numerical and morphological determinacy of those neurons. Defining the complete synaptic
networks, or connectomes, of behaviourally significant regions of the fly's brain requires us to
reconstruct neurons at EM level, which is now routinely possible, albeit a highly labour intensive
process. The product is a connectome, one that is complete, accurate and permanent (CAP:
Brenner). Our work on this approach, now continued at the Janelia Research Campus of HHMI,
has already identified neuron candidates for the Reichardt elementary motion detector circuit for
directional selectivity, a longstanding mathematical model that for 50 years lacked biological
implementation. Of course, an anatomical network is only a map, incomplete insofar as it lacks
temporal information, necessary but not sufficient to analyse function. It is required for that
analysis because it identifies neurons that then must be tested functionally. An early example
identified a neuron required for fly UV phototaxis. This approach identifies a causal basis for
behaviour, in a way that behavioural analyses alone cannot, nor can electro/optophysiological
correlates of behaviour. In addition to enabling the interpretation of behaviour, a connectome is
also predictive, identifying surprise pathways for which no behaviour has yet been reported, but
which can then be tested.

title: The not very hungry caterpillar: Illness-induced anorexia may allow the caterpillar Manduca sexta to shift molecular resources to immunity
abstract:
Like other animals, larval M. sexta lose their appetite after an immune challenge, yet the biological
function of this illness-induced anorexia is poorly understood. This research examines the
hypothesis that illness-induced anorexia allows the caterpillar M. sexta to shift molecular resources
from food detoxification to immune function. Appetite loss occurred after injection of either
sephadex beads, which induce encapsulation, heat-killed Gram negative bacteria (Serratia
marcescens) or Gram positive bacteria (Bacillus cereus), which induce nodulation. Therefore,
illness-induced anorexia appears to be a general response to immune activation in M. sexta, as it
is in vertebrates. Caterpillars that received an immune challenge were more likely to die after
ingesting permethrin than that those did not. Conversely, a sublethal dose of permethrin reduced
resistance to the Gram negative bacterium S. marcescens. These results suggest that there is a
negative interaction between detoxification and immune defense. During an immune challenge,
caterpillars were less likely to consume food containing traces of quinine. We are in the preliminary
stages of using qPCR to test for a potential conflict between detoxification and immune defense.

authors_raw: Catherine Reeve, Jenna MacDonald, Kurtis Turnbull, and Shelley Adamo
title: Changes in food consumption, immune response, and immune gene regulation due to predator stress in the Tobacco Hornworm, Manduca sexta.
abstract:
When faced with the threat of predation, animals alter their behaviour and physiology in ways that
are believed to maximize short-term survival and growth. Furthermore, adaptive changes in
immune response are likely, as predation risk increases the chance of wounding. The current study
examined the effects of simulated predator stress on food consumption, weight gain, immune
response, and immune gene regulation in the Tobacco Hornworm, Manduca sexta. A predator rich
environment was simulated by subjecting newly molted fifth instar hornworms to a series of leg
pinches for three consecutive days. Subsequently, hemolymph was collected and a sample of fat
body tissue was extracted. The hemolymph was used to test the effect of simulated predator stress
on melanzation, number of hemocytes, total protein, PO activity, glutathione concentration,
lysozyme activity, and clotting time. RNA was extracted from the fat body tissue and, after cDNA
was generated, the relative expression of four immune genes (PAP3, Serpin3, Attacin, and
Lysozyme) were measured using qPCR. Results revealed that caterpillars in the simulated
predator stress group did not eat less than caterpillars in the control group, but gained significantly
less weight. The concentration of glutathione in the hemolymph was decreased in caterpillars in the
simulated predator stress group compared to the control group, and the hemolymph of caterpillars
in the simulated predator stress group clotted faster than the hemolymph of the caterpillars in the
control group. The simulated predator stress did not effect the regulation of the immune genes
examined.

authors_raw: Janusz Borycz, Jolanta Borycz and Ian A. Meinertzhagen
title: Searching the function of the gene inebriated in the Drosophila eye.
abstract:
Histamine (HA) is a neurotransmitter released at photoreceptor terminals in Drosophila. We have
previously found that as for vertebrate photoreceptor glutamate, most HA in the fly's eye is
recycled via a glial shuttle pathway. In the process two enzymes are involved. The enzyme Ebony,
which is expressed in epithelial glia that surround the photoreceptor terminals, conjugates b-
alanine (b-Ala) to HA, to synthesise b-alanyl histamine (carcinine, CA). Then the enzyme Tan,
which is expressed in the photoreceptors, hydrolyses CA to release HA and b-Ala. Transporters
are essential to maintain the process, but these are largely unknown. Recently, we have found
that carcinine is transported into photoreceptors by a transporter called CarT. We have also found
a layered pattern of expression of a second transporter called Inebriated in the Drosophila
eye. The inebriated (ine) gene encodes a Na+/Cl--dependent SLC6 family transporter and is
translated as two protein isoforms, long (P1) and short (P2). Photoreceptors specifically express
the P2 isoform whereas P1 is expressed in non-neuronal cells. Using Drosophila brain
synaptosome preparations we have found that Inebriated may be involved in transporting b-Ala out
of photoreceptors and at the same time may mediate photoreceptor uptake of CA. This function
would overlap that of CarT but Ine expression is distributed differently in the fly's eye and also from
vertebrate research on fast neurotransmitters, we know that glutamate and GABA are often
transported by multiple transporters to provide rapid elimination from the synaptic cleft.

title: Impulses buried in the electroretinogram (ERG) of Drosophila come from amacrine neurons.
abstract:
Amacrine neurons (Am) in Drosophila's first visual synaptic zone receive many inputs directly from
photoreceptors R1-6 at ~50 'tetrad' synapslets per axon terminal. Each tetrad also involves output
neurons L1-3, as a quartet in which R1-6 L1, L2, Am, L3. Each postsynaptic participant except
Am possesses ort histamine receptors, so can respond to histamine released from R1-6 during
illumination, but Am is obviously excluded. Frequent neurites from amacrines feed back directly
upon the photoreceptor axons, probably responsible for transiently cutting off R1-6's output, with a
delay observed of only ≤ 2 milliseconds. Histamine is recycled or made with three enzymes that
are individually defective in Drosophila mutants tan, ebony and hdc. As expected, the ON- and
OFF-transients in the ERG associated with L1-L3's histaminergic responses are missing in all three
mutants, but Hotta & Benzer (1969) nonetheless detected enigmatic wobbles on the initial phase of
the residual light response. Improved techniques using white mutants like w, hdc910 now reveal
these as bursts of extracellular impulses, the short initial latency of which confirms a monosynaptic
connection from R1-6. These therefore must be impulses from amacrines, the only tetrad neurons
not histamine-blocked. While L1-3 hyperpolarize, amacrines depolarize and spike, responding to a
neurotransmitter that is not histamine.

title: Is Alzheimer's a disease of the spleen?
abstract:
There are at least 10 theories about the causes of Alzheimer's Disease (AD), but those involving
amyloid beta and tau receive the most attention. Some time ago a professor of medicine told me
how kidney disease can cause neural damage. We have found that our 3x-Tg- AD mice have
enlarged spleens. As a joke I said that maybe AD is a disease of the spleen, BUT more and more
evidence points to abnormalities of the spleen in AD. This talk examines the spleen, the enteric
nervous system (ENS) and the brain-gut connections in AD. The role of the spleen, ENS and brain-
gut system in AD may be a red herring OR it may be the missing link in understanding the
development of AD and possibly other neurodegenerative disorders.

authors_raw: NESC: Zajdlik K, Lausch S, Crouse C, Elia T, Aronitz E, Ellis S, Fish J, Lagman P, MacDonald J, McGroarty E, Mustafa D, Piercey M, Bassil S, Connell L, Lee S, MacLellan K, Pappin J, Ribeiro G, Kraeutner S, Duffy KR
title: Investigation of neuron structural modifications within the visually deprived superior colliculus.
abstract:
Neurons within the mammalian primary visual pathway exhibit a high capacity for neural plasticity early in postnatal
development. Even a short duration of monocular deprivation (MD) can led to structural and functional neural
modifications that result in a profound visual impairment called amblyopia. Although modification of neurons with
the superior colliculus (SC) has been suggested to underlie ocular motor deficits associated with amblyogenic rearing,
identification of structural abnormalities within the SC has been elusive. In this study we imposed a complete form of
MD by inactivating one eye for 10 days. We reasoned that this extreme form of amblyogenic deprivation would be
more likely to precipitate measureable structural abnormalities within the SC. Following 10 days of MD we observed
a reduction in the size of neuron somata within the contralateral SC that receives most input from the inactivated eye.
We also showed that this form of MD is sufficient to cause a loss of neurofilament protein within the SC contralateral
to the inactivated eye. These results demonstrate that a profound monocular deprivation is sufficient to catalize
neuron structural modification within the superior colliculus, and this alteration may be enabled by a reduction of
neurofilament protein that destabilizes the cytoskeleton and raises the capacity for plasticity.

title: A Brain Museum Tour of Europe
abstract:
Europe has a rich history of neuroscience research and clinical neurology, but where can the history of European
neuroscience be found? The historical artifacts, documents and discoveries of European neuroscience exist in many
museums, but these are often forgotten or neglected within Europe and relatively unknown outside of Europe. The
purpose of this project is to present a tour of the brain museums of Europe on a WEBSITE, showing the museums
with materials relevant to the history of neuroscience in each country. The history of neuroscience relies of objects
from the past and this website describes the collections related to brain research in European museums. Using this
website will enable students and researchers to locate historical objects in museums and plan visits to these museums
for teaching and research. The presentation will consist of a poster/oral presentation and a website which meeting
participants can browse for information. The present poster/Website contains information on 31 brain museums in 18
countries, with more being added as we find them. The website is a work in progress and we hope that users will
provide us with information about brain museums which we have not yet discovered. If you are planning a trip to one
of the European cities with a brain museum, this website will guide you to the location and the exhibitions on
view. Enjoy your tour of Brain Museums in Europe! This project is sponsored by the FENS History of Neuroscience
Committee. If you know of brain museums not presented on this poster, please contact Richard Brown
at rebrown@dal.ca.

authors_raw: Puddicombe E, Pratt T, Longard J, Moore C
title: Does active sharing lead to increased positive affect in children?
abstract:
Although accumulating evidence suggests sharing can lead to increased positive affect in adults and children, it
remains unclear whether children must be active participants in the sharing, or if they might show the same affective
benefits when observing sharing. The current study investigated whether active sharing has an additional affective
benefit above and beyond passively observing sharing or passively receiving rewards. Participants included 63
typically-developing 5- and 6-year-olds. We used a between-subjects yoked design to match participants across the
three experimental conditions to control the number of stickers children received across 10 trials of a resource-
allocation task. Two independent coders rated children's videotaped facial affect. We found children in the active
sharing condition showed significantly higher positive affect than those in the passive sharing condition, and children
in the passive sharing condition showed significantly higher positive affect than those in the passive receiving
condition. Interestingly, children did not display more positive affect on sharing as opposed to no sharing trials.
Another factor, such as active choice, may account for the affective benefit in this context.

authors_raw: Rigney G, Chambers CT, Godbout R, Gruber R, Hall W, Reid G, Stremler R, Weiss SK, Witmans M, Begum EA, Corkum P
title: eHealth intervention recruitment: Relative success of digital, media, and traditional recruitment methods for Better Nights, Better Days
abstract:
Introduction
Approximately 25% of children experience insomnia, which can impact their physical and psychosocial health, as
well as their caregiver's well-being. Few children receive evidence-based interventions, due in part to a shortage of
available resources. Internet service delivery (eHealth) has the potential to be a powerful tool to overcome barriers to
treatment. To this end, we developed Better Nights, Better Days (BNBD) - an innovative eHealth program for
primary caregivers aiming to provide accessible and evidence-based care for insomnia in typically developing
children aged 1-10 years. We were interested in the uptake for this intervention delivery method.
Methods
A randomized controlled trial (RCT) is being conducted to evaluate the efficacy of BNBD, targeting 400 English
speaking primary caregivers across Canada. As BNBD is an eHealth intervention, the recruitment strategy was
primarily based on online tools. A comprehensive social media campaign was developed and executed in
collaboration with social media experts. As part of the online screening questionnaire, individuals were asked to
indicate how they heard about the study (e.g., advertisement, Facebook, family member or friend). The success of
recruitment was measured based on participant engagement and report.
Results
The BNBD RCT launched September 2016. Within three months over 2,000 individuals entered the study database to
begin screening, and the target number of 400 English speaking primary caregivers had consented, with their study
participation currently ongoing. Results from the online screening questionnaire indicated 36% heard about BNBD
through social media platforms (i.e., Facebook and Twitter), 26% through media and advertisements, and 25% from a
family member or friend.
Conclusion
The high rate of recruitment speaks to both the need for intervention, and the effectiveness of online recruitment
methods. Connecting to millennial parents requires the use of online resources, particularly social media. This
strategy increases study visibility through a dynamic online presence.

authors_raw: McCormick C, Redden R, Lawrence M, Klein R
title: On the Time-Course of Cued Temporal Attention
abstract:
A fruitful tool for studying endogenous control of temporal attention is the Kingstone paradigm (1992). Kingstone's
cues had two functions: providing information about the foreperiod and signalling to begin timing of that foreperiod.
Interested in how exogenous alerting might interact with endogenous alerting we implemented two novel
manipulations. First, we separated these two cue functions. Second, borrowing from Lawrence & Klein (2013) we
manipulated warning signal intensity: one signal entailed an increase in intensity (I^), which generates alerting
exogenously; the other signal entailed a qualitative change with no change in intensity (NO), which would not be
expected to generate alerting exogenously. When intensity was manipulated randomly from trial-to-trial, endogenous
shifts of attention in time were unexpectedly absent. We hypothesized that participants invested substantial cognitive
resources to detect the NO intensity signals and that interfered with their endogenous utilization of the pre-cues. To
test this idea, in the second experiment we blocked the manipulation of signal-intensity. Consistent with our
hypothesis, the Kingstone pattern was replicated in the I^ condition.

authors_raw: Shapiro JT, Korgan AC, King JL, Crowder NA
title: Novice Inter-Rater Reliability in Spike Sorting: Tetrodes vs. Single Channel Electrodes
abstract:
Measuring the response properties of individual neurons within the brain remains a fundamental technique in
neuroscience. A challenge in analyzing this in vivo electrophysiological data, called spike sorting, involves
distinguishing which action potentials arise from the jumbled activity of several simultaneously recorded neurons.
Most modern spike sorting software uses semi-automated tools that require a skilled analyst, and thus spike sorting
accuracy depends on subjective judgments. Multi-channel electrodes (e.g. tetrodes) have the capacity to yield more
data than single-channel electrodes, but it may be more difficult for analysts to learn to spike sort multi-channel data
because of the additional complexity. In this study two novice analysts with similar levels of training sorted identical
recordings from mouse visual cortex that were acquired with single-channel electrodes or tetrodes to determine
whether their accuracy was dependent on the type of data being analyzed. The novice analysts performed similarly
with both kinds of electrophysiological data

authors_raw: Patrick E, Cutmore L, Kroeker J, Jones S, Salmon J, Eskes G
title: Assessing Attention: A tablet-based proof of concept with the Dalhousie Computerized Attention Battery (DalCAB)
abstract:
Attention deficits can occur with medical, neurological or psychiatric illnesses and limit a person's independence and
quality of life. We developed the Dalhousie Computerized Attention Battery (DalCAB) to measure various aspects of
attention and have established the validity and reliability of a desktop version of DalCAB in healthy younger and
older adults. In this project we sought to investigate whether performance on a touch-screen tablet version of the
DalCAB would generate similar performance patterns compared to a normative data set collected on a desktop
computer. One hundred healthy young adults (mean(SD) age = 22(3.7) yrs) participated in two studies. Study one
(n=40) examined the optimal position of the 2-response touch buttons on a Surface Pro 4 touchpad screen and Study
two (n=60) explored the effect of tablet position and screen size. Performance patterns were analyzed within each
study and also compared to normative data from fifty young adults (mean(SD) age = 21.78(2.9) yrs) previously
collected on an Apple iMac desktop version with a mouse. Within study analyses of tablet data showed that all
expected performance patterns were obtained consistently across both studies, and these patterns were not strongly
affected by the location of the response touch buttons, the tablet placement or the screen size. Reaction time (RT)
performance patterns from the tablet were similar to the desktop patterns, although RTs on the tablet were slower
overall, sometimes resulting in greater differences between task variables. These results indicate the robustness of
attention performance patterns, and the DalCAB tablet version is a promising alternative to the desktop version. Thus,
this study extends the potential applicability and marketability of the DalCAB to different populations and
environments.

title: 15 Years of ANT Research: Exploring the evolution of the Attention Network Test
abstract:
The Attention Network Test (ANT) is a computer-based tool widely used to disassociate and measure three networks
of attention: alerting, orienting and executive functioning. Originally developed 15 years ago by Posner and
his colleagues (Fan et al., 2002) the ANT has been cited for use in over 500 experiments. The present survey
investigates the evolution of the ANT and the ways in which it has been employed and extended. Through the
development of a comprehensive database, we are able to explore: the various modifications from original ANT that
have been simulated, the applications to which it has been used, and attention network scores across
various conditions, groups, and regions.

title: Utilization of Research Electronic Data Capture (REDCap) in a web-based Randomized Controlled Trial
abstract:
Research Electronic Data Capture (REDCap) is a web-based electronic data capture software that allows researchers
to build project-specific data collection instruments to manage research data, store data securely, and facilitate data
analysis. Its randomization module makes it an idea tool for longitudinal studies and randomized controlled trials
(RCTs). This software is currently being utilized in a national RCT, Better Nights, Better Days, coordinated through
Corkum LABS. This resource has demonstrated capabilities to facilitate study procedures while maintaining the
security of study data, reducing staff time, and incidental costs. Measures are housed in the REDCap platform and
administered automatically to participants in a sequential manner throughout the study. Randomization was
performed within REDCap. REDCap also provides features to export data in multiple formats (SPSS, Excel, R
Statistical Software, SAS, or STATA). The RECap database can be easily modified for future studies.

authors_raw: Horne, JA, McLin S, Wiederman M, Borycz JA, Tarnogorska D, FlyEM Project Team, Plaza S, Xu S, Hess H, Meinertzhagen IA
title: Major connections in glomerulus VA1v in the Drosophila antennal lobe
abstract: 1:45--3:00 Session 5 Chair: Dr. Ian Weaver 1:45 T23) The roles of parvalbumin and somatostatin expressing interneurons in modulating contrast adaptation in mouse primary visual cortex King JL, Korgan AC, Crowder NA 2:00 T24) The Effect of French Immersion on Native English Processing in the Brain Tagarelli KM, Lazar-Kurz Z, Newman AJ 2:15 T25) Exploring Parent-Reported Perioperative Distress Behaviours in Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder Cameron E, Snow S, Smith I, Chorney J 2:30 T26) Potential molecular conflict between detoxification and immune defense in Manduca sexta Hall LE, Adamo SA 2:45 T27) Illness-induced anorexia may help promote immune function by reducing the use of glutathione, a limited resource, by detoxification pathways. Miller D, Hall L, Adamo S 3:00--3:30 Coffee Break (LSC 4316--Study Centre) 3:30--4:45 Session 6 Chair: Dr. Leslie Phillmore 3:30 T28) Susceptibility to monocular deprivation following immersion in darkness just prior to the critical period peak Lingley A, Duffy KR 3:45 T29) Drinking frequency, drinking quantity, and alcohol-related problems as trait-states:A 4-wave, 18-month longitudinal study of female undergraduates Bartel, SJ, Sherry SB, Nealis LJ, Collins JL, Lee-Baggley DL, Stewart SH 4:00 T30) Orthographic knowledge as a predictor of gains in lexical access MacKay E, Deacon H, Conrad N 4:15 T31) The Connectome of a Ciona intestinalis larva Ryan K 4:30 T32) A second complete connectome: the larval CNS of the ascidian Ciona intestinalis Meinertzhagen IA, Lu Z, Ryan K 5:00-7:00 Dinner and Reception 2nd Floor Lounge

authors_raw: Vannier SA, Adare K, Rosen NO
title: First-time mothers' attributions for postpartum sexual changes: Associations with sexual and relationship well-being
abstract:
The transition to parenthood presents women with notable sexual and relationship challenges. There is considerable
research examining physiological predictors of postpartum sexual and relationship well-being, but less is known
about psychological factors. Causal attributions for sexual changes may be of particular importance because one
specific cause may be difficult to pinpoint given the wide range of postpartum sexual concerns and problems among
first-time parents. Attributions can be conceptualized on four dimensions: internal/external (i.e., is the event due to the
self or the situation), stable/unstable (i.e., will the event occur again in the future), global/specific (i.e. will the cause
of the event affect other situations), and partner responsibility (i.e., is the partner the cause). In the current study 117
first-time mothers completed measures assessing attributions for postpartum sexual changes, sexual satisfaction,
functioning, and distress, and relationship satisfaction. Internal, stable, and global attributions were associated with
greater sexual distress and lower relationship satisfaction. Partner attributions were associated with lower sexual and
relationship satisfaction. These associations remained after controlling for time postpartum, breastfeeding, sexual
frequency, and relationship duration. As postpartum sexual changes are common, attributions for these changes may
be a valuable target for intervention by clinicians and healthcare professionals.

title: Understanding the Neural Bases for Social Deficits in Alzheimer's Disease
abstract:
Alzheimer's Disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by abnormal accumulation of b-
amyloid protein in the brain. Due to the widespread nature of the disease, lesions can be seen throughout the brain,
causing a variety of deficits. In order to characterize these deficits, a number of mouse models--such as the 5xFAD
strain--have been developed; however, most studies have focused on motor behaviour, cognition, or sensory
perception, but little consideration has been given to social deficits. Mice--like humans--are highly social animals,
and research has linked social isolation and defeat with a number of behavioural, cognitive, and physiological changes.
As a result, this study aims to elucidate the impact of neurodegeneration on social behaviours in the 5xFAD mouse
strain. In particular, this work examines responses to olfactory social cues, social vs nonsocial preference, social
memory, and aggression in male and female 5xFAD transgenic and wild-type control mice at 3, 6, 9, and 12 months
of age.

authors_raw: Patrick E, Drake R, Kroeker J, Chisholm J, Klein R, Westwood D, Eskes G
title: RadarWatch: Validating an attention training game with a focus on vigilance decrement
abstract:
Attention deficits can occur with medical, neurological or psychiatric illnesses and limit a person's independence and
quality of life. Thus, rehabilitation of these deficits would be beneficial. Posner and colleagues have provided a
framework for the attention system that involves three independent but interacting attentional brain networks. The
alerting or vigilance network underlies the ability to develop and sustain a state of mental alertness, which
consequently produces more rapid detection, selection, and responding. We have recently developed a vigilance game,
Radar Watch, that involves tracking a radar screen for designated targets over extended periods of time. Typically in
vigilance tasks, the ability to detect and respond to targets decreases as time on the task increases, a phenomenon
known as the vigilance decrement. Research has demonstrated that this decrement can occur within the first 5 - 30
minutes of a task and is influenced by how often a target appears. The purpose of the current study was to examine for
the presence of a vigilance decrement in Radar Watch. We also investigated if the target rate (high or low) influenced
the vigilance decrement. Twenty young adults (mean(SD) age = 20.7(4.3) yrs) were randomly assigned to one of two
groups, high target rate or low target rate, and were asked to monitor the radar display for a designated target for
approximately one hour. Reaction time (RT) and accuracy of target detection was measured over 6 blocks of 60 radar
sweep trials. Preliminary but promising results, as well as future applications, will be discussed in the context of the
alerting network and stroke rehabilitation.

title: God and Karma: Exploring Generosity Levels among Christians and Buddhists in Hong Kong
abstract:
In most religions around the world, generosity toward others is a moral virtue. Social psychologists are currently
working to better understand the factors motivating religious believers to act prosocially toward their ingroup
members, as well as others. This study investigated generosity behavior among 40 Christians and 40 Buddhists in
Hong Kong, China. Participants played four iterations of a dictator game, in which they divided the equivalent of one
day's wage between themselves, a nearby member of their own religion, a faraway member of their own religion, and
a faraway member of a different religion. Buddhists kept significantly less money for themselves than Christians. I
discuss possible reasons for this outcome, including belief types, religious identity, and methodological concerns. My
results highlight a need within the psychology of religion for additional research investigating motivating factors
among believers of religions with agentic versus non-agentic moralizing forces.

title: Item-based Rasch Analysis of the Merrill-Palmer-Revised Scales of Development for Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract:
Gold-standard diagnostic assessment for children suspected of having autism spectrum disorder (ASD) includes
cognitive testing. Psychometrically strong cognitive measures are also indispensable in longitudinal research.
Assessment of cognition in ASD is complicated by variable skill levels and uneven profiles of strengths and
weaknesses, as well as inherent social communication differences. Few tests have been validated for use with this
population. The Merrill-Palmer-Revised Scales of Development (M-P-R) is a standardized cognitive assessment that
is well-validated and reliable for use with typically developing preschoolers. However, the suitability of the M-P-R
for assessing children with ASD has not been well-researched. The current study's aim is to use an application of
Item Response Theory, Rasch Analysis, to investigate the psychometric properties of the M-P-R in a large group of
preschoolers with ASD. This presentation will focus on preliminary results of the Rasch Analysis.

title: When white is red in the eye of the Drosophila beholder, and philosophy 101
abstract:
A persistent belief in the fly's-eye community has been that certain useful, easily assayed electrical responses come
from particular neurons lying postsynaptic to the fly's photoreceptors -- vertebrate bipolar neuron equivalents. This
belief stems from two failures, one observational and one 'philosophical'.
The supposed gold-standard test for a feature like this electrical signature is to delete the putative gene for it (ort1 in
Drosophila), show that responses vanish, then reintroduce ort1 again by clever genetics, rescuing responsiveness. In
order to mark reintroduction, groups who did this used a gene marker along with ort1, the readily detectable white-
eye phenotype. This assumes that the electrical responses from white are identical to those from red-eyed, wild-type
flies, but the ON-OFF visual responses from the latter reveal very different properties from those in w1118 flies: they
must have different cellular origins. Despite its cachet, the deletion-rescue formula fails to disclose the origins of
visual responses in non-mutant flies.
As to why "gold-standard" genetics produces disappointing results, the formula fits a category well known to
logicians -- "the fallacy of affirming the consequent". If you assume that there is only a single solution to the
question posed when there actually could be 2 or more alternative solutions, you've bought into your own story, #1.
The correct answer could instead be #2 (or #3…), which you failed to eliminate.

title: FoxP2 Protein Expression in Zebra Finches Reared by Single Parents
abstract:
The zebra finch (Taeniopygia guttata), a sexually dimorphic songbird native to Australia, is one of the best-studied
models of vocal learning. Finches have specialized neural circuitry critical for song learning; of significant
importance is the basal ganglia component, Area X. In addition, songbird brains express FoxP2, a transcription factor
that belongs to a family of genes involved in vocal communication in both humans and songbirds. Levels of FoxP2
protein in Area X decrease across the period of song development, however, it is unclear whether abnormal rearing,
such as lack of appropriate tutor modelling, affects typical FoxP2 protein expression. Therefore, the purpose of this
study was to analyze FoxP2 protein expression in zebra finches reared in typical biparental nests, and atypical
paternal-only, or maternal-only nests. To do so, FoxP2 immunohistochemistry and quantification was completed with
birds that had been reared to adulthood under these conditions. FoxP2 protein expression was not robust enough to
detect Area X as a discernable nucleus in any birds. Therefore, an exploratory search for FoxP2 protein labelling was
completed. There was evidence of lateralized expression of FoxP2 in the striatum of typically reared birds, but birds
reared by a single father or single mother did not show this lateralization.

title: Cue-induced Functional Connectivity in the Nucleus Accumbens and Anterior Cingulate Gyrus in Smokers with and without Psychosis
abstract:
The rate of smoking in patients with psychosis is 70 % (Moran, Sampath, Stein & Hong, 2012). Smoking stimuli
presented to smokers will elicit a craving response (McBride, Barrett, Kelly, Aw, & Dagher, 2006). The mesolimbic
dopamine pathway is implicated in smoking and psychosis and hyperdopamingeria is seen in both groups (Chambers,
Krystal & Self, 2001; Fonder et al., 2005). Six patient smokers and six control smokers underwent an fMRI, during
which participants were presented with cues. fMRI data was analyzed using FSL and the anterior cingulate gyrus and
nucleus accumbens were regions of interest (ROI) for seed based functional connectivity. Patients demonstrate a
higher degree of functional connectivity between the ROIs and brain regions involved in emotion, memory and
craving. This research broadens the understanding of the neurobiological mechanisms behind the high smoking rate in
psychotic individuals and helps to gain a greater understanding of the addiction pathways in these individuals.

authors_raw: Habibnezhad M, Lawrence M, Klein R
title: Employing Rescorla's truly random condition to measure truly exogenous covert attention
abstract:
In studies measuring the exogenous attention, cues are employed to capture attention. Ideally, cues are supposed to be
both spatially and temporally uninformative of the target stimulus. Studies carried out to measure exogenous attention
have successfully made the cues spatially uninformative, while temporal contingency between the cue and the target
has been minimized with different measures, none of which guarantees complete uninformativeness of the cues. The
present study used Rescorla's truly random condition to produce such cues.

authors_raw: Isaacs J, Corkum P, Godbout R, Witmans M
title: Pediatric Insomnia Treatment in the Context of Obstructive Sleep Apnea: A Usability and Pilot Study
abstract:
1-5% of children suffer from obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), a disorder marked by upper airway blockage during
sleep. Over 50% of children with OSA exhibit insomnia symptoms. Of the children with both OSA and insomnia, 20-
25% still have insomnia after OSA treatment (i.e., CPAP, adenotonsillectomy). Access to treatment for residual
insomnia among children previously treated for OSA is limited. Better Nights, Better Days - Obstructive Sleep
Apnea (BNBD-OSA), is a parent-directed online intervention for treating residual insomnia following successful OSA
treatment. The intervention represents an extension of BNBD, an online program which treats primary childhood
insomnia. In order to test the usability and initial success of BNBD-OSA, 10 parents will complete the program and
describe their experiences with it. Changes in children's sleep patterns will be directly measured with a sleep diary
and actigraphy. BNBD-OSA will allow distance treatment for pediatric insomnia in the context of OSA, significantly
reducing the personal and economic costs of childhood sleep disorders.

title: The influence of Pavlov on Lashley and Hebb
abstract:
Ivan P. Pavlov was awarded the Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine in December, 1904 "in recognition of his
work on the physiology of digestion". By this time Pavlov had started to study reflexes and he gave the first definition
of the "conditioned reflex" and described how he came to study "psychical reflexes" in his "Madrid speech" (April
1903). In 1906, Pavlov [spelled Pawlow] gave the Huxley Lecture, entitled "The scientific investigation of the
psychical faculties or processes in the higher animals" which was published in The Lancet (6 Oct. 1906). The
English-speaking world thus became aware of Pavlov's work on conditioned reflexes, and in 1909, Robert M. Yerkes
and Sergius Morgulis published a review of Pavlov's research in The Psychological Bulletin entitled "The method of
Pawlow in animal psychology". One of the earliest proponents of classical conditioning in the USA was John B.
Watson, the founder of "Behaviorism", whose 1915 Presidential Address to the American Psychological Association
was entitled "The place of the conditioned reflex in Psychology" (The Psychological Review, March 1916). Watson
described his experiments on conditioned motor reflexes using the method of Bechterev, and the research of Karl S.
Lashley on conditioned salivary reflexes in humans. Watson conducted his most infamous experiment on
"Conditioned emotional reactions" on "little Albert" in 1920. Pavlov visited America in 1923 and 1929 and his
Lectures on conditioned reflexes were published in English in 1928. As discussed by Ruiz et al (2003. Spanish J.
Psychol. 6, 99-111), Pavlov provided American psychologists with three approaches to the study of brain and
behaviour: (1) an objective philosophical approach as opposed to subjective methods such as introspection; and (2) a
methodological approach through the use of conditioned reflexes. While these were embraced by Americans, (3) the
theoretical approach to the study of higher nervous activity through conditioned reflexes, was not so readily
embraced. Although Pavlov's methods of conditioned reflexes influenced the theories of Edwin Guthrie, B.F. Skinner,
Clark Hull and Kenneth Spence, Guthrie and Lashley (1930) were critical of Pavlov's theory of neural function.
Pavlov did not believe in the American theory of Behaviorism and wrote a scathing reply to these critiques (1932).
Meanwhile, Pavlov's student, Boris Babkin moved to Canada where he influenced Donald Hebb. Hebb
developed a neurophysiological theory that integrated the ideas of Pavlov and Sherrington on reflexes with the
theories of Lashley, Kohler and Tolman into a coherent theory that provided a neural mechanism that explained
perception, attention, learning and memory. Hebb considered the neural mechanisms of perceptual integration and
came up with a theory that reconciled the two opposing theories of neural function in associative learning: the S-R
telephone exchange theory and the field theory of equipotentiality. Hebb developed three ideas to provide a "modern"
view of the neural changes underlying cognitive function: (1) the "Hebb synapse" or "Hebb learning rule"; (2) the cell
assembly and (3) the phase sequence.
The Rescorla - Wagner model (1972) stimulated new behavioural research into classical conditioning while
neurobiologists use classical conditioning to study the neural mechanisms of learning and memory in both
invertebrates and vertebrates. Today Pavlovian conditioning is used to study the neurobiology of learning and
memory at four levels: (1) behavioural; (2) neural systems and circuits; (3) cellular mechanisms of synaptic plasticity;
and (4) molecular mechanisms of neural plasticity underlying learning and memory. Of note are Kandel's studies of
the cellular and molecular mechanisms of memory in Aplysia; the work of LeDoux, Faneslow and others on the
neural circuits of conditioned fear and the work of Thompson on the role of the cerebellum in conditioned motor
responses. Pavlovian conditioning is also used to understand abnormal behaviour and to develop clinical methods of
behaviour therapy. Thus, much of the history of Psychology and Neuroscience in America has been influenced by the
concepts of classical conditioning as developed by Pavlov, criticized by Lashley and incorporated into Hebb's
neurophysiological theory.

authors_raw: Lee K, Weaver I, Goguen D
title: The impact of fetal and neonatal experience on mammalian brain development and stress responsivity
abstract:
In humans, experimental and prospective epidemiological research suggests that gestational maternal stress has been
shown to produce stable, long-term effects on the physical and psychological development of offspring. Our current
research focuses on the role of the transcription factor and chromatin remodeling protein, ATRX, in hippocampal
neuron survival in response to psychological stress exposure during the third trimester. Using wild-type and ATRX
heterozygous mice, we will examine the effects of psychological gestational stress (PSG) on the quality of maternal
care, and on cognitive ability and the expression of anxiety-like behaviors in offspring. Additionally, we will attempt
to reverse the effects of PSG by performing rescue experiments either by ATRX overexpression, or by H2AX
ubiquitination/histone deacetylase inhibition to promote repair protein recruitment to DNA damage sites in vivo.

title: Emotion Regulation in Couples Affected by Female Sexual Interest and Arousal Disorder
abstract:
Introduction. Female sexual interest/arousal disorder (SIAD) is a sexual dysfunction characterized by absent or
significantly reduced sexual interest or arousal, accompanied by significant distress. Women with clinically low
sexual desire and arousal have reported more difficulties with emotion regulation than controls; however, whether
emotion regulation was associated with women's (and partners') adjustment to the condition has not been assessed.
The symptoms of SIAD typically occur in the context of a romantic relationship, meaning that partners are also
impacted and will have their own emotional responses to the condition. Therefore, it is essential to take a dyadic
approach - which past research has neglected to do - in the study of emotion regulation in SIAD.
Aim. The current study aims to investigate how emotion regulation in couples affected by SIAD relates to their
relational and sexual wellbeing (e.g., sexual satisfaction, distress, and desire).
Methods. Women with SIAD and their partners (N =102) will complete standardized self-report questionnaires
assessing emotion regulation, relationship satisfaction, sexual satisfaction, sexual desire, and sexual distress. Data will
be analyzed per the Actor-Partner Interdependence model using multilevel modeling.
Predicted Results. Individuals with more effective emotion regulation will report greater relationship satisfaction and
sexual wellbeing. Further, individuals with more effective emotion regulation will have partners who report greater
relational and sexual wellbeing, compared to those with poorer emotion regulation.

authors_raw: Strickland NJ, Stewart SH, Kehayes I-L, Wekerle C, Moore C, Caring Campus Team
title: The Moderating Role of Self-Compassion on the Relation between Alcohol-Involved Sexual Assault and Negative Emotional Outcomes in Undergraduate Men and Women
abstract:
Approximately 15% of students have experienced alcohol-involved sexual assault (AISA; Elliott et al., 2004). Victim-
drinking AISA may result in higher rates of self-blame, and self-compassion (SC; i.e., extending the same kindness
and care towards oneself that one would to someone else in a similar situation) may promote resilience by attenuating
the association between AISA and negative emotional outcomes. Undergraduates (N = 1,255) completed measures
tapping past-term victim-drinking AISA (see Kehayes et al., under review), SC (i.e., Self-Compassion Scale; SCS;
Neff, 2003b), anxiety and depression (K-10; Kessler et al., 2002), and self-perceived mental health (Ahmad et al.,
2014). It was hypothesized that: AISA would be positively associated with anxiety, depression, and poor self-
perceived mental health (H1), high SC would be positively related to mental health, and negatively related to anxiety
and depression (H2), and SC would moderate (i.e., buffer) the association between AISA and these negative outcomes
(H3). Preliminary results showed that, partially supportive of H1, AISA was related to higher depression (r = .07, p
< .05). In addition, consistent with H2, high SC was associated with better mental health (r = .56, p < .05), and lower
depression (r = -.61, p < .05) and anxiety (r = -.47, p < .05). In partial support of H3, tests of moderation showed a
significant interaction between the self-criticism scale of the SCS and AISA on self-perceived mental health.
However, instead of the hypothesized buffering effect, it was found that endorsing the self-criticism subscale of the
SCS predicted lower levels of mental health, regardless of whether AISA was experienced, but those who had low
levels of self-criticism only had poor mental health if they have experienced AISA. These results suggest that for
people who are not already self-critical, experiencing AISA can lead to worse outcomes. Results will be further
explored following confirmatory factor analyses of the SCS.

authors_raw: Wheeler RV, Alam S, Weaver ICG, Franklin TB
title: Behavioural, Molecular and Epigenetic consequences of Perinatal Infection throughout Development and into Adulthood.
abstract:
Human studies have indicated a link between perinatal infection (PI) and the development of autism-spectrum
disorder (ASD) suggesting an epigenetic etiology. Similarly, mice exposed to PI have an ASD-like phenotype,
behaviourally and molecularly. In the current study, we injected pregnant C57/BL6 mice with lipopolysaccharide or
polyinosinic:polycytidylic acid to induce a bacterial or viral response, respectively. The offspring were tested during
development and at adulthood for social communication and interaction, and repetitive behaviours. Additionally, the
expression of several synaptic and regulatory epigenetic genes were analyzed in the brains of offspring at post-natal
day zero. PI mice displayed abnormal social behaviours, repetitive behaviours and gene expression compared to
control mice. Future directions investigating the potential for PI-induced epigenetic alterations will also be discussed.

authors_raw: Rehan S, Rubinfeld L, Eskes G
title: Is there a relationship between working memory and reading in university students?
abstract:
University students with a history of reading difficulties (HRD) experience reading and academic difficulties in the
university setting and are in need of support; however, without a diagnosis of a learning disability, they are not
eligible for academic accommodations. A critical relationship between working memory and reading has been well-
documented and working memory (WM) deficits are common in individuals with reading deficits. We are conducting
a study that aims to characterize the reading, attention, WM abilities of HRD undergraduate students and to
investigate the effectiveness of core WM training in improving these students' working memory and reading abilities.
As a first step, we have explored the relationship between WM and reading in a control group of 23 undergraduate
students without a history of reading difficulties (NRD; M(SD) age = 20.7(1.1)). Hierarchical linear regression
analyses revealed that verbal working memory performance using the Operation Span was a significant predictor of
performance on all measures of word reading (b = .49), non-word reading (b = .59), reading rate (b = .61), and
reading comprehension (b = .76). In contrast, visual working memory performance on the Symmetry Span was not a
significant predictor of reading performance (ps>.05). Results confirm the relationship between verbal working
memory and reading and support our plan to investigating WM training in HRD undergraduate students.

title: Male Experience, Female Investment and Offspring Anxiety Behavior: Where Father's Nature Meets Mother's Nurture
abstract:
Detecting past experiences may inform a female about prevailing ecological conditions, in addition to paternally-
induced phenotypes that may be disseminated to offspring. We examined whether a male rat's exposure to metabolic
stressor (high-fat diet; HFD) influences a female's attraction towards a male, subsequent mother-infant interactions
and the development of defensive (emotional) responses in the offspring. Females displayed less interest in males
following HFD conditions. Mothers that reared young in larger, semi-naturalistic housing provided more licking and
grooming and active arched-back-nursing behavior toward their offspring compared to dams housed in standard
housing, although some effects interacted with paternal diet exposure. Paternal diet and maternal rearing environment
revealed sex-dependent differences in offspring weight, juvenile social interactions and anxiety-like behavior in
adolescence. Additionally, paternal predator experience and maternal housing independently affected variations
in crf gene promoter acetylation and crf gene expression in response to an acute stressor in offspring. Our results
show for the first time in mammals that male HFD consumption may contribute to stable behavioral variation among
females in preference for mates and maternal care, even when the females are not directly exposed to HFD. These
results, together with our previous findings, suggest that paternal HFD exposure and maternal rearing conditions can
influence maternal behavior and development of defensive responses of offspring.

authors_raw: Vidovic V, Smith MM, Sherry SB, Stewart SH, Saklofske DH
title: Perfectionistic Concerns Confer Risk for Anxiety Symptoms: A Meta-Analysis of 11 Longitudinal Studies
abstract:
Over 30 years of case reports, theoretical accounts, and empirical research implicate perfectionism in anxiety, yet the
extent to which perfectionism predicts longitudinal changes in anxiety remains unclear. To address this, we conducted
a meta-analysis of research testing whether socially prescribed perfectionism, concern over mistakes, doubts about
actions, self-oriented perfectionism, and personal standards predict change in anxiety, after controlling for baseline
anxiety. Our literature search yielded 11 relevant studies for inclusion (N = 3, 144). Meta-analysis, using random-
effects models, revealed socially prescribed perfectionism, concern over mistakes, and doubts about actions, but not
self-oriented perfectionism or personal standards, displayed small positive relationships with follow-up anxiety, after
controlling for baseline anxiety. Thus, our study highlights the unique role of different perfectionism dimensions in
psychopathology, and offers novel insight into the pre-morbid personality of individuals particularly prone to
symptoms of anxiety.

authors_raw: Richardson HM, Vannier SA, Rosen NO
title: Examining Links Between Sexual Savouring and Sexual and Relationship Well-Being
abstract:
Savouring refers to an individual's active ability to anticipate, focus on, and appreciate positive experiences.
Savouring has three components: anticipation (e.g., enjoyment of events before they occur), savouring the moment
(e.g., prolonging enjoyment by own effort), and reminiscing (e.g., enjoying looking back on happy times). Savouring
has been linked to positive outcomes such as greater life satisfaction, self-esteem, and happiness. There has been no
research examining savouring of positive sexual experiences, however, the previous literature linking savouring to
general positive outcomes suggests that more savouring may promote greater sexual and relationship well-being. In
the current study, 303 participants completed online measures assessing sexual savouring, distress, frequency,
functioning, satisfaction, and relationship quality. Sexual anticipation was associated with higher sexual functioning
and satisfaction. Sexual savouring of the moment was associated with higher sexual frequency, functioning, and
satisfaction, greater relationship quality, and lower sexual distress. Sexual reminiscing was associated with lower
male sexual functioning. These results suggest that individuals who anticipate and savour their positive sexual
experiences may experience greater overall sexual and relationship well-being.

authors_raw: Pridy C, Watt M, Stewart SH
title: Self-Distraction During Exercise in Clients with High (versus Low) Anxiety Sensitivity
abstract:
Anxiety sensitivity (AS) is fear of sensations experienced during emotional arousal or physical activity. High AS is
associated with increased risk of psychopathology (Reiss, 1991). People with high AS are avoid exercise because it
induces feared sensations (Smits, et al., 2010). Running has, however, been employed as interoceptive exposure (IE)
to enhance efficacy of a brief CBT intervention for high AS (e.g., Watt et al., 2006). Self-distraciton during IE trials
may enhance or detract from their effectiveness. Stevinson and Biddle (1998; 1999) outlined a comprehensive model
of four exercise-related cognitive styles combining the two dimensions of attentional focus (internal/external) and task
relevancy(relevant/irrelevant). The proposed project will develop and validate a 20-item Attention and Distraction
during Exercise Questionnaire (ADEQ), tapping the four cognitive styles outlined by Stevinson and Biddle, plus
qualities of music used for listening during exercise. We will test predictions that high (vs. low) AS participants will
favour a self-distractive, externally-focused task irrelevant cognitive style; that high AS participants will prefer
an externally-focused and task irrelevant style when deliberately self-distracting from distressing interoceptive cues
or an internally-focused and task-relevant style when catastrophizing about feared exercise-induced sensations; and
