that an externally-focused and task irrelevant style will moderate the known, direct relationship between AS and
exercise avoidance. Baseline data gathered during the proposed study will assist in future efforts to determine
whether limiting self-distraction during an exercise-based intervention for high AS can help to maximize treatment
benefits.

authors_raw: MacNeil H, Klein R, d'Entremont G
title: Investigating the neural signature of Inhibition of Return using a multimodal paradigm
abstract:
Inhibition of Return (IOR) is the phenomenon whereby attention is inhibited at locations that have previously been
attended to as a mechanism to reduce unnecessary attention. Previous research looking at event-related potentials
(ERPs) using electroencephalography (EEG) has suggested that P1 reductions are the electrophysiological markers of
IOR. However, recent work has demonstrated that P1 reductions may simply be the result of repetitive sensory
stimulation rather than of IOR itself. This suggestion has been examined using multi-modal IOR paradigms to
compare P1 components in conditions in which the modality is repeated to conditions in which IOR is generated
across modalities. The present study aimed to expand on these findings. It was hypothesized that IOR would be
observed across all four conditions of Cue Modality and Cue-Target Onset Asynchronies (CTOA) of either 700 ms or
1000 ms, and that P1 reductions would only be seen in the visual-visual conditions. The results supported the
hypothesis that IOR effects would be generated across all four conditions. The effect of Cue Modality on P1
amplitude was insignificant, however it did show a trend suggesting increased P1 cueing effects for the Visual Cues
compared to the Tactile Cues. The results of this study lay out the framework to further test this hypothesis in future
research using a larger sample size.

title: On the choice of animal models in neuroscience
abstract:
I recently was a member of a panel that reviewed the value of various animal models for study of the neural basis of
human amblyopia and its treatment. This talk will summarize our findings as well as our recommendations for future
research to provide cohesion across animal models and suggestions for a protocol to guide the steps towards
translations of results from animal models to human clinical trials. Our recommendations on the latter may generalize
to animal models of other neurological disorders.

authors_raw: King JL, Korgan AC, Crowder NA
title: The roles of parvalbumin and somatostatin expressing interneurons in modulating contrast adaptation in mouse primary visual cortex
abstract:
Contrast adaptation is a visual phenomenon induced by prolonged viewing of a high contrast stimulus, which then
decreases the perceived contrast of a subsequently presented stimulus. At the cellular level it has been well
established that contrast adaptation causes characteristic shifts in the contrast response functions of primary visual
cortex (V1) neurons, but the neural circuitry responsible for this shift is still unclear. Our lab recently demonstrated
that when V1 is silenced during adaptation, via optogenetically stimulating GABAergic interneurons, the magnitude
of adaptation is attenuated, and the adaptation that does occur is similar to that measured in thalamic inputs. The
current work is aimed at building on these findings and untangling the local interneuron circuitry contributing to
contrast adaptation in V1. To determine the roles of specific GABAergic interneurons, we partially suppressed V1
during the adaptation period by weakly activity either parvalbumin (Pvalb+) or somatostatin (Sst+) expressing
interneurons optogenetically. Our preliminary results suggest that neither Pvalb+ or Sst+ neurons are solely
responsible for the shift in neural contrast response functions after adaptation, but the gain control mediated by
Pvalb+ interneuron activation has a larger impact on adaptation than Sst+ activation.

authors_raw: Tagarelli KM, Lazar-Kurz Z, Newman AJ
title: The Effect of French Immersion on Native English Processing in the Brain
abstract:
French Immersion programs have been in place across Canada since the 1970s, and they are particularly popular in
the Maritimes, where up to 32% of English-language schools have such programs. Beyond developing second
language skills, students in French Immersion programs have been shown to outperform their English-only peers in
first language and other academic skills. However, we know virtually nothing about how this early and prolonged
exposure to a second language influences brain development and processing. The current study uses event-related
potentials to examine language processing in individuals who have gone through French Immersion, compared to
those who have not.

authors_raw: Cameron E, Snow S, Smith I, Chorney J
title: Exploring Parent-Reported Perioperative Distress Behaviours in Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract:
The perioperative experience can be distressing for children. Reports state that between 40% and 50% of typically
developing children experience perioperative distress. While perioperative distress has been well outlined in typically
developing children, similar observations have not been made in children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD; a
heterogeneous neurodevelopmental disorder).
This study aimed to report the frequency of, and define distress behaviours in children with ASD. Participants in this
study included 45 families of children with ASD undergoing day surgery in the IWK Health Centre. Parents
completed the Perioperative-Distress Questionnaire to assess their child's perioperative distress.
Ninety percent of children with ASD experienced distress during one-or-more time points throughout the surgical
experience. Commonly reported distress behaviours included: disorientation, aggression, and verbal requests to leave.
This is the first study to examine the frequency of, and describe perioperative distress in children with ASD. Children
with ASD seem to have greater perioperative distress levels compared to typically developing children.

title: Potential molecular conflict between detoxification and immune defense in Manduca sexta
abstract:
Like other animals, larval M. sexta lose their appetite after an immune challenge, yet the biological function of this
illness-induced anorexia is poorly understood. This research examines the hypothesis that illness-induced anorexia
allows the caterpillar M. sexta to shift molecular resources from food detoxification to immune function. Caterpillars
that received an immune challenge were more likely to die after ingesting permethrin than that those did not.
Conversely, a sublethal dose of permethrin reduced resistance to the Gram negative bacterium S. marcescens. These
results suggest that there is a negative interaction between detoxification and immune defense. We used qPCR to test
for a potential molecular conflict between detoxification and immune pathways. While expression of immune genes
appear unaltered by a combined immune-toxin challenge, expression of certain detoxification genes are lessened in
the presence of a combined challenge. These results indicate a potential molecular resource trade-off between
immunity and detoxification pathways.

authors_raw: Miller D, Hall L, Adamo S
title: Illness-induced anorexia may help promote immune function by reducing the use of glutathione, a limited resource, by detoxification pathways.
abstract:
In response to an immune challenge, most animals have reduced appetite, a behaviour called 'illness induced
anorexia'. Despite the ubiquity of this response, its potential biological function is not well understood. The
caterpillar M. sexta shows increased susceptibility to pesticides when the immune system becomes activated.
Similarly, sublethal amounts of pesticide increase susceptibility to disease. Recent qPCR data suggests a molecular
conflict between the pathways for food detoxification and immune defense, centered on the use and availability of
glutathione (GSH). GSH is an antioxidant tripeptide used in both detoxification and immune-response pathways. To
test the hypothesis that limitations in the availability of GSH leads to trade-offs between immune function and
detoxification, we compared whether supplemental GSH could reduce the increased morbidity and mortality of
caterpillars given both an immune and toxic challenge simultaneously. Preliminary data suggest that supplemental
GSH can rescue caterpillars exposed to both challenges, suggesting that GSH may drive a tradeoff between the two
pathways. Illness-induced anorexia would shift GSH resources toward immune function.

title: Susceptibility to monocular deprivation following immersion in darkness just prior to the critical period peak
abstract:
Immersion of animals in complete darkness reveals that the maturation and plastic capacity of visual circuitry depends
on binocular visual experience. Recent investigations have shown that imposing 10-days of darkness immersion
during juvenile life instates a neurobiological context wherein an animal's susceptibility to subsequent modification
of visual experience is augmented. For instance, our previous study showed that immersion of 12-week old cats in
complete darkness for 10-days promotes neurobiological changes that render the effects of subsequent monocular
deprivation (MD) much more severe than those observed in age-matched controls. This result suggested that darkness
immersion promotes an animal's capacity for plasticity, recapitulating a developmental state akin to a much younger
animal. The current study sought to explore whether short-term darkness immersion could similarly promote visual
plasticity at the peak of the critical period (p30), when an animal's sensitivity to visual perturbation is at its biological
maximum. Although 7-days of MD at this age produced potent alterations of neuronal soma size in the dorsal lateral
geniculate nucleus (dLGN), 10-days of preceding darkness immersion had no influence on the subsequent MD effect.
Similarly, MD imposed following darkness-induced changes in dLGN neurofilament immunoreactivity equivalent,
but not greater than those observed in animals subject to MD alone. These results suggest that the propensity of
darkness to promote susceptibility to MD depends on the animal's initial capacity for visual plasticity. Further, it
appears that the ceiling for critical period plasticity may be constrained by factors resistant to the effects of darkness.

authors_raw: Bartel, SJ, Sherry SB, Nealis LJ, Collins JL, Lee-Baggley DL, Stewart SH
title: Drinking frequency, drinking quantity, and alcohol-related problems as trait-states:A 4-wave, 18-month longitudinal study of female undergraduates
abstract:
Background: Drinking behavior and alcohol-related problems in female undergraduates are topics of growing interest
and importance. Research in this area widely assumes that alcohol frequency, alcohol quantity, and alcohol-related
problems are trait-like (i.e., strongly temporally stable). Under the assumption of trait-like stability, a single
assessment is enough to capture a person's usual pattern of drinking behavior and alcohol-related problems; however,
in making this assumption, research in this area fails to consider that there may be substantive state-like variability in
drinking behavior and alcohol-related problems. To address this shortcoming, we tested a trait-state model of drinking
behaviors and alcohol-related problems in female undergraduates using a 4-wave, 18-month longitudinal design. We
hypothesized alcohol frequency, alcohol quantity, and alcohol-related problems would show both trait-like stability
and state-like variability across our four waves of data collection, thereby supporting the trait-state model. Methods:
A sample of 207 female undergraduate drinkers completed self-report measures of alcohol frequency, alcohol
quantity, and alcohol-related problems four times (i.e. every 6-months for 18 months). Results: Generalizability
theory analyses indicated alcohol frequency, alcohol quantity, and alcohol-related problems all exhibited both trait-
like stability and state-like variability. While a greater proportion of the variance in alcohol quantity and alcohol-
related problems was attributable to trait-like stability, a greater proportion of the variance in alcohol frequency was
attributable to state-like fluctuations. Conclusions: Drinking patterns of female undergraduates tend to fluctuate over
time, particularly frequency of use. It should not be assumed that a single assessment of drinking behavior and
alcohol-related problems is a valid representation of a young woman's drinking patterns.
Keywords: trait, state, trait-state, alcohol, alcohol problems, generalizability theory

authors_raw: MacKay E, Deacon H, Conrad N
title: Orthographic knowledge as a predictor of gains in lexical access
abstract:
Word reading is a foundational skill in children's reading development. One construct related to and important for
word reading is lexical access. Lexical access is defined as the activation and/or retrieval of words in the mental
lexicon (Perfetti, 1992). Theoretically, orthographic knowledge, or the knowledge of specific and general letter
patterns, has been suggested as important for successful and fast lexical access. As such, we will longitudinally
examine if orthographic knowledge predicts gains in fast lexical access for 104 English-speaking children from
grades four to five. We will do so with an autoregressor cross-lag design. To form our composite of orthographic
knowledge, we administered measures of lexical and sublexical orthographic knowledge in grade four. To assess
lexical access, we administrated a measure of word naming measure to students in grade four and five. A measure of
phonological awareness and a measure estimating nonverbal ability were administered as controls. To examine if
orthographic knowledge predicts gain in fast lexical access, we will employ Structural Equation Modeling. Our model
will include orthographic knowledge at grade four as the independent variable and lexical access at grade five as the
dependent variable. Fourth grade lexical access, phonological awareness, and nonverbal ability will be entered as
controls. Given theoretical predictions, we expect to find that orthographic knowledge predicts gains in speed of
lexical access. The results of this study will inform key theories of reading development by providing novel support
for the role of orthographic knowledge in successful and timely lexical access.

title: The Connectome of a Ciona intestinalis larva
abstract:
The nervous systems that drive animal behaviour are founded on synaptic connections between cells therein. Small
nervous systems allow us to examine the entirety of a nervous system in great detail, revealing both ultrastructural
and network details. We have used anatomical connectomic approaches by means of serial-section EM (ssEM) to
analyze the number and distribution of synapses within the networks of the larval brain of Ciona intestinalis. Larval
responses to environmental cues rely on the network of underlying neuronal connections that translates these cues
into motion. The anatomical connectome reveals the components and connections of pathways for sensory
integration of visual, gravity, and peripheral information both to and from pathways of other sensory systems. These
sensory pathways feed into motor networks described through the current analysis that are involved in central pattern
generation and a putative escape response. Cell by cell analysis of morphology and all synaptic connections enables
the identification of new cell types and the classification of all neurons in this small model chordate nervous system.
The network complexity allowing integration at multiple levels provides a substrate for more complex larval
behaviour based on interactions between pathways that converge on the motor generating network of the Ciona
intestinalislarva.

authors_raw: Meinertzhagen IA, Lu Z, Ryan K
title: A second complete connectome: the larval CNS of the ascidian Ciona intestinalis
abstract:
The diminutive brains of invertebrates, especially those of larval stages with few cells, are Nature's bounty for
connectomic analyses. Pride of place for the first complete connectome report goes to C. elegans (White et al.,
1986, Phil. Trans. Roy. Soc.). Drosophila also supports powerful genetic methods and interesting behaviours,
especially for vision, olfaction and courtship, but its neurons branch profusely to yield many slender neurites that
require special methods to reconstruct at EM level. Pursuing an alternative possibility of an interesting simple brain
in a model species, we have used ssEM to compile a complete densely reconstructed connectome for the CNS in the
tadpole larva of Ciona intestinalis (Ryan et al., 2016, eLife), a chordate tunicate, a sister group of vertebrates. The
dorsal, tubular CNS of the ~1-mm tadpole larva is built on a similar plan to vertebrate brains, its ~330 cells
distributed rostrocaudally in three centres: a brain vesicle, motor ganglion and caudal nerve cord. Although early
neural development is well studied, detailed cellular organization of the swimming larva's CNS is hitherto
unreported. Neurons are unipolar with at most a single dendrite and few synapses. The 177 neurons formed 6618
CNS synapses including 1772 neuromuscular junctions, augmented by 1206 gap junctions. Some synapses are
bidirectional, others form reciprocal or serial motifs; 922 were polyadic. Axo-axonal synapses predominate. Most
neurons have ciliary organelles, and many features lack structural specialization. Glia and myelin especially are both
lacking. Despite equal cell numbers on both sides, neuron identities and pathways differ left/right, asymmetries
probably associated with the extreme reduction in cell numbers. Brain vesicle asymmetries include a right ocellus
and left coronet cells. Some neuron types show homology with vertebrate neurons, as revealed by their similar
circuits.
Posters

title: Psychology & Neuroscience: Where we've been and where we're going
Expressions of Distress among Indigenous and Non-Indigenous Adults Living in an Urban
abstract:
Despite high levels of reported psychological distress in Indigenous peoples in Canada,
research has not explored potential differences in expressions of psychological distress in this diverse
population. This study seeks to explore: 1) how Indigenous adults express their distress through
somatic versus psychological symptoms relative to non-Indigenous adults; 2) how symptom type
relates to single-item mental/physical health self-ratings in both groups; and 3) whether associations
vary by cultural orientation (acculturation and/or enculturation). Ongoing participant recruitment
involves Indigenous and non-Indigenous adults experiencing symptoms of psychological distress,
anxiety, and/or depression within the Halifax Regional Municipality. Measures include the
Vancouver Index of Acculturation, the Kessler Psychological Distress Scale, single-item self-rated
physical/mental health, and a demographics questionnaire (N=150). A series of regression analyses
will assess the research questions and the potential moderation by cultural group, acculturation, and/
or enculturation. Implications for more culturally responsive mental health care and health research
will be discussed.

authors_raw: Toni C. Spinella, Sean P. Barrett
title: The impact of cannabidiol (CBD) and stimulus expectancy on subjective and physiological
abstract:
Despite the increasing availability and upcoming legalization of recreational cannabis
use, research findings are mixed on its potential risks and benefits. Additionally, the mechanisms
underlying the potential therapeutic effects of cannabidiol (CBD) remain unclear. This study seeks to
examine the independent and combined influences of CBD-related expectation and pharmacology
on stress response. Adult participants will be recruited from the Halifax community. Prior to stress
induction participants will be randomly assigned to one of four conditions in a balanced placebo
design, where the content and instructions regarding the content of the oral pill will vary between
participants. Subjective ratings of stress will be collected, and heart rate and cortisol levels will be
assessed before and after the stress induction paradigm. Findings from this study will provide insight
into how CBD impacts subjective and physiological responses to stress, a critical step towards
informing future research, clinical practice, and public health policy.

authors_raw: Christopher EJ DeWolfe, Margo C Watt, Pablo Romero-Sanchiz, Sherry H Stewart
title: Gender Differences in Physical Activity are Partially Explained by Anxiety Sensitivity
abstract:
Objectives - Women engage in less physical activity than men. Given that women have
greater anxiety sensitivity (i.e., fear of arousal-based body sensations) and anxiety sensitivity is
inversely related to physical activity participation, this study sought to determine if anxiety sensitivity
mediates gender differences in physical activity. Method - A sample of 802 undergraduates
completed the Anxiety Sensitivity Index-3 (Taylor et al., 2007) and a Lifestyles Questionnaire
assessing physical activity levels. Results - A significant indirect effect of gender on physical activity
Women engaged in significantly less physical activity and had significantly greater anxiety sensitivity.
44th
In-‐House
Conference
Psychology
Neuroscience
Anxiety sensitivity was significantly and inversely related to physical activity. Conclusions - Results
suggest that anxiety sensitivity partially explains gender differences in physical activity. Anxiety
sensitivity reduction interventions might increase physical activity participation and reduce the
existing gender gap.

authors_raw: Elizabeth M. Myles, Tunde Apantaku-Olajide, Caroline Brunelle
title: Patterns and Functions of Non-Suicidal Self-Injury among Adolescents with Concurrent
abstract:
In adolescents who engage in non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI), the prevalence of comorbid
substance use can be as high as 60%. The co-occurrence of both behaviours in the same individual
increases the risk of harmful consequences. The purpose of the current study was to examine
adolescents exhibiting non-suicidal self-injury (with or without concurrent substance use) and
compare the principal motives underlying its use. A total of 53 participants (Mage = 15.23, SD =
1.59), who underwent psychiatric assessment at the Child and Youth Addiction and Mental Health
Services, between 2012 and 2015, completed a series of relevant questionnaires. Significant
differences in intrapersonal reinforcement functions between the unitary NSSI and the comorbid
groups, specifically with respect to positive reinforcement motives, were found. Based on these results,
the nature of self-injurious behaviour may be experienced differently in individuals who also engage
in substance use.

title: Effects of probiotic treatment on maternal care, with outcomes for adolescent stress resiliency and gut health
abstract: Abstract: Prenatal and early postnatal events are important for shaping our development, including the stress response. The mechanisms by which early life events shape both the brain and stress response involve maternal care behaviour provided to offspring. The connection between the brain and intestinal tract, the "gut-brain axis", has also demonstrated modulatory effects on stress responding. The administration of probiotics in pregnancy, or early life has demonstrated to provide protective effects for early life stress, resulting in positive outcomes for offspring. To date, there has been no investigation into the effects of probiotics on maternal care behaviour quality, and subsequent offspring outcomes, such as anxiety-like behaviours, corticosterone levels, and gut health. To investigate these effects, we propose the following research aims: a) investigate differences in maternal care behaviours due to probiotic administration, and gut health outcomes in dams/ offspring and b) investigate offspring anxiety-like behaviours/stress responding, and biomarkers of stress and inflammation.

title: Better Nights, Better Days: An accessible and sustainable transdiagnostic intervention?
abstract:
Introduction: Behavioural insomnia in children is a common and impairing problem.
Providing intervention in an accessible manner is critical. As such, we have been building and
evaluating a suite of eHealth Interventions, called Better Nights, Better Days (BNBD). Methods:
We are currently conducting a Canada-wide randomized control trial (RCT) for parents of typically
developing children (BNBD-TD), have conducted four foundational studies to inform the
development of BNBD for children with neurodevelopmental disorders (BNBD-NDD) and
conducted focus groups to inform the development of a version for adolescents and young adults
(BNBD-Youth). Results: While the RCT is still in progress, the results from the foundational studies
have provided strong support for BNBD. Conclusions: BNBD shows promise as an accessible and
effective eHealth intervention across ages and diagnoses. Sustainability of this program will be
discussed.
Psychology
Neuroscience
44th
In-‐House
Conference

title: The influence of role-playing games on perceived social competence.
abstract:
This study examined the relation between tabletop role-playing games and social
competence in young adults. A mixed methods approach was used to extend previous research in this
area. The Strengths and Difficulties Questionnaire was used to assess perceived social competence
from participants and semi-structured interviews were conducted to collect detailed qualitative data
about experiences with role-playing games. Six young adults (3 men, 3 women) between the ages of
19 and 24 (M = 21.2, SD = 2.6) participated. Responses on the SDQ indicated that people who play
role-playing games have typical strengths and difficulties with respect to social competence. The
qualitative description indicated the emergence of four themes: Content Focus, Social Focus,
Creativity Focus, and Identity Focus. These themes are consistent with findings from previous case
study research, in that role-playing games may have the potential to improve social competence.

authors_raw: Lydia Muyingo, Sherry Stewart, Simon Sherry
title: A Meta-analysis of Partner Effects on Alcohol Use
abstract:
The Partner Influence Hypothesis postulates that one partner's alcohol use (AU)
influences the other's AU over time. While studies have documented romantic partner influences on
drinking, results about the magnitude and sex-specific nature of such effects remains unclear. A
meta-analysis (MA) is proposed to clarify the existence of partner influences on drinking in romantic
couples, provide a more definite answer about the magnitude of such effects and to examine sex
differences in the extent of partner influence. A comprehensive search will be conducted across six
databases. Studies that have measured both members of a couple's AU across at least two time-points
will be included. Data will be analyzed using Actor-Partner Interdependence Modelling and pooled
effect sizes, and effect moderators will be examined. The results of this study will clarify the existence
and strength of partner influences on AU. Moreover, the proposed MA could demonstrate how
interpersonal relationships affect risky behavior.

authors_raw: Aaron Shephard, Carol Morriscey, Sean Barrett
title: Caffeine cue reactivity: The impact of caffeine-related stimuli and expectancies on caffeine
abstract:
Caffeine is consumed by approximately 90% of adult, yet its potentially addictive
properties have been understudied. Cue reactivity is an observable physiological and subjective
response that can be used to measure a drug's addictive potential. Although cue reactivity is
documented with many different substances, including cocaine and tobacco, no previous studies have
examined this effect with caffeine. This study aims to address gap. Participants will be adult daily
coffee drinkers. Following 12-hour caffeine abstinence, participants' caffeine craving will be assessed.
They will then receive either caffeine-containing or placebo gum and be given either accurate or
inaccurate information regarding the gum's caffeine content. Following an absorption period, they
will be exposed to neutral and coffee related stimuli, before having their level of craving measured
again. It is hypothesized that coffee cues will induce craving in participants and that this will occur
regardless of actual or perceived caffeine consumption.

authors_raw: Colin B. Pridy, Margo C. Watt, Pablo Romero-Sanchiz, Christopher J. Lively, and Sherry
title: Music's charms vary by anxiety sensitivity level
abstract:
Music's many uses include mood- and arousal-regulation. Anxiety sensitivity (AS; fear of
arousal-related somatic sensations) is a risk factor for anxiety-related disorders. This study examined
relations between AS dimensions (physical, cognitive, social concerns) and music-listening
motivations. Undergraduates (N = 817; 77.8% female; Mage = 19.06, SDage = 2.71) completed the
Anxiety Sensitivity Index-3 and three validated music-listening motive measures. An exploratory
44th
In-‐House
Conference
Psychology
Neuroscience
factor analysis (EFA) was conducted to determine factors underlying these various music-listening
motive measures. Six underlying music-listening motives: coping, revitalization, connection, social
enhancement, conformity, and sensory-motor. Regressions tested the relation of each AS dimension
to these music-listening motive dimensions. AS cognitive concerns uniquely predicted coping,
connection, and conformity music-listening motives while AS social concerns uniquely predicted
coping and revitalization music-listening motives. Results add music-listening to the growing list of
behaviours (e.g., alcohol use, smoking) linked to higher AS that are aimed at arousal- and negative
mood-regulation.

authors_raw: Richard S. Drake, Kim Parker, Kerry Clifton, James Adderson, Anita Mountain, Gail A
title: Does AFO support for walking reduce dual-task costs in individuals post-stroke?
abstract:
Cognitive-motor interference, as measured by dual-task walking (performing a mental
task while walking), is common with hemiplegia due to stroke. Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) are
custom-made splint devices that provide stability to the foot and ankle and help prevent foot drop, a
gait deficit common after stroke. AFO use has been shown to improve gait parameters such as speed
and step time, which are often negatively impacted by dual-task walking. We investigated the
protective effect of AFOs (i.e., whether AFOs could reduce cognitive-motor interference) in dual-task
walking in 21 individuals with post-stroke hemiplegia using a 2 (with/without AFO) by 2 (with/
without dual task) repeated-measures design. The results suggest that the use of an AFO improves
gait overall (e.g., improved step length), particularly while dual-tasking, although its effect on gait
variability (e.g., stride regularity) is less straightforward. Theoretical and clinical implications of dual-
task effects during walking will be discussed.

authors_raw: Meghan Rossi, Kathleen Merwin, & Natalie Rosen
title: Partner Perceptions of Women's Pain Self-Efficacy in Postpartum Dyspareunia: A Longitudinal
abstract:
Childbirth is a significant risk factor for the development of dyspareunia (pain during
intercourse). Women with postpartum dyspareunia that are higher in pain self-efficacy (PSE) may be
more motivated to seek out methods of reducing pain and exhibit more adaptive cognitive-affective
reactions to pain. Partner perceptions of women's greater PSE have been linked to women's lower
pain during intercourse. Prior research suggests that partners can be biased in their perceptions of
the patient's pain experience, which may have consequences for the couples' well-being. This study
aims to examine whether partner perceptions of women's PSE are biased at 3 months postpartum
and whether this bias predicts women's pain and functional impairments in both members of the
couple at 6 months postpartum. Data will be analyzed according to the Truth and Bias Model of
Judgment via Response Surface Analysis. Findings may improve our understanding of how couples
manage pain after childbirth.

authors_raw: Yvonne Brandelli, Christine Chambers, Adam Huber, Jennifer Stinson, Jennifer Parker
title: Parental Perceptions of Pain and Treatment Adherence in Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
abstract:
Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA), a disease characterized by pain and swollen joints,
affects over 24,000 children across Canada. While pharmacological and non-pharmacological
treatments aim to manage the pain and prevent irreversible joint damage, adherence is often a
challenge. This study will examine the parents' role in treatment adherence. It is hypothesized that
there will be less treatment adherence in families where parents demonstrate pain catastrophizing
and fears of pain, and that medication, side effects, and levels of pain will moderate these
relationships, and barriers to treatment will mediate these relationships. One-hundred-and-fifty
caregivers of children under the age of 18 with JIA will be recruited via social media to complete a
Psychology
Neuroscience
44th
In-‐House
Conference
20-minute battery of questionnaires. Data will be analyzed through multiple linear regressions and
moderated mediation analyses. This study will identify how parental attitudes around pain relate to
treatment adherence, which can have long-term effects on their child's health and well-being.

authors_raw: S. Hélène Deacon, Catherine Mimeau, Sheila Cira Chung, Xi Chen
title: Reading and learning in the wild: Young readers learning of the spellings and meanings of
abstract:
We explore whether younger readers can learn the spellings and meanings of words
through their independent reading. A total of 66 Grade 1 and 2 English-speaking children read
stories containing novel words referring to new inventions (e.g., a veap, used to clean fish tanks).
Following on this independent reading, children in both grades were above chance in choosing
correct spellings and meanings for the new words. We also found that individual differences in skill
in learning the spellings of novel words were related to word reading. In contrast, individual
differences in skill in learning the meanings of the novel words were related to specifically to reading
comprehension. These findings suggest that independent reading is a means through which children
can acquire rich information about the meanings and spellings of new words and that children's skill
in this learning might support reading skills in general.

title: Prosodic Cues in Text: The Missing Link Between Prosodic Sensitivity and Reading
abstract:
Reading comprehension, or the ability to understand the meaning of text, is one of the
most important skills children learn in school. Understanding how various metalinguistic skills
support reading comprehension is essential to improving literacy curriculum. One skill which is
related to reading comprehension in young readers is prosodic sensitivity, which is an awareness of
the rhythmic cues in speech. However, the reason for this relationship is unclear. One possibility is
that young readers are aware of how prosodic cues are represented in text, e.g. through punctuation.
The proposed research will test the possibility that awareness of how prosodic cues are represented in
text mediates the relationship between prosodic sensitivity and reading comprehension in young
readers. This will be done by testing 150 grade 3-5 students individually in schools and examining
their reading comprehension, prosodic sensitivity, and awareness of how prosodic cues are
represented in text.
44th
In-‐House
Conference
Psychology
Neuroscience

authors_raw: VC Patterson, A Pencer, B Pavlova, A Awadia, A Zwicker, E Howes Vallis, N Campbell, V
title: A psychometric validation of a developmental psychopathology measure: The Youth
abstract:
Severe mental illness (SMI) is defined as any major psychotic or affective disorder.
Antecedents, early manifestations of risk for SMI, may allow for targeted intervention to prevent the
onset of SMI. However, there are no brief self-report measures that screen for antecedents in youth.
The Youth Experience Tracker Instrument (YETI) measures six antecedents: affective lability,
anxiety, basic symptoms, psychotic-like experiences, insomnia, and depressive symptoms. This
study aims to establish the psychometric properties, factor structure, and optimal cut-off points of the
YETI. The sample was 208 offspring of parents with and without SMI aged 8 to 17-years
participating in the FORBOW study. A confirmatory factor analysis procedure was used to establish
the questionnaire's structure. Receiver operating curves were used to select a cut-off point for the
scale. This study's findings will validate a new instrument that may facilitate detection of youth at
high risk for the development of SMI.

authors_raw: Jasmine R. Aziz, Raymond M. Klein, Gail A. Eskes
title: Role of Working Memory in Visual Search among Older Adults
abstract:
Visual search is a goal-directed process whereby we scan for and locate important objects
(targets) in our environment, from choosing products on shelves at the grocery store to scanning for
pedestrians while driving. Working memory involves temporarily holding and/or manipulating
information in mind. During visual search, working memory helps us to remember target features
and previously searched locations. Visual search and working memory abilities both decline with age,
which can interfere with daily activities (e.g., shopping, driving). This study will examine the role of
working memory in visual search in older adults. We will manipulate working memory demands by
varying whether a picture of the target (target template) is displayed during the search. We will relate
this template effect to search performance, working memory abilities, and how frequently
participants look at the template when displayed. Findings may assist in developing strategies
promoting working memory and visual search abilities in aging.

authors_raw: T Anderson, K McKearney, P Tibbo, D Fisher
title: Effects of Caffeine on Sensory Gating in Schizophrenia
abstract:
A predominant symptom of schizophrenia (SZ) is a deficit in sensory gating, or an
inability to selectively filter out extraneous stimuli. Sensory gating also has deep connections to
neurological functioning and is a large component of alerting effects of caffeine consumption. On
average, individuals with SZ consume nearly double the norm of caffeine compared to typical
populations (predicted self-medication). The EEG-derived event-related potential P50 is a robust
index of sensory gating and provides the high temporal resolution needed to investigate this early
automatic process. 15 SZ patients and 15 healthy controls were given caffeine and placebo
treatments in two EEG recording sessions. Participants were recorded passively listening auditory
click stimuli (typical populations suppress attentional response). P50 amelioration from caffeine was
only seen in patients and not healthy controls. Indicating that a dose of 250 mg of caffeine was
sufficient to ameliorate P50 indexed sensory gating in patients but not controls.

authors_raw: Mohammad Habibnezhad, Michael A Lawrence and Raymond M Klein
title: Employing Rescorla's truly random condition to measure truly exogenous covert attention
abstract:
Attention prioritizes processing of some information over the other. Attention can be
engaged either exogenously (by an external event e.g. a loud noise) or endogenously (intention to
focus on a task e.g. reading). Because endogenous control is thought to be engaged when stimulus
Psychology
Neuroscience
44th
In-‐House
Conference
properties (such as spatial location) can be predicted, measuring purely exogenous orienting requires
removing such predictability. Following this guide, previous studies of exogenous spatial orienting
have used peripheral cues that were spatially uninformative about the upcoming target's
location. However, these cues were almost always temporally informative about the upcoming
target's time of presentation and this may have elicited some degree of endogenous control. We
employed Rescorla's truly random procedure to eliminate the temporal information communicated
by cue and target contingency. Forty participants were recruited to complete a variation of Posner's
cueing paradigm, in which cues and targets were presented temporally randomly in one of two
equiprobable peripheral locations. The results demonstrated a biphasic pattern of early facilitation
and late inhibition (IOR). To evaluate the effect of removing temporal contingencies, we intend to
implement the next experiment using fixed SOAs, mixed SOAs and non-aging paradigm.

authors_raw: Jack P. Solomon, Sarah N. Kraeutner, Timothy Bardouille, Shaun G. Boe
title: Motor Execution and Imagery are Unique Processes
abstract:
Motor Imagery (MI), the mental rehearsal of movement, is a method of motor learning
that has been thought to parallel motor execution (ME). The neural mechanism(s) underlying the
primary difference between MI and ME, the lack of movement in MI, is not well understood. Here
we generated evidence from neuroimaging data to test two theories that exist to explain inhibition of
movement in MI. We hypothesized MI and ME would diverge at the early stages of performance.
Eighteen participants performed a grasping task using both MI and ME while we recorded their
brain activity to ascertain common measures of brain activity that relate to movement planning and
execution. Our results show that in the frequency domain, MI and ME differ early in the preparation
phase for movement but the field strength only differed post-movement. This refutes our hypothesis
but, still challenges the assertion that MI and ME are parallel processes.

authors_raw: M Al-Hamdani, S Smith, SH Stewart
title: The effects of self-affirmation messaging and illness sensitivity on drinking intentions
abstract:
Little is known about the direct/indirect effects of self-affirmation alcoholic beverage
labels, or illness sensitivity, on intentions to reduce drinking. Two studies addressed these gaps.
Experiment 1 examined the effect of self-affirmation labels on alcohol appeal and intentions to
reduce drinking. Labels were designed to increase attention to harms followed by an attempt to
maintain self-integrity. Experiment 2 compared two models of the effects of self-affirmation labels
and illness sensitivity on drinking-reduction intentions: direct vs. indirect via negative affect. In
Experiment 1, self-affirmation reduced alcohol appeal and increased intentions to reduce drinking.
In Experiment 2, negative affect mediated effects of illness sensitivity/self-affirmation on drinking-
reduction intentions and the direct and indirect models were equally plausible. Results support the
use of self-affirmation messaging in alcohol labelling practice to deter problematic drinking. Results
also emphasize the importance of negative affect as a mechanism through which self-affirmation/
injury sensitivity increase intentions to reduce drinking.

authors_raw: Kayla Joyce, Amanda Hudson, Roisin O'Connor, Kara Thompson, Megan Hodgin, Tara
title: Changes in coping and social motives for drinking and alcohol consumption across the
abstract:
Alcohol use increases during the (pre-)menstrual (characterized by negative affect) and
ovulatory (characterized by positive affect) phases of the menstrual cycle (MC), suggesting women
may drink for particular emotion-focused reasons during specific phases. Ninety-four normally-
cycling women completed daily dairy surveys, examining state drinking motives and alcohol
consumed across an entire MC. Women reported increases in drinking to cope premenstrually,
peaking menstrually, and declining mid-cycle. Women reported a slight increasing trend across the
44th
In-‐House
Conference
Psychology
Neuroscience
cycle in social motives, while enhancement motives remained stable across the cycle. Drinking to
cope predicted greater alcohol quantities menstrually. While social motives predicted greater alcohol
quantities during the follicular and ovulatory phases, enhancement motives were unrelated to
drinking quantity. Results indicate that clinicians should be attentive to MC phase when treating
reproductive-aged women with alcohol use disorders (AUD). Findings may help develop new, and
improve existing, interventions for women with/at risk of, comorbid mood and AUD.

title: Making Science Accessible: A-RAD-ical Approach
abstract:
Dissemination of scientific research findings from the community of experts to the
broader public is challenging, especially since the source literature makes for intimidating reading.
Therefore people turn to headlines or websites for information, which often provide oversimplified or
erroneous explanations. The Accessible Research Article Database (ARAD) is a collaboration
between faculty from Dalhousie University and Science Atlantic, with the aim of building a
collection of easy-to-understand, primary research articles suitable for undergraduate teaching.
Contributions come from course instructors based upon two criteria: 1) article is relatively short and
2) contributor deems it to be accessible to an undergraduate science student. Then novice science
students evaluate the articles for difficulty, identify jargon, and record reading time. To date, there
are over 90 evaluated articles for Psychology and Neuroscience, organized by topic based on
introductory textbook chapters, and housed in a shareable Google spreadsheet. The project is
expanding into other scientific disciplines (Biology and Physics) and the creation of a searchable
database.

title: The Art and Science of Behaviour Change
abstract:
Dr. Dayna Lee-Baggley, Assistant Professor in Faculty of Medicine, is a new cross-
appointee in the Department of Psychology and Neuroscience. She will provide an overview of her
clinical work as a health psychologist and her research on behaviour change. She has an active
research program on chronic disease, behavior change, obesity, professional resiliency and
Acceptance and Commitment Therapy. Clinical and research opportunities for students will be
reviewed.

title: Pornography Consumption and Attitudes Supportive of Violence against
abstract:
College-aged males consume pornography often (>4-5 a week) and for a considerable
duration (>30 minutes at a time). We correlated consumption and type of pornography with
attitudes towards women. We tested 28 male undergraduates (18-26 years old, mean age 22) using
the Dalhousie Pornographic Typology and Consumption Scale (DPTCS). We collected consumption
habits (i.e., frequency, duration, frequency of masturbation, internet search preferences, and
consumption preferences) and viewing preferences based on 33 search terms (e.g., Softcore,
Submission, Amateur Lesbians) commonly found on pornography streaming sites (e.g.,
Pornhub.com). Some search terms target DREVA pornography (Degrading, Rough, Extreme,
Violent, and Abusive). Participants used the 33 search terms in an extremeness card sort and ratings
weighted consumption scores. Weighted consumption was correlated with attitude scores on the
Illinois Rape Myth Acceptance Scale and the Attitudes Supportive of Violence Scale. Results showed
a positive correlation between the card sort and attitudes (r = 0.35). However, consumption habits
negatively correlated with attitudes (r = -0.42), and DREVA-only consumption correlated negatively
with attitudes (r = -0.33). Results suggest college-aged males recognize extremeness but consumption
Psychology
Neuroscience
44th
In-‐House
Conference
is not detrimental to their attitudes toward women. This is a pilot study; results should be interpreted
with caution.

title: Exploring the Effects of Afterlife Beliefs on Generosity and Prudence
abstract:
Priming thoughts of supernatural monitoring and punishment has been shown to
increase prosocial behaviour among certain religious adherents. This study investigated whether
priming thoughts of the afterlife might have similar effect. 80 Christians and 80 atheists were
recruited from the undergraduate population. Participants completed a survey, including a writing
prime, in which half described their expected situation after graduation, and half described their
expected situation after death. Preliminary results in pre- and post-prime generosity and prudence
measures suggest that contemplating the afterlife may facilitate generosity toward strangers among
Christians, while inhibiting money-saving behaviour among atheists. Implications and follow-up
directions are discussed.

authors_raw: Emre Fertan, Aimee A. Wong, Richard E. Brown
title: What do we actually measure when "measuring behaviour"?
abstract:
Behavioural tests are commonly used in neuroscience to study the function of brain
regions, validate animal models of neurological diseases, and test therapeutics. One common
problem with these behavioural tests is that researchers often choose the easiest test to measure a
specific behaviour and fail to take any external factors into account when interpreting the results. In
this talk, I will provide three examples from our laboratory which demonstrates that test results may
not always show what we think they do.
1. In the triple test of anxiety, 5xFAD mice are not less anxious, as found in other studies, but they
are avoiding the closed arms of the EPM.
2. In the Hebb-Williams maze female 3xTg-AD mice have low food motivation, which results in
poor cognitive performance.
3. By treating glaucoma in DBA mice, we showed that spatial learning in the MWM is confounded
by vision deficits.

title: Everything your undergrad students are (supposed to be) learning about statistics: Part 1
Everything your undergrad students are (supposed to be) learning about statistics: Part 2
abstract:
Statistical Methods II (PSYO 3502) is a mandatory course for our psychology and
neuroscience honours students. In 2012 the course was heavily revised with incremental
improvements ongoing. It focuses on understanding the edge cases (p = .051), thinking meta-
analytically, figuring out how to analyze data on your own, and reading results sections critically,
44th
In-‐House
Conference
Psychology
Neuroscience
including checking to see if authors both back up what they say, and making logically coherent
arguments. It builds on the simulation techniques that were introduced in 2501 but has the students
generate the simulation themselves. It does not build a large library of statistical techniques. It
expands from 2501 with repeated measures designs, factorial designs, and logistic regression. A small
amount of the time is spent at the end of the course demonstrating multi-level designs and model
comparison.

authors_raw: Tovah Kashetsky, Nafisah Ali, Will Brien, and Leslie Phillmore
title: Do songbirds care about human singing?
abstract:
In songbirds, the immediate-early gene ZENK is expressed in neural regions critical for
auditory processing after playback of acoustic stimuli. Expression levels change with breeding
condition or sex of the bird and complexity of stimuli, but most studies find that own-species, or
conspecific, vocalizations produce the most ZENK immunoreactivity (Zenk-ir) compared to
vocalizations of other species (heterospecific), white noise, pure tones, or silence. In this study
we asked whether stimuli considered heterospecific to a songbird, the zebra finch, could induce
higher-than-expected Zenk-ir if the stimuli were made meaningful to the bird. To do this,
we quantified Zenk-ir (and another immediate-early gene c-fos) in response to playback of human
vocalizations the birds had learned to associate with reward or punishment in an operant
conditioning paradigm. Compared to birds that had never heard these stimuli before, operant
consequences appear to increase Zenk, but not c-fos response to heterospecific stimuli, meaning these
regions are important for processing meaningful, not necessarily conspecific, stimuli.

authors_raw: Ralph S Redden, Austin J Hurst & Raymond M Klein
title: Using temporal order judgments to dissociate two inhibitions of return
abstract:
Early work on inhibition of return (IOR) using a temporal order judgment (TOJ) task
showed no effect on arrival time judgments, suggesting this inhibitory effect is acting at a post-
perceptual information processing stage. Recent work, however, suggests that there are two forms of
IOR; one affecting the quality of inputs and the other affecting responding, whereby the type of
effect that is manifest is contingent upon the activation state of the reflexive oculomotor system. We
tested this theory in a TOJ task, where subjects were required to either make a prosaccade (output
form) or antisaccade (input form) after the onset a spatially uninformative peripheral cue, and
subsequently execute a TOJ response. We found inhibited perceptual processing when the input form
was generated, but no effect on TOJs in the group that elicited the output form. These findings
provide converging evidence that there are two forms of IOR: an input effect operating on a saliency
map, and an output effect operating on a priority map.

title: The Effect of Memory Instruction on Spatial Associations
abstract:
In a directed forgetting paradigm, participants are presented with a series of words and
instructed to remember or forget each word. When instructed to forget, attention is withdrawn from
the word as well as the location the word was presented in. Certain words have associations to
specific locations in space, such as "sky" having an "up" association. The present study examined the
effect memory instruction has on spatial associations. Participants were presented with an item-
method directed forgetting paradigm. Words had an association to upper or lower space, or no
spatial association. Following an instruction to remember or forget each word, participants localized
a target that appeared above, below, or at center and response times were collected. A subsequent
memory test revealed a directed forgetting effect: participants recognized more words that were
instructed to-be-remembered than words instructed to-be-forgotten. Contrary to the literature,
response time analyses revealed no indication that memory instruction has an effect on congruency.
Psychology
Neuroscience
44th
In-‐House
Conference

authors_raw: Caitriona Douglas, Antoine Tremblay, Aaron Newman
title: Measuring the N400 effect in a naturalistic setting
abstract:
Recently, neuroscientists researching social cognition have begun using a two-way EEG
technique called hyperscanning to record the brain activity from two participants simultaneously to
better understand naturalistic social interactions. Researchers have demonstrated that it is possible to
detect ERPs using hyperscanning in a social joint-task scenario (Loehr et al., 2015). Given that much
of psycholinguistic research involves studying ERPs, we developed a hyperscanning method to detect
ERPs in a naturalistic conversational setting. We recruited pairs of participants to have a scripted
conversation using a script that contained specific amounts of low and high frequency nouns as
stimuli because a frequency-modulated N400 effect had been previously reported in the literature
(Rugg 1990; Hendrix et al. 2017; Van Petten et al. 2014) We successfully detected the N400 ERP in
a conversational setting using hyperscanning, but did not find a significant difference in amplitude
between the low and high frequency conditions.

authors_raw: Raymond M Klein, Colin McCormick, Swasti Arora & Sana Rehan
title: Testing the networks of attention: From the ANT to the Attention-Trip
abstract:
Even though W. James asserted that "everyone knows what attention is" the term,
attention, is not a single, simple concept. Rather, attention refers to several basic neuro-cognitive
processes. Perhaps the most influential taxonomy of attention, according to which there are three
isolable networks of attention (alerting, orienting and executive control), has been advanced by
Posner and colleagues. The Attention Network Test (ANT) was developed to serve as a quick and
easy to administer assay of these networks. The ANT has been widely used to measure the efficacy of
these three attention networks. World-wide and local interest in this test will be described and some
limitations will be discussed. Our development of a more engaging version, that we call the
Attention-Trip, will be presented along with some recent data we have collected using this game-like
task.

authors_raw: Alena Galilee, Lisa Beck, Catherine Mimeau, S. Hélène Deacon, Aaron J Newman
title: Ortho-semantic learning of novel words in Grade 3 students: An ERP study
abstract:
It has been proposed that reading development field should move from examining
established knowledge of orthography and semantics to assessing children's capacity to learn the meaning
of both spelling and meaning of new words, referred as ortho-semantic learning (Deacon, 2012).
Our main objective was to examine how event-related potentials (ERP) change with ortho-semantic
learning. Thirteen native English speaking students in Grade 3 learned novel word spellings and
meanings by reading short stories. Immediately after learning, participants completed a lexical
decision task in which they were presented with real words, novel words from the stories, non-words,
consonant strings, and false fonts. Two different ERP components were investigated: N170 (word
recognition) and N400 (lexical semantics). Our preliminary results suggest that after brief exposure,
the ortho-semantically novel words were processed similarly to real words. Additional analyses will
examine relationships between individual differences in word reading, ortho-semantic learning, and
the ERPs.

title: A provisional equivalent circuit for the fly's eye space.
abstract:
Why elements of the fly's visual electroretinogram (ERG) occur with the sizes and
polarities that they do remains unexplained, because the equivalent circuit of the eye's extracellular
space is not understood. Exploring this involves locating where extracellular barrier(s) are formed,
and demonstrating how both photocurrents and microelectrode currents escape from the eye.
Measured resistance depth profiles and dye diffusion point to an extracellular resistance barrier
(Rbarrier) in and around the lamina synaptic zone. The conventional suggested current path runs from
44th
In-‐House
Conference
Psychology
Neuroscience
the retina through the optic lobe, but results here point to a new route, currents escaping via the
cornea. This pathway has a 3-5 fold lower resistance (Rcornea) than the deep route through the optic
lobe (Rbarrier + Rdeep). Responses are measured relative to a reference electrode in the haemolymph,
but this ground reference is necessarily interposed between Rdeep and Rcornea. The resulting
equivalent circuit dictates that circulating photocurrents produce positive voltages around the lamina
synapse, which then invert negative out into the retina. This correctly predicts the observed polarity
of ERG responses from the photoreceptors and some other sources.

authors_raw: K Kosel, P Torres Munoz, JR Yang & TB Franklin
title: Characterization of social deficits in the 5xFAD mouse model of Alzheimer's disease
abstract:
Alzheimer's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that is characterized by a
number of cognitive and behavioural effects; while memory loss is the most well known, patients also
present with a number of neuropsychiatric symptoms, including depressive symptoms, anxiety,
irritability, agitation, and aggression. This study examines whether a transgenic mouse model of
Alzheimer's disease (5xFAD) that exhibits learning and memory deficits can also be used to model
social changes in humans with the disease. We tested 5xFAD mice in behavioural tests for olfactory
perception, responses to odour cues, sociability, social novelty preference, and free social interaction.
Results indicate that transgenic female mice may exhibit altered processing and/or responses to
social cues compared to wild-type controls, and that transgenic males may exhibit increased
aggression compared to wild-type controls. This suggests that the 5xFAD strain may provide a useful
model of social deficits present in Alzheimer's disease patients.

authors_raw: Jillian L King, Cheryl Gill, Jared T Shapiro, Nathan A Crowder
title: Modulating GABAergic interneuron activity in mouse primary visual cortex alters response to
abstract:
The visual system has long been used to study cortical information processing. The
genetic tools available in mice have allowed researchers to examine the roles of specific types of
neurons in the primary visual cortex (V1) with a level of control that was previously inconceivable.
Studies that have optogenetically modulated the activity of parvalbumin (Pvb+) and somatostatin
(Sst+) expressing GABAergic interneurons indicate that these two populations may counteract the
activity of pyramidal neurons (Pyr) differently, although there have been contradictory findings.
Vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP+) expressing GABAergic interneurons have been under-examined
because it is believed that they have a negligible role. In the current study, we combined single-unit
electrophysiology with optogenetic modulation of either Pvb+, Sst+ or VIP+ interneurons to
provide additional data to help resolve the contradictory findings regarding Pvb+ and Sst+, and to
determine whether VIP+ interneurons have a role in Pyr responsivity.

authors_raw: Richard E Brown, Alice E Kane, Aimee A. Wong, Susan E Howlett
title: Frailty as a measure of healthspan and lifespan in transgenic mice
abstract:
Mouse models of AD have differences in life expectancy. Frailty is a measure of aging
which is independent of chronological age and can be used to compare age-related changes in mice
with different lifespans. This study used the mouse frailty index (FI) which is based on deficit
accumulation to explore genotype and sex differences in lifespan and healthspan of 3xTg-AD mice.
Male 3xTg-AD mice aged 300-600 days had a higher FI score than either male wild-type or female
3xTg-AD mice, and the elevated frailty scores were accompanied by parallel increases in mortality.
Frailty increased exponentially with age and higher rates of deficit accumulation elevated mortality
risk in all groups. When mice were stratified by FI score, frailty predicted mortality, at least in
females. The mouse clinical FI provides a valuable tool that can be used to evaluate healthspan in
mice of different strains and in transgenic mice.
Psychology
Neuroscience
44th
In-‐House
Conference

title: Hubel & Wiesel - Their Contributions to Visual Research
abstract:
David H. Hubel and Torsten Wiesel were pioneers in studying the function and plasticity
of the visual system. In 1958, at Johns Hopkins University, they began a collaboration that would
continue for over 20 years. Their seminal work, including the 1959 paper "Receptive fields of single
neurones in the cat's striate cortex" was some of the first research demonstrating that simple stimuli
could have profound effects on the brain and behaviour. These findings ultimately led to the 1981
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine "for their discoveries concerning information processing in the visual
system". Their influences have extended to the Dalhousie community, influencing the research
conducted by Donald Mitchell, Kevin Duffy and Nathan Crowder concerning the visual system. In
1998, David H. Hubel was awarded an honorary degree from Dalhousie University, and visited the
medical school in 2004 as a Picchione Scholar to speak about his research on the influences of the
environment on early-life brain development. Overall, the research of Hubel and Wiesel was crucial
in developing the field of visual neuroscience and their lifetime contributions continue to impact and
guide research within the Dalhousie community.

title: Freud's Rat Man And The Meaning Of The Rat Torture
abstract:
Freud's 1909 case of obsessional neurosis of a patient called the Rat Man is unique in
being the only case for which he saved his process notes. I provide a novel interpretation to the 'rat
torture', which is the signature obsession in this case. This torture involves rats entering the anus of a
prone victim from a pot. Freud provided an elaborate account of why the patient feared that this
punishment would be given to his lady love and dead father. I propose, instead, that the patient's
father engaged in anal sexual abuse with his children and provide evidence from the process notes
that supports this hypothesis. I point out how this explanation is more congruent with Freud's
original "seduction" theory than to his later "fantasy" theory of the basis for neuroses, and relate
these two theories to recent debates over the possibility of recovered memories of childhood sexual
abuse.
Posters

authors_raw: Raquel Nogueira-Arjona, Trevor CJ Shannon, Simon B Sherry Ivy-Lee Kehayes, Sherry
title: Drinking to keep pace: Testing the moderating influences of sensation seeking and extroversion
abstract:
Peterson et al. (2005) showed that modelling contributes to college students' alcohol use,
with highly extroverted males being most susceptible to matching strangers' drinking levels in a lab-
based context. We examined if: a) there was a significant relationship between target and buddy
drinking levels in college students' dyadic drinking situations; b) both target extraversion and
sensation-seeking positively moderate this target-buddy drinking levels relationship. Data were
collected from 149 targets (74% F) and their same-sex drinking buddy. The target completed
personality measures, and both completed a past 30-day Timeline Follow Back examining drinking
levels and context. Linear mixed-effects modeling confirmed the first social matching hypothesis, and
revealed that extraversion, but not sensation-seeking, positively moderated the relationship between
buddy and target daily dyadic drinking levels. Findings extend previous research to a real-world
context, modeling of a buddy's drinking levels, and to women. Results may guide modifications to
personality-based interventions for college drinking.
44th
In-‐House
Conference
Psychology

authors_raw: Ioan Mahu, Sean Barrett, Patricia Conrod, Mike Lawrence, Annie Chinneck, Julie
title: Specificity of Four Personality Correlates of Concurrent Substance Use Among Opiate
abstract:
The leading treatment for opioid addiction in Canada is methadone maintenance
therapy (MMT). While efficacious for treating opiate addiction, MMT fails to address patients'
concurrent polysubstance use. This study was designed to validate the Substance Use Risk Profile
Scale (SURPS; Woicik et al., 2009) in a MMT sample, and to demonstrate theoretically-relevant
relationships of personality to recent substance use. One-hundred-thirty-eight MMT patients from
four clinics in Quebec and Nova Scotia participated. Bayesian confirmatory factor analysis
established the structural validity of the four-factor personality risk model in this population. SURPS
subscales were then used as predictors for specific forms of recent substance use. Hopelessness
predicted past 30-day opiate and tranquilizer use, anxiety sensitivity predicted past 30-day
tranquilizer use, and sensation seeking predicted past 30-day stimulant, alcohol, and marijuana use.
Impulsivity predicted past 30-day injection drug use (particularly stimulant injection). Thus,
personality influences substance use patterns, even at latter stages of addiction.

authors_raw: Marco Lee, Ann Johnsen Co-supervisors: Shelley Adamo, Atsushi Miyashita
title: Do I Want Babies Now? Reproductive Investment of Gryllus texensis under the Interaction of
abstract:
To maintain maximum fitness in a variable environment, individuals must adapt to
environmental cues by altering investments into current reproduction, somatic maintenance and
future reproduction. The terminal investment hypothesis predicts that as prospects of future
reproduction decrease, investment into reproduction should increase. However, the threshold for
terminal investment may shift with age and disease risk. Interactions between immune challenge (IC)
and age were examined by monitoring lifetime reproductive investments of female crickets that were
exposed to an IC at different ages. We predicted that younger females would over-invest in immunity
relative to older females because younger females will lose proportionately more of their total
reproductive output if they die of disease than will older females who have already laid many of
their eggs. We also predicted that the increased investment in immunity would lead to younger
females having lower current reproduction compared with older females. Regardless of treatment,
older females laid more eggs over a 7-day period than did young females. Contrary to our
prediction, phenoloxidase, an immune enzyme, showed greater activity in older females.

authors_raw: Clara J Lownie, Alena Galilee, Aaron J Newman
title: An ERP study of reading ability and polyunsaturated fatty acid consumption in childhood
abstract:
Diet is one of many environmental factors influencing a child's ability to acquire
proficient reading skills. Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA) are a group of nutrients that are critical
to the neurodevelopment of several cognitive abilities, such as reading. This study used
electroencephalography to record electrical activity in the brain following visual word stimuli within
a reading task. Specifically, this research focuses on one event-related potential component, the
N170, associated with two reading related phenomena; print tuning and lexical tuning. The
amplitude of the N170, behavioural measures of reading, and estimated consumption of PUFA in
children grades 2 to 6 in the Halifax Regional Municipality were analyzed. While the findings of this
exploratory study do not provide conclusive evidence to support the hypothesis that estimated PUFA
consumption is correlated to print or lexical tuning, these results suggest that print and lexical tuning
effects may actually be negatively correlated to PUFA consumption.

authors_raw: Tristan Park, Sherry H Stewart, Mohammed Al-Hamdani, Kara Thompson, Christine
title: Investigating the Path from Childhood Maltreatment to Alcohol Problems in a Sample Child
abstract:
PTSD symptoms and drinking motives have both been identified as mediators in the
relationship between trauma and alcohol misuse. We sought to extend recent research suggesting that
both PTSD symptoms and, in turn, drinking motives mediate the relationship between childhood
maltreatment and alcohol misuse. Five-hundred and sixty-four adolescents (54% female; mean [SD]
age = 15.9 [1.1] years) in the care of child welfare services completed validated measures of
childhood maltreatment, PTSD symptoms, drinking motives, and alcohol misuse as part of the
Maltreatment Adolescent Pathways longitudinal study. Results supported a chained mediation model
where childhood maltreatment was associated with elevated PTSD symptoms, which in turn
predicted higher coping motives, which was associated with higher alcohol misuse. Single mediator
models for PTSD symptoms and coping motives were not significant. The results suggest that both
PTSD symptoms and coping motives are necessary to explain the relationship between childhood
maltreatment and alcohol misuse.

title: Self-compassion as a resilience factor for alcohol-involved sexual assault and negative emotional
abstract:
Experiencing alcohol-involved sexual assault (AISA) may result in self-blame, while self-
compassion (SC; extending kindness towards oneself) may promote resilience from the negative
emotional consequences of AISA by attenuating and/or counteracting the association between AISA
and negative emotional outcomes. Undergraduates (N = 785) completed measures tapping past-term
AISA (Kehayes et al., in press), SC (i.e., Self-Compassion Scale; SCS; Neff, 2003), and anxiety and
depression (K10; Kessler et al., 2002). The SCS was scored as two higher-order domains (SC, self-
criticism) and six lower-order facets (self-kindness, mindfulness, common humanity, over-
identification, self-judgement, isolation). Rather than attenuation, the higher-order SCS domains
showed main effects: high SC and low self-criticism counteracted the adverse effects of AISA on
anxiety and depression. Similarly, the lower-order SCS facets of high self-kindness and low over-
identification counteracted the adverse effects of AISA on anxiety/depression - with the additional
SCS facets of low self-judgement and isolation counteracting the effect of AISA on depression.

authors_raw: Emily Puddicombe, Robin Curtis, Chris Moore
title: What Influences Children's Sensitivity to Violations of Authority as a Moral Foundation?
abstract:
According to Moral Foundations Theory (Haidt, 2012), human beings are
innately sensitive to five core moral domains, which can be influenced by upbringing and culture.
One of these foundations is authority. However, limited research has investigated children's sensitivity
to authority as a moral foundation. Studies have found that children's age, potential punishments,
and authoritative figure's status influence children's response towards authority. The current study
used the Moral Foundation Questionnaire for Kids to measure 31 children's sensitivity to authority
when presented with six vignettes depicting violations of authority towards teachers and parents.
Badness ratings and punishment choices were taken for each vignette. A mixed two by two ANOVA
revealed no main effect of age on children's badness ratings and no main effect of authoritative
figure type on children's overall badness ratings. However, an independent samples t-test found older
children were significantly more punishing than younger children. Limitations and implications are
discussed.
44th

Conference

authors_raw: N Hanzal, KM Joyce, SH Stewart
title: Changes in Anxious Mood, Stress, and Cannabis Use Across the Menstrual Cycle: A Daily
abstract:
Addictions research has predominantly focused on substance abuse in male-dominated
research samples, indicating that findings may not generalize to women. Menstrual cycle (MC)
phase appears to influence changes in addictive/risky behaviour. The current study examined
phase-specific changes in anxious mood, stress, and cannabis use quantity across the MC.
Consistent with the self-medication hypothesis (SMH), it was predicted that the quantity of
cannabis used would increase during the (pre-)menstrual phases relative to other MC phases. It
was also predicted that this increase in quantity of cannabis use would be associated with an
increase in stress levels and anxious affect. For the current study, data were obtained from 14
female cannabis users using a 32-day daily diary method, with questions pertaining to anxious
mood, stress, and quantity of cannabis used. Findings suggest that the quantity of cannabis used
is phase-specific, while stress nor anxious affect are phase-specific variables.

authors_raw: Lindsey Puddicombe, Bronwyn O'Connor, Samantha Howard, Piper Sawchyn
title: Alertness and Human Performance
abstract:
Alerting is an attentional mechanism that increases arousal and helps us prepare to make
responses. Human performance can be measured using reaction time and accuracy. A prior study by
Posner et. al(1973) looked at the relationship between reaction time and accuracy when alerting at
different time intervals. They found when reaction time was fastest; error rate was highest, which is a
speed- accuracy trade-off (SAT). Our follow-up experiment required participants to indicate whether
targets appeared on the left or right side, using either compatible or incompatible responses. Our
manipulation was our method for alerting: participants would hear either an exogenous or
endogenous warning signal. The goal of our study was to see if these different signal types had
differing effects on human performance. The interval duration and the compatibility cues had a
significant effect on both reaction time and accuracy. The differences between endogenous and
exogenous signals are further considered.

title: Investigating the Effect of Visual Noise on Cognitive Load and Memory using Item-Method
abstract:
The present study investigated the effect of visual noise induced cognitive load on item-
method directed forgetting. Study words were presented to participants superimposed on one of
three levels of visual noise. Study words were followed by a memory instruction to remember or
forget the previously seen word. Participants' completed a recognition test for both remember and
forget words. The directed forgetting effect, defined as better recognition of remember words than of
forget words, was seen in all levels of visual noise. Recognition accuracy was significantly lower in the
high noise condition than in both the medium and low condition. These findings suggest that high
levels of visual noise can facilitate item-method directed forgetting.

authors_raw: Zoë Lazar-Kurz, Gail Eskes, Aaron Newman, Stephan MacLean, Katie MacKearney
title: Prism Adaptation in Older Adults: an ERP Analysis
abstract:
In this study, we measured prism adaptation (PA) in older adults as a marker for
neuroplasticity. Currently, little is known about the neural correlates of PA, and even less in older
adults. Event related potentials (ERPs) were recorded in response to a target reaching task while
wearing prism or clear lenses. Past research in young adults suggested two ERP components facilitate
PA: the P300 and the feedback error related negativity (fERN; MacLean et al., 2015). In our study of
Psychology
Neuroscience
44th
In-‐House
Conference
older adults, while behavioural tests converged with young adults, only the fERN was elicited, not the
P300. As seen in young adults, the fERN amplitude was related to spatial accuracy of the pointing
in relation to the location of the target. These preliminary results may suggest the neural
mechanisms associated with PA differ in older and younger adults. However, these results need to be
verified by directly comparing younger participants with the same procedure.

title: In vivo application of the photoswitch BENAQ in a rat model of acquired retinal degeneration
abstract: Abstract: Millions of people worldwide suffer from degenerative retinal diseases, such as retinitis pigmentosa (RP) and age-related macular degeneration (AMD). There are currently no therapies available to reverse vision loss, leaving individuals with the prospect of irreversible blindness. AMD and RP specifically target the photoreceptor cells of the retina and induce progressive deterioration. As the primary light sensitive cells of the retina, photoreceptor loss results in decreased visual acuity and severe visual impairment. To reduce the consequences of retinal degeneration, a series of azobenzene photoswitch drugs have been developed as a potential therapeutic strategy. Photoswitches are able to confer light sensitivity onto non-light responsive cells, such as retinal ganglion cells, causing them to fire in response to light stimulus.
