However, the effects of photoswitches have only been demonstrated in congenital models of photoreceptor degeneration, despite the acquired nature of some retinal diseases, including AMD. Additionally, it is not well understood how the visual cortex responds when a photoswitch is applied to the retina. Therefore, this project sought to test cortical response to photoswitch application in a model of acquired retinal degeneration.

title: Investigating the ability of superoxide to pass dendritic spine plasma membranes in
abstract:
Reactive oxygen species such as superoxide have been shown to carry out individual
processes involved in long-term potentiation (LTP). Superoxide in neurons is produced extracellularly
and until recently, it was assumed that it could not pass through the membrane. Recent findings
suggest that superoxide can pass membranes in epithelial cells via chloride channels. I hypothesized
that a similar mechanism occurs in hippocampal neurons, allowing superoxide to perform distinct
functions in intracellular signalling involved in LTP. The current study tested the hypothesis that
superoxide can pass through the plasma membrane in spines of rat hippocampal neurons by
detecting superoxide with fluorescent dye and imaging the cells before and after superoxide
production. The trend of the results supports the original hypothesis, as a significant increase in
florescence was observed in some putative spines 10 seconds after superoxide production, and this
increase was eliminated when chloride channels were blocked.

authors_raw: Sydney Harris, Annette Kolar, Dylan Quinn, Stefan Krueger
title: The unresolved role of the cell adhesion molecule family, neurexins, and activity on the
abstract:
Synapse elimination and formation is a necessary part of post-natal development. It
optimizes the wiring of neuronal circuits, ensuring proper processing of sensory input and
behavioural output. The process of synaptic refinement is regulated by neural activity, which affects
the structural and functional integrity of synapses through possibly global homeostatic mechanisms.
Besides activity, cell adhesion molecules critically influence synapse refinement. Neurexins, a family
of cell adhesion molecules, previously implicated in modulation of synaptic transmission, have been
shown to stabilize synapses. It is presently unclear whether neurexins affect synapse refinement
structurally or by modulating synaptic transmission. To address this, we inhibited AMPA-type and
NMDA-type glutamate receptors in hippocampal neurons in the presence and absence of neurexin
44th
In-‐House
Conference
Psychology
Neuroscience
knockdowns. Although chronic blockade of glutamate receptors led to a decrease in eliminated
synapses, the density was unaltered. Moreover, only one of two neurexin knockdowns caused
significant decrease in synapse density and significant increase in synapse elimination in a glutamate
receptor blockade, preventing us from providing a conclusive result.

authors_raw: Michaela K. Purdon, Emre Fertan, Richard E. Brown
title: Behavioural phenotypes of the MDGA2+/- mouse model of autism spectrum disorder (ASD)
abstract:
A MAM domain-containing glycosylphosphatidylinositol anchor 2 (MDGA2) gene has
been linked to ASD in humans [Bucan M. et al., 2009, PLOS. 5:6]. Connor S. A. et al. [2016, Neuron
91:5, 1052-1068] showed that haploinsufficency in the MDGA2+/- mouse model produced
behavioural phenotypes paralleling those seen in humans with ASD. The MDGA2+/- mice used by
Connor et al. (2016) were bred using a C57BL/6N background which has an rd8 mutation that
causes retinal spotting. We used 2-month old transgenic (MDGA2+/-) mice and wildtype
(MDGA2+/+) littermate controls on a C57BL/6J background to determine whether visual
impairment was a confounding variable in the Connor et al. (2016) study. Results show differences on
tests of sociability, motor ability, and cognition, indicating visual impairments and/or background
strain may influence the behavioural phenotype of this model.

authors_raw: Kristen Lee, Donna Goguen and Ian Weaver
title: Effects of Atrx haploinsufficiency and mother-pup interactions on cognitive, social and
abstract:
A child's early-life conditions both before birth and in infancy has profound effects on
long-term physical and mental health outcomes of an individual. The relationship between the
quality of early environment and long-term developmental programming appears to be mediated, in
part, by the degree of positive attachment in parent-infant bonding and parental investment during
early life. We have previously shown that offspring of low maternal care mothers have reduced
forebrain Atrx levels . Children born with mutations in the Atrx gene have intellectual disability and
autistic-like behavior. Using wildtype and Atrx heterozygous C57/B6 mice, we examined the role of
ATRX and prenatal stress in mitigating the effects of variations in maternal behavior during early
brain development and social emotional and cognitive behaviors into adulthood. Our results suggest
that prenatally stressed offspring have social and behavioral deficits associated with reduced Atrx
levels.

authors_raw: Raymond M. Klein, Richard E. Brown
title: Using the Hebb Legacy to Integrate Psychological Science
abstract:
The birth of our relatively young science has been described (Boring, 1950) as the
product of a marriage between physiology and philosophy. Whereas we might be described as an
incredibly successful offspring we are presently experiencing something of a disintegrating identity
crisis. As proposed by Posner & Rothbart (2004, 2007) the framework advocated by D. O. Hebb
(1949), in the "Organization of Behavior: A Neuropsychological Theory", and other works, can
provide the foundation for the integration of our discipline and the framework for averting this crisis.
Hebb (1949) proposed that synaptic change is the basis of neural activity (which we now call the
"Hebb synapse") and developed the idea of a "cell assembly" or neural network to account for the
ability of the brain to retain information in the absence of sensory stimulation. Hebb sought to
explain psychological concepts, from learning and memory to brain development, motivation,
emotion and brain disorders in neuroscientific terms. As noted by Posner & Rothbart, technological
advances in neuroimaging, genetics and computational modeling, offer a firm and realistic
foundation for research and training aimed at understanding how environment and heredity interact
to shape the neural networks that underlie the organization and disorganization of behaviour,
cognition and affect.

authors_raw: Raymond M. Klein, Brett Feltmate, Austin Hurst, Maelle Kopf & Jean-François Gagnon
title: On the potential for wearable technology in the modeling of vigilance and fatigue for aeronautic applications
abstract:
Fatigue-related decreases in vigilance (hypovigilance) and their negative effects on performance are widely recognized
as posing a serious risk to aviation safety. Scientists at Thales Research & Technology (TRT) Canada (Quebec) collaborated with the
Klein laboratory to design and conduct a research project in which participants performed tasks over an 8 hour period during which a
variety of physiological, oculomotor and performance parameters, and self-reports of fatigue, were collected either continuously or
regularly. Thales scientists then developed a mathematical model that from the physiological data could predict fatigue-related
hypovigilance; a model that was successful when generalized to "new" data and that was recently tested in a more ecologically valid
scenario (an intercontinental flight). In a followup project the Dalhousie-Thales team plans to test the efficacy of mitigation measures
of hypovigilance based on this model's on-line analysis of wearable data collecting during performance on a driving-like task.

authors_raw: Megan Vincent (Supervised by Dr. Omisade and Dr. Fisk)
title: Prognostic biomarkers of seizure recurrence after first seizure and in New Onset Epilepsy
abstract:
This study seeks to establish prognostic markers of developing epilepsy after a first seizure (FS) or seizure recurrence
despite treatment in patients with new onset epilepsy (NOE). Predicting seizure recurrence may facilitate early treatment and reduce
disease burden. Our primary objective is to determine if cognitive impairment, mood, and socioeconomic status (SES) can predict
seizure recurrence after FS or NOE. We will select 50 patients with NOE or FS at the Halifax First Seizure Clinic. Baseline testing will
be conduced and includes measures of major cognitive areas and mood symptoms. Follow-up testing using the same measures will be
conducted after 1 year. Chart review will determine 1) seizure recurrence since initial visit 2) baseline results and 3) clinical and
demographic measures (diagnostic test results, seizure frequency, SES). The patients will be divided into two groups based on seizure
recurrence since baseline: no seizures vs 1+ seizures. Regression analyses will determine if baseline measures are predictive of seizure
recurrence.

authors_raw: Brett B. T. Feltmate, Ralph S. Redden, Raymond M. Klein
title: Estimating the Probability and Fidelity of Encoding during the Attentional Blink
abstract:
When participants are required to report the presence or identity of two targets presented in close temporal sequence,
they are often able to recall that of the first (T1), but not the second (T2). Referred to at the 'attentional blink', this phenomenon is
thought to reflect the tying up of resources needed for target processing by T1 leaving them unavailable for T2 processing. Herein we
argue that in conventional 'blink' tasks success is measured in dichotomous fashion, thereby conflating two potential sources of recall
error: the probability of, and the fidelity to which, T2 is encoded into visual short-term memory. We attempted to generate
independent measures of probability and fidelity by making colour our relevant target domain. Target colouring was randomly
sampled from within a colour wheel, and responses were made by clicking within the same wheel allowing us to record the degree of
error in a given response. Obtained estimates of our parameters are assessed in exploratory fashion and their implications are discussed
herein. All pre-registered materials can be found on the OSF at https://bit.ly/2BSHIKH.

title: On Building a Person: Recent Advances and Limitations in the Construction of Robots that Attempt to Mimic Capacities of Human Persons
abstract:
Based on criteria of personhood that robotic agents should pass for them to match us in social intelligence, I describe
some recent successes as well as difficulties for the future with respect to these criteria. I give illustrations of research in several areas
where advances have been made: 1) Evolutionary robotics; 2) Developmental robotics; 3) Knowledge sharing; 4) Robo-science.

authors_raw: Matt Orr, Sarah Brine, Nicole Ali, and Penny Corkum
title: A Systematic Review Examining In-Class Interventions for Students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder in the Mainstream Classroom
abstract:
It is difficult for teachers to meet the needs of students with ADHD and in-class interventions could help to provide
effective support. However, access to information about evidence-based in-class interventions is limited. A systematic review was
conducted to determine what evidence-based in-class interventions for ADHD are present in the literature. Fourteen studies
examining in-class interventions for ADHD were identified and reviewed. These studies featured a variety of interventions such as
tactile cueing, self-management strategies, and peer monitoring. Studies were of moderate-to-high methodological quality. The
available evidence does not meet the criteria of evidence-based interventions, suggesting that the summarized studies cannot be
confidently recommended as in-class interventions for students with ADHD.

authors_raw: Amanda E.F. Hagen, Sherry H. Stewart
title: Dose-Response Relationship of Alcohol on Self-Relevant Negative Thought Content in Major Depressive Disorder
abstract:
Major depressive disorder (MDD) and alcohol use disorder co-occur and are associated with poorer outcomes. Of
possible underlying mechanisms, the self-medication hypothesis posits that individuals with low mood may drink to improve their
mood. There is evidence that low doses improve mood, but that mood decreases at higher doses. Individuals with MDD may find
alcohol reinforcing due to the alleviation of cognitive symptoms, e.g. negative self-thinking. One study showed that alcohol dampens
negative self-relevant thinking at moderate doses in individuals with mild depression symptomology. Extending this, the current study
will studying those with MDD and examine different doses of alcohol. We hypothesize the relationship between mood and dose will be
extended to cognitive factors. Individuals with depression will be randomly assigned to either no alcohol, low alcohol, or high alcohol
conditions, before completing the word task. This study will help understand mechanisms of this comorbidity to inform specific
interventions.

title: Feasibility testing of the ABCs of SLEEPING tool as an intervention
abstract:
Behaviourally-based sleep problems are common among school-aged children, are highly treatable, but due to a
number of barriers, very few children receive evidence-based behavioural interventions. The ABCs of SLEEPING tool was developed
to facilitate the assessment of sleep problems by identifying healthy sleep practice strengths and areas in need of improvement, and to
provide parents with evidence-based recommendations. Using Bowen and colleagues (2010) "Does It Work?" feasibility framework,
the tool will be tested for its acceptability, implementation, and limited-efficacy, using a cohort, pre-test/post-test design.

title: Effective solutions for managing procedural pain related to vaccinations
abstract:
Children often experience pain during vaccinations, however many parents are unaware of evidence-based strategies
available for managing vaccination pain. A knowledge translation (KT) resource was previously developed to summarize evidence-
based strategies for managing children's vaccination pain. This study aimed to examine parents' perceptions and plans to use this KT
resource using the Information Assessment Method (IAM) to assess the relevance, cognitions, utility, and benefits of the resource.
Attitudes toward vaccines were also measured. 312 parents completed this survey and 97% demonstrated little to no vaccine hesitancy.
Vaccine attitudes were negatively correlated with relevance of the KT resource (rs = -.17, p = .003) and intention to use the
information (rs = -.14, p = .012), indicating that vaccine accepting parents were more likely to be receptive and planned to use the
strategies. This study highlights key factors which impact parents' plans to use KT resources for vaccination pain management.

title: Examination of NMDA receptor subunit composition in the developing cat visual cortex.
abstract:
Many brain regions exhibit a period during early development called a 'critical period', when the capacity for neural
plasticity is high, and neural circuitry is rapidly modified in response to neural activity. In the primary visual cortex of mammals, this
capacity for rapid modification has allowed for the refinement of functional characteristics of neural circuitry that optimize visual
performance. Following waning and closure of the critical period, neural circuitry within the visual system exhibits minimal capacity
for modification. Changes in plasticity that occur throughout development are thought to occur as a result of a change in the subunit
composition of the NMDA receptor. We will employ a novel approach to quantifying the ratio of NMDA receptor subunits across
development that involves multiplex fluorescence imaging.

authors_raw: Jared T. Shapiro, Jillian L. King, Nathan A. Crowder
title: Optogenetic Modulation of the Excitatory-Inhibitory Balance of Mouse V1 Responses to Drifting Sinusoidal Gratings
abstract:
The primary visual cortex (V1) has served as a model brain region for studying cortical information processing because
it is the first cortical stage of the visual pathway, and a region where several novel computations arise. Recent work has investigated the
neuronal circuits underlying receptive field properties of mouse V1 neurons due to the many genetic tools available in this species that
enable cell-specific targeting and perturbation. One such genetic tool is optogenetics, which has been previously used to demonstrate
how distinct classes of inhibitory GABAergic interneurons can affect the excitatory drive of V1 pyramidal cells. Importantly, this past
work has focused on optogenetic modulation of the onset and sustained responses of V1 pyramidal cells, but has given no attention to
the pattern of activity evoked after photostimulation is terminated. We used optogenetics and in vivo electrophysiology to investigate the
role of parvalbumin-expressing interneurons (PV+) in V1 circuitry, and specifically how these interneurons affect pyramidal cell after-
responses. We have demonstrated that termination of photostimulation produces a "rebound effect" on pyramidal cell activity, which
facilitates their after-responses to flashed bars and sinusoidal gratings. We investigate whether this "rebound effect" is also produced
from drifting sinusoidal gratings.

authors_raw: Steve Shaw ABSTRACT: The network of amacrine neurons in the first synaptic zone (lamina) of the fly's compound eye makes reciprocal feedback synapses into each visual column, upon the six photoreceptor terminals (R1-R6) that drive the amacrines in the first place, Am D R1-6. Previous results indicate that following light activation, the amacrine net activates 1-2 inhibitory impulses, which rapidly but transiently suppress R1-6 terminals. This causes R1-6 input to the main lamina output cells L1-3 to become highly phasic. Fast active presynaptic suppression of R1-6 can be detected starting at moderate light intensity in the rapid downstroke of the Early Positive Response (EPR), the field potential from L1-3. The delay between light input at one side of the eye and complete response suppression at the other side is ~2 milliseconds, comprising the impulse conduction delay followed by the monosynaptic delay: these two values are not known separately. By recording feedback oscillations caused by the Am D R1-6 reciprocal interaction at opposite ends of the eye, the conduction velocity along the amacrine network is estimated at ~1.3 meters per second. Maximum horizontal conduction delay at equatorial level is then ~0.6 milliseconds. Average synaptic delay becomes 1.4 milliseconds, but may be different for R1-6 and Am. ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- TITLE: Role of working memory in visual search among older adults AUTHORS: Jasmine Aziz, Raymond Klein, Gail Eskes
title: Conduction velocity and synaptic delay at photoreceptor D amacrine reciprocal synapses in the fly's eye.
abstract:
Visual search and working memory abilities decline with age, which can interfere with daily activities. During visual
search, working memory helps us remember target features and previously searched locations. Thus, we investigated whether working
memory abilities would predict visual search efficiency in healthy older adults. Thirty participants (55-78 yrs) completed working
memory measures and a visual search task. We varied working memory load by manipulating whether a reference picture of the target
(i.e., target template) was displayed during the search. We also recorded eye movements to determine how frequently participants
looked at the target template. Visuospatial working memory abilities predicted better conjunction search performance, and verbal
working memory abilities interacted with the template presence/absence, although independently of template fixation time. Findings
clarify how visual search abilities relate to working memory in older adults and could be helpful for developing ways to support these
abilities in aging and relevant clinical populations.

authors_raw: Emre Fertan, Richard E. Brown, Ian C.G. Weaver
title: Understanding the role of amyloid beta in Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis: teaching an old dog new tricks
abstract:
Alzheimer's disease (AD) was first described by Dr. Alzheimer in the beginning of the 20th century, based on the
observation of "military fossa" made of a "special substance". In the 1980's this substance was identified as amyloid beta, which gave
birth to the amyloid cascade hypothesis. However, for many years, the exact role of amyloid beta in the deficits seen in AD remained
unknown and many believed that it functioned as a physical barrier in the synapse, hindering the chemical transmission. To explain
the other, seemingly unrelated, symptoms, many other theories have been proposed regarding immune dysfunction, oxidative stress,
tau phosphorylation. Recently, studies began to appreciate the role of amyloid beta in these "unrelated" disease mechanisms. My talk
will focus on our recent work, in which we studied the connection between the increase in amyloid beta, oxidative stress, immune
dysfunction and tau phosphorylation in terms of the kynurenine and TRX reduction pathways.

authors_raw: Wheeler RV, Weaver ICG, Franklin TB.
title: Effects of LPS-Induced Maternal Immune Activation on Expression of Autism-Linked Genes and Autism-Like Behaviours in Neonates and Adulthood
abstract:
Epidemiological studies have revealed a link between maternal immune activation (MIA) and autism-spectrum disorder
(ASD) risk. To date, the literature has primarily focused on the effects of LPS-MIA in adult male mice; there are few reports describing
the behavioural effects of LPS-MIA in female mice or early in development. Further, the involvement of the mammalian target of
rapamycin (mTOR) signaling pathway, a risk factor for ASD, has not been studied in this model. In the current study, we injected
pregnant C57BL/6J mice with 75µg/kg of LPS on embryonic day 11.5 and 12. Male and female offspring were tested during
development and at adulthood for the three core symptoms of ASD: social communication, social interaction, and repetitive
behaviours. Additionally, the expression, protein abundance and activity-induced phosphorylation of mTOR and its downstream
effect on post-synaptic density 95 (PSD95) was analysed in hippocampal and cortical areas of offspring at postnatal day zero (P0) and

authors_raw: Gloria Rodrigues, Kristen Lee, Hillary Maillet, Donna Goguen and Ian Weaver
title: Effects of forebrain ATRX deficiency in C57/B6 male mice on offspring ATRX expression and forebrain development: maternal nurture meets offspring nature
abstract:
Early childhood experience and degree of parental-infant attachment influence the developing brain, notably in brain
areas that support stress regulation, cognition and social behaviours. Siblings of children with autism have an increased relative risk of
autism themselves, suggesting a role of gene x environment interactions in the etiology of autism. Although the exact mechanism is not
completely understood, the autism risk gene, Atrx, is regulated by maternal behaviour. ATRX is a chromatin remodeling factor that is
essential for brain development and plasticity; even small changes in expression and signaling can cause synaptic deficits and
contribute to neurodevelopmental disorders. My talk will focus on our recent work, in which we studied the connection between
maternal care and the development of brain regions relevant to autistic-like behaviours in the adult offspring of litters containing either
wildtype female and male mice or wildtype female and Atrx hemizygous male mice. Our findings demonstrate a persistent effect on
forebrain growth, Atrx gene expression and promoter methylation, not only in the ATRX deficient male mice, but also in their
heterozygous female litter mates. These results suggest a role of early life environment and ATRX expression in pathological
mechanisms underlying long-term chromatin plasticity and neural growth relevant to neurodevelopmental disorders such as autism.

authors_raw: Kosel, F., Hamilton, J., Godin, V., Harrison, S., & Franklin, T.B.
title: Phenotypic contagion of aggression in male 5xFAD mice
abstract:
Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that is characterized by a number of cognitive and
behavioural effects, including increased agitation and aggression, as well as depressive symptoms, anxiety, irritability, and other
neuropsychiatric symptoms. We previously found that females of the 5xFAD transgenic mouse model of Alzheimer's disease exhibit
decreased social investigation that mimics the social withdrawal observed in AD patients. This study examines whether transgenic
5xFAD males also exhibit decreased social investigation, and whether transgenic 5xFAD males model the increased aggression
observed in many male AD patients. We tested male 5xFAD mice in behavioural tests for sociability, social novelty preference, and
free social interaction, as well as examining home-cage interactions. Results suggest that transgenic males exhibit decreased social
interest and altered aggressive behaviours compared to wild-type controls, suggesting that they may provide a useful model of
aggression and social withdrawal in AD patients.

authors_raw: Colin McCormick, Ralph Redden, Raymond Klein
title: Reducing Cognitive Demand to Explore the Speed Accuracy Trade-off in Endogenous and Exogenous Temporal Cueing
abstract:
Recently (McCormick, Redden, Lawrence, and Klein, 2018), we observed the independence of exogenous and
endogenous temporal attention when implementing a novel signalling method (Lawrence & Klein, 2013) within the canonical
temporal cueing paradigm (Kingstone, 1992). However, in an attempt to alleviate task demand, we were limited to the analysis of
detection responses instead of the more informative discrimination responses. This is because we were unable to obtain TCEs in the
endogenous condition when using discrimination responses, which we suspect was due to the large cognitive load generated from a
combination of stimulus and task demands. For the current proposed experiment, we have modified methodological components to
alleviate cognitive demand to allow for the implementation of discrimination targets in the Kingstone paradigm, providing opportunity
to analyze speed-accuracy trade-off differences for endogenous and exogenous temporal attention. All project materials can be found
on the Open Science Framework (https://OSF.io).

title: What Information Escapes An Instruction to Forget?
abstract:
The directed forgetting effect is a phenomena characterized by participants recalling or recognizing more remember
instructed than forget instructed items. However, not all forget instructed items are removed from memory and while it can be
concluded that an instruction to forget decreases the probability of a memory representation later being retrieved, standard recall or
recognition tests reveal nothing about the fidelity of those representations. In looking at what aspects of words (sound, appearance,
meaning) escape initial attempts to prevent encoding when the intention is to forget, we expect that the retrieved representations of
forget instructed words will be impoverished on the aspects investigated. The results of a recognition test following a typical item-
method study phase will be analyzed to compare the mean false alarms of critical foils against those of unrelated foils, with a greater
false alarm rate to critical foils suggesting that the single shared aspect between the study and test word is part of what was encoded.

authors_raw: Sinan Aslam, Ralph S. Redden, Raymond M. Klein
title: On the relationship between spatial compatibility effects and Inhibition of Return
abstract:
Inhibition of return (IOR) is an increase in reaction time when orienting to targets that have been previously attended
to. The Simon effect is a tendency to respond faster to targets presented in a location spatially compatible with the response. We
explored the consequences of the Simon effect on targets suffering from IOR when the frequency of targets is altered. This was
accomplished by having participants distributed amongst three conditions where the Simon target was presented in either 20%, 50%
or 80% of trials. The remaining trials required a reporting the direction of a centrally-presented arrow. We expect to see a linear
change the Simon effect as a consequence of target frequency, where the Simon effect may increase with more directional responses
(80% arrows), or the Simon effect may increase with more peripheral events (80% Simon targets). Furthermore, the degree to which
IOR impacts this change will be explored.

title: Auditory Language Processing in the Visual Cortex of Blind Individuals
abstract:
In people who are blind, the visual cortex takes on a new linguistic function and becomes involved in processing speech,
something that is not seen in sighted people. However, the exact role of the visual cortex in auditory language processing in people
who are blind is unclear. Establishing how the visual cortex is integrated into the existing language network may shed light on its
specific role. We collected fMRI data from two groups of participants, congenitally blind adults (n = 6) and sighted controls (n=10).
Participants made matching decisions about auditorily presented pairs of words (meaning, rhyme, speaker) and performed a n-back
task. We found visual cortex activity in the blind group but not the sighted group during linguistic and verbal memory tasks. Our next
step is to determine how the visual cortex is connected to other regions in the language network (via dynamic causal modelling) and
how those connections might change depending on what type of language processing is occurring.

authors_raw: Rosenberg, Lindsay, Levesque, Kyle, & Deacon, Helene
title: How Does Morphological Awareness Contribute to Reading Comprehension in Beginning Readers: A Mediation Analysis
abstract:
Reading comprehension, the ability to comprehend the message that written words convey (Nation, Cocksey, Taylor, &
Bishop, 2010), is the primary goal of reading. Morphological awareness is the awareness of, and ability to manipulate, meaningful units
in spoken language (teach + -er = teacher) (Carlisle, 1995). Morphological analysis is using awareness to infer the meaning of a word (e.g.
treelet = small tree, because -let means small). Both morphological awareness and analysis are associated with reading comprehension.
Building on previous research with grade 3 students, this study will examine how morphological awareness contributes to reading
comprehension at the grade 1 level. The study will assess whether morphological awareness and analysis are separate dimensions for
beginning readers and whether morphological analysis and word reading ability are mediating factors in the relationship between
morphological awareness and reading comprehension. This study will assess 200 grade 1 students on morphological analysis,
morphological awareness, word reading, and reading comprehension. It is predicted that morphological analysis will have a larger
mediating role compared to word reading.

title: The Relation Between Hot and Cool Inhibitory Control and Temperament in 3- to 7-Year-Olds
abstract:
Temperament refers to children's individual differences in emotional, motor, and attentional reactivity and their ability to
self-regulate those reactions (Rothbart, 2007). In this study, we examine the relation between Rothbart's three temperament
dimensions and their individual relations with hot and cool inhibitory control processes in children aged 3-7 years. Hot and cool inhibitory
control processes are cognitive processes that allow children to withhold dominant responses to stimuli when required to solve novel
and difficult problems in affective and neutral contexts, respectively. Three- to 7-year-olds were given hot and cool versions of three
inhibitory control tasks: the Flanker task, the Stroop task, and the Go-No-Go task and their parents were given the short form of
Rothbart's Children Behaviour Questionnaire (Putnam & Rothbart, 2006). We predicted a negative correlation between scores on the
two affective dimensions of temperament (negative affect and surgency), and performance on both versions of the inhibitory control
tasks, with stronger negative correlations for the hot versions. However, we predicted a positive correlation between performance on
both versions of the inhibitory control tasks, and effortful control, the self-regulatory dimension of temperament. Only some of the
predicted correlations were significant with the sample we collected to date.

title: The Software Wars: SPSS vs. R (and beyond)
abstract:
There is increasing, vociferous debate on which statistical software to use in the social sciences. On one side are the
SPSS users, who are accused of using antiquated methods, having non-transparent workflows, and producing extremely ugly graphs.
On the other side are the R users, who can in theory create better final products, but in practice take an enormous amount of time
unless they are experienced coders (who frequently use their remaining time to tell SPSS users why they are bad at statistics). I will
discuss my experiences with both software types in research and teaching, with an eye to finding solutions that are (a) reproducible, (b)
make use of modern statistical practices; and are (c) easy to learn and time-efficient. I will highlight a new free point-and-click statistical
software "Jamovi" that provides a hybrid of point-and-click efficiency with the flexibility of R coding that may prove useful for
teaching.

authors_raw: Lyam M. Bailey, Laura E. McMillan, & Aaron J. Newman
title: Sinister Participants: Is modern neuroimaging a right-handed tool?
abstract:
Approximately 10% of humans are left-handed or ambidextrous (adextral). It has been suggested that this demographic
is largely avoided by researchers within the neuroimaging community. To date, however, no formal effort has been made to quantify
the extent to which adextrals are excluded from neuroimaging-based research. Here we aim to address this question in a review of
over 1,000 recent articles published in high-impact, peer-reviewed, neuroimaging-focused journals. Specifically, we sought to ascertain
whether, and the extent to which adextrals are underrepresented in neuroimaging study samples. Handedness data was available for
over 30,000 research subjects; only around 3% of these individuals were adextral - considerably less than the 10% benchmark one
would expect if neuroimaging samples were truly representative of the general population. This observation was generally consistent
across different areas of research, but was modulated by the demographic characteristics of participants. The epistemological and
ethical implications of these findings are discussed.

authors_raw: Swasti Arora, Jennifer Stamp, Leanne Stevens, Marcus Cormier & Gisela Jones
title: Does Question Phrasing Matter? A mixed-methods analysis of Student Ratings of Instruction.
abstract:
The efficacy of Student Ratings of Instructions (SRIs) is often questioned by teaching professionals. Much research has
been done on this topic, and meta-analyses have supported the use of SRIs as indicators for teaching effectiveness and tools for
course development (Cohen et al. 1981). However, the colloquial discussion shows a divided opinion among instructors and faculty
(Theall, M. (2001). The current study explores the importance of SRI question structure as a predictor of course feedback depth and
content. Facilitated by the use of qualitative analysis software, 2,780 student responses collectively were coded from 2015 and 2016
SRIs from an Introductory Psychology course. A codebook of 23 codes, associated with 7 overarching themes and 7 contexts to which
codes could correspond was established. Rates of code occurrence and co-occurrence were then calculated between open-ended
questions and questions which were structured to address specific aspects of course material and development.

title: The Psychophysiology of Mind Wandering During Online Lectures
abstract:
Mind wandering is a construct which describes presence of task unrelated thoughts, and is often identified as an
experience that is detrimental to the efficacy of education. However, there has been relatively little research into the impact on mind
wandering on education because it is difficult to measure. In this presentation, we will describe the results of two experiments that
establish the foundation a covert auditory attention EEG measure which is correlated with reported mind wandering states. In the
experiments, participants watched an online lecture video while task-irrelevant audio tones were played in the background. We found
significant differences in Event Related Potentials evoked from auditory stimuli during reported mind wandering. The results present a
foundation for a larger project in exploring the potential of real-time EEG detection for use in the improvement of education
technologies or other information technologies, as well as new types of brain-computer interfaces.

title: How do children become good word readers: The role of learning about how print works.
abstract:
A core question in models of word reading lies in which skills predict individual differences in reading acquisition:
specifically, why is it that some children struggle to learn to read, while others acquire reading skill with apparent ease? Most models
point to orthographic processing, or knowledge of letter- and word-patterns, as key to children's progress in word reading
development. Unfortunately for these models, empirical evidence has not born out this prediction, with multiple studies now showing
that orthographic processing is an outcome, rather than a determiner of word reading, at least when measured with traditional tasks.
In this talk, I present a new theoretical conceptualisation of orthographic processing, one that includes children's first steps into
learning about how printed words work. I also show new evidence that children's appreciation of these early aspects of orthographic
features predicts their progress in learning to read.

authors_raw: Ilne L. Barnard, Lauren J. Goodyear, Rhiannon N. Holt, Aliana Kanji, Katelyn MacNeil, Devan R. Pancura, Madison Presunka, Alexandra C. Proctor, Madelaine K. Robertson, Kevin R. Duffy
title: An examination of spectrin protein across development in human visual cortex, and following monocular deprivation in cat lateral geniculate nucleus.
abstract:
Disruption to normal vision during so-called critical or sensitive periods can modify the structure of neurons and neural
connections, and can lead to a severe vision impairment called amblyopia. Decades of research on synaptic plasticity mechanisms have
revealed the NMDA receptor plays a critical role in mediating the refinement modification of visual neural circuitry during the critical
period. Spectrin is an actin-binding protein that has been implicated as a regulator of NMDA channel activity through its stabilizing
effect on the receptor. Spectrin therefore is understood to play an enabling role in visual neural plasticity. In the current study, we
quantified the level of spectrin protein in samples of human primary visual cortex (V1) across the lifespan, and we also measured the
effect of monocular deprivation on spectrin labeling within the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN). Our examination of human V1
revealed maximum spectrin at ages when plasticity levels are high. Further, we discovered that a brief period of monocular
deprivation, sufficient to provoke structural modification, reduced spectrin within layers of the LGN serving the deprived eye. In
aggregate, these results demonstrate a relationship between spectrin and neural modification in V1 and LGN, consistent with playing a
regulatory role in visual system plasticity.

authors_raw: Spencer L. Brown, Qendressa Sahiti, Richard E. Brown
title: Behavioural characterization of the Nrxn1+/- mouse model of autism spectrum disorder
abstract:
Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder that is characterized by deficits in social interaction,
increased repetitive and stereotypical behaviours, and abnormal responses to sensory stimulation. Previous research has linked the
causation of ASD to complex gene-environment interactions, which are not fully understood. Neurobiologically, ASD has been
associated with abnormalities in synaptic connectivity in the brain, and mutations in the neurexin cell adhesion protein family have
been identified in individuals with ASD. The Nrxn1+/- mouse is a novel knock-out model of ASD that carries only one functional
allele of neurexin 1. The behavioural phenotype of this mouse is unknown and knowledge of the behavioural phenotype is essential
because the diagnosis of ASD is based on behavioural symptoms. In this study we tested 20 Nrxn1+/- mice (10M, 10F) and 20
C57BL/6J wild-type control mice (10M, 10F) at two months of age in a behavioural test battery. This included 16 tasks that evaluated
cognitive and motor performance, mood and anxiety, stereotypical behaviour, social interaction, and visual ability. While the
Nrxn1+/- mice showed no cognitive or social deficits compared to the wild- type mice, they performed worse on some motor tests and
showed increased levels of anxiety. These preliminary results give some indication of the behavioural effects of the expression of
neurexin 1. We are currently extending our test battery and testing mice at other ages to determine if they show age-related changes in
their behavioural phenotype.

title: Why Don't We Spend Time In Nature?: A Thematic Theory-Driven Analysis
abstract:
Spending time in nature has been shown to have several health-enhancing benefits, including improved physical health,
mental health, cognitive functions, and overall well-being. However, despite a stated interest in spending time outside and in natural
environments, people spend only 5% of their time in nature. The objective of this study was to better understand why people do not
spend time in nature. We developed a semi-structured interview based on the Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB) to examine beliefs
and attitudes, as well as subjective norms, intentions, and perceived behavioural control about health-related behaviours, and
specifically, spending time in nature in an undergraduate sample. Thematic analysis was employed to examine the interview responses.
An improved understanding of nature disconnection will aid in the development of behaviour change interventions that will target
increasing time spent in nature in order to improve mental, physical, and cognitive health.

authors_raw: Brianne Derby, Amanda Hudson, & Sophie Jacques
title: The Emergence of Envy and Schadenfreude Through Social Comparison and the Disappointing Gift Paradigm
abstract:
Previous studies have found that social emotions such as envy and Schadenfreude typically emerge in situations of social
comparison. The purpose of our study was to examine children's ability to control their displays of these negative social emotions.
While envy is considered to be discontent with other's successes, Schadenfreude is the pleasure of another's misfortune. We hypothesized
that older children would be better at masking social emotions. Younger children aged 49 to 78 months and older children aged 78 to
132 months were tested using the disappointing gift paradigm in situations of social comparison. Results found that older children
were more advanced at masking emotions of envy and Schadenfreude whereas younger children were more influenced by social
comparison in the disappointing gift paradigm. Future research should compare children's reactions in the disappointing gift
paradigm when comparing themselves to adults in contrast to other children.

authors_raw: Richard S. Drake, Sean P. MacKinnon, & Gail A. Eskes
title: Seeing the forest for the trees in large cognitive datasets
abstract:
Computerized cognitive assessments produce a rich set of data that can be difficult to condense. Each variable typically
generates two scores, response speed and accuracy, and many variables are needed to adequately measure performance on target
constructs (e.g., subcomponents of working memory or attention). Experimental manipulations such as variable response-stimulus
intervals further expand the dataset. Using the output of the Dalhousie Computerized Attention Battery (DalCAB) as an exemplar, we
showcase a method of maintaining and manipulating a database, selecting and combining relevant variables, using planned contrasts
to visualize the overall pattern of results, and testing for differences using a multilevel modelling approach wherein individuals are
nested within variables. The described approach may be used in both single-measurement and intervention (pre-post) research. Using
our method, we report the results of a three-week intervention study comparing a dual n-back working memory training game to
passive and active control groups.

authors_raw: Evan F. Forward, Julia C. M. Greenham, Marcus V. Alves, Tracy L. Taylor, Jonathan M. Fawcett
title: Relating Pupillometric and Behavioural Indices of Effort in Item-Method Directed Forgetting
abstract:
Forgetting in an item-method directed forgetting paradigm has been shown to slow responses in the period immediately
following the forget instruction and to facilitate removal of attention from the representation of the preceding study item, including its
spatial location. These findings have been interpreted as evidence of an active control process associated with the cessation of
rehearsal. However, recent findings using pupillometry have also produced conflicting evidence favouring relatively reduced
processing demands immediately following the forget instruction as evidence by relatively larger pupil dilation following the remember
instruction. The current experiment sought to compare these findings directly by integrating both pupillometry and a secondary probe
response task into the same directed forgetting task. Both effects replicated: In addition to a significant directed forgetting effect - with
greater recognition of remember than forget items at test - we observed longer response times following forget instructions but larger
pupil dilations following remember instructions. We discuss ways in which these findings may be integrated into a more coherent
theoretical framework.

authors_raw: Samantha Good, Jasmine Aziz, Gail Eskes
title: Searching for a Target: Do Older Adults Check the Template More Often?
abstract:
Some cognitive processes important for daily tasks deteriorate with age, including working memory and visual search
abilities. Working memory is required to maintain a target template (i.e., image of target object) in mind during visual search. We
examined age differences in visual reliance on a target template displayed during search to enhance our knowledge of changes in visual
search ability with age. Ten younger (18-35 yrs) and 38 older (55-80 yrs) participants (to date) completed a visual search task while eye
movements were monitored. We manipulated the number of target features (one or three) and the number of distractors in the visual
scene. Older adults were expected to show increased reliance on the target template (i.e., more eye movements towards the template
and longer fixations) than younger adults, particularly as the number of features and distractors increased. Results will provide further
knowledge on the changes in cognitive functions with age and can be implemented to promote maintenance of cognitive performance
across the life-span.

authors_raw: Aydan Idrisova, Kiyana Kamali, Olivia Macdonald, Briana G. Ross, Brett B.T. Feltmate, Colin R. McCormick & Ralph S. Redden
title: Language and cognition in dogs (Canis familiaris) AUTHORS: Linda Hutchinson, Gabrielle Moulton, Oliver Schnare ABSTRACT: In human infants, a correlation has been found between the development of language and object permanence, or the ability to recognize that objects still exist when they cannot be seen. Like human children, dogs present ample variability in both language ability and level of object permanence, making them an optimal comparison species for determining whether the correlation between two variables extends to another species. For this study, language ability was measured through the number of words a dogs recognizes, as reported by their owners in a survey. Cognition was measured through a search game comprised of tasks of increasing complexity that challenged dogs' levels of object permanence. We also examined links between dog breed and attachment on object permanence. We report findings related to these research questions and propose future directions. ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- TITLE: The Influence of Varying Foreperiods on Error Rate and Reaction Time
abstract:
Attention is defined as the process of focusing on one stimulus while simultaneously ignoring others. In 1973, Posner,
Summers, Klein, and Buggie investigated how a warning signal affects alertness and accuracy. We aimed to replicate their first
experiment which provided evidence of speed-accuracy tradeoff and confirmed that rapid response times did not improve accuracy.
Reaction time and error rate were measured during the manipulation of foreperiods, as well as compatible or incompatible response
conditions. Seventeen subjects participated. The response condition was cued by the border of the screen. A warning signal was played
prior to the presentation of the target stimulus. The subjects responded to the stimulus by pressing the key on the same side as the
target, or the opposite side. Error rate was the smallest for shorter foreperiods but these reaction times were the longest, therefore
supporting the speed-accuracy tradeoff found by Posner et al.

authors_raw: Kirsten M. Lebelle, Morgan G. MacIntosh, Samantha S. Tse, Sana M. Fashir, Brett B.T. Feltmate, Colin R. McCormick & Ralph S. Redden
title: Even Further Evidence in Favor of Prior Entry from Endogenous Attention to a Location in Space: A Replication Study
abstract:
The laws of prior entry state that attended objects are perceived faster than unattended objects. Previous research has
provided contradictory results about the presence of prior entry (Shore et al., 2001; Schneider & Bavelier, 2003). The goal of our
current experiment was to replicate a previous study by Redden et al. (2017) which incorporated an orthogonal color probe task into
the original TOJ paradigm in order to settle the discrepancy in previous literature and determine if prior entry exists under
endogenous cueing. We hypothesized that prior entry would show effects under the endogenous cueing condition. The present study
used a modified TOJ task with an orthogonal color probe task to further reduce any response biases and assess prior entry independent
of response mechanisms. Effects of attention on prior entry and probability of color encoding were replicated from Redden et al.
(2017). Other findings from the experiment will be discussed.

authors_raw: Kate MacDiarmid, Fatema Al-Shammary, Cathryn LeBel, Isabella Rossi
title: On the independence of attentional mechanisms in temporal orienting: A replication study
abstract:
Attention has been theorized to be composed of two networks: the exogenous network, which involves bottom-up
processing; and the endogenous network, which involves top-down processing. A replication of a 2018 study by McCormick and
colleagues was performed to provide further evidence for the independence of endogenous and exogenous networks in temporal
attention (attention in time). We hypothesized that endogenous and exogenous temporal attention would be found to be independent
from one another, as demonstrated by the effects of two measures: cue type and signal type. Fifteen participants performed a
computerized task that measured reaction time as these two measures were manipulated. Data analysis revealed a significant effect of
both cue and signal independence, as well as the cue and signal variance. Implications and limitations are discussed.

authors_raw: Alexandra M. Ryken, S. Hélène Deacon, and Lesly Wade-Woolley
title: Reading comprehension, prosodic sensitivity, and the role of prosodic cues in text
abstract:
Reading comprehension, or the ability to understand the meaning of text, is one of the most important skills children
learn in school. Understanding how various language skills support children's reading comprehension is essential to supporting early
literacy development. One language skill which is related to reading comprehension in young readers is prosodic sensitivity, which is
an awareness of the rhythmic cues in speech. However, the reason for this relationship is unclear. This study tested the possibility that
children's awareness of how rhythmic cues are represented in text, e.g. through punctuation, may mediate the relationship between
prosodic sensitivity and reading comprehension. Approximately 150 students in grades 3-5 were assessed, and awareness of prosodic
cues in text did emerge as a partial mediator in the relationship between prosodic sensitivity and reading comprehension. Children
may be using their awareness of rhythmic cues in text when attempting to understand what they read.

title: Knowledge of Spoken Words by Domestic Dogs: A New Instrument for Use With Dog Owners
abstract:
Dogs are sensitive to human social cues (Miklosi & Topal, 2013) and can interpret physical and verbal cues from
humans to gain information about new situations (Merola et al., 2012), or successfully complete tasks (Kaminski & Nitzschner, 2013).
No reliable tool exists for determining which and how many words dogs know. The current study included a new measure designed to
assess the number of words to which dogs respond differentially and systematically. Our tool was modeled after the MacArthur-Bates
CDI (Fenson et al., 1994) for use with preverbal infants. Our first goal was to assess the content validity and usability of our online
instrument, while our second was to use owners' responses to identify predictors of dogs' word understanding. Participants included
168 owners of dogs of various ages and breeds. Owners answered questions about themselves and their dog, and identified and rated
the level at which their dog responded to each word in a checklist. Owners reported that their dogs responded selectively to 18 to 202
(M=89 words; SD=36) and variables such as dog owners' experience with dog training, how quickly dogs reportedly learned new
tricks, and breed type showed predictive utility. Principal component analyses revealed that words to which dogs respond loaded onto
3 distinct, yet related components: general words, verbs, and nouns. Our tool not only allows for a standardized measurement of dogs'
receptive abilities, but may eventually also become an important tool used to predict the potential of working dogs.

authors_raw: Kaelen Watters, Anjali Pandey, Tracy Taylor-Helmick
title: Forgetting on purpose; How and what do we intentionally forget?
abstract:
This study examined the extent to which the visual characteristics of to-be-remembered and to-be-forgotten words
are encoded into long-term memory. Using an item-method directed forgetting task, participants completed a study phase consisting of
list of words presented one at a time, each followed by an instruction to remember or forget. In a subsequent test phase, participants
then made yes-no recognition decisions to: 1) 'remember' and 'forget' words from the study phase; 2) unstudied foil words; and, 3)
unstudied foil words that looked similar to the 'remember' and 'forget' words. We expected a directed forgetting effect, with greater
recognition of 'remember' words than 'forget' words. However, our main interest was in determining whether visual similarity
to studied words increases the false alarms made to unstudied words and, if so, whether this tendency is influenced by whether the
unstudied word looks similar to a 'remember' item or a 'forget' item.

authors_raw: Francesco Usai, Heather Ouellette and Aaron J. Newman
title: Investigating the neural bases of self-monitoring of speech in people with aphasia: an ERP study
abstract:
Aphasia is a communication disorder that can impair the ability to find the right words, name things correctly, and to
monitor one's own speech for the presence of errors. Little is known about the neural bases of self-monitoring of speech in aphasia. To
address this gap, we recorded EEG from people with aphasia (current n=2) while performing a picture-naming task, and observed the
effect of response accuracy on the error-related negativity (ERN), an EEG effect associated with performance monitoring across
different cognitive domains. The time course and scalp topographies of EEG were contrasted for incorrect and correct responses. For
one participant, this contrast resembled the ERN, while the other showed an effect with a similar time course but with opposite
polarity (i.e., a positivity). These differences may be explained by the different neuropsychological profile of the two subjects, although
these preliminary results provide encouraging evidence that the ERN may be a useful marker of self-monitoring in aphasia.

authors_raw: Samantha Good, Swasti Arora, Jeanne Townsend, Lisa Mash & Raymond Klein
title: Examining Autism Spectrum Disorder Using the Attention Network Test: A Meta-
abstract:
Attentional deficits are common in autism spectrum disorder (ASD) however the nature of
these deficits is yet to be fully understood. The present study aimed to assess the three attention
networks (alerting, orienting, and executive control) as identified by Posner and Peterson. We conducted
a Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis of studies that implemented the Attention Network Test or its
variants to explore whether individuals with ASD exhibited differences in any of the attentional networks
when compared to typically developing controls. Eight studies were included in our analysis, comprising
322 children and young adults with the average age ranging from 4.76 - 30 years (Mdn = 13.24). There
were no credible differences between participants with ASD versus typically developing controls in the
alerting network. Orienting scores were not credibly different, however there was a trend suggesting
less efficient attentional response to spatial information in ASD. Executive control scores were not
credibly different, but there was a trend showing higher average scores in people with ASD. Although
the results from this meta-analysis add to the growing literature on the nature of attentional deficits in
people with ASD, our findings are limited by the relatively small number of studies.

title: Re-Analysis of Body Representation in the Somatosensory Cortex: An Investigation of
abstract:
The original 2-D somatosensory homunculus based on Penfield's work lacks representation
of uniquely female anatomy. For this reason, we created a quantitative 2-D map of female-specific
anatomy in the primary somatosensory cortex (S1) based upon a systematic review of published
neuroimaging literature, that comprised 11 studies. These studies reported localization of
representations of the clitoris, cervix, vagina, dorsal pudendal nerve, perineal nerve, nipple, and breast
in the medial wall of S1 in the paracentral lobule. Representations of the dorsal clitoral nerve, posterior
vulva, clitoris, breast, and nipple were also found in the secondary somatosensory cortex. Because there
were inconsistencies between the locations of anatomy between studies, we proposed a research plan
that comprehensively maps female specific anatomy in the somatosensory cortices. The creation of a
female somatosensory "femunculus" is necessary considering the implications it has for assessing
neuroplasticity after changes to female anatomy in mastectomies and gender affirmation surgery.

authors_raw: Hélène Deacon, Stef Hartlin, Alex Ryken, Mariam Elgendi, Elizabeth MacKay, Klaudia Krenca
title: Reading Through the Elementary School Years, With a Break for COVID
abstract:
In 2019, we optimistically launched a longitudinal study with over 300 children in Grade 1 at
18 schools around the province. In partnership with the Dep't of Education, our goal was to follow them
through to Grade 6. But, of course, COVID hit when the children were in Grade 2, midway through
testing. I report here on our findings from our first couple of years and how we are working to recover
from this change in plans. The oral language skills that we report here on an IURN and SSHRC-funded
research study designed to investigate the language skills that children use in their reading. We
recruited over 300 elementary school aged children, initially tested in Grade 1 (in winter of 2019). We
report here on analyses exploring the relations between early language skills and Grade 2 reading levels.
We also explore the achievement and opportunity gap in Nova Scotia by contrasting results for African
Nova Scotians with those for other Nova Scotian children. Our goal is to provide another lens into the
skills that support all children in learning to read.

title: Lifestyle Changes That Can Impact Your Attention
abstract:
This talk will summarize a literature review on how different lifestyle factors impact the
networks of attention. With the help of the 'ANT Database' developed in the Klein Lab (found
at AttentionNetwork.ca), 70+ studies were included that fit into five main categories: meditation,
exercise, drug use, sleep, and environmental or social factors. All three networks showed that they were
sensitive to different changes in lifestyle. This review also reveals gaps in the literature for
understanding how attention is being impacted by our day-to-day choices, as well as the importance of
consistent reporting of results and the availability of experimental data.

authors_raw: Julie Blais, Luke R. Mungall & Scott Pruysers
title: Personality, Political Ideology, and Partisanship in Canada
abstract:
Personality traits reliably predict political ideology and partisanship around the world, but
studies investigating these relationships among Canadians used poor measures of personality, or did not
examine the contribution of antagonistic personality traits (i.e., psychopathy, narcissism,
Machiavellianism). We surveyed Canadians (N = 2551) on their ideological self-placement and political
partisanship using robust measures of HEXACO traits and antagonistic traits. The findings for political
ideology were mostly consistent with previous studies. Canadians who placed themselves on the
ideological right were lower in openness to experience, honesty-humility, and impulsivity, but higher in
extraversion, narcissism, antisocial tendencies, and blunted affect. Nuanced relationships emerged
between personality and support for specific political parties (i.e., partisanship). The personality traits of
Liberal, Conservative, and Green partisans were not always consistent with the personality traits of their
party's ideological wing. Overall, we demonstrated a crucial distinction between ideology and
partisanship, and provided further evidence that personality matters for political choice in Canada.

title: A History of Grading Practices at Dalhousie University
abstract:
This session will provide a historical overview of grading practices at Dalhousie. In the early
1900s, Dalhousie was using a variant of the British system, where 65% or higher was required for
"distinction." By the late 1940s, additional subdivisions were created (First, Second, and Third Divisions),
and the percentage grade cutoff for the highest grade moved to 80%. The 1970s were an experimental
phase, where percentage grades were officially abolished and replaced with letter grades. After a period
of virtually no standardization in grading across departments, the university moved towards
standardization (i.e., the 4.3 GPA system and standardized percentage-to-letter conversion schemes) by
present day. Hopefully, the historical context of how modern grading came to be will help us create new
approaches that optimally meet both needs. Preprint: https://edarxiv.org/fmgv8/

authors_raw: Oliver Schnare & Dr. Richard E. Brown
title: Measuring Memory Strength in 5xfad Mice Through Olfactory Discrimination Tasks
abstract:
We investigated behavioural and epigenetic signs of olfactory learning and memory in
5xFAD model mice and wildtype mice (9-16 months of age). Mice were classically conditioned on a
single day using methods adapted from Schellinck et al. (2001) to associate the CS+ odour pot with a
sugar reward, and the CS- odour pot with no reward. The behavioural signs of learning and memory
were measured as their preference for the CS+ odour pot on 24-hour and 7-day memory tests.
Epigenetic signs of learning and memory were measured as the normalized gene expression of Atrx in
the hippocampus obtained from RT-qPCR analysis. Previously, mice with reduced Atrx expression display
impairments to long-term potentiation in the hippocampus. 5xFAD mice spent significantly more time
digging in the CS+ odour pot than wildtype mice during the acquisition stage, however their
performances on memory tests did not differ. Wildtype mice displayed higher levels of Atrx expression
than 5xFAD mice in the experimental groups, however Atrx expression served minimal predictive value
to performance on memory tests overall. Our results suggest that 5xFAD mice do not have impairments
to olfactory learning and memory, and that Atrx expression in the hippocampus may not be related to
olfactory learning and memory capabilities.

authors_raw: Miller, D.W., Zbarsky, J., Cohen, A.M., Penny, S. & Adamo, S.A.
title: Hemolymph Nutrient and Metabolite Changes in Caterpillars Experiencing Parasite-
abstract:
Parasitic wasp larvae (Cotesia congregata) permanently suppress host caterpillar (Manduca
sexta) feeding via an unknown mechanism(s) as the larvae emerge. This study investigated whether any
changes in caterpillar hemolymph after wasps emerge may help explain this feeding behaviour change.
Plasma samples were collected from five groups of caterpillars. Two control groups (feeding
unparasitized caterpillars and food deprived caterpillars the parasitized non-feeding states) were
compared to three groups of parasitized caterpillars: before wasp emergence, 1, and 3 days after
emergence. Plasma metabolomics (via liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry)
identified nearly 200 metabolites in all five groups, including amino acids, nitrogenous waste, lipid
metabolism products, and immune signaling molecules (i.e., adenosine). The same groups were also
assayed for sugar content-elevated hemolymph nutrients in the hemolymph suppress caterpillar
feeding. These found that post-emergent caterpillars tended to have lower levels of sugars and amino
acids compared to controls. The metabolomics also suggested an elevated immune activation signal for
at least 3 days following parasite emergence. Immune activation can temporarily reduce or suppress
feeding, and adenosine (immune signaling molecule) injection suppressed feeding for 2 hours in healthy
caterpillars. These results provide insight into how wasps may be influencing their host's behaviour.

authors_raw: Michaud, Nicole M. & Crowder, Nathan A.
title: Measuring Size Perception in Unrestrained Wild-type Mice Using a Novel Two-
abstract:
Due to the availability of genetic tools used to explore neural circuits within and between
brain areas, the mouse has become an invaluable animal model in vision research. However, to link
neural circuits to visual perception, novel behavioural tasks must first be developed. To begin
investigating the neural underpinnings of visual size perception, we modified the steering wheel
paradigm developed by Burgess et al. (2017) to create a two-alternative forced choice size
discrimination task in unrestrained wild-type mice. We assessed the ability of mice to correctly select
the larger of two simultaneously presented grating patches, which allowed the generation of
psychometric functions from perceptually driven behaviour. Mice displayed successful learning and
generalization of the task when the reward stimulus size remained constant. However, when the size of
the reward stimulus was varied and mice were required to select the relatively larger grating patch, task
accuracy failed to reach criterion.

title: Inhibition of Nav1.7 and Nav1.8 Decreases Pain-Related Behaviour in Speke's Hinge-
abstract:
Voltage-gated sodium ion channels in the peripheral nervous system have great potential as
targets for eliminating pain with minimal side effects. Highly selective ion channel blockers, have been
used to explore the involvement of a range of ion channels in nociception. Comparative studies are
important to fully elucidate performance of such molecules and physiological mechanisms of particular
ion channels. The tortoise has been demonstrated to be a good animal model for nociception. In this
study, NAV 26 and A803467 selective blockers for Nav1.7 and Nav1.8 ion channels were used to
investigate their ability to reduce nociception in the Speke's hinge back tortoise. The time spent in pain-
related behavior during formalin, capsaicin and hot plate tests was recorded and analyzed. The results
show that both blockers significantly reduced the mean time spent in pain-related behaviour in the
formalin test and capsaicin test. Only A803467 caused a statistically significant decrease in the mean
time spent in pain related behaviour in the hot plate test. In conclusion, Nav1.7 and Nav1.8 are
significantly involved in processing of chemical induced inflammatory pain in the Speke's hinge back
tortoise. However, only Nav 1.8 appears to participate in processing of thermally induced pain.

authors_raw: M. Elizabeth O'Leary, Elizabeth M. Myles, Isaac D. Romkey, Kiyana Kamali & Tara S. Perrot
title: Acute Probiotic Treatment During the Perinatal Period Affects Maternal Care and Gut
abstract:
In rodents, active maternal care is crucial for offspring; and a mother's behaviour is sensitive
to her external environment. Therefore, the external environment can influence how a mother interacts
with her offspring. It is essential to understand what impacts maternal care since low maternal care
disrupts offspring development. Probiotics have the potential to alter the gut microbiota, and their
downstream effects may impact maternal health and behaviour. We investigated the effects of
probiotics on maternal care, offspring developmental outcomes and maternal gut microbiota. We found
that probiotic-treated mothers performed increased amounts of active nursing and licking/grooming
compared to their placebo-treated counterparts. At weaning, offspring from probiotic mothers weighed
significantly more than those from placebo-treated mothers. Lastly, we found that probiotic-treated
mothers' microbiota contained increased amounts of Bifidobacterium. These results contribute to our
understanding of the external factors that may influence the mother, her health and behaviours and
affect offspring outcomes.

authors_raw: Broderick M. B. Parks & Leslie S. Phillmore
title: Natural Photoperiodic Regulation of Hippocampal Plasticity in Wild Black-Capped
abstract:
In black-capped chickadees (Poecile atricapillus), food-storing behaviour fluctuates
seasonally; supporting this behavioural change is a seasonal change in the hippocampus (Hp), critical for
spatial behaviour and memory. Seasonal change in Hp is well-documented; we cannot however easily
compare findings due to the use of captivity in some studies, and captivity is known to alter Hp
plasticity. Also underlying this brain-behaviour interaction are the breeding-associated changes in avian
physiology driven by photoperiod (day length). Here, we captured wild black-capped chickadees (N=48)
across three distinct photoperiodic conditions: photostimulated (March-April), photorefractory (August-
September), and photosensitive (December-January). All birds were sacrificed shortly after capture; we
stained neural tissue with cresyl violet and quantified measures of Hp plasticity including volume and
neuron number. Our findings are among the first to quantify seasonal Hp plasticity in a large sample of
wild chickadees not introduced into any sort of captivity, and provides key evidence in assessing
whether food-storing behaviour and its impact on Hp occur independently of photoperiod-driven
seasonal regulation of breeding in food-storing birds.

title: Improvements to Rodent Housing Have Significant Effects on Development of the
abstract:
The value of rodents for modeling human neuropsychiatric disorders and unraveling
underlying molecular mechanisms is not questioned. Attempts (not always successful) are made to
standardize the conditions under which rats or mice are housed to facilitate comparing results
generated in different labs all over the world. While the merits of standardizing housing seem obvious,
the costs are generally not considered. Over the last 10 years, we repeatedly have demonstrated
significant effects of maintaining rats in semi-naturalistic housing, compared to standard housing, on the
quality of maternal care. Decades of prior research has demonstrated the importance of maternal care
quality for developing offspring, especially with respect to stress-related outcomes. Our work has
revealed significant differences in stress-related brain and behaviour measures in offspring raised in
semi-naturalistic housing versus standard housing. Here, I argue that standard housing is impoverished,
and the conclusions drawn from results generated using standard housing should reflect that.

authors_raw: Raymond M. Klein, Brett B. T. Feltmate, Yoko Ishigami & Nicholas E. Murray
title: Is There One "Beam" of Attention for Searching in Space and Time?
abstract:
In their pure forms, searching in space entails the allocation of attention to items distributed
in space and presented at the same time whereas searching in time entails the allocation of attention to
items distributed in time and presented at the same location. In independent projects we explored
whether the metaphorical "beams" operating the domains of space and time might be independent or
the same. In one project we used a differential approach. Early research using spatial (Snyder, 1972) and
temporal search tasks (McLean, Broadbent & Broadbent, 1983) reported a substantial degree of
sloppiness (binding errors). Our participants performed both of these tasks to see if the frequency of
these binding errors in space and time were correlated. We replicated both early findings of binding
errors in space and in time, but their frequency of occurrence in the two domains was not significantly
correlated. In the other project, we used an experimental approach. With the participation of DISP
students we explored whether the principles described by Duncan & Humphreys for searching in space
would apply similarly to searching in time. Not surprisingly, performance in spatial search conformed to
the predictions of D&H's principles. Importantly, temporal search performance followed the same
pattern, suggesting that D&H's principles generalize to temporal search. Why do you think these two
approaches yield different answers to the question posed in our title?

authors_raw: Jessica F. Garden, Ian C. G. Weaver & Richard. E. Brown
title: Pavlovian Conditioned Odour Memory in the Neurexin1+/- Mouse Model of Autism
abstract:
Neurexins (NRXNs) are presynaptic cell-adhesion molecules that bind with postsynaptic
ligands to connect synapses and regulate synaptic transmission. Altered Nrxn gene expression is linked
to neurodevelopmental disorders, such as autism spectrum disorder (ASD), in which learning and
memory are impaired. The Nrxn1+/- transgenic mouse model, with the loss of one Nrxn1 allele, is a
novel model of ASD that is utilized in this research. Mice conditioned to dig in an odor pot to receive a
reward during a 1-day Pavlovian conditioning paradigm using the method of Schellinck et al. (2001
Chem. Senses 26:663-72), were tested for short- and long-term olfactory memory. Given the recently
identified role of NRXN in synaptic stability, we hypothesised that neurexin would be implicated in
molecular processes required for long term memory, and thus, the Nrxn1 +/- mice would have impaired
long-term odour memory. Preliminary analyses reveal that Nrxn1 +/- mice have reduced short-term
memory during olfactory digging tasks compared to age- and sex-matched controls, and we expect to
observe even greater deficits in long-term olfactory memory in Nrxn1+/- mice.

authors_raw: Richard S. Drake, Gail A. Eskes & Raymond M. Klein
title: Reconciling Expectation and Interpretation in the Cognitive Training Meta-Analyses
abstract:
The cognitive enhancement potential of computerized, game-based working memory
training tasks continues to attract researchers and investors alike. Laboratory and commercial
endeavours of the past two decades have been both basic and applied, with goals ranging from
understanding the mechanisms of learning and transfer to improving academic performance or reducing
age-related cognitive decline. Hundreds of scholars have participated in the debate of whether working
memory training is effective. Putting aside valid criticisms of the debated question itself ("effective" is
not specific and is only meaningful when paired with clearly defined goals), there is evidence to support
both sides. However, on the basis of an in-progress systematic review of the working memory training
meta-analyses, we argue that the controversy has more to do with differences of interpretation, rather
than numeric differences. We thus qualify the alleged quantitative controversy (or "quantroversy")
accordingly. We will contextualize our preliminary findings with respect to transfer theories and recent
best practice recommendations for cognitive training.

authors_raw: Laura Elliott, Alena Galilee, Lisa Beck, Clara Lownie, Catherine Mimeau, S. Helene Deacon &
title: Lexical Decisions in Ortho-Semantic Learning: A Behavioural Study of Grade 3
abstract:
English speaking children in grade 3, in English and French immersion, completed a novel
ortho- semantic learning task (Mimeau et al., 2018). They read passages introducing novel words and
their meaning, followed by a test on spellings and meanings of the words. Next, participants completed
a lexical decision task (LDT) with the novel words, real words, non-words, consonant strings, and false
fonts. While reaction times on the LDT for English students were significantly faster, accuracy did not
differ significantly between groups. In the absence of a significant difference, French students
demonstrated a trend towards higher accuracy for novel words, and lower accuracy for non-words.
French immersion students receive formal reading education in a second language, and these results
suggest it may reduce their speed for making judgements on English words. Furthermore, French
