In the present study, we investigated a vulnerability-stress model, testing whether perfectionism (self-critical and rigid) moderates the relationship between stressor severity (academic and interpersonal) and academic outcomes.
Method: A sample of 384 post-secondary students completed a cross-sectional survey involving questionnaires assessing perfectionism, stress, academic self-efficacy, academic burnout, and academic self-concept. Results: Main effects of stressor severity (both academic and interpersonal) were found to predict decreased academic self-efficacy and academic self-concept, as well as increased academic burnout. Main effects of self-critical perfectionism were found to predict decreased academic self-efficacy and increased academic burnout. Main effects of rigid perfectionism generally predicted increased academic self-efficacy and increased academic self-concept when controlling for stressor severity. None of the proposed interaction effects were statistically significant.
Discussion: Findings suggest stressor severity (both academic and interpersonal) and self-critical perfectionism are strong predictors of maladaptive academic outcomes, and risk factors for poor academic functioning. The lack of an interaction effect suggests that a vulnerability-stress model does not explain why perfectionism leads to maladaptive academic outcomes.
Ritsema, Vanessa

authors_raw: Ritsema, V., Woryke, R., & Weatherhead, D.E.
title: Accent Intelligibility: Understanding the effects of race-based expectations and linguistic background
abstract: Race-based expectations and linguistic background can both influence accent intelligibility. However, past research on accent intelligibility has yielded conflicting results, and the underlying mechanisms of accented-speech processing remain unclear. Furthermore, the effects of race-based expectations and linguistic background have been studied largely in isolation, with past research focusing on monolingual listeners. The current study seeks to better understand the combined effects of race-based expectations and linguistic background on accent intelligibility by replicating the experimental design of McGowen (2015) with bilingual participants. The current study recruited 127 American participants fluent in English and Norwegian who listened to and transcribed 60 Mandarin-accented English sentences in noise. Sentences were presented with a White face or an East Asian face, with each condition designed to match or violate listeners' race-based expectations. Following sentence transcription, participants were asked to rate how strong they found accent to be from the experiment on a Likert scale. Participants also completed a short questionnaire that assessed their language usage, proficiency, familiarity, and attitudes. For analysis, a generalized linear mixed effects model was fitted to the data with condition, sex, accentedness rating, and participant language use as predictor variables. Results suggested that speaker race is not a salient speaker cue for bilingual listeners, and that bilingual listeners may therefore have different associations between ethnicity and accent compared to monolingual listeners.

authors_raw: Broderick M. B. Parks1*, T. Adam Liddle2, Tyler J. Stevenson2, Jason R. Treberg3, Debbie M. Kelly4, Leslie S.
title: Ageing, metabolic challenge, and neural plasticity: transcriptomic insights from domesticated pigeons (Columba livia domestica)
abstract:
Domesticated pigeons (Columba livia domestica) are valuable models for studying spatial cognition and
neural plasticity. Unlike mammals, birds exhibit extensive plasticity throughout adulthood, but it remains unclear
whether the avian brain shows any evidence of (mammal-like) decline with age. While food deprivation is often used to
experimentally motivate spatial behaviour, its effects across different life stages are also unclear. Physiological
differences between adult and aged birds suggests a role for the mediobasal hypothalamus, a key regulator of energy
balance, but whether age-related physiological differences (and/or responses to food deprivation) reflect molecular
changes in this structure remains unexplored. Here, we examined how a standard 90% food deprivation affected neural
plasticity in adult (1-4 years) and aged (8-18 years) pigeons. Birds (N=24) were assigned to one of three food conditions
over ~16 weeks: (i) recent food deprivation; (ii) past food deprivation; or (iii) ad libitum feed. Using Nanopore RNA
sequencing, we profiled all RNA transcripts in the mediobasal hypothalamus and hippocampus, identifying hundreds of
differentially expressed genes based on age, food condition, and their interaction. These findings reveal previously
unknown molecular signatures of aging and metabolic challenge in the avian brain, providing new insights into neural
plasticity and energy regulation across the lifespan.

title: Chemical signals, olfactory learning and individual differences: 50 years of research. [Chemical Signals in Vertebrates 16. Edited by B. Schaal et al. In Press, May 2025.]
abstract:
This talk describes 50 years of research in my lab on chemical signals in rodents These began with experiments
on the role of odour preferences and urine-marking in the socio-sexual behaviour of rats. Olfactory communication
involves both endocrine and experiential factors as shown by our studies on gonadectomy and isolation rearing. The
importance of individual recognition by odours was studied through the rat pups' response to "maternal pheromones"
and by examining the role of the major histocompatibility complex in odours of individuality. The initial studies done using
habituation-dishabituation tests were continued using an operant olfactometer to study odour discrimination learning
using the urine odours of rats and mice. We also investigated the importance of individual recognition in puberty
acceleration in juvenile female mice. We then began to examine the use of the olfactometer to study working memory in
rats and learning set formation in mice. We are now examining olfactory reversal learning in mice using signal detection
methods to identify sex, age and genotype differences in learning strategies. We also use the Pavlovian conditioned odour
preference task developed by Heather Schellinck to study olfactory learning and memory. Our studies have attempted to
integrate mechanisms of olfactory communication (releaser effects), neuroendocrine responses (primer effects) and
learning and memory (cognitive processes) in olfactory signalling in rodents.

authors_raw: Holly Carbyn Abiaz Hossain, Patricia Lingley-Pottie, Lisa D. Palmer, Sophie Ayoub, Andrea Shugar, Donna M.
title: Evaluating the Impact of an Online Coaching Intervention for Families Affected by 22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome
abstract:
22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome (22q11DS) is the most common microdeletion syndrome, with broad phenotypic
variability often involving high levels of internalizing/externalizing symptoms in children. Caregivers of children with
22q11DS are at increased risk of poor mental health, yet caregiver-focused supports remain scarce. This pilot study
evaluated the effectiveness of an online coaching intervention for families affected by 22q11DS using a repeated-
measures design, examining its impact on caregiver mental health, parenting practices, and child
internalizing/externalizing symptoms. Eighteen caregivers completed the intervention, which included 12 weekly modules
with coached group phone calls. Outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3-, and 6-month follow-up using the DASS-21
(parent distress), PAFAS (parenting practices), and SDQ (child symptoms). Linear mixed models revealed significant
reductions in caregiver stress at both follow-ups and a marginal decrease in anxiety at 6 months. Children's externalizing
behaviours declined significantly over time. Findings suggest the online intervention may alleviate caregiver distress and
improve child behaviour in families affected by 22q11DS. Larger studies (RCTs) are warranted to confirm results.

authors_raw: KC Collings1,2,a, Broderick M. B. Parks2, Leslie S. Phillmore2
title: Brains in Tune with Season: Seasonal Regulation of Neurogenesis Within Vocal Control and Auditory Perceptual Regions of Wild Black-capped Chickadees (Poecileatricapillus)
abstract:
Songbird vocal behaviour often changes seasonally, with birds singing more in the spring to attract mates
and defend territories. This behavioural plasticity is supported by neural plasticity specifically within the vocal control
system, the interconnected regions and circuits supporting songbird vocal behaviour. One measure of neural plasticity is
neurogenesis: the proliferation, differentiation, migration, and incorporation of new neurons. Here, we evaluated
seasonal differences in neurogenesis within four brain regions: two vocal control nuclei (HVC [proper name] and Area X)
and two auditory perceptual regions (caudomedial nidopallium and caudomedial mesopallium) in adult male and female
black-capped chickadees (Poecile atricapillus). We captured birds across three seasons (spring, summer, and winter) and
quantified neurogenesis by labelling brain tissue for doublecortin (DCX), a marker of newborn neurons. We counted
three morphologies of DCX+ cells (multipolar, fusiform, and round) and found the distribution of these morphologies
differed in each brain region. We also found seasonal changes in HVC multipolar cells, and sex-specific seasonal
differences in Area X round cells. These results suggest that seasonal patterns of neurogenesis relate to the functional
roles of the different brain regions and also vary between sexes, broadening our overall understanding of avian
physiology, behaviour, and neural plasticity.

authors_raw: Richard S. Drake, Sophie Bhaskara, Raymond M. Klein, & Gail A. Eskes
title: Content Variability as Desirable Difficulty in N-Back Training
abstract:
Working memory training research has been a grand quest for transfer (improvements on related but
untrained tasks). Recent culture shifts in the field have reprioritized mechanistic studies probing transfer theories, and
one underexplored area involves manipulations of training variability. Our VARIA project is a 5-day training study that
draws together multiple learning theories, from Schmidt and Bjork's work on desirable difficulties in motor and
vocabulary learning to Gathercole's theory of novel subroutines as transferable units of cognitive skill learning. We
embedded a categorization task within a traditional n-back paradigm to create a novel "varback" training program that
we used to test the hypothesis that training with high variability (3x as much as a lower variability group) will boost
retention and near transfer measures of working memory. We will present and contextualize preliminary findings with
respect to contemporary debates in working memory training.

authors_raw: Ethier-Gagnon, M. A., Perry, R.N., Helmick, C., Crocker, C., Good, K., Stewart, S. H., & Barrett, S. P.
title: The Effects of Cannabidiol (CBD) Expectancy on Subjective Stress, Anxiety, and Intrinsic Connectivity Network Functional Connectivity in Healthy Adults
abstract:
Cannabidiol (CBD) may be a promising candidate for stress/anxiety disorder treatment. CBD expectancy alone
impacts subjective, physiological, and endocrine markers of stress/anxiety. We aimed to delineate CBD expectancy effects
on subjective state and resting state functional connectivity (rsFC) within and between the default mode network (DMN),
salience network (SN), and central executive network (CEN). Healthy adults (N=32) were randomized to receive accurate
or misleading instructions regarding the CBD content of a CBD-free oil. rsFC was assessed at baseline and following a stress
task. Subjective state was measured at multiple timepoints. In the Told CBD-free (control) condition, the SN showed
increased rsFC with both the CEN and DMN. In the Told CBD (expectancy) condition, decreases in stress and anxiety were
reported following oil administration and during stressor recovery, and increased rsFC was observed within and between
the DMN and CEN. Results suggest CBD expectancy may engage neural pathways involved in stress-regulation.

title: The Relationship of Perfectionism, Culture, Acculturative Stress and Depression in University Students
abstract:
The stress generation hypothesis suggests that perfectionistic tendencies can create stress, increasing the
risk of depression. This may apply to international students who experience acculturative stress, defined as a stress
reaction towards struggles associated with adapting to a different culture. This study investigated whether acculturative
stress mediates the relationship between perfectionism and depression. Participants were 119 Dalhousie University
students who identified as international students or first-generation immigrants. They completed an online survey
including measures of perfectionism (Big Three Perfectionism Scale), acculturative stress (Riverside Acculturative Stress
Inventory), and depression (Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale). Mediation analysis revealed no indirect
effect for rigid or self-critical perfectionism. However, narcissistic perfectionism was found to indirectly predict
depression through acculturative stress. When controlling for time in Canada, this indirect effect was no longer
significant. These findings highlight a potential link between narcissistic perfectionism and stress generation in the
context of cultural adjustment and suggest that acculturative stress may be a pathway to depression for this
perfectionism subtype.

authors_raw: Good, K., Couture, M.-E., Stewart, S. H., Barrett, S. P., Tibbo, P., Milliken, H., Alexiadis, M., & Robertson, N.
title: Smoking Cue Reactivity and Smoking Motives in Patients with Early Phase Psychotic Disorder: A Behavioural Study
abstract:
Rates of tobacco smoking and unsuccessful smoking cessation attempts are high in individuals with psychosis.
Exposure to smoking-related cues reliably enhances cigarette cravings in dependent smokers. Smoking-related cues do
not affect smokers with chronic schizophrenia to the same degree as for non-psychotic smokers. However, less is known
about individuals in the early phase of psychotic (EPP) illness. Additionally, it is unknown whether the smoking motives of
EPP differ from those of non-psychiatric smokers. Cue-elicited cigarette cravings, and self-reported motives for smoking,
were compared between EPP individuals (n=21) and non-psychiatric participants (NC; n=40). EPP individuals were less
sensitive than NC to the cue-reactivity paradigm, suggesting individuals in the early phase of illness may not be affected
by tobacco cues to the same degree as non-psychiatric smokers. Further, smoking motives did not differ between EPP and
non-psychiatric smoker controls. Findings have implications for differences in smoking cessation interventions for these
two populations.

authors_raw: Mackenzie R. Hartley, Caden Whitfield, Pouria Vallapour Karimi, Tamara B. Franklin
title: The Impacts of Chronic Late Adolescent Stress on Adult Sociability in Mice
abstract:
Adolescents are more sensitive to stress than adults and experiencing stress during this developmental
period can have lasting effects on behaviour, including increased depression-like and anxiety-like behaviours. However,
its long-term impact on sociability has not been well studied. The present research aims to determine the effects of
chronic late adolescent stress on adult peer-to-peer sociability using male and female, stressed and control mice. Male
and female mice (n=48) were evenly split into stress and control groups. From post-natal day (PND) 48-60, we exposed
stressed mice to a chronic predator stress paradigm while controls were left undisturbed in their home cages. Once mice
reached adulthood (PND 96), we performed behavioural testing aimed at quantifying social dominance and social
interest in same-sex and opposite-sex peers. Results suggest that chronic adolescent stress may lead to increased
interest in same-sex peers and decreased social dominance. These findings could inform research on the lasting effects
of human childhood and adolescent chronic stressors such as unsafe home environments and mental health struggles.

title: 50 years in Psychology (and Neuroscience) at Dalhousie
abstract:
My time at Dalhousie coincides with the history of the Department's annual, and widely copied, In-House
Conference. I plan to entertain you with facts, figures and stories that cover three histories: the Department's, the In-
House's and mine.

authors_raw: Hope Lewis, Emily Wei, Mobina Ali, Aaron Newman
title: Changes in the N400 Event-Related Potential Found Over a Two Day Spanish Language Learning Study
abstract:
The N400 is an event-related potential (ERP) associated with semantic processing. Before training in an
unfamiliar language (UL), learners do not show this N400 effect when assessed with UL words, but such effects have
been shown to emerge at least after 10 days of training. To determine how much exposure to UL words are required for
new learners to show an N400 effect, the current study recorded EEG throughout a two-day UL training period. Spanish
words were taught by being paired with images associated with their meaning. Semantic mismatch tasks were used to
elicit ERPs in Spanish and in English. A significant N400 effect for trained Spanish words emerged at the end of day 2 of
training. This N400 effect was delayed and smaller in amplitude compared to English, perhaps reflecting a difference in
proficiency. Follow-up study will assess participants over a semester of language learning to determine longer term
development.

authors_raw: Yao Li1, Jorn Diedrichsen2, Carlos Hernandez-Castillo1
title: Cerebellar Isolation Combining Multi-Modality MRI Using Deep Learning
abstract:
The cerebellum is involved in motor, cognitive and social behavior, and understanding its development
across the lifespan aids in diagnosing neurological disorders. Accurate cerebellum isolation from whole-brain MRI scans
is a crucial first step for accurate cerebellar analysis. The current isolation tools have two limitations. First, they were
developed on healthy adults' brains. Second, most of them do not utilize multi-modality image inputs. This project
introduces a robust deep learning approach using a 3D U-Net architecture to isolate the cerebellum from multi-modal
MRI data (T1- and T2-weighted images). The dual-channel input enhances tissue differentiation by capturing spatial
features from both modalities. Evaluated using Dice Score and Hausdorff Distance, our model outperforms existing tools
in accuracy, particularly by reducing mislabeling of nearby structures. It also generalizes well across different age groups,
including neonates, addressing key limitations of previous methods and offering a more reliable solution for cerebellar
segmentation.

authors_raw: Sean P. Mackinnon & Andy J. Kim
title: Comparing Structural Equation Models to Network Analysis Models of Depression, Anxiety, and Perfectionism
abstract:
Background: We use network analysis to identify bridge symptoms connecting anxiety, depression and
perfectionism; that is, are there specific symptoms connecting these constructs? Moreover, we compare network
models to traditional structural equation models using latent variables to see how findings differ.
Methods: Two undergrad samples (N=774; N=759) completed online measures of anxiety, depression, and
perfectionism (concerns over mistakes, doubts about actions, & personal standards).
SEM Results: After controlling for other perfectionism variables, concern over mistakes and doubts about actions were
positively associated with depressive and anxiety symptoms. Personal standards were negatively associated with
depression and non-significantly related to anxiety.
Network Results: Four items emerged as bridge symptoms: difficulty taking initiative to do activities, feeling worthless,
feeling close to panic, and doubts about simple everyday activities.
Conclusions/Impact: Traditional multiple regression-style models are replicable but obscure item-specific relationships.
Using network models in place of reflective latent variable models may produce novel insights.

authors_raw: Madison B. MacLachlan, Dr. Drew Weatherhead
title: The Impact of Language Background and Prosocial Behaviours on Preschoolers' Affiliation Preference Towards Unfamiliar Accented Speakers
abstract:
Preschoolers prefer people who speak with the same accent as them (e.g., Kinzler, et. al., 2019) and who
behave kindly towards others (e.g., Van de Vondervoort & Hamlin, 2017). Recently, it has been found that a
preschoolers' language background (i.e., whether they are monolingual or multilingual) may impact these preferences.
Bilinguals are more flexible in their preferences based on language (e.g., Byers et al., 2017) and show more sensitivity to
kindness cues (Singh, 2020). The present study examined the impact of language background and social behaviours on
preschoolers' preferences towards animated characters with an unfamiliar accent. N= 158 preschoolers answered
questions regarding their preferences toward the group. Participants significantly preferred prosocial and neutral
characters over antisocial characters and bilinguals showed significantly greater affiliation preference towards the
accented characters compared to the monolinguals. This suggests that bilinguals may be more accepting socially of
speakers with accents different than their own.

authors_raw: Hannah Millar1,2, Broderick M. B. Parks2, Casandra Hayar2, Ainsley N. Monchesky2, Jason R. Treberg3, Debbie
title: Volumetric Plasticity of the Septum Across Age and Feeding Conditions in Domesticated Pigeons (Columba Livia Domestica)
abstract:
Domesticated pigeons (Columba livia domestica) are common models to investigate the interaction between
spatial memory and its underlying neural substrates, particularly the hippocampus. Relative to the hippocampus, much
less is known about the avian septum, another structure that has been linked to general spatial specialization in birds, is
highly interconnected with the hippocampus, and shows some evidence of neural plasticity. Here, I will present data
from two ongoing studies examining the effects of age, sex, and food deprivation history on neural plasticity in the
pigeon septum. The first study examined age-related changes in overall septum volume (as measured by Nissl staining)
in pigeons (N=30) ranging in age from 2-22 years old. The second study examined how food deprivation affected septum
volume in a separate sample (N=22) of adult (1-4 years) and aged (8-18 years) pigeons. While overall telencephalon
volume varied with both age and food condition, we found no effects on septum volume. We did find some evidence of
septum plasticity in response to food deprivation in adult, but not aged birds, however further methodological
refinements may be necessary to confirm this effect. Overall these findings suggest the septum may be resilient to
potentially detrimental effects of age and metabolic challenge.

title: The kitten breeding colony in the Psychology Department: the first fifty years.
abstract:
The establishment of a kitten breeding colony at Dal was a crucial initial step in order to study of the role that
early visual experience plays in the development of the mammalian central visual pathways of binocular species such as
humans. As described in a recent paper (https://doi.org/10.1523/ENEURO.0576-24.2025 ) it was also necessary to
develop a method to allow measurement of the changes in the perceptual abilities of kittens following different forms of
early visual exposure. In 1970 there were no commercial cat breeding colonies in Canada and we had not yet moved into
the LSB. I describe how and from where we acquired intact cats for the initiation of a breeding colony and the progressive
changes made in the ensuing years.

authors_raw: Nicholas E. Murray, Mah Parsa, Mohamed Muzamil, JianLi Wang, Sandra M. Meier
title: Objective Behavioural Comparison of Youth and Adult Anxiety: A Mobile Sensing Approach
abstract:
Widely used subjective measures of anxiety are limited by recall biases and an inability to capture real-time
fluctuations in anxiety-related behaviours. This study used the PROSIT app to examine behavioural indicators of anxiety
in 65 youth (15-21 years) and 96 adults (26-40 years) with clinically significant anxiety. Participants completed baseline
measures of mental health and used the app for 14 days, enabling passive sensing of mobility, sociability, physical
activity, screen use, and sleep, alongside daily self-reports. Features were extracted to predict outcomes related to
baseline and momentary anxiety. Notably, screen use predicted anxiety in youth only; greater mobility predicted greater
momentary anxiety variance in youth, and lesser variance in adults. These results- along with other findings to be
discussed - indicate that mobile sensing features can predict anxiety in youth and adults, and that there are measurable
differences in the expression of anxiety between youth and adults consistent with prior literature.

authors_raw: Perry, R.N., Tanner A., van Staden, F., O'Connor, G., & Barrett, S.P.
title: Menthol Enhances Sensory Reinforcement and Craving Reduction from Nicotine Inhalers
abstract:
Flavours, particularly menthol, may influence the reinforcing properties of electronic nicotine delivery systems
(ENDS), though findings in human users are mixed. We examined whether menthol alters reinforcement from nicotine
inhalers in adult ENDS users. Fifty-three ENDS users self-administered a 4mg nicotine inhaler paired with a menthol or
unflavoured inhaler across two within-subject sessions. Craving and withdrawal were assessed pre- and post-inhaler;
subjective effects (liking, wanting, stimulation, sensory effects) were rated post-inhaler. Craving and withdrawal
decreased pre- to post-inhaler in both flavour conditions (p<0.001). Planned comparisons between flavours showed lower
post-inhaler craving in the menthol condition (p=0.04). Menthol also elicited cooling sensory effects, improved the taste
of nicotine inhalers, and independently increased ratings of stimulation, liking, and wanting (p<.001). Thus, menthol may
enhance the sensory appeal and reinforcing effects of inhaled nicotine, including craving reduction. Findings support
regulatory concerns about flavoured ENDS while highlighting nuanced implications for cessation and harm reduction.

authors_raw: Peterson, J., Shephard A., Ethier-Gagnon M. A., Zhevoka, R., & Barrett S. P.
title: Examining Patterns of Substance Use Among Simultaneous Alcohol and Cannabis Co-Users
abstract:
Simultaneous co-use of alcohol and cannabis has been associated with greater substance use and dependence
relative to concurrent or single substance use. We examined use patterns among simultaneous co-users to identify factors
that may contribute to this elevated risk. Structured interviews (n = 200) were conducted in monthly alcohol-cannabis co-
users. General linear models were used to analyze differences in alcohol and cannabis dependence between concurrent
and simultaneous users, and to examine how specific use patterns influenced substance quantity among simultaneous
users. Relative to concurrent users, simultaneous users demonstrated more problematic alcohol and cannabis use. Among
simultaneous users, order in which alcohol and cannabis were administered impacted quantity used, such that the
substance administered first was used more in the session. Finally, we found that more alcohol was consumed in group
vs. solitary settings. Findings highlight key risk factors associated with simultaneous co-use and may inform targeted
psychoeducation and harm-reduction strategies.

authors_raw: Julia Byron, Anna MacGillivray, Laura Lambe and Ralph Redden
title: Standing up in the Virtual World: Observing Social-Cognitive Factors Influencing Peer Defending Behaviours Using Virtual Reality
abstract:
Bullying victimization is a serious threat to Canadian youth. Bullying most often occurs in the presence of
peer bystanders, who can intervene to defend their victimized peers. Although rare, peer defending is an effective way
to combat bullying, ending the bullying episode rapidly when a bystander intervenes. Past research has used passive
viewing methods (e.g. vignettes) to study factors associated with peer defending, limiting our understanding of how
social attention contributes to peer defending in real-time. The present study used a virtual reality (VR) paradigm to
examine how social attention is related to peer defending in a bullying scenario. Multi-method data were collected
during the VR task, including eye tracking data, state affect, and various defending behaviours in response to a social
exclusion scenario. Results indicate that there is a correlation between gaze allocation and some types of peer
defending, as well as a correlation between specific peer defending behaviours and negative affect during social
exclusion. Results from this study are among the first to demonstrate the social cognitive processes underlying peer
defending behaviour as it unfolds in real-time, showing that gaze allocation in dynamic social situations can be sensitive
to witnessing peer victimization.

title: Brains in Conversation: Science Communication and Public Impact
abstract:
This presentation explores the role of science communication in making neuroscience more accessible and
engaging to the public. Drawing on my involvement with outreach initiatives such as SURGE, Dalhousie Science Scholars
and Science Leaders Program (DSSLP), the Brain Repair Centre, and high school workshops, I highlight how interactive
demonstrations using EEG and brain-computer interface (BCI) technology can spark curiosity and enhance science
literacy among young people. As a neuroscience major with minors in entrepreneurship and education, I aim to bridge
the gap between complex scientific research and community understanding. The presentation also reflects on the
impact of hands-on activities and youth-centred programming in fostering future interest in neuroscience. These
experiences demonstrate how science communication can expand the reach of research, inspire young minds, and
foster stronger public engagement with neuroscience.

title: Using AI to Detect AI Use in Online Exams
abstract:
Unproctored online examinations are highly susceptible to covert artificial-intelligence (AI) use and peer
collaboration, undermining assessment integrity. Although manual review can uncover isolated incidents, its scalability
is prohibitive. To bridge this gap, I developed Zapper, a custom AI-based advanced data analysis tool that processes
textual student responses to flag question- and student-level signatures of AI usage and collaboration. For a class of
~300 students, Zapper reduces ~8,000 person-hours of manual inspection into a review of its outputs and student work
lasting only a few hours. Applied over three semesters in two distinct courses with close to 700 students in total, Zapper
consistently achieved high accuracy. By integrating advanced AI-detection capabilities into a scalable framework, Zapper
advances the integrity of unproctored online assessments.

authors_raw: Stewart, S. H., Zack, M., McGrath, D., Seale, I., Collins, P., Barrett, S. P., & Al-hamdani, M.
title: Cross-Priming of Stimulant Cravings by Gambling in Regular Stimulant Users
abstract:
Administration of addictive drugs can prime motivation to use other drugs with similar properties (i.e., "cross-
priming"; Barrett et al., 2006). Amphetamine administration increases urge to gamble in problem gamblers (Zack & Poulos,
2004). We tested whether motivation for stimulant use would be similarly cross primed by gambling in regular stimulant
users. Stimulant users (n=32) and n=31 controls (n=15-16 of each sex/group): engaged in lab-based video lottery terminal
gambling; reported gambling, stimulant, and alcohol craving pre- and post-gambling; and provided heart rate and blood
pressure samples at both timepoints. Gambling increased desire to gamble. While heart rate and systolic blood pressure
declined pre- to post-gambling in controls, they remained elevated post-gambling in stimulant users. Gambling also cross-
primed increased craving for stimulants and alcohol but only in the female stimulant users. Results bolster arguments that
problem gambling shares common mechanisms with psychostimulant addiction and suggest cross-priming may be
particularly important in females.

title: Improving Functional Connectivity Estimation in rs-fMRI for Biomarker Discovery Across Neurological Disorders
abstract:
Neurological disorders, caused by central and peripheral nervous system abnormalities, often impair brain
function. Functional connectivity (FC) form Resting-state functional MRI (rs-fMRI) is a promising tool for biomarker
discovery in neurological disorders. However, current methods to calculate FC may overlook subtle connectivity patterns
leading to weak predictive power. Using three different rs-fMRI datasets, we evaluate different FC estimation methods:

authors_raw: Tanner, A. B., Shephard, A. S., Barrett, S. P., Sherry, S., & Stewart, S. H.
title: Cannabidiol Expectancy Attenuates Users' Anxiety Response to Cannabis Cues
abstract:
Cannabidiol (CBD) dampens psychophysiological arousal and substance craving; however, the extent to
which these effects are attributable to placebo effects remains unclear. CBD expectancy alone has been shown to elicit
anxiolysis and attenuate cue-induced cannabis craving. We examined whether CBD expectancy influences stress or
anxiety responses to cannabis-related cues. Forty-six cannabis users self-administered a vaporizer containing propylene
glycol; half were falsely informed that the vaporizer contained CBD. Participants then viewed neutral and cannabis-
related cues. Stress and anxiety were assessed at baseline, following vaporizer administration, and after each cue.
Participants who believed they received CBD showed significant reductions in anxiety following drug (but not neutral)
cue exposure compared to baseline, while those who believed they received placebo maintained higher anxiety ratings
post-drug cue. Findings suggest CBD expectancy alone is sufficient to dampen anxiety to cannabis-related cues,
underscoring the need to control for expectancy effects in clinical research investigating CBD's therapeutic efficacy.

authors_raw: Lucas Tennen, BA1, Nick Murphy, BSc2, Samantha Horne, BSc1, Julia DuJohn1, Joy Boyce, BSc (OT)3, Sherri
title: Developing an Online Treatment Approach for Spatial Neglect Using a Single Case Design
abstract:
Spatial neglect, a common consequence of right hemisphere stroke, leads to attentional deficits and poor
rehabilitation outcomes. While visual scanning treatment (VST) is a potential intervention, its effectiveness typically
depends on therapist-guided sessions exceeding 15 hours, posing challenges in resource-limited settings. SpyTrain, an
online VST program, offers intensive, adaptive, game-like scanning practice and has shown validity in healthy controls
(Aziz et al., 2021), and early efficacy in stroke patients. This study is assessing the feasibility and effectiveness of SpyTrain
for treating spatial neglect, focusing on the application of strategies. Patients with right hemisphere stroke and neglect
are randomized to three different baseline lengths using a non-concurrent multiple baseline single-case design, followed
by 5 weeks of online VST (5 sessions/week). Outcomes will are measured across phases and analyzed using Tau-U, a
nonparametric test assessing improvement trends. If effective, SpyTrain could offer an accessible, home-based
treatment to support recovery and independence in stroke survivors with neglect.
POSTER ABSTRACTS
All Posters Will Be Presented From 12:30 pm to 2 pm in the Lilyan E. White Study Centre on the 4th floor.

authors_raw: Mohammed Ali Ahmed, Simone Shaw-Litzgus, Wyatt Ortibus, and Richard E. Brown
title: Age-Related Changes in Olfactory Discrimination and Reversal Learning Performance in Neurexin1 +/- Transgenic Mice
abstract:
Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder marked by challenges in social
communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviours. Nrxn1 is a presynaptic cell adhesion protein that interacts
with postsynaptic neuroligins to regulate synaptic signalling. Nrxn1 is vital for learning and memory and has been
associated with ASD. Nrxn1+/- transgenic mice have a knockdown of the α, β, and γ isoforms, leading to impaired synaptic
transmission. Our study examined whether age (young vs. old) and sex (male vs. female) affected performance of
Nrxn1+/- mice in olfactory discrimination and reversal learning tasks compared to C57BL/6J wildtype controls. Results
showed that old mice had a significantly lower completion rate in the learning tasks, compared to young mice. Among the
old mice, Nrxn1+/− mice had a lower completion rate than wildtype mice. The old mice had a significantly higher number
of errors to the 85% criterion than the young mice in an initial discrimination task. These results suggest that as mice age,
they face impairments in cognitive function and flexibility, indicating that this procedure may be used as a test for novel
drugs which might increase cognitive function during aging.

authors_raw: Isabel F. Atwell, Nicole M. Michaud, Isabel F. Atwell, Daisy J. Gorski, Mark R. ten Haaf, Laura C. Rezende, Prem
title: Does Inhibiting Parvalbumin-Expressing Interneurons in Visual Cortex Alter Performance on a Perceptual Contrast Detection Task in Mice?
abstract:
The application of optogenetic techniques in mouse primary visual cortex (V1) has allowed us to probe the
roles for specific neuron subtypes within the neural circuits underlying sensory processing. Past research has identified
inhibitory interneurons expressing parvalbumin (Pvalb+), somatostatin (SOM+) and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP+) as
members of an inhibitory neural circuit that modulates excitatory pyramidal cell activity. We recently found that SOM+
inactivation alters perceptual contrast detection in a manner consistent with a visual phosphene effect, likely caused by
release from inhibition of pyramidal cells. In this study we investigate whether Pvalb+ inactivation produced similar
perceptual deficits using the same perceptual task. We show for the first time that Pvalb+ inactivation impairs
performance on a contrast detection task.

authors_raw: Simon B. Sherry; Aislin R. Mushquash; Christopher J. Mushquash; Daniel S. McGrath; Sherry H. Stewart
title: Characterizing Problematic Internet and Social Media Use Among Young Adults
abstract:
The Substance Use Risk Profile Scale (SURPS) is a measure that is used to select youth for personality-targeted
interventions (i.e., PreVenture) that have been found to prevent or reduce risky substance use in young people. We tested
whether the SURPS impulsivity subscale would predict changes in binge drinking and binge eating over time.
Undergraduates (N = 410, M age = 20.7 years, SD = 5.5, women n = 307, men, n = 103) from two universities completed
a longitudinal study with two weeks separating baseline and follow-up. At baseline, participants completed the SURPS
impulsivity subscale and binge drinking and binge eating measures. At follow up, participants completed the measures
again. Linear regression analyses showed that SURPS impulsivity (B = .12, p = .02) significantly predicted binge drinking
and binge eating at follow up. Findings reveal that the SURPS impulsivity subscale is a useful tool in predicting increases
in binge behaviour.

authors_raw: Mya Dockrill (Dalhousie University), Brendan Flores (Universite of Montreal), Youssouf Aidara (Universite of
title: Sleep Disparities in Canadian Children under 18 Years: A Scoping Review
abstract:
Insufficient sleep is prevalent in children and has many adverse health outcomes. Children facing health
inequities (e.g., from a low socioeconomic background, or a racial/ethnic minority) have more sleep difficulties and are
less likely to receive treatment. There is a lack of research investigating pediatric sleep and health inequities, particularly
in Canada. Thus, the objective was to summarize the extant literature in this area. A list of health inequities was developed
following the Canadian Institute of Health Research EDI dimensions and in consultation with the Canadian Sleep Research
Consortium. Four key electronic databases (PubMed, EMBASE, PsycInfo, CINAHL) were searched. Studies were included if
they were 1) in English or French, 2) original studies (e.g., empirical data collected), 3) conducted in Canada, 4) with
children under 18 years, 5) population-based studies, and 6) reported on sleep outcomes and health inequities. Three
reviewers screened 15,448 abstracts and titles resulting in 357 articles full-text articles. After screening, 25 studies were
included in this review, focusing on sleep in the context of low socioeconomic status (18 studies), gender/sex (17 studies),
family composition/caretaking (9 studies), housing/neighbourhood characteristics (8 studies), race/ethnicity (8 studies),
immigrants/refugees (3 studies), and language (1 study). Of note, no distinction was made between sex and gender in the
literature. Much of the research has been done with Caucasian, adolescents and school-aged children, in Quebec or
Ontario. Generally, it appears that sleep health and disordered sleep are negatively affected by low socioeconomic status,
poor housing/neighbourhood conditions, and in non-Caucasian children. The findings are varied in terms of sex differences
with most studies suggesting no differences (76%) and some suggesting differences between boys and girls with 67% of
the literature suggesting that boys sleep better. There is no clear link between sleep disturbances and family composition
and caretaking. This is the first review to investigate the impact of health inequities on sleep in Canadian children.
Identifying and understanding this link will inform the modification and development of sleep interventions to better meet
the needs of these diverse individuals.

title: The Canadian Settler Guilt Scale: Measuring Settler Guilt in a Canadian Population
abstract:
Indigenous peoples in Canada have endured significant negative impacts stemming from the colonization of
North America in the 1700s and cultural assimilation efforts by European colonizers. Research in Australia has
demonstrated that some individuals of European ancestry feel guilt about the injustices and maltreatment that Indigenous
populations have endured. This type of guilt, referred to as settler guilt, may be an important avenue towards
reconciliation efforts between Indigenous and non-Indigenous populations. However, no research to our knowledge exists
on the topic of settler guilt in Canada. To address this issue, the newly designed Canadian Settler Guilt Scale was
administered to a sample of undergraduate university students at Dalhousie University to measure settler guilt in a
Canadian population. The scale demonstrated strong construct validity and internal consistency through exploratory
factor analysis and Cronbach's alpha calculations, and differences in settler guilt scores were observed across various
demographic variables providing a strong foundation for future research on the topic.

authors_raw: Ana Faustova, Wyatt Ortibus, Mohammed Ali Ahmed, Salma Ismail, Ian Weaver, and Richard E. Brown
title: Seizure Symptoms in Neurexin-1 Knockdown and "Rescue" Mice with Slice Site 5 Modification: Determining the Relationship of Age, Genotype and Sex Using the Brown Lab Scale and KCNQ-1 Expression
abstract:
The Neurexins (NRXNs) are a family of membrane protein synaptic organizers encoded by the Nrxn genes.
Nrxn-1 mutations are associated with Autism Spectrum Disorder and Schizophrenia. We recorded seizures in male and
female mouse models with a knockout of one Nrxn1 gene (Nrxn1+/- mice), wildtype C57Bl/6J controls (Nrxn1+/+), a_n_d_
_N_r_x_n_1_ _"r_e_s_c_u_e_" _m_o_d_e_l_ _w_i_t_h_ _a_ _s_p_l_i_c_e_ _s_i_t_e_ _5_ _a_l_t_e_r_a_t_i_o_n_ _(DS5/-
and DS5/DS5 mice) (Lu, H., et al., 2023. Cell Reports, 42: 112714; Lu, H., et al. Molecular Psychiatry, 15 April 2025). The
DS5 alteration increases NRXN-1 interaction with presynaptic ligands, counteracting the knockdown behavioral phenotype.
Mice carrying the DS5 recovery were more likely to exhibit age-related seizures compared to Nrxn +/+ and Nrxn +/- mice
(48% DS5, 31% Nrxn1+/-, 25% Nrxn1+/+). There were no sex differences, but seizure risk increased in micee with age (17.5
months). No Mendelian heritability pattern was uncovered. The epigenetic analysis showed a complex interaction
between seizures, KCNQ-1, and genotype. This mouse model therefore may be important for the study of age-related
spontaneous seizures.

authors_raw: Fernandes, O., Snooks, T., Stewart, S. H., Yunus, F., Moore, M., Keough, M., Krank, M., Thompson, K., &
title: Personality and Emotional Expression Across Languages: Comparing French and English Emerging Adults During COVID-19
abstract:
This study examined how personality traits influence emotional expression in written narratives about COVID-
19 pandemic impacts among French-Canadian undergraduates and whether these associations differ from those
previously documented in English Canadian undergraduates. Drawing on Ekman's Basic Emotions Theory and Plutchik's
Emotion Wheel, which posit universal emotional responses, we hypothesized that associations between personality traits
and emotional word use would replicate cross-linguistically. Participants (N=1185; ages 18-25) from 5 Canadian
universities (1 French-speaking; N=122) completed a free-response prompt describing the impact of the COVID-19
pandemic. Using the Substance Use Risk Profile Scale, we explored relationships between four traits, Anxiety Sensitivity,

authors_raw: Brett Feltmate, Paige Dobson, Mia Bartlett, Alyssa Dober, Elise Windybank, Ray Klein
title: Is the Nature of the Vigilance Decrement Affected by Target Probability?
abstract:
In tasks requiring vigilant detection of rare and noisy signals, humans ubiquitously commit more errors as time
progresses. A consistent theme throughout the literature is how best to characterize this "vigilance" decrement: is it a
decline in one's ability to discriminate signals, or a conservative shift in their criterion to respond? Given that both accounts
have empirical support, it is more likely that task factors moderate how the vigilance decrement is felt. We explored if/how
people's ability to discriminate signals, and their bias in categorizing them as such, are differentially sensitive to target
likelihood. Participants signalled whether a central line was sufficiently offset from those flanking it, which occurred on
(between-Ss) 25/50/75% of trials (900/ participant). Results hint toward discriminability and response biases dissociating
over time, as target likelihood approaches either limit.

authors_raw: Sarah M. Haggett, Christine L. Lackner
title: Heightened Error-Monitoring Following Childhood Adversity: An ERP Investigation of the ERN
abstract:
Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) are linked to disruptions in brain regions involved in self-regulation and
performance monitoring. The error-related negativity (ERN) is a neural marker of error awareness. Although self-
regulation is associated with ERN amplitude, findings on the ACE-ERN relationship are inconsistent, possibly due to
simplified ACE measures. This study is the first to examine four ACE dimensions - type, timing, extent, and subjective
evaluation - in relation to ERN. One hundred young adults will complete ACE questionnaires and perform four Go/NoGo
tasks during EEG recording. Tasks will vary in emotional salience (neutral, emotional) and sensory modality (auditory,
visual). We hypothesize that ACE extent and subjective severity will negatively correlate with ERN across tasks. The timing-
ERN relationship will depend on age at exposure. ACE type effects will depend on task salience, with greater effects during
emotional tasks, and on sensory modality, with more negative ERNs when modality aligns with experienced ACEs.

title: Leveraging Speech Analysis for Cognitive Load Measurement in Young Adults: A Sex-Based Comparative Study
abstract:
This study explored how cognitive load impacts speech production and whether sex or baseline fatigue
moderates this relationship. Thirty young adults completed low and high cognitive load speech tasks, which involved
spontaneous speech and dual-task conditions with arithmetic and verbal fluency challenges. Acoustic (e.g., speech rate,
pitch) and disfluency features (e.g., filled/silent pauses) were analyzed using PRAAT and LIWC, while perceived load was
assessed with the NASA-TLX. Results showed significant decreases in speech rate and increases in filled pauses under
higher cognitive load. Participants with higher fatigue scores exhibited greater changes in speech rate and pitch
modulation. These findings support the use of speech as a non-invasive marker of cognitive load and point toward
potential applications in workload monitoring across clinical, educational, and occupational settings.

authors_raw: Inness, B., Montalvo, A., Mason, L., Khoury, J., Stewart, S. H., & Meier, S.
title: Longitudinal Effects of Parental Smartphone Usage on Child Socioemotional Wellbeing Proposal
abstract:
Parental connection and responsiveness have been well-established to play a central role in children's social
wellbeing and emotional health. However, integrating smartphone technology into daily life has caused concern about
how these devices might significantly interrupt parental responsiveness. Research has found differences in parenting
practices due to smartphone use, consequently impacting children's socioemotional health. There are a variety of
mechanisms that might explain these impacts, however, the details have yet to be identified. This study will investigate
the two primary postulated mechanisms: "technoference", the interruption of interactions with children by a smartphone,
and "immersion", the absorption of parents' attention by the smartphone. Using a multimodal longitudinal design, 150
parent dyads with 12 to 18-month-olds will complete online questionnaires, download a mobile sensing app, and be
observed interacting with their child. It is hypothesized that heavy parental smartphone use, objectively recorded via
mobile sensing, will predict poor parental responsiveness and child socioemotional development over time.
Technoference and immersion are expected to mediate the association between smartphone use, parental
responsiveness, and child socioemotional wellbeing. This study aims to inform parents, policymakers, clinicians, and
researchers on how and when parental smartphone use impacts child socioemotional development.

authors_raw: Salma Ismail, William Grendon and Richard E. Brown
title: Effects of Age on Motor Coordination and Motor Learning in Mouse Models of Autism
abstract:
Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder that impairs social-emotional behaviour,
motor coordination, visual-spatial learning and memory. Approximately 2.5% of the human adult population has ASD and
adults with ASD have a reduced lifespan, increased frailty and motor gait impairments. The present study assessed motor
coordination and motor learning using the Rotarod in 21-month-old male and female Nrxn1+/- transgenic mice; wildtype
(C57BL/6J) controls; knockdown rescue ΔS5- ; and double rescue ΔS5/ΔS5 mice. The mice were placed in one of the 4
compartments of the Rotarod, which accelerated to a maximum of 36 rpm and the latency to fall was recorded. There
were 6 trials per day over 7 days and body weight was taken prior to testing each day. Performance improved for all mice
over days, however, there was a significant negative correlation between motor scores and body weight (r = -0.43, df= 97,
p<0.001) with lighter mice performing better than heavier mice. Females performed better than males, but improvement
in performance over days was greater in males. Female Ds5/- mice performed better than female WT, Nrxn1+/- and
DS5/DS5 mice, but there were no genotype differences in male mice. At 2 and 9 month of age, female mice outperformed
males but there were no differences between Nrxn1+/- and WT mice. There results indicate that there are no age-related
deficits in motor learning and coordination in these autistic mouse models.

title: Investigation of Astrocyte Activation in the Visual Cortex Following Monocular Retinal Inactivation
abstract:
Early discordant vision can cause amblyopia, a lasting impairment usually affecting one eye. In animal models
of amblyopia, inactivating the dominant retina with tetrodotoxin elicits better recovery than conventional patching
treatment. Despite inactivating the retina, connectivity within the visual system is preserved. Astrocyte activation may
support this preservation through homeostatic mechanisms. Our lab previously found elevated glial fibrillary acidic protein
(GFAP), a marker of astrocyte activation, in layers of the lateral geniculate nucleus serving the inactivated eye. The current
study investigated whether astrocyte activation also occurs in the visual cortex (V1). We compared GFAP labeling between
control animals and those subjected to 10 days of MI. While young animals showed no difference, older animals exhibited
significantly reduced GFAP after MI. We propose that this decrease in GFAP reflects an upstream synaptic compensation
in response to inactivation-induced changes elicited within the lateral geniculate nucleus.

authors_raw: Longino, Kirsty (presenter); Dr. Giazitzidou, Sophia; Hoferek, Katie; Dr. Deacon, Hélène.
title: Exploring How to Assess Orthographic Learning in Grade 1
abstract:
Orthographic learning refers to children's skill in learning the spelling of novel words through their
independent reading, and it has been described as central to reading development. Measuring this skill in young readers
is challenging, as it requires--by definition--independent reading. The aim of this pilot study was to develop an
appropriate task to assess orthographic learning in English-speaking children in Grade 1. As in prior studies, we asked
students to read 12 stories that contained made-up inventions. The first method required students to read independently.
25% of students could not accomplish this due to limited reading ability and loss of assent associated with reading difficulty.
The second method shifted to shared reading for those who did not have the necessary reading skills, but had them decode
the target words independently. This enabled all students to participate, with only one case of lost assent. In both methods,
students were assessed immediately following the stories, and the following day, on the spelling and meaning of the
inventions. Results showed that only independent reading demonstrated reliability (Cronbach's alpha >.7) while
highlighting the importance of multiple methods to ensure full participation.

authors_raw: MacDougall, Erin (presenter); Elgendi, Mariam; Dr. Stewart, Sherry; Dr. Deacon, Hélène
title: Does Hazardous Drinking in First Year University Students Predict Academic Performance into Their Fourth Year? A Study of Students with and Without a History of Reading Difficulties
abstract:
Background: Both problematic drinking and a history of reading difficulties have been associated with
academic challenges in university students. Here we build on these two bodies of work to examine the longitudinal effects
of alcohol use on academic performance in a study comparing students with and without a self-reported history of reading
difficulties (HRD and NRD, respectively). Methods: We report on a four-year longitudinal study following 93 university
students' (46 HRD, 47 NRD) GPA in Year 1 and Year 4. In Year 1, students completed online questionnaires assessing both
hazardous drinking and a self-reported history of reading difficulties. Results: Linear regression analyses showed that
hazardous drinking was negatively associated with changes in GPA from Year 1 to Year 4, for both students with a without
a history of reading difficulties. Follow-up analyses identified drinking quantity as the primary contributor. Discussion:
Overall, findings emphasize the need for university-based interventions targeting drinking behaviour to support academic
outcomes, regardless of reading history.

title: Decision Making in Mice: A Detailed Analysis of Behaviour in Mice Navigating a Visual Discrimination Task
abstract:
Behavioural studies using mouse models commonly involve visual stimuli, even though mice are not known to
be visually guided animals. Wong & Brown 2006, Genes Brain and Behaviour 5, 389-403 examined the ability of different
strains of mice to discriminate between visual stimuli in a forced-choice swim task. How mice make decisions while
learning this type of task remains under-researched. We analyzed swim paths to determine decision-making tactics in the
visual water box. We recorded 1) correct decision-making, 2) errors, 3) early error correction, 4) late error correction, 5)
vicarious trial and error (VTE), and 6) thigmotaxis in 12 strains of mice with varying visual abilities. Using digitized footage,
we coded the swim paths and compared the time taken to complete correct paths between visual ability groups. Mice
were scored based on their search strategies and the time taken to complete the correct path. We found that visually
impaired mice relied more on non-visual search strategies and made more error paths than those with good vision, who
were likelier to use VTE and early error corrections. These findings show that mice with differing visual abilities use
different search strategies. Mice with good vision showed longer times for VTE and error paths, which suggests more
deliberative strategies. Learning trends varied by visual ability across trials. Despite limitations due to video quality and
sample size, these results provide more information on how search strategies of mice differ based on visual acuity.

authors_raw: Can Sozuer, Vikas Pandey, Yoshiyuki Kubota, Kazue Semba
title: Compartmental Modeling of the Whole-Cell Activity of Wake-Promoting Orexin Neurons in Response to the Sleep Deprivation-Induced Astrocytic Inhibition of Their Excitatory Inputs
abstract:
Electron microscopy and electrophysiology revealed that six-hour sleep deprivation induces astrocyte
withdrawal from perisynaptic regions and presynaptic inhibition at excitatory synapses onto wake-promoting orexin
neurons (Briggs et al., 2018, JNeurosci; Semba et al., 2023, SfN). Computational synaptic modeling demonstrated that
astrocyte withdrawal drives presynaptic inhibition by modulating glutamate uptake. Although these alterations may
homeostatically and substantially attenuate orexin neuron firing, their functional consequences remain unclear. To bridge
synaptic changes and neuronal output, we developed the first detailed conductance-based compartmental model of an
orexin neuron in our NEURON simulation environment, replicating D- and H-type firing from intrinsic membrane channel
and receptor mechanisms. We stimulated this model with excitatory post-synaptic currents reflecting varying degrees of
astrocyte withdrawal--holding other parameters constant and incorporating additional sleep-deprivation-induced
physiological changes. By linking astrocyte withdrawal to orexin neuron firing, we extend synaptic-level observations into
a detailed multiscale integrative framework, illuminating how nanoscale synaptic alterations may potentially impact
wakefulness.
ten Haaf, Mark
POSTER TITLE: Optogenetic Activation of Somatostatin Expressing Interneurons in V1 Impairs Size Perception in Transgenic
Mice
AUTHORS: ten Haaf, M.R., Michaud, N.M., Atwell, I.F., Dang, L.T.J., Gorski, D.J., Rezende, L.C., Singh, P.K., Zeisberger, L.J.,

authors_raw: Caden Whitfield, Tasia MacLean, Ewa Adedeji, Mackenzie R. Hartley, Tamara B. Franklin
title: The Impact of Communal Nesting on Adolescent Stress-Induced Intergenerational Effects in Male and Female Mice
abstract:
Chronic stress during the adolescent period has proven to be impactful to mental health and behaviour
throughout adulthood. Previous research in our lab has demonstrated that chronic adolescent stress in mice induced
behaviour similar to postpartum depression, with the dams showing reduced motivation and providing less maternal care
to their pups. Altered behaviour states during the postpartum period impact the pups' behaviour in adulthood, with pups
demonstrating increased anxiety-like behaviour. The current research focuses on assessing interventions to help counter
these intergenerational effects by investigating the potential of communal nesting as a model for social support in the
postpartum period. F0 generation mice undergo a predator stress protocol daily over the course of 12 days during the
early adolescent period to mimic the experiences of chronic adolescent psychological stress. These mice are bred in same-
treatment pairings and once pups are born, are either placed alone in standard housing or housing with another dam and
their pups. As adults, the offspring were tested in a battery of tests aimed at assessing exploration, anxiety-like behaviours
and depressive-like behaviours: olfactory habituation/dishabituation, open field, sucrose splash test and the elevated plus
maze. This study is ongoing, however preliminary results from this behavioural testing suggest that communal nesting can
modulate some, but not all of the effects of ancestral stress.

authors_raw: Drew Weatherhead: drew.weatherhead@dal.ca, Dalhousie University, Emma Reeves: em831170@dal.ca,
title: Comparing Attitudes Toward Diverse and Homogeneous Groups Varying in Race, Accent, and Sex/Gender Among Canadian Primary-School-Aged Children
abstract:
Children in Canada are learning language and interacting with others in far more socially (e.g., race and
ethnicity) and linguistically (e.g., languages and accents) diverse environments than ever before. We explored children's
attitudes toward diverse and homogeneous social groups using ecologically valid methods that reflect the real-world
contexts in which diversity is experienced. Data collection is ongoing. Fifty-five Canadian children, aged 5 to 8 years (males
n = 30, females n = 25, M age = 7.8 years), completed three blocks of trials varying by social dimension: sex/gender, race,
or accent. On each trial, participants were presented with cartoon images of groups that differed according to the social
dimension being evaluated within each block: one group was homogeneous, and the other was diverse. Participants were
then asked to rate how much they liked each group using a Likert-response scale consisting of emoticons. Wilcoxon Signed-
Rank tests will be used to determine whether responses differ significantly between the two types of stimuli (e.g., diverse
vs. homogenous groups) and across age groups. Findings can have implications for educational practice, policy-making,
and early interventions that promote acceptance and support for diversity.

authors_raw: S. Deniz Yildirim, Luke Mungall, Julie Blais
title: Psychopathic Traits and Cooperation: The Role of Punishment Risk in Social Decision-Making
abstract:
Cooperation is integral to a functioning society, but people higher in psychopathic traits (e.g., showing callous
affect, manipulativeness, impulsivity, antisocial traits) may be unwilling to cooperate and consistently violate social norms.
The current study investigated the extent to which cooperative decision-making is influenced by psychopathy scores and
punishment risk, as measured through three hypothetical Public Goods Game (PGG) scenarios with varying certainties of
(monetary) punishment risk (i.e., low-, moderate-, and high-risk). The sample consisted of 455 undergraduate students,
recruited through SONA. Participants completed the three hypothetical PGG scenarios and a multifaceted psychopathy
measure: the Self-Report Psychopathy Scale - Short Form (SRP-SF). As hypothesized, participants contributed more points
in conditions that offered increased risk of punishment. However, total psychopathy scores did not significantly predict
lower contributions averaged across the three PGG conditions. Higher psychopathy scores were also not associated with
the difference in contributions between low- and high-risk conditions, nor between the low- and moderate-risk conditions.
Nonetheless, higher psychopathy scores, specifically on the Erratic Lifestyle facet, were correlated with lower
contributions in the moderate-risk condition. Our findings highlight the complex nature of psychopathy and suggest that
psychopathy does not uniformly predict cooperative behaviour but may be influenced by the likelihood of punishment as
well as certain psychopathy facets.

title: Remembering Kailey Simon (2000-2025)
abstract: Kailey Simon was from Elsipogtog First Nation in New Brunswick. She was a student in our program, working toward her BA in Psychology with a Certificate in Indigenous Studies. Her involvement at Dal spanned widely: She was a TA for Indigenous Studies, an RA on a project with Math & Stats and Oceanography, and worked with the Office of Sustainability, contributing art that you'll find on signage for the pollinator garden behind our building. Kailey was a persistent student who took intellectual ownership of her work in ways I've rarely seen, and who never shied away from a challenge in pursuit of her academic goals. Her peers in the Indigenous Student Society memorialized Kailey saying, "[s]he brought light, laughter, and care into our community … Kailey was so proud to be L'nu, and her confidence in who she was helped many others feel comfortable in their own skin. Her legacy will live on in everyone who had the privilege of knowing her." I had the privilege of working with Kailey for three years, across contexts. In this talk I'll introduce you to Kailey as I knew her, drawing from my experience watching her navigate my classes and our undergraduate program.

authors_raw: Julie Blais, Scott Pruysers, and Luke Mungall
title: Personalized Persuasion? Testing the Efficacy of Personality-Targeted Political Advertisements
abstract: Before we can evaluate the harm that psychographic microtargeting may entail, we must first empirically examine whether political advertising can be tailored to resonate with people at the level of their personality. In Study 1, we conducted a survey among personality experts (N = 86) to evaluate the face validity of real-world targeted ads used for a US midterm election. In Study 2, we conducted a pre- registered between-subjects experimental design with a nationally representative sample of over 2,000 US citizens to assess whether advertisements that match participants' personality traits are more effective (i.e., vote intention, likability, etc.). Study 1 indicated that a majority (63%) of experts in personality psychology were unable to correctly identify the Big Five trait that was targeted in the video. In Study 2, there were no significant aggregate effects of the matching variable, once controlling for participant personality. However, a classification tree analysis did identify a small sub-group of participants where matched ads had higher efficacy. While concerns about psychological voter manipulation are widespread, the results of these studies suggest that such fears are likely overstated. However, even small and subtle effects when scaled to tens of millions of voters may influence election outcomes. In-House Conference | Friday, May 15, 2026 5 RETURN TO SESSION A SCHEDULE

authors_raw: Isabella Bossom, Sarah Enouy, Julie Blais, Michael Wohl, Scott Pruysers, and Nassim Tabri
title: Explaining Unique and Common Variance in Financially Focused Self-Concept from Dark Triad and Honesty-Humility Personality Traits Using Commonality Analyses
abstract: We investigated the personality correlates of financially focused self-concept, a construct describing individuals who overemphasize financial success as central to their self-definition and self-worth. Although some of the consequences of a financially focused self-concept have been studied, little is known about the personality characteristic of financially focused people. To address this gap, we examined the unique and shared associations between financially focused self- concept and the Dark Triad traits (Machiavellianism, psychopathy, and narcissism) as well as Honesty-Humility at the facet level to provide greater specificity than trait- level analysis. Using cross-sectional data from 1,725 Canadian adults who completed validated self-report measures, we conducted commonality analyses. Results revealed significant associations between financially focused self-concept and Machiavellianism facets (antagonism, planfulness), all psychopathy facets, narcissistic vulnerability, and lower levels of Honesty-Humility (greed-avoidance and modesty). These findings enhance understanding of the personality profiles of individuals who prioritize financial success excessively.

authors_raw: Christopher Park, Chris Giacomantonio, and Lori Wozney
title: The Emotional Demands of Civilian Crisis Work: Exploring Burnout, Role Clarity, and Support in Civilian Crisis Intervention Teams
abstract: Civilian Crisis Intervention Teams (CCITs) are rapidly expanding as alternatives to police-led mental health crisis response across Canada and the United States, yet little is known about responder well-being. Grounded in the Job Demands-Resources (JD-R) framework, this study examined burnout, perceived organizational support (POS), and role clarity during police collaboration among CCIT responders. Participants (N = 50) were current or recent civilian crisis responders who completed an anonymous online survey including the Copenhagen Burnout Inventory, the Perceived Organizational Support Scale-10, the Brief Role Clarity in Police Collaboration Scale, and demographic items. Descriptive statistics, reliability analyses, Pearson correlations, and hierarchical multiple regression were conducted. Responders reported moderate-to-high burnout, with the highest levels in personal and work-related domains. POS was significantly and negatively associated with burnout and remained a significant predictor in a model explaining 28.6% of burnout variance, even after accounting for years of experience. In contrast, role clarity did not significantly predict burnout, despite being positively associated with POS. These findings suggest POS may be a central factor in understanding burnout among CCIT responders. As CCIT's continue to expand, strengthening POS may be critical for sustaining CCIT models. In-House Conference | Friday, May 15, 2026 6 RETURN TO SESSION A SCHEDULE

authors_raw: Sasha Stephen, Sakshi Walia, and Sean P. Mackinnon
title: Experimental Manipulations to Improve Participant Comprehension of Informed Consent Forms
abstract: Though informed consent is a critical part of research ethics, research suggests that comprehension is low. The present study explores methods to improve the comprehension of consent forms via supplementary quizzes and videos. Using an online survey, undergraduates (n = 533) were given a standard consent form. Each participant was randomized to one of 4 conditions: (a) a control condition with standard consent; (b) adding a practice quiz; (c) watching a summary video with a male or (d) female actor. The primary outcome was scores on a multiple-choice quiz of facts related to the consent form. A secondary outcome was whether participants chose to share their data in a repository. Both video conditions were superior to control, with the male video condition having the best comprehension. The quiz condition was not statistically different from control. Though comprehension on most questions was good (~70-80%), understanding of open-access repositories was generally poor (~40%). Despite poor understanding, data sharing in repositories was high (~94%) and there were no differences by condition. Using video summaries following a written consent form can improve comprehension of the consent form's content. However, more work needs to be done to improve participants comprehension of broad consent. In-House Conference | Friday, May 15, 2026 7 RETURN TO SESSION B SCHEDULE

title: Experimental Investigations of Smartphone Use and Attention
abstract: A review of research and literature on the interaction between smartphone use and attention. Includes an analysis of present limitations in the field when aiming to assess causality. Review of most commonly practiced experimental designs on the subject: notification, mere presence (brain drain), acute use, prolonged use. Results are interpreted using a contemporary taxonomy of attention (Klein, 2022), and suggest a negative influence on endogenously allocated attention and executive control. Consideration of future directions concludes the review.

authors_raw: Reyhana Wilson, Heather Neyedli, and Cory Munroe
title: The Effects of Acute Short-Form Video Viewing on Go/No-Go Performance
abstract: Short-form videos (SFVs), typically lasting from a few seconds to a few minutes, have become increasingly popular across social media platforms. Although SFV use has been associated with poorer cognition, particularly inhibitory control and attention (Nguyen et al., 2025), experimental evidence testing its acute effects remains limited. This study examined whether brief SFV viewing, relative to a reading control, impairs Go/No-Go task performance, which requires response inhibition and sustained attention. Using a counterbalanced crossover design, participants (N = 36, Mage = 18.6, SD = 1.38) completed two 15-minute smartphone-based conditions: SFV viewing and reading. Immediately after each condition, participants completed a Go/No-Go task and self-report questionnaires. Preliminary analyses showed that participants reported an average of 31.8 hours (SD = 15.05) of smartphone use per week, with 44% of this time spent viewing SFVs (M = 14.07, SD = 9.98). The most viewed SFV genres included entertainment, music, people/blogs, comedy and news/politics. Our sample reported substantial SFV viewing, highlighting the popularity of this form of media. The effects of acute SFV viewing on Go/No-Go performance will also be presented. In-House Conference | Friday, May 15, 2026 8 RETURN TO SESSION B SCHEDULE

authors_raw: Kyra Battist, Veda Haggarty, Julia Byron, and Ralph Redden
title: From Bonding to Focus: Examining the Relationship Between Attachment Styles and Networks of Attention
abstract: Relationship attachment style has been found to influence performance associated with two of the three networks of attention. Attachment avoidance has been shown to influence the executive network of attention, wherein individuals higher in attachment avoidance show reduced interference in the standard Eriksen Flanker task. Attachment anxiety has been shown to influence the executive network as well, in addition to the orienting network of attention, wherein individuals higher in attachment anxiety show larger endogenous cueing effects. The current study was designed to replicate these prior findings, and to examine the heretofore untested relationship between attachment styles and the alerting network of attention, all in a single task. AttentionTrip is an engaging, gamified version of the Attention Network Task, played on an iPad tablet. Attachment styles were assessed using the Experience in Close Relationships-Revised Questionnaire. Robust network scores were observed for each network of attention. Attachment styles were associated with inverse relationships with overall RT. Moreover, orienting and executive control interacted differently with both attachment styles, whereas neither attachment style reliably influenced the alerting network. These findings support the hypothesis that the two forms of insecure attachment are associated with different underlying attentional functioning.

authors_raw: Nicholas Murray, Sarah DeGrace, Melissa Burke, Isabella Bossom, Mikaela Ethier-
title: Gagnon, Raymond Klein, and Sherry Stewart Assessing Attentional Bias for Cannabis Activated by Trauma Cue Exposure in Cannabis Users with Trauma Histories
abstract: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and cannabis use disorder (CUD) co-occur at rates five times higher than chance. An integrated self-medication/attentional bias (iSM-AB) model suggests that trauma reminders may shift attention toward cannabis cues in cannabis users with trauma histories, increasing cravings and intentions to use. This project tests the iSM-AB model using a personalized cue reactivity paradigm and an Addiction Stroop Task. To date, 17 (aiming to continue recruitment for a total of 50) participants completed both sessions. In Session 1, participants completed questionnaires and a semi-structured interview to generate personalized trauma and neutral scripts. In Session 2, participants were exposed to trauma and neutral cues, each followed by an Addiction Stroop Task with cannabis-related and neutral words. Preliminary linear mixed model results indicate that among individuals with higher PTSD symptoms, trauma cue exposure (but not neutral exposure) increases cannabis- related attentional bias (β = 0.43, p = .04). Post-cue negative affect was also positively associated with attentional bias, but only in the trauma condition (r = 0.46, p = .03). These preliminary findings support the iSM-AB model and suggest attentional bias modification training may be best targeted to individuals with more severe PTSD symptoms when preceded by trauma cue exposure. In-House Conference | Friday, May 15, 2026 9 RETURN TO SESSION B SCHEDULE

title: Developing a Deep-Learning Model for Individual Recognition in Canadian Geese: Extending Avian Observational Research Through Computer Vision
abstract: Individual recognition is a foundational cognitive pillar that supports complex social architectures across the animal kingdom, enabling behaviours such as long-term pair bonding, reciprocal altruism, and hierarchy maintenance. Studying these behaviours requires both establishing cognitive capacity and developing non-invasive tools to observe undisturbed behaviour. Avian ethology has emphasized auditory recognition, yet mounting evidence shows visual signals also carry identity information. Work on the Greylag Goose (Anser anser) has demonstrated bill- and face-level individuality with high algorithmic accuracy. The Canadian Goose (Branta canadensis) presents as a similar candidate and remains unexplored. Here we report a computational study comparing two deep-learning architectures trained and tested on identical data, as a step toward non-invasive individual identification. Individually-labelled images were assembled from a reference dataset and from video-derived exports produced by a purpose-built annotation tool. We tuned a ResNet-50 classifier (closed-set assignment) and a MegaDescriptor-L-384 retrieval model (gallery matching). Both reached >90% accuracy on novel test images. Saliency maps emphasized the cheek- patch boundary, bill, and facial regions as candidate identity cues. Together, the results support a passive, camera-based alternative for avian observational research.
